posts.summary: McKenzie documents the first three days of building his first software business, spending about 18 hours coding and setting up infrastructure. He chose GoDaddy for hosting at $10.02 total, rationalizing that he'd break even after one sale. He evaluates payment processors, finding eSellerate's workflow impressive but slow, and discovers Payloadz as a cheaper alternative that works through PayPal. The post reveals his early thinking about conversion optimization — wanting to minimize checkout steps between the 'Buy now' click and credit card entry.
key_points: He launched his first micro-ISV with only $10.02 in hosting costs, deliberately minimizing upfront investment before proving demand
His instinct for conversion optimization was present from day one — every extra page in checkout is 'an opportunity for second thoughts'
He compared payment processors on effective percentage rates at different revenue tiers, showing analytical rigor even at the earliest stage
He rationalized the time investment by comparing it to World of Warcraft — framing entrepreneurship as a more productive use of leisure hours
money_quotes: So I'll be in the black after my first sale, if you assume my time is worth nothing since the 18 hours would otherwise have been spent on an unproductive addiction.
No sense spending serious money on the project before I have some indication that there are actual, living, not-related-to-me people who want to spend money on it.
I don't need a shopping cart — every page that pops up from the time someone clicks my 'Buy now' link is an opportunity for second thoughts.
I want to show the product, pricing, and start getting the credit card information immediately.
Paypal is a trusted name (ok, so maybe not THAT trusted) and people have (hopefully) purchased from one of their shopping carts before, reducing barriers to the sale.
posts.key_insight: Even at the very beginning of his entrepreneurial career, McKenzie's instinct was to minimize friction in the purchase flow and keep costs near zero until customers proved demand — principles that would define his later writing on software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes the origin story of his first micro-ISV (uISV), which grew from noticing an unmet need in the ESL teaching community in Japan. A teacher's email requesting software to automate a tedious classroom task revealed a genuine market gap — no good solutions existed despite the problem being straightforward to solve programmatically. After building a rough prototype in six hours that generated overwhelming demand from a small mailing list, he later decided to rebuild it properly as a commercial product, reaching 70% completion in just four days. The post emphasizes targeting niche markets where major developers won't bother competing.
key_points: Real business ideas come from observing unmet needs in communities you already belong to, not from abstract brainstorming
A prototype built in six hours generated overwhelming demand from a 60-person mailing list, validating the market before any commercial investment
Targeting niche markets is the key strategic advantage for solo developers — problems too small for big companies are perfect for uISVs
99% of the work in simple applications is user interface design, not business logic
Strong design documents written before coding are essential — skipping UI mockups led to spaghetti code in the View layer
money_quotes: One tip: 99% of the work in designing this sort of simple application is user interface.
The main tip I can give is to target a niche market.

There aren't 5 million people in the US who will ever do X in their lives, so its so below the radar screen of major software developers and even uISVs who haven't been teachers themselves that nobody has ever decided to spend ~30 developer hours and do a proper job of X.

I mailed the teacher the program and, on a lark, responded to the mailing list that I had something which would do X and to get a hold of me if you needed it. I had 3 messages by the time I left work.
The next day I showed up to work, opened my inbox, and the folder which handles replies to that mailing list was stuffed.
Please please please let me have it! was the general tone.
Strong design documents, don't leave home without them.
posts.key_insight: The best micro-ISV opportunities hide in niche communities where a straightforward technical solution to a tedious manual task can generate passionate demand precisely because the market is too small for anyone else to bother serving.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes the early bootstrapping of his first software product, Bingo Card Creator, with a self-imposed budget of $60 covering hosting, images, and advertising. He planned to outsource graphic design through Rentacoder but was saved when a talented coworker at his day job in Japan volunteered to create four professional images in ten minutes. The post captures the emotional moment when seeing polished visuals transformed his side project from throwaway code into something that felt like a real business. He also reveals his ambitious timeline of eight days from opening his IDE to launch.
key_points: McKenzie set a strict $60 budget for his entire product launch, broken into hosting, images, and advertising
Professional-looking design assets are critical for credibility but nearly impossible to obtain cheaply through traditional channels
A coworker's spontaneous generosity with graphic design work eliminated the need for outsourcing and saved both time and money
Visual polish was the psychological tipping point that made the project feel like a real business rather than disposable hobby code
The entire project was planned on an eight-day timeline from first code to launch
money_quotes: I am trying to bring the whole thing to market on the cost of one video game purchase or less, which is about $60 to my mind.
Nothing says "professional" like a smartly designed image.
So I decided to complete by transition to Capitalist Robber Baron and outsource.
Surely somewhere, in this wide world of ours, there is a factory where graphics designers sit chained to desk churning out 24×24 icons by the millions for uISVs.
Ten minutes later he sent me the four most beautiful images I've ever seen.

Until those images got delivered, this was just another couple thousand lines of code like I've created and thrown away for a hundred little projects over the years, but visually seeing the splash screen with the little Copyright (c) Me on it makes me feel like a proud expectant father.
posts.key_insight: Professional visual design is the psychological inflection point that transforms a side project from disposable code into something that feels like a real product worth launching.

posts.summary: McKenzie lays out a practical, ground-level guide for aspiring micro independent software vendors (mISVs) covering everything from web hosting to payment processing. Writing at the very start of his own journey into software entrepreneurship, he walks through the essential non-coding tasks: setting up a professional website, SEO and Google AdWords marketing, installer tooling, and choosing a payment processor. The post emphasizes that building the software is only one piece of the puzzle, and that marketing, presentation, and frictionless purchasing matter enormously. He advocates for scrappy, cost-conscious choices while warning against looking cheap where it counts, particularly on your sales website.
key_points: A professional-looking website is essential for converting visitors into paying customers — looking amateurish kills trust at the point of purchase
Google AdWords on niche long-tail keywords can be extraordinarily cheap (a penny per click) because competitors haven't thought to bid on them
Payment processing involves real tradeoffs between merchant accounts (cheaper per-transaction) and third-party processors (simpler setup), with meaningful vendor lock-in risks
Free, open-source tools like NSIS and Launch4j can replace expensive commercial alternatives without sacrificing professionalism
Word of mouth is unavailable to new mISVs, making search engine optimization and paid search the critical early customer acquisition channels
money_quotes: You absolutely, positively need a web site to promote your software. It will probably also be your number one sales channel.
In 2006, having a nice professional looking website is a) not very expensive and b) worth its weight in gold when you're trying to convince someone to hand over their credit card number to you.
With no userbase to start out with you're cut out of the most important marketing tool: word of mouth.

If you've choosen your software niche well, you'll be going up against moderate competition with your main phrases and, this is key, no competition on phrases folks search but haven't thought to market to yet.
Why spend a couple hundred dollars when you can get something functionally identical and very professional looking for free.
Watch your conversion rate soar as you take the hassle out of the critical 'launching the bloody program' step.
posts.key_insight: Building software is the easy part of a micro-ISV — the real work is in the unsexy infrastructure of web presence, search marketing on overlooked long-tail keywords, and frictionless payment processing.

posts.summary: McKenzie outlines his development plan for launching Bingo Card Creator, his first software product. He prioritizes shipping a minimum viable product by deferring features like Print Preview and document templates to version 2.0. The plan covers finishing core printing code, building the website with SEO-optimized content, and setting up advertising through Google AdWords and eBay. He estimates 15 hours of work remaining, targeting a launch by the end of the week.
key_points: Deliberately defers nice-to-have features (Print Preview, templates) to v2.0 in favor of shipping sooner and getting feedback
Website content strategy includes 'SEO bait' sample lesson plans alongside standard product pages
Marketing channels planned from day one: Google AdWords, eBay, Payloadz, and eSellerate
Demonstrates disciplined time estimation and scope management for a solo developer launch
money_quotes: they're wonderful features for my customers but the program is usable and worthwhile without them, and the sooner I can put this out on the Internet the sooner I can start getting feedback
sample lesson plans (SEO bait mostly)
Yay for projects which stay on schedule
posts.key_insight: Ship the minimum viable product quickly to start getting feedback, deferring polish and nice-to-have features to future versions.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes using Open Source Web Design (OSWD) to find a freely available CSS template for his website, since he lacks design skills despite being competent at HTML. He found a Creative Commons licensed design that was 90% of what he needed and could handle the remaining 10% of customization himself. The post reflects his early bootstrapping days where he followed pragmatic shortcuts due to budget constraints, even when they contradicted his own advice to hire experts.
key_points: Open source design resources like OSWD can substitute for hiring a professional designer when on a shoestring budget
Knowing your own limitations (coding competence vs. design incompetence) helps you make better resource allocation decisions
Finding something 90% right and customizing the last 10% is a practical bootstrapping strategy
He deliberately targeted 800x600 resolution for the consumer/educational market he was building for
money_quotes: I'm barely competent enough to pick a color coordinating outfit in the morning, much less design an aesthetically pleasing website.
I found a nice, bright, inviting site design available through the Creative Commons license which was about 90% of what I wanted in a site design.
I am a fairly competent HTML programmer and was going to bang out my web site by myself (contrary to my own advice below, which was hire an expert: I just don't have it in my shoestring budget).
posts.key_insight: When bootstrapping on a tight budget, leveraging open source resources to get 90% of the way there and customizing the rest yourself is a pragmatic alternative to hiring specialists.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his inspiration from Brian Plexico's story of releasing a profitable software product in just seven days. He argues that the key to microISV success is finding an unfilled niche and targeting it aggressively. If you're the sole provider of software that solves a genuine pain point, people will pay regardless of how trivial the problem seems to outsiders. He notes that the world is full of niches that have never attracted someone with basic programming skills.
key_points: A profitable software product can be conceived and released in as little as seven days if the niche is right
Success requires finding an unfilled niche and targeting it aggressively — being the sole provider for a real pain point
The perceived triviality of a problem to outsiders is irrelevant if the people experiencing it are willing to pay
Competition is a later-stage concern; the initial opportunity lies in the vast number of niches no programmer has bothered to serve
money_quotes: If you are the sole source in the world for software to fill your need, and there are people who are actually hurt by that need, then people will be happy to pay you money to ease it for them.
It doesn't matter if the need sounds trivial to people who don't feel it, or whether another developer could do the same thing in their lunch break.
Its a wide, wide world out there and there are an embarassment of niches which have never caught the eye of somebody with rudimentary programming expertise.
I think the key here is a) finding an unfilled niche and b) targetting it aggressively.
posts.key_insight: The world is full of profitable software niches too small or obscure to attract attention, and serving even one of them as the sole provider is enough to build a business.

posts.summary: McKenzie weighs the pros and cons of choosing Java for his micro-ISV (uISV) desktop application, Bingo Card Creator. He systematically addresses common objections—JRE installation requirements, speed concerns, and ugly Swing UIs—and argues each is manageable with practical workarounds. He notes that JRE penetration is higher than critics assume, Launch4j can wrap JARs in native Windows executables, and Java's speed is adequate for his use case. He also highlights Java's cross-platform portability and free development tools as decisive advantages over Visual Studio .NET, which he can't legally use at home.
key_points: JRE installation is less of a barrier than commonly claimed—95% of tracked users had some Java version, and tools like Launch4j can guide users through installation
Java's perceived slowness is irrelevant for his application; startup delay is masked with splash screens that double as upgrade prompts
Cross-platform portability is a genuine advantage, especially for targeting both Mac and Windows from a single codebase
Free tooling (Eclipse, NetBeans) gives Java a decisive edge over Visual Studio .NET for a bootstrapped solo developer who needs legal licenses at home
Practical engineering tradeoffs matter more than theoretical language debates—every objection to Java has a workable mitigation
money_quotes: Getting accurate statistics on Internet users is like herding cats.
It weighs in at 18 MB, which is more than a coffee break.
Java is just not slow at what I am doing if you are using a computer which is anything close to recent.
I display another splash screen for 5 seconds offering them the opportunity to upgrade to the full version before dumping them into the main window.
I'm just not enough of an expert with the Windows APIs to do a GUI in a reasonable amount of time without the .NET safety-net under me.
posts.key_insight: Technology choices for a micro-ISV should be driven by practical constraints like licensing costs, developer familiarity, and cross-platform needs rather than by conventional wisdom about a language's theoretical shortcomings.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his frustrating experience implementing printing functionality in Java for his desktop application. He spent four hours wrestling with Java's printing API, trying multiple approaches including HTML rendering, EditorPane, and finally building a custom printing object. The post highlights how Java's printing capabilities are surprisingly limited once you move beyond plain text on a default printer. He frames this technical difficulty as a competitive advantage, noting it creates a barrier to entry that explains why most competitors use web apps instead.
key_points: Java printing beyond plain text is surprisingly difficult, with a 'dead zone' in the API around printer discovery and configuration
Technical difficulty serves as a barrier to entry — most competitors avoid the problem by building web apps that delegate printing to the browser
After trying three different approaches (HTML printing, EditorPane rendering, custom printing object), the simplest custom approach with hard-won knowledge cut 80% of the code
EditorPane renders HTML differently than browsers and can't handle basic elements like table cell dimensions correctly
money_quotes: Four hours was spent writing printing code, which is suprisingly difficult in Java (as soon as you're not printing plain text on the default printer God help you).
Writing mountains of boring code is one barrier to entry.
Their program, on Download.com, lists as one of its limitations (paraphrased) 'sometimes introduces random output into printed documents'. Thats hopefully something I can avoid.
Applying painful experience that I got along the way, stripped out 80% of my code and had it done in about 2 hours.

There is a dead zone in the Java API as far as printing services are concerned: Java will format the output and get it to the printer for you but you have to accomplish things like, e.g., printer discovery, etc more or less on your own.
posts.key_insight: Tedious technical problems like Java printing serve as natural barriers to entry that protect products built by developers willing to push through the pain.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents reaching feature completeness on his bingo card software, focusing on a frustrating 6.5-hour debugging session fixing text truncation in Java Swing's JLabel component. The bug surfaced when long Japanese/English words overflowed fixed-width containers in print output. He details the technical solution involving custom LayoutManager subclasses and dynamic font resizing. He reflects on whether spending four hours fixing edge cases in a freebie data set was worth it, concluding that the polish differentiates him from competitors and enables more promotional content.
key_points: Java Swing silently truncates text in JLabels without any notification or change to getText(), making the bug only visible in rendered or printed output
The fix required overriding LayoutManager.layoutContainer to dynamically check FontMetrics and resize fonts when text exceeds component width
Japanese-to-English localization creates particularly challenging string width assumptions due to dramatic differences in character physical widths
Spending four hours on edge cases in a freebie data set was justified because it adds polish competitors lack and enables more promotional content
Having a virtual PDF printer saved enormous waste during the iterative debugging of print output issues
money_quotes: Fixing two little edge cases in one little data set in a freebie I'm giving to customers took me four hours.
Was this productive use of my time, given that I could have achieved the same effect just by not including that data set and hoping customers didn't notice on their own?
This adds a bit of spit-and-polish my competitors in many cases lack and allows me to make use of more freebies than I would be able to otherwise.
Localizing Japanese to English and English to Japanese makes for some very fun times with anything that relies on any assumptions about physical width of strings, let me tell you!
The first chance you'll have to notice 'Hey, that looks funny' is when the word appears on the screen like 'Java decided to tr...'
This was pure pain to have to discover but I think its worth the price of admission for this week by itself.
posts.key_insight: Investing hours fixing obscure edge cases in promotional materials is worthwhile because the resulting polish differentiates your product from competitors and expands what you can offer customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie analyzes the competitive landscape for his first software product, Bingo Card Creator, a tool for teachers to generate printable bingo cards. He systematically evaluates existing solutions—free web tools, paid educational software packages, and physical bingo sets—identifying specific usability failures and missing features in each. His core argument is that a well-defined niche (teachers needing quick, customizable bingo card generation) is being underserved despite existing competitors. He positions his product by focusing on workflow efficiency: reducing clicks, saving paper, and letting teachers store word lists.
key_points: The top-ranking competitor requires 75 extra clicks to print a classroom set of cards, revealing how small UX failures create exploitable market gaps
Competing products either bundle bingo as a minor feature in expensive packages or offer free tools with crippling limitations, leaving a gap for a focused, affordable solution
Understanding the customer's actual workflow (paper budgets, font sizes for young students, reusable word lists) matters more than raw feature counts
Non-software alternatives like physical bingo sets prove market demand exists, but are inflexible and expensive per-use compared to software
A microISV's free trial is its most dangerous competitor, a topic he flags for the next post
money_quotes: I'm setting out to do one thing very well and intend to do so, but my product won't be right for everybody.
Printing 25 copies (a classroom worth) requires seventy five additional mouseclicks and 25 page loads. They can't offer an entire lesson in 5 minutes like I can.
Do you know how much money the average school spends on paper every year? I used to work for an office supply company. Our educational orders would boggle your mind.
This is quite possibly an excellent resource for a teacher seeking a package of instructional aids which includes a passable bingo creator, but isn't attractive just for bingo.
All uISVs with a free trial need to understand that they compete with themselves for their customers.
The fact that they do proves the existence of a market.
posts.key_insight: A focused product that solves one specific workflow pain point better than bloated competitors or free tools with poor UX can carve out a viable market niche.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the unveiling of his first software product, Bingo Card Creator, after just five days of development. The product lets users print custom bingo cards for educational purposes. He explains his deliberate decision to target the educational market rather than the gambling-adjacent bingo market, noting that gambling bingo has high barriers to entry including advertising restrictions on search engines. The post captures the earliest days of what would become one of the most instructive micro-ISV case studies in the indie software world.
key_points: Built a fully functional product in roughly five days from zero code to working software
Chose the product name 'Bingo Card Creator' deliberately for organic search optimization
Made a strategic early decision to target educational users rather than the gambling bingo market
Discovered that search engines restrict paid advertising for gambling products, creating unexpected barriers to entry in the gambling niche
Structured the product around customization — users supply their own word lists or choose from provided educational lists
money_quotes: It seems unreal that something like five days ago I had no code and today I have a fully functional program to do what I set out to do.
Bingo Card Creator lets you print custom bingo cards on your own PC or Mac.
I made a decision very early on that I was going to target primarily educational users.
There is obviously a gigantic market out there for bingo as a gambling product — it is well served, has high barriers to entry.
Many search engines don't let you use paid search to promote gambling — I'm applying for a dispensation as we speak.
posts.key_insight: Choosing a narrow, underserved niche (educational bingo) over a large but well-defended market (gambling bingo) was a deliberate strategic decision that shaped the entire product from day one.

posts.summary: McKenzie outlines his conversion funnel strategy for Bingo Card Creator, moving visitors from free PDF downloads to trial software to paid purchases. He details five specific trial limitations designed to let users verify the product works while making it insufficient for actual classroom use. He explains why a usage-based limit (number of cards) works better than a time-based trial for his product, since the value proposition is saving time during recurring classroom needs. He also makes the case for offering an unconditional money-back guarantee, arguing it costs almost nothing while reducing purchase anxiety for buyers dealing with an unknown internet vendor.
key_points: Free resources serve as top-of-funnel content that drives organic search traffic into a natural progression toward the paid product

Trial limitations should be designed around the product's core value proposition — for a classroom tool, limiting card count below class size makes the trial verifiably functional but practically useless
A money-back guarantee costs nothing when you're not making sales, and roughly 10% of one sale's profit when you are — a trivial cost that reduces purchase friction
Most dissatisfied customers self-select during the free trial, so refund requests remain rare by design
Social psychology works in the seller's favor: most people feel deeply uncomfortable asking for refunds, making guarantee abuse a non-issue
money_quotes: You can't go to your room of 25 3rd graders and say 'Sorry kids, I know we were supposed to play bingo today but you're going to have to share 15 cards'.
A guarantee costs me absolutely nothing if I'm not already making money.
Every time they are in a crisis and turn to my program for a fun, rewarding lesson whipped up in a jiffy, thats another opportunity for me to make a sale.
The maximally finicky ones will self-select away from sending me money.
Handing over your money to a no-name stranger on the Internet can be a scary experience. The guarantee reduces the perceived risk of that action to very close to zero.
If it motivates more than 1 marginal person to convert for every 10 people who excercize their right to return the software, the guarantee has made me money.
posts.key_insight: Trial limitations should be calibrated so the product demonstrably works but remains practically useless for the customer's real need, creating natural purchase pressure without antagonizing users.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie walks through two methods of estimating market size for his Bingo Card Creator software, aimed at convincing skeptics (starting with his father) that a niche exists. Using a top-down approach, he starts with 1.5 million US elementary school teachers and winnows through filters — bingo usage, internet access, willingness to pay, and budget authority — arriving at roughly 15,000 potential customers. He then validates with a bottom-up approach using Yahoo search volume data, estimating about 2,000 unique monthly searchers for educational bingo software. Both methods confirm a viable niche exists, though he's careful to distinguish market size from sales projections.
key_points: Top-down market sizing starts with a large identifiable population and applies successive filters to narrow to realistic buyers
Bottom-up estimation using search volume data provides an independent validation of market size from a completely different angle
The gap between theoretical market size and actual sales is enormous — most potential customers will never find you, never trust you, or never complete a purchase
Free alternatives are the biggest competitive threat in niche software, eliminating the majority of potential customers before price even enters the equation
Having access to real data (BLS statistics, search volume tools) beats guessing, but educated guesses are still necessary for the filters in between
money_quotes: Patrick, you know I love you, but how many teachers are there that play bingo?
One method of estimating a market starts with identifying your minimum qualifying feature and winnowing from there.
There are 1,486,650 elementary school teachers in the United States. Well, that was certainly easy. So I'm going to sell 1.5 million copies? No.
Free is probably good enough for most teachers — call it 60% that would never spend a penny on bingo software.
If I can sell to 1 in 10 of them, my market size is about 1,500 users.

That is, incidentally, not a sales projection – there are other gates they have to make it through, from not buying from a competitor first to finding my software exists to installing it to handing their credit card numbers over to a barely trusted party.
There does indeed exist a niche to target. Can I do it successfully? Well, we'll find out soon.
posts.key_insight: Market sizing for niche software requires ruthlessly winnowing a large addressable population through realistic filters — willingness to pay, ability to pay, and likelihood of finding you — which typically reduces your market by two orders of magnitude from the headline number.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces the beta launch of his Bingo Card Creator website, built with minimal tools (Notepad and a free design template) in about four hours. He shares his detailed pre-launch checklist covering website pages, payment processing setup, data files, and platform support. The post captures the scrappy, ship-fast ethos of a solo developer getting a product to market. He candidly admits to forgetting about needing a software license until hours before launch.
key_points: A functional product website can be built with free tools and minimal time investment — perfection is not required for launch
The 'beta' label serves as permission to ship something half-finished and iterate
Payment processing logistics (Payloadz, eSellerate) are real friction points for solo software businesses
Strategic decisions about free vs. paid content tiers matter even for simple products
Last-minute oversights like forgetting a software license are normal parts of the solo founder experience
money_quotes: If you're wondering how good of a website you can get accomplished with Notepad, a free design from www.oswd.org, and about four hours of tinkering… well, here's your chance to find out!
Half is good enough to slap the word "beta" on and shove it out the door.
I believe it to be of shippable quality, at least on PCs.
Unlikely I'll be getting many registrations on launch day.
It just hit me about three hours ago that I should probably include a license. I'm mildly panic stricken as to the prospect.
posts.key_insight: Launching a software product as a solo developer means shipping something imperfect quickly and solving problems as they arise, rather than waiting for everything to be polished.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a development update as he nears the launch of his first software product, Bingo Card Creator. After a burst of energy-fueled coding, he reports the Windows version is complete with bugs fixed, the website is nearly done, payment processing is mostly configured, and a Google AdWords campaign is ready to launch. The total project has taken roughly 45 hours of development time. He expresses particular pride in his Free Resources page as a marketing strategy.
key_points: The entire product from conception to near-launch took approximately 45 hours of development time, demonstrating the lean approach to building a micro-ISV
Last-minute bug discovery came from testing edge cases he personally never encountered due to his own consistent usage patterns — a common blind spot for solo developers
The Free Resources page is identified as the single best idea in the project, hinting at his early understanding of content marketing and SEO as acquisition channels
Multiple payment processors (PayPal/Payloadz and eSellerate) are being set up simultaneously to avoid single points of failure
He is ready to spend launch day actively experimenting with paid acquisition via Google AdWords and Yahoo ads
money_quotes: I succumbed to a bit of a crunch time mentality even though this is supposed to be a non-crunch activity.
This brings the total time spent on this project to something in the vicinity of 45 hours, not including the daydreaming I have been allowing myself at work.
I quashed three bugs discovered in last minute testing (user interface things that I hadn't realized *could* exist because I generally use certain interface elements in a consistent way).
If I do say so myself, the Free Resources page is my best single idea in this entire project.
I wasn't expecting a large stream of site licenses to start pouring in.
posts.key_insight: A functional software product can go from concept to launch-ready in under 50 hours when you keep scope tight, ship fast, and focus your creative energy on marketing strategies like free resource pages rather than over-engineering the product.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his early micro-ISV expenses, totaling just $32.22 to launch a business, and describes a marketing insight about selling Fourth of July bingo cards on eBay. He identifies that millions of consumers will search for holiday activities in a compressed three-day window, creating a traffic spike he couldn't afford to buy through advertising. He plans to price the product at $9.99 to capture impulse purchases, calculating roughly $7 profit per sale after fees. The post captures the scrappy, analytical mindset of a bootstrapped founder calculating every dollar while hunting for asymmetric marketing opportunities.
key_points: Total business launch costs were just $32.22, split between a fax, Google AdWords deposit, and domain registration — illustrating how cheaply a micro-ISV could start in 2006
Seasonal search spikes around holidays create predictable surges in demand that can be exploited with targeted products
eBay's existing market of home consumers buying bingo cards at $8+$5 shipping validates demand while representing a different segment than his core teacher market
The downside risk is trivial (the cost of five AdWords clicks) while the upside could make the business cash-flow positive on day two
money_quotes: 2000 yen = $17.20! Thats almost a third of my budget, and while I'll make it up on my first transaction it was rather irksome.
This brings my total expenditure to date to $32.22.
If you check the search engine results, you get crazy spikes around all major holidays for exactly those seasonal bingo games — strange but true!
Short time frame for one-use impulse-purchase Americana… I SMELL EBAY!
Millions of them are going to be searching for Fourth of July activities over a three day period. I can't even buy that kind of traffic.
If it turns out to have been a stupid idea, oh well, I'm out the price of about five clicks from Google AdWords.
And wouldn't that be a funny thing to announce on day two of owning your own business.
posts.key_insight: The best early-stage marketing bets have trivial downside and asymmetric upside — a seasonal product targeting a predictable demand spike costs almost nothing to test but could make the business profitable immediately.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts a brief cautionary tale about accidentally overwriting critical data files by using the wrong mv command variant during deployment preparation. The post argues that even solo developers should use source control, illustrated by his own mistake of confusing two similar but functionally different shell commands. He notes the incident cost him three hours but expresses confidence in still meeting his launch deadline thanks to schedule slack.
key_points: A subtle difference between mv commands can clobber existing files in a deployment directory
Even solo developers need source control as a safety net against their own mistakes
Building slack into project schedules provides crucial buffer for recovering from unexpected setbacks
money_quotes: One of puts some critical data files into the directory I'm about to zip for deployment. The other puts some critical data files into the directory I'm about to zip for deployment… and clobbers the identically named data files already there.
Can you spot the difference? This addled Windows programmer did about two seconds too late.
Yay for building some slack into the schedule.
posts.key_insight: Source control is essential even for solo developers because humans inevitably make destructive mistakes, and version history is your cheapest insurance policy.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares the raw statistics from building Bingo Card Creator in its first week of development. The post reveals a remarkably lean operation: 47 hours of work, 2,226 lines of Java code, and a total budget where the most expensive item was a $17.20 international fax. The brevity of the post itself mirrors the minimalist approach to launching the product.
key_points: The entire product was built in 47 hours split across programming (31), website (10), and payment processor setup (6)
The codebase was modest at 2,226 lines of Java with roughly 50 total classes
The most expensive cost was a $17.20 fax between Japan and Nebraska for a contract, highlighting the near-zero startup costs of a micro-ISV
money_quotes: Hours worked: 47 hours (programming 31, website 10, payment processors 6)
Most expensive line item on budget: $17.20. One 10 page fax of a contract from Gifu Prefecture to Nebraska.
posts.key_insight: A viable software product can be launched with minimal investment — 47 hours of work and less than $20 in expenses — demonstrating the extraordinarily low barrier to entry for micro-ISV businesses.

posts.summary: McKenzie uses an extended metaphor of cows, milk, cookies, and pails to explain his conversion strategy for Bingo Card Creator. Rather than trying to sell software directly to people searching for bingo card software, he attracts teachers searching for free classroom activities (cookies), then guides them through increasingly valuable free offerings until they hit a natural limit that requires purchase. The strategy targets users who don't know they need his product by solving their immediate problem first, then revealing the paid solution at the exact moment of need. He argues this approach will generate far more conversions than marketing to people already shopping for bingo card software.
key_points: Free samples should attract people searching for solutions to their actual problem, not people shopping for your product category
The conversion funnel should escalate commitment gradually: free web sample → free download → purchase, with each step proving value before asking for more
The paywall should appear at the exact moment of demonstrated need, not before the user understands the product's value
Most potential customers don't know your product category exists, so market the problem you solve rather than the solution you sell
money_quotes: We're all sitting by the side of the road with a big sign saying 'Get your free milk!' Sometimes somebody ambles down, sees our sign, and says 'Sure, I'll take some free milk.' And then he drinks it and leaves before we can even tell him how well-tempered of a cow she is.
I am not in the business of giving away milk, or selling cows. My philosophy with Bingo Card Creator is that I give away cookies.
Few people get up in the morning and say 'I think I'll go take a stroll around the neighborhood to find an independent farmer to buy milk from'.
Put up a sign saying free cookies and you'll be out of them before you can say boo.
She came to my site because I offer free activities to do exactly what she wants to do.
She's got the proof literally in her hands by this point if she printed the PDFs.
I anticipate this process will generate a heck of a lot more conversions over time than actually selling to people who know they're in the market for 'make my own printable bingo cards'.
posts.key_insight: The most effective conversion strategy attracts users searching for solutions to their immediate problem, delivers escalating free value to build trust, then reveals the purchase option at the precise moment of demonstrated need.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes an early day running his Bingo Card Creator business, making site improvements based on community feedback and experimenting with Google AdWords targeting. He fixed an Internet Explorer display bug and wrote highly targeted ad copy using niche vocabulary that only reading teachers or homeschool mothers would recognize. After leaving the business to run itself for six hours, he returned to find an impressively high click-through rate on his targeted ad, validating his approach of writing ads that self-select their audience.
key_points: Highly targeted ad copy that uses niche vocabulary can self-select the right audience, filtering out irrelevant clicks
Offering a freebie in the ad title combined with a pain point ('Tired of paying $10 each') drives engagement
Even a one-person software business can run itself while the founder steps away, generating data passively
Early iteration on a micro-ISV involves constant small optimizations across site design, ads, and conversion funnels
money_quotes: Let the business run itself for six hours.
Its almost guaranteed to hit either a reading teacher or a homeschool mother because nobody else knows one of the words in it.
Tired of paying $10 each for vocab cards? Try out a better way for free.
Holy mother of God.
Now I just need to keep optimizing the web site to get my reading teachers to convert… and then start figuring out obscure words known only by elementary math teachers.
posts.key_insight: The most effective ads use language so specific to the target audience that they act as a filter, ensuring only the right people click through.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on setting up conversion tracking for his newly launched Bingo Card Creator website. He distinguishes between two key conversions: trial downloads and paid purchases. After just 30 hours live, he has no sales yet but is seeing demo downloads, mostly from Joel on Software readers. He praises Google AdWords for making tracking easy and notes his ad costs are remarkably low, with keywords ranging from sub-nickel to 25 cents.
key_points: Conversion tracking should distinguish between intermediate actions (demo downloads) and final goals (purchases), and both matter for understanding your funnel
Google AdWords offered incredibly cheap keyword costs for niche markets, with many keywords under 5 cents and the most expensive at 25 cents
Even with the most expensive keywords, profitability requires only a slightly-over-1% conversion rate, making the economics highly favorable
Early traffic sources need to be separated (paid ads vs. organic vs. referral) to understand what's actually working
money_quotes: Conversion means when somebody takes the action you desire when you market to them.

So now I can track whether demo downloaders are me (doesn't count), sent in by my Google AdWords ads (which is a percentage I'm *keenly* interested in, for obvious reasons), or whether they came in from "organic" (not-paid-for) searches or even links.
Google made this all stupidly easy to set up.
I'm profitable with my most expensive words at a final conversion rate of slightly over 1%, and I'm profitable at my least expensive words with a conversion rate of, well, anything really.
The kind of words I'm targetting typically cap out at about 10-15 cents with many in the sub-nickle range, with my most expensive ones being about a quarter.
posts.key_insight: Niche keyword markets on Google AdWords can offer such low costs per click that even modest conversion rates make paid acquisition profitable from day one.

posts.summary: McKenzie compares Yahoo and Google's advertising platforms after signing up for Yahoo's promotional credit. He systematically demonstrates why Google dominates online advertising through superior keyword tools, cleaner URL display, easier ad creation and testing, faster approval times, and the inclusion of Google Analytics. The post uses his direct experience as a small software business owner to illustrate the practical differences between the two platforms. His conclusion is implicit but clear: Google's advertising infrastructure is vastly more sophisticated and user-friendly than Yahoo's.
key_points: Google's keyword suggestion tools are far superior, finding semantically related terms that yield cheaper, less competitive advertising opportunities
Google enables A/B testing of ad variations with automatic optimization, while Yahoo requires repeating the entire setup process
Google Analytics bundled with AdWords provides conversion tracking and ROI measurement that Yahoo completely lacks
Small details like URL display and approval speed compound into a vastly better advertiser experience on Google
money_quotes: It was pretty immediately apparent why Google is kicking Yahoo's hindquarters.
Those words are cheaper than anything as nobody is targetting obscure teaching terminology… except me.
Google Analytics is worth keeping an AdWords account running for even if nobody ever converts from it, because it beats whatever web stats software you are currently using hands down.
Google will have you up in minutes. Yahoo will have you up and running in 72 hours.
If you've already got one ad in a keyword group, throw in another and Google will rotate them for you trying to find the one that gets clicked on most. Yahoo, not so much.
posts.key_insight: Google's advertising dominance stems not from any single feature but from compounding advantages in tooling, speed, analytics, and advertiser experience that make Yahoo's platform feel like a generation behind.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes adding a 'Wizards menu' feature to Bingo Card Creator to simplify the user experience of accessing pre-loaded word lists. The original four-step file navigation process was too complex for his non-technical target users. He built the feature in about an hour late at night, using a Factory pattern for extensibility. The post illustrates his philosophy of reducing friction for end users and designing for future feature expansion from the start.
key_points: Four steps to open a file is trivial for programmers but a significant barrier for non-technical users
Good OO design with extensibility in mind enables rapid feature development comparable to frameworks like Rails
Investing small amounts of extra time in patterns like Factory classes pays off by enabling future features without refactoring
New features should be evaluated not just for current utility but as potential market expansions (e.g., math problem generators)
money_quotes: Sure, four steps is nothing for a computer programmer, but my users… aren't.
If you start with extensibility and code reuse in mind, Java or any other OO language can be used to rapidly code in new features almost as quickly as Rails or the other new hotness these days.
If you don't, you end up with hundreds of classes full of totally unmaintainable spaghetti code.
Why code a factory when I could have just subclassed JMenuItem for a single purpose? Because with a Factory I can decide tomorrow to make another type of wizard.
Hey, wait, I think there could be a market for that one…
posts.key_insight: Small investments in extensible design and user-experience simplification compound into significant product advantages over time.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his Bingo Card Creator software has reached the first page of Google search results for its target keyword, ranking #8 through his Payloadz download listing. However, he tempers the excitement by noting the listing isn't ideal because the Payloadz web store design is suboptimal. He identifies this as something to optimize but acknowledges limited control over a third-party storefront.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator reached page one of Google for 'bingo card creator' within 11 days of launch
The ranking came through a third-party payment processor (Payloadz) rather than his own site
He recognizes that ranking alone isn't enough — the landing experience matters for conversions
He notes the tradeoff of using third-party platforms: limited control over presentation but beneficial backlinks
money_quotes: If you search "bingo card creator", I'm now result #8 through my download link at Payloadz.
Its not that useful to me, though, because the web store design is not so hot.
posts.key_insight: Ranking on Google's first page matters less than the quality of the page users actually land on.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his first 48 hours of Google AdWords results for Bingo Card Creator, breaking down two ad groups: one targeting people directly searching for bingo card creation, and another targeting elementary teachers searching for Dolch sight word lists. He reveals his early click-through rates, cost-per-click strategies, and ad copy variations. The post demonstrates his nascent but already sharp thinking about keyword arbitrage — finding underpriced search terms that reach his exact customer. He's testing multiple ad variations and setting up conversion tracking, laying the groundwork for the data-driven marketing approach he'd later become known for.
key_points: Two distinct ad groups target different customer intents: direct product searches at $0.20/click vs. niche educational terminology at $0.04-0.10/click
Keyword arbitrage exists because competitors bid on obvious terms ('sight words') but not domain-specific synonyms ('Dolch words') that teachers actually search for
Ad copy that speaks the customer's specific language outperforms generic ads, yielding click-through rates several multiples above industry average
Google's automatic optimization of ad variations means testing multiple versions costs nothing extra while generating valuable data
The gambling industry inflates costs for any keyword containing 'bingo', creating a pricing distortion that savvy niche marketers can work around
money_quotes: Surprisingly, folks trying to sell to first grade reading teachers don't speak First Grade Reading teacher.
They bid up 'sight words' but not 'Dolch words' despite the fact that the two are synonymous to my customers.
There is money in gambling, which drives up the price of any string involving 'bingo', but there is a lot less money in teaching first graders to read.

Dolch is an education bigwig from the 50s who came up with the eponymous list of 220 most common English words, and if you're not an elementary reading teacher you will NEVER hear his name in your life but if you are he has name recognition approaching that of Einstein.
If you prep more than you teach, try out a better way for free.
What, you thought teachers actually stayed home in the summer? Ha, ha, ha, good one.
posts.key_insight: The biggest marketing advantage for niche software comes from speaking your customer's specialized language better than competitors do, finding underpriced keywords that map perfectly to customer intent.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on the early metrics of his Bingo Card Creator software business, noting that only 6% of visitors and 10% of paid visitors download a trial. At roughly $1 per trial download, he calculates he needs at least a 5% trial-to-purchase conversion rate to break even. He identifies landing page optimization as the key lever to improve these economics.
key_points: Only 6% of all visitors and 10% of paid traffic converted to trial downloads, establishing a baseline conversion rate
The cost per trial download worked out to approximately $1, setting a clear economic constraint on the business
A 5% trial-to-purchase conversion rate is the minimum threshold needed to make the unit economics work
Landing page optimization is identified as the primary lever for improving the funnel
money_quotes: Since I added my conversion checking code roughly 6% of all visitors (and 10%ish of the ones I pay for) actually download a trial.
To make any money at that rate at all, I need to have a conversion of trial downloads to purchasers above 5%.
I really need to work on my landing pages I think.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software businesses live or die by their conversion funnels, and understanding the math connecting acquisition cost to trial-to-purchase conversion is essential for viability.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes posting a free classified ad on a popular teacher message board to promote his Bingo Card Creator software. Despite low expectations about anyone clicking through to find his software, he received a response within two hours. He notes the SEO benefit of the profile links and his willingness to provide hands-on customer support when the respondent needed help.
key_points: Free marketing channels like classified ads on niche community boards can yield surprisingly fast results
Even low-effort marketing tactics have secondary SEO benefits through profile links
Early-stage software businesses require hands-on customer support and willingness to 'handhold' users
money_quotes: I wasn't seriously expecting anyone to drill down two levels to get to my software, but hey, what could it have hurt?
They needed a little handholding, so I handheld.
posts.key_insight: Free, low-effort marketing experiments in niche communities can generate customer contact surprisingly quickly, and early founders should be ready to provide personal support when they do.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes creating an optimized landing page for a specific AdWords ad group targeting teachers searching for Dolch sight words. He's tailoring the page as a sales presentation for people already in the mood for classroom bingo activities. He notes with self-aware humor that he's beginning to think like an SEO professional, using descriptive anchor text and seeking quality inbound links.
key_points: Creating targeted landing pages for specific ad groups improves conversion by matching visitor intent
Even when your product isn't exactly what someone searched for, honest ad copy can still capture relevant demand
SEO thinking — descriptive anchor text, quality inbound links — becomes instinctive once you start optimizing for search
money_quotes: I spent a bit of time making basically a sales presentation to a person in the mood for Dolch Sight Word Bingo.
I am starting to think like an SEO person. This is beginning to freak me out.
posts.key_insight: Effective online marketing means creating dedicated landing pages that match the specific intent of each ad group rather than sending all traffic to a generic homepage.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a quick UX improvement to his Bingo Card Creator website after his brother couldn't find the download button. He created a more visible download button using open-source clip art and plans to apply similar visual improvements to other trial and buy links across the site. He also notes he's removing links to broken features until they're fixed.
key_points: A real user (his brother) couldn't find the download button, revealing a usability problem
He used open-source clip art to create a more prominent call-to-action button
He plans to add similar visual buttons to all trial and buy links across the site
Broken feature links are being hidden until the features actually work
money_quotes: My little brother, no computer slouch, asked to see my website and couldn't find the download button. Thats a bug, not a feature.
I expect I'll be buttonizing my trial and buy links.
The links for the two things which are currently broken are going away until they're not broken anymore.
posts.key_insight: If a competent user can't find your download button, that's a conversion-killing usability bug that needs an immediate, visible fix.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his newly registered domain has reached position #4 on Google for 'Bingo Card Creator' after just two weeks, achieved partly through soliciting links from education sites that collect resources for teachers. He distinguishes between ranking for his brand name versus his target commercial keyword ('make bingo cards'), showing early understanding of search intent and keyword value. He recommends Google Sitemaps as a tool for ensuring clean crawling.
key_points: Link building through relevant niche directories (education resource sites) can produce fast SEO results even for brand-new domains
Ranking for your brand name is far less valuable than ranking for the commercial intent keyword people actually search for
Being transparent about commercial intent when soliciting links is both ethical and apparently effective
Google Sitemaps helps ensure error-free crawling, which is foundational for new site SEO
money_quotes: I'm aiming for 100% honesty in the process so I always picked commercial software and made it clear the link was an advertisement.
Apparently that just paid off, because my daily search for Bingo Card Creator puts my home page at #4 on the Internet (!) after that domain has been registered for approximately two weeks (!!)
Now, thats not quite my target search term ("make bingo cards" gets searched far, far more often, as I'm gradually learning from paying for people searching for it)
You want to get crawled without any errors — make it easy for Google to do so as soon as humanly possible.
posts.key_insight: Early SEO wins from branded searches feel exciting but the real value comes from ranking for commercial-intent keywords that actual customers are searching for.

posts.summary: McKenzie identifies a business opportunity for a micro-ISV to clone Payloadz's digital delivery service but built on Google Checkout instead of PayPal. The core appeal is Google Checkout's 2% cost advantage through its AdWords tie-in, where every $1 spent on AdWords offsets $10 in processing fees. He also debates whether to build his own delivery system to replace Payloadz, concluding the $15/month savings isn't worth the effort but the ability to customize post-purchase redirects might be. The post closes with a resigned acknowledgment that success in his business may depend more on adapting to Google's ecosystem than on product quality.
key_points: Google Checkout offered a meaningful cost advantage over PayPal through its AdWords credit tie-in, effectively eliminating processing fees for AdWords advertisers
Build-vs-buy decisions for early micro-ISVs should weigh not just cost savings but also the flexibility limitations of third-party tools
McKenzie already recognized Google's outsized influence on small software businesses, noting success would come from adapting to Google's ecosystem as much as from product quality
money_quotes: I would gladly opt for Google Checkout over Paypal if customers could be convinced to use it, for one reason and one reason alone: its going to be 2% extra profit on every sale because of their tie-in with AdWords.
The question is: does saving perhaps $15 a month justify the amount of effort it will take me to hack together some Perl scripts to do exactly the same thing Payloadz does? Answer: No.

Is the hacking justified by it letting me get around stupid limitations in the Payloadz UI, like being unable to redirect my customers to my site after the order has completed? Answer: Quite possibly.
I think if this ever becomes successful it will be as much because I successfully adapted to The Big G as because I have a quality piece of software.
posts.key_insight: For early-stage micro-ISVs, adapting to the dominant platform ecosystem matters as much as product quality, and payment processing economics can meaningfully shift which platforms win.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a brief accounting update on his Bingo Card Creator side project, noting he's spent about 10 hours and $43 total since launch. He describes two free marketing channels he discovered: PAD files for automatic software directory listings, and teacher bulletin boards from the mid-90s that accept commercial software posts. The bulletin board strategy yielded high-PageRank inbound links and actual customer inquiries. He reflects with surprise that honest, clearly-labeled advertising actually generates responses.
key_points: Total project expenditure remains remarkably low at $43.22 across Google, Yahoo, and eBay advertising experiments
PAD files provide free, automated exposure across software download sites with minimal effort — a high-leverage tactic for shareware developers
Niche community bulletin boards from the mid-90s offer free posting opportunities with high PageRank backlinks and real customer engagement
Honest, straightforward advertising in the right context can outperform expectations — people do read ads when they're properly classified
money_quotes: Since launch I've probably spent ~10 hours (some of them at work — sorry boss!) obsessing over the project and doing various tweaks here and there.
That was a great deal of exposure for not a penny and not a lot of effort.
I highly recommend anybody who writes consumer-oriented shareware to give this a whirl.
Completely on the up and up and got me several nice inbound links from sites with obscene PRs (a handful of 7s and a bag full of 6s).
People actually read advertisements when they are classified as advertisements? That amazes me, but it really shouldn't seeing that I'm paying for folks to do it every day.
Its heartening to know you can still do well for yourself by being honest with folks.
posts.key_insight: Free, honest promotion in niche communities where your target audience already gathers can yield disproportionate returns in both SEO value and actual customer interest.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experiment selling Bingo Card Creator software on eBay as a secondary sales channel. He dropped the price to $14.95 to compete with physical bingo card products, noting that eBay fees eat into margins but production and shipping costs remain zero. He acknowledges eBay sales won't scale meaningfully but views it as a way to test selling copy and generate small amounts of cash for advertising. The post reflects the scrappy, experimental mindset of a bootstrapper willing to try unconventional channels.
key_points: eBay fees (listing + percentage cuts) significantly reduce per-sale profit compared to direct website sales ($9-10 vs $20)
Zero marginal cost for digital products means even low-margin channels can be worth experimenting with
eBay serves as a testing ground for sales copy even though the audience differs from his target market
Early-stage bootstrappers need to generate any revenue they can to fund advertising and infrastructure
money_quotes: shipping cost is still zero and production costs are still zero, which means I still clear ~9-10 profit per sale
At current volumes, there is no way it will ever be worth my time.
it lets me quickly try out selling copy
Sure, I could fund this project with my day job until the sun goes nova, but whats the fun in that?
posts.key_insight: Even unprofitable sales channels can serve as cheap testing grounds for copy and messaging when you're bootstrapping a software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes removing nag screens from his Bingo Card Creator software after realizing they were counterproductive. He had 5-second delays at program start and end, but recognized that his existing conversion mechanism—prompting users when they attempt restricted features—was far more effective. He also added a help menu link to the website that doubles as a tracking mechanism to distinguish registered from unregistered users.
key_points: Nag screens that delay users before they even reach the main screen are counterproductive to conversion
Context-sensitive purchase prompts triggered by restricted actions are a superior sales mechanism to blanket nagging
Tracking user behavior through clever URL routing provides actionable data on software usage
Early-stage software businesses struggle to tell if anyone is actually using the product
money_quotes: With that in place, why annoy people so much that they never get to the main screen?

I have a much, much better sales mechanism built in: every time the user tries to do one of my forbidden actions they get the error message and a gentle prompt that takes them straight to my purchasing screen.
That way I can see if anybody is actually using my software, which is hard to tell now unless they're one of the two people who routinely click the 'purchase this now' button and then do nothing.
posts.key_insight: Friction-based nag screens drive users away before they experience value, while context-sensitive purchase prompts at the moment of need are a far more effective conversion mechanism.

posts.summary: McKenzie reflects on a weekend camping trip while his Bingo Card Creator software business ran unattended, highlighting a key advantage of software over physical businesses. He outlines his priorities for the week: creating more product wizards, optimizing his Dolch sight words landing page, updating his sitemap for Google, expanding into non-reading bingo use cases, and launching eBay listings. The post reveals his early-stage marketing experimentation with SEO, content marketing, and paid advertising channels.
key_points: Software businesses can run unattended unlike physical franchises, but his wasn't yet generating meaningful sales
His Dolch Sight Words keyword strategy underperformed expectations despite cheap advertising costs, teaching him that cheap traffic isn't necessarily effective traffic
He planned to use free content (word lists) as lead generation to attract links and search traffic
He was experimenting across multiple sales channels simultaneously: Google Ads, SEO, content marketing, and eBay listings
money_quotes: One of the nice things about running an uISV as opposed to, say, a fast food franchise is if you decide to take the weekend off to go camping the "store" keeps happily running by itself.
Unlike your local McDonalds I have neither billions and billions served, nor actual sales.
My clickthrough rate on those advertisements is above 10%, and roughly 25-50% of clickers go on to download the demo.
At the worst I'll get some people to link to me just to get the word lists, and perhaps some minor search engine traffic.
All it requires is a GUI and a for loop.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software businesses benefit from relentless experimentation across multiple marketing channels, using free content as a funnel even when individual tactics underperform expectations.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on early conversion rate optimization efforts for his Bingo Card Creator software. He describes improvements from tracking conversions more reliably, cutting underperforming keywords, and optimizing landing page design. His trial download conversion rates sit at 15% from Google and 10% from Yahoo, with wide variance by keyword. He calculates that at $1 per trial download, he needs a 5% trial-to-purchase conversion to break even.
key_points: Graphical download buttons doubled conversion rates compared to text links, demonstrating how small design changes have outsized impact
Conversion rates vary dramatically by keyword intent: 40% for high-intent 'make your own bingo card' versus 5% for generic teacher-related terms
At $1 cost per trial download, the unit economics require 5% of trial users to convert to paid customers for profitability
Continuous optimization across keywords, tracking, and design is the core strategy rather than any single big bet
money_quotes: Having a graphical button increases your visitor's propensity to download by 100% versus having an understated text link.
I convert at close to 40% for 'make your own bingo card' and ~5% for generic elementary school teacher words.
My cost per conversion is about a dollar.
At $1 per trial download, I need to convince 5% of buyers to pay up to make a profit at my price point.
Thats, eh, possible I guess. We'll see. Constant tweaking will continue.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software marketing is a game of continuous small optimizations across ad spend, landing page design, and keyword targeting, where each incremental improvement compounds toward viable unit economics.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports his first organic search traffic to Bingo Card Creator from non-branded queries. He describes getting a hit from MSN for 'free Dolch sight word lists,' content he deliberately published as search engine bait to attract potential customers. The strategy of offering free educational content to capture search traffic and convert visitors into paying customers is working as intended.
key_points: First organic search traffic arrived from non-branded queries, marking a milestone for the business
McKenzie deliberately published free Dolch sight word lists as SEO bait, converting previously gated content into public pages
The content marketing strategy is simple: offer free educational resources that teachers search for, then convert visitors into paying customers
money_quotes: I decided to put the actual list up for search engine bait and hope some people convert while they're reading.
posts.key_insight: Publishing free, searchable content that your target audience actively seeks is a viable customer acquisition strategy, even for a brand-new microISV.

posts.summary: McKenzie outlines his plan to localize Bingo Card Creator for the Japanese market, where he lives. He built the software with internationalization from the start, making the technical work trivial. The real opportunity is an unserved market of English teachers in Japan who need classroom materials. The main challenges are localizing the marketing website and finding payment processors that support Japanese payment methods, not the software itself.
key_points: Building software with localization support from day one makes internationalization nearly trivial later
The Japanese market for English teaching materials is completely unserved, representing a greenfield opportunity
Marketing localization is far harder than product localization — writing persuasive copy in another language is a different skill than translating UI strings
Payment processing in Japan requires supporting alternative payment methods beyond credit cards
Advertising costs for niche keywords in non-English markets can be dramatically lower due to less competition
money_quotes: My market is underserved in the US, but it just isn't served at all in Japan.
Think of it: how many people are bidding on English teaching keywords in Japan? Answer: not really that many, except folks recruiting for jobs.
I'm a lot less confident in my ability to market in Japanese than in my ability to write a Save As dialog in Japanese.
Going by the results of my marketing in English, I've not exactly got natural talent for it.
Credit card penetration is not quite 100% here (there are a witches brew of microcurrencies and alternative payment methods).
posts.key_insight: The hard part of entering a foreign market isn't translating the product — it's translating the marketing and adapting to local payment infrastructure.

posts.summary: McKenzie briefly updates on setting up his eSellerate account for selling software. He finds the mechanics of listing a product straightforward, estimating he could do it in under 30 minutes. However, he criticizes eSellerate's checkout flow for requiring too many page clicks, noting this likely hurts conversion rates. The main customization challenge is skinning the checkout to match his website's look and feel.
key_points: eSellerate's product listing process is smooth and can be completed in under 30 minutes
Skinning the checkout to match your website's branding is the main customization challenge
The multi-step checkout flow (minimum 5 pages) is a serious conversion rate concern
money_quotes: The actual mechanics of getting the product up and running are smooth as silk, and I estimate that I have played around with them for about 4-5 hours and could get a product listed in under 30 minutes if I really wanted to, start to finish.
My one complain about eSellerate — the number of pages you have to click through just screams "Abandon this software purchase!".
The lowest I've been able to force it to is about 5 steps. Yeah, egads.
posts.key_insight: Even when a payment platform's setup is easy, a lengthy checkout flow with too many steps actively drives customers away from completing purchases.

posts.summary: McKenzie receives his first feature request for Bingo Card Creator from a customer who needs custom header text on bingo cards. The feature doesn't exist yet and the customer needs it by tomorrow, making it impossible to deliver in time. Rather than leaving her without a solution, he researches a competitor's product, installs their trial to verify it works, and refers her to them. He reflects on the bittersweet nature of helping a potential customer while losing the sale.
key_points: Early-stage products inevitably face feature gaps that cost real sales
McKenzie prioritizes the customer's needs over his own revenue by referring her to a competitor
He validates the competitor's product himself before recommending it, showing thoroughness even in losing a sale
The incident reveals his product roadmap awareness — the feature was already planned but not yet built
money_quotes: I ended up pointing her to one of my competitor's products which appears to have the feature she needs (I even installed their trial to make sure, and it appears it will work for her).
This makes me happy-sad: happy I was able to give her at least an option, very sad it wasn't me.
posts.key_insight: Helping a customer find the right solution — even from a competitor — is the instinct that builds long-term trust and reputation in a small software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his early discovery of long-tail search traffic for his Bingo Card Creator software business. After obsessing over his lack of ranking for competitive paid keywords, he notices that 20% of his traffic comes from obscure, highly specific organic search queries where his site is one of only a few relevant results in the world. He catalogs the specific long-tail queries from Google and MSN that are sending him free traffic, noting that many align perfectly with his product's niche features. The post captures the moment of realization that targeting the long tail of search can be more valuable than competing for expensive head terms.
key_points: Long-tail search queries with very low competition can drive significant traffic (20% of his total) for free, compared to expensive paid keywords
Creating targeted pages for specific search queries (like 'first grade sight words') is an effective SEO strategy for small businesses
MSN ranked his site much higher than Google for competitive terms, though MSN had far less traffic
Many organic search queries he received were ones he hadn't even thought to bid on, revealing untapped keyword opportunities
The long tail works because for highly specific queries, there may only be a handful of relevant pages in the entire world
money_quotes: They're all from the looooooooooooooooooooong tail of search requests, things for which there are literally perhaps 3 relevant pages in the entire world.
I love SEO — I made a page just to catch this query, and catch it did.
I'm #4 for a key search term after 2 weeks. Not bad. A pity MSN gets so little traffic.
I had not bid for this combination yet. FIXED.
Thats what I pay good money for ($12.50 so far… come on, uISV gods, let me have a sale soon…)
posts.key_insight: For a small software business, the long tail of highly specific search queries can deliver substantial free traffic because there are so few competing pages for each obscure query.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his first sale of Bingo Card Creator, which came unexpectedly on a weekend just over two weeks after launch. The sale was at full price through his website, netting $23.93 after PayPal fees. He notes a Google tracking misconfiguration prevented him from identifying the conversion source. The profit from this single sale covered about 80% of his first month's advertising budget.
key_points: First sale arrived two weeks and one day after launch, on a weekend he nearly turned off ads for
Net profit of $23.93 after PayPal fees on a $24.95 sale, with zero hosting costs via free Payloadz tier
A tracking misconfiguration meant he lost attribution data on his first paying customer
The single sale nearly covered his entire monthly advertising spend, suggesting very low initial ad budget
money_quotes: That was quite unexpected (I had almost turned off my ads for the weekend on the assumption they would not lead to money).
For those keeping track, thats two weeks and a day after launch.
At the moment (with a free Payloadz account until I get, hmm, 3 more sales), that means I clear 24.95 – 1.02 (paypal fees) = $23.93 profit.
Thats about 80% of my advertising budget for my first month, which is now half completed.
posts.key_insight: Even a single sale at a modest price point can nearly recoup early advertising costs when you keep your overhead minimal, validating the microISV model of starting extremely lean.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a transparent accounting of his first software business's early finances. With a single sale of $24.95 and expenses totaling $66.02, he's deeply in the red. He evaluates each marketing channel — abandoning eBay auctions despite views, questioning Yahoo search ads, and noting Google drives half his trial downloads. The post captures the unglamorous reality of bootstrapping a micro-ISV with roughly 50 hours invested.
key_points: First sale netted $23.93 after PayPal fees against $66.02 in expenses, showing the harsh early economics of a solo software business
eBay proved ineffective despite generating views — people weren't willing to pay his price, leading him to cut losses
Google was the standout marketing channel, driving roughly 50% of trial downloads at $30/month, while Yahoo underperformed
About 50 hours of total work invested, putting the effective hourly rate deep into negative territory
money_quotes: People on eBay are just not willing to pay any price I'm willing to charge (I get 20+ views per auction but no sales).
I just can't stand them next to Google and their performance compared with Google sucks.
$30 — my expected first month expense of working with Google, which drives probably 50% of my trial downloads.
posts.key_insight: The earliest days of a bootstrapped software business are defined by methodical experimentation with marketing channels and honest accounting of losses, not overnight success.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a weekend spent on minor but important fixes to his Bingo Card Creator product and website. He's expanding the Wizard functionality to support math classes beyond reading.
