He discovered his website links weren't underlined, making them invisible as clickable elements, and plans to fix this after seeing clickstream data showing users repeatedly clicking tabs trying to navigate.
key_points: Inconsistency in product UI naming (Dolch Word Lists vs Dolch Sight Words) needed cleanup, though website inconsistency is deliberately kept for SEO synonym capture
Website links lacked underlines, making them unrecognizable as links — a basic usability failure he missed for weeks
Clickstream data revealed users double-clicking tabs, suggesting navigation confusion rather than user error
Previously doubled downloads in a day just by adding buttons, reinforcing that visual affordances matter enormously
money_quotes: While inconsistency is fine for the website (got to catch search engine synonyms) I wanted the UI to look a wee bit more professional.
Here's the other thing I just figured out: my links do not look like links, because they are not underlined.
I can't believe that I've missed that for the entire time I've been working on my website.
Many many people trying to get to the free trial, for example, click the Free Trial tab and then click it again.
I doubled my downloads in a day by putting up those buttons — making the links scream "I'm a link!" can't possibly hurt.
posts.key_insight: Basic visual affordances like underlined links and clear buttons have an outsized impact on conversion — small usability fixes that seem trivial can dramatically change user behavior.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide to running profitable Google AdWords campaigns for small software businesses. He emphasizes that analytics and conversion tracking are prerequisites, not optional extras. The core argument is that conversions and cost per acquisition are the only metrics that matter—everything else, including click-through rate, primarily benefits Google rather than the advertiser. He walks through campaign structure, ad copy testing, landing page optimization, and budget management, drawing from his direct experience selling Bingo Card Creator to teachers.
key_points: Conversion rate and cost per acquisition are the only two numbers that matter; CTR primarily makes Google money, not the advertiser
The Google Content Network (AdSense sites) should be disabled because clicks from Made-for-AdSense pages almost never convert
Segmenting campaigns into thematically coherent ad groups with matching landing pages dramatically improves conversion rates
Google's automatic budgeting will bid up your CPC to spend your full budget, which can push you past profitability—manual control is better
Ad copy should filter out unqualified buyers rather than maximize clicks, since you only want people likely to convert clicking your ads
money_quotes: There are only two numbers you care about: CR and CPA. Everything else is noise.
If it costs you $30 per trial download and you sell a $24.95 product, congratulations, you should a) get serious about optimizing your ads or b) cancel AdWords today.
You've got five seconds to overwhelm someone's inborn defenses against spending time/money on your product. Make the use of them.
CTR is money for Google, not for you. Ideally, you'd want ad text that turned off 100% of people who would not convert while still capturing 100% of people who would.
You only want qualified buyers to click your ads.
God darn it Jim I'm an engineer, not a marketer.
It doesn't matter if you're in 1st position, 2nd position, 3rd position, or 17th position if you're still maxing out your budget every day.
posts.key_insight: Successful AdWords campaigns optimize for conversions and cost per acquisition rather than click-through rate, because high CTR enriches Google while only conversions make the advertiser money.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his Bingo Card Creator software was cracked by a Chinese warez group, who even translated some of his marketing materials into simplified Chinese. While he acknowledges this led to his highest traffic day ever, the traffic is worthless since it comes from piracy seekers. He considers retaliatory redirects but lacks the technical means on his Windows hosting. The post captures the early frustrations of a solo software developer dealing with software piracy for the first time.
key_points: His software was cracked and redistributed by a Chinese warez group shortly after launch
The piracy ironically generated his highest traffic day, though the traffic had no commercial value
He lacked the server configuration knowledge on Windows hosting to implement countermeasures
He treated the situation with humor rather than panic, suggesting it wouldn't meaningfully impact his business
money_quotes: My software got cracked yesterday by a Chinese warez group. They even went to the trouble of translating some of my marketing material into simplified Chinese — thanks guys, I think…
Its really annoying to have my highest traffic day ever as a result of a steady stream of traffic from their website.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software piracy is more of an annoyance than an existential threat for a micro-ISV, and vanity metrics like traffic spikes are meaningless when driven by the wrong audience.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports halving his cost per trial download from $1 to $0.50 in one week through iterative optimization of his advertising and landing pages. He attributes the improvement to dropping non-performing ads, optimizing for conversion rate instead of click-through rate, and tuning landing pages. He shares a specific finding that underlining hyperlinks increased trial downloads by 10%, on top of a previous 100% increase from adding a download button. He projects further cost reduction to $0.30 per trial the following week.
key_points: Halved cost per trial download in one week through systematic optimization
Optimizing for conversion rate (CR) rather than click-through rate (CTR) is a key lever for reducing acquisition costs
Small UI changes like underlining hyperlinks yielded a 10% improvement in trial downloads
Making the trial download a prominent button previously doubled conversions, showing that reducing friction compounds
money_quotes: Last week I was paying roughly $1 per trial download. Now I'm paying $.50. I hope by next week to be paying about $.30.
The secrets, such that they are, are mostly in the post right below: dropping non-performing ads, optimizing for CR instead of CTR, and tuning my landing pages.
I found another no-brainer: underlining hyperlinks, which is probably partially responsible for the a 10% increase in people successfully finding the trial.
This is on top of the 100% increase I got from making that trial a button, but apparently some folks actually read the text I spent so much time writing…
posts.key_insight: Systematic iteration on small, measurable changes to ads, landing pages, and UI elements can rapidly compound to dramatically reduce customer acquisition costs.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a frustrating CSS rendering bug in Internet Explorer caused by using a standards-compliant DOCTYPE declaration. Despite following web standards best practices, IE ignored his CSS margin:auto centering. The fix requires changing the DOCTYPE to XHTML, which in turn demands updating all self-closing tags to pass validation. It's a brief vent about the cascading annoyances of cross-browser compatibility in the mid-2000s web.
key_points: Using a standards-compliant HTML 4.01 Transitional DOCTYPE caused IE to ignore CSS margin:auto for centering
Fixing the issue requires switching to an XHTML DOCTYPE, which creates additional markup changes
Being a 'good web citizen' with standards compliance paradoxically broke the page in the dominant browser
money_quotes: It seems that when I was a good web citizen and made all my pages nice and standards compliant via liberal use of DOCTYPE I apparently instructed IE to please ignore my CSS settings (come again?!)
posts.key_insight: In the mid-2000s web, following standards compliance could paradoxically break your site in Internet Explorer, forcing developers into cascading workarounds.

posts.summary: McKenzie notices his Bingo Card Creator website is broken in Internet Explorer, with the layout appearing left-aligned instead of centered. The post is a brief, frustrated plea for help debugging a cross-browser CSS issue. He notes Firefox renders correctly while IE does not, despite the layout working fine previously. He asks readers using IE to check the site and report back.
key_points: Cross-browser compatibility between IE and Firefox was a major pain point for early web developers
McKenzie is learning web development on the fly while building his first software product
He leverages his blog audience as informal QA testers for his product
money_quotes: I am really starting to hate web development.
I just noticed that my page looks totally borked in IE — instead of being nice and centered its clumped at the left hand portion of the screen.
Firefox is, as usual, not effected.
posts.key_insight: Building a real product forces you to confront the unglamorous realities of cross-browser web development, even when your core product has nothing to do with web technology.

posts.summary: Google's AdWords Quality Score update has eliminated many of McKenzie's competitors by raising their minimum keyword bids dramatically. Sites engaging in ad arbitrage, scraper sites, and affiliate marketers were hit hardest, while his legitimate bingo card business benefited. His click-through rates jumped to 25% due to improved ad positioning, forcing him to actually lower his bids to stay within his $1/day budget. The net effect is lower cost per acquisition for his software.
key_points: Google's Quality Score update punished low-quality advertisers (scrapers, MFA sites, ad arbitrage) by raising their minimum bids from $0.15 to $5
Legitimate small businesses benefit when platforms crack down on spam and arbitrage competitors
Higher ad position isn't always desirable — a 25% CTR can blow through a tiny daily budget too fast
Strategic bid reduction can maintain budget discipline while benefiting from reduced competition
money_quotes: scrapper sites, MFA, and affiliate marketers on the other hand I have rather little sympathy for
As of today, these competitors no longer exist.
Putting two and two together, I'm assuming this is because they're now seeing $5 minimum bids for their keywords, where they had previously been paying $.15.
25% CTRs, somebody save me!
I've reduced by bids in an effort to get back down to my previous, thank-you-sir-I-still-deplete-my-budget-daily ad position
posts.key_insight: Platform quality enforcement that punishes low-value advertisers creates a windfall for legitimate small businesses competing in the same keyword space.

posts.summary: McKenzie lists five free software programs he pledges to eventually donate $20 each to, after one of them saved him from IE-related HTML layout problems. The post is an early, lighthearted reflection on the value of free tools to a bootstrapping solo developer. He describes fixing cross-browser compatibility issues using an HTML cleaner and credits the broader ecosystem of free tools that made his project possible. The tone is self-deprecating and humorous, characteristic of his early blogging voice before he became known for business strategy writing.
key_points: Free and open-source tools were essential infrastructure for early solo software businesses
The 'I'll donate later when I have money' pledge reflects the classic bootstrapper's cash-constrained reality
Cross-browser IE compatibility was a significant time sink for web developers in 2006
McKenzie valued tools that saved him time over tools with the most features, preferring simplicity over power (Paint.net over GIMP, NVU over alternatives)
money_quotes: I hereby pledge $20 to each of them 'in the indefinite future, when I have the money to spend', because I've gotten that much use and more out of them and I am feeling in a profoundly appreciative mood to people who save me time at the moment.
Photoshop, on a budget of 'free', for people who have less artistic talent than an avante garde artiste who outsources his creative efforts to housepets.
As to why I don't use GIMP: I just don't bloody understand HOW to start using that application.
Saved me from having to write gawk scripts to do it for me, which is probably worth four hours of my time.
It doesn't have everything I need, but it has 99% of it, and it beats the heck out of notepad.
posts.key_insight: Even small free tools that save a solo developer a few hours of work deliver outsized value relative to their cost, though the economics of voluntarily paying for free software remain aspirational for cash-strapped bootstrappers.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a practical AdWords optimization technique: using Google's Position Preferences feature to deliberately target lower ad positions instead of always competing for the top spot. By excluding top positions, advertisers can dramatically reduce their cost-per-click while stretching limited daily budgets to get more total clicks. He demonstrates with his own data that dropping from position 1.2 to position 2.8 cut his CPC by roughly 70% and reduced his cost per trial download from $0.50 to $0.30.
key_points: Google's default bidding strategy optimizes for the highest possible ad position, which burns through limited budgets quickly
Using Position Preferences to exclude top ad slots forces Google to bid lower, resulting in significantly cheaper clicks
Lower position doesn't necessarily mean worse results — his cost per trial download improved from $0.50 to $0.30 despite the lower ranking
For budget-constrained advertisers, total click volume matters more than winning the top position
money_quotes: If you're like me, you can't afford at the moment to actually absorb a click from everyone who wants to click on your AdWords ad.
Go to your favorite ad group, check all the keywords, and click Edit Keyword Settings. Now turn Position Preferences on.
I've been doing this for about 5 days now and I rather like it. Lots of things are constantly changing in my AdWords account, but I've cut my CPC for my top keywords literally by 70%.
I was averaging position 1.2… and running out of money within 3 hours of my ads turning on. Not good!
I'm paying nine cents each, for about a 2.8 average position. I'm still getting my budget's worth of clicks every day, except there's a lot more of them.
My cost per trial download has declined from $.50 to $.30.
posts.key_insight: Budget-constrained advertisers get better results by deliberately targeting lower ad positions, trading top ranking for dramatically more clicks per dollar spent.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch payment processing from Payloadz to e-junkie for his software business. The three driving factors are automatic post-purchase redirection for analytics and onboarding, simpler flat-rate pricing versus Payloadz's tiered model, and full control over customer-facing communications. He notes that neither processor's branding conveys the professional image he wants. He also calculates that building his own solution isn't worth the time given e-junkie's $5/month cost.
key_points: Post-purchase redirect capability is critical for tracking conversions and improving customer onboarding
Flat-rate pricing ($5/month) is preferable to percentage-based tiers that penalize growth
Controlling the entire customer experience—emails, branding, tone—matters for perceived professionalism
Build-vs-buy decisions should factor in the real cost of developer time against ongoing fees
money_quotes: I don't know where my monthly sales are going to stabilize and don't want them stabilizing at a point where I routinely pay 12-15% of sales to somebody doing, well, essentially not a heck of a lot for me.
Neither Payloadz nor e-junkie screams 'responsible businessman of the sort I am glad to pay to get educational materials from', which is sort of what I'm going for.
I figure that will take me 2 hours, and $5 a month is not really worth 2 hours of my time…
Its absolutely crucial to me that I be able to redirect customers back to my site after the transaction is completed.
e-junkie let me write my own email template, soup to nuts.
posts.key_insight: When choosing payment infrastructure, optimize for customer experience control and predictable pricing over raw features, because your processor's limitations become your brand's limitations.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his software business (Bingo Card Creator) has become cash-flow positive within its first month of real operation. After discounting one-time startup costs, July sales have covered all monthly expenses including a failed eBay selling experiment. He analyzes his conversion rates, noting roughly 5% when excluding non-target traffic like pirates and blog readers. He outlines plans to shift advertising budget from Overture to Google and scale up spending, confident that four sales per month will cover increased costs.
key_points: The business reached profitability within the first month, covering all recurring costs from sales revenue
Conversion rate analysis requires filtering out non-representative traffic like pirates and blog readers to get a meaningful 5% figure
Strategic ad spend reallocation from Overture to Google, tripling the budget to $90/month, reflects early data-driven optimization
The entire monthly cost structure is under $110, demonstrating the extremely low overhead of a one-person software business
money_quotes: Discounting the one-time startup costs (dang international fax that cost $17 or so) I'm currently profitable.

If you exclude all downloads from people referred by my weblog (JoSers who are not exactly in the market for elementary reading software) and by the pirates, my conversion rate is, and this is a back of the envelope calculation, about 5%.
My plan is, within the next week, to cancel my account with Overture and triple my budget on Google to $90 per month.
That would put my marginal costs at about $95 a month ($110 if sales exceed $100, and I rather expect that they will), which is slightly less than what I get from four sales.

I think thats easily achievable, considering that four sales was my initial goal for August and I'm already halfway there with a third of the advertising in a month where my target market is very not-motivated to buy.
posts.key_insight: A one-person software business can reach profitability remarkably fast when overhead is kept minimal and conversion metrics are analyzed honestly by filtering out non-representative traffic.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that his Google AdWords optimization has been so effective at minimizing cost-per-click that his daily budget can now cover all available clicks, leaving money unspent. He shares detailed metrics for two ad groups promoting Bingo Card Creator, with strong click-through and conversion rates. He shifts strategy from minimizing CPC to maximizing total conversions, and considers expanding to international English-speaking markets.
key_points: Over-optimizing for low CPC can leave budget unspent when you've exhausted available impressions at that price point
His Dolch word list ad group generates 3.5x more impressions but converts at a lower rate than the direct bingo card group
Both ad groups maintain cost-per-acquisition under $0.52, showing strong unit economics for a micro-ISV
The natural next step after mastering CPC is shifting focus to maximizing total conversion volume
money_quotes: It turns out that I was so successful at minimizing my CPC I am now able to pay for all the clicks my ads would generate in a day, with money left over.
Time to see if I can't increase the CTR a bit without increasing my cost per conversion that much…
That reminds me, I should start advertising in Australia/Great Britain soon.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing for the lowest possible cost-per-click eventually hits a ceiling where the real constraint becomes total conversion volume, requiring a strategic shift in ad spending approach.

posts.summary: McKenzie reconsiders his negative opinion of Yahoo Search Marketing (YSM/Overture) after seeing strong performance metrics. Despite the platform's terrible interface, 3-day ad approval delays, and broken analytics integration, his Yahoo ads achieved 29 cents CPA with 40% conversion rate — significantly outperforming his Google AdWords at 50 cents CPA and 20% conversion. He decides to continue spending on both platforms, allocating $30 to Yahoo and $90 to Google for the next month. The post also touches on the accounting complexity of when to recognize ad expenses.
key_points: Yahoo Search Marketing delivered dramatically better results than Google AdWords: 29 cents CPA vs 50 cents, and 40% conversion vs 20%
Platform usability and metrics performance can be completely uncorrelated — the worse platform produced better results
His target audience (teachers) appeared to be disproportionately concentrated on Yahoo rather than Google
Ad expense accounting is genuinely ambiguous: timing of click, mental commitment, or credit card charge each give different budget pictures
money_quotes: Yes, their interface is terrible. Yes, their three day delay between me putting in a new ad and it showing up makes constant refinement impossible. Yes, their integration into Analytics sucks.
But, well, numbers do not lie.
So, comparing by comparison, a week's worth of my barely-optimized Yahoo ads: 29 cents. 40% conversion. Good God.
Apparently the teachers are all over Yahoo.
Google is more fun than some games I've paid money for… cheaper, too, come to think of it.
Do you expense the ad when the click comes in? Or when you mentally commit yourself to spending X over a certain period? Or when your credit card is actually charged?
posts.key_insight: Let performance data override your qualitative judgments about platforms — a terrible user experience for the advertiser can still deliver superior results if that's where your customers are.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his delight at discovering that a fellow Joel on Software forum regular makes niche software for chimney sweeps. He finds the ultra-specific niche charming and references the Mary Poppins chimney sweep song to underscore the whimsy of the product.
key_points: Celebrates the existence of extremely niche micro-ISV software targeting chimney sweeps
Highlights the charm and viability of serving overlooked, tiny markets with specialized software
References the Joel on Software community as a hub for independent software developers
money_quotes: If the prospect of this doesn't make you smile, you have neither soul nor taste in Disney movies.
posts.key_insight: There is real business opportunity and delight in building software for the most unexpected, hyper-specific niches.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a simple technique for implementing update checking in desktop software using static HTML files on a website. Each version number gets its own HTML page, with only the latest version's page showing an 'up to date' message while all others prompt users to update. The approach requires no server-side programming, takes seconds per release to maintain, and doubles as analytics by letting you track successful installations via web logs. It's a pragmatic hack that trades sophistication for speed of implementation.
key_points: Update checking can be implemented with zero server-side code by using static HTML files named after version numbers
Web server logs from update checks double as installation analytics, letting you confirm installs succeeded
Every customer touchpoint is a selling opportunity — even the 'you're up to date' confirmation page
The technique requires only renaming files and swapping content when releasing new versions, taking about 10 seconds per update
Pragmatic, low-tech solutions that ship today beat sophisticated ones that never get built
money_quotes: Checking for updates is a wonderful thing to enable, both because it makes sure your customers have the best-and-brightest version of your product available, and because it lets you see on your web logs 'Ahah, that install actually succeeded'.
The latest one says 'Yep, your software is up to date', has the tracking code, and maybe gives a bit of advice or something (hey, why miss an opportunity to sell to people).
BAM. This takes only 10 seconds per update, requires no additional programming, and can be done in 100% static HTML.
Its not quite as seemless to the user as, say, Firefox's automatic updating, but it's a heck of a lot simpler for you.
I wanted to have it ready for my first release but couldn't figure out a way to do it simply.
posts.key_insight: The simplest possible implementation that ships today — even static HTML files on a web server — beats a sophisticated solution that never gets built.

posts.summary: McKenzie analyzes a legal filing where Google's lawyers inadvertently revealed that Google checked Gmail users' inboxes to determine whether settlement notification emails had been caught by spam filters. In a class-action click fraud case, Google hired a firm to notify affected advertisers about a settlement, and when emails went to spam folders, Google accessed 75,000+ Gmail accounts to identify and re-send the notices. McKenzie argues this constitutes a privacy violation under Google's own privacy policy, which requires opt-in consent for sharing sensitive personal information. He dissects the privacy policy's exemptions and finds none that legitimately cover this action, particularly since Google's own lawyers admitted the inbox-checking exceeded what the court required.
key_points: Google's legal team publicly admitted in court filings that they checked Gmail accounts to see if settlement notification emails were caught by spam filters, affecting at least 75,000 accounts
The settlement notifications were arguably spam themselves — class members never opted in, and the emails served a commercial purpose of dividing settlement funds
Google's own privacy policy requires opt-in consent for sharing sensitive personal information, and none of the stated exemptions cleanly apply to this disclosure
Google's lawyers undermined their own privacy defense by bragging they did 'more than was required' — eliminating the legal necessity exemption
The precedent is alarming: if Google can peek at inboxes whenever its lawyers decide it serves Google's legal interests, the privacy policy is effectively meaningless
money_quotes: Google algorithmically peaked at all the accounts on the list their agent had developed which they had access to, to see if the mail was marked spam or not.
Unhinged rantings of a conspiracy nut? Well, no. Google's lawyers bragged about this in a recent document they filed to the court regarding the settlement.
I'm sorry Google, I just don't remember telling you you could go peeking at the mail, even to 'provide the best notice practicable'.
You've already explained in your own words that the peeping was more than the court required, so excuse #3 is out.
If you were, then this exemption swallows the entirety of the policy!

I'm less than happy, and now seriously wondering if all those business documents I've got floating around my Gmail inbox are going to end up in the hands of your lawyers without so much as a by-your-leave.
Do no evil, indeed.
posts.key_insight: Google's own court filings reveal they accessed Gmail users' inboxes without consent to check spam filter status, and their privacy policy's exemptions don't cover voluntary actions their lawyers admitted exceeded legal requirements.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers he failed to upload the correct installer for Bingo Card Creator after making major changes. The deployment process was manual, and he missed the step of copying the new build to the web directory before syncing to the FTP server. As a result, all users had been downloading the old version for days. He takes the mistake in stride rather than dwelling on it.
key_points: Manual deployment processes are error-prone and can lead to shipping the wrong build
A missed step in a multi-step manual workflow went unnoticed for days
Early-stage solo software businesses often lack deployment automation
money_quotes: Unfortunately, this is largely a manual process and I missed the critical "copy the new installer from the build directory to the web directory before sync'ing the local copy of my website and the FTP server".
posts.key_insight: Manual deployment processes without checklists or automation will inevitably lead to shipping the wrong build.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts an unexpected brush with internet fame after commenting on Matt Cutts' blog during a lunch break, only to find himself on the front page of WordPress by evening. He quickly pivots back to his regular business concerns, noting issues with his AdWords conversion tracking showing 0% and Google Analytics reporting zero traffic for three days, which he attributes to system glitches rather than actual problems.
key_points: A casual blog comment about Google legal papers unexpectedly went viral, landing him on the WordPress front page
He deliberately returns focus to his core business of selling software to teachers rather than chasing internet attention
His AdWords and Analytics data appear corrupted, showing impossible 0% conversion rates and zero traffic
He applies statistical reasoning to recognize the data must be a system error rather than real performance decline
money_quotes: I finished my workday, went out to bid fairwell to a friend leaving the country, and when I get back to my computer I'm on the front page of WordPress.
Google certainly inspires some passion in people, what can I say.
Anyhow, back to comfortable anonymity and the pressing business of saving teachers time and money.
I'm showing a conversion rate of 0%, which is highly statistically improbable.
Ahh well, kinks in the system, I'm reasonably sure they'll get cleared up soon.
posts.key_insight: Unexpected internet attention is a distraction; the real work is getting back to building your business and monitoring your metrics.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares five quick website tweaks that measurably improved his conversion rate for Bingo Card Creator, his software product for teachers. He advocates for empirically-driven iterative improvements over gut feelings, testing changes over multi-day periods. The tweaks range from making download links into clickable button images (100% boost) to ensuring screenshots appear above the fold (25% impact). He emphasizes that every page should have a conversion path, since a significant minority of visitors explore extensively before converting. The post closes with a pitch for analytics as the prerequisite for any data-driven optimization.
key_points: Replacing a text download link with a button image doubled his conversion rate, demonstrating how small visual changes have outsized effects
29% of visitors who eventually download explore multiple pages first, including unlikely pages like the privacy policy, so every page needs a conversion path
Screenshot placement above the fold matters enormously — moving it below the fold caused a 25% drop in conversions
A significant number of non-technical users in 2006 didn't know how to use the back button, making thumbnail UX a conversion killer
None of these optimizations are possible without analytics — measurement is the prerequisite for improvement
money_quotes: Its not A/B testing but its, shall we say, empirically driven iterative improvements.
Make your "download now" link an image. This delivered roughly a 100% boost in my conversion rate. Yeah, you read that right.
Some people will read your entire page, but a lot are just sort of browsing, and they'll randomly click on whatever looks interesting — and you might as well have that be your goal.
I have a non-trivial number of users who hit every accessible page in the site before downloading, including a rather suprising number who go through every link in the nav bar in order.
This has happened on every page I have written, from my free teacher resources to my about me blurb to… deciding to download after reading my privacy policy.
You would be absolutely shocked at the percentage of users who in 2006 have not heard of the "back" button.
You'll notice that this data-driven improvement can't happen without, well, data. Do you have analytics software installed yet?
posts.key_insight: Small, testable changes to download button design, link placement, and screenshot positioning can yield dramatic conversion improvements, but only if you have analytics in place to measure the results.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his organic search traffic has surpassed his paid search (PPC) traffic for the first time. He discovered that his blog ranked higher than his main site for key search terms, so he added internal links from blog posts to product pages. Within 48 hours of being re-crawled, his site jumped from page 3 to page 1 of search results. He sees significant upside in capturing even a fraction of the organic search volume.
key_points: Organic search traffic exceeded PPC traffic for the first time, marking a milestone in the site's growth
Blog content inadvertently outranked the main product site, revealing the SEO power of regularly updated content
Strategic internal linking from blog to product pages produced rapid ranking improvements within 48 hours
He deliberately obfuscates his target keywords to avoid attracting competition to his niche
money_quotes: Today was the first day I got more organic hits than this, and they appear to be legitimate (i.e. not from pirates for a change).
I discovered that it ranks higher than my site for key search terms (as a result of a post similar to this one, ironically)
I made about 10 links for those terms from the blog to the appropriate content pages.
And, bam, 48 hours later I got crawled and my site went from the 3rd page of search results to the 1st page.
I know from my PPC search impressions that some of these words get traffic in the hundreds per day, which if I could capture 10% of would be excellent.
posts.key_insight: A blog's higher domain authority can be leveraged through strategic internal linking to rapidly boost a product site's organic search rankings, reducing dependence on paid traffic.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide to on-page SEO tactics for small businesses, drawn from his direct experience ranking Bingo Card Creator on Google, Yahoo, and MSN. He covers six core techniques: unique keyword-rich page titles, keyword-optimized URLs, attention to pluralization and search term variations, consistent site navigation templates, and avoiding duplicate content penalties. The post demonstrates each tactic with specific ranking results from his own site, showing how small changes like word order in titles or dashes versus underscores in URLs produce measurable ranking improvements. He explains Google's 'supplemental index' penalty for duplicate content and offers a clever workaround: rewriting pages to be semantically identical but lexically different, exploiting the gap between human comprehension and algorithmic text matching.
key_points: Every page should have a unique, keyword-rich title with the most important keywords placed first, since search engines weight words at the beginning of titles more heavily
URL structure matters significantly for rankings — use dashes (not underscores) to separate words, and embed target keywords directly in the URL path
Search engines do not reliably perform stemming, so the difference between singular and plural forms of keywords can shift rankings dramatically — pick the variant with more search volume
Duplicate content triggers Google's supplemental index penalty, but pages can be rewritten to convey identical meaning with entirely different vocabulary to avoid detection
Consistent site-wide navigation templates help search engines strip boilerplate and focus on actual page content for ranking purposes
money_quotes: If your users and your desire to SEO every come into conflict, you have done something terribly wrong.
Going supplemental means your page gets quarantined out of the main search index for Google, and is put in basically a low-quality jail.
There is really no ghost in the machine, and some tasks require actual understanding rather than even the coolest applications of statistics.
The difference between SERP for 'cheap red widget' and 'cheap red widgets' is night-and-day.
How many interior pages do you have on your site which get read once in a blue moon? A simple change of their title to include some keywords and, bam, free traffic.
MSN is the world's cheapest date, who will practically faint if you show any interest in her at all.
posts.key_insight: Small, systematic on-page optimizations — unique titles, keyword-rich URLs, precise pluralization choices, and non-duplicate content — compound into significant organic search traffic for small businesses without requiring any ethically questionable tactics.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his first month of business statistics for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $74.85 in gross sales against $68.02 in expenses for a net profit of $3.77. He breaks down his costs across signup fees, website hosting, and advertising, while detailing impressive conversion rates from both paid and organic search. The post reveals the raw economics of a bootstrapped micro-ISV in its earliest days, with surprisingly strong marketing metrics despite minimal revenue. He sets modest goals of 10-20 sales per month for August while planning to expand the product's educational scope.
key_points: First month revenue was $74.85 gross with only $3.77 profit after expenses, showing the razor-thin margins of a brand-new micro-ISV
Organic search already converted at 17.5% on Google and 13% on MSN, demonstrating strong product-market fit despite minimal traffic
Yahoo Search Marketing outperformed Google AdWords on conversion rate (24.8% vs 17.61%) at a lower cost per acquisition
Post-launch customer support took only 15 minutes total, validating his investment in building an easy-to-use product
His goal of 20 sales per month representing a 'meaningful change in standard of living' reveals the modest ambitions that would eventually scale into a significant business
money_quotes: Profit: $3.77.  Take that, Bill Gates!
Post-launch support: 15 minutes (easy-to-use, mostly bug-free program = I write "Thank you for saying thank you" a lot)
10 sales a month makes this an amusing hobby, 20 sales a month is a meaningful change in my standard of living
I got a large spike of hits from pirates when my software was cracked.  They accounted for roughly 120 downloads and a couple of hundred visits.
if folks wondering if they can make the leap or not want to see some actual numbers, here they are
posts.key_insight: Radical transparency about the unglamorous early economics of a bootstrapped software business — $3.77 in first-month profit — combined with strong conversion metrics that hint at the scalable foundation underneath.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces that Bingo Card Creator has become profitable, exactly one month after launching the business. He had been worried after increasing his advertising budget but received enough orders to cross into profitability. He thanks the Joel on Software community for advice and credits teachers as his core customers, closing with a plug for his icebreaker bingo product.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator reached profitability one month after launch, despite concerns about increased ad spending
The advertising budget increase was modest — about 5 sales worth per month — showing how small the margins were at this stage
Community support from Joel on Software forums played a meaningful role in the business's early success
McKenzie naturally weaves product promotion into genuine gratitude toward his teacher customer base
money_quotes: I got some orders today and I'm now profitable and will continue to be for the forseeable future.
I also like that its one month to the day after opening my business.
posts.key_insight: A tiny software business can reach profitability in just one month with modest advertising spend and a clearly defined niche customer base.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a day of programming work on his Bingo Card Creator application, tackling minor but customer-impacting issues. He focuses on fixing a print-related UI flicker caused by JFrame focus stealing, optimizing memory usage during batch printing for customers with low-end hardware, and adding a custom application icon. These are small polish items that improve the overall user experience.
key_points: UI polish matters: a JFrame grabbing focus during printing causes visible flickering that annoys customers
Resource optimization must account for customers' actual hardware, not just the developer's machine
Small improvements like custom icons contribute to a more professional product feel
He explicitly notes the value of stepping away from marketing to do hands-on development work
money_quotes: Nice to get away from marketing for a bit.
I have a bunch of stuff which is neither sexy nor fun but will improve the customer experience.
While this isn't a problem on my souped-up development machine at least some of my customers are on P3s with 128 MB of ram, so I'll optimize that.
posts.key_insight: The unglamorous work of fixing UI quirks and optimizing for low-end hardware is what separates a polished product from a developer's side project.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers a typo in his Google Analytics conversion tracking URL that caused lost data, and separately notes that submitting a PAD file to the Association of Shareware Professionals led to a customer acquisition via a high-PageRank shareware directory. The post captures two small but practical lessons from running a micro-ISV: attention to detail in analytics configuration matters, and low-effort distribution channels like PAD files can punch above their weight for discoverability.
key_points: A typo in his Analytics conversion URL (http::/foo instead of http://foo) caused tracking data loss that Google won't retroactively fix
PAD file submission to the ASP directory resulted in an actual customer acquisition with minimal effort
High-PageRank shareware directories can outrank your own site for relevant keywords, making them valuable free distribution channels
For micro-ISVs with tiny conversion volumes, manual tracking can compensate for analytics failures
money_quotes: Turns out I had marked my conversion page as http::/foo instead of http://foo. Haha, regular expression FAILURE.
Google won't go back and rerun data after you fix filters like that, so the information is lost forever.

Considering that file took all of two minutes to generate using their free tool and that PR5 site ranks higher than me for some keywords thanks to the advertising copy I included in the PAD file, thats not a bad idea for people bootstrapping up a mISV.
posts.key_insight: Low-effort distribution tactics like PAD file submissions can deliver real customers for bootstrapped software businesses, making them worth the trivial time investment.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his first experience processing a refund and turns it into a masterclass on customer service for micro-ISVs. Drawing on his formative experience at Quill Corporation, he argues that refunds should be processed immediately and graciously, never contested. He explains the practical reasons (avoiding chargebacks and fees) and the strategic ones (word-of-mouth reputation in niche markets). He illustrates with a Quill anecdote where a small accommodation on a toner order led to a six-figure annual account. The post closes with practical advice on keeping sufficient funds available for refunds and a brief case for why money-back guarantees are worth offering.
key_points: Process refunds immediately and without argument — the alternative is a chargeback that costs you far more in fees and reputation
Word-of-mouth from even an ex-customer is the most influential advertising a small business will ever receive
Small acts of generosity in customer service can yield outsized returns, as demonstrated by Quill's toner-order-to-six-figure-account story
Always keep enough cash in your account to cover your most damaging possible refund without delays
Money-back guarantees generate far more marginal customers than they cost in refund payouts
money_quotes: Any time your customer asks to invoke the guarantee, process the refund immediately, then write back to your customer.
There is no question that your customer can get money from you: the question is whether they go through you or they chargeback.
I can scarcely find words to describe how much better for you it is that they go through you.
You're not Quill with 6 figures worth of customers, but you are in a niche market, and your niche talks to each other.
This is the most influential form of advertising you will ever get in your life.
Do you know how much profit a $X00,000 account makes in a year? It pays for a whole lot of cookies.
The marginal number of customers the refund offer will get you outweighs by many orders of magnitude the amount of charges you will eat processing refunds.
posts.key_insight: Generous, no-questions-asked refund policies are not a cost center but a powerful customer acquisition strategy, because the word-of-mouth from graceful exits far outweighs the price of processing returns.

posts.summary: McKenzie reminisces about working at Quill Corporation, an office supply company fanatically devoted to customer service. He describes their metrics wall, their policy of crediting individual employees mentioned in customer letters, and his favorite artifact: a prayer card from a convent of nuns who were so impressed by Quill's service they dedicated prayers to the staff. The nuns became so beloved that employees fought over who got to handle their account, and workers would bend rules to ensure the Sisters always received free cookies with their orders. The post illustrates how genuine customer obsession creates loyalty that transcends transactional relationships.
key_points: Quill tracked granular customer satisfaction metrics daily and publicly posted them alongside customer letters, creating accountability and pride
The company's culture valued customer satisfaction so highly that policies were treated as flexible guidelines when they conflicted with serving customers well
The nuns' prayer card became McKenzie's personal metric for customer excellence: 'Nuns Praying For You' (NPFY), a measure of service so good it inspires genuine devotion
Employees competed to handle the Sisters' account, requiring a specially-written CRM rule to prevent wiki-style revert wars over account ownership
Workers would creatively bend promotion rules to ensure loyal customers received every possible benefit, showing how culture trumps policy
money_quotes: Thats my personal metric for customer excellence: Nuns Praying For You (NPFY). Paypal, yeah, there's a NPFY-0 business if I ever saw one.
Keep in mind we were earning NPFY60+ for, well, prompt delivery of paper and pens.
When stacked against customer satisfaction policies were more like guidelines and guidelines were more like advice and advice was more like suggestions and suggestions were more like comments.

If they had asked Order Entry 'We'd like an 18-wheeler filled full of printer paper and the most we're willing to pay is, hmm, nothing. Oh, overnight delivery, too.' the only thing we would have said is 'But Sister, where will we put your cookies?'
The Sisters' account history look like a Wiki revert war ('They're MINE!' 'NO, they're MINE!')
Quill would FIND you: there was at least one letter addressed to 'that lovely Spanish woman with the Texan accent' and, by God, she got the credit for it.
posts.key_insight: Extraordinary customer service isn't about policies and metrics — it's about building a culture where employees genuinely care so much that customers respond with loyalty that borders on devotion.

posts.summary: McKenzie distinguishes between 'support' (a cost center to be minimized) and 'customer service' (a scalable investment in reputation). He argues that every customer interaction is an opportunity to demonstrate excellence, not a drain on resources. The post uses a concrete example where brushing off a low-profit customer gracefully led to hundreds of thousands of dollars in future business. He advocates for making products intuitive to reduce support volume, while treating every remaining interaction as a chance to build loyalty. The core thesis is that promising excellent customer service scales the same way guarantees do — most people won't need it, but everyone is reassured by its existence.
key_points: The average phone support call costs $12, often exceeding the profit on small orders — so the goal is to resolve interactions quickly while leaving customers delighted, not to avoid them
The best support investment is improving your product so customers don't need help in the first place — if you're copy-pasting the same answer repeatedly, fix the UX
Politeness and framing cost nothing but dramatically change customer perception — the same refund can feel like a rejection or like exceptional service depending on how it's communicated
Customer service promises scale like money-back guarantees: most people won't use them, but their existence reassures everyone and builds trust
Even at 1,000 orders/month with 5% needy customers averaging 4 inquiries each, that's only 7 emails a day — a trivial cost for massive reputational returns
money_quotes: Thats the difference between support and customer service. As a support incident, that call was a waste of time/money. As an opportunity for demonstrating you've got an unparalleled dedication to customer service, that call is as good an opportunity as every other customer contact you make.
Your first line of defense against 'wasting time' in support is producing a quality product.
If you consistently find yourself copy-pasting a canned response, you should probably go about making it more obvious.
I'd strongly suggest defaulting to yes and requiring good reasons to say no, rather than the other way around.
You can't afford for everyone to ask for their money back, but you know in advance that the overwhelming majority of people will not ask for their money back.

Your customers have been trained for years to distrust software, despite the fact that most customers will not have any problems: software is impersonal, software gets in the way of them doing what they need to do, software breaks, and when software breaks they spend 10 hours in tech-support heck talking to people who hate them and don't have any answers.
If you make excellence the routine in your customer service, people will know the difference.
posts.key_insight: Every customer interaction — even unprofitable ones — is an investment in reputation that compounds over time, making excellent customer service one of the most scalable assets a small software business can build.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents how the back-to-school season dramatically shifted search behavior for his educational software product, causing his ad conversion rates to plummet. He experiments with two creative approaches to Google AdWords copy: a minimalist white-space ad that triples his click-through rate, and a playful rhyming ad inspired by Japanese poetry. The white-space approach of giving searchers exactly what they want (free DSW lists) while upselling proves surprisingly effective. He observes that most AdWords advertisers write identical, uncreative copy and sees an opportunity in standing out.
key_points: Seasonal shifts (back-to-school) can dramatically change search volume and conversion behavior, requiring rapid ad optimization
Matching ad copy precisely to search intent and using unconventional formatting (white space) can triple CTR while also improving conversion rates
Most AdWords advertisers waste the creative opportunity by writing identical 'We sell X, come get it' copy
Giving away free content (DSW lists) as a loss leader to upsell the paid product can outperform direct selling approaches
money_quotes: Given that almost everybody is using the full character allotment in every line, the white space immediately catches the eye.
I noticed that every single ad on Google AdWords is the same: 'We sell X. Come get it!'. Boo for lack of creativity.
The Japanese have been writing sub-17 letter poems from what is effectively a 200 word vocabulary for 500 years and they haven't gotten bored of them yet.
I decided to test actually giving them the lists, since I have them on my website and the page they are on tries to 'upsell' people to the free demo about every other word.
The early results suggest that I am a better programmer than a poet.
posts.key_insight: Standing out in a sea of identical ads — through unconventional formatting, precise intent matching, and creative copy — can dramatically improve both click-through and conversion rates.

posts.summary: McKenzie draws a parallel between Toyota's Single Minute Exchange of Dies (SMED) manufacturing philosophy and micro-ISV software development. The core argument is that investing in systems — automated order processing, marketing infrastructure, and build pipelines — pays compounding dividends by eliminating repetitive manual work. He illustrates this with his own painful 50-click, 30-minute build process that causes him to batch changes and delay bug fixes. The post advocates for treating your development workflow like Toyota treats its assembly line: optimize the changeover process so you can deploy frequently and fix defects immediately rather than letting them accumulate.
key_points: Systems scale while manual effort doesn't — building a system once generates returns on every subsequent use without additional work
A painful deployment process (50 clicks, 30 minutes) forces developers to batch changes, which increases risk and delays critical fixes
Order management should be fully automated for B2C businesses — manual order fulfillment doesn't scale
Marketing systems like simple link-sharing prompts can passively generate backlinks without ongoing effort
Toyota's SMED philosophy of making die changes fast enough to do anytime directly applies to software deployment — if deploys are cheap, you fix defects immediately
money_quotes: That is the beautiful thing about systems: systems scale.
Work on your system once and you keep getting value out of it for an extended period of time, without inputting more work.
If you sell a piece of software that crashes, you have to answer an email 'Why did my software crash?' Or, you could stop your software from crashing.
Thats in the vicinity of fifty mouse clicks, taking 15-30 minutes, to make a one-character change to my published executable.
Neat new features which are tested and done sit on my local machine for a week because I can't spare the time at the moment.
For a B2C company I cannot imagine a reason why you would not totally automate the order process. Customer pays you, customer gets license key, you wake up in the morning a little richer.

The day I got a link from a PR5 ESL site I was busy doing the important things for my uISV's success: drinking cocoa and reading the best series of books about the Napoleonic Wars fought with dragons ever written.
posts.key_insight: The highest-leverage work a solo developer can do is building systems that eliminate repetitive manual steps, because a painful process doesn't just waste time — it actively prevents you from shipping fixes and improvements.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers suspiciously high conversion rates in his Yahoo Search Marketing dashboard, with some long-tail keywords showing impossible 300% conversion rates. He traces the issue to a bug in Yahoo's conversion tracking that counts multiple hits when users visit the download page more than once, likely due to IE users double-clicking links. This leaves him unable to determine whether YSM is actually performing well or if the data is entirely unreliable.
key_points: Yahoo Search Marketing's conversion tracking had a bug that inflated conversion rates, sometimes showing impossible 300%+ rates
The bug likely stemmed from counting multiple page visits as multiple conversions, exacerbated by IE users who double-click links
Long-tail search terms with low click volumes were most visibly affected, making the distortion obvious
Unreliable analytics make it impossible to evaluate whether an advertising channel is actually performing
money_quotes: I was pleasantly suprised to see a weekly conversion rate in the 60% region, spiking to 80% on many days…
80%?! No, sorry, something is rotten in the land of Denmark.
I got down to the Long Tail of searchers, the kind that get clicked on once in a week… and which had 300% conversion rates.
Some bug in Yahoo's CR tracking has been causing them to pick up multiple hits nearly every time somebody visits my download page.
So now I've got to figure out some way to test whether YSM is actually performing decently or if its entirely a mirage caused by poor data analysis.
posts.key_insight: When ad platform analytics show impossibly good numbers, the most likely explanation is a tracking bug, not actual performance — and unreliable data is worse than no data because it prevents sound spending decisions.

posts.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews Direct Access, a keyboard shortcut launcher from Nagarasoft that lets users type keywords followed by a hotkey to launch applications, open folders, or insert text snippets. He describes how he configured it to replace tedious navigation through cluttered Windows screens, bookmark lookups, and even IDE code snippets. The post paints a vivid picture of a developer's daily friction—deep folder paths, modal dialogs blocking the desktop, and underused IDE features—and how a simple launcher eliminates it. He concludes that the $40 price, which initially seemed steep, would be cheap at twice the cost given the productivity gains.
key_points: A simple keyboard launcher that maps typed keywords to actions can dramatically reduce the friction of navigating a cluttered development environment
Developer productivity often suffers not from hard problems but from mundane navigation overhead—deep folder paths, bookmark management, and Start Menu clutter
The tool can be extended beyond app launching to serve as a portable macro system for code snippets across different IDEs
Software that initially seems expensive for a 'single-use toy' can prove to be a bargain once you experience the compounding productivity gains
money_quotes: My productivity has not increased so much since I learned to Alt-Tab.
The first time I saw that, before actually using it, I thought 'God, thats a lot of money for a single-use toy'. Now I think it would be cheap at twice the price.
I offered to write them a robot to hit the page 100 times a day to allow me to set mine to Google or something but noooooo they said that would be cheating.
I'm sort of obsessive-compulsive about having access to data, and yet I underuse bookmarks hideously.
I don't know about you but on a scale of 1-10 I probably use all my IDEs at about a 3, because I never put in enough time to learn the guts of them.
posts.key_insight: The biggest productivity gains often come not from better algorithms or frameworks but from eliminating the small, repeated friction of navigating your own tools.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces the release of Bingo Card Creator 1.03, which fixes memory issues when printing large batches, adds threaded printing to prevent UI lockups, and eliminates visual flickering. The improvements were directly motivated by his first product return, where a customer cited these issues. He notes the value of customer feedback even when it comes in the form of a refund request, and mentions that PayPal refunds don't cost processing fees.
key_points: Customer returns provide valuable product feedback that should be acted on immediately
Untested edge cases (like printing 50 cards at once) reveal assumptions developers make about user behavior
UI responsiveness and polish matter even for simple utility software — flickering and lockups erode trust
PayPal refunds are fee-free, reducing the financial sting of returns
money_quotes: I hadn't anticipated anybody would try to do this and so didn't test it… doh?
They got kicked to the front burner directly after being cited as reasons for my first return.
Yay for useful feedback, especially useful feedback that doesn't technically cost me any money.
posts.key_insight: Customer complaints and returns are among the most actionable forms of product feedback, turning known technical debt into urgent priorities.

posts.summary: McKenzie evaluates the economics of submitting shareware to download sites versus paid advertising channels like Google AdWords. He argues that most download sites offer a poor value proposition by charging listing fees and commissions while providing unverifiable traffic quality. He makes an exception for high-traffic sites like Download.com, which generated significant downloads, and Tucows, despite its slow processing. He ultimately submits to 89 free sites via RoboSoft as a low-risk experiment while expressing clear preference for Google ads where ROI is measurable.
key_points: Most download sites invert the value exchange by charging authors for listings, when authors are the ones providing free content that attracts visitors
Google AdWords offers superior value because it provides measurable, controllable ROI compared to unverifiable download site traffic
Download.com proved its worth by generating downloads equal to 50% of direct distribution in just 3 weeks
Free listings are worth pursuing as low-risk experiments, but paying for listings without quality data is irrational
money_quotes: What I bring to the table: I give you software for free, and this lets you attract visitors to your pages, not necessarily in the search for my software but in the search for an aggregator of lots of software.
I would much rather spend the $20~$100 you guys want on Google ads, where I have 100% control of my advertising and can guarantee that I'll get X number of downloads for my dollar.

Funny, given that there are hundreds upon hundreds of download sites on the net, and I have no efficient method of evaluating quality of traffic I'll get, this does not strike me as a good proposition.
All I need is for one of you to go 'bad neighborhood' to screw my business royally.
Tucows says they'll have my application processed at some point in 2007. Thanks fellas. I love you, too.
In any event, free is free. My site is now available at 89 places more than it was this morning.
posts.key_insight: When you can't measure the quality of a marketing channel but can measure an alternative, spend your money where you have data and use the unmeasurable channels only when they're free.

posts.summary: McKenzie advocates tracking which paths within trial software lead users to click the Buy Now button, arguing this data is essential for optimizing conversions. He presents a simple technique: append query parameters to the purchase page URL from different locations in the app, then use web analytics to see which paths drive the most purchase intent. The method requires no server-side changes since web servers log and ignore parameters on static HTML pages. He notes this takes about 10 seconds to implement and provides actionable data for A/B testing nag screens, exit screens, and UI highlighting.
key_points: Tracking which in-app paths lead to purchase clicks is critical data most small software vendors neglect to collect
Appending dummy query parameters to static HTML URLs is a zero-infrastructure way to track referral sources from within your application
Web servers will happily accept and log arbitrary parameters on static file requests, making this technique trivially easy
The data enables testing specific conversion tactics: nag screens, exit screens, highlighted menu items, and tip-of-the-day prompts
Search engine concerns about dynamic parameters don't apply since bots don't spider your desktop application
money_quotes: From your perspective thats the most important mouse click in your program there is.
We're computer programmers because we are too lazy to do things like that when there are 10 second solutions.
Did you know that your web server will happily take any number of parameters to a request for a static HTML file and promptly log and ignore them? Yeah, who knew.
Does a nag screen produce appreciable results? How about an exit screen? Do people click the button more when the menu item is highlighted in blue (*cough* yes *cough*)?
Since GoogleBot isn't spidering your application, you can go hog wild with referrals from there and it will never be the wiser.
posts.key_insight: You can track which paths in your trial software lead to purchases with zero infrastructure by appending query parameters to your static purchase page URL and reading the results in Google Analytics.

posts.summary: McKenzie responds to a suggestion that he should avoid Java/.NET dependencies by programming in C++ instead. He argues with data from his own analytics that the concern about runtime deployment is outdated: only 10% of his visitors use dialup, 97% already have Java installed, and his target market of educators already uses Java-dependent software. He concludes that the development time savings from using Java far outweigh the tiny number of potential customers who might be lost.
key_points: Only 10% of his visitors use dialup, and half of those are from countries where he won't sell software anyway, making the bandwidth concern negligible
Java deployment rates among his visitors are 97%, far higher than outdated internet statistics from 2000 suggest
Educational software commonly requires Java, so his target market of teachers already has it installed
Development speed matters more than eliminating a runtime dependency — he'd still be months from launch if he'd used MFC/C++
Decisions should be driven by your own analytics data, not received wisdom from years-old internet forum posts
money_quotes: This would have been a valid consideration… in 1998.
Don't believe the statistics from 2000 that you read on the Internet about 50% deployment rates — Java, like Flash or .NET or anything, is a very viral bit of software.
You'll eventually stumble across content which you want to access that needs it, and after you do this you're set until your next computer upgrade.
I guarantee you that if I had to do my interface in MFC I'd still be months away from launch.
At current traffic rates, assuming every one of my dialup customers had no JRE and refused to install, that would cost me a total of 5 successful trial installations a month.
I get more installations originating from AdSense spam sites every week.
posts.key_insight: Technology adoption decisions should be based on your actual user data and development velocity, not on outdated conventional wisdom about what customers can or cannot run.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on the early results of using RoboSoft's auto-submission service to distribute his Bingo Card Creator software to download sites. Traffic hit an all-time high, roughly 25% above his previous peak and double his traffic from a week prior. The increase came not from paid ads or organic search but from direct hits (trial installs phoning home) and referrals from download sites. He estimates more trial installs in 24 hours than in the previous two weeks, validating the auto-submission experiment.
key_points: Auto-submission to download sites drove traffic to an all-time high, surpassing even a previous viral spike
Paid advertising and organic search remained flat — the entire increase came from download site distribution
Direct hits increased by nearly an order of magnitude, representing trial installs phoning home from off-site downloads
Download sites hosting the installer themselves makes raw HTTP log analysis unreliable for measuring true distribution
money_quotes: I had my highest traffic ever today, surpassing even the spike from the pirate kings.
The number of 'direct' hits I got on my website increased by almost an order of magnitude.
I had more trial installs in the last 24 hours than I've had in the last two weeks.
Some download sites download the exe from my site and host it themselves, so if they give out 100 trials they only generate one result in my logs.
Well, that certainly worked out well. If any significant portion of this new traffic converts RoboSoft is getting my money as promised.
posts.key_insight: Automated submission to software download sites can produce a dramatic, immediate spike in trial installs that dwarfs both paid advertising and organic search traffic.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares an amusing anecdote about discovering his software (Bingo Card Creator) being reviewed on a Chinese warez/cracking site. The review gives his product 3 out of 5 stars, noting it's light on features but good for its niche of English learners. He finds humor in the Chinese hacker's colorful pseudonym and admits he can only roughly translate the Chinese review by recognizing shared characters with Japanese.
key_points: His software was pirated and reviewed on a Chinese cracking site, which he treats with bemusement rather than outrage
The review itself is fairly accurate — his product is niche and simple but effective for its target audience
He demonstrates his cross-cultural literacy by attempting to translate Chinese through his knowledge of Japanese kanji
money_quotes: Say what you will about Chinese hackers, at the very least they have more amusing pseudonyms than our home-grown miscreants.
I can just sort of get the gist of it by picking out words in isolation which happen to be the same in Japanese, in much the same manner that you can sort-of understand Portuguese by way of Spanish.
posts.key_insight: When your software gets pirated and reviewed by international crackers, it's a backhanded sign that your product has enough reach to attract attention beyond your intended market.

posts.summary: McKenzie analyzes the gap between his 1,000+ trial downloads of Bingo Card Creator and the mere 18 confirmed active users detected through update checking. He systematically diagnoses why downloads don't convert to installations — IE security blocking auto-downloads, users failing to install, Java runtime requirements creating friction, and users opting out of update checks. He proposes a 'Bonus Pack' of extra word lists as an honest incentive for users to phone home, giving him better installation metrics while providing genuine value.
key_points: The gap between reported downloads and confirmed installations reveals massive invisible friction in the software distribution pipeline
IE's security model silently blocks auto-downloads, creating failures the developer can never detect or recover from
Java runtime dependencies add multiple steps of friction that kill conversions before the user ever sees the product
Tracking actual installations requires creative incentive design that balances business needs with user honesty
New word lists are low-effort 'features' (~10 minutes each) that provide outsized perceived value to users
money_quotes: This is obviously the worst sort of failure — its a failure I'll never know about and can't recover from.
I obviously can't sit on folks shoulders and make them press the 6 buttons it will take to get the 18MB JRE installed.
It would be the simplest thing in the world for me to ping my server regardless of whether they want to check for updates or not, but its not honest.
They're 'new features' from the perspective of my users and only take ~10 minutes to compile each.
Its the same 'tell me you exist and I'll give you additional benefits' transaction as the download-the-bonus-pack option is, but it seems to be based on a deception.
posts.key_insight: The most dangerous conversion failures are the invisible ones — when users silently bounce before ever experiencing your product, you lose both the customer and the data to diagnose the problem.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares Jonathan Coulton's 'Code Monkey' song as the unofficial anthem of his software business, finding humor and inspiration in its depiction of programmer life. The post is a lighthearted interlude in his business blog, but he slips in a sharp observation about pricing psychology: despite loving the song, he never paid for it because it was free, illustrating how free pricing signals low value and discourages voluntary payment. He notes this lesson is relevant to the micro-ISV community debating pricing strategies.
key_points: McKenzie identifies deeply with the 'Code Monkey' song as capturing the indie developer experience
He makes a pointed observation about his own behavior: he loved the song but never paid because it was free, demonstrating that free pricing undermines voluntary payment
He frames this as a cautionary lesson for micro-ISV developers who underprice or give away their software
The post shows McKenzie's early ability to extract business insights from everyday experiences
money_quotes: I try not to listen to it when actually coding because the tears of laughter make it hard to see semicolons.
After one listen to it I would have gladly paid money for it. However, since its free, I haven't taken time to send the guy so much as a $1 Paypal thanking him for his time.
I think there is a lesson here for a segment of the uISV community regarding pricing-as-signal.
posts.key_insight: When you price something at free, even people who genuinely love it and would have paid will default to paying nothing — price is a signal, not just a number.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares two simple installer tweaks that significantly improved his software's activation rate. The first is adding a desktop shortcut in addition to a start menu link, which he implemented in 45 seconds. The second is offering to run the program immediately after installation completes. He argues that non-technical users will get distracted or lost if forced to find the program themselves, and that these micro-optimizations doubled the percentage of downloads that resulted in verified program executions.
key_points: Adding a desktop shortcut alongside the start menu link doubled verified execution rates from downloads
Non-technical users have cluttered desktops and will get distracted if not immediately guided to the program
The installer itself is your best tool for getting users into your product — remove every friction point between download and first use
Small, easily-implemented changes (this one took 45 seconds) can have outsized effects on conversion
money_quotes: It also literally doubled the percentage of my downloads which I can verify were executed.
Have you ever seen the desktop of a non-technical user? Its a rather cluttered place.

Don't force them to hunt for your program, because there's a large chance that they'll get distracted away from the task at hand (getting hooked on your product) and will instead resume browsing the Internet, leave the computer and forget they downloaded anything, etc.
So take all the guesswork out of getting to your very best sales vehicle.
Trust me, it will work out much better for the majority of your customers than 'Here's a ZIP file, hope you know what to do', at least if you've got non-technical users like me.
posts.key_insight: Tiny reductions in friction between downloading software and actually running it — like auto-launching after install — can dramatically increase activation and sales.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports results from two experimental AdWords ads for his Bingo Card Creator software. A 'poetry' ad with campy rhyming copy doubled click-through rates versus his optimized control while maintaining similar cost-per-acquisition. A 'white space' ad offering free Dolch sight word lists achieved a remarkable 5.8% CTR by leading with a free resource rather than directly selling software. Both experiments cost under $5 total while generating nearly $48 in revenue, demonstrating that creative ad copy and indirect conversion funnels can outperform conventional approaches.
key_points: Unconventional ad copy (campy poetry) doubled CTR versus the optimized control ad while maintaining nearly identical cost-per-acquisition
Leading with a free resource rather than a direct product pitch achieved 5.8% CTR, far exceeding the control's 1.1%, despite a lower conversion rate
The total cost of running both experiments was $4.61, generating $48 in revenue — demonstrating how cheap it is to test marketing hypotheses at small scale
White space in ad copy and differentiation from competitors' dense formatting creates visual standout that drives clicks
Indirect conversion paths (free resource → product) can be viable even when conversion rates are lower, if click volume compensates
money_quotes: Marketing should be more fun than that.
And, hey, if it didn't work I can always kill it in a week and not lose more than a cup of cocoa.
Something about the combination of 'free stuff you can use' with the eyecatching nature of the ad draws in teachers like moths to the flame.
The CTR is about 5.8%, which is just jawdropping for an ad averaging in the 3rd position.
And how much money did finding out these insights cost me? At last count, $4.61.
Oh yeah, how much money did these ads make me over the week? A little digging in Google analytics says… a hair under $48.
posts.key_insight: Creative differentiation in ad copy and offering free value upfront can dramatically outperform conventional optimized ads, and testing these hypotheses costs almost nothing at small scale.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovered that a single keyword he added to his AdWords campaign on a whim was responsible for a 50% increase in his advertising costs over two weeks. The keyword had a low 9% conversion rate despite generating many clicks, dragging down his overall cost per action from $0.40 to $0.60+. Rather than building a landing page for traffic that likely wanted a different product (printable numeric bingo cards for gambling), he killed the ads entirely. He also notes uncertainty about whether AdWords is actually generating completed sales, suspecting a tracking misconfiguration with cross-session cookie attribution.
key_points: A single poorly-performing keyword can silently destroy the economics of an otherwise healthy ad campaign
High click-through rate is not inherently good — effective ads that attract the wrong audience waste money
Sometimes the right business decision is to abandon a market segment rather than invest in converting it
Conversion tracking across sessions is a real technical challenge that can mask whether paid advertising is actually working
Regular monitoring of ad spend metrics is essential — two weeks of inattention cost him significantly
money_quotes: Business is business, but a little bit of humanity goes a long way. However, investments, on the other hand, have neither feelings nor moral standing.
The culprit was one single keyword which I had added two weeks ago on a lark and forgotten about.
I had a very effective ad for it, and the very effective ad was very effective at getting people to click to my site, and then most clicked right out again.
