Averaging those abyssmal results with another ~50 keywords all performing as well as ever increased my advertising costs to the tune of 50%.
So I had two choices… make a better landing page or get out of that market.
I decided against the landing page, figuring that most people were looking for cards to print and just didn't want to get invested in software to print cards.
posts.key_insight: Ruthless optimization of ad spend means quickly killing campaigns that attract the wrong audience, no matter how effective they are at generating clicks.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues against the prevailing trend of writing list-style blog posts optimized for social media sites like Digg, contending that such content attracts fleeting attention from readers who won't remember your name. He makes the case that investing in quality, in-depth writing builds lasting value through blogroll links (which provide permanent SEO benefit unlike ephemeral social media links), loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues attracting readers long after publication. The post reflects his early thinking on content marketing strategy, arguing that building trust through sustained quality writing is the path to monetizing a blog audience. He acknowledges list posts are easier to produce but urges writers to develop genuine craft instead.
key_points: List-style posts optimized for Digg generate traffic from readers who leave in 90 seconds and can't recall your name two minutes later
Blogroll links are the most valuable SEO asset because they never fall to archives, unlike social media links that expire in hours
RSS readers represent a disruptive shift that makes every reader their own aggregator, raising the stakes for consistent quality
Well-written posts have compounding returns because they continue attracting readers long after publication, while list posts require constant production
Building trust through quality writing expands throughout your niche and eventually converts to revenue
money_quotes: If you write for people with the attention span of an ADHD flea who is hopped up on methamphetamines, then this is who you'll end up with for readers.
You don't get blogrolled after the first visit any more than you get to meet the parents after the first date.
You know the one type of link which never falls to the archives? Blogrolling.
RSS readers make every reader into their own aggregator. I cannot overemphasize how fundamental a change this is.
Poor writers ignore the rules, good writers follow the rules, great writers break the rules, and the very best writers make the rules.
If you constantly keep cranking out the top 10 lists to an audience who thinks yesterday was ages ago, you'll have to produce something new every day or the readers will stop coming.
Write in a way that shows off your own voice, personality, and ideas, the better to stand out against a sea of mediocrity.
posts.key_insight: Investing in quality writing over clickbait list posts builds compounding returns through permanent blogroll links, loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues working for you long after publication.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a mid-month statistics update for his Bingo Card Creator software business, now six weeks old. Sales have tripled each two-week period, from 1 to 2 to 6, putting him well ahead of his monthly goal of 4 sales. His expenses run about $110/month across hosting, advertising on Google and Yahoo, and e-junkie, requiring roughly 4.5 sales to break even. He projects consistent profitability of around $1,500/year with minimal ongoing effort, and breaks down his sales sources as roughly half from Google ads, a quarter from download sites, and a quarter from a single teacher message board.
key_points: Sales are growing exponentially in the early weeks: 1, then 2, then 6 per two-week period, tripling his original monthly target
Monthly expenses of ~$110 across hosting, Google/Yahoo ads, and payment processing create a low breakeven threshold of 4.5 sales
Word-of-mouth from a single teacher message board accounts for 25% of sales, validating Seth Godin's approach of making products easy to recommend
Download-to-purchase conversion sits at roughly 3%, with Google ad cost per acquisition ($10-15 per purchaser) leaving comfortable margins
Yahoo advertising is underperforming and will be cut, showing early discipline about dropping unprofitable channels
money_quotes: My first two weeks in business I saw one sale. My next two weeks I saw 2 sales. These past two weeks, lets see, 6 sales? I'm going to shoot waaaaaaay past my personal goal for August, which was originally 4 sales.
I now feel rather confident that I can make at least 10 sales a month, consistently, for about as long as I want with rather little additional work.

By my quick back-of-the-envelope math, thats about $1,500 a year, which won't exactly have me sipping iced cocoa on the beach. Actually, check that, that is enough for me to afford a quick jaunt out to Okinawa every year.
Seth Godin, a marketing genius whose key insight is 'make it easy for people to tell their friends about you', would be proud.
Total number of support requests: 0
posts.key_insight: A micro-ISV can reach sustainable profitability remarkably quickly with low fixed costs, disciplined ad spend, and organic word-of-mouth from a niche community.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that software developers systematically underprice their products due to psychological biases rather than market reality. He contends that customers don't know or care how long software took to build — they only care whether it solves their problem. Using the analogy of a heart surgeon who charges $50,000 for implanting $5 worth of parts, he demonstrates that value-based pricing is the norm in professional services. He explains that software's near-zero marginal cost means developers can pick any point on the demand curve, and that the demand curve is external to development effort. The core message is that developers compare their shipped product to their original vision and feel it falls short, leading them to underprice — a phenomenon he calls 'coder's remorse.'
key_points: Customers can only hold development time against you if they know about it — there's no reason to volunteer that information
Non-developer customers see software as black magic and will pay for working solutions rather than attempt to build their own
The demand curve is external to you — it doesn't shift based on how much time or money you spent on development
Value-based pricing means your price should reflect what the customer gets out of it, not what it cost you to make
Developers systematically undervalue their own software by comparing it to their original imagined ideal rather than to what actually exists in the market
money_quotes: Your heart surgeon doesn't tell you he's billing you $50,000 for three hours' labor to implant 32 cents worth of plastic and a $5 chip made in Taiwan.
Everything that happens inside of the computer is black magic to them and you are the shaman they rely on for magical amulets to keep the evil spirits at bay.
The demand curve doesn't magically shift up or shift down just because you spent 10 years or $3 million on the development effort — the demand curve is external to you.
Regardless of what it took to make my product, my price is a 'fair' one because you'll get oh so much more out of it.
I know of at least one product out there which would probably see higher sales if it were priced at triple what it is right now.
There is no competitor who released your ImaginationWare into the market — you're competing only with what actually exists, not with what could exist.
Don't fall for coder's remorse.
posts.key_insight: Software pricing should be based on the value customers receive, not on development cost or time, and most developers underprice because they compare their product to an imagined ideal rather than to the actual market.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a day-in-the-life vignette from his dual existence as a Japanese salaryman and indie software entrepreneur. At his day job, he spent an hour helping a Japanese student with a school project about foreign countries, fielding questions about American schools. Meanwhile, his side business Bingo Card Creator continued generating daily sales, customer thank-you notes, and support requests without his active involvement. He marvels at reaching consistent daily sales and realizes the revenue is becoming significant enough to require professional tax and legal advice.
key_points: McKenzie's life in Japan as a foreigner creates unique cultural exchange moments that enrich his day job experience
His software business has reached the milestone of consistent daily sales (1-2 per day for five straight weekdays)
The back-of-envelope math ($24x30x12 > $8,000/year) shows his side project crossing from hobby into real taxable income
Customer emails arriving unsolicited (thank-you notes, referrals) signal organic word-of-mouth growth
money_quotes: This whole "get a sale every day" thing is starting to get a little overwhelming.
$24x30x12 > $8,000 per annum, and thats enough for, e.g., the government to start wondering "Hey, where is that all coming from".
I'll have to get legal before legal gets me.
I'm telling all my homeschooling friends!
I love both my jobs, actually.
posts.key_insight: The moment a side project starts generating consistent daily revenue is the inflection point where a hobby becomes a real business requiring professional infrastructure.

posts.summary: McKenzie briefly announces he'll be away from blogging for about a week due to his brother visiting Japan and an upcoming business trip for his day job to a remote location. Before leaving, he notes with excitement that his software business has been performing exceptionally well, with sales notifications arriving every time he checks email. He promises to explain what's been driving the unexpected success when he returns.
key_points: McKenzie is balancing his day job in Japan with his side software business
His Bingo Card Creator business is experiencing a sudden and significant uptick in sales
He teases a future post analyzing what strategies have been outperforming his expectations
money_quotes: Literally every single time I have checked my email the last couple of days its been You've Got Money.
When I've got some time after I get back I'll explain what has been going mostly right and what has been outperforming all of my expectations.
posts.key_insight: Even a simple side business can surprise its creator with unexpectedly strong sales momentum once the right pieces fall into place.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues forcefully that software developers who want to make money must limit their trial versions, rejecting the popular notion that donation-based models work. He cites Colin Messitt's controlled experiment showing limited versions outsold unlimited ones five to one, and Movable Type's average donation of 38 cents as evidence. He then explains his own approach with Bingo Card Creator, where he allows enough functionality to demonstrate value but not enough to complete the actual task (teaching a class). The post concludes that the right limitation strategy depends on deep knowledge of your target market and how they use your software.
key_points: A controlled experiment showed feature-limited shareware outsold unlimited donationware five to one, costing the developer $17,000 in lost sales from the unlimited group
Donation-based models produce negligible revenue — Movable Type's average donation was 38 cents despite powering thousands of blogs
The best trial limitation renders software 'attractive but very close to useless' for the actual job the customer needs done
Time-limited trials work best when the software becomes indispensable through daily use and accumulated configuration investment
Choosing the right limitation strategy requires deep understanding of how your specific target market actually uses the product
money_quotes: If you intend to make money by selling software, you need to limit your trial version. The only question is how.
The limited version outsold the unlimited one. Five to one. Colin calculates that the experiment cost him $17,000 in sales versus having 100% of the installations be limited.
Of all the people who thought Movable Type was worth paying real honest-to-God money for, the average donation was… 38 cents.

The reason I can limit my software like this is because I know enough about my target market to know what feature I can disable which will keep the software attractive but render it very close to useless.
If you're the kind of person who really needs Direct Access, after you've used it for a week you can't. Live. Without. It.
When you run into that 30 day limit and the convenience you have been relying on for 3+ weeks just shuts down, you whip out the credit card.
posts.key_insight: Successful software trial design requires limiting the product just enough that users can verify its value but cannot complete their actual task without paying — and choosing the right limitation requires deep knowledge of how customers use the product.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie returns from a five-day trip to Kyoto with his brother, noting the costs were offset by automated sales of his Bingo Card Creator software. He reflects on handling customer emails while traveling, reports August sales of $300, and enthusiastically recommends Robosoft's software submission service as a worthwhile $99 investment that paid for itself within a week through increased downloads and SEO benefits.
key_points: Passive income from a software product can offset travel expenses, creating a tangible emotional reward ('Suzie from Ohio just bought us lunch')
Even a one-person software business requires responsive customer communication while away, making web-based email essential
Software submission services like Robosoft can pay for themselves quickly through increased distribution and organic search ranking benefits from inbound links
Early-stage microISV revenue milestones ($300/month) feel significant when they demonstrably cover real-world costs
money_quotes: I really love that feeling: 'Hey Tim, Suzie from Ohio just bought us lunch!'
$99 might sound expensive but it paid for itself in about a week and thats not even counting the increase in organic search rankings that about 100 links will get me.
Two people, five days, lots of travelling, well, you do the math.
August sales are now $300 and climbing.
I dealt with about 5 emails, mostly 'Can your software do this?' (No, but working on it), questions about bingo in a large group (hey, I guess I am the de-facto expert now), and a refund.
posts.key_insight: A software product that generates sales while you sleep transforms travel from a pure expense into a partially self-funding experience, reinforcing the appeal of the microISV lifestyle.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts returning to his office to find his development machine destroyed by a power surge, the third computer lost in a week to the same cause. Despite losing $10,000 in hardware, management kept plugging machines into the faulty outlet without investigating. McKenzie's suggestion to check the electrical outlet with a voltmeter confirmed dangerous power spikes. The incident left his entire team idled, giving him unexpected free time to blog.
key_points: Three computers were destroyed in one week by power surges from a faulty electrical outlet, with no one investigating the root cause between failures
Management's failure to diagnose the problem before plugging in additional machines wasted $10,000 in hardware
A simple voltmeter check — prompted by McKenzie's suggestion — immediately identified the issue
The incident highlights the gap between engineering thinking (isolate the cause) and management thinking (keep plugging things in)
money_quotes: After losing $10,000 of hardware I personally would have, hmm, tried to isolate the cause of the problem before frying additional machines but, hey, thats why I'm an engineer and not management.
The reason we have no computers plugged in on this table is that they are all yakiniku.
The odds of me meeting my day job's next deadline are now about 10,000:1 against (100% of my team is now idled) and I get a day or two to catch up on blogging.
Hey, boss, I took an electrical engineering course in college. Maybe the socket here is getting power spikes.
Ha, ha, good thing nothing was plugged in here, otherwise your computers would be yakiniku.
posts.key_insight: When repeated failures share the same symptoms, the engineering instinct to diagnose root cause before continuing operations is worth more than the management instinct to just keep going.

posts.summary: McKenzie debunks myths from both the open source and micro-ISV communities about their supposed incompatibility. He argues that open source and small commercial software vendors coexist naturally because they serve fundamentally different needs and audiences. Most OSS is built by corporate-funded developers for programmer needs, while uISVs serve non-technical niches that OSS ignores. The real competitive moat for uISVs isn't code complexity but marketing, documentation, and user-facing work that OSS developers avoid. He uses his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study, showing how its open source competitor languishes despite being technically viable, because nobody is motivated to do the unglamorous work of reaching actual users.
key_points: Most major open source projects are primarily funded and developed by large corporations, not volunteer hobbyists working out of altruism
Programmers are great at building software for their own needs but poor at building for non-technical users, creating permanent niches for commercial software
The overwhelming majority of open source projects are inactive and abandoned, making the 'many eyes' benefit largely theoretical
Writing the software is roughly 10% of the battle — marketing, documentation, and user support are where uISVs create durable competitive advantage
OSS developers won't clone niche commercial software because the work is boring and the domains are too obscure to motivate intrinsic contribution
money_quotes: Programmers are very good at producing software for their own needs… and a lot less good at producing software for other's needs.

Most successful uISVs have long since learned the lesson that writing the program is about 10% of the battle: you've then got to get it in front of prospective users, write documentation, help them with their problems, market market and market some more.
Intrinsic motivation only takes the typical OSS developer as far as closing the IDE.
Here's a dirty little secret: most big-name OSS projects are produced primarily by programmers at large corporations working at the direction of their bosses in exchange for cash money.
I'm sure any competent C developer could take the bingo-cards codebase and make it the equal of Bingo Card Creator in less than a man-week. I'm equally sure that they won't.
IBM is no more interested in bankrolling bingo-cards than they are interested in rolling out their own Proprietary Bingo Solution because the niche is just too tiny.
I have the strong suspicion that, as the Texas saying has it, they have a lot of hat but no cattle.
posts.key_insight: Open source and commercial software don't compete because OSS developers lack the motivation to do the unglamorous marketing, documentation, and user-support work that makes niche software actually reach the people who need it.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes being approached by Bits du Jour, a daily software deal site, to feature Bingo Card Creator during their back-to-school week at a 40% discount. He explains why he generally avoids affiliate programs — the small per-sale incentive doesn't motivate affiliates to do quality marketing — but was persuaded by their low-friction pitch and trust-based approach. He sets modest expectations of roughly 2 sales given his niche product, but agrees to the experiment since it costs him almost nothing. He commits to sharing the results publicly.
key_points: The Bits du Jour model applies Woot's one-deal-per-day urgency concept to software, taking an affiliate commission while letting authors keep their own shopping cart and payment infrastructure
McKenzie's general anti-affiliate rationale: a $5 per-sale incentive doesn't motivate quality promotion, and he'd rather spend that money on AdWords where he controls the entire funnel
The pitch succeeded because it required minimal effort (under 10 minutes of setup), used trust-based terms, and carried essentially zero downside risk
Niche products face a natural ceiling on deal sites — unless the audience happens to include hundreds of teachers, a daily deal site's general traffic won't convert well for specialized software
McKenzie frames business decisions as small experiments with bounded downside rather than strategic commitments
money_quotes: I have a $23.93 incentive to sell one marginal copy of my software. Any given affiliate has, say, a $5 incentive. $5 doesn't motivate someone to put any investment or thought into actually getting my software in front of people who need it.
I'd much rather spend the affiliate's commission on AdWords, where I have total control of the experience from beginning to end and only have to deal with myself.
Nothing motivates people like the words 'I trust you'. Particularly when its 'I trust that we will have such a successful relationship that you will choose to pay me at the end of it'.
I'm personally betting on 2, which means about $23 for me and $6 to Bits du Jour — I actually mentioned that to them and they said they were happy to experiment.
That is the sort of transparency that many Internet companies brag about but few actually achieve.
posts.key_insight: Affiliate programs are generally a bad deal for niche software because the small per-sale commission doesn't motivate quality promotion, but low-effort experiments with bounded downside are always worth trying.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes how he resisted the urge to brush off an enthusiastic but demanding customer ('Sally') who sent daily emails with feature requests and questions about Bingo Card Creator. Instead, he followed his own advice about customer service, implemented her feature requests (which took only 4 hours), and sent her a preview of the improved software. Sally turned out to work for a state government agency that oversees hundreds of institutions, and she began evangelizing the product across her entire professional network. The post argues that treating every customer interaction as an opportunity rather than an annoyance can yield outsized returns.
key_points: Feature requests that seem tedious often turn out to be less work than expected and genuinely improve the product
A single enthusiastic customer with the right network can become a more powerful distribution channel than any marketing spend
Customer service patience is an investment with unpredictable but potentially enormous ROI
With only 15 copies sold at the time, one well-connected advocate could transform the business trajectory
money_quotes: I am a weak person and was tempted to ask Sally to be a bit less enthusiastic after the first couple of emails.

Well, it turns out after actually sitting down and coding them (took about 4 hours total) they were a lot less of an excercize in frustration than I thought they would be and they really, seriously improve the program.
Sally was happy. Like, deliriously happy. Like, so happy she is recommending Bingo Card Creator to everyone she knows happy.
Do you know how many fire departments are in a mid-sized American state? Well, it turns out there is a publicly available list of them. The list runs to 100 pages, single-spaced.
Sally is busily working her email lists and telling people at her conferences that I'm the best thing for fire departments since the dalmation.
Before you tell a customer 'no' out of laziness, are you sure you're not turning away your very own Sally?
posts.key_insight: The customer who seems like the biggest nuisance may turn out to be your most valuable evangelist — never dismiss an enthusiastic user out of laziness.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV developers should aggressively outsource tasks that aren't core to their business. He presents four heuristics for deciding what to outsource: when customers can't tell the difference, when the cost is trivially low, when outsourced quality exceeds what you could produce, and when the effective hourly rate of doing it yourself is insultingly low. He illustrates with examples from his own Bingo Card Creator business, including paying $5/month for payment notification handling and $29.95 for professional stock icons. He describes a 'cookie jar' technique where you physically pay yourself per hour to internalize the opportunity cost of DIY work on low-value tasks.
key_points: Your time as a solo developer is your scarcest resource — spend it on what makes your business special, not on commoditized tasks
Four outsourcing heuristics: customer-invisible work, trivially cheap services, quality improvements, and low implied hourly rate
Never outsource customer-facing support or core product logic — those are your competitive advantages as a small business
Programmers have a psychological blind spot where they'll tolerate weeks of wheel-reinvention but can't stand 15 seconds of repetitive nuisance
The 'cookie jar trick' of physically paying yourself per hour makes the opportunity cost of low-value work viscerally real
money_quotes: My question is what you'd have to be smoking to want to.
For things you are less good at, outsource them. Now.
The cost of outsourcing is lower than the cost to even consider whether you're capable of insourcing.
Have you ever noticed that computer programmers can't tolerate 15 seconds of nuisance an hour but will happily spend man-weeks reinventing the wheel?
I wouldn't outsource programming the key features of my program. But everything else is fair game.
I'm not an artist — the one thing in my life I have ever successfully drawn is a goomba.
After 1,000 yen was in the cup I surrendered and just downloaded this guy's solution.
The 1,000 yen stupidity tax is lunch today. I'm thinking chicken.
posts.key_insight: Solo software developers systematically undervalue their own time and should outsource any task where the cost is lower than the opportunity cost of doing it themselves.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on unexpected SEO success with MSN search, which has become his top sales source despite initially dismissing it. He notes a sudden spike in Google organic traffic suggesting a PageRank improvement, though conversion from Google searches is poor at 10% due to mismatched search intent. He also addresses users searching for registration keys, turning a potential piracy signal into a customer service opportunity.
key_points: MSN search proved surprisingly valuable for reaching non-technical audiences, becoming the #1 sales source despite being initially dismissed
Google organic traffic surged but converted poorly because visitors searching for educational content (Dolch sight word lists) had no intent to buy software
Search intent mismatch can make high traffic nearly worthless for conversions
People searching for registration keys may be confused customers rather than pirates, and addressing them publicly is smart customer service
money_quotes: Long time readers of this blog will probably remember me describing MSN as a very cheap date in terms of SEO. Well, perhaps I shouldn't have been so dismissive: she's now my #3 source of hits and my #1 source of sales for last week.
Apparently she's rather popular with non-technical audiences, just judging from the search queries I'm getting.
I'm flabbergasted that I get the rank I do for some of these: you would think "bingo cards" would be a fairly competitive search term but somehow I'm still on the first page.
Ironically, my conversion for Google organic searches is terrible (10%) because most people are looking for Dolch sight word lists and get them and leave without trying my software.
For the 5 people who found this blog looking for "Bingo Card Creator registration key": you'll find it in the mail you got after you purchased the software.
posts.key_insight: A search engine dismissed as unimportant can become your best sales channel when its audience better matches your customer profile, while high-traffic sources with mismatched intent convert poorly.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that apologizing to customers is one of the most powerful and cost-free tools in customer service. He builds on advice from TryBeta.com about taking blame for all mistakes, expanding it into a broader philosophy: sincere apologies combined with immediate action plans create lasting positive impressions. He advocates minimizing ego involvement, offering refunds proactively (which few customers actually take), and using small scripted apologies to smooth over minor friction. He draws on his experience at both his software business and his day job, plus observations about Japanese customer service culture.
key_points: Sincere apologies are free and create disproportionately positive customer impressions that last for the entire business relationship
Offering refunds proactively is a power move — very few customers take you up on it, but they remember the offer indefinitely
Ego is the main barrier to effective apologizing; separating your identity from your product makes apologies natural
Small scripted apologies for minor inconveniences are a form of excellence — getting the little things right compounds
Blaming inanimate objects for customer mistakes is the customer service equivalent of a white lie and is perfectly acceptable
money_quotes: Apologizing literally works magic on people, particularly sincere apologizing (not 'I'm sorry you're too stupid to use our software but thats the way it is').
Very few people will actually take you up on that offer, but they will remember you making it for the duration they do business with you.
Its a little thing, but excellence is getting the little things right.
My software is my baby, but its not so much a part of me that I can't count to five when someone says they can't use it.
'The computer is acting up' is the 'No, honey, that dress looks great on you' of customer service.
Inanimate objects which you own rarely resent being blamed for your customers' mistakes.
And it costs absolutely nothing to do.
posts.key_insight: A sincere apology costs nothing but creates lasting goodwill, and proactively offering to make things right builds more loyalty than never making mistakes would.

posts.summary: McKenzie shows before-and-after screenshots of his Bingo Card Creator software after purchasing stock icons for $29.95. He demonstrates how the visual upgrade from version 1.03 to 1.04 makes the interface look more colorful and professional. He emphasizes that this particular screenshot is his most important sales asset, placed above the fold on his main page. He also notes a side benefit: changing the card preview to white backgrounds better reflects printed output.
key_points: A $29.95 investment in stock icons can meaningfully improve the perceived quality of software
The most prominent screenshot on a product page is a critical sales tool and deserves investment
Visual polish makes software look more substantial to potential customers, independent of functionality
Side-effect improvements (white card preview matching print output) can be kept when they enhance both aesthetics and usability
money_quotes: This is the screenshot which directly sells my program more than any other.
Its my interface looking at its best and brightest and it hints to the teachers exactly how much use they can get out of my program.
Now doesn't that look more colorful and vibrant?
I will keep that interface element because it makes my program look more substantial and because it greatly simplifies the workflow.
I took advantage of icons-icons's August sale to get a pile of stock icons for $29.95.
posts.key_insight: Small, inexpensive visual improvements like stock icons can disproportionately improve how professional and substantial your software appears to potential customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes porting Bingo Card Creator to Mac OS X, motivated by a dedicated customer who bought the software before the Mac version existed. Rather than learning the Mac toolchain himself, he outsourced the native wrapper creation on RentACoder for $25. The Java codebase required zero lines of code changes for the port. He notes that Mac users represent about 2% of his site traffic but feels committed to the platform given he already has a paying customer. The post also briefly covers progress on version 1.04 with new features and word lists.
key_points: A single dedicated customer who pre-purchased the Mac version created enough commitment to justify the port
The entire Mac port required zero code changes thanks to Java's cross-platform nature, only a native wrapper
Outsourcing the native wrapper creation via RentACoder cost just $25, demonstrating extreme cost efficiency for a micro-ISV
Mac market share among his audience was only ~2%, making the business case marginal but the effort trivial enough to justify
money_quotes: She asked me whether I would have the Mac version done soon and I told her "Well, I certainly hope so". And then she went ahead and bought it, sight unseen.
I tried to refund her money because I can't take money for a product which doesn't officially exist yet but she would have none of it.
She sends me updates on how her son is loving reading and math nowadays.
I offered the project up at $25 to port Bingo Card Creator (where "port" means "spend five minutes creating a native wrapper").
Number of lines of codes changed for the Mac version? Erm, zero. I like being a Java Success Story (TM).
Is there a market for Bingo Card Creator on the Mac? Well, even if there wasn't, I've got one customer who bought it already and so I'm rather committed.
posts.key_insight: Even tiny markets are worth serving when the marginal cost of entry is near zero, and a single passionate customer can justify an entire platform port.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares an amusing example of a Japanese web service that converts text messages into schoolgirl leetspeak, charging about 5 cents per message via phone billing. Despite the service being trivially simple to build—essentially a short Perl script and a translation table—it processes 20 million messages monthly. The company also charges to decrypt the gibberish back into readable Japanese, effectively monetizing both directions of the same problem.
key_points: A trivially simple hosted service (15 lines of Perl + a lookup table) generates massive revenue by processing 20 million messages monthly at 5 cents each
The service solves a real pain point: typing stylized text is annoying on Japanese cellphones, so automation has genuine value
Charging for both encryption and decryption of the same gibberish is a brilliantly absurd double-dip business model
Mobile carrier billing infrastructure enables frictionless micropayments that make the economics work
money_quotes: Sounds like a complete waste of time, right? Yeah, probably… unless you process 20 million messages a month.
For a service which is, approximately, a 15 line Perl script and a 100 line Japanese-to-gibberish table of rules.
I was particularly impressed by the for-pay option to decrypt what your friend was trying to say to you in case you were in no mood to parse it yourself.
posts.key_insight: Enormous businesses can be built from trivially simple software when you solve a genuine friction point at scale with frictionless payments.

posts.summary: McKenzie lists the feature roadmap for version 1.04 of Bingo Card Creator, prompted by customer feedback. The update includes visual improvements, font choices, word wrap options, page positioning, and calling card printing. He teases readers with a puzzle about development time, noting one feature took 4 hours while another took just 5 minutes. The post illustrates the unpredictable nature of software development effort.
key_points: Customer feedback directly drove the feature priorities for the next release
The gap between perceived complexity and actual implementation time is often counterintuitive — one feature took 4 hours, another 5 minutes
Some features are flagged as doubtful for the release, showing disciplined scope management even while adding features
The post implicitly demonstrates the tension between responding to customer requests and managing feature creep
money_quotes: A customer convinced me to throw another one on the barbie, as it were.
One of the features on this list took 4 solid hours to implement. Another took 5 minutes. Any guesses which was which?
posts.key_insight: The actual development cost of features is wildly unpredictable and often inversely correlated with how complex they appear to users.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV websites with only a handful of pages are leaving money on the table by not creating content that targets long-tail search queries. He demonstrates with his own Bingo Card Creator site that even 200 words about a niche use case like 'icebreaker bingo' can generate consistent weekly revenue through organic search traffic. He explains that content serves three purposes: capturing long-tail searches, ranking for keywords you haven't anticipated, and creating linkable resources that generate natural backlinks. He contrasts his organic search results favorably against his paid AdWords spending, showing organic traffic converting better at lower cost.
key_points: Long-tail search queries drive the majority of organic traffic, but you need actual content on your site to rank for them
Even minimal content (200 words) about a niche use case can generate consistent recurring value that compounds over time
Natural English content captures unpredictable search queries that no amount of deliberate SEO optimization can target
Content creates linkability — people share useful articles but rarely share trial download pages
Organic search traffic converted to two sales that week while $20 in AdWords spending generated zero sales
money_quotes: It makes me probably $50 a week, which is not a bad return for writing a few extra pages about elementary school bingo variants.
The rest were, in general, a veritable deluge of once-in-a-lifetime queries… the kind that natural English snaps up like hotcakes and all the SEO in the world won't get you.
Very few people are going to say 'Wow, check out this trial demo download' (if they do about your software, mazeltov, you are going to be rich like a king).
If you put up, say, a page about how to use bingo to assess reading difficulties, teachers will swap it via email, link it on their blogs, and chat about it around the water cooler.
Content also has a nice property called linkability.

Remember, serving pages is essentially free at the margin. For the 80% of folks who visit my Dolch sight word lists and leave without viewing another page on my website I pay, well, absolutely nothing.
If its not natural to do so, maybe you should be writing different content.
posts.key_insight: Creating useful content for your target audience is the cheapest and most sustainable customer acquisition channel because it captures the long tail of search queries that paid advertising and minimal websites can never reach.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his August 2006 financial results for Bingo Card Creator, his first month of detailed public accounting. Despite expecting a small profit, he discovers a $1.90 loss after writing everything down, though cash flow remains positive due to timing differences in expense recognition. He breaks down sales ($333.54 net from 14 sales), expenses ($335.44 including advertising, hosting, and one-time costs), and detailed advertising and website traffic statistics. The post demonstrates the granular economics of a one-person micro-ISV in its earliest days.
key_points: First full month of business yielded a near-breakeven result: $333.54 net sales against $335.44 in expenses, a $1.90 loss
Google AdWords was the largest recurring expense at $90/month, with continuous optimization of ad text, landing pages, and keyword culling driving CPA toward his $0.30 target
A single teacher bulletin board post converted at 27%, far outperforming every paid channel — demonstrating the power of reaching your exact audience
The Long Tail applies to download sites: the top 5 sites account for roughly 40% of downloads, while the next 40 sites collectively contribute the remaining 60%
Yahoo Search Marketing was canceled due to untrustworthy analytics, consolidating spend on Google AdWords
money_quotes: Well, honestly, I thought I was going to post a minor paper profit. Hmm, I guess thats why we write things down.
The secret: continuous improvement of ad texts and landing pages, plus a recent ruthless culling of every keyword which wasn't performing well enough to justify keeping.
Single Post On Teacher Bulletin Board: 150 (27%) — I love my adoring fans.
Incidentally, the next 40 download sites on the list sum to about 60% of the total of these. Yep, Long Tail in action.
Basically it ended up being $30 given to charity for PR value.
I'm sorry to give you analog accuracy in a digital world, but its the best I can do without spending too much time playing accountant.
posts.key_insight: Radical transparency about the economics of a micro-business reveals that even near-breakeven months contain valuable lessons about channel optimization, conversion tracking, and the importance of actually writing down your numbers.

posts.summary: McKenzie responds to Seth Godin's commentary on Starbucks' failed email coupon promotion that went viral beyond its intended audience. He proposes a lightweight solution: ask customers for their first name and last four phone digits before honoring the coupon, creating just enough friction to deter mass abuse without actually building a verification system. He argues the real business calculus favors honoring the drinks since the marginal cost is trivial compared to brand damage from visibly canceling a promotion. The post demonstrates his characteristic focus on understanding the actual economics of a business decision rather than the surface-level problem.
key_points: Starbucks created a viral coupon problem by designing an email promotion as a chain letter with no controls on distribution
The optimal response is minimal friction that keeps honest people honest, not a real verification system — the appearance of tracking is sufficient deterrence
The marginal cost of honoring abused coupons is trivial (drinks cost ~10 cents to make) compared to the brand damage of publicly canceling a promotion
Security theater has legitimate business applications when the cost of actual enforcement exceeds the cost of the fraud
money_quotes: The only purpose of this system is to keep honest men honest and to remind folks that there is no presumptive right to free Starbucks.
Here's the secret to Starbucks: no matter how much they charge you, making the drink didn't cost more than 10 cents!
The damage to the brand from having to put that sign up everywhere, on the other hand, is at literally many orders of magnitude above the individual store.
The impact of one scammer who realizes he can beat the system (and, if you think of it, there is no system to beat here) is one ice drink per store.
That plus the fact that it is recorded is just enough to make them remember 'Oh, thats right, I'm being watched'.
posts.key_insight: When the cost of fraud is trivially small per incident but the brand damage of visibly fighting it is enormous, the right move is to honor the promotion with just enough friction to discourage casual abuse.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces the release of Bingo Card Creator version 1.04, distributed through Robosoft for automated submission to download sites. The update includes a Mac version, improved visual design with stock icons, and several user-requested features like font selection and single-card centering. He notes a practical tip about creating separate PAD files for Windows and Mac versions. The post reflects his early-stage microISV development process of iterating quickly based on customer feedback.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator 1.04 adds Mac support, expanding the potential customer base
Customer feedback directly drives feature development — a specific customer (Sally) requested single-card centering and got it
Automated submission tools like Robosoft save significant time when distributing to download sites
Practical PAD file tip: separate files for Windows and Mac versions simplifies submission
money_quotes: What Sally wants, Sally gets.
I love not having to submit to half a zillion places by myself
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software businesses grow by responding directly to individual customer requests and systematizing distribution to maximize reach.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide to software registration and copy protection for micro-ISVs, arguing that registration systems should be thought of as salesmen rather than security measures. He categorizes users into four types — from perfectly honest to dedicated pirates — and explains that protection schemes should target the middle groups while accepting that determined pirates are unbeatable. The post covers crack types (leaked keys, keygens, patches, cracked executables), defensive strategies (user-specific data, code obfuscation, frequent binary changes), and key generation approaches from public-key crypto to simple modular arithmetic. His core message is that developers should invest proportionally in protection, aiming to deter casual piracy rather than achieve impossible perfection.
key_points: Registration systems are marketing tools (salesmen), not security mechanisms — overly aggressive DRM harms paying customers more than it deters pirates
Users fall into four groups: perfectly honest, mostly honest, opportunistic pirates, and dedicated pirates — protection schemes primarily convert the 'mostly honest' group into paying customers
Every protection scheme will eventually be cracked; the goal is to keep circumvention obscure enough that casual users won't find or use cracks
Developers should force attackers toward less convenient crack types (from leaked keys up to cracked executables) through user-specific data, obfuscation, and frequent binary changes
The emotional violation developers feel about piracy leads to irrational over-investment in protection that punishes legitimate customers
money_quotes: You will have an easier time conceptualizing your registration scheme if you think of it as primarily a marketing, rather than technical, measure. Its your salesman that encourages folks to pay you money.
Salesmen do not typically kick their prospective customers where the sun doesn't shine, and many registration schemes do.

Someone who downloads your software and cracks it hasn't cost you any more money than someone who picks your door and walks around your apartment for 20 minutes without touching anything, yet the feeling that your rights have been violated is the same.
No protection scheme will survive indefinite contact with the adversary.
Your goal in instituting a protection scheme is not to achieve 0 utilization of your software by the Jolly Rogers of the world.

Defeating the cracker does not make you an appreciable amount of money. You just need to defeat the casual user in most instances, and the casual user does not have access to a debugger nor know how to use one.
Consider carefully how much pain you will authorize him to inflict.
posts.key_insight: Software copy protection is a marketing tool that should convert mostly-honest users into paying customers, not a security system that tries — and inevitably fails — to stop determined pirates.

posts.summary: McKenzie teases his next software project, which would be significantly more ambitious than Bingo Card Creator, targeting a small but growing community of less price-sensitive customers. He plans a multi-tier pricing model including freemium and subscription options, while keeping costs extremely low by reusing infrastructure from his first product. The timeline is delayed until January because he's studying for the JLPT Level 1 Japanese proficiency exam. He acknowledges the risk of depending on a third-party platform but remains enthusiastic about the planning process.
key_points: The new project targets a niche community of ~4,000 users experiencing rapid growth, with some prospects handling near $500K — a fundamentally different market than Bingo Card Creator's price-sensitive audience
He plans a sophisticated pricing strategy with four tiers (free, freemium, premium, premium + subscription), recognizing the product screams for a SaaS model
Bootstrap economics remain central: $500 budget for six months by reusing learnings and assets from Bingo Card Creator
He identifies platform dependency as the key risk — a third party could fail or eliminate the pain points he plans to solve
The extended JLPT digression reveals how deeply embedded he is in Japan, navigating professional life while functionally illiterate in written Japanese
money_quotes: There is no possible way I can or would do the 'full time job on top of a full time job' deathmarch for that long — it nearly hospitalized me the first time.
I think I can do this for probably $500 for the first 6 months.
This application screams 'Make part of me a web service and charge by the month.'
I'd be scarily dependent on a third-party not totally wrecking me by either failing or radically altering their own software/business to eliminate the pains I'm going to solve.
I'm going to get JLPT level 1 this year or perish in the attempt.
Try to imagine doing the same as a grown professional in real time with the boss standing over your shoulder.
Level 1: 'I can read Kirin's annual shareholder guidance and scoff at their inadequate protection against currency fluctuations.'
posts.key_insight: Even in 2006, McKenzie was already thinking in terms of SaaS pricing tiers and platform risk — foundational business strategy concepts that most indie developers wouldn't internalize for years.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a creative SEO-based anti-piracy tactic for his Bingo Card Creator software. He proposes 'Google bombing' piracy-related search terms by linking them to a funny YouTube video instead of actual cracks. He encourages other shareware authors to do the same for their software, creating a collective defense against software pirates. He also notes that crackers eventually updated their keygen for his new version, but treats it as a minor nuisance he can quickly counter.
key_points: Software piracy can be combated through SEO tactics rather than just technical protection — flooding search results for crack-related terms with decoy links
Google bombs work because piracy sites rarely get trusted inbound links, making it easy for legitimate blogs to outrank them
Download sites can help by including serial/keygen keywords naturally on their pages, further burying actual piracy results
Technical protection like breaking keygens is a cat-and-mouse game, but SEO manipulation provides a more durable defense
money_quotes: If you are a shareware author, an uISV, or if you just want to take a stand against software piracy, you can do so in thirty seconds.
Blam, instant Google bomb — or should we say, Google Cannon.

Just a few people linking a small program will get that as the #1 result, and a few more people will cause it to rank highly for any query including words like keygen, serial, etc, because our poor pirate friends don't typically get trusted links from anybody.

It seems like that cracker group finally got around to realizing that their old keygen didn't work anymore (the comments I saw were priceless — unrepeatable, but priceless), so they went ahead and cracked version 1.04.
I think every download site should implement similar language, because doing so would make it impossible to find the needle in the haystack.
posts.key_insight: SEO can be weaponized as an anti-piracy tool by flooding crack-related search terms with legitimate links, exploiting the fact that piracy sites have weak link authority.

posts.summary: This post is a proof-of-concept for a 'customer-focused blog' strategy, demonstrating how to create useful content that naturally incorporates a product recommendation. McKenzie teaches a Japanese kanji (任, meaning 'to entrust' or 'leave up to someone') while plugging ReadWrite Kanji software. The post includes readings, JLPT test vocabulary, and trivia connecting the kanji to Nintendo's name etymology. An editor's note at the end breaks the fourth wall to explain the marketing techniques being demonstrated, including calls to action.
key_points: The post is an explicit demonstration of content marketing: creating genuinely useful content that naturally incorporates a product recommendation
The kanji 任 connects to Nintendo's name (任天堂), meaning 'the house of leaving one's fortune to heaven,' derived from a poetic expression about gambling on fate
McKenzie transparently discloses his motivations, noting the post is a proof-of-concept for a blog series on customer-focused blogging
The editor's note reveals the meta-layer: the call to action at the end is itself a teaching example of effective blog marketing techniques
money_quotes: This association of submission before heaven and gambling probably had something to do with the naming of a certain playing card company 任天堂, 'the house of leaving one's fortune to heaven', which is probably better known as the company which went on to make two Italian plumbers household names in every nation on earth.
Editor's note: there won't be a kanji of the day tomorrow. Its an example of an effective call to action.
Everything which follows this disclaimer, including my representations as to my opinion of the quality of that software, is true.
posts.key_insight: Effective content marketing creates genuinely useful content where the product recommendation feels natural rather than forced, as demonstrated by embedding a software plug within a Japanese language lesson.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his strategy for passing the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT), arguing that mastering kanji through daily spaced-repetition practice is the most efficient path to passing. He breaks down the test's scoring structure to show that the listening section is easy enough to coast on, meaning candidates only need slightly better than 50% on the remaining sections. He recommends a specific flashcard software called ReadWrite Kanji for daily drilling, emphasizing that true mastery means instant recall without hesitation. The post is explicitly a proof-of-concept for his advice on writing customer-focused blog content that naturally incorporates product recommendations.
key_points: The JLPT listening section is disproportionately easy, so a strategic test-taker should focus effort on kanji mastery to cover the remaining points needed
True kanji mastery means instant, unhesitating recall — not vague recognition — and the test is specifically designed to punish fuzzy knowledge with near-miss answer choices
Spaced-repetition software beats physical flashcards by eliminating the overhead of manually sorting cards into mastery tiers
The post itself is a deliberate exercise in writing content marketing that provides genuine value while naturally recommending a product
money_quotes: Taking level 4 is a waste of your time and money, because nobody cares that you have a piece of paper that says you can order a beer.
The listening comprehension section is a joke.
People hate rote memorization, and they hate getting up every day and doing 20 minutes of kanji practice. But if you do it, and if you start early enough, you'll steamroll the JLPT.
No hesitation, no fudging, no peaking at the other side of your flashcard and saying 'Oh, yeah, I knew that': you look at that problem for 3 seconds and say BAM.
With only kana you're not even good enough to be called functionally illiterate.
Whoops, sorry, that was my freshman year Japanese textbooks. No, its actually $16.
posts.key_insight: Strategic test preparation means identifying the easiest scoring sections to coast on and concentrating effort on the highest-leverage skill — in this case, kanji mastery through daily spaced-repetition drilling.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that software businesses should write blogs aimed at their customers rather than at fellow developers or entrepreneurs. He explains the 'Pyramid of Participation' concept — most blog audiences are passive consumers, not creators — and advocates inverting it to convert passive readers into engaged customers. Using Declan Software's Japanese language learning tools as a concrete example, he demonstrates how a customer-focused blog provides genuinely useful content that builds trust, drives organic search traffic, and creates word-of-mouth referrals. The post emphasizes writing in your customers' language, having a clear goal for every post, and offering a mix of content types to sustain engagement.
key_points: Most software blogs make the mistake of writing for other developers and bloggers rather than for their actual paying customers, who are typically non-technical
The Pyramid of Participation shows that 1% of any community creates content, 10% contributes, and the rest passively consume — successful customer blogs invert this by activating passive readers
Providing genuinely useful free content builds trust and creates organic search engine traffic, since Google ranks blog content for long-tail queries surprisingly quickly
Writing in your customers' language rather than tech jargon improves both SEO (matching how customers actually search) and relatability
Every blog post should have a clear goal and call to action — whether generating a trial download, encouraging a return visit, or prompting readers to share with peers
money_quotes: Writing a blog about and for your customers, as opposed to about and for your business, is a great marketing investment.
My typical customer has no clue who Joel Spolsky is. She thinks '/.' is what happens when her kitty or 6 year old start banging away on the keyboard.

Blogging is traditionally for bloggers. And this is bad, because unless you're pitching products to the 53,651 members of the Silicon Valley echo chamber, not counting the Squidoo Lenses and other 'I can't believe its not blog' innovations.

Your customer likely does not care about the technology you are using, the difficulties of porting your code to .NET, the bug that nearly wiped out your version control system. Your customer likes to hear about themselves.
If you write stuff of use to your target audience, how-to's, things they are genuinely interested in hearing, they will beat a path to your door.
Do you know where your user base hangs out? Because if you don't, I'm worried for you.
The ads are content by then, because your blog is about things your customers like and find useful and your customers are obviously interested in what you have to sell.
posts.key_insight: A business blog should be written for and about your customers — not your industry peers — providing genuinely useful content in their language to build trust, drive organic search traffic, and convert passive readers into buyers.

posts.summary: McKenzie celebrates a particularly good sales day for his Bingo Card Creator software, where he earned more money sleeping on a Friday morning than he would teaching an English lesson. He reflects on the economics of his side business, noting that his monthly AdWords costs are covered and everything else is profit. He contrasts the agility of being a one-person company with the bureaucratic overhead of large corporations, where even a simple customer interaction required HR, legal, and VP-level approval.
key_points: Passive income from software sales can outperform hourly service work like English tutoring, especially when factoring in time investment
Running costs for a micro-ISV can be remarkably low — $90/month AdWords plus $5/month for e-junkie, with hosting prepaid
A one-person company can respond to customers in any language instantly, while large companies require layers of approval for the same action
The 'do a little work once and keep getting paid for it' model of software sales has fundamental economic advantages over trading time for money
money_quotes: This whole "do a little work once and keep getting paid for it for a while" passive income thing certainly has its benefits.

This is one of the lovely things about being a teeny-tiny slip of a company: you can outmaneuver the guys who have sixteen levels of managers to report to before anything as significant as a pleasantry can be exchanged.
I earned rather more than $80 for… sleeping on a Friday morning, and firing off two emails when I got up.
Even great companies, and I am convinced that employer was and is a great company, have their hangups.
Everything I made for the rest of the month would be gravy, since I don't have any capital purchases planned.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental advantage of a one-person software business is that it decouples income from hours worked and eliminates the bureaucratic overhead that prevents larger companies from being responsive.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on his participation in a Bits Du Jour promotional deal, where Bingo Card Creator was offered at a 40% discount ($14.95 instead of $24.95). He praises the promotional copy written by Ellen, noting how she expanded his target market beyond teachers to parents, daycare providers, and corporate event coordinators. He reflects on the economics of time-limited promotions and smart market segmentation, noting that some use cases become viable at a lower price point. He expresses surprise at an early sale at 2 AM and anticipates the promotion will outperform his conservative expectation of two sales.
key_points: Time-limited discount promotions can expand your addressable market by making the product viable for use cases that don't justify the full price

Letting someone else write your promotional copy can reveal market segments you hadn't considered — Ellen identified parents, daycare providers, and corporate coordinators as audiences beyond teachers
Small experiments with capped downside (30 minutes of effort) are always worth trying, even if expectations are low
Customer purchase timing patterns reveal behavioral insights — teachers buy during lunch, after school, or after putting kids to bed
The promotional copy successfully reframes a niche educational tool as broadly useful through concrete, relatable scenarios
money_quotes: I'm always willing to take total losses on 30 minute experiments.

She originally started with what I had on my website as a base, then made a slight addition at my suggestion, and then went on to 'embrace and extend' my market to places where I hadn't even considered it would be useful.
It might not have been useful at $24.95, but was swingable at $14.95 — yay for smart market segmentation.

Teachers generally buy on their lunchbreak, shortly after school ends when planning lessons for the next day, or between the hours of 9 and 11 pm at night which I think is shorthand for 'after I have put the kids to sleep'.
Who orders Bingo Card Creator at 2 AM in the morning local time? Apparently, someone who saw the 40% discount and just said 'I gotta have it!'
posts.key_insight: Low-risk promotional experiments can reveal untapped market segments, especially when a lower price point makes the product viable for use cases that wouldn't justify the full price.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports the results of running a Bits du Jour promotional deal for Bingo Card Creator, getting exactly the 2 discounted sales he expected from the promotion. He also had a record 5 regular-price sales the same day, suspecting some customers may have downloaded from Bits du Jour but purchased through the app without using the discount. He debates whether to credit those customers with the promotional price and notes the promotion drove about 60 visits total.
key_points: The Bits du Jour promotion yielded 2 sales at the discounted price, matching expectations for a niche product
5 additional full-price sales on the same day suggest some promotional traffic converted without using the discount
Attribution challenges arise when customers enter through one channel but convert through another
About 60 visits from Bits du Jour indicates the platform has meaningful traffic for less niche products
money_quotes: End result of the Bits du Jour promotion: 2 sales, which was exactly what I had been expecting.
Ahh well, I am a wee bit niche, after all.
I also had 5 sales from "regular" customers on the same day, which is an all time record.
For those of you who sell a somewhat less niche product, Bits du Jour could potentially make you a nice bit of money.
posts.key_insight: Promotional platforms can drive sales even for niche products, but attribution gaps between discovery channel and purchase channel can obscure the true impact of a promotion.

posts.summary: McKenzie praises e-junkie's customer service after experiencing email delivery issues with his registration codes. He describes a technical problem where customers couldn't receive registration emails, and how he faced the prospect of writing complex Perl code to work around it. After requesting a feature to pass registration codes as URL parameters, e-junkie not only responded quickly but implemented the feature within 24 hours, apologizing for the 'delay.' The post serves as a testimonial for responsive vendor support.
key_points: Email delivery failures for registration codes created a real business problem requiring a technical workaround
The proposed Perl workaround involved race conditions and timing issues that made it fragile and complex
A simple feature request (passing one additional URL parameter) eliminated the need for complex server-side code entirely
e-junkie implemented a custom feature request within 24 hours for a $5/month customer, demonstrating exceptional service
money_quotes: Aside from this involving evil Perl code (is there any other kind?), there were some potentially nasty timing issues involved.
Pop quiz: what happens if the customer arrives before I have the request from e-junkie to generate the registration code?
If they did that, fixing this problem would be a matter of writing ten lines of Javascript.

I definately wasn't expecting the next email to say 'We were going to mail you back when this was implemented, but it turns out our engineering team will require 24 hours to get to it. Sorry for the delay.'
So there you have it: e-junkie, the best $5 per month I ever spent.
posts.key_insight: Exceptional vendor responsiveness — implementing a custom feature request in 24 hours for a $5/month customer — can turn a minor service into a passionate advocate's recommendation.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a recurring pattern of customers not receiving their automated registration key emails after purchase, leading to support issues and even a double purchase. After three incidents in quick succession — a basic support request, a bounced email from a school's spam filter, and a customer who repurchased thinking their first transaction failed — he decides to display registration keys directly on the website rather than relying solely on email delivery.
key_points: Email delivery of registration keys is unreliable, especially to institutional addresses with aggressive spam filters
A customer purchasing twice likely signals a delivery failure, not enthusiasm — and ignoring it risks chargebacks
When a customer accidentally double-purchases, proactively crediting them builds loyalty worth far more than the refund amount
Three similar incidents constitute a pattern requiring a systemic fix, not individual support responses
Displaying registration keys inline on the website eliminates dependency on email delivery
money_quotes: 505 — 'This message will be archived and *may* be presented to the recipient', surely one of the least informative error messages of all time.
Every additional copy of my software sold is free money.

A customer who has already demonstrated their willingness to purchase *two* copies from you, on different days no less, has a huge likelihood to purchase a third, which will pay for that $6 discount 3 times over.
The jawdroppingly excellent customer service you've just provided them makes them feel all warm and fuzzy inside and likely to drive a fourth, fifth, seventy-second purchase along your way.
I want to rely less on the automated email reaching my customers, which means displaying their registration key inline in my website.
This is going to actually take some web programming, which I hate with a burning passion (Perl still gives me hives).
posts.key_insight: When the same customer problem recurs three times, stop treating it as individual support incidents and fix the system — the cost of a small engineering improvement is far less than the compounding cost of lost customers and chargebacks.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that Google AdWords unexpectedly doubled minimum bid prices on many of his keywords, destroying the profitability he had carefully optimized over weeks. After finally achieving a profitable cost-per-acquisition of 30 cents per download, his CPA crept back up to 37 cents due to Google's quality penalty raising minimum bids from 7-9 cents to 15 cents. He finds this particularly frustrating because some of the penalized keywords had strong click-through rates and highly relevant landing pages. He suspects either large portfolio bidders like eBay raised prices across the board, or Google applied a low-quality penalty incorrectly.
key_points: Google silently raised minimum bids on half his vocabulary campaign keywords from 7-9 cents to 15 cents, erasing weeks of optimization work
Even keywords with 6% CTR and highly relevant, hand-crafted landing pages were hit with the increased minimum bids
His only AdWords competition comes from companies bidding on massive keyword portfolios numbering in the millions, not direct competitors
The new CPC rates may make it mathematically impossible to run the campaign profitably
money_quotes: After finally achieving 30 cents per download (where AdWords is actually profitable) and keeping it there for a week my CPA creeped up again to 37 cents.
It turns out that Google turned off half of my words in the vocabulary campaign, requiring a minimum bid of 15 cents for what used to cost 7-9.

I got my cost doubled on "Dolch sight word bingo", of all things, which is a) 6% CTR on a bad day and b) my landing page for that is human-generated and about as responsive to the query as you can possibly get.
My only competition for AdWords is people bidding on gigantic portfolios, ranging probably in the millions of search strings.
I've sunk dozens of hours into optimizing that campaign to get it where it is, and I don't think its even mathematically possible to make it have positive returns at the new-and-improved CPC rate.
posts.key_insight: Platform dependency creates existential risk for small businesses when algorithmic pricing changes can instantly destroy carefully optimized unit economics with no recourse.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his Mac version is performing surprisingly well, accounting for about 10% of his Windows user base despite minimal promotion. He discovers this through OS tracking he added to update requests. The Mac version isn't listed on major download sites yet, and the download process actively discourages Mac users by defaulting to the Windows installer. Despite these obstacles, Mac adoption significantly outpaces the 2% Mac share of his site visitors, prompting him to improve the Mac download experience with JavaScript-based OS detection.
key_points: Simple OS tracking in update requests revealed Mac users were 10% of Windows users despite only 2% of site visitors being on Macs
The Mac version was underperforming its potential due to a download flow that defaulted to Windows and required Mac users to find a secondary link
McKenzie's instinct to simplify by having one download button was actually costing him conversions from Mac users
Small instrumentation changes can reveal surprising user behavior that contradicts assumptions
money_quotes: That would put my Mac popularity at about 10% of my windows one, which is pretty phenomenal given that a) my Mac version isn't on the major download sites yet b) I make it almost painfully difficult to get the Mac version and c) 2% of my visitors use Macs.
I think I might have to revisit that whole "make getting the Mac version painfully difficult" thing.
I figure my users are not exactly savvy and having multiple download buttons confuses more users than the Mac downloads are worth.
While I still think that, inconveniencing 10% of my user base doesn't sit well with me, so I'm going to add a bit of Javascript to that download page and redirect Mac users to the correct package.
There is a note in bold telling Mac users "Sorry guys, that download is useless to you, click here".
posts.key_insight: Measuring actual user behavior through simple instrumentation can reveal that design assumptions are actively hurting a significant segment of your customer base.

posts.summary: McKenzie warns fellow AdWords advertisers about Google's aggressive pricing response to click-through rate dips. He discovered that even his best-performing keyword had its cost quadrupled after briefly dipping below 1% CTR due to random fluctuations. He advises proactively scrubbing keywords that fall under the 1% CTR threshold, noting that paying inflated costs to rehabilitate a keyword offers no guarantee the minimum bid will decrease.
key_points: Google quadruples keyword costs when CTR dips below 1%, even temporarily
Random statistical fluctuations in CTR can trigger permanent cost increases
Paying the inflated price to rehabilitate a keyword has no guaranteed payoff
Proactive scrubbing of underperforming keywords is essential AdWords hygiene
money_quotes: Go through your lists, look for words which are under 1% CTR, and scrub.
Google responded by quadrupling the cost of it.
Even if I pay that for weeks to rehabilitate the keyword there is no guarantee that the minimum bid will ever go down.
posts.key_insight: Google's AdWords algorithm punishes even temporary CTR dips with steep, potentially permanent cost increases, making proactive keyword pruning essential for small advertisers.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a mid-month financial update for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $332 in net sales and $290 in profit — a dramatic improvement over his previous months of $25 and -$2. He tracks a promising sales progression of 1, 2, 6, 8, 16 sales per two-week interval since launch, while cautioning against assuming geometric growth will continue. He outlines plans to reach $600 in monthly sales through Mac distribution, Halloween-themed SEO content, new advertising campaigns, and a CD version of the product. The post closes with a personal note about using the profits to pay off his student loan early and buy a Nintendo Wii.
key_points: Net sales reached $332 mid-month with only $42 in expenses, yielding nearly $290 in profit — orders of magnitude better than prior months
Sales have followed a roughly doubling pattern (1, 2, 6, 8, 16 per two-week interval) but he warns against extrapolating geometric growth
His growth strategy is multi-pronged: Mac distribution, seasonal SEO content (Halloween bingo), new ad campaigns, CD product launch, and purchase page optimization
Total business expenses remain remarkably low at under $50/month between hosting and AdWords
money_quotes: Well, that's certainly a sliiiiiight increase from my last two months ($25 and -$2, as I recall).
Unfortunately, I think the 'it will continue increasing geometrically!' assumption you might have from high school algebra is a tad aggressive.
I think I'm well below the maximum amount of sales I can get with reasonable effort.
I've got more promotions to try, more advertising campaigns to run, more tricks up my sleeve, more products to launch.
I'm getting my Halloween bingo page ready this weekend so it gets search engine rankings in advance of the actual day.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software businesses can achieve meaningful profit growth through disciplined, incremental marketing efforts even when starting from nearly zero revenue.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes setting up CD fulfillment for Bingo Card Creator after his first physical media sale. He found a third-party CD fulfillment service that handles burning, labeling, and shipping for $4.47 per disc, charging customers $5 for the option. He explains the practical economics of physical media sales for a micro-ISV, including his policy of eating the cost on returns rather than processing physical returns. The post illustrates his pragmatic approach to solving business problems with minimal effort and maximum customer goodwill.
key_points: Some customers prefer physical media because it feels 'real' and serves as a backup, even when digital delivery is available
Third-party CD fulfillment services eliminate the need for a solo developer to handle physical logistics like burning, labeling, and shipping
At $4.47 cost versus $5 price, the CD option is essentially break-even after accounting for time spent processing orders
For low-value physical items, absorbing the cost of returns rather than processing them is better economics and better customer service
money_quotes: A lot of people who aren't the most technologically-savvy in the world prefer to have physical media, as its a) 'real' in a way the download is not and b) proof against something going wrong on their computer.

Its certainly not worth sending me out to the video store to buy a CD or a stack of CDs, hand-write a license key on it, and then trudge it out to the post office, buy a padded mailer, and ship it abroad to the US, praying it doesn't get a 25% tariff or get dropped in the freaking ocean like the last CD I sent to America.

About ten minutes of research later I signed up with them, sent them a CD image over the Interweb, and now every time I get an order I just have to log into my account, fill out the order details, and hit 'Send'.
How do you handle a return of a CD? Answer: you don't.
There is no point even thinking about going to the post office for a five dollar item, and your customers will appreciate it immensely.
If a customer buys the CD and wants to use the 30-day guarantee, I will tell them to please dispose of the CD and I will take the loss ($4.47).
posts.key_insight: When selling low-cost physical goods, outsource fulfillment to eliminate logistics headaches and absorb return costs rather than process them — the customer goodwill is worth more than the few dollars lost.

posts.summary: A brief status update where McKenzie notes his blog has been quiet due to work and social obligations while his Bingo Card Creator business runs on autopilot.
