He lists near-term priorities including evaluating text link ads, getting listed on Tucows, diagnosing a sharp decline in MSN traffic, and fixing broken Google Analytics tracking code. He notes the Mac version of his software is accounting for half his sales, validating his earlier decision to port it.
key_points: Diversifying traffic sources matters: a 75% crash in MSN clicks was offset by Google gains, but concentration risk is real
The Mac port proved its value by generating 50% of weekly sales during a slow period
Fixing the payment/registration flow eliminated his top support issue entirely, showing how infrastructure improvements compound
Even tiny businesses require ongoing attention to analytics, marketing channels, and tracking code
money_quotes: The business has been more or less running itself.
75% decline from my largest source of clicks is worrisome, even if I did have a gain in Google that offset the decline.
Thank my lucky stars for having the foresight to create the Mac version. 50% of my (limp) sales this week.
Apparently I broke Google's tracking code *again*.
My #1 support issue has vanished totally.
posts.key_insight: A solo software business can largely run itself once the fundamentals are in place, but requires constant small adjustments to traffic sources, analytics, and distribution channels to stay healthy.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experiment purchasing text links from Text Link Ads as a marketing channel for his software business. After spending $35 on two links, he found the service's inventory poor for his education niche, with links placed on irrelevant or low-quality sites. The service obscures which sites you're buying from to prevent disintermediation, leading to mismatched placements. He concludes the experiment was a loss and he won't continue, though he acknowledges the service might work better in other niches like tech accessories.
key_points: Text Link Ads charges $15-20/month per link, splitting revenue 50/50 with site owners, but deliberately obscures site identity to prevent direct deals
The education/teacher niche inventory was extremely poor — irrelevant sites, spam-heavy pages, and links buried at the bottom competing with unrelated advertisers
McKenzie's AdWords CPA had recovered to $0.40, which he describes as survivable but not very profitable
The service might work better in tech-savvy niches where inventory quality is higher, such as Mac fan forums with large active communities
money_quotes: I'm almost leery to mention this because the company approaches the seedier side of the SEO industry.
Text Link Ads makes it less than transparent to figure out exactly who you're buying the link from, so that you can't contact that person off the site and cut Text Link Ads out of the deal.
I have a realistic expectation of the likelihood of that link generating one marginal sale in a month.
Most of the pages are chock-full of links already.
Well, you win some, you lose some. I'm probably not going to be continuing this experiment.
posts.key_insight: Paid link-buying services suffer from adverse selection — the inventory available in niche markets tends to be low-quality sites that exist to profit from ads rather than serve real audiences, making them poor marketing channels.

posts.summary: McKenzie reflects on a two-week lull in his Bingo Card Creator business, during which sales slowed to 7 units—a number that would have thrilled him earlier but now feels disappointing given recent momentum. He outlines four tactical plans to get back on track: resolving a payment dispute with Tucows, renegotiating text link advertising, creating seasonal Halloween content with supporting AdWords campaigns, and overhauling his underperforming advertising spend. The post captures the psychological recalibration that happens as a small business owner's expectations shift upward with early success.
key_points: Hedonic adaptation in business: 7 sales in two weeks went from exciting to disappointing as his baseline shifted after a strong September
Seasonal content strategy—creating Halloween bingo pages a month early to build search engine placement before peak demand
His $20/week AdWords spend is generating no sales, representing pure waste that needs systematic overhaul
Lesson learned on payment methods: paying Tucows via debit card removed the chargeback leverage a credit card would have provided
money_quotes: 7 in the last two weeks, which oddly enough would have made me ecstatic 2 months ago but after my first two weeks of September is a wee bit disappointing.
I am now severely regretting both paying them and paying them via debit card, because if I had paid them with a credit card I could at least charge them back.
I'm currently spending $20 a week and getting approximately nothing from it in terms of sales, and thats just throwing away money at present.
I'm decently positive that I made it work once and can make it work again, Google and their penalty algorithms be darned.
If I make the page about a month in advance and then link it from this blog with descriptive anchor text, it will get premium placement on the search results for at least MSN.
posts.key_insight: Rising expectations are a natural byproduct of early business success, and the tactical response to a sales lull is to double down on seasonal content, advertising optimization, and resolving operational friction rather than panic.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his September 2006 business stats for Bingo Card Creator, his first software product. The month saw 22 sales totaling $533.90 gross, with $313.94 in profit after expenses. The first half of the month performed well but the second half slowed down, partly due to Google AdWords issues. He notes that Text Link Ads were not profitable while Tucows submission proved valuable for both its PageRank link and download traffic.
key_points: 22 sales with $533.90 gross revenue and $313.94 profit for September 2006, showing a solo software business can be modestly profitable early on
Google AdWords disruptions in the second half of the month significantly impacted performance, highlighting dependence on paid acquisition
Text Link Ads were deemed unprofitable while Tucows provided outsized value through both SEO (PR9 link) and direct download traffic
Transparent accounting with $192.02 in total expenses broken down across hosting, payment processing, advertising, and distribution channels
money_quotes: My first two weeks went like gangbusters, my last two weeks have been pretty limp.
Google AdWords has thrown me for a loop and I haven't had the hours in the day to spend to recover from it yet.
Text Link Ads were not a very profitable expenditure.
Tucows, on the other hand, a direct PR9 link plus significant download traffic means a very happy uISV.
I take no responsbility for the accuracy of these figures until it comes time to chat to the IRS and zemusho at the end of the year.
posts.key_insight: Even a modest solo software business can turn a profit in its early months, but heavy reliance on a single paid acquisition channel like Google AdWords creates fragility when that channel hiccups.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experience resolving a three-week delay with Tucows after paying for an expedited software submission. After contacting their webmaster on a fellow developer's recommendation, he received excellent customer service including an apology, a refund offer, and a $100 credit option. He chose the credit, recognizing it as a smart retention move, and plans to share his support letter as a template for effective customer communication.
key_points: Networking with other independent software vendors provided a practical shortcut to resolving a customer service issue
Tucows' response exemplified good customer service recovery: apology, refund option, and a credit that preserved the business relationship
The $100 credit vs. $52 refund choice was a smart retention tactic that McKenzie recognized and rewarded by staying invested
How you phrase a customer support request materially affects the outcome you get
money_quotes: It included an apology, an offer to give me a total refund, a choice between the refund and a $100 credit against further services (smart move: it keeps me invested in my business relationship with them — I took it), and listing the software immediately.
Even business owners are customers roughly half the time, right?
posts.key_insight: Effective customer service recovery — apology, options, and a retention-smart credit offer — can turn a frustrated customer into an advocate.

posts.summary: McKenzie asks his blog readers for help debugging his Mac version of Bingo Card Creator after receiving bug reports from VersionTracker.com users. Despite having some successful Mac users, two users report the app dies on startup due to Java being unable to find the main class. He suspects an issue with the Mac-specific pinfo.list file but cannot reproduce or diagnose it since he has no Mac experience. The post is a straightforward request for community testing assistance.
key_points: VersionTracker.com listing generated multiple sales within 12 hours, demonstrating the value of software distribution sites for indie developers
Cross-platform Java deployment creates platform-specific debugging challenges, especially when the developer lacks access to the target platform
McKenzie leverages his blog audience as a free QA resource, a practical tactic for solo software developers with limited hardware access
money_quotes: I've already got multiple sales (!) and also multiple bug reports that it doesn't work on various versions of Mac OS X.
These numbers might as well be Greek to me since I have no experience using Macs.
This is behavior I am unfortunately distressingly familiar with on Windows, and it is generally the result of a faulty manifest file.
posts.key_insight: Solo software developers can compensate for limited resources by leveraging their blog audience for cross-platform QA testing they cannot do themselves.

posts.summary: McKenzie reflects on a mistake he made by hosting his blog on a separate domain from his business site, Bingo Card Creator. Despite consistently advising others to put their blog on their main business domain to share PageRank, he failed to follow his own advice. The result: his blog achieved a higher PageRank (PR5) than his product site (PR4), meaning potential customers find his blog first and must click through to the product, losing traffic along the way.
key_points: Hosting a business blog on the same domain as your product shares PageRank and consolidates SEO authority
McKenzie's blog earned PR5 organically through valuable content while his product site only reached PR4 despite deliberate link-building efforts
The gap between PR4 and PR5 represents significant search traffic differences, especially in low-competition niches
Valuable content naturally attracts links from other blogs and aggregators, often outperforming deliberate SEO tactics
money_quotes: What can I say, I really wish that circa June, 2007 I had known enough about my own advice to take it.
The difference between PR4 and PR5 is a whole lot of searches, particularly for low-competition niches.
I now get an absolutely insane number of people who search for my very favorite search terms and come here first, then follow the link to my actual product site.
This is utterly insane not because of the magnitude, but because I know I must be losing a huge percentage of the potential traffic there.
Don't let it happen to you!
posts.key_insight: Host your business blog on your main product domain so that the SEO authority your content earns directly benefits your product pages rather than forcing visitors through an unnecessary redirect.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his blog was hit by a massive spam attack within seconds of bringing it back online, receiving over 200 spam comment submissions about gambling. Most of the spam bypassed his existing spam filter. Rather than implementing intrusive anti-spam measures like registration requirements, he opted for a simple keyword ban on three gambling-related terms.
key_points: The blog received 200+ spam comments within 45 seconds of coming back online, suggesting automated bot monitoring
The existing spam filter failed badly, letting 180 of 200+ spam comments through
McKenzie chose the least intrusive solution — banning three specific words — over requiring user registration
money_quotes: Within 45 seconds of bringing my blog back from the dead I got over 200 spam comment submissions, mostly to old posts.

To prevent me getting repetitive stress disorder from hitting the 'mark as spam' button and keep this blog easy to comment on without requiring registration or anything intrusive, I've banned the following three words from comments: poker, casino, and gambling.
posts.key_insight: When fighting spam, prefer the simplest targeted fix that preserves user experience over heavy-handed measures like mandatory registration.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his approach to investing his small but growing software business earnings after paying off his student loans. He explains his reasoning for choosing index funds over actively managed mutual funds, citing market efficiency and the poor odds of active managers beating the market. He diversifies away from his day job's exposure to the Japanese economy and tech sector by investing in S&P 500 index funds through ING and Sharebuilder. The post is a practical walkthrough of a young micro-ISV owner's first steps into investing, grounded in self-awareness about his limitations as a stock picker.
key_points: The stock market aggregates information so efficiently that individual stock picking is a losing game for amateurs, making index funds the rational choice
Actively managed mutual funds charge ~2% annually with only about a 25% chance of matching market returns, making them poor value compared to index funds
Smart diversification means investing away from your existing risk exposures — if your career depends on Japan and tech, invest in broad US indices
Even tiny amounts of investment income from a micro-ISV are worth investing thoughtfully rather than leaving in a low-interest checking account
Fee minimization matters enormously for small investors — both ING and Sharebuilder offered low-cost access to index investing
money_quotes: The market aggregates information with efficiency and scale which makes Google look like a single-celled organism.
Paying 2% of my investment every year to somebody who has about a 1 in 4 chance of matching the return of the market does not appeal to me.
I'm going to do the same thing with stock picking that I do with stock icons: outsource.
I am not terrible at investing in the way I am terrible at art. No, I am worse than terrible: I had a mock portfolio in 8th grade and managed to lose 15% of it in an up market in 6 weeks.
An index fund: pretty much a mathematical guarantee of being about a point below the market, as measured by whatever the tracked index is.
If those two countries are ever both down simultaneously then the world as I know it is pretty much screwed anyhow.
posts.key_insight: For small software entrepreneurs, the rational investment strategy is low-cost index funds that diversify away from your existing career risks, not stock picking or expensive active management.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes the accounting system he set up for his micro-ISV (Bingo Card Creator) to simplify tax filing. He got a dedicated credit card exclusively for business expenses, which consolidates all spending into one statement and makes Schedule C filing trivial. He explains the cost of forgetting deductions ('idiot tax') and how the credit card smooths cash flow between his Japan-based day job income and US-denominated business expenses. He also maintains simple paper and digital files printed monthly to reconstruct income and expenses in minutes.
key_points: A dedicated business credit card consolidates all expenses into one statement, making tax filing straightforward
Every forgotten business deduction costs you your marginal tax rate on that amount — a hidden 'idiot tax' for poor record-keeping
Credit cards smooth cash flow timing mismatches between irregular income and fixed business expenses
Simple monthly paper and digital records (sales tracking, PayPal statements, credit card statements) make reconstruction trivial
Living abroad with accounts in multiple countries and currencies makes clean expense separation even more critical
money_quotes: Its crucial that you be able to account for every dime you spent on your business, so that you can properly claim every dime worth of business expenses.
Thats $7.50 worth of idiot tax for forgetting a single transaction. Don't pay the idiot tax, keep copious records in an easy-to-access place!
I will seriously be reading my own blog when filling out my Schedule C, thats how bad it is to reconcile my credit card statements.
With everything on one card, all you have to do is pull your statements online and sum. This ensures that you don't miss a single expense.
That allows me to reconstruct income and expenses in about 3 minutes per month.
posts.key_insight: The simplest accounting system for a solo software business is a dedicated credit card for all expenses plus monthly printed statements — minimizing the 'idiot tax' of forgotten deductions.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares lessons from selling low-cost software ($24.95) to non-technical users who don't typically buy software — people whose computer use is limited to email, documents, and eBay. He argues that visual polish matters far more than features for this audience, that personal accessibility and responsive customer support are essential for converting hesitant buyers, and that the purchasing process must be streamlined to near-foolproof simplicity. The post challenges the common uISV assumption that the target market is other developers.
key_points: 80% of his customers don't identify as software buyers — they use computers for basic tasks and don't seek out new applications
Visual polish drives more positive customer response than any feature addition, even though no one explicitly asks for better aesthetics
Skittish non-technical customers often send a single support email as a trust test before purchasing — proving a real person is behind the product
The ordering pathway must be failure-proof: minimal options, no unnecessary fields, and every click should lead toward purchase
Selling to non-developers avoids the OSS competition and 'I could build that myself' mentality of developer customers
money_quotes: Roughly 80% of my customers do not buy software. I don't mean this in the sense that they are pirates. I mean this in the sense that their relationship to their computer is not primarily mediated through applications.
Do you know how many people ever mailed me saying 'Your program looks kind of drab'? Not a single one.

Some people say that if you're selling at my price point ($24.95) you cannot afford to offer personalized customer support. I say you can't afford not to: your skittish prospective customers seeking reassurance will run away.
My theory on this is that people want to know that the program is being actively maintained by a real live human being, and they want to know that if there is a problem somebody will be on the case.
You'll be forever worrying about OSS coming along and eating your lunch, and your customers are in general loathe to spend money to accomplish something they think they can just whip up themselves.
Practically anything you click within the main body of the page will take you directly to the ordering page.
posts.key_insight: Selling software to non-technical users requires prioritizing visual polish, personal accessibility, and a frictionless purchase path over features — because these customers are buying trust and reassurance, not capabilities.

posts.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically recommends Direct Access, a productivity application he uses constantly throughout his workday. He links to a previous glowing review and notes a 50% off sale on Bits Du Jour. He acknowledges his lack of objectivity about the product and discloses that the plug is unpaid.
key_points: McKenzie uses Direct Access shortcuts every 30 seconds to 5 minutes while working, making it central to his workflow
He openly admits his recommendation lacks objectivity, comparing his affection for the software to his love of chocolate
He proactively discloses that he is not paid for product endorsements on his blog
money_quotes: Perhaps review is the wrong word to use: that implies objectivity, and I've got none here: I love the program like I love chocolate.
As usual, when I plug something here I'm not paid to do it.
posts.key_insight: Authentic, unpaid endorsements built on genuine daily use carry more weight than formal reviews, and transparent disclosure of bias paradoxically increases credibility.

posts.summary: McKenzie briefly explains his absence from blogging, citing intense pressure at his day job where deliverables are severely behind schedule. Final acceptance testing was supposed to start in August but the team is still working on completing features in mid-October. His side business (uISV) activity has been reduced to answering a few emails and transferring PayPal earnings. He promises to post his business stats for the first half of October soon.
key_points: Day job project is significantly behind schedule, with acceptance testing delayed by nearly two months
Side business runs on minimal maintenance during crunch periods — just email responses and bank transfers
Despite neglect, the uISV continues generating revenue passively, demonstrating the value of software products over services
money_quotes: final acceptance testing was supposed to start on, oh, August 31st? Yep, you could say we're under a weeeee bit of pressure to get things finished.
my time on my uISV consists of responding to an email or three a week, and the odd transfer from Paypal to my bank account.
posts.key_insight: A well-built software product continues generating revenue even when its creator can only spare minimal attention, underscoring the passive income potential of product businesses.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides practical advice on dealing with email spam, recommending SpamAssassin for those who administer their own email servers and Popfile for everyone else. He explains how Bayesian statistical filtering works in practice, noting both tools achieve over 99% accuracy when properly trained. He walks through Popfile's setup as a local email proxy that classifies messages into customizable buckets. He strongly warns against challenge/response systems, arguing they create more problems than they solve and alienate customers.
key_points: The spam filtering decision is simple: SpamAssassin for server administrators, Popfile for everyone else, both achieving 99%+ accuracy
Popfile's bucket system goes beyond spam/ham classification, enabling automatic sorting of business emails, family messages, mailing lists, and auto-generated notifications
The biggest risk with any anti-spam system is false positives on legitimate emails, which can be mitigated with manual magnets for critical contacts
Challenge/response systems should never be used by businesses because end-users hate them and the problems scale with adoption
money_quotes: If you are comfortable administering your own email server (and already do), use SpamAssassin. If you are not, use Popfile.
There, you're done and didn't even have to pay me my company's consulting rate.
The main problem with ANY anti-spam system is dropping legitimate emails.
After you classify a shockingly small number of emails, POPfile gets the hang of things very quickly.
DO NOT USE THESE. The potential problems are numerous and they get even more numerous the more people who use them.
Since if you're reading this you are a business, you should know that end-users HATE THEM with a burning passion.
posts.key_insight: Effective spam filtering is a solved problem with freely available Bayesian tools, and the real risk is not spam getting through but legitimate messages being lost.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his mid-October sales statistics for Bingo Card Creator, his micro-ISV product. He reports 13 units sold with one return, generating $311.59 in net sales against $105.02 in expenses for a net profit of $206.57. He notes business is stronger than the previous month and sets a target of 25-30 copies and $500 profit for the full month. A planned Halloween promotion has been delayed due to his day job commitments.
key_points: Mid-month net profit of $206.57 puts him on track to exceed previous months' performance
Expenses remain low at ~$105/month split between hosting, e-commerce platform, and AdWords
The tension between day job and side business is limiting his ability to execute marketing promotions
He is tracking toward 25-30 unit sales for the month, showing steady growth in the micro-ISV
money_quotes: Business this month is going substantially stronger than last month.
The Halloween promotion I was hoping to do has been mostly eclipsed by my work at work, but we'll see if I can't get something figured out this weekend.
posts.key_insight: Even with minimal marketing effort and day-job constraints, a well-positioned micro-ISV can generate consistent and growing monthly profit from a simple software product.

posts.summary: McKenzie asks for help with a Perl programming problem at his day job. He's running an HTTP server using Perl's HTTP::Daemon module and needs to extract uploaded text files from HTTP POST requests on the server side. Despite weeks of effort, searching CPAN modules, and experimentation, he hasn't found a clean solution. The issue is blocking other deliverables since the server is 98% complete but the remaining 2% is critical for testing.
key_points: He's using Perl's HTTP::Daemon to run an HTTP server that receives uploads from a rich client application
The client-side file upload works fine but server-side extraction of the uploaded file from the HTTP request is the blocker
The problem has persisted for weeks and is holding up other deliverables and client testing
He takes a parting shot at Perl's documentation quality
money_quotes: My experiments have been a bunch of failures, I can't seem to find any CPAN Module which will do this cleanly.
That last pesky 2% is critical to testing the client application, sadly.
Perl's documentation is, well… up to its usual standard of lucidity.
posts.key_insight: Even a technically capable developer can get stuck for weeks on seemingly simple integration problems when documentation is poor and the ecosystem doesn't offer clean abstractions.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes expanding Bingo Card Creator to serve international customers by adding native currency pricing. He explains his approach to handling VAT (letting customers choose between eSellerate which collects it and PayPal which doesn't), his pricing strategy of converting USD amounts and rounding to convenient numbers, and his decision to absorb currency conversion fees as a small customer-friendly gesture. He also touches on tax compliance across multiple jurisdictions and the challenges of potentially incorporating in Japan.
key_points: Adding native currency pricing removes friction for international customers and eliminates bank currency exchange fees they'd otherwise pay
Small gestures like absorbing currency conversion costs (pennies per sale) can disproportionately build customer goodwill
VAT compliance for a small international software seller is a pragmatic choice: offer one payment method that handles it and one that doesn't
Psychological pricing conventions (ending in .95) apply across currencies, not just USD
money_quotes: I hate currency exchange fees with a burning passion in my soul, having been burned by them plenty.
VAT, for value added tax, is a freaking gigantic consumption-esque tax you have to worry about if you sell in Europe.

This is a matter for me of a few dimes worth of profit per sale, less than nothing really, but its a small thing you can do to really endear yourself to a fraction of your customers. I'm all about that.
My position on VAT is simple: I'm a small businessman, I have never set foot in Europe, and I do not want to deal with the hassle of collecting this tax.
Proof of 3 million yen worth of business capital… hmm, well, can I count all the money I spent on college as human capital? Because thats the only way I've had nearly $30,000 at any point in my life.
posts.key_insight: Absorbing small costs like currency conversion fees is a trivially cheap way to reduce friction and build goodwill with international customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes using Unix command-line tools for a practical web maintenance task: replacing HTML snippets across multiple static pages. He walks through progressively simpler approaches, starting with a multi-file gawk script, then showing how sed accomplishes the same thing in a single line. The post is a casual tutorial aimed at developers who may not be familiar with Unix text processing utilities. He also takes a lighthearted jab at Perl's notoriously unreadable syntax.
key_points: Static HTML sites without templating create painful maintenance burdens when navigation elements need updating across many pages
Sed provides a simpler, less error-prone alternative to gawk for straightforward find-and-replace operations across files
Using the right tool for the job matters — gawk is powerful but overkill for simple substitutions
McKenzie was running Cygwin on Windows to get Unix tools, preferring Windows for iTunes and games over Linux
money_quotes: I use Cygwin to have them available on my Windows box, because while Linux is a lovely operating system in many ways I rather enjoy having iTunes and games available.
But that is two scripts, and two scripts is too complicated!
This saves about half of your typing time versus doing it in gawk, and you can't accidentally bork the matching logic.
Of course, you could always write it in Perl. That would probably take a single line and look like your cat had just tap danced across your keyboard.
But if you want a simple, intuitive way to do fairly powerful data processing, you'll see sed is your man… err, I mean, you should see man sed.
posts.key_insight: When doing simple text substitutions across files, sed is the right tool — simpler and less error-prone than more powerful alternatives like gawk or Perl.

posts.summary: McKenzie reflects on whether he has too narrowly defined his target market for Bingo Card Creator around elementary school teachers. While the tight focus gives him deep understanding of his core customer, he's discovering unexpected use cases: corporate events, rehabilitation tracking, custom placemats, and even advertising for niche organizations. He considers creating alternate website presentations for non-teacher audiences while maintaining his primary educational focus.
key_points: A tightly defined target market enables deep customer empathy — knowing their schedule, comfort level, language, and motivations
Unexpected customer segments often self-select into products not designed for them, revealing latent demand
A product perceived as single-use by its creator may serve diverse purposes the creator never imagined
Market expansion doesn't require abandoning your core — neutral landing pages for secondary audiences can capture growth without diluting focus
money_quotes: I can make assumptions, like assuming that she (I can assume she is a she! Sort of a rarity in software development, I would think) is not as comfortable using her computer as I am using mine.
I know how she spends her day (she visits Bingo Card Creator during planning periods, lunch, or when lesson planning at home at about 9:00 pm), I know what her key problems are.
I sell a few copies to folks who are not teachers but nonetheless click through 100% teacher-oriented advertising copy to buy anyhow.

I had certainly been of the opinion BCC was a one-use program (it makes bingo cards, and does nothing else, by design), but I know of folks who are currently using it to make worksheets, track patient progress in rehabilitation, make customized placemats.
Synergize your best-of-breed team-building paradigm with Bingo Card Creator and achieve six sigmas of internal customer empowerment!
posts.key_insight: A narrowly defined target market gives you deep customer insight, but paying attention to the customers who buy despite not fitting your target reveals organic expansion opportunities.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that his trial executable was silently corrupted on his web server — a single byte changed — causing installation failures for potential customers. He only found out because a user wrote in after downloading it three times. A hex diff confirmed the corruption, and the NSIS installer's checksum caught the bad byte. He reflects on Murphy's Law and the importance of knowing about problems rather than letting them silently cost you customers.
key_points: A single byte of disk corruption on a web server silently broke his trial installer for up to a week before anyone reported it
He only discovered the issue because a particularly persistent user downloaded three times and then emailed about it
The NSIS installer's checksum verification caught the corruption and refused to install, which is the correct behavior
Most users who encountered the broken download likely just left without saying anything
money_quotes: If one file on your web server gets corrupted, it WILL be your trial executable, without fail.
One freaking byte on disk got corrupted at some point within the last week and I just found out about it today.
Well, better to know about it and be able to fix it than to not know about it, right?
posts.key_insight: Silent failures in your sales funnel — like a corrupted download — can cost you customers for days before anyone bothers to tell you, because most people just leave.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a tip about getting a free PageRank 8 backlink by submitting your website to Open Source Web Design's links page. He advocates for using free open-source web designs over hiring expensive web designers, citing his positive experience with Bingo Card Creator. The post was later updated to note the trick doesn't actually work as described. It reflects his early-stage SEO obsession and bootstrapper mindset of minimizing costs while maximizing marketing value.
key_points: Using open-source web designs from OSWD can eliminate a major expense without significantly compromising customer experience
Hiring a web designer is often not worth it unless you get functionality beyond aesthetics, below-market rates, or exceptional design
A high PageRank backlink from a PR8 site like OSWD has significant SEO value that a paid designer wouldn't provide
The tip turned out not to work as hoped, demonstrating the trial-and-error nature of early SEO tactics
money_quotes: One of the best decisions I made during the creation of Bingo Card Creator was cutting my web site designer out of the budget.
This is my advice to anyone who is wondering how to cut a major expense item out of their budget while not compromising the customer experience much.
Here is my trump card for the superiority of oswd over a designer: the designer won't give you a PR8 link to your site for nothing.
At the moment it appears to be close to the best kept SEO secret on the Internet.
Which part of 'free PR8 link' are you complaining about?
posts.key_insight: Bootstrappers should evaluate every business decision — including web design — not just on direct value but on secondary marketing benefits like SEO backlinks.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that his trial software installer has been corrupted again on GoDaddy's hosting, explaining a three-day sales drought. He notes this is a recurring problem and plans to contact GoDaddy to resolve the issue, as he's confident the file was intact shortly after upload.
key_points: Corrupted trial installers directly cause lost sales, with a three-day gap traced to this single issue
Unreliable hosting infrastructure can silently kill revenue for a software business
Monitoring your download files should be part of routine business operations
money_quotes: Hmm, I wonder why I've gone three days without a sale. I don't suppose it could be that my trial version got corrupted again.
I'm positive that that file was good an hour after I uploaded it.
posts.key_insight: A corrupted trial download is a silent revenue killer that can go undetected for days, making file integrity monitoring essential for software businesses.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his October 2006 stats for Bingo Card Creator, showing $500 net sales and ~$350 profit despite losing nearly a week of sales to hosting problems. He sold 22 downloads and 2 CDs with 3 refunds, narrowly missing his monthly goal by one unit. His AdWords costs are trending down thanks to Quality Score improvements, and his trial-to-purchase conversion rate sits at approximately 2.5%. The Mac version shows remarkably higher conversion rates than Windows, with about 1 in 2 Mac trial users converting versus 1 in 10 for Windows.
key_points: Hosting downtime directly costs sales — you can't sell software people can't reach
Mac trial users convert at roughly 5x the rate of Windows users (1 in 2 vs 1 in 10), suggesting a less price-sensitive or more committed audience
Google's Quality Score algorithm reduced his CPC from $0.15 to $0.10, pushing AdWords closer to positive ROI
Good customer support has measurable revenue impact — visitors who contact support generate roughly $8 each
Licensing terms obsession is misplaced: the license page is one of the least visited yet doesn't hurt conversions
money_quotes: You can't sell it if people can't get to it!
somebody was actually willing to pay $29.95 for the CD and $24.95 for the program on it, which shocked and amazed me!
Click on 'Purchase Now' from Mac Trial: $10.64 (i.e. about 1 in 2 converts)
This should be instructive for folks who obsess about their licensing terms — make them fair, make them short, and you can safely forget about them.
Good support pays.
Not bad for missing most of a week of sales in there.
posts.key_insight: Granular per-page revenue tracking reveals that Mac users convert at 5x the Windows rate and that customer support is a significant revenue driver, not just a cost center.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts a humorous experience pre-ordering a Nintendo Wii at a Japanese mall, presented as a dialogue between himself and a store manager. The exchange highlights the dramatic contrast between Japanese and American retail pre-order practices. In Japan, the process was frictionless: no deposit, no forced bundles, just a name and phone number. McKenzie plays up his disbelief as an American accustomed to exploitative pre-order practices like mandatory expensive bundles and full deposits. The post uses gentle comedy to illustrate cultural differences in consumer retail experiences.
key_points: Japanese retail pre-orders required no deposit and no bundling of unwanted products, unlike common American practices
The store manager proactively found McKenzie to inform him pre-orders had opened, demonstrating attentive Japanese customer service
The post implicitly critiques American retail practices like forced bundles and large deposits as exploitative rather than necessary
Living in rural Japan offered unexpectedly pleasant consumer experiences compared to major US markets
money_quotes: So this is the point where you ask me to sign up for a $500 bundle including $200 of games that you would not otherwise be able to sell and pay all of it as a deposit, right?
Oh, you silly Americans and your sense of humor.
I'm sorry sir, I hadn't realized you didn't know what "pre-order" meant.
Wait, you mean you let me pre-order just the console, without putting any money down, and you guarantee that it will be available on launch day?
You've got to be kidding me.
posts.key_insight: Japanese retail culture treats pre-orders as a simple reservation rather than an opportunity to extract maximum upfront payment from captive customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that despite neglecting active promotion of Bingo Card Creator due to time constraints, his organic search traffic has grown significantly to 100+ daily hits from Google. Sales are down about a third from October due to ceased promotion, but long-tail SEO is compensating as his site ages out of Google's sandbox. He argues that writing natural language content for target audiences is more effective than obsessing over keyword optimization. Recent SERP improvements have driven a spike in downloads that he hopes will convert to sales.
key_points: A software business can operate like a vending machine during busy periods, generating passive income with minimal maintenance
Long-tail search queries collectively drive far more traffic than optimizing for a single high-volume keyword
Writing genuinely useful content for your target market produces natural SEO gains over time without complex optimization
Google's sandbox effect means new sites need patience — incoming links and domain age gradually improve rankings across the board
money_quotes: I'm largely operating it like a vending machine — I collect the change at the end of the week and, once and a while, write out a two-paragraph email to somebody with a question.
5% of my traffic comes from that extremely common query with me being at number 9/10 on page one, and the rest of it comes from very uncommon queries which I'm an insta #1 on.

You can spend all the time in the world optimizing for a certain phrase and fight for every additional place in the rankings you climb. Or you can write stuff which is useful to your target market and rank naturally over time.
Not instant and not easy but not complicated, either.
My best guess is that as my website ages its way out of the sandbox and the incoming links folks put up age, I'm slowly gaining in the SERPs pretty much across the board.
posts.key_insight: Writing genuinely useful content for your target audience is a more sustainable SEO strategy than chasing individual keyword rankings, because long-tail queries collectively outperform any single optimized phrase.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains how to track outbound link clicks using Google Analytics' urchinTracker function, which was not natively supported at the time. By tagging offsite links with fake local paths, you can see which specific buttons and text elements drive users to complete purchases. He discovered that his customers were more motivated by reassuring text about payment security than by prominently placed buy buttons, leading him to plan repositioning that messaging earlier on the page.
key_points: Google Analytics can track outbound clicks by tagging links with urchinTracker and fake local paths, revealing which elements drive conversions
Button placement alone doesn't determine click-through — reassuring copy about payment security outperformed the most prominent buy button
Understanding customer psychology (hesitance to share credit cards with strangers) directly informs page layout and copy decisions
Tracking should extend beyond on-site behavior to include exits along the conversion pathway, especially to payment processors
money_quotes: Anybody who clicks to read my license terms has a 50-50 shot of buying from me.

Its NOT the top-corner button (who knew! I always expected folks would go for the easiest button to reach) but rather the part later on in the text where I describe Paypal as a safe, secure place to shop online.
My customers are a bit hesitant to give over their credit card number to somebody they've never met before.
I guess I understand why Paypal trumpets that so much in their marketing now!
Tagging your outbound links only takes a few seconds per link, and you can learn valuable stuff about your customers' behavior.
posts.key_insight: Tracking outbound link clicks reveals that customer trust signals in copy often outperform prominent button placement in driving conversions.

posts.summary: McKenzie evaluates Payloadz's new Google Checkout integration and concludes it's not worth switching from his current e-junkie/PayPal setup. He breaks down the cost comparison, showing Payloadz's tiered pricing is significantly more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month. He also weighs customer service quality, branding control, and payment provider trust among his target demographic. The post demonstrates his characteristic approach of evaluating business tools through multiple lenses: cost, customer experience, and market fit. He concludes by noting he'd reconsider if e-junkie itself added Google Checkout support.
key_points: Payloadz's tiered pricing ($15-$29/month based on sales volume) compares poorly to e-junkie's flat $5/month for equivalent functionality
E-junkie's exceptional customer service — making a custom API change within 48 hours — creates strong switching costs that competitors can't easily overcome
Brand control matters: Payloadz's non-customizable messaging and visible URLs undermine the professional image needed when selling to school teachers
PayPal has massive trust and penetration in his market (70% verified addresses) while Google Checkout has essentially 0% adoption among his customers
The right payment stack decision depends on your specific customer demographic, not just on which provider has the best technology or brand name
money_quotes: e-junkie made an API change for me within 48 hours just because I asked for it. That sets a pretty darn high bar for me in terms of what you would have to provide as a competitor.
Seth Godin, feel free to put that in your next book about making your customer feel special, because it sure as heck worked.
neither Payloadz nor e-junkie is a name which says 'I am an upstanding businessman, wouldn't you agree Mrs. Middle School Teacher'
Google has a market penetration of, and this is just a round number estimate, 0%.
I don't know that either says 'You can trust me with your money, Mrs. Doesn't Really Use The Computer Much Mother Of Two' yet.
getting whalloped for $29 for what I can get for $5 doesn't strike me as a great deal
posts.key_insight: Payment provider decisions should be evaluated holistically — pricing, customer service responsiveness, branding control, and your specific customers' trust relationships all matter more than headline features.

posts.summary: McKenzie returns to coding after a month away to fix a showstopper printing bug for a customer, which turned out to be much simpler than expected. The bug involved an incorrect assumption that teachers always print to their default printer. He reflects on how procrastinating the fix was a mistake since it took less than an hour. He plans to use an upcoming holiday to release version 1.05 with new features and timely holiday content.
key_points: A severe customer-facing bug caused by an edge case assumption took far less time to fix than anticipated
When working in painful codebases, bundle multiple improvements together to justify the effort of diving in
Timely content updates (holiday-themed bingo cards) aligned with seasonal demand can drive sales
Automated deployment tools like RoboSoft reduce the friction of releasing updates
money_quotes: my printing code is a big ugly hairy beast so when I go to fight with it I intend to come out with a freaking pile of treasure to justify my time
I had considered fixing it in 1.04 but thought it would take up too much time to do so. It ended up taking less than an hour.
posts.key_insight: Tasks we procrastinate on for fear of complexity often turn out to be much simpler than expected — just do them.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his blog and Bingo Card Creator business have unexpectedly generated four job inquiries from software ventures and micro-ISVs in a single week. He originally started the blog as a potential writing portfolio but is surprised that people are actively seeking him out based on it. He notes he's too busy to take on new projects until August, between his day job, BCC, and an upcoming job search. The post serves as an early, brief observation about how building in public and blogging about business can function as professional marketing.
key_points: Blogging about a small software business generated unsolicited job offers, demonstrating the professional networking power of writing publicly
McKenzie originally intended the blog as a writing portfolio but discovered it functioned as something far more valuable — active recruitment bait
Even a modest side project (Bingo Card Creator) combined with transparent writing about it can build professional credibility quickly
The post is an early example of McKenzie recognizing that building in public creates career optionality
money_quotes: I've gotten no less than four inquiries from people at taking up various positions at their various software ventures/uISVs/etc, and all of them have cited Bingo Card Creator (the business more than the software) or this blog as the reason for extending the offer.

When I started this blog I had vague dreams of perhaps eventually using it as a portfolio of sorts in case my next position required writing on a regular basis, but I hadn't ever thought people would actively seek me out over it.
I'm quite flattered, and almost scared (c.f. Groucho Marx, on his opinion of a political party which would have him as a member).
posts.key_insight: Writing publicly about running a small software business can generate more professional opportunities than the business itself, turning a blog into an unintentional but powerful career marketing tool.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV founders waste enormous time worrying about things that don't matter for their business success. He systematically dismantles common fears: license terms nobody reads, competitors stealing code, tax complications, and programming language choice. The core argument is that 90% of a software business is marketing and sales, not development, and founders should allocate their worry accordingly. He grounds each point in practical reality checks — comparing software licensing to buying hamburgers, and tax fears to eBay sellers. The post is aimed at solo developers who over-index on technical and legal concerns while under-investing in what actually drives revenue.
key_points: License terms are irrelevant for consumer software because no one reads them and enforcing them costs more than they're worth
Code protection and competitor fears are overblown because 90% of a software business happens outside the IDE, making code theft impractical
Tax fears are irrational for small operators — governments provide free resources and aren't trying to entrap micro-businesses
Programming language choice doesn't matter because development time is a tiny fraction of total business effort
The market in any viable niche is far larger than a solo operator can absorb, so competitor-watching is wasted energy
money_quotes: Here is the thing about babies: while most people absolutely love their baby they've got negative a billion desire to have anyone else's baby.
10% of this business, on the outside, happens in the IDE. The other 90% is where you make your money.
Do you know what the terms and conditions for your hamburgers are? No, because there are none.
In my entire professional career I have never seen debating compile successfully.
Why on earth would you go ahead and consult a lawyer about your license terms — might as well take $1,000 in small bills and burn it as incense.
Your customers lack these, which is why they want to pay you to make their problem go away.

After you have significant revenues, I think hiring a professional to do your taxes and/or your accounting is a good use of money, because the alternative is a poor use of time, and you ideally have a lot of the former but little of the latter.
posts.key_insight: Solo software founders squander their scarcest resource — time — on technical and legal anxieties that have near-zero impact on revenue, when they should be focused on marketing and selling to customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie highlights the success of Printer Friendly software after being featured on Bits du Jour, a deal-of-the-day site for software. He references his own more modest experience being featured on the same platform and acknowledges that products with broader mass-market appeal are better positioned to capitalize on such promotional opportunities. The post is a brief, generous congratulation to fellow micro-ISV entrepreneurs.
key_points: Deal-of-the-day sites like Bits du Jour can drive significant sales for independent software vendors
Mass-market appeal is a key factor in whether promotional features translate to blockbuster sales days
The Bits du Jour business model creates win-win relationships between the platform and software vendors
money_quotes: I could definately see the potential for the business model to work with an application with more mass-market appeal.
posts.key_insight: Promotional platforms like deal-of-the-day sites can be powerful sales channels, but their impact scales with how broadly appealing the featured product is.

posts.summary: McKenzie notes a Microsoft adCenter promotion offering $200 in ad credit for a $5 signup fee. He plans to take advantage of this effectively free money to test whether Microsoft's ad platform can deliver positive ROI for his software business. He contrasts this with his negative experience with Yahoo Search Marketing (YSM), which he considers inferior to Google AdWords in every dimension. His plan is to run a controlled $50/month experiment over four months.
key_points: Microsoft adCenter is offering $200 in ad credit for a $5 signup fee, making it essentially free to test
McKenzie categorically rejects Yahoo Search Marketing as inferior to AdWords in price, performance, features, reporting, and workflow
He plans a disciplined 4-month test at $50/month to evaluate ROI before committing long-term
The downside risk is trivial — the cost of a cup of cocoa — making it a rational experiment
money_quotes: I categorically refuse to use YSM again ("Like AdWords, except inferior in every way — price, performance, features, reporting, and workflow")
I think I'll give them 4 months at $50 a month or so to see if I can get positive ROI.
If not, its an experiment that costs me a cup of cocoa.
posts.key_insight: When promotional credits make the downside risk trivial, it's rational to run controlled experiments on new advertising platforms to discover potential ROI.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces his acceptance into the beta of Google's Website Optimizer, a split-testing product. He explains the concept of A/B testing for readers unfamiliar with it, using his own Bingo Card Creator site as an example. He notes his previous inability to do true split testing, relying instead on sequential experiments that were vulnerable to confounding variables. He plans to use the tool extensively during his Christmas break to optimize his site's conversion rate.
key_points: Google's Website Optimizer beta offered free split-testing capabilities to small software businesses that previously couldn't do rigorous A/B testing
Sequential before/after experiments are unreliable because external factors like traffic spikes from crackers can confound results
True randomized split testing eliminates confounding variables by showing variants simultaneously to randomly assigned visitors
Even small conversion improvements (like 2%) compound meaningfully when applied systematically across multiple page elements
money_quotes: I hate guessing and love data.
Split testing, for those that don't know the term, is taking a page you have and making an A version and a B version.
When I do an experiment it means hoping that my website is stable for a week other than the thing I'm experimenting on. That is a dubious proposition in the best of times.

A sudden surge of crackers can throw your conversion way off, which means you either get to throw out the data for the week and start over or just make a 'best guess' that the new version 'feels' like it converts better or worse than the old one.
posts.key_insight: True split testing removes guesswork from conversion optimization by eliminating the confounding variables that plague sequential before/after experiments.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his November 2006 stats for Bingo Card Creator, noting it was a disappointing month with only 20 sales and $349 net profit. He attributes the slowdown to the Thanksgiving holiday and predicts December will also be weak. A bright spot is that he began ranking for high-volume Google searches including 'bingo cards,' doubling his organic traffic. He provides detailed conversion rate breakdowns across traffic sources and analyzes his purchasing page funnel, noting an impressive 60%+ conversion rate once customers enter the checkout process.
key_points: November sales were weak at 20 units/$499 gross, attributed to the Thanksgiving holiday killing traffic after the 20th
Organic Google traffic doubled after ranking for high-volume head terms like 'bingo cards,' but this less-targeted traffic converted at lower rates (16%) than his usual long-tail queries
His Amigo newsletter advertising experiment failed due to lack of geotargeting, resulting in clicks from Spanish newsletters with 3-second average visits
Once customers entered the purchase funnel, conversion exceeded 60% — a rate he attributes to a frictionless checkout process and PayPal trust
Trial-to-purchase conversion of 1.25-1.5% disappointed him, with a 2% target as his benchmark
money_quotes: November was a bad month for sales, principally due to Thanksgiving I think (I got essentially nothing after the 20th.)
They don't play bingo in Spain. Average time spent on site by those visitors: 3 seconds.
I focus on CPA like a maniac
It is possible that if Google continues sending me weakly qualified visitors relative to my usual focused traffic that I may have to adjust to the reality and lower my expectations.
Conversion Given That Customer Has Entered The Funnel: In excess of 60%. Ahem, booyah.
There are some major merchants who would die for a 40% cart abandonment rate.
I like to think this is because I keep the process as easy as humanly possible (enter your info, click confirm, congratulations you're done)
posts.key_insight: High-volume search rankings can double traffic but dilute conversion rates, making the quality-versus-quantity tradeoff a constant tension for small software businesses optimizing on cost per acquisition.

posts.summary: A brief holiday announcement from Patrick McKenzie letting readers know he will be traveling from Japan to the United States for Christmas from December 19-30. He notes that blog posting and email responses will be minimal during this period. He wishes readers peace for the holiday season.
key_points: McKenzie makes an annual trip from Japan to the US for Christmas
He maintains availability for customer contact even during vacation
The post reveals his commitment to consistent communication with his audience even when stepping away
money_quotes: Peace be with you and your families this year and always.
posts.key_insight: Even a brief holiday notice reveals McKenzie's customer-first mindset, explicitly noting he remains available for customer contact during personal travel.

posts.summary: McKenzie celebrates selling 100 copies of Bingo Card Creator, netting about $1,500 which he used to pay off student loans. He lays out detailed business goals for 2007 covering product improvements, website optimization, and financial targets. He aims for $1,000/month in sales by April and $10,000 yearly, while planning to get legally established in both Japan and the US. He also discusses personal goals including finding a new day job in Nagoya and starting a more ambitious software project in consumer finance.
key_points: 100 copies sold at roughly $15 average represents meaningful side income that paid off student loans, validating the micro-ISV model
His product roadmap focuses on broadening the market beyond teachers to capture existing search traffic for general bingo content
He plans to eliminate the underperforming eSellerate sales channel, showing willingness to simplify rather than maintain unused complexity
Tax and legal compliance across Japan and the US becomes necessary as revenue approaches Japan's hobby income threshold of ~$8,300
Seasonal revenue volatility is a key concern, with summer months threatening zero income from a teaching-focused product
money_quotes: Aside from being great fun and a nice learning experience, thats about $1500 or so that I wouldn't have had otherwise.
Blog some stuff of use to folks, out of my totally charitable desire to collect free links improve the sum of human knowledge.
AdWords is still at $.40-$.45 a conversion and I want to fight that down to $.30 again.
If you are a US taxperson, you run a uISV as a hobby, and you don't have an IRA, for the love of all that is holy fix that this year.
The Japanese taxman 'Oh, thats just a hobby, don't worry about it' immunity phases out at 1 million yen.
A legal entity in the US for my software company will make launching a more ambitious product much easier.
I've got a thought for one at the moment but am wondering if the market is big enough (think 'consumer finance' and a ticket price, uh, a wee bit above $24.95).
posts.key_insight: A micro-ISV generating $1,500 from 100 sales validates the business model enough to set ambitious growth targets while forcing the founder to confront real-world complexities like international tax compliance and seasonal revenue dependence.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a personal finance tip about saving on brokerage commissions through ShareBuilder's discounted annual pricing. He explains his investment strategy of regularly investing profits from Bingo Card Creator into index fund ETFs, having recently paid off student loans. The post demonstrates his characteristic penny-pinching approach by calculating the long-term compound value of a $40 savings. He also analyzes why ShareBuilder offers the discount as a gift certificate rather than online, connecting it to customer acquisition strategy and platform stickiness.
key_points: Regular investing of software profits into index funds via low-cost brokerage, starting at $200/month and scaling to $600/month
The compound interest argument: $40 saved today becomes $1,256 over 40 years at 9% returns, making small optimizations worthwhile
Transaction costs should be capped at 2% of the investment amount, per Motley Fool guidance
ShareBuilder's discount strategy targets new customer acquisition rather than existing customer retention because their platform is naturally sticky
Software subscriptions and brokerage accounts have fundamentally different churn dynamics, which explains different pricing strategies
money_quotes: Not a stock, ick, I trust the market to routinely beat the bejeezus out of my picks.
I've been dumping $200 a month into them the last several months — thank you, bingo cards.

Why trouble myself with even thinking about $40? Well, contribute the extra $40 at a 9% rate of return for 40 years, and when I'm ready to retire thats $1,256 or so. $1,256 is worth a few minutes of my time.
Their service is naturally very sticky (who wants to have to change brokers after you have an account with one?).

This is very different than most software subscription models, where the software is not sticky and the price differential rewards the customer partly with reduced card processing charges and partially compensates them for promising not to cancel.
Incidentally: $5 shipping on a gift certificate is something I would never, ever do. It costs you a postage stamp plus a trivial amount of labor.
posts.key_insight: Small financial optimizations compound dramatically over decades, and understanding why businesses structure their pricing the way they do reveals whether you're the target customer or just subsidizing acquisition of new ones.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie walks through his first year of tax preparation as a solo software business owner, detailing which expenses he deducted and which he didn't for his Bingo Card Creator side business. He reports $2,480.05 in gross income on his Schedule C-EZ and methodically evaluates each potential deduction with the help of a CPA friend. The post reveals the mundane reality of running a tiny software business — penny-pinching over $4.50 tax savings on a WinZip license while honestly acknowledging that his internet connection serves his World of Warcraft habit more than his business. He promises to share the final Schedule C-EZ overview once complete.
key_points: A first-year micro-ISV generated $2,480.05 in gross revenue, making tax preparation a useful learning exercise in business expense tracking
Honest categorization of deductions matters more than aggressive claiming — he skips plausible-but-dishonest deductions like home internet
Keeping copious records and reviewing old blog posts can surface forgotten deductible expenses like the $99 Robosoft purchase
Even tiny tax savings ($4.50 on WinZip) add up when you're methodical about tracking every business expense
money_quotes: You can come to great tax savings if you keep copious records.
I have a level 60 gnome mage who would argue violently that the 50MB/s connection I pay for is not business related.
Good thing I don't have to declare DKP as income.
$99 expenditure = $15 tax savings. Not bad for 2 minutes of work.
I had totally forgotten about this expenditure until I was browsing my blog's 'stats' category looking for stuff I had missed.
posts.key_insight: Running a micro-ISV means sweating the small stuff on taxes — keeping meticulous records and being honest about personal-versus-business use turns even trivial expenses into legitimate deductions.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports that January 2007 is proving to be an exceptionally strong month for his Bingo Card Creator business after a poor December. He has already surpassed December's total sales in the first eleven days of January, driven by either seasonal demand from teachers starting new terms or his achievement of a front-page Google ranking for 'bingo cards.' He notes that another micro-ISV blogger also experiences strong Januaries, suggesting a seasonal pattern. He plans to release a software update incorporating user-requested improvements to printing and layout.
key_points: Achieving front-page Google ranking for a key search term doubled his traffic and likely drove a sales surge
Seasonal patterns in software sales are real — December holidays depress sales while January back-to-school creates demand spikes
Understanding your customer's calendar (teachers starting new terms) is essential for interpreting sales fluctuations
Software updates that respond to user feature requests serve dual purposes: download spikes and long-term conversion rate improvements
money_quotes: January has been going like gangbusters. I've had my two highest sales days ever yesterday and today, and have already sold more in January than the entire month of December.

I'm not entirely sure whether this is due to a spike in interest due to the new term starting (which means teachers need a new first day of the term game) or whether it is due to me now being on the front page of the search for bingo cards on Google, which has resulted in a sustained doubling of my traffic.
I hope to get a new version of the software out, which will provide a short-term spike in downloads and hopefully a modest long-term improvement in conversion rates.
It incorporates many of the improvements in printing and layout that folks have been requesting.
posts.key_insight: For software serving a specific professional audience like teachers, understanding their seasonal calendar and achieving strong organic search rankings are the two most powerful drivers of sales growth.

posts.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews e-junkie's new 'Fat Free Cart' feature, which adds a slick AJAX-powered shopping cart overlay to his Bingo Card Creator website. The integration took only 45 seconds via copy-pasted JavaScript, replacing his previous clunky ordering flow. He uses this as an opportunity to drop his secondary payment processor eSellerate, consolidating everything through e-junkie which now supports both PayPal and Google Checkout. He strongly recommends e-junkie for micro-ISV developers as a cheap, effective way to handle payment processing without distraction from core product work.
key_points: e-junkie's new Fat Free Cart provides a polished AJAX popup checkout experience that took only 45 seconds to integrate via copy-paste JavaScript
The cart unifies PayPal and Google Checkout into a single flow, eliminating the need to maintain separate payment providers
McKenzie dropped eSellerate entirely, forfeiting ~$90 in unpaid earnings to escape its quirky interface and long ordering path
For micro-ISVs, the key advantage of e-junkie is getting payment processing solved cheaply ($5/month) so you can focus on building your product
money_quotes: Unfortunately, I have the web-programming ability of a newborn squirrel, so I was resigned to always having my website look and feel a wee bit frumpy.
The best part: it took me 45 seconds to integrate the cart with my page. Take Javascript code, copy-paste. Update Buy Now URLs. Done.
I'm quite glad to be done with their quirky interface and murderously long ordering path.
From the POV of my customers, Paypal and Google Checkout are distinct. From my point of view, I control both through my e-junkie control panel.

I continue to advise anybody and everybody just starting out on their uISV to use e-junkie. Its cheap ($5/month) and it gets you back to doing your business in a matter of minutes, rather than worrying about boring payment processing trivia.
posts.key_insight: For solo software entrepreneurs, the best payment infrastructure is whatever gets you back to building your product fastest — optimize for integration simplicity and consolidation, not features.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents a Paypal bug where he couldn't withdraw money despite having already confirmed his primary email address. The system simultaneously told him he needed to confirm his email and that it was already confirmed. He found a workaround by adding a new disposable email address to the account, which resolved the withdrawal block without disrupting his payment notifications. He shares the fix publicly hoping Google will surface it for others stuck in the same catch-22.
key_points: Paypal had a bug creating a catch-22: withdrawal required email confirmation, but the email was already confirmed and couldn't be re-confirmed
The workaround was adding a new disposable email address to the account, which unblocked withdrawals without changing the primary address
McKenzie notes the dread of having to contact Paypal customer service, reflecting the notoriously poor support experience
He left the disposable address in place rather than removing it immediately, fearing rapid account changes could trigger a fraud review
money_quotes: God knows a Paypal customer with a problem has not a friend in the world.
I faced the unpleasant prospect of having to communicate with Paypal customer service — oh no!
I have the totally unsubstantiated worry that adding and removing addresses in a rapid fashion could cause a fraud review.
posts.key_insight: Payment platforms like Paypal can trap users in absurd catch-22 situations where the only escape is creative workarounds, since customer support is effectively unreachable.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts a day where he used Perl both at his day job and for personal projects, finding himself unexpectedly productive and enjoying the experience. At work, he used Perl to process 15,000 emails from mailing list archives for spam filter testing, replacing what would have been a laborious gawk-based approach with a single regular expression. At home, he used Perl to quickly modify JavaScript across his web pages for an e-junkie shopping cart integration. He humorously expresses guilt about enjoying Perl's notoriously unreadable syntax.
key_points: Perl's regular expression capabilities made a complex email parsing task trivial compared to alternatives like gawk
The right tool for the job can compress hours of work into minutes, even if that tool has an ugly reputation
McKenzie was running his Bingo Card Creator business while working a full-time programming job in Japan
The self-deprecating humor about Perl's readability reflects the broader developer culture tension between pragmatism and code aesthetics
money_quotes: I had to code Perl at my day job today… and was productive… and enjoyed it. Ewwwww.
That task took a single regular expression — that whole 'matching the stuff in parentheses' is really magic.
So I decided 'Hey, this will only take a minute in Perl'. And it actually only did take a minute.
Someone please pass me a flail, I need to be beaten before all my code looks like newspaper characters cussing.
posts.key_insight: Pragmatic tool choice trumps aesthetic preferences — Perl's ugly syntax is worth tolerating when it turns hours of text processing into minutes of work.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie defends his choice to use PayPal for his software business despite widespread community fears about account freezes. He argues that alternative payment processors like eSellerate, Avantage, and Plimus cost significantly more — hundreds of dollars extra on modest profits — while offering worse customer experiences. He frames PayPal as a calculated risk comparable to depending on any other business infrastructure, and notes that 70% of his customers already have PayPal accounts, making it a trust signal. He concludes with practical risk mitigation strategies like auto-sweeping balances to a bank account.
key_points: Alternative payment processors would have cost $150-$270 extra on ~$1,250 in pre-tax profit, a massive percentage hit that scales linearly with sales
Customer trust for PayPal far exceeds trust for an unknown indie developer — 70% of his customers already have PayPal accounts
Every business relationship involves calculated risk; PayPal freezes are no different from Google de-indexing you or your bank losing records
PayPal's ecosystem spawned useful services like e-junkie and Payloadz that further reduce costs and friction
Risk can be mitigated practically by auto-sweeping balances daily to a bank account, capping maximum exposure to ~$50
money_quotes: Trust is currency, because without trust I get no currency.
When phenomenally successful Andy Brice asks 'What am I getting for an extra $880 per thousand transactions?' that is not a hypothetical question.
My relationship with Paypal, like my relationship with my bank, with Google, and with the Java community, is a calculated risk.

Whereas I have never lost a dime on Paypal in 7 years, I have never successfully recouped a penny off of close to $100 in sales on eSellerate due to obtusely difficult processes to connect me to my money.
The Paypal name means 'Buy here and you're covered' to the astoundingly high seventy percent of my customers who have an account with them.

This limits my maximum losses on Paypal to approximately $50, plus whatever damage to my business continuity the freeze would cause before I could type 'mv purchasing_esellerate.htm purchasing.htm' into a terminal somewhere.
posts.key_insight: For a small software business, the concrete costs of alternative payment processors and the trust value of a recognized brand like PayPal far outweigh the speculative risk of an account freeze, especially when that risk can be mitigated to near-zero with simple balance sweeping.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on the results of reorganizing his software purchase page. Moving the CD purchase option from the bottom to a prominent position dramatically increased CD orders, though the margins are razor-thin at $0.50 per disc. He also notes that Google Checkout captured half his sales surprisingly quickly, with most buyers creating new accounts just to complete the purchase, suggesting a longer-than-necessary checkout flow.
key_points: Positioning a purchase option prominently on the page dramatically increased its uptake — visibility drives conversions more than demand
The CD option operates at near-zero margin ($0.50/unit) but may be worth subsidizing if it converts customers who wouldn't otherwise buy the software license
Google Checkout captured half of sales immediately, but users were creating new accounts to use it, adding unnecessary friction to the purchase flow
Even obvious calls to action confuse some customers — he receives emails asking how to click a button to order a CD
money_quotes: I moved the option to buy a CD from purgatory at the bottom of the page to front-and-center. This has greatly increased the number of CDs bought.
Yes, I really do get emails saying 'I see this option to get a CD. Will you really send me a CD? What do I do to order a CD?' 'Click the link or push the button.' 'Thank you!'
Currently, they cost me $4.50 each and I charge $5.00, and $.50 doesn't even cover the effort for me to go to cd-fulfillment.com and input the order.
If the existence of it encourages people who wouldn't otherwise buy to buy the software, then I'll subsidize CD orders night and day just to sell the associated license.
People are rather shockingly likely to pick Checkout through Google.

All of the users seem to have had their account for 0 days so I'm guessing they made the account just to place the order with me. Good news for Google, bad news for me — that means the ordering pathway is twice as long as it should be!
posts.key_insight: Page placement is a more powerful conversion lever than most people realize — moving a purchase option from the bottom to the top of a page can dramatically change buying behavior, even when the underlying product and price remain identical.

posts.summary: McKenzie reflects on his growing dependency on Google for his Bingo Card Creator business, noting that Google properties account for 65% of his web traffic and over half of his sales. He acknowledges that while this dependency is manageable because he has a day job as a safety net, it's concerning for his long-term goal of making his business his primary income. He evaluates several mitigation strategies including diversifying ad providers, maintaining backup payment systems, and finding non-search customer acquisition channels. The post concludes with the uncomfortable realization that even word-of-mouth referrals ultimately funnel back through Google search.
key_points: Google properties account for 65% of web traffic and over half of sales, creating dangerous single-vendor dependency
Having a day job provides a safety net that makes the risk tolerable but doesn't solve the underlying problem for someone planning to go full-time
Backup plans for payment processing are feasible (45-second recovery to PayPal), but backup plans for organic search traffic are essentially impossible
Even diversified customer acquisition channels like word-of-mouth ultimately route back through Google search
