Microsoft's algorithm changes already demonstrated how a single search engine tweak can damage valuable rankings
money_quotes: I'm dependent on Google. Google properties send me 65% of my web traffic and account for over half of my sales.
If Google ever tweaks their algorithms in a similar fashion, I'm 31 flavors of screwed.
The idea of having my business at the mercy of one player who is not me is rather disconcerting.
Bingo Card Creator could close up shop tomorrow night and while I'd regret it I'd still be able to pay the rent, buy food, and save for retirement.
In the event of catastrophic failure at Google Checkout I can be recovered in under 45 seconds.
Unfortunately, when my users tell their friends to go download Bingo Card Creator, their friends Google for it. D'oh!
posts.key_insight: Platform dependency on a single traffic source is an existential business risk that even diversified acquisition strategies struggle to mitigate when that platform dominates the entire discovery layer.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares personal facts about himself as part of a blog meme chain in the micro-ISV community. He reveals his intense dislike of Tokyo despite living in Japan, his extensive job history of 16+ positions, his functional illiteracy in written Japanese despite working as a translator, his World of Warcraft guild leadership that preceded founding Bingo Card Creator, and his freakish ability to memorize entire books as a child. The post humanizes him beyond his professional persona and provides early glimpses of the personality traits that would define his later career.
key_points: McKenzie's departure from World of Warcraft directly led to founding Bingo Card Creator — the sudden surplus of free time and need for intellectual challenge drove him to build software
Despite working professionally as a translator/interpreter in Japan, he considers his Japanese literacy his biggest professional failing, able to handle technical jargon but not everyday instructions
He had held at least 16 different jobs by his mid-twenties, with almost all ending on good terms, suggesting a pattern of restless experimentation that would define his career
His early attempt at newspaper columnism ended partly due to death threats, foreshadowing his later preference for independent publishing on his own terms
money_quotes: I would rather live in Baghdad than Tokyo.
People who think videogames are mindless entertainment which teach no valuable skills have never attempted to create an equitable DKP system.
I could translate this post into Japanese without difficulty, given sufficient time and access to a computerized dictionary. I could not do it by hand without sounding like a slow third grader.
One of my major instigations for doing so was I suddenly had so much free time and nothing I could really sink my teeth into in terms of intellectual problems.
Professional relationships are about more than just accumulating Google juice and career advancement… not that those are bad things!
The pay is lousy, the deadlines are irksome, editors are a bear to deal with, and the death threats I got caused needless distress for my family, so I found greener pastures.
posts.key_insight: The restless intellectual energy that drove McKenzie through 16 jobs, World of Warcraft guild leadership, and memorizing entire books is the same energy that would eventually make him a successful software entrepreneur — he just needed to find the right outlet.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents three specific Java cross-platform compatibility issues he encountered when releasing his Bingo Card Creator software on Mac. The post covers file path handling differences between Windows and Mac JARs, printer service lookup inconsistencies across platforms, and the danger of assuming individually-tested components will work correctly in combination. These are practical lessons learned from shipping real software to customers on multiple platforms.
key_points: File path handling with ./ behaves differently in Mac JARs versus Windows, defaulting to system root instead of the JAR's directory
Java's printer service lookup doesn't guarantee the default printer appears in the available printer list on Mac, unlike Windows
Individually tested components can introduce showstopper bugs when combined, even when no direct changes were made to the failing logic
Cross-platform Java development requires testing on each target platform rather than assuming library behavior is consistent
money_quotes: "don't use . here, causes problems" is not a maximally useful comment.

If you were careless like me and assumed that the default printer was on that list if the default printer wasn't null, you will have your GUI thread choke and die with an index out of bounds error thrown by the list component.

Seemingly innocuous changes to one part of your code can have a ripple effect causing the libraries to break assumptions you have made in other parts of your code, resulting in potential showstoppers.

Swing is a known good component, the default printer is a known good component, and the printer enumeration methods are known good… but the combination of the three introduced a showstopper on the Mac.
I'm going to recode my default printer selection logic to use least-distance string comparison to get around this little feature.
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform Java development reveals that individually correct components can combine to produce showstopper bugs, making platform-specific testing essential even for supposedly portable code.

posts.summary: McKenzie warns micro-ISV owners against misinterpreting website traffic data by failing to account for natural market cycles. Using his own Google organic traffic as an example, he shows how cyclical patterns like holiday breaks, weekends, and long weekends create noise that can be mistaken for the effects of algorithm changes or your own optimizations. He argues that before attributing any traffic change to a specific cause, you must first establish what the baseline pattern would look like without intervention. He illustrates this with his own recent meta description change, noting he doesn't yet have enough data to draw conclusions.
key_points: Traffic data contains natural cycles (weekends, holidays, school breaks) that can easily be mistaken for the impact of deliberate changes or external forces
Before measuring the effect of any change, you must understand what the data would have looked like without the change — establishing a proper baseline
Attributing traffic spikes to Google algorithm changes or your own tweaks without controlling for cyclical patterns leads to false conclusions
Even when you make a deliberate change, you need sufficient data over time before concluding it had a meaningful effect
money_quotes: One thing that is even more critical is that you understand what the data would have looked like had you not made the change, because otherwise you could be changing for a spurious reason.
What is actually happening here is that you are seeing the combination of natural cycles in my market combined with a sustained massive sustained increase in overall traffic since early December.
Resist the temptation — first, understand what the underlying pattern of your data should be absent any influence from you or the suspected outside force.
Its easy to get caught in the trap of saying that any momentary spike is either a reflection of your own action or of some outside force.
As of yet, focusing on just the last week of results, I do not have sufficient data to conclude that this change has produced a meaningful push in either direction.
posts.key_insight: Before attributing any change in your metrics to a specific cause, you must first establish the baseline pattern your data follows naturally, or you risk optimizing based on noise.

posts.summary: McKenzie compares his experience using PayPal and Google Checkout side by side for processing payments on Bingo Card Creator. PayPal offers faster access to funds through instant ATM card spending or 3-4 day ACH transfers, while Google Checkout bundles transactions with longer delays. Google's free payment processing is a significant advantage, especially for cross-border transactions where PayPal charges higher fees. However, Google Checkout's merchant interface is poorly designed, making basic tasks like searching transactions or querying sales totals unnecessarily difficult. Both services create customer communication challenges through email obfuscation or outdated addresses. The overall assessment is that Google Checkout's cost savings are attractive but its tooling reflects a product built by engineers unfamiliar with small business operations.
key_points: PayPal provides significantly faster access to funds than Google Checkout, with instant spending via ATM card versus Google's multi-day batched ACH transfers
Google Checkout's free payment processing is a major cost advantage, especially for international transactions where PayPal charges premium cross-border fees
Google Checkout's merchant dashboard is severely lacking — you can't search by customer name, partial email, or invoice number, making basic operations like processing refunds painful
Both platforms create customer communication problems: Google uses disposable forwarding addresses, PayPal users often have outdated email addresses
Customer acceptance of both payment methods is high, even among non-technical B2C customers
money_quotes: Free payment processing is nice.
Given that payment processing is my #2 expense every month after AdWords (#3 if you count taxes, I suppose) I'm quite happy seeing it get slashed.
I get the feeling it was designed by an engineer, not someone who has ever run a small business.
It is difficult to ask fairly trivial questions like 'How much did I sell in January?' without exporting your sales records to CSV. Exporting records to CSV doesn't appear to actually work.
The biggest head-scratcher is that the search interface for transactions is terrible.
If Bob of the obfuscated email address comes to you in a month to ask for a refund, and he doesn't know his Google transaction number, you get to browse for it by hand. On Google, of all places.
Compared to the typical 'We'll pay you once a month' policy at a lot of the 'real' shareware payment processors either of these policies are quite speedy.
posts.key_insight: Google Checkout's free processing makes it financially attractive for small software businesses, but its merchant tools are so poorly designed that basic operations like finding a transaction become absurdly difficult — ironic for the world's leading search company.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that a new version of his software has broken critical features on the Mac platform, including saving, printing, and displaying. The bug resulted in at least one customer refund, which he finds particularly painful since it means losing someone who had already committed to purchasing. He reverts the Mac version and PAD file while working on a fix, crediting a colleague for help debugging.
key_points: Cross-platform Java development creates platform-specific bugs that are hard to catch before release
Losing an existing paying customer is far worse than failing to convert a prospect
Quick reversion to a known-good state is the pragmatic response to a broken release
money_quotes: Just when I think its safe to push out a new version I find that the Mac code has been severely compromised somehow and minor features like saving, printing, and displaying no longer work.
annoying people who pay money for software they haven't even downloaded yet, thats like kicking yourself firmly in the teeth
posts.key_insight: Paying customers who encounter broken software represent a uniquely painful failure because you've already won their trust and money, then immediately betrayed both.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month yet for Bingo Card Creator, with $553.70 in profit from 28 sales on roughly 6 hours of work. His purchase page redesign successfully drove CD sales, and Google Checkout is saving money on fulfillment costs. However, Google AdWords is performing terribly with only 1 sale attributable to $90 in spend, giving it a heavily negative ROI. He's seeing strong organic growth with downloads hitting 1,100 (up from 200 in September), though visitor-to-download conversion rates are falling as less qualified traffic increases. He outlines priorities for February including fixing AdWords ROI, redesigning the download page, and finally adopting source control.
key_points: Revenue hit $698.65 gross on 28 sales with $553.70 profit, tracking toward his $1,000/month goal by April, all on roughly 6 hours of work
Google AdWords generated only 1 sale against $90 in spend, making it a clear money-loser that needs to be fixed or cut entirely
Organic search traffic is growing rapidly but converting at lower rates (12%) compared to highly qualified CPC traffic (25%), dragging overall conversion down to 16%
Downloads grew from 200 in September to 1,100 in January, showing strong compounding growth driven by improving search rankings
He's still not using source control and nearly lost work trying to revert a Mac build, prompting him to finally prioritize adopting it
money_quotes: Yes, Virginia, that IS equivalent to taking $65 out my my wallet and setting fire to it.
No reason to pay Google a tax just for the privilege of paying them a tax.
AdWords needs to have positive ROI or it needs to get cut, it is as simple as that.
Hard to believe that back in September I was ecstatic when this number hit 200.
No less than a quarter of my paying customer base is checking for a new version at least monthly. This gives me a warm fuzzy feeling inside.
I nearly had a heart attack trying to revert to a Mac version that was known to be good.
Need to find a new method of attack to get some more sales growth rather than just resting on my laurels, which candidly is a fairly accurate description of my marketing for 3 months now.
posts.key_insight: Even with strong organic growth compounding month over month, every marketing channel must justify its ROI or be cut — paying for traffic that doesn't convert is just burning money.

posts.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically recommends Paint.NET 3.0 as a free graphics editing tool that bridges the gap between MSPaint's simplicity and Photoshop's power. He highlights its features like layers, full undo, and competent resizing as perfect for the small graphic tasks that micro-ISVs regularly face. He also follows through on a previous promise to donate to the project, tying it to his most profitable month ever.
key_points: Paint.NET fills a practical niche between MSPaint and Photoshop for small software businesses that need basic but competent graphics editing
McKenzie practices what he preaches about supporting free software by donating $25 after his most profitable month
The framing of the donation as '2-3% of a Photoshop license' is a clever anchoring technique that makes donating feel like a bargain
money_quotes: Its about as easy to use as MSPaint, with many, many more features (layers! Full undo capability! Competent resizing algorithms!), and its perfect for the sort of 'I have an app icon but need to make it a CD logo' type chores that you're probably spending too much time on right now.
Artistic ability still not included, unfortunately.
I just had my most profitable month ever.
If you get as much use out of them as I have, consider dropping 2-3% or so of the cost of a Photoshop license in their tipjar.
posts.key_insight: Micro-ISV operators should invest in efficient, free tools for routine tasks like graphics editing, and support the open-source projects that save them money.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes redesigning the CD label image for Bingo Card Creator's physical CD distribution, noting concerns about text density on the new design. He also evaluates switching CD fulfillment providers from cd-fulfillment.com to SwiftCD, weighing automation benefits against higher costs and reduced customization. The post illustrates the mundane operational decisions involved in running a one-person software business.
key_points: A professional-looking CD label matters for technically-disinclined customers who need visual guidance through installation
Switching fulfillment providers involves tradeoffs between automation convenience, cost ($4.50 vs $4.99), and feature differences like custom invoice instructions
Integration between services (e-junkie and fulfillment) drives operational efficiency decisions for solo software businesses
DIY automation via scripting is considered but deferred due to time constraints
money_quotes: While its utilitarian, it doesn't exactly present a great image of my software or help my technically-disinclined customers through the process of getting the software to work.
I'm concerned that there is too much text and that it will cause folks' eyes to glaze over.

I have resisted doing it so far because the reason is inherently selfish — SwiftCD integrates with e-junkie, which means I would no longer have to type order information into cd-fulfillment.com's interface.
The downsides are that the CDs would not look quite as nice, the price to me would go up from $4.50 (with shipping) to $4.99 (without).
I suppose I could go ahead and create a Perl script accepting information from e-junkie, then firing off an email to cd-fulfillment to get them to create and mail the CD…
posts.key_insight: Even small operational decisions like CD label design and fulfillment provider selection involve meaningful tradeoffs between cost, customer experience, and automation that solo software entrepreneurs must navigate constantly.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his refund rate data from selling Bingo Card Creator, a $24.95 educational software product. Over six months, roughly 5% of customers requested refunds, though he considers this inflated by early software bugs, with the mature version seeing under 2%. He argues strongly for unconditional money-back guarantees, explaining that they actually increase sales by letting uncertain customers externalize risk. The most common refund reason — 'it doesn't do what I want' — proves the guarantee is working as intended, converting hesitant browsers into buyers.
key_points: Refund rates for mature shareware products can be under 2%, with 5% being the upper bound during buggy early releases
Unconditional money-back guarantees increase net sales by letting risk-averse customers buy before they're fully convinced
The most common refund reason ('doesn't do what I want') demonstrates the guarantee is converting otherwise-lost prospects into buyers
Processing refunds costs the developer nothing — PayPal refunds transaction fees on initiated refunds, making guarantees pure upside
money_quotes: If any customer were to ask for their money back after that period, I would go to any length of effort up to and including mailing them a personal check to get them their money back.
In the last six months, roughly 5% of my customers have requested a money-back guarantee.
For the most mature version of my software, my refund rate was under 2%.
It means that at least some of your customers are buying your product when they are not yet convinced that it will meet their needs.
The guarantee thus provides a safety net, allowing the customer to externalize the risk of their extended trial period to you, the developer.

They think 'Well, if it doesn't work out, I can always return it'. You should be happy to bear this 'risk', as a return costs you little or nothing to process, meaning that this is pure upside from your point of view.
I have never paid a penny as a result of processing a return.
posts.key_insight: Money-back guarantees are pure upside for software sellers because they convert hesitant prospects into buyers while costing virtually nothing to honor.

posts.summary: McKenzie notes a wave of interviews in the micro-ISV community, including Joel Spolsky's appearance on the MicroISV Show and three recent interviews with McKenzie himself. He uses the occasion to caution readers that all advice is shaped by the speaker's specific circumstances. He contrasts Spolsky's perspective as a multi-million-dollar company head with his own as a humble sole proprietor, arguing that success can take many forms. He closes with self-deprecating humor about his limited entrepreneurial experience.
key_points: All business advice is filtered through the speaker's own experience and circumstances, and listeners should account for that bias
Success in software business can be defined many ways — even one-person shops can be highly profitable
Joel Spolsky's claim that a micro-ISV needs at least two people is challenged by McKenzie's experience and observations of successful solo operators
Reading is a faster way to process technically dense material than listening, even for someone who values oral communication
money_quotes: Remember that everyone speaks on the basis of their own experience.
I am of the opinion that you can be successful under almost any configuration for the appropriate definition of 'successful'.
Even if that definition of successful is 'go to sleep on a mattress packed solid with dollar bills you've made', I know a number of one-man shops who qualify.
The reports of desktop applications being dead are greatly exaggerated.
'80% of your business is non-code' vastly overestimates the importance of the code.
You should keep in mind when reading any interviews with me that I have more experience being a paperboy than being an entrepeneur, and that I was a total failure as a paperboy.
posts.key_insight: All business advice is autobiographical — filter every expert's recommendations through their specific circumstances before applying them to your own.

posts.summary: Google has added a backlink viewing tool through its Sitemaps/Webmaster Console, allowing site owners to see who links to their website. McKenzie finds this useful for tracking the SEO impact of his marketing efforts. He notes that most of his backlinks come from download sites or spam blogs exploiting bingo-related keywords to boost casino sites. However, he finds some genuine links from real customers and useful content, including an elementary school linking to his Dolch sight word list page.
key_points: Google's Webmaster Console now lets site owners view their backlinks, previously opaque data
Backlinks are critical for SEO and can reveal whether marketing efforts are working
Most backlinks for small software sites come from low-quality sources like download directories and spam blogs
Creating genuinely useful content (like the Dolch sight word list) earns organic backlinks from real users
money_quotes: This is useful as backlinks are SEO mana from heaven and you can spy on whether your marketing is having any effect.
The overwhelming majority are either download sites or spam blogs.
They search for bingo related keywords and link to them with the hope of getting Google to rank their casino site — I hate this.
The Dolch sight word list, for example, got linked to by an elementary school.
posts.key_insight: Backlink data reveals both the spam ecosystem gaming search engines and the genuine organic links earned by creating useful content for your target audience.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a link to his interview on StartupSpark, where he discusses his experience as a micro-ISV (uISV) developer. He appreciates the interviewer's bingo-themed pun in the title. The post highlights his belief that the sales and ordering process matters more than product quality for software businesses. He acknowledges he still has room to improve his own ordering pathway.
key_points: McKenzie was interviewed by StartupSpark about his micro-ISV experience and views on the field
He emphasizes that a flawless ordering pathway is more important than having the best product in its class
He advocates for treating the purchase process as seriously as the product itself, including having a design document for it
He admits his own ordering process still needs improvement, reflecting an incremental approach to business optimization
money_quotes: It is more important that your ordering pathway be flawless than your application be the best in its class.
Do you have a design document for your ordering pathway? Why not?
Getting better, bit by bit and month by month.
posts.key_insight: A flawless purchasing experience matters more to software sales than having the best product, yet most developers neglect their ordering pathway entirely.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts losing track of what day it is after a grueling month of 12-16 hour workdays culminating in a major presentation. The presentation went well and resulted in a functional spam filter that will be open-sourced. He reflects on how this experience highlights the appeal of self-employment, where such extreme work schedules wouldn't be necessary. He notes he still likes his day job despite the exhaustion.
key_points: A month of 12-16 hour days caused McKenzie to lose track of what day of the week it was
The intense work period produced a functional spam filter soon to be released as open source
The experience reinforced his desire for self-employment as an escape from unsustainable work schedules
money_quotes: You know what I really like about the prospect of self-employment? No months of 12 to 16 hour days, and no forgetting what day it is.
posts.key_insight: Grueling day-job schedules that cause you to lose track of time reinforce the appeal of self-employment and working on your own terms.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports fixing his underperforming AdWords campaign with two simple changes that dramatically improved economics. By enabling position preference to target the #2-3 ad spots instead of #1, he cut cost per download by 50% while actually improving conversion rates. He also ruthlessly killed keywords with cost-per-acquisition above his expected profit per download. The combined result: spending is down 50%, CPA is halved, and he now earns $2 in purchases for every $1 spent on AdWords, up from $0.60-0.80.
key_points: Deliberately choosing the #2-3 ad position over #1 reduced costs by a third while improving conversion rates from 22% to 30%, because less eager clickers converted better
Ruthlessly cutting keywords based on CPA vs. expected profit per download, regardless of personal intuition about keyword quality, eliminated unprofitable spend
The #1 ad position is often a trap: competitors like Amazon mass-bid on keywords and absorb the premium, while smaller businesses can profit more from lower positions
Once AdWords spending becomes sustainably profitable, the rational move is to increase budget and recruit new keywords to the profitable set
money_quotes: I get $2 in purchases for every $1 on AdWords, as opposed to $0.60-0.80 as of a month ago.
So I said, you know, let someone ELSE have that #1 spot.
Most of my competitors, incidentally, are folks like Amazon who mass bid on every keyword under the sun.
It also comes with customers who appear to be more likely to convert (30% vs. 22% for trial).
If they had a CPA greater than my expected profit per download, no matter how good I thought they would be personally, I nixed them.
My only remaining keywords are my A-team, and I need to see if I can't recruit a few members this week.
As long as its actually sustainably profitable it makes sense to increase it, right?
posts.key_insight: In paid search advertising, the #1 position often has worse unit economics than lower positions — deliberately targeting #2-3 can cut costs while improving conversion quality.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch from NSIS to Inno Setup for his software installer, arguing that visual appearance matters because the installer is the user's first impression. He draws an analogy to his stock icons making software feel 'bright and happy' and notes that in consumer software, looks sell. He acknowledges the tradeoff that Inno Setup requires two extra clicks but plans to optimize that.
key_points: The installer is the first impression customers get of your software and deserves serious attention
In the consumer market, visual polish directly impacts sales — looks sell software
Inno Setup looks more professional than NSIS, justifying the switch despite added clicks
Every additional mouseclick forced on a customer is friction worth eliminating
money_quotes: I haven't really spent all that much time on my installer, which is somewhat silly because its the first look people get of my software and I should really want to nail it.
Especially in the consumer market, looks sell software.
Additional mouseclicks are also something I really hate forcing on my customer.
posts.key_insight: Your installer is your software's first impression — visual polish matters as much there as anywhere else in the product.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes how to create musical note bingo cards using Bingo Card Creator with a special Bach font. Several music teachers had requested this feature, so he provides both step-by-step instructions for the DIY approach and a pre-made set of 32 cards as a free download. The post serves as both customer support content and a subtle SEO play targeting music teachers searching for musical bingo cards.
key_points: Customer requests from music teachers revealed an unserved niche for musical note bingo cards
Rather than building native support, McKenzie created a workaround using a third-party font and provided free pre-made cards
The post doubles as SEO-optimized content targeting a specific long-tail search query while providing genuine value
Licensing constraints (Bach font is academic-use only) prevented bundling the solution directly into the product
money_quotes: In the last six months I have had several music teachers write in to ask me if Bingo Card Creator can create cards with musical notes on them. The answer is "yes", but the process is rather difficult.
As this process is rather arduous for many teachers to perform, I have made 32 musical note bingo cards myself.
posts.key_insight: Responding to repeated customer requests with free content that solves their problem serves simultaneously as customer support, SEO content targeting a long-tail niche, and a soft funnel back to the paid product.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces plans to start a new software project over the summer, a subscription-based web app targeting a specific vertical market. He estimates a $1,500 launch budget with modest monthly advertising spend, and projects the app could exceed his salary within a year. He plans to build it on LAMP, hire a lawyer and professional designer, and be as transparent about the process as he has been with Bingo Card Creator.
key_points: The new project is a subscription-based web app with a 1-2 month free trial, targeting a specific vertical with the value proposition of making customers more money
He projects exceeding his current salary within a year of launch with modest customer volume assumptions and a $1,500 launch budget
He acknowledges key risks: unfamiliarity with web development, less domain expertise than his teaching vertical, and dependency on a major corporation
He believes he has three distinct competitive advantages over cheaper alternatives that are readily apparent to customers
money_quotes: I'm going to make it easier for you to make a lot of money. You're going to pay me a trivial amount every month and be happy to do it. We'll be doing business together for YEARS.
The better option of the two, from my current research, would (with rather modest assumptions made about customer volume) probably exceed my current salary within a year of launch.

I have competition offering services which can accomplish the same general goal for cheaper, but I have at least three ideas why my method is uniquely superior, in a way which is readily apparent to the customer.
I will be unhealthily dependent on a major corporation whose name, for once, does NOT start with G.
This is sort of a memo to myself to not spend summer on videogames.
posts.key_insight: A subscription web app that demonstrably helps customers make money can command loyal, long-term paying customers even against cheaper competition, provided the value proposition is clearly superior.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares a fan-created tool that reorganizes his blog into chronological order. A reader named Rizal built a 'MicroISV on a Shoestring Reader' mashup that displays the blog from oldest to newest, solving the problem that blog software defaults to reverse-chronological order. McKenzie appreciates this because he often directs people to read his blog from the beginning to understand his uISV journey.
key_points: McKenzie's blog serves as the narrative of how his micro-ISV came about, and he regularly directs people to read it from the start
Blog software's default reverse-chronological ordering is a poor fit for following a business journey over time
A community member independently built a tool to solve this UX problem, demonstrating early reader engagement
money_quotes: When folks ask me "So how exactly did your uISV come about?", I generally tell them to go to the start of the blog and start reading.
posts.key_insight: A blog documenting a micro-ISV journey is best read chronologically, but blog software defaults work against this, creating an opportunity for reader-built tools.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie documents his switch from cd-fulfillment.com to SwiftCD for physical CD fulfillment of his Bingo Card Creator software. The primary motivation was automating order processing—half his customers were requesting CDs, creating a daily manual data entry burden that quadrupled his support workload. SwiftCD's integration with e-junkie eliminated the need to wake up early and manually copy-paste shipping details. He notes SwiftCD actively courted him (likely for the blog coverage), and he's willing to absorb a small per-unit loss on CDs for the convenience and sleep.
key_points: Automating fulfillment eliminated his largest support burden—CD order entry was 4x the volume of all other customer emails combined
SwiftCD aggressively courted him with custom CD label redesigns and CEO outreach, likely anticipating positive blog coverage—and he transparently discloses this
The entire e-junkie integration took under a minute, replacing a daily 30-minute manual process
He's willing to lose money on each CD sale ($5 charge vs $5.38 cost) because the CD option drives purchases he wouldn't otherwise get
He maintains cd-fulfillment.com as a backup and gives them a fair, positive evaluation despite switching away
money_quotes: I did order entry for two years and hate it (love talking to customers, hate typing addresses).
This literally quadrupled my support burden and added no value to the customers.

Full disclosure: I think that is probably because they think I would probably write up a positive evaluation of them, and hey, thats what I'm doing, so discount this report to whatever degree you think appropriate.

Now rather than waking up 30 minutes early and checking my email to see if any CDs were purchased so I can copy, paste, copy, paste, copy paste name, email, address, transaction number, etc etc, e-junkie forwards the shipping details directly to SwiftCD and I can get some extra sleep in the morning.
I will happily underwrite the difference for the moment, since the convenience is important to me and many of my customers won't buy without the CD option.
posts.key_insight: Automate fulfillment operations as soon as manual processes become a daily burden—the time and mental overhead cost far more than the per-unit price difference.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that software business owners should always refund small amounts to unhappy customers without argument, using a real example from Dropsend where the owner escalated a $5 refund request into a hostile exchange. He contends that suppressing your ego and immediately resolving the complaint costs almost nothing compared to the revenue lost from an angry customer who cancels or files a chargeback. McKenzie advocates adopting a pre-committed strategy of obsequiousness in customer service, drawing on the Japanese concept of tatemae. He dismisses common objections like company refund policies and slippery-slope fears, noting that as a business owner you can override any policy at will. The core message is that you can never win an argument with a customer, so don't start one.
key_points: A $5 refund costs far less than losing a $50/year revenue stream plus potential chargeback fees and stress
Pre-commit to a strategy of immediate resolution to prevent escalation — decide beforehand to be obsequious
You are the boss of your own refund policy; override it whenever the accommodation advances business goals
The slippery slope argument ('if I do it for one I'll have to do it for everyone') is a fiction — customers mostly talk about great or terrible service, not how to exploit you
Suppressing your ego feels hard for about two weeks, then becomes habit and protects your mental health long-term
money_quotes: The only thing that matters is you keeping your cool, and this can frequently require having the patience of Job and less ego than a blade of grass.
You can win an argument with your boss. You can win an argument with your wife. You can even win an argument with God. But you will never win an argument with your customer.

In the pursuit of a lousy five stinking bucks you've just lost a $50 revenue stream over the next year, which is almost pure profit because you are selling a software service which requires no marginal work.
I often wish English had a word for when you need to be a spineless craven lickspittle in a good way.
Here is the great thing about running your own business: you are the boss. If your company has a policy against refunds, you can break it at will.
Your customers are an incestuous bunch but they mostly don't talk about how easy it is to screw you over.
If you learn how to do this, you can work in a call center for your entire life and not get tired. If you don't, you will burn out in a matter of months.
A nice prominent guarantee like 'We'll refund your last payment anytime, for any reason.' gets seen by every prospect who is worried 'Hmm, what if I'm not happy?'
posts.key_insight: Winning an argument with a customer is impossible — the only winning move is to immediately resolve their complaint, because the cost of ego is always higher than the cost of a refund.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a mid-February 2007 financial update for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $294 in gross sales from 11 units with roughly $195 in profit after expenses. Sales dipped around Valentine's Day, highlighting the need to anticipate holidays with special versions and ads. He details his cost structure including CD fulfillment, PayPal fees, AdWords, and hosting. Traffic has grown significantly, averaging 400 weekday visitors compared to 100 in November, and a blog post about problem customers went viral with 5,000 views reaching audiences far beyond his usual demographic.
key_points: Holiday periods like Valentine's Day cause sales to drop to zero, suggesting opportunity in creating holiday-specific versions and marketing
The business is profitable but modest: $294 gross on 11 sales with ~$195 profit after ~$108 in expenses including fulfillment, payment processing, and advertising
Website traffic has quadrupled from ~100 weekday visitors in November to ~400, with download peaks typically preceding sales peaks by about a week
A blog post about dealing with problem customers went viral (5,000 views), reaching audiences well beyond his core demographic of teachers and micro-ISV owners
money_quotes: I really need to start anticipating holidays and releasing special versions/ads for them, because otherwise my sales tend to decline to zero around them.
Wish me luck — typically, my sales peaks trail download peaks by about a week.

The one immediately below about dealing with problem customers has had about 5,000 views and, to judge by the email responses I am getting, has rocketed far out of my normal English-teacher-or-uISV-owner demographic.
I'm getting notes of commiseration from people as diverse as real estate agents and medical malpractice attourneys.
The other two are replacement CDs for two customers who got CDs from cd-fulfillment.com with a graphic that I had sized correctly, resulting in labels which are below my quality standards.
posts.key_insight: Even a tiny software business can be consistently profitable when expenses are kept low, and content marketing through blogging can drive traffic growth that far exceeds paid advertising.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a brief revenue update for his software business Bingo Card Creator, showing that in just the first 45 days of 2007 he has already earned half of his entire 2006 revenue. He sets targets of $1,000/month by April and $10,000 for the full year, noting that profits are tracking similarly well against the prior year.
key_points: 2007 revenue in 45 days ($1,050) already equals half of all 2006 revenue ($2,100)
He sets concrete, escalating financial targets: $1,000/month by April and $10,000 for the year
He distinguishes revenue from profit, noting profits are a separate and harder matter
money_quotes: Sales for the entire year, 2006: $2,100 or so
Sales from January 1st, 2007 through February 15th, 2007: $1,050
Still got quite a bit to go if I'm going to make my $1,000 in April and $10,000 in 2007 targets but I think I can do it.
Profits are, of course, another matter.
I have this funny feeling I will do better than last year.
posts.key_insight: Tracking and publicly sharing concrete revenue milestones creates accountability and makes growth tangible, even at very small scales.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his experience receiving a SwiftCD physical copy of his Bingo Card Creator software, noting the impressive print quality. He documents the shipping timeline from order to delivery in Japan, marveling at the speed and low cost of international shipping for his small software business's physical product fulfillment.
key_points: Physical product fulfillment through SwiftCD produced professional-looking results even for a tiny software business
International shipping from the US to rural Japan took about a week at minimal cost (84 cents)
Even small details like print clarity on the CD contributed to a professional appearance for his product
money_quotes: What an age to be living in where you can get a package to a little ricefield on the other side of the world in about a week for 84 cents.
The printing is astonishingly clear (even the teeny-tiny text on the CD is easily legible) and in person it looks very professionally done.
Did I mention I live in Japan? Phones here can practically perform brain surgery.
posts.key_insight: Modern fulfillment services let even the smallest software businesses deliver professional physical products globally at negligible cost.

posts.summary: McKenzie outlines his development timetable and budget for his next software project (which would become Kalzumeus), planned alongside his day job. He commits to building a Rails application in 20 hours per week with an April 1st alpha target and summer launch. He details plans to develop semi-publicly, sharing market analysis, specs, and sales figures while keeping the project name private. The total initial budget is estimated at $1,500-$2,000, with monthly costs kept low enough to reach profitability quickly.
key_points: Plans to build a Rails app in 20 hours/week alongside his day job, with disciplined time tracking
Will develop semi-publicly—sharing market, spec, and sales figures but not source code or project name
Strategic sequencing: build the app first, then bring in outside experts (designer, possibly lawyer) to turn it into a business
Total budget of $1,500-$2,000 covering hosting, design, legal, and LLC filing costs
Self-aware observation that growth strategy is the most important part yet has received the least planning
money_quotes: I've drunken deeply from the Rails koolaid and am now afflicted with an acute condition known as programming fever.
I'm decided on developing this semi-publicly — I'll be publishing the market, the spec, the thinking behind it, pretty much everything but the source code.
The little bureaucratic hoops you have to jump through to make money are so annoying.
If I hire a lawyer, he is going to be put on a strict $500 leash.
Grow business. This is the most important part and so naturally I have given it the smallest section of this post and have not done much writing in my design notebook about it. D'oh.
I expect revenue growth to be a significant multiple of Bingo Card Creator's.
posts.key_insight: A bootstrapped software business can be launched on a shoestring budget of under $2,000 by building the product yourself, sequencing expenses carefully, and bringing in outside help only after the core application is proven.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares an example of proactive customer service email he sent when a customer's credit card had issues with Google Checkout, resulting in triple charges. He canceled the duplicate orders and delivered the software immediately on trust, rather than waiting for payment resolution. He then critiques his own email, noting flaws in its language and tone. The post advocates for taking control of customer interactions when circumstances go wrong, rather than letting process failures damage the customer relationship.
key_points: When payment processing fails, proactively resolve the situation in the customer's favor rather than waiting for the system to sort itself out
Conditional apologies are worse than no apology — they signal you're trying to avoid fault rather than fix the problem
Shop-talk and jargon should be eliminated from customer communications in favor of plain language
When circumstances beyond your control make a customer interaction imperfect, take control of the circumstances yourself
money_quotes: Heck yes I am fanatical about customer service. Are you?
When circumstances beyond your control make your customer interaction less than absolutely perfect, control the freaking circumstances.
Clumsy is forgivable but conditional apologies are the worst thing ever — they make it seem like you're trying to avoid fault.
I'm going to trust that Google will resolve it to our mutual satisfaction, and deliver the software to you now.
If Google cannot resolve the issue and we are forced to cancel the order, feel free to keep the software, and accept my apologies for the inconvenience.
I love Checkout but I love my customers more.
posts.key_insight: Great customer service means proactively resolving problems in the customer's favor and controlling the narrative, even when the failure is someone else's fault.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovered that a critical feature of Bingo Card Creator had been silently broken for two weeks due to a single-character error in his installer script. The bug caused new downloads to lack the Wizards menu, which pre-loads word lists and eliminates the 'empty screen problem' that kills conversions. His sales dropped to 20-30% of normal despite record traffic, essentially running an involuntary A/B test proving the feature's importance. The bug went undetected because his testing always ran on machines that retained the old correct file structure. The experience taught him hard lessons about version-controlling build scripts, testing on clean environments, and listening to customers.
key_points: A single misplaced character in an installer script silently disabled a key feature for all new users for two weeks, cutting sales by 70-80%
The bug was invisible in testing because existing installations retained the correct directory structure, masking the problem
The 'empty screen problem' is a conversion killer: users who can't immediately see output from software won't invest time to learn it
Build and deployment scripts deserve the same version control discipline as application source code
Customer communication channels are essential safety nets that catch bugs your testing infrastructure misses
money_quotes: I was performing an involuntary A-B test: A with the feature, and B without, based on what day you had first downloaded the software. A wins by a longshot.
I essentially can't sell to someone who hasn't seen a card printed out, and if you have to type in 25 words before you can print a card then you're much less likely to invest the time.
If no files are found at all, the Wizard menu doesn't show, because nothing ticks people off like non-functional programs, right?
I accidentally introduced a single extra character into my Inno Setup script. Since my setup script is not in my Subversion I never even noticed that I had made the change.
Then new people downloaded the installer and, boom, no Wizards for two weeks.
Keep the lines of communication open with customers. They can save you from yourself.
posts.key_insight: Build artifacts and deployment scripts need the same version control and testing rigor as source code, because a single untracked change can silently destroy your conversion funnel.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that Microsoft's AdCenter advertising platform, despite its clunky interface and low volume, delivers downloads at half the cost per acquisition of Google AdWords. His simple ad for Bingo Card Creator achieved a 4% click-through rate and under 30 cents per download with zero optimization. He notes that large advertisers like Amazon bid on every keyword, but his targeted ad still performs well. He's cautiously optimistic but uncertain whether the low volume justifies further time investment in optimization.
key_points: Microsoft AdCenter delivers half the cost-per-download of Google AdWords despite a worse interface
A simple, unoptimized ad achieved 4% CTR and sub-30-cent cost per download
Low volume on AdCenter raises questions about whether optimization time is worthwhile
Large advertisers bidding on broad keyword sets don't necessarily crowd out niche software ads
money_quotes: Volume? Low. Interface? Annoying. Work flow? Terrible. Cost per download? Half of what Google is, and this required no tweaking whatsoever.
I'm buried under about four ads for places like Amazon which apparently bid on every word in the English language.
That is my target for reasonably profitability with advertising.
This might do a bit better if I did some optimization. I don't know if the time invested is worth the volume, though.
posts.key_insight: Less popular advertising platforms can deliver dramatically lower acquisition costs precisely because fewer advertisers compete there, even if the volume and tooling are inferior.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares an SEO tip from WebsiteGrader suggesting that domain registrations expiring within a year may be penalized by search engines as potential spam sites. He extended his domain registration by two years and prepaid hosting, noting his Google rankings improved afterward. He honestly acknowledges he cannot prove causation and hasn't seen this tip corroborated by trusted SEO sources. He frames it as a low-risk experiment since the $8 cost would be spent eventually regardless.
key_points: Search engines may penalize domains with short remaining registration periods, viewing them as potential spam sites
Extending domain registration is a near-zero-risk SEO experiment since the cost is deferred payment you'd make anyway
McKenzie is careful to distinguish correlation from causation in his own results
The tip came from HubSpot's WebsiteGrader tool but wasn't corroborated by other trusted SEO sources
money_quotes: Correlation != causation and all that aside, it only cost me about $8 so maybe you want to try it too.
Google and other search engines like to see domains that have been registered for extended periods of time as this shows a committment to the domain name.
If it isn't true the most you are out is about $8 which you were going to pay eventually anyway, right?
posts.key_insight: Low-cost, low-risk SEO experiments like extending domain registration are worth trying even without definitive proof they work, since the downside is negligible.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares early transaction data comparing Google Checkout and PayPal adoption among his Bingo Card Creator customers. From 29 transactions, PayPal slightly edged out Google Checkout (16 vs 13), with the notable finding that no Google Checkout users had preexisting accounts while about 60% of PayPal users did. He interprets this as evidence that existing PayPal users actively seek it out when given comparable alternatives. He also notes that his customer demographics (middle-aged women familiar with eBay) likely skew more PayPal-friendly than the tech community.
key_points: PayPal slightly outperformed Google Checkout in raw transaction numbers (16 vs 13 out of 29), despite symmetrical presentation
Zero Google Checkout customers had preexisting accounts, while roughly 60% of PayPal customers did, suggesting PayPal's installed base was a major competitive advantage
Customers with existing PayPal accounts actively seek it out when given comparable alternatives, demonstrating the power of payment habit lock-in
Most customers likely click whichever payment button is in the expected position rather than making a deliberate provider choice
Customer demographics heavily influence payment preferences — his middle-aged female customers were far more PayPal-friendly than the developer community
money_quotes: People who already use Paypal are likely to seek it out as an option when given comparable alternatives.

I suspect many customers are just mashing on any button that they know is in the contextually accurate place for Continue This Transaction Darn It rather than making a conscious decision for or against either provider.
Thats fine by me, incidentally, since I hate having customers have to decide things prior to giving me money.
Many software developers, for example, hate Paypal like the plague for some reason. I think this viewpoint is rather less prevalent among middle aged ladies, who make up a good deal of my customers.
If I had my druthers that order would be randomized for every load of the cart.
posts.key_insight: Payment provider preference is driven far more by existing habits and familiarity than by conscious evaluation, which means reducing friction at checkout matters more than which provider you offer.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his February 2007 financial results for Bingo Card Creator, showing $454 in gross sales and approximately $345 in profit. Sales were down due to a self-inflicted technical error that broke the download for most visitors. Despite the revenue setback, website traffic hit an all-time high, and AdWords spending improved. He estimates he could have hit $800-900 in sales without the download bug, and closes with a charming aside about attending an early St. Patrick's Day parade in Nagoya, Japan.
key_points: A broken download link significantly depressed conversion rates, cutting potential revenue roughly in half
Website traffic reached its best month ever despite the revenue decline, showing growing organic reach
AdWords spending was optimized, reducing expenses and partially offsetting the sales drop
The business generated approximately $345 in profit on $454 gross revenue with minimal expenses
money_quotes: Owing to my carelessness sales are quite down this month.
If I hadn't borked the download most of my visitors were getting and my conversion rate had remained steady I would have hit about $800-900 in sales without breaking a sweat.
The notion of Japanese people throwing a St. Patrick's Day parade is so charming as to forgive the fact that they miss the actual day by quite a margin every single year.
posts.key_insight: A single careless technical error on your download funnel can cut your revenue in half even when traffic is at an all-time high, underscoring how conversion mechanics matter more than raw visitor counts.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts a St. Patrick's Day evening out in Nagoya, Japan, returning to find his inbox full of order confirmations for Bingo Card Creator. He reflects on the satisfying feeling of earning passive income while socializing. A customer support request about a corrupted file raises concerns about his hosting provider GoDaddy potentially mangling his executable files again.
key_points: Passive income from software sales can exceed the cost of a night out, a tangible milestone for a solo software business
GoDaddy hosting has a history of corrupting his downloadable EXE files, creating reliability concerns
Even small software businesses face the compounding problem of their own bugs plus infrastructure failures
money_quotes: I kind of like the feeling of making more money while spending the evening out than the evening out actually costs.
With the frequency that I break my software I can't afford for my hosting provider to break it, too.
posts.key_insight: A solo software business reaching the point where passive overnight sales exceed your evening's spending is a meaningful early milestone.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a technical gotcha with Inno Setup installer software where shortcuts don't automatically set the working directory to the program directory. This caused quirky behavior for some users because Java's facility for locating the working directory was returning unexpected paths on certain systems. The issue was masked during testing because Inno Setup sets a sensible default when launching directly from the installer. He announces a patch release (1.052) to fix the problem.
key_points: Inno Setup shortcuts don't automatically default the working directory to the program directory on all systems
Cross-platform compatibility fixes (Mac port) introduced an unintended regression on Windows
The bug was hard to catch because the installer itself masked the problem by setting the correct working directory
money_quotes: I had assumed Windows would automatically default to the program directory. This is apparently not the case on some systems.
I hadn't noticed this problem because Inno Setup actually does set a sensible default working directory when you execute the program directly from the installer.
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform compatibility fixes can introduce subtle regressions that are invisible during testing but affect real users, especially when the testing environment masks the problem.

posts.summary: McKenzie predicts he will hit $800-$1,000 in sales for March, which would smash his previous records. He attributes the growth to natural increases in Google traffic and a higher trial-to-purchase conversion rate, noting downloads are up 10%. He observes that order volume has doubled from the typical rate. He also mentions having an Easter promotion planned for April's $1,000 target but suspects organic growth alone may carry him there.
key_points: Organic search traffic growth from Google is driving record sales without additional marketing spend
Conversion rates from trial to purchase improved alongside raw traffic increases, compounding the revenue effect
Order velocity doubled from 2-3 per three business days to 6, suggesting a meaningful inflection point
Having a promotion ready but possibly not needing it illustrates the power of compounding organic growth
money_quotes: I think I'll hit $800 to $1,000 in sales for March, smashing my previous records.
This is on the strength of what appears to just be natural growth in traffic from Google and apparently a higher conversion from trial to purchase rate.
I've gotten 6 orders in the last 3 business days where a more typical number would be 2 to 3.
While I'll still do it, I rather doubt I'll need to just to make that target.
posts.key_insight: Sustained investment in SEO and conversion optimization can produce compounding organic growth that eventually outpaces what promotions alone can deliver.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie notes that by mid-March 2007, his software business profits have already exceeded all of 2006. He projects March will be his best month ever, with sales between $800-$1,000 against roughly $150 in expenses. This very brief post captures an early milestone in the growth of Bingo Card Creator, his first software product.
key_points: By March 13, 2007, cumulative profits for the year had already surpassed all of 2006
Projected monthly sales of $800-$1,000 with ~$150 in expenses, showing strong margins
Physical CD sales were still a meaningful cost driver at this early stage of the business
money_quotes: Its March 13th in Japan and my profits for 2007 just exceeded my profits for 2006.
March is shaping up to be my best month ever.
posts.key_insight: Even at modest absolute numbers, rapid year-over-year growth in a solo software business signals compounding momentum.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie quantifies exactly how much customer support his micro-ISV (Bingo Card Creator) requires, finding it far less burdensome than skeptics assume. Over a six-week period with 600+ installations, 2,000 downloads, and 32 sales, he wrote only 27 emails total. He breaks down the emails by category and calculates his effective hourly rate for support at roughly $370/hour. He argues that even scaling to $100,000 in annual revenue would only mean about 11 emails per day, and suspects support scales sub-linearly with customers.
key_points: Customer support for a simple B2C software product required only 27 emails over six weeks across 600+ installations and 32 sales — about 0.84 emails per sale
The median support email takes 3 minutes to write, and judicious use of auto-text templates makes responses both faster and more consistent for customers
When multiple customers ask about the same issue, the right response is to fix the root cause (better documentation, clearer instructions) rather than just answering each email
Support burden likely scales sub-linearly with customers — closer to constant or logarithmic rather than linear
Customer expectations for support on a $24.95 product are inherently low, which works in the solo founder's favor
money_quotes: If you assume my average is about 4 minutes an email, which is pretty close to accurate, then I am paying myself roughly $370 an hour, give or take, to support Bingo Card Creator.
If it scaled linearly with customers and I was selling 5,000 units a year (income in excess of $100,000 USD) I'd be writing a backbreaking 11 emails a day.
I have strong doubts, incidentally, that support emails scale linearly with customers: my intuition says its actually closer to constant or perhaps logarithmic.

People aren't paying you money so that Direct Access can have a conversation with them. You need to read, understand, and resolve their issue rather than skimming, classifying, and auto-replying to their issue.
When I get multiple inquiries about a single subject I figure out how I can avoid getting them again.
Suffice it to say that no school district has ever contemplated a Service Level Agreement for their mission critical bingo card needs.
I follow the rule that the CS rep should always reply to every email.
posts.key_insight: Customer support for a simple B2C software product is far less burdensome than most people assume, and the real leverage comes from systematically eliminating repeat inquiries rather than just answering them faster.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie created a comprehensive SEO-driven resource page listing free bingo cards available through his product Bingo Card Creator. The page serves as a landing page targeting various long-tail search queries like 'Christmas bingo cards,' 'Harry Potter bingo cards,' and 'sight words bingo cards.' Each section links to free resources while funneling visitors toward downloading the free trial of his software. The post demonstrates his strategy of creating content that matches specific search intent while converting organic traffic into product trials.
key_points: Long-tail SEO content targeting specific bingo card niches (holidays, educational topics) drives organic traffic to his product
Free resources serve as lead magnets that funnel users toward the paid product's free trial
He created an entire separate domain (christmasbingocards.com) for seasonal SEO targeting
The page targets teachers and parents as the core audience, matching their search behavior with ready-made solutions
money_quotes: Google has since picked up on this and that sends a lot of folks here who are looking for many types of bingo cards.
You won't find them in Bingo Card Creator as I have moral qualms about profiting from J.K.Rowling's work.
If you want one which is custom made, you can write one yourself in approximately 3 minutes with the free trial of Bingo Card Creator.
I keep a constantly updated track of which of the 200+ bingo cards are recently the most popular with my customers.
I sort of went hog-wild and made a whole darn website about these.
posts.key_insight: Creating free, search-optimized content pages that target specific long-tail queries is an effective strategy for converting organic search traffic into product trial downloads.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator sales as of mid-March 2007, with 19 sales generating approximately $475 in gross income and $360 in profit. He attributes the success to organic growth and improvements to his AdWords campaign rather than any specific new initiative. He expresses optimism about potentially hitting his $1,000 monthly sales target in March instead of April. He also notes his next project is on hold while he searches for a day job.
key_points: Consistent daily sales replaced the usual feast-or-famine pattern, suggesting growing organic demand
AdWords campaign optimization and fixing demo bugs from the previous month contributed to improved sales
Operating expenses remained low at $108/month, with AdWords being the dominant cost at $90
The $1,000 monthly sales milestone was within reach, potentially a month ahead of schedule
His side project development was deprioritized in favor of day job searching
money_quotes: I'm having my best month ever.
Unlike most months the sales are not in fits and spurts — every day when I wake up its BAM! You've got money!
I attribute this success more to organic growth than anything I have done.
I think by the end of the month I will probably have in excess of $800 worth of sales and possibly, knock on wood, I will make my $1,000 sales target in March rather than in April.
Wow, its been 9 months already… where has the time gone.
posts.key_insight: Consistent organic growth and small optimizations to paid acquisition can compound into milestone months without requiring dramatic new initiatives.

posts.summary: McKenzie alerts readers to a commonly overlooked IRS tax credit called the Telephone Excise Tax Refund, available to Americans who paid for long distance service between 2003 and 2006. He explains the two calculation methods and recommends taking the standard refund since most people don't keep old phone bills. The post walks through the exact lines on the 1040 and 1040EZ where the credit should be entered. He emphasizes that this free money won't appear automatically on your return.
key_points: The IRS offered a Telephone Excise Tax Refund credit for long distance service paid between Feb 2003 and Aug 2006
Standard refund amounts range from $30 to $60 based on number of exemptions claimed
The credit must be manually claimed on your tax return — it won't be added automatically
Taking the standard refund is simpler than gathering old phone bills for exact calculation
money_quotes: Its very easy to forget, particularly if you're doing your own taxes, but the IRS is offering a credit against your taxes (or a refund if you don't owe taxes) if you paid for long distance service in the US.
Don't have your phone bills? Yeah, me neither. So take the standard refund.
It won't be automatically added to your return if you or your tax adviser don't put it there!
posts.key_insight: Small tax credits like the Telephone Excise Tax Refund are easy to overlook but free money if you remember to claim them on your return.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the name of his new LLC, Kalzumeus Software, ending months of referring to his upcoming venture as 'the second project.' He explains his philosophy that product names matter far more than company names for micro-ISVs, since the company is just a legal fiction for interfacing with governments and corporations. The name comes from a talking dragon in a long-running RPG campaign, chosen partly because it was easy to secure as a domain name due to its uniqueness. He outlines plans to transfer Bingo Card Creator to the new LLC and launch a second product under the Kalzumeus banner.
key_points: Product names matter more than company names for micro-ISVs because customers identify with products, not corporate entities
A company is fundamentally a legal fiction for relating to governments and corporations who expect entity-to-entity relationships
Unique, hard-to-spell names have the advantage of easy domain acquisition and zero Google competition
McKenzie plans to transfer Bingo Card Creator to the new LLC and launch a second product simultaneously
money_quotes: I am an ardent proponent of the theory that product names are more important for branding purposes than company names as a uISV.

After all, the company is just a legal fiction designed to give me an easier way to relate government authorities and other corporations who expect entity-to-entity rather than person-to-person relationships.
Almost all of the folks who I'd really want to be interested in my ongoing concern rather than my products identify it by my name.
Its 9 letters long, easy to mispell, and means nothing to most people.
Unless this post has been indexed by the time you read this, its totally unique on Google.
As the dragon was smart, cheeky, and just a little insane I thought that was as good a mascot for a software company as anything.
posts.key_insight: For a micro-ISV, the company name is just legal plumbing — what matters for branding is the product name, since that's what customers actually encounter and remember.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his market selection process for Kalzumeus, his second software product. He identifies a niche in the investment space where small businesses face high-value problems that existing solutions address poorly due to prohibitive startup costs. He plans to build a web application targeting these underserved small fish with simple pricing and lower barriers to entry. His strategic thinking reveals a deliberate shift from hobby software (Bingo Card Creator) toward building a business that could eventually replace full-time employment.
key_points: The best pricing justification comes from saving customers multiples of what you charge — saving $2000 makes a $250 price easy to justify
Fragmented markets with many small competitors are ideal for micro-ISVs because no single player dominates
When potential customers are actively complaining about their problems on forums, that's a strong signal of unmet demand
Simple, transparent pricing can be a competitive advantage when incumbents make customers do math to figure out costs
Targeting customers that incumbents consider too small to bother with creates a viable niche with low competitive pressure
money_quotes: It is far easier to justify someone paying you $250 if you save them $2000 than it is to justify someone paying you $24.95 if you save them $10-15 a few times a year.
Many of the solutions involve calculations on paper, elbow grease, and very expensive professionals doing things that could be done by a trained monkey with a slide rule.

Small businesses or individuals not using these services, instead preferring to fix the problem by themselves, and then complaining about it to anyone who will listen on their forums. Ahh, music to my ears.
Fragmented markets are the uISV's best friend.
There is something about replacing paper and slide-rule totting monkeys with computers that suggests a plausible increase in process efficiency.
The simplicity of 'We have ONE price for ALL customers and are interested in YOUR business even if our competitors think you're too small to be bothered with' might be a decent differentiator.
I could cover my burn rate with about 200 customers and will replace my next job's salary well before hitting a thousand.
posts.key_insight: The most promising micro-ISV opportunities exist where incumbents have high fixed costs that exclude small customers, creating an underserved niche that actively complains about unsolved problems.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovered that a configuration error in his e-junkie payment links had silently broken CD purchases for weeks, despite having his best sales month ever. When he switched item numbers to accommodate SwiftCD integration, he forgot to update the corresponding links on his site. The bug went undetected because most customers simply opted for the download instead, and the failure mode was invisible to him as the seller. He reflects on the difficulty of monitoring opaque third-party system integrations and praises the SwiftCD/e-junkie automation that eliminated manual CD fulfillment work.
key_points: A simple item number mismatch silently broke CD purchases for weeks, costing unknown sales with no visible error on the seller's side
Most customers who encountered the broken CD purchase link likely just bought the download instead, masking the bug's impact
Third-party integrations with opaque internals make bugs hard to predict and detect — regular monitoring is the only defense
Automating CD fulfillment with SwiftCD eliminated repetitive manual work that was disproportionately annoying relative to its actual time cost
money_quotes: I've had my best month of sales ever, but only 1 CD in that time. Typically about half of my customers get the CD.
This is one of those bugs that just makes me want to die inside as a programmer.
The systems involved have well-understood interfaces but the inner workings are complex and totally opaque to me.
Bugs are hard to predict except by seeing them, and if their visibility is obscured by whatever system interaction happens its likely that I'll be the last to know.
I guess the only solution to that is regular monitoring and applying enough concentration to know when the process is out of control.
Minor repetitive nuisances like that grate on me far more than their absolute time required would suggest.
posts.key_insight: Silent failures in third-party integrations can cost you sales for weeks without triggering any alerts, making regular monitoring of your entire purchase flow essential.

posts.summary: McKenzie experiments with Squidoo, a content platform created by Seth Godin, as a tool for organic SEO linkbuilding. He describes the platform as a hybrid between wikis and blogs that allows users to create 'lenses' on specific topics. While he finds the concept promising for SEO since Squidoo lenses rank well on Google, he criticizes the AJAX-heavy editing interface as frustratingly slow and limiting. He creates a lens on teaching dolch sight words, strategically seeding it with links to his own properties. He notes that the platform's most popular content is dominated by spammy make-money-online schemes.
key_points: Squidoo lenses were reaching Google's first page for less competitive keywords, making it a viable SEO linkbuilding tool
The platform's AJAX-heavy interface was a case study in why web apps couldn't yet replace desktop software, with frustrating editing limitations like 2500 character paragraph caps
McKenzie strategically created educational content as a vehicle for SEO-valuable backlinks to his own properties
Despite Seth Godin's vision, Squidoo's popularity metrics were dominated by spammy credit repair and make-money-online content
money_quotes: Squidoo is a wonderful technology for Seth Godin and, well, somewhat less wonderful for its users.
Its also a poster-child for Why AJAX Will Not Replace Your Desktop In 2007.
I felt like I was spending as much time struggling against the platform as I was creating content.
Whats the worst that could happen? I spend an hour producing materials that help teachers and don't get paid for it. That strikes me as an hour well-spent.
I have handily preseeded it with two links controlled by myself, which is the payload of the entire lens for me.
Most of the lenses on top of the popularity metrics are Repair Your Credit And Make Lots of $$$ Online With No Money Down with a very high shady factor.
posts.key_insight: Content platforms designed for user-generated knowledge inevitably become SEO arbitrage tools, useful primarily as linkbuilding vehicles rather than for their stated educational purpose.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that a librarian's email newsletter drove 1,000 visitors to his blog in 24 hours after featuring his customer service post. He reverse-engineers the referral chain from a librarian blog to the newsletter, and uses the unexpected traffic spike to illustrate two points: every software entrepreneur should have a blog, and non-technical professions like librarians represent viable, overlooked software markets.
key_points: A single link in a librarian's email newsletter drove 1,000 visitors in 24 hours, implying a readership in the high five to low six figures
Non-technical professional communities like librarians have their own thriving corners of the internet with large, engaged audiences
Software entrepreneurs systematically underestimate non-technical audiences because they project their own tech-centric worldview onto the market
Having a blog that could reach 1,000 potential customers is achievable and worth pursuing immediately
money_quotes: Given that literally one thousand readers have come to take a gander at that post over the last 24 hours, most attributable to that email newsletter, I would estimate that librarian's reach at probably in the high five to low six digits worth of extraordinarily dedicated readers.

Librarians, like school teachers, are quite smart folks who are not typically described as being on the leading edge of the technology curve. However, apparently there are an awful lot of them out there, and they have their own little pocket of cyberspace which is actually not little at all.
They are an addressable audience on the Internet, they read blogs at least part of the time, and they almost certainly have problems which can be solved by software.
Do you have a blog that 1,000 potential customers of your software could be reading tomorrow? If not, what exactly is keeping you from writing one?
Often uISVs have a mindset which is overly informed by being quite techy people, and they forget that the rest of the world uses computers, too.
Would you have written librarians off as a potential niche for a uISV, because they probably don't buy software anyhow?
posts.key_insight: Software entrepreneurs miss huge markets by assuming non-technical professionals aren't viable customers, when in reality these communities are large, engaged, and reachable through their own thriving online networks.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator, with 30 sales generating $760 gross and $673 net profit in March 2007.
