He notes that accidentally disabling CD orders cost him significant sales, and gives himself 50-50 odds of hitting $1,000 in April. AdWords is performing well at $0.34 per demo download, while SEO gains are bringing organic links from school districts directly to his landing pages. The post also celebrates his blog hitting 100,000 page views and thanks his community of readers.
key_points: March 2007 was his best month ever with $673 net profit on 30 sales, despite accidentally disabling CD orders for most of the month
AdWords cost efficiency reached near-best levels at $0.34 per demo download, but the $24.95 price point limits how aggressively he can bid on keywords
School districts are organically linking to his AdWords landing pages, providing both qualified traffic and SEO authority — the ideal combination of content marketing
His blog reached 100,000 page views and is becoming a meaningful traffic source, competing with MSN and Yahoo for third-largest source of downloads
The thin margin between ad spend and product price creates a hard ceiling on paid acquisition growth for low-priced software
money_quotes: Best month ever. Would have done better had I not disabled CD orders inadvertently for most of it.
There is not too much play in $24.95 to go crazy with keywords costing, e.g., 15 to 25 cents apiece.
They put my software in front of hundreds of prospective customers AND they give me an authority link. Content, its the gift that keeps on giving.
The majority of my readers continue to be uISVs or folks thinking about taking the plunge, but I'm getting increasing mentions from folks as diverse as librarians and customer advocates.
I'm overjoyed and humbled by your support, and couldn't do it without you.
posts.key_insight: At low price points, the economics of paid acquisition create a hard ceiling on growth, making organic channels like SEO and content marketing disproportionately valuable.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports progress on his second software project, Kalzumeus, built with Ruby on Rails. He estimates the project at roughly 15 classes, similar in complexity to Bingo Card Creator. He discusses his IDE choice (Netbeans), the tradeoffs between Ruby and Java development workflows, and his plan to save money by using a free open-source web design template instead of hiring a designer. He also briefly entertains the idea of going full-time on his software business but decides it's too risky for now.
key_points: Kalzumeus is comparable in complexity to Bingo Card Creator (~15 classes) but Rails development trades boilerplate coding time for debugging typos and misnamed variables
He chose Netbeans over Eclipse for Rails development and created custom macros to reduce Rails verbosity
He plans to save hundreds of dollars by using a free OSWD template instead of hiring a UI designer, with the option to upgrade later if the product succeeds
He calculates he could survive about a year going full-time on his software business but considers it too risky as a primary plan
He draws an insightful comparison between Java/Ruby (optimizing for maintenance programmer comprehension) versus Perl (optimizing for creation programmer keystroke efficiency)
money_quotes: Much more code is actually sticking to the screen and its been a matter of hours since I did a rm -rf * on the project directory.
I've noticed so far that I'm spending much, much less time on nuts&bolts programming than I typically do in Java, and much, much more time on chasing down misnamed variables, typos, and the like.
I do generally like the idea of prioritizing maintenance programmer brainsweat over creation programmer finger time.
There will probably be exactly one class which will require anything close to cleverness while coding.
I actually have a perverse affection for concurrency issues. They're one of the only places where I'm a halfway decent programmer.
I also have found a way to save myself a few hundred dollars for launch: cut out the UI designer!
If Kalzumeus takes off then I can always hire myself a designer later to make it look prettier and more unique.
The downside is that if I failed I would be poor, unemployed, and on the wrong side of the ocean for finding convenient work opportunities.
posts.key_insight: Early-stage software entrepreneurs should minimize upfront costs and launch with 'good enough' solutions—free templates, simple architecture—while keeping a conventional income safety net until the business proves itself.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports progress on his side project Kalzumeus, having built user authentication, account creation, and login functionality in Ruby on Rails during a sick half-day off work. He discusses the tradeoffs of rolling your own authentication versus using plugins, touches on web security concerns like XSS and SQL injection, and reflects on his struggle to adopt test-driven development. The post also highlights his appreciation for Rails scaffolding as a development tool and ends with a cultural aside about Japanese adoption of English gaming terminology.
key_points: Building authentication from scratch felt easier than evaluating and adapting existing Rails plugins, even though it violates the principle of not writing code others have already written
Web security requires active vigilance — SMTP is inherently insecure for transmitting credentials, and XSS/SQL injection vulnerabilities need systematic testing on every form
Rails scaffolding serves as an effective admin interface during development, preferable to console-based data inspection
Test-driven development is a habit he recognizes as valuable but struggles to adopt, having relied on manual run-and-fix cycles
Side projects can serve dual purposes: building a business while developing skills applicable to day job advancement
money_quotes: One would think that since essentially every webapp needs this functionality there would be an easy, non-repetitive way to do it in Ruby but since the precise specifics differ for every application I think its probably easiest to roll your own.

Web application developers who allow that or its close cousin an email with your user name and password written in it need to have 'SMTP is not secure' carved onto their eyeballs so that they will not forget.

As for cross-site scripting and SQL injection attacks, well, put it this way: I'm afraid, very afraid, and will be creating a test script whose only purpose is to try both on every form and see what pops out.
Testing for me has always been 'Run, see what breaks, fix, repeat'. This is one of the bad habits I hope to fix by doing this project.
No reason you can't SkillUp for your day job while working for your uISV.
posts.key_insight: Even experienced developers face the recurring tension between building authentication from scratch versus using existing solutions, because the specifics of every application make generic solutions harder to adapt than to rebuild.

posts.summary: McKenzie reviews CrazyEgg, a visual analytics tool that competes with Google's free offerings. While he praises CrazyEgg's exceptional usability and heatmap visualization, he notes that Google Analytics and Google Website Optimizer already provide the core functionality for free. He highlights the fundamental challenge of competing with a free product from Google, even when your user experience is significantly better. The post also explains why proper A/B split testing (randomized allocation) is superior to sequential testing, especially for businesses with cyclical traffic patterns.
key_points: CrazyEgg's onboarding is remarkably smooth — email, password, two-line form, one line of HTML, and you're running in 30 seconds — setting a usability standard Google should learn from
Competing with free Google products is an existential challenge: even superior UX struggles to justify a paid subscription when the free alternative already works

Proper A/B testing requires randomized splitting of concurrent visitors, not sequential batches, because cyclical business patterns (like teacher traffic dropping on weekends) will skew sequential results
Google Website Optimizer has the right methodology (true randomized splitting) but is so painful to use that McKenzie has procrastinated for months on implementing it
money_quotes: Put in email address, put in password, fill out two line form about page to test, copy one line of provided HTML into page, upload. 30 seconds or less to the shiny.

I have already climbed Mt. Analytics Learning Curve and can now bask in the awesome view — such as dicing visitors down by where they came from, watching search terms trends, and tracking click paths around my site. For free.
It comes down to the 'Do I really want to pay more than my web hosting budget for an analytics program?' question. Which would be 'Oh, heck yes!'… if I didn't already have Analytics.

I have been meaning to start using Website Optimizer for months but every time I do I get to the start of the process, figure its going to take me two hours to get anywhere, and then get distracted by other demands on my time.

If they could just bring their considerable usability chops to solving the biggest problem with Website Optimizer, which is that it is a freaking pain in the keister to use, I'd give them $9 a month and a gold star.
posts.key_insight: A paid product can have vastly superior usability and still lose to a free competitor that's already entrenched — but there's a real opportunity in making powerful-but-painful free tools actually usable.

posts.summary: Patrick describes a common usability problem with software screenshot pages: customers click thumbnail images and get lost viewing the full-size image, unable to navigate back. He notes his screenshots had 80%+ bounce rates until he addressed this. He recommends Lightbox, a JavaScript widget that overlays the screenshot on the current page with a grayed-out background, keeping the sales context visible. He chose the simpler Lightbox over Lightbox2, which he found to be a usability nightmare for his non-technical audience.
key_points: Screenshot thumbnails on software sites exist more to prove a real product exists than to convey detailed information
Non-technical customers often cannot navigate back from a full-size screenshot image, causing 80%+ bounce rates
Opening screenshots in new windows is a compromise but distracts from the sales pitch and risks losing the customer entirely
Lightbox overlays screenshots while keeping the underlying page visible, maintaining sales context and enabling easy dismissal
Simpler tools often beat feature-rich alternatives when your audience is non-technical
money_quotes: These are generally teeny-tiny thumbnails which exist more to demonstrate the fact that there is a real purchasable product than anything else.
For the non-technical B2C market, 'bad stuff' generally means 'Prospective customer cannot find their way out of the image'.
Yes, I know, I know, they have a back button. They may not KNOW they have a back button, though.
My screenshots, for the longest time, had 80%+ bounce rates until I figured this out.
I'd rather they have a visual reminder of what exactly they were doing so they don't get distracted, switch to their email, and go away.
posts.key_insight: Every UI element on your sales page should keep customers moving toward purchase, and even something as mundane as screenshot presentation can cause massive bounce rates if it breaks the customer's flow.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie demystifies tax filing for micro-ISV (uISV) owners, arguing that the process is far simpler than most aspiring entrepreneurs fear. He walks through his actual 2006 tax forms for Bingo Card Creator, showing that only four forms were needed and the entire process took about three hours. His Schedule C-EZ shows $2,480 in gross receipts, $1,225 in expenses, and $1,255 in net profit, with self-employment tax of $177. He acknowledges that complex situations like AMT or home offices would warrant hiring an accountant, but for most small software businesses, taxes are straightforward.
key_points: Filing taxes for a small software business requires only a few basic forms (Schedule C-EZ, Schedule SE, 1040) and simple arithmetic
The IRS is actually 'decently taxpayer-focused' and forms are designed for a sixth-grade reading level, contrary to popular fear
Patrick's entire 2006 tax preparation for Bingo Card Creator took only three hours including research and data collection
Fear of tax complexity is an unnecessary barrier that stops people from starting small software businesses
Profitable uISVs should expect to pay estimated taxes in their second year of business
money_quotes: Many folks think you have to be superman to navigate the IRS' maze, when they are actually decently taxpayer-focused and charged by regulations to make things easy for someone with a six grade reading level.
Reports of the impossibility of taxes have been greatly exaggerated.
When I do, I'll pay an accountant to have the headache on my behalf.
Its more aimed at allaying the fears of folks who are thinking of starting a uISV, but worried that the IRS will steal their lunch money after making them fill out 10,000 pages of forms.

On the outside, counting the two blog posts, data collection, downloading forms, and reading up on the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion that I have to do every year, my 2006 taxes took me three hours to prepare.
posts.key_insight: Tax filing for a small software business is far simpler than most people fear, requiring only basic arithmetic and a few forms, and should not be a barrier to starting a business.

posts.summary: Patrick briefly shares that he nailed a job interview, noting that his current boss's relationship with the prospective employer and a former coworker's hiring authority helped. He uses this as a springboard to make a broader point about professional networking: the surprisingly high probability that people in related fields will cross paths again is reason enough to maintain impeccable professional conduct at all times.
key_points: Personal connections and prior working relationships significantly influence hiring outcomes
The importance of networking in hiring is common in both Japan and America, though Americans tend to downplay it
The high likelihood of re-encountering people in your field is a strong practical argument for being consistently polite and fair
money_quotes: The freakishly high chances that two people in a related field will again do business with each other are as good a reason as anything to be faultlessly polite and fair in all of your business dealings.
posts.key_insight: The professional world is small enough that you should treat every business interaction as if you'll encounter that person again — because you probably will.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a small JavaScript enhancement to the Lightbox library that allows users to dismiss images by pressing the escape key. He encountered browser compatibility issues between Firefox and IE when implementing this feature and shares the fix publicly. The post documents specific JavaScript quirks around keyboard event handling across browsers. It's a brief, practical technical note aimed at other developers using Lightbox on their sites.
key_points: Added escape key dismissal to the Lightbox image preview library
IE and Firefox handle keyboard events differently: IE uses integer keyCodes while Firefox requires DOM constants like event.DOM_VK_ESCAPE
IE captures non-printing keys in keypressed events while Firefox only captures them in keydown events
McKenzie shares the fix publicly to save other developers the debugging time
money_quotes: Doing that required working around some compatibility quirks in Firefox versus IE, so to save anyone else the trouble of doing it you can just grab it from my site.
IE will let you test a keyCode against an integer, including a non-printing ASCII code such as 27 (the escape key). Firefox requires you to test against a constant.
IE captures non-printing keyboard keys (other than F1-F12, apparently) in the keypressed event, but Firefox only captures them in the keydown event.
posts.key_insight: Cross-browser JavaScript compatibility issues, even for simple features like keyboard event handling, require careful attention to each browser's distinct event model.

posts.summary: McKenzie dissects a case study where an RSS reader developer's aggressive product activation system and accusatory customer service email turned a deliriously happy customer into a vocal public critic. The post traces how a paying customer who actively recommended the product was flagged by activation code, then received an email implying license breach, leading to a Digg-amplified PR disaster. McKenzie argues the developer had multiple opportunities to avoid the situation: using better activation methods, accepting some key abuse as a cost of business, or simply responding with trust and grace. He provides example emails showing how to handle activation concerns without accusation, demonstrating that assuming good faith costs nothing while suspicion can cost everything.
key_points: Product activation systems that punish legitimate customers are a bigger business risk than the piracy they aim to prevent
Customer service emails should lead with trust and helpfulness, never with accusation or legal-sounding language like 'breach of license'
When you've wronged a customer publicly, the recovery must be proportional: offer a refund, a free license, and an apology rather than doubling down
Words like 'suspiciously' and 'breach of license' in customer communications are relationship-ending moves that can cascade into PR disasters
The same factual concern (multiple activations) can be raised as a helpful inquiry rather than an accusation, with dramatically different outcomes
money_quotes: I find money salves ego pretty well. Its like a cold compress made of cash, and without good customer service you have less cash to compress!
The author could have accepted that some keys get abused as a cost of doing business.
If you absolutely must continue, let trust be your watchword.

Insinuating that if there is anyone to blame it is probably you costs you absolutely nothing but defuses a potentially volatile situation a lot more than words like suspiciously and breach of license.
I don't know what happens next but its sure not '… Profit!'
posts.key_insight: Treating paying customers as suspected pirates costs far more in lost goodwill and public reputation than any piracy ever would, so default to trust in every customer interaction.

posts.summary: McKenzie compares CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking with Google Analytics' site overlay for understanding user behavior on his Bingo Card Creator website. He discovers that Analytics' per-URL click tracking was giving him fundamentally misleading data, while CrazyEgg's per-coordinate tracking revealed that his screenshot image was getting clicked by 33% of visitors—a catastrophic bounce risk—and that a text link he thought was performing well had zero clicks. Based on these insights, he installed Lightbox to prevent screenshot bounces, planned to rewrite his page copy, and concluded that CrazyEgg's $9/month price was trivially justified by the conversion improvements it enabled.
key_points: Google Analytics' per-URL click tracking can produce misleading overlay data by combining clicks from different page elements that link to the same URL
CrazyEgg's coordinate-based heatmap revealed that 33% of visitors clicked the screenshot image, which was bouncing them off the page—a problem invisible in Analytics
A text link McKenzie thought was performing moderately well actually received zero clicks out of two thousand visitors, masked by Analytics combining it with a nearby button linking to the same URL
Small, cheap tools that provide genuinely different data perspectives can justify themselves quickly through even marginal conversion improvements
Understanding the technical implementation differences between analytics tools (per-URL vs per-coordinate tracking) is essential to correctly interpreting their output
money_quotes: The test has showed me several places where my website was broken where I thought it was working perfectly.
The download a free trial link in the first paragraph went totally ignored — not one click out of two thousand visitors!

The screenshot in the middle performs extraordinarily well — almost 33% of visitors to the page will at least click it to see what it looks like! This was catastrophically bad news for me when I heard it.
Clicking a screenshot is the quickest way to bounce a prospect, since so many of my visitors have limited web-navigation-fu.
Take a look at how many people are banging on the New button in my screenshot. Yeah. Believe it or not, I've gotten emails about that before — 'The New button doesn't work.'

Analytics treats that blue button and the first paragraph 'download the free trial' link as the same place, so it munges their stats together when displaying them, which makes it not-obvious that the Big Blue Button is an awesome success and that the first paragraph text link is a crushing failure.
All I would need to do is decrease my bounce rates anywhere in the funnel by about 1-5% and it would pay for itself.
posts.key_insight: Different analytics tools can tell fundamentally different stories about user behavior due to their underlying tracking mechanisms, and choosing the wrong one—or not understanding how it works—can leave you optimizing based on faulty data.

posts.summary: McKenzie reviews ByteCommerce, a digital download service that pitched him via email, and finds its supposedly innovative pricing structure uncompetitive. The service takes every 20th sale as its fee, effectively a 5% commission, which McKenzie calculates is more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month for anyone making over $100/month. He also identifies a practical problem with refunds on sales collected by a third party. The post concludes with broader lessons about why competing on price alone is dangerous without a structural cost advantage.
key_points: ByteCommerce's 'every 20th sale' pricing is effectively a 5% commission, which is more expensive than e-junkie's flat $5/month for most real businesses
Trying to compete with established firms only on price in software is suicidal, especially when you aren't actually cheaper
Being the cheapest is only a competitive moat if you have a structural advantage that lets you always remain the cheapest
The pricing model creates practical problems like the inability to easily refund charges collected by a third party
money_quotes: Trying to compete with established firms only on price in software is kind of suicidal.
I don't choose business partners based on innovative pricing, I choose them based on cheap pricing, if I consider pricing at all.
"We're the cheapest!" is only a competitive moat to the extent that you have some sort of structural advantage that will let you always remain the cheapest, like Wal-Mart does.
Pricing is not good ground to pick a fight on in selling software.
$5 a month is less than 5% of gross sales for any number of gross sales greater than $100 a month, and thats been me since the second month I was in business.

He at least went to the trouble to get my name right and connect the sales pitch tangentially to my main concern, so I'll charitably overlook the unsolicited nature of the email and comment on the product. Unfortunately, its not going to be a very positive comment.
posts.key_insight: Competing on price only works as a strategy if you have a structural cost advantage that ensures you can always be the cheapest; otherwise, a flat-fee competitor will inevitably undercut any percentage-based pricing model as customers scale.

posts.summary: Patrick describes his busiest customer support day, handling two issues: a customer who couldn't receive their registration key despite multiple attempts, and a CD that was never delivered. He shares his approach to handling shipping errors—absorbing the cost to maintain goodwill—and briefly notes that April sales were hurt by Easter but are recovering toward a potential new record.
key_points: Customer input errors are the leading cause of non-delivery, and support should diplomatically verify addresses rather than assign blame
Absorbing the cost of shipping mistakes is worthwhile because it builds customer goodwill and amounts to a rounding error in overall business costs
Even a tiny software business requires real customer support effort that can't always be handled in a five-minute email
Seasonal events like Easter can significantly impact sales for a small software business
money_quotes: I had not one but two issues that couldn't be taken care of in a five minute email. Oh no!

Customer error in the input field is the #1 cause of non-delivery, by far, so any time you get a report of non-delivery you should suggest in a non-confrontational way 'Could you just confirm your address for me so I can send this out?'
Yes, at my expense. Yes, that will eat up most if not all of the profit from this order.
Shipping bloopers fall into rounding errors in the greater scheme of things and you earn so much customer goodwill by addressing them promptly at cost.

My birthday is April 16th, which after adjusting for timezone issues means that I was karaoke-ing until the break of dawn when I 'should have' been telling you that sales for the first half of the month were crushed by Easter.
posts.key_insight: When shipping errors happen in a small software business, eating the cost to make it right is trivially cheap compared to the customer goodwill it generates.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a brief progress update on his Kalzumeus side project, noting it has reached 25% feature completion but is far from being user-ready. The interface exists as functional skeletons but needs significant polish before public exposure. He plans to reveal the project publicly at 50% completion, potentially within a week. He mentions spending Japan's Golden Week holiday coding and writing resumes rather than vacationing.
key_points: Feature completeness and user-readiness are different metrics — having the right form elements doesn't mean the product is ready for users
McKenzie planned to go public with the project concept at 50% completion rather than waiting for a full launch
He was balancing side project development with job searching (writing resumes) during his holiday time
money_quotes: Its certainly 25% feature complete but its not 25% of the way ready to actually have users.
I have functional skeletons up for most of it but they are too ugly to ever show to people, even if they do have the right form elements on them.
Its Golden Week and this year instead of taking a well-deserved vacation I'll be coding and writing resumes, bleh.
posts.key_insight: Feature completion percentage is misleading because having functional code is only a fraction of the work needed to make software ready for actual users.

posts.summary: Patrick discovers Postful, a service that converts emails into physical postal mail, solving a key feature requirement for his Kalzumeus webapp. He had been planning to use Japan Post's NetPost API for automated letter sending, a feature his customers need for legal compliance and would find highly valuable. Postful's simpler interface (send an email, they mail a letter) at a lower cost ($0.99 vs $2.25) eliminates the need to integrate another complex API. The simplicity means the feature can ship in the 1.0 release rather than being deferred to 1.1.
key_points: Automated physical mail sending has legal significance for some customers and obvious value for all of them
Building vs. buying: doing it yourself transforms a highly-paid developer into a low-paid postal clerk
API complexity is a real cost — each new integration is another failure point with potentially severe consequences
Simpler integrations (email-based vs. full API) can accelerate feature delivery from future releases into the current one
The per-unit cost of enabling a feature is often dwarfed by the sales it generates
money_quotes: Obviously, doing it myself would transform me from a highly-paid software developer to a low-paid postal clerk, which is frankly stupid when there are plenty of businesses out there which offer ways to do it without ever having to lick a stamp.
I really hate having to learn another API. It is one more thing that can go wrong with my application, and the consequences of a letter not being sent when my app reports it sent are rather severe.
$2.25 is dwarfed by the number of sales this will get me, and I can probably charge extra for it for customers who use it on a regular basis.
All of my customers would see the ability and go 'Oooh, that would save me so much hassle'.
Composing an email to my requirements is trivial, since I already have the exact same functionality elsewhere in the program to deliver this notification via email.
posts.key_insight: The best technical solution isn't always the most powerful API — a simpler integration that reuses existing code can ship faster, cost less, and reduce failure modes.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his process of upgrading the Buy Now and Download Free Trial buttons on Bingo Card Creator's website. He purchased Axialis Icon Workshop on community recommendations to create Web 2.0-style buttons, replacing his earlier Paint.NET creations. The post shows before-and-after comparisons and frames the redesign as an A/B test to see if newer button styles convert better. It's a brief, practical update typical of his early blogging about incremental improvements to his software business.
key_points: Upgraded call-to-action buttons from basic Paint.NET designs to polished Web 2.0 style using Axialis Icon Workshop
Framed the visual redesign explicitly as a conversion experiment to measure whether new buttons sell better
Leveraged community recommendations (Business of Software forum) for tooling decisions
Even small UI elements like buttons were treated as optimization opportunities worth investing in
money_quotes: I decided to redo my sort of frumpy Buy Now! and Download Free Trial buttons for Bingo Card Creator, and see if the new versions aren't any more successful.
posts.key_insight: Even minor visual elements like purchase buttons deserve deliberate design investment and testing, because they sit at the critical point of conversion.

posts.summary: McKenzie demonstrates the business value of blogging for micro-ISVs by sharing concrete results from a single blog post. His 'Free Bingo Cards' post generated nearly 1,000 hits in one month, converting to about 100 trials and 5 sales—roughly 20% of his monthly revenue. He emphasizes that the post is 'evergreen' content that continues generating traffic and sales indefinitely through search engines and sharing.
key_points: A single well-targeted blog post generated 20% of monthly sales two months after publication
Evergreen content compounds in value over time through search engine hits, links, and word-of-mouth sharing
The conversion funnel from blog post was roughly 1,000 hits → 100 trials → 5 sales (10% trial rate, 5% conversion)
Blogging creates a durable marketing asset that 'never goes bad' unlike paid advertising
money_quotes: That post is an evergreen — it never goes bad.
It continues to pick up search engine hits, links, emails to friends, yadda yadda, and keeps making me money.
This resulted in ~100 trials of my program and 5 sales, or roughly 20% of my sales for the month.
posts.key_insight: A single piece of evergreen blog content can become a compounding marketing asset that generates a significant share of ongoing revenue with zero marginal cost.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts how he originally considered naming his software 'Teacher's Pet' before settling on 'Bingo Card Creator' after finding better domain names taken. He discovers that teachers-pet.org has linked to his site, driving steady referral traffic that converts at 14%, nearly matching his organic search conversion rate. The post reflects on the value of unsolicited inbound links from relevant communities.
key_points: McKenzie's naming journey went from Teacher's Pet to Bingo Card Maker to Bingo Card Creator, largely driven by domain availability
An unsolicited backlink from a small but engaged community site drove traffic comparable to his paid AdWords campaign
The referral traffic converted at 14%, close to organic search traffic, suggesting highly relevant audience targeting
Small niche community sites with engaged users can outperform their PageRank in actual referral value
money_quotes: I have since learned about good practices for naming B2C software and that idea strikes me as extraordinarily silly now.

Apparently despite the PR2 they've got quite a number of regular users, because they put up a link to me about a week ago and its getting fairly steady traffic at about the same level as my AdWords campaign, and converting pretty well, too.
Its always nice when folks take time out of their day to do your marketing for you.
posts.key_insight: Unsolicited links from small but highly relevant community sites can drive traffic and conversions on par with paid advertising, making organic community presence invaluable for niche software businesses.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports on the results of changing the icons/buttons on his Bingo Card Creator website. The new download button saw a 1% decrease in clicks (from 9.5% to 8.5%), but text links compensated to keep overall conversion steady. The Buy Now button saw a significant increase from 1% to 3% click-through. He notes uncertainty about whether the improvement came from the visual redesign or the copy change from 'Purchase Now' to 'Buy Now!'
key_points: New download trial button decreased clicks by ~1%, but text links picked up the slack keeping overall conversion constant
Buy Now button clicks tripled from 1% to 3%, a significant improvement
He can't isolate whether the gain came from the visual redesign or the copy change from 'Purchase Now' to 'Buy Now!'
This reflects early-stage A/B testing habits before he had more rigorous split-testing infrastructure
money_quotes: 2% more people (1 -> 3) are clicking the Buy Now! button.
I'm not entirely sure whether that is a result of just the redesigned buttons or the result of changing Purchase Now to Buy Now! but either way, what can I say, I like it.
posts.key_insight: Small changes to button design and copy can meaningfully move conversion metrics, though isolating which variable caused the change requires more disciplined testing.

posts.summary: Patrick reports April 2007 stats for Bingo Card Creator, showing 26 sales and $544 profit after expenses. Easter week severely hurt sales with only one purchase during the holiday. He breaks down traffic sources, noting AdWords visitors convert best while organic Google traffic converts poorly despite being the largest segment. He observes the long tail effect where roughly 30% of Google traffic and downloads come from the top 10 search terms, with the rest spread across hundreds of niche queries.
key_points: Holiday periods like Easter cause dramatic drops in sales for education-focused software, similar to Christmas vacation effects
AdWords visitors convert at much higher rates than organic Google traffic, despite organic being the largest traffic segment
Google Analytics attribution is flawed because users who download the trial and return later get classified as 'direct' traffic rather than credited to their original source
The long tail of search is significant: only 30% of Google traffic comes from the top 10 terms, with the remaining 70% from hundreds of niche queries
Content network advertising worked surprisingly well, while advertising on a competitor's site did not
money_quotes: Week-long holidays like Easter hurt like crazy — I had exactly one sale during Holy Week.
Advertising directly on a competing site did not. Constant improvement on the main campaign did. Content network did, to my surprise.
I generally make most of my decisions based on the G2 column (i.e. free trials downloaded).
I perform fairly poorly in terms of conversion rates on the high traffic queries like 'bingo cards' and very well on long tail queries.
In a very Long Tail-esque moment, I get about 30% of my Google hits and trial downloads from the top 10 search terms.

Probably one third of my downloaders are from the top 10 search terms, one third are from less common but still fairly popular search terms, and one third are looking for a phrase that only exists for them.
posts.key_insight: For niche software businesses, the long tail of search queries collectively drives as much value as the obvious high-volume terms, and understanding attribution across the customer journey matters more than raw traffic numbers.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie redesigned the download page for Bingo Card Creator during a bout of insomnia. He replaced automatic downloading with explicit PC vs Mac trial download buttons and added Analytics tracking to measure the impact. The post is a brief late-night update sharing the change and asking for reader feedback.
key_points: Replaced automatic downloads with explicit platform-specific buttons for PC and Mac trials
Hooked up the new download page to Analytics to measure whether the change improves conversions
Demonstrates the iterative, data-driven approach to optimizing even small details of the sales funnel
money_quotes: I've disabled automatic downloading, given the users big bright buttons to find the PC vs Mac trials, and hooked it up to Analytics to see if I'm insane.
posts.key_insight: Every page in the conversion funnel deserves deliberate design and measurement, even the download confirmation page.

posts.summary: McKenzie reacts negatively to Google Analytics' redesigned interface, finding it less usable than the previous version. The new widgetized reporting format buries information that was previously accessible through straightforward drill-downs. He specifically struggles to answer basic business questions like month-over-month conversion rate comparisons. He hopes to eventually master the new interface but is frustrated by the productivity loss in the meantime.
key_points: Google Analytics' redesigned interface prioritizes a trendy Web 2.0 widgetized format over practical usability
Key business metrics like conversion rate comparisons became harder to access after the redesign
Redesigns that force users to relearn workflows impose real productivity costs on businesses
money_quotes: Information which was previously available through drilling down is apparently being forced into a new widgetized reporting format, which might be great but seems like it has a learning curve steeper than Mt. Everest.

Getting to the information which is of paramount interest to me, like "Am I doing better this month than last month on Google, in terms of conversion rates?" seems to be much more difficult than it used to be.
posts.key_insight: A product redesign that makes core workflows harder to accomplish is a step backward, regardless of how modern the new interface looks.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovered that a usability bug in his e-junkie shopping cart was silently destroying CD sales by requiring an unintuitive extra step (clicking 'update cart' before checkout) that 94% of interested buyers failed to complete. The cart displayed error messages when customers with shipping items clicked checkout without first entering their zip code and updating, causing massive abandonment at each step. He traced the problem through conversion data showing CD order rates had plummeted from 50% to single digits. After reporting the issue to e-junkie, they pushed a fix to all their hosted carts. He also notes the counterintuitive finding that a stepped checkout process (choose product, then choose payment) converts better than presenting all options at once, due to decision paralysis.
key_points: A two-step cart interaction requirement (update cart, then checkout) caused 94% abandonment among customers who wanted to buy CDs
Users don't read error messages — roughly half abandon at the first error, and 80% of the remainder fail to follow the corrective instructions
Small usability friction in checkout funnels compounds multiplicatively, turning minor issues into catastrophic revenue losses
A stepped decision process (choose product first, then payment method) outperforms presenting all options simultaneously, likely due to decision paralysis
Hosted web apps allow vendors like e-junkie to push fixes globally, benefiting all users at once
money_quotes: You've got to sand down the rough edges in your checkout funnel or they'll bleed you to death.
I lose NINETY FOUR PERCENT of the people who have expressed interest in buying a CD.
So you click on the checkout button and are instantly whisked to… an error message.

Like the old public service announcement said, reading is fundamental. You have to click update cart then click checkout. Only 20% of the people who reach this stage of the game are capable of completing those two steps in order.
If, however, you offer a bewildering array of options at the start of the process, customers can get decision paralysis.
You get one choice to make, you make it, and after you've committed to that the virtual salesman gives you another minor little prompt and we're off to the races.
Clearly, this is a suboptimal state of affairs for me.
posts.key_insight: Minor usability friction in checkout flows compounds multiplicatively — each small confusion point doesn't just lose some customers, it cascades to destroy nearly all conversions.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his iterative process of redesigning download buttons for Bingo Card Creator, focusing on pixel-perfect alignment and adding explanatory text for non-technical users. He describes spending significant time on per-pixel adjustments to ensure visual consistency between button variants. The post captures the obsessive attention to detail required when optimizing conversion elements for unsophisticated users. He also candidly shares mistakes made during the process, including a flopped layout he didn't catch initially. The post illustrates how seemingly trivial UI elements can consume disproportionate effort when precision matters for conversions.
key_points: Adding redundant explanatory text to buttons helps non-technical users understand clickable elements
Pixel-perfect alignment between button variants requires disproportionate effort but matters for professional appearance
Using the right tools (layers in image editors) dramatically reduces time spent on precise visual composition
Small UI details like an embossed cursor icon on a button will attract a significant percentage of clicks
money_quotes: When your customers are not the technically sharpest knives in the drawer, sometimes I think a little redundancy can be a good thing.
I guarantee you 25% of the clicks on the button will hit the cursor.
When you need images to line up perfectly, you need layers.
Eight pixels is hard to see!
I sometimes get tunnel vision for little details.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing conversion elements for non-technical users requires obsessive attention to visual details and redundant cues that technical people would consider unnecessary.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive playbook for micro-ISV developers looking to increase software sales beyond their initial launch. He covers seven key strategies: installing analytics, SEO optimization, link building through valuable content, blogging with evergreen content, eliminating checkout barriers, offering money-back guarantees, and optimizing AdWords campaigns. The advice is grounded in his own experience selling Bingo Card Creator and emphasizes writing content in the language customers actually use rather than marketing jargon. He stresses that improving sales is a long-term iterative process of testing and observation, not a quick fix.
key_points: Write content using the exact language your customers use in search queries rather than corporate marketing speak — matching their natural language is the key to SEO success
Create evergreen content that remains valuable for years rather than chasing trending topics; today's labor should pay dividends every day thereafter
Sites don't attract links — content attracts links; create genuinely useful resources for your niche that people want to share with colleagues
Eliminate every friction point in checkout: offer multiple payment processors, display credit card logos, deliver instantly, and make sure customers know the process is fast and easy
Improving sales is an iterative long-term process of testing and observation, not something you can accomplish in a single burst of effort
money_quotes: Congratulations on finishing an application that real people think was worth spending money on. That's an accomplishment. It is also the easiest part about running a software business.
Write about what your customers care about in language similar to what they use.
If you write your page like they write their search queries, you win.
Nobody writes 'new paradigm in best-of-breed B2C customer empowerment synergies' in Google!
Sites don't attract links. Content attracts links.
Writing evergreens is like investing in yourself — it is a way for today's labor to pay dividends tomorrow and every day thereafter.
You don't get the benefits of having your process be instant if you don't mention them to your customers before checkout!
Consistent sustained improvement is the key to long-term success.
posts.key_insight: Increasing software sales is fundamentally about creating genuinely useful content in the language your customers actually use, then systematically removing every barrier between discovery and purchase.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides updates on several fronts: his SEO article ranked #2 on Google for a competitive search term within 48 hours, demonstrating the blogging-for-SEO strategy he advocates. He discusses redesigning website icons after trademark guideline concerns, a CrazyEgg/e-junkie compatibility bug affecting his sales funnel, and shares good news about a promising job interview with a Japanese software company. The post illustrates the day-to-day reality of running a micro-ISV while job hunting in Japan.
key_points: A blog post targeting 'increase software sales' reached Google's #2 position in 48 hours, proving the SEO-through-blogging strategy works even for competitive terms with 30 million results
WordPress's URL structure, title tags, and keyword repetition combined with high-pagerank inbound links drove rapid Google ranking gains
He redesigned purchase page icons after being told they violated Microsoft and Apple trademark guidelines, noting the guidelines are broader than what the law actually requires
A JavaScript incompatibility between e-junkie's cart and CrazyEgg was potentially costing him money, showing how tool conflicts in the sales funnel can silently hurt revenue
His job search in Japan was progressing well, with a strong interview at a 250-person company that valued both his Java skills and his side-project web development experience
money_quotes: I predicted I would be on the front page of Google for the string 'increase software sales' within a week. It actually took about 48 hours and I'm number two.
Your niche also has competitive search words which a decently popular blog article could crack fairly quickly.
Its my personal opinion that their guidelines are poorly considered and substantially broader than the rights the law actually gives them, but I'm following them as a professional courtesy.
As the incompatibility was potentially costing me money I disabled CrazyEgg and sent them an email.
They also have dreams of Web 2.0ifying a few of their properties and were extraordinarily pleased to hear that my 'extracurricular interests' had me learning Javascript, Rails, and MVC frameworks.
posts.key_insight: A well-targeted blog post with proper SEO fundamentals can rank on Google's front page for competitive terms within days, demonstrating that content marketing is one of the most accessible growth levers for small software businesses.

posts.summary: Patrick reports mid-month sales stats for May 2007, showing 15 total sales (10 downloads, 5 CDs) generating about $399 in gross revenue and $312 in profit. His recent focus on fixing usability bugs has noticeably boosted sales. CD sales are increasing but cut into margins since he subsidizes each one by about fifty cents. His AdWords campaign is performing exceptionally well at 22 cents per trial download, well below his 30-cent target, making customer acquisition highly profitable.
key_points: Fixing usability bugs directly translated into higher sales, demonstrating the ROI of polish over new features
AdWords cost per trial download dropped to $0.22, meaning each $24.95 sale costs only about $7.30 to acquire — a roughly 3.4x return
CD sales are growing but slightly reduce profit margins due to per-unit subsidization of about fifty cents
Monthly expenses are modest at roughly $88, keeping the business highly profitable relative to revenue
money_quotes: Sales have been nicely juiced by my crusade against usability bugs these last two weeks.
Twenty. Two. Cents.
At $.22 it costs me about $7.30 to buy a $24.95 sale. As you might expect, I'm happy to do that all day long.
I'm trying to tweak the campaign to increase number of clicks now because at $.22 it costs me about $7.30 to buy a $24.95 sale.
Higher CD sales will likely lead to higher absolute sales numbers and slightly lower profits since I subsidize every one by about fifty cents relative to downloads.
posts.key_insight: When your customer acquisition cost is less than a third of your sale price, the rational move is to scale spending aggressively rather than optimize further.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes changing his website's link colors from a custom orange scheme back to the default blue to reduce visitor confusion and lower bounce rates. He notes that non-standard link colors, while aesthetically matching his site, may confuse users accustomed to the convention that blue underlined text is clickable. He also reports that his redesigned download buttons are performing well, with the 'Download Windows Free Trial' button generating the most clicks on his entire site according to CrazyEgg heatmap data.
key_points: Custom link colors that match a site's design can confuse users who expect the standard blue-underlined convention for clickable links
Bounce rate on the front page was higher than desired, motivating the change back to default link colors
Redesigned download buttons are performing well based on CrazyEgg heatmap data, with the free trial button being the hottest click target on the site
Small UI conventions like link color can have measurable impact on user behavior and conversion
money_quotes: I have been told that having them other colors has a tendency to confuse people who are used to the convention that anything blue and underlined can be clicked.
That Download Windows Free Trial button is, incidentally, the most incandescent place anywhere on my website.
posts.key_insight: Respecting established web conventions like default link colors can reduce user confusion and improve click-through rates, even when custom colors look better aesthetically.

posts.summary: McKenzie notes that his education-focused software business (Bingo Card Creator) experiences severe seasonality, with sales dropping to near zero during summer when schools are out of session. He has gone roughly a week without a sale and expects the drought to last until mid-August when the new school term begins. He plans to use the downtime productively by improving his website and marketing. He also mentions needing to find a job by late July.
key_points: Education market software has extreme seasonality — sales virtually stop when school is not in session
The summer sales drought is predictable and lasts roughly three months
Downtime can be used productively for website improvements and marketing work
At this stage McKenzie still needed a day job alongside his software business
money_quotes: One of the unfortunate facts of the education market is that it has pretty severe seasonality: sales drop to close to nothing when school is not in session.
I have had roughly a week without a sale now, and expect that sales will remain severely depressed until roughly August 15th.
Start of the new term means cash money.
Oh well, more time to improve the website, marketing, and there is that minor issue of finding myself a job by July 25th…
posts.key_insight: Education-market software businesses face severe seasonal revenue drops during summer, making it essential to use downtime for improving the product and marketing ahead of the next buying cycle.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that directly marketing to online communities you don't belong to is almost always ineffective and often counterproductive. He compares the return on time from posting ads on forums (1 trial download per hour invested per month) against creating useful content like blog posts and resource pages (which generate 75-200x better returns). The most effective community-driven marketing comes from trusted members recommending your product organically, not from outsiders dropping in advertisements. He counsels that trust within a community is the key asset, and aggressively promoting your product to people who know you will destroy that trust for negligible financial gain. Instead, he recommends focusing on content creation and letting satisfied customers do the marketing for you.
key_points: Marketing directly to a community you're not part of risks violating social norms and is almost certainly a waste of time compared to alternatives
Content creation (blog posts, resource pages) generates 75-200x more trial downloads per hour invested than posting ads on community forums
The most effective community referral in his portfolio was one he didn't write — a trusted community member recommending his product carried far more weight than any outsider post could
Trust is the scarce resource in community marketing: if you have it, spending it on self-promotion destroys it; if you don't have it, building it takes too long relative to better alternatives
Your existing customers are your best marketing team because they are already trusted in communities you can't even access
money_quotes: It is ineffective to directly market to a community which you are not a part of, be that a forum community, a mailing list, or a Usenet group, regardless of what you are selling.

That community has its little social rituals, in-jokes, standards of acceptable behavior, shared history, friendships, rivalries, dramas, a whole tapestry of meaning for its members that you, the outsider, know nothing about.
The reason the link is so effective is because someone who was trusted in their community put their reputation on the line and said 'Hey, check out this site, it will help you educate your children'.
I'm trusted precisely because I have not been a self-promoter for the last three years, and I see no reason to throw that trust away now for a piddling amount of money.
Don't aggressively push your product at anybody who knows you and could possibly use it. That makes you into the Internet equivalent of the Mary Kay lady.
Customers are the first, last, and best marketing team you will ever have. They are already trusted in more communities than you can even conceive of.

Think of that: one hour per trial download per month. By comparison, there are many, many better ways to deploy one hour of my scarce time. One way would be to work at McDonalds, because a trial download is only worth about fifty cents to me.
posts.key_insight: Community trust cannot be manufactured by outsiders, so software marketers should invest their time in creating useful content and serving customers well rather than posting ads in communities they don't belong to.

posts.summary: McKenzie walks through the technical process of renaming a popular web page for SEO purposes, changing 'Free Resources' to 'Printable Bingo Cards.' He shows the bash commands for bulk-updating HTML files, emphasizes the critical importance of 301 redirects to preserve PageRank and user experience, and warns against over-optimizing filenames with keyword stuffing. The post is a practical tutorial wrapped around a real change he made to his own site.
key_points: Renaming pages to match search-friendly keywords (like 'printable bingo cards') is a concrete SEO tactic that costs almost nothing to implement
301 redirects are essential when renaming pages — failing to add them wastes hard-earned PageRank and breaks third-party links
There's a threshold for keyword density in URLs beyond which Google will penalize you, making over-optimization counterproductive
Always resubmit your updated sitemap to Google after making URL changes
money_quotes: If you do not do it, not only are you hurting user experience on third party sites which trusted you with a link, you're squandering PageRank that you've worked so hard to gain.
Adding in another rewrite rule takes like 5 seconds, so do it!
Repetition in the URL gives no useful information to human users, and it is not a 'natural' design technique for a website either.
I suspect that Google will eventually toss it out as a useless SEO technique, the same way they habitually ignore overly-long-file-names-stuffed-with-keywords-like-this-one.htm.
This is a fairly key step for any change you make that affects a popular page.
posts.key_insight: When renaming web pages for SEO, the 301 redirect preserving your existing PageRank and external links is just as important as choosing the better keyword-rich URL.

posts.summary: McKenzie draws parallels between startup life in Columbus, Ohio and Central Japan, noting that both regions share similar challenges of being outside major tech hubs. He references an article about what Silicon Valley could learn from Columbus and finds it directly applicable to his experience in Nagoya. He also teases an upcoming long-form piece about how small independent software vendors can maximize web applications.
key_points: Startup challenges outside Silicon Valley are universal — what applies to Columbus, Ohio also applies to Central Japan
Nagoya is more comparable to Detroit than Silicon Valley as a tech ecosystem
Even well-known web platforms like Craigslist and Mixi have limited awareness outside tech-centric regions
McKenzie is developing ideas about the advantages small software businesses have with web apps
money_quotes: What works in Columbus, Ohio is also very true for life in Central Japan.
Even Nagoya is more of a Detroit than it is a Silicon Valley.
Nobody here knows what Craigslist is, either. Most don't even know Mixi, which is Myspace for Japan.
I suppose I should wait until I've actually got a web app to be giving advice about how to make web apps, though.
posts.key_insight: The challenges and opportunities of building tech businesses outside major hubs are remarkably similar whether you're in the American Midwest or Central Japan.

posts.summary: McKenzie breaks down his organic search traffic into three tiers: competitive head terms, niche long-tail queries he targets deliberately, and 'snowflake queries' — unique, never-repeated searches that account for 60-70% of his organic traffic. He proposes a systematic approach to capturing these snowflake queries by data-mining search logs, clustering similar queries, and writing targeted content pages optimized for clusters of related long-tail searches. Rather than building his own clustering tool, he discovers HitTail, a webapp that already solves this problem. He plans to apply this strategy to his next product, Kalzumeus, which operates adjacent to a highly competitive market space.
key_points: 60-70% of organic search traffic comes from unique 'snowflake queries' that are never repeated by another person, yet collectively they drive more conversions than head terms
Rather than haphazardly sprinkling synonyms, you can systematically data-mine search logs to find clusters of similar snowflake queries and write content targeting 100 variations at once
Search logs reveal vocabulary your customers actually use that you would never guess — like 'bingo boards' instead of 'bingo cards'
The smart move is to pay for existing tools rather than reinventing solutions to hard problems when your competitive advantage lies in domain knowledge, not NLP engineering
When entering competitive markets, targeting snowflake query clusters lets you go around established players rather than competing head-on for expensive keywords
money_quotes: I get a huge percentage of my organic search traffic, about 60-70%, from arbitrary organic search queries. The majority of them are never repeated by another person.
I like to mentally think of these as snowflake queries — every one is unique but if you look at a lot of them at once they certainly look a good deal similar.
Snowflakes account for more than half of my organic search conversions.
Some people call bingo cards 'bingo boards'. Who knew? Certainly not me, as I went through my entire life without hearing that usage, but my search logs do not lie.

I should really pay them the money for the tool and spend my time actually writing the text which will clump up the snowflakes (which only I can do, since I'm the guy who presumably has the domain knowledge) rather than reinventing a solution to a Certified Hard Problem.
Hey, wait a second, I can rank for a snowflake query with a really trivial amount of work. Put query in title, use in body text, don't spam, done.
Time to go around the obstruction rather than running straight into it.
posts.key_insight: The majority of organic search value comes not from competitive head terms but from systematically clustering and targeting the vast long tail of unique 'snowflake' queries that collectively dwarf any single keyword.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his experience setting up hosting infrastructure for his new Kalzumeus project, including purchasing TextDrive hosting and wrestling with SVN repository migration and Rails development quirks. He shares practical observations about infrastructure costs for small software businesses, arguing that the common 10% of revenue guideline overstates what a uISV actually needs. The post chronicles a frustrating evening of tool-wrangling with SVN, Netbeans, and Cygwin, followed by debugging Rails' confusingly similar but subtly different object creation methods. He announces plans to launch a separate blog at kalzumeus.com for programming and business content, keeping his Bingo Card Creator blog customer-focused.
key_points: Infrastructure costs for small software businesses can be far lower than the commonly cited 10% of revenue — closer to $30-50/month even at $10,000 in monthly sales
TextDrive offers far more control than GoDaddy but at significantly higher setup complexity, a tradeoff that matters when running Rails apps with custom server configurations
Rails' syntactic shortcuts like build vs new vs create have deeply meaningful but poorly documented behavioral differences that cost real debugging time
Separating a developer-focused blog from a customer-focused product blog is a deliberate content strategy decision
money_quotes: Its a wonderful age we live in where you can buy 95% of the tools you need to run a business for less than $10 a month.
I'm pretty sure you can get by on about $30~$50 a month for $10,000 in sales as a uISV with some care given to app selection.
These are 100% my doing — I bit off a lot more than I could chew with technologies I do not have a full conceptual understanding of yet.
Rails takes an awful lot of syntatic shortcuts which are deeply meaningful but documented in, I kid you not, exactly one throw away line in The Book.
The emphasis on 'create code whose intent is crystal clear' gets lost in some of these shortcuts.
The difference in the level of control you get for TextDrive is night and day though.
posts.key_insight: Running a small software business requires surprisingly little infrastructure spending, but the real cost is the time lost wrestling with poorly documented tools and frameworks you haven't fully mastered yet.

posts.summary: McKenzie enthusiastically reviews Direct Access, a productivity tool that saves time by replacing multi-step file/program opening sequences with short keyboard abbreviations. He demonstrates that the tool saved him 15 minutes during a 3-hour programming session, roughly a 10% productivity gain. He also highlights the tool's autotext feature for boilerplate code snippets, noting it's less useful in Ruby than Java due to less boilerplate. The post doubles as a self-reminder about salesmanship: showing users concrete ROI data is brilliantly simple marketing.
key_points: Small time savings from keyboard shortcuts compound significantly — 15 minutes saved in a 3-hour session represents a measurable 10% productivity boost
Showing users their own ROI data is brilliantly simple salesmanship, and McKenzie notes he should apply this to his own product's upgrade screen
Ruby/Rails requires far less boilerplate than Java, making code snippet macros less valuable but the program-launching shortcuts still highly useful
When you're the boss, every second of friction has a direct cost — unlike salaried employment where productivity losses are someone else's problem
money_quotes: You wouldn't think shaving a few seconds here and a few seconds there would really matter that much, but the stats don't lie.
I get paid regardless of whether I'm being productive or not at the day job. When I'm the boss, heck, time spent clicking is time spent not doing more important things.
The third time I type the same freaking snippet out it goes in Direct Access with a memorable abbreviation.
Scrape user accounts for Kalzumeus, generate similar ROI-proving eyecandy, and put at the top of the Upgrade Your Account screen. Its brilliantly simple salesmanship.
You can keep your OS X, vim, and bash, I'm puttering around in a deliciously iconoclastic Windows XP… at Unix sysop speeds.
As usual, product placements on this blog are 100% uncompensated. I like it, I use it, it saves me time and money, I blog about it.
posts.key_insight: Small workflow optimizations that save seconds per interaction compound into significant productivity gains, and showing users concrete data about those gains is powerful salesmanship.

posts.summary: Patrick reports his May 2007 financials for Bingo Card Creator, showing $600 in gross sales and $452 in profit after expenses. Sales were flat due to the school year ending, but he teases that June is already performing better than expected. He highlights that switching to e-junkie's cart system significantly boosted CD sales. The post is a brief monthly accounting update with a forward-looking tease about summer traffic trends.
key_points: Monthly revenue hit $600 with 22 sales including 10 CD orders, showing the physical product channel was meaningful
The e-junkie cart change dramatically improved CD sales conversion, demonstrating how small checkout flow changes impact revenue
Total expenses of $138 yielded healthy ~75% profit margins on a micro-scale software business
June was already tracking ahead of May despite expectations of a seasonal slowdown
money_quotes: note that that e-junkie cart change made ALL the difference!
I was expecting sales for June to be barely enough to cover my AdWords campaign. They are already over half of May's.
posts.key_insight: Small changes to the purchase flow, like switching cart systems, can have outsized effects on conversion and revenue even for a tiny software business.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his first-year financial and traffic statistics for Bingo Card Creator in visual form. The business generated $6,333 in sales against $1,720 in expenses for $4,613 in profit, with 63,429 visits over the year. He notes traffic spikes from Slashdot and Joel on Software mentions, and observes that the expected summer traffic trough hasn't materialized yet. The post is characteristically transparent about the limitations of his accounting methods.
key_points: First-year totals: $6,333 sales, $1,720 expenses, $4,613 profit from a solo side project
Blog traffic is roughly double the product site traffic, with occasional spikes from tech community mentions
Seasonal patterns show clear troughs at Christmas, Easter, and US public holidays, but summer hasn't produced the expected dip yet
Cash vs. accrual accounting differences create small discrepancies between blog-reported and IRS-reported numbers
money_quotes: Same disclaimers as normally: these were prepared for a blog post, not the IRS, and I did not exactly go to the extra mile to ensure their accuracy.
The blog gets about twice as many hits as the product site.

The dog that didn't bark is 'Why is Patrick not seeing a trough of visitors (and sales!) now that school is out for summer, much like he saw troughs at Christmas, Easter, and every single US public holiday?'
Again, you're not the IRS, so please don't audit me.
I did the uberscientific 'multiply sales and expenses by two' to estimate income for the month.
posts.key_insight: Radical transparency about business metrics — even modest ones like $4,613 in annual profit from a side project — builds audience trust and creates a valuable record for understanding seasonal patterns and growth trajectories.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie introduces Kalzumeus, a web application designed to simplify property management for small landlords by making rent collection as easy as selling items on eBay. The product targets the underserved market of non-professional landlords who own one or two properties and find existing property management software too complex and expensive. He outlines the feature set, pricing model, competitive landscape, and marketing strategy, emphasizing low startup costs and a path to profitability from the first customer. The post also covers his approach to Ruby on Rails development, demo-driven marketing, and his goal of reaching 300 paying customers to potentially quit his day job.
key_points: The vast majority of property management software targets professionals managing hundreds of units, leaving small landlords with one or two properties underserved
Startup costs were under $250 including a year of hosting, with break-even at just one customer — a deliberate shoestring approach
The product leverages PayPal for payment processing, trading higher transaction fees (2.9%) for dramatically simpler setup compared to ACH services
Marketing strategy focuses on SEO for long-tail queries like 'pay rent with credit cards' rather than competing head-on for 'property management software'
Demo-driven onboarding targets 90 seconds from signup to having automatic billing configured for a renter
money_quotes: Being a landlord sucks.
Kalzumeus makes being a landlord as easy as selling Beanies on eBay.
The vast majority of my competitors sell chainsaws and there are a lot of landlords who really need butter knives.
I'm not so much a landlord as I am a teacher who happens to own a house which I rent to people.
As scary as this is to say, 300 paying customers would be enough for me to quit my dayjob.
I don't need or want to compete with the largest players on their own turf — for the moment I'll be quite happily picking and choosing crumbs dropped from the table.
I generally like to set small and achievable goals and scale up from there.
Test driven development has proven to be a lot like going to the gym — I didn't enjoy starting it but the results are easily visible at the annual physical.
posts.key_insight: The biggest opportunity in crowded markets is finding the segment that's overserved by complex solutions and building something simple enough for non-specialists.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes reaching a point where his automated sales notification emails are becoming overwhelming due to increased sales volume. He receives three emails per transaction and is now getting enough sales (26 by the 20th of the month, approaching his best month of 30) that the notifications are interfering with his ability to spot actionable emails. He decides to turn off one of the three notifications, noting he'll reduce to one per transaction if sales increase another 25%. It's a brief, lighthearted milestone update about Bingo Card Creator's growing sales.
key_points: Sales volume has grown enough that three automated emails per transaction are becoming unmanageable
He's on pace to beat his best month ever (30 sales) with 26 sales by the 20th of the month
The practical problem of distinguishing actionable emails from informational ones emerges at even modest scale
money_quotes: I always had a brief catch in my breath when I saw Thunderbird downloading '1/3 mails on the server'.
Not that I mind every other email I received telling me that I had money, but it was getting harder to spot the mails which actually required action from the ones which were just for my reference.
So I turned off one of the three notifications. If my sales go up about 25% more, I'll be going down to one email per transaction.
posts.key_insight: Even at micro-scale, growing sales force you to shift from savoring each individual transaction to building systems that let you focus on what requires action.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on the first anniversary of Bingo Card Creator, his small software business. The product earned just under $6,500 in its first year against roughly $2,500 in costs, far exceeding his original goal of $250/month. He highlights the diverse uses of the software across education, corporate events, and international contexts. Beyond revenue, he values the community of micro-ISVs he joined and the people he inspired to start their own businesses. He notes that costs have dropped as startup expenses are behind him and he's mastered AdWords, and he's optimistic about future growth as the school year resumes.
key_points: First-year revenue of ~$6,500 wildly exceeded the original goal of ~$250/month, with costs around $2,500
Over 270 customers purchased the software, with an estimated 5,000-10,000 people playing bingo using cards it generated
Costs relative to sales dropped significantly once startup expenses were paid and AdWords was optimized
The micro-ISV community provided substantial value beyond revenue, including advice, connections, and the satisfaction of inspiring others
Seasonal patterns in education create predictable demand cycles, with summer being slow but fall promising growth
money_quotes: Sales for the first year were a hair under $6,500. That wildly exceeds my original expectation/goal, which was about 10 sales a month (~$250).
Every last person who wasn't happy got a refund delivered with a smile on the same day.
My best estimate is that somewhere between 5,000 and 10,000 people played a game of bingo this year using a card printed on Bingo Card Creator.
Without giving away my day job salary, suffice it to say that while my standard of living is identical my monthly retirement contribution is hundreds of dollars over what it used to be.
But the money was never my big reason for doing this.
What can I say: its a bit of work, a lot of fun, and beats spending your time on World of Warcraft.
I was tantalizing close to my $1,000 sales goal in June, which is almost disturbing since school is out for summer and my core market shouldn't be buying now.
posts.key_insight: A tiny niche software product can far exceed modest expectations by serving a real need, and the community and learning gained along the way matter as much as the revenue.

posts.summary: SWREG, a payment processor used by micro-ISVs, introduced a deceptive upsell that silently signed customers up for a $9/month 'Reservation Rewards' subscription through manipulative UI design. McKenzie argues this fundamentally betrays the trust relationship between software developers and their customers, since SWREG is exploiting trust that the uISV earned. He dissects exactly how the dark pattern works — large call-to-action buttons paired with tiny disclosure text — and explains why checkout flow context makes users especially vulnerable. The post includes devastating customer testimonials showing real harm, including a soldier serving in Iraq. McKenzie concludes that payment processors have an inherent conflict of interest with the businesses they serve, and urges developers to evaluate whether their processor would ever sacrifice customer trust for revenue.
key_points: Payment processors face a structural conflict: they can't raise their cut without losing vendors, so they seek revenue by exploiting the vendor's customers through cross-sells and scams
The deceptive UI leveraged a well-known principle: users in mid-transaction scan rather than read, clicking through pages reflexively, making post-checkout upsells especially dangerous
The trust being exploited belongs to the uISV developer, not SWREG — customers don't distinguish between the processor and the software company, so the developer bears the reputational damage
Credit card entry serves as a critical trust signal and safety valve in e-commerce; piggybacking on an existing transaction deliberately circumvents this protection
Even Google Checkout attempted a milder version with carbon offset cross-selling, suggesting this conflict of interest is inherent to the payment processor business model
money_quotes: WebScamInc could never get 'millions of satisfied customers' to authorize the $9 for nothing purchase with their lack of service, so they piggyback on the trust the customer has in you.
A large block of small text font on a web page, placed against a blue button with a strong call to action, isn't asking to be read. Its asking to be missed.
If that button had said, in 48 point font, 'Click here to format C:\' I still could have gotten 5% conversion with it!
Our industry has trained them for decades that nothing they are about to see is important.

They can't increase their cut of the transaction size, or you will flee to one of their competitors. So they are constantly on the lookout for new revenue streams, and both aggressive cross-selling to your customers and selling them down the river to scumbuckets.com are apparently options on the table.
Give your e-commerce provider a jaundiced look and a quick assessment of whether they would ever stab your customers in the back.

Those are the words of a man you can trust. That is the tone that we strive to strike as little honest fish in a stormy ocean filled with unscrupulous sharks trying to take a bite off of anyone doing business on the Internet.
posts.key_insight: Payment processors have an inherent conflict of interest with the businesses they serve because they can monetize the trust that developers painstakingly built with their customers, and developers must vigilantly guard against this betrayal.

posts.summary: McKenzie asks his blog readers for help testing Bingo Card Creator on Mac OS X 10.3.9. A user reported a Java error specific to that OS version, and McKenzie needs to determine whether the bug is machine-specific or a genuine incompatibility with that version of OS X.
key_points: A customer reported a Java error on Mac OS X 10.3.9 that doesn't occur on 10.4
