McKenzie leverages his blog audience as a free QA resource for cross-platform testing
He methodically tries to isolate whether the bug is environment-specific or a true compatibility issue
money_quotes: A user of mine is reporting a quirky Java error on that operating system but not on another OS X 10.4 box they have access to, and I'm trying to isolate whether it is their machine that has the issue or whether Bingo Card Creator has a hidden incompatibility with something on a particular version of OS X.
posts.key_insight: A solo developer's blog audience doubles as a free cross-platform QA team, turning community engagement into a practical debugging resource.

posts.summary: Patrick reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator, earning $918.25 gross and $756.35 net profit from 36 sales in June 2007. Despite summer vacation reducing teacher-related search traffic, he grew overall traffic and sales by rewriting a single page to capture long-tail 'snowflake' search queries. The rewritten page doubled its pageviews from 12% to 27% of total site traffic. He attributes his sales growth directly to this SEO content strategy and is eager to expand it further.
key_points: Best month ever with $756.35 net profit on 36 sales despite being in the seasonal low period for his teacher-focused product
A single page rewrite targeting long-tail 'snowflake' queries generated 3,000 additional pageviews, offsetting seasonal traffic decline
Trial-to-purchase conversion held at ~2.5%, with 9% of visitors who reached the purchasing page completing a purchase
Total expenses remained low at $161.90, with AdWords and SwiftCD being the largest costs
money_quotes: Easily my best month ever, and its summer vacation. I'm really looking forward to the start of the term in August.

The "extra" 3,000 page views were a major part of the reason that, despite the fact traffic on my major teacher-related search terms declined since teachers are out of school, my overall traffic stayed mostly flat while my sales increased markedly.
I can't wait to get some time to write some more content and catch myself some more snowflakes.
posts.key_insight: A single page rewrite targeting long-tail search queries can offset seasonal traffic declines and drive meaningful sales growth for a small software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie discusses the surprising profitability of freeware software, prompted by discovering that Paint.NET creator Rick Brewster has been quietly making good money from his free product. He uses the occasion to reflect on his own financial philosophy, revealing that Bingo Card Creator profits paid off his student loans and that he prioritizes saving over spending. The post offers a glimpse into McKenzie's frugal mindset and his belief that software business income should go toward eliminating debt and building savings rather than lifestyle inflation.
key_points: Freeware software can generate meaningful revenue through indirect monetization, as demonstrated by Paint.NET's creator
McKenzie used his Bingo Card Creator profits to pay off student loans, reflecting his debt-averse financial philosophy
The indie software community is surprisingly interconnected, with creators unknowingly supporting each other's work
McKenzie advocates extreme frugality: no car, no luxury purchases, savings as the primary use of business income
money_quotes: Rick wrote Paint.NET, a fairly powerful freeware image editing program which I am a major user of and contributed to, and so we have been unknowingly supporting each other's businesses for months.
Bingo Card Creator paid off my last bit of student loans in December.
I have an irrational hatred of debt and had paid essentially all of my surplus salary since graduation towards the loans.
Saving is my favorite use of money. What can I say, I'm a boring guy to shop with.
I would get myself an itty-bitty used Japanese car for about $500, run it into the ground, and repeat.
posts.key_insight: Side business income is most powerful when directed toward eliminating debt and building savings rather than lifestyle inflation, and even freeware can generate surprising revenue through indirect monetization.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie notes that Bob Walsh, author of the definitive book on micro-ISVs, has launched a consultancy called 47hats aimed at helping small software businesses. While McKenzie has some disagreements with Walsh's priorities—particularly around incorporation emphasis and intuition-based website advice—he acknowledges Walsh's contributions to the community. McKenzie sees value in any resource that helps software entrepreneurs cross the gap from zero to first revenue.
key_points: Bob Walsh launched 47hats, a consultancy specifically targeting micro-ISV software businesses
McKenzie values data-driven decisions over intuition-based advice for software businesses
The hardest challenge for new software entrepreneurs is crossing from $0 to $1 in revenue
Walsh's book on micro-ISVs could save new founders significant time on administrative decisions like payment processors and hosting
money_quotes: One more resource for uISVs trying to cross the chasm from $0 to $1 in revenue is always a good thing.
And there are plenty of chasms after that one, too.
I think some of the recommendations he makes in his super-helpful series of website reviews for uISVs are based more on intuition than on data, and I suspect the intuition is flawed.
My understanding is it would have shaved a heck of a lot of time off my first week of nailing down administrivia decisions like payment processors, bank accounts, hosting, etc.
posts.key_insight: The biggest challenge for micro-ISV founders is crossing the chasm from $0 to $1 in revenue, and community resources that help with that transition—even imperfect ones—are valuable.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains his reduced blogging frequency due to simultaneously planning a month-long US trip, changing jobs, and moving apartments — all conducted in Japanese. He notes he'll spend the time in the US visiting family and working on Kalzumeus, then return to a new OSS consultancy job in Nagoya at a higher salary. The post is a brief personal update with a humorous footnote about bureaucratic mishaps in a second language.
key_points: McKenzie is juggling a US trip, job change, and apartment move simultaneously — all in Japanese
He is transitioning to an OSS-based computer consultancy in Nagoya at a higher salary
He plans to use his month in the US to work on Kalzumeus alongside family time
Bureaucratic complications (travel agent errors, landlord contract tricks, visa misinformation) compounded the stress of operating in a second language
money_quotes: Try imagining the stress of planning a month-long vacation, a job hop, and a move at the same time — now add in the wrinkle that it is all done in a second language.

Just when you think "Oh, it can't be so bad, I am a qualified translator after all" Murphy's Law says that the travel agent's database will forget you exist, the landlord will try sneaking a rider onto page 8 of the contract to charge 10% extra, and the immigration bureau will erroneously tell you that their visa guidelines have changed to disallow your trip.
posts.key_insight: Even fluency in a second language doesn't prevent bureaucratic chaos when juggling major life transitions abroad.

posts.summary: McKenzie uses the release of Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows as an opportunity to promote his Bingo Card Creator product's free Harry Potter-themed bingo cards. He briefly reviews the book, finding it emotionally moving despite some rushed elements, and credits Rowling for advancing children's literacy. The post is a lightweight marketing tie-in to a cultural moment, directed at teachers and parents.
key_points: Leverages a major cultural event (Harry Potter book release) to drive traffic to his product's free bingo card offerings
Provides a brief, personal review of Deathly Hallows as generally positive despite pacing issues near the end
Signals to his regular audience of software entrepreneurs that normal business content will resume soon
money_quotes: I am indebted to Ms. Rowling for inviting us to share in the magic, and in the very real good she has worked for the cause of children's literacy.
There is a really good book out there if you want to take your mind off of conversion rates and SEO for a while.
posts.key_insight: Smart content marketing means tying your product to major cultural moments, even when the connection is tangential.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie addresses practical banking questions for micro-ISV (uISV) operators, particularly those running international businesses. He explains that non-US residents can obtain US bank accounts despite common fears about regulations like the Patriot Act, with the key obstacle being Know Your Customer requirements. He recommends E-Trade's brokerage-to-banking pathway as a workaround for international entrepreneurs. For US-based operators, he recommends ING Direct's Electric Orange checking account for its fast ACH processing and interest rates, and advises all uISVs to use cash-back credit cards for expense tracking and savings.
key_points: Non-US residents can open US bank accounts by leveraging brokerage accounts at firms like E-Trade, which use existing investment accounts as KYC verification
Know Your Customer regulations are the main obstacle for international entrepreneurs, but banks interpret these requirements differently and some are more accommodating than others
ING Direct's Electric Orange checking account offers fast ACH processing for PayPal and Google payments, interest on checking balances, and strong online banking
Cash-back credit cards provide easy record-keeping, 1% savings on expenses, and transaction dispute protection for business purchases
Keeping large balances in PayPal accounts to use their debit card is inadvisable despite the 1% cash back benefit
money_quotes: America didn't become the world's largest economy by saying no to greenbacks, and billions of dollars passes through millions of foreigner-controlled accounts every day.
Forget what you've heard about the Patriot Act, if you're not on a watchlist this actually isn't that hard, provided you can find a bank which will let you.
Many banks interpret that as meaning you need a branch visit, but that is hardly universal.
You can open an E-Trade investment account from just about anywhere.
I could literally go the rest of my life without needing teller help for simple transactions.

While I don't recommend ever carrying a balance on your credit card, paying all of those recurring monthly expenses and software purchases on a credit card makes for easy record keeping, 1% off your expenses every month, and gives you some protection should a transaction go sour.
posts.key_insight: International micro-ISV operators can access US banking by leveraging online brokerages as a backdoor past Know Your Customer requirements, while US-based operators should optimize for fast ACH processing and cash-back rewards.

posts.summary: McKenzie congratulates E-junkie, a payment processing service for small software vendors, on reaching one million dollars in monthly transaction volume. He notes his own modest contribution to that figure and frames it with self-deprecating humor about his small share of their business.
key_points: E-junkie crossed $1 million/month in transaction processing, a milestone for a small vendor-focused service
McKenzie positions himself as both a satisfied customer and a contributor to their growth
His own sales (~$425 in 15 days) represent roughly 0.1% of E-junkie's volume, highlighting the long tail of small software businesses
money_quotes: Now I just need to get back to the business of getting them closer to two million a month.
At $425 in 15 days, I'm almost a whole tenth of a percent of their business. Woohoo!
posts.key_insight: Even tiny software businesses collectively add up to meaningful transaction volume for the platforms that serve them.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents how he set up a WordPress site for his brother's superhero novel on the same cheap VPS already running his Rails application. The post is a detailed technical walkthrough of configuring Apache virtual hosts, mod_rewrite rules, and WordPress installation on a Slicehost VPS with only 256MB of RAM. He advocates for self-hosted WordPress over WordPress.com for SEO control and future flexibility. The broader context is applying his software marketing mindset to help his brother market a product that doesn't even exist yet.
key_points: Self-hosted WordPress gives you SEO control, analytics integration, and room to grow compared to WordPress.com
Moving a blog off WordPress.com later is much harder than setting up self-hosted WordPress from the start
Two websites on very different technology stacks (Rails and PHP/WordPress) can coexist on a single $20/month VPS with proper Apache configuration
Marketing should start before the product exists — setting up the web presence early builds SEO juice over time
Use descriptive, high-value keyword directory names for your blog path rather than generic names, for SEO benefit
money_quotes: I'd like to recount what we're doing to market a product that, strictly speaking, doesn't exist yet.
If you're just starting your own site, for SEO purposes if nothing else, put it on a domain you control rather than wordpress.com.
It is a pain in the butt to set up, but moving a blog OFF wordpress is infinitely harder than getting WordPress up and running somewhere else and then making the changes you want down the road.

Don't do these instructions on a production box without testing it first, OK? They worked for me, but if they don't for you my liability is limited to refunding the amount you paid for this blog post.
For reasons only God knows WordPress.org dies with a silent error if PHP can't mail you your password during an install.
posts.key_insight: Start marketing before your product exists by establishing a self-hosted web presence you fully control, because the SEO and platform flexibility benefits compound over time.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his return to Japan to start a new software consulting contract in Nagoya. The position involves Java server programming and managing an Indian outsourcing team at an enterprise software consultancy. He notes the overwhelming dominance of Toyota in Nagoya's economy and shares that the salary exceeds his Bingo Card Creator income. The post is brief and conversational, serving as a networking update for his blog audience.
key_points: McKenzie is taking a dual role: Java J2EE server programming and serving as the English-Japanese interface with an Indian outsourcing team
Nagoya's economy is so dominated by Toyota that 18 of 20+ job positions he reviewed involved automobile manufacturing
Foreign residents are so rare in his area of Japan that mail addressed only to his name and prefecture reaches him without a street address
He frames the contract as an opportunity to gain management experience alongside technical skills
money_quotes: White guys are so rare where I live that letters from the Social Security Administration to Patrick McKenzie @ my prefecture get to me, no address necessary.
Nagoya is owned lock, stock, and barrel by the Toyota group.
I have always hated this question. Ask me in five years.
Half Java server programmer, half being interface with the Indian outsourcing team which doesn't speak any Japanese.
More than I make with Bingo Card Creator.
posts.key_insight: McKenzie's early career in Japan involved navigating the intersection of enterprise software consulting and cross-cultural communication in a regional economy completely dominated by a single corporation.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best single day of sales for Bingo Card Creator, driven by AdWords campaign changes that increased clicks from 10 to 60 per day. August 2007 brought in $813 in revenue with $157 in expenses for $656 profit. He shares two customer service stories: paying $15 to UPS a replacement CD to an upset customer, and giving away a free CD rather than dealing with a school's purchase order system. Both decisions sacrificed short-term profit for long-term goodwill and word-of-mouth marketing.
key_points: Simple AdWords campaign tweaks increased daily clicks 6x, driving his best sales day ever with 500+ visits and ~100 trial downloads
Monthly profit of $656 on 33 sales demonstrates a sustainable micro-ISV business with low overhead
Spending $15 to overnight-ship a replacement CD that ate most of the sale's profit was worth it to convert an angry customer into a happy one
Giving away a free CD rather than processing a school purchase order was more economically rational than spending hours navigating institutional bureaucracy for $29.95
Purchase orders from institutions are so painful to process that shareware processors charge $20/month just to enable the feature
money_quotes: 100 trial downloads implies 2.5 sales implies about $70 in revenue, and believe me, if I could sustain that I would be sitting pretty.
Yeah, that eats most of the profit from the sale, but they're now very pleased with my responsiveness rather than thinking I'm a shiftless shipment-forgetting Internet conman.

I just sent them a free CD with my compliments. Now I've got a team full of teachers who love me and are hopefully plugging my software to parents, friends, and colleagues… who pay with credit cards, like normal people.
Rather than spending hours of my life getting that mailed to me, then dealing with the school's payment clerks to actually get my $29.95, I just sent them a free CD with my compliments.
A PO is essentially a 'Send us this stuff and then we'll send you the exact amount of money on this document' transactional instrument. Dealing with them is a pain in the hindquarters.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most profitable customer service decision is to eat the cost of a sale in exchange for goodwill and word-of-mouth marketing.

posts.summary: Microsoft's AdCenter announced a feature allowing direct import of campaigns from competing paid search programs like Google AdWords. McKenzie sees this as a surprisingly smart move from Microsoft, as it acknowledges their competitive disadvantage while simultaneously making it trivially easy for advertisers to expand to their platform. The feature halves the work required to run campaigns on both platforms.
key_points: Microsoft AdCenter added the ability to import campaigns directly from Google AdWords
The feature is strategically smart because it acknowledges Microsoft's weaker market position while lowering the barrier to adoption
Advertisers benefit from doing campaign work in AdWords' superior interface and then reaping lower competition on AdCenter
money_quotes: That is genius and honestly something I would never have expected from Microsoft, because it acknowledges that their competition is beating them soundly.

The ability to import campaigns directly from Adwords literally halves the amount of work required, AND it means you get to do your work where the work is easy (AdWords' vastly superior interface) and then reap the rewards of lower competition on AdCenter.
posts.key_insight: The smartest competitive move a second-place platform can make is to reduce switching costs from the leader, even if it means acknowledging inferiority.

posts.summary: Patrick describes updating his PayPal payment buttons on his Bingo Card Creator purchasing page to newer, larger, more descriptive versions. He notes the visual improvements and plans to A/B test the old versus new buttons using CrazyEgg. He also updates the Google Checkout button but notes Google's brand guidelines prevent him from resizing it to match the PayPal button.
key_points: Newer PayPal buttons are larger, bolder, and more descriptive than the old defaults
A/B testing button changes with CrazyEgg rather than assuming the new design is better
Payment processor brand guidelines can conflict with merchant conversion optimization
Even small visual elements like payment buttons deserve deliberate testing and iteration
money_quotes: Now, personally, I kind of liked having the credit card icon logos above and slightly dominating the call to action but, hey, this is what CrazyEgg was made for.
Thanks a lot, guys, I'm only trying to make you money…
posts.key_insight: Even minor UI elements like payment buttons should be deliberately tested rather than changed on instinct alone.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experience setting up Google's Website Optimizer to split test his Bingo Card Creator purchasing page with new PayPal icons. He explains that Google's newer, simpler redirect-based approach is much easier than the old markup-heavy method. However, he identifies a critical SEO risk: having two nearly identical pages could trigger Google's duplicate content penalty. He solves this by blocking the alternate page in robots.txt and plans to use a 302 redirect after the test concludes.
key_points: Google Website Optimizer's new redirect-based split testing is much simpler than the old approach of marking up page sections
Having two near-identical pages for A/B testing creates a duplicate content penalty risk that could outweigh any conversion gains
A simple robots.txt disallow rule prevents search engines from indexing the alternate test page
After testing, a 302 redirect preserves any bookmarks or links to the alternate page without maintaining a stale duplicate
money_quotes: A split test is when you randomly send half of all prospects to one version of a page and half to another to determine, in a rigorously scientific and statistical manner, which of two alternatives is better.

Having two pages on your site with 95% similar HTML is an excellent way to get smacked down by the duplicate content penalty, and that would hurt me oh-so-much more than getting a modest bump in conversions from rigorous testing.
They recommend that you leave the second page up indefinitely, because folks could conceivably link or bookmark it. I first thought that was good advice. Then I realized DANGER WILL ROBINSON.
After I'm done with the test, I'm going to use .htaccess to 302 redirect the alternate page to the main page rather than leaving the alternate up forever.
posts.key_insight: A/B testing tools can inadvertently create duplicate content that harms SEO, so always block test pages from search engine indexing.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains why his blog posting frequency has declined, citing a month-long family visit home, returning to a demanding salaryman job in Japan, and his preference not to post without substance. He notes the business is stable but he continues tweaking things. He responds to being called a 'hero of the MicroISV blogging community' with characteristic humility.
key_points: Family time during a rare month of leisure took priority over blogging and business
The demanding Japanese salaryman work schedule (returning home at 9 PM, up at 6:30 AM) leaves little time for side projects
He follows a principle of only posting when he has something substantive to say rather than posting for the sake of consistency
Despite reduced posting, he continues experimenting with his business, including testing Google's Content Network
money_quotes: I generally don't like posting to say 'Well, I've got nothing to say'.
Went home for a month and spending time with my family was much more important to me than the uISV.
This is what I get for joining the private sector and leaving my cushy government job.
I'm not a hero by any means, but I'm glad folks find some of the ramblings around here to be of use. That is one major reason I keep writing them.
I came back to Japan and immediately got thrown into the salaryman thing, which I got back from today at about 9:00 pm — unseasonably early!
posts.key_insight: Running a side business while working a demanding Japanese salaryman job means accepting that some things — family, sleep, the day job — simply take priority over content creation.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes giving a free license to a confused customer who had actually purchased from a competitor, arguing this is sound business strategy rather than charity. He outlines three mercenary reasons: it costs nothing (marginal cost of software is zero), it ends a potential multi-email support thread quickly, and it creates a passionate advocate far more effectively than paid advertising. He compares the cost-effectiveness to his AdWords spending where 99.4% of ad viewers won't even click, while this gesture likely creates a lifetime evangelist. He closes with softer arguments about karma and supporting the small business ecosystem.
key_points: The marginal cost of serving one additional software customer is literally zero, making generosity with free licenses economically rational
Creating a passionate advocate through unexpected generosity is far more cost-effective than paid advertising, where $10 buys mostly disinterested eyeballs
Ending support conversations quickly by making people 'deliriously happy' is better than exchanging multiple polite but fruitless emails
Remarkable customer service is a 'purple cow' — it spreads virally because people naturally share stories of unexpectedly generous treatment
Small software businesses benefit collectively when customers learn to trust independent vendors over big companies
money_quotes: One of the beauties of the software business is that serving your 307th customer is, quite literally, free. Its that first customer who costs you millions… or in my case, about sixty bucks.
99.4% of the people who saw one of these advertisements weren't even willing to part with the five seconds! And it was still a smart business decision to do it.
Why wouldn't I do something which is much cheaper than $10 to achieve something which is much more valuable than catching the corner of the eyeball of a disinterested MySpace browser?
Making people deliriously happy works wonders for ending conversations as quickly as practical.
When is the last time anybody you did business with gave you what you wanted, for free, without you having to ask for it, and without expecting anything in return?
I've been off the deep end for years, and I love it, the water is fine.

Every little marginal step that gets taken to make Joe and Jane Consumer more willing to trust their credit card details with an anonymous little shop on the Internet helps all of us move our conversion rates to the next level.
posts.key_insight: Giving away a zero-marginal-cost product to a competitor's confused customer is vastly more cost-effective at creating a passionate advocate than any amount of paid advertising.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a link to a survey of 96 micro-ISVs (micro independent software vendors) conducted by someone on the Business of Software forums. The preliminary results cover demographics, with sales data still to come. He encourages other bloggers to link to the survey as a way of rewarding community contributions.
key_points: A community member surveyed 96 micro-ISVs on demographics and business topics
McKenzie emphasizes that community contributors who do substantial work deserve recognition through links
He nudges fellow bloggers to support useful community research by linking to it
money_quotes: Anybody who does that much work for the community deserves links early and often.
posts.key_insight: Community members who contribute substantial research deserve active support and promotion from fellow bloggers.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes how using CrazyEgg heat maps revealed that the first hyperlink on his second-most-trafficked page was sending 10% of visitors to Adobe Acrobat's download page instead of his own free trial. After a one-minute fix to make the first link point to his free trial instead, he estimates gaining roughly 13 extra sales per month. The post illustrates how visual analytics tools can expose obvious conversion leaks that are invisible when just looking at raw data or source code.
key_points: The first hyperlink in a page's main content area gets disproportionate clicks because users reflexively click underlined blue text
CrazyEgg heat maps revealed 10% of visitors to his second-most-trafficked page were clicking away to Adobe Acrobat instead of engaging with his product
A one-minute fix redirecting that first link to his free trial page could yield approximately 13 extra sales per month
Visual heat map tools surface conversion problems that are obvious in retrospect but invisible without the right instrumentation
money_quotes: One simple conversion trick is to put the goal for the page as the first hyperlink in the main content area.
This largely works because Internet browsers will almost reflexively jump on anything that is underlined in blue.
And they were happily profiting from my largesse, with 10% of the visitors to the 2nd most trafficked page in my site going off to download Acrobat Reader.
That was a moment of forehead slapping stupidity.

Not a bad minute's work, and it's a minute's work that is made much, much easier by seeing obvious visual clues like a huge dead zone on your heat map with a bright red island over a graphic that doesn't even link to your own site.
posts.key_insight: Visual analytics tools like heat maps can instantly reveal conversion-killing mistakes that are obvious in retrospect but invisible without instrumentation, turning a minute of work into meaningful revenue gains.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that a Spanish-language blog has translated and commented on one of his earlier posts about giving a free software license to a confused customer. He shares the original Spanish text and attempts his own translation back into English, demonstrating his multilingual capabilities while noting his Spanish has atrophied after years in Japan. The post highlights how the internet connects small business stories across language barriers in ways that would have been impossible a generation earlier.
key_points: A Spanish-language blog translated McKenzie's story about giving a free license to a competitor's confused customer, illustrating how small business stories spread globally online
The Spanish blogger recognized the customer service move as commercially brilliant: creating a customer for life through generosity
McKenzie demonstrates comfort working across English, Japanese, and Spanish, while self-deprecatingly noting his Spanish skills have atrophied
The post reflects on how the internet has made it possible for a tiny one-person software company in Japan to get international attention and coverage
money_quotes: Now tell me, back in my father's day, what were the odds that you would find someone translating the meat of a wee little article about a wee little company run by an American in Japan into Spanish?

From a purely commercial point of view, this is a fantastic reaction: this gentleman has just found himself a customer for life, and she is a customer who can be counted on to sing his praises at every occasion.
I'm afraid you are not my client, ma'am, but I am sending you a free copy with my thanks for your continued support of small businesses.
If you ever want to comment on or translate anything you see here in Spanish or any other language, please feel free to.
It might mean 'knows everything' — echoed the message.
posts.key_insight: The internet enables even the smallest one-person businesses to build international reputations through generous customer service stories that cross language barriers organically.

posts.summary: McKenzie exposes a deceptive AdSense technique where publishers use CSS manipulation to make advertisements appear as page content, tricking visitors into clicking ads they mistake for navigation or download links. He discovered the scheme when his Content Network CTR inexplicably jumped from 1% to 15%, leading him to investigate. The technique generates roughly $40 CPM for publishers versus $2.50 for honest sites, effectively stealing from advertisers whose visitors immediately bounce. McKenzie details how this cost him money by converting only 2% of deceived visitors versus 22% of genuinely interested ones, making each sale cost $50 for a $25 product. He provides practical countermeasures including monitoring site placement reports and banning offending domains, while calling on Google to implement algorithmic protections.
key_points: Publishers use CSS tricks to clip legitimate content below the fold, making AdSense ads appear to be the page's actual content, resulting in CTRs of 15-16% versus the normal 0.5-1%
The economic damage to advertisers is severe: deceived visitors convert at 2% versus 22% for genuine traffic, turning profitable ad spend into losses
Google's own algorithms exacerbate the problem by preferring high-CTR placements, creating an 'unholy alliance' that drains advertiser budgets on fraudulent sites within hours
Advertisers can defend themselves by monitoring Site Placement reports for abnormally high CTRs and banning offending domains
The technique is more damaging than click fraud because it wastes real advertiser money on visitors who will never convert
money_quotes: That couldn't possibly be natural. Remember, an AdSense ad is, by definition, being shown to someone who is at least partially interested in something related to your product but has not expressed any interest in being sold to yet.
Bamboozling visitors to click on my ads hurts me more than errors ever could.
I was paying approximately $50 to get a sale of a $25 product — I guess I can make the loss up on volume?
This unholy, and I hope unintended, alliance of Google and the publishers using this trick sucked my budget dry within the first two hours of every day.

Google's automated algorithms helpfully suggested I increase my spending by a factor of ten to compensate, so that instead of spending $15 a day to make $7.50 I would be spending $150 a day to make $75, for a monthly loss in the $2,000 range.
And when I say generating, I mean 'taking it from the advertisers'.
It strains credulity to think that eight people independently accidentally developed cross-browser compliant CSS and liquid layouts to achieve this effect.
posts.key_insight: Deceptive ad placement that tricks users into clicking ads they mistake for content is more damaging to advertisers than click fraud, because it drains budgets on visitors who will never convert while Google's own optimization algorithms amplify the effect.

posts.summary: McKenzie discusses Google's new Conversion Optimizer feature for AdWords, which allows advertisers to set Cost-Per-Acquisition bids instead of Cost-Per-Click bids. He explains the difference between true CPA (affiliate-style) advertising and this approximation, which uses Google's algorithms to optimize click bidding based on conversion history. He signs up his Content Network campaign to test it, setting a target CPA of 35 cents against his current 41-cent average. The post also marks a milestone of crossing $1,000 in monthly sales for the first time.
key_points: Google's Conversion Optimizer lets advertisers set CPA bids instead of CPC bids, with Google's algorithm deciding what each click is worth based on campaign history
The feature requires 300 conversions in the last 30 days to qualify, indicating it's aimed at advertisers with meaningful spend
McKenzie distinguishes between true CPA (paying only for sales) and Google's approximation (algorithmically optimized CPC bidding)
He crosses $1,000 in monthly sales for the first time while spending approximately $280 on advertising
money_quotes: The idea would be pay folks $10 or so for every copy of Bingo Card Creator their site sells as opposed to 10 cents every time someone clicks on over to me.
Google uses some black magic to decide what a click at a certain day, time, and website is likely to be worth to you, based on your campaign history.
While $280 (and counting!) of advertising expenditure this month is giving me a bit of heartburn, on the plus side, I've just crested $1,000 in sales for the first time ever.
posts.key_insight: Google's Conversion Optimizer represents a meaningful step toward performance-based advertising by letting the platform algorithmically optimize bids toward a target acquisition cost rather than requiring manual per-click bid management.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces that Bingo Card Creator is getting its own product blog, built on WordPress. He details his progress after ninety minutes of work porting the site template, despite limited PHP and CSS knowledge. He outlines remaining tasks including styling, SEO-friendly URL setup, choosing a title optimized for search, and actually writing content. He asks readers to link to the blog once launched to boost its SEO.
key_points: Adding a dedicated product blog to Bingo Card Creator's website, built on WordPress
Strategically choosing the blog title for SEO value ('Teaching Resources') versus style ('Teacher's Pet')
Deliberately avoiding linking directly to the unfinished blog to prevent premature Google indexing
Soliciting backlinks from 'disinterested third parties' to boost search rankings
money_quotes: Not a bad ninety minutes, actually, especially for someone who doesn't really know PHP or CSS.
I'm leaning towards 'Teaching Resources' for raw SEO punch or 'Teacher's Pet' for something which has a bit of style.
I am not putting a direct link to that because that is not going to be the final name and I'm not quite ready for it to be seen by Google, and hence customers, yet.
Links directly at it from 'disinterested third parties' will really help my SEO efforts.
Oh, yeah, write some content.
posts.key_insight: Every decision about a product blog—its title, URL structure, linking strategy, and launch timing—should be made with SEO implications in mind.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports dramatic early results from Google's new Conversion Optimizer for AdWords, which switches bidding from cost-per-click to cost-per-action on the content network. His daily ad spend dropped 50% while click-through rates improved, cutting his cost per acquisition from 45-50 cents to 25 cents. He walks through the unit economics showing how this doubled his profit margin without any effort. He also flags the strategic risk: by telling Google exactly what a conversion is worth, advertisers hand Google the information needed for perfect price discrimination.
key_points: Google's Conversion Optimizer cut advertising costs by 50% while improving CTR by 50%, dramatically improving unit economics
At 45-50 cents per trial, Google and content publishers captured most of the value from each sale; at 25 cents, profit margins doubled
CPA bidding naturally aligns incentives between Google, content publishers, and advertisers by rewarding relevant ad placements over spam
The strategic risk is that sharing conversion values with Google gives them the data needed for perfect price discrimination, eliminating consumer surplus
The optimizer effectively solves the spam site problem by automatically directing spend away from low-converting placements
money_quotes: This is the best thing to happen to AdWords since AdWords.

If I pay 45 cents for a trial, then I am paying $18 to make a sale worth approximately $24 to me. I still make money, but Google and the content publisher get most of the value from the sale, and I do all of the work.
That doubles my profit margin without me lifting a finger, other than to turn Conversion Optimizer on.
For once the interests of Google, the content provider, and the advertiser are in perfect alignment here.
Visitors at Mrs. Lindle's Reading Tips are much more likely to want to play bingo today than folks reading snazzygurl413's MySpace page.
To do this, Google needs to know exactly how much a conversion is worth to me. That would, theoretically, allow them to achieve the holy grail of monopolies: perfect price discrimination.
When Google does things right, wow, do they do things right.
My advertising has gone from barely above water to, what is the vernacular, making money hats.
posts.key_insight: CPA-based advertising aligns incentives across the entire ad ecosystem by rewarding relevant placements, but the tradeoff is handing your monopolist supplier the exact information they need to extract maximum value from you.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his process for optimizing the conversion funnel on his Bingo Card Creator homepage. He identifies that visitors who click the prominent screenshot often fail to proceed to downloading the free trial, instead closing the browser or wandering the site. His solution is to replace the busy, text-heavy screenshot overlay with a cleaner version containing a clear call-to-action directing users to download the trial. He then walks through setting up a Google Website Optimizer A/B test to measure whether the change increases visits to the Free Trial page.
key_points: The homepage screenshot captures massive attention (25% of visitors click it) but fails to direct users toward the next conversion step
Replacing a wall of text with a single clear instruction puts the customer 'on rails' toward the desired action
Pretty visuals matter enormously — prettier icons alone doubled his sales
A/B testing should be repeated continuously, not treated as a one-time exercise
Even imperfect conversion metrics (visits to trial page vs. actual downloads) are worth testing when ideal tracking isn't feasible
money_quotes: I know what I want the customers to do, so why not tell them?
Do not discount the effect of pretty buttons! Doubled my sales!
Its a wall of text and, as we all know, nobody reads on the Internet.
The visitor, not too computer-savvy and left without direction, just closes the browser, hits the back button, or ambles on to read more from my website.
I was thinking 'Is there any step in this process which could be easier or more streamlined?' And of course there was — there always is.
Do it again, and again, and again.
posts.key_insight: Every step in a conversion funnel should explicitly tell the customer what to do next, because visitors left without clear direction will simply leave.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a detailed freelance job listing on his blog, seeking contractors to create educational bingo card word lists and associated PDF/GIF files for his Bingo Card Creator software. Rather than using RentACoder and paying a 10% finder's fee, he leverages his blog audience's trust to find talent directly. He offers $30 for approximately two hours of work, with promises of larger follow-up orders for quality performers. The post doubles as a masterclass in writing clear freelance specifications, with detailed deliverable requirements, quality criteria, and legal terms.
key_points: McKenzie bypasses freelance marketplaces by leveraging his blog audience's trust, saving the middleman fee while getting higher-quality candidates who already know his work
The task is content creation for SEO and product value — educational word lists that will become product features and likely drive organic search traffic
He structures the offer to incentivize quality through bonuses and the promise of much larger follow-up orders, creating competition among applicants
The detailed specification demonstrates his approach to outsourcing: exhaustive upfront requirements to minimize back-and-forth and ensure consistent output
The overwhelming response validated that his blog audience was an untapped labor marketplace, eliminating the trust gap that platforms like RentACoder exist to solve
money_quotes: What is the purpose of RentACoder and similar? To connect money with talent while bridging the trust gap. I've got money, you've got talent, and if you are reading this you presumably know me to be an upstanding sort.
I see no reason to pay RentACoder a 10% finder's fee for finding you.
I work crazy hours at a Japanese company and, frankly, while I have expectations that this venture will pay off handsomely for me I have very little desire to do drudgework after I get home at 10 PM.
Spelling and factual accuracy are absolutely critical. We can't have children learning that Berrlin is a Major European Capitol.
You should end up making $15 an hour, which is half again my best paying part time job from my college days.
Due to overwhelming interest, I'm going to have to withdraw the offer for additional people to work on this, or I will be unable to pay everyone who has offered to work.
posts.key_insight: A trusted personal brand can replace freelance marketplaces entirely, eliminating fees and attracting higher-quality talent who self-select through familiarity with your work.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a customer support interaction where a customer didn't read his detailed email containing their registration key and instead placed a duplicate order. Rather than argue or try to recoup the $5 loss from the extra CD, he simply accommodated the customer gracefully. He uses this as an example of not sweating minor costs and irritations in running a small software business, and closes with a recommendation for SwiftCD's fulfillment service.
key_points: Customers will sometimes fail to read even clear, detailed support emails, leading to unnecessary duplicate orders and extra costs
Absorbing small losses ($5) gracefully is better business than creating friction or making customers feel bad about mistakes
Automating fulfillment through services like SwiftCD means 95% of orders require zero manual intervention
Minor irritations and miscommunications are par for the course when running a customer-facing business
money_quotes: A certain amount of minor irritations and miscommunications are par for the course.
Of course I can do that for you. You will end up receiving two CDs — feel free to discard the one you don't need.
The amount of work I do on 95 out of 100 CD orders is… nothing. It just happens and I get a bill at the end of the month.
No sense making too much extra work for them.
I'm out an extra $5 for the second CD, because my customer apparently did not understand that my first email resolved their issue.
posts.key_insight: Small financial losses from customer miscommunications are worth absorbing gracefully rather than creating friction, because goodwill and smooth operations matter more than recovering a few dollars.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports crossing the $1,000 monthly sales mark with Bingo Card Creator, though heavy AdWords experimentation cut net profit to $604. He breaks down his traffic sources, conversion funnel, and the economics of paid acquisition versus organic search. The long tail of Google search keywords drives most of his traffic, with thousands of unique queries averaging just three hits each. He highlights the power of content marketing, noting a single blog post generates roughly $35-50/month in revenue. His biggest frustration is the inability to accurately track AdWords ROI due to noisy, limited data.
key_points: Crossing $1,000 in gross monthly sales on a niche software product, with $604 net profit after $435 in expenses dominated by AdWords spend
Google's Conversion Optimizer brought acquisition costs down to 24 cents per free trial, implying less than $12 cost per $24 sale
Long tail search dominates traffic — Google keywords averaged three hits each, with the top 10 keywords accounting for only 40% of total search traffic
A single blog post about free bingo cards generates 1,500 hits, 500 clickthroughs, 50 trials, and ~$35-50/month in revenue
Attribution remains a major unsolved problem — Google Analytics only tracks 60% of sales, making AdWords ROI calculations unreliable
money_quotes: I am paying something like 24 cents a free trial now for the last week. That implies a cost of less than $12 per additional $24 profit sale.
You have to watch the content network like a hawk but if you do, especially using their new Conversion Optimizer, it can be amazing.
Long Tail! Google keywords averaged three hits apiece. The top 10 keywords counted for only 40% of the total and it falls very, very rapidly from there.

Not all of that is 'lost' so much as it is temporarily negative cashflow — there are literally thousands of my trial versions now installed as a result of that expenditure, and some portion of them are going to convert in the future.
Not bad for one single post, and I will finally get off my duff and launch the Bingo Card Creator blog this weekend.
Anecdotally, I definately see the sales numbers go up when I increase AdWords spending, but I'm not sure if the increase is .9x or 1.5x since the data is too darn noisy and limited to boot.
posts.key_insight: Even a tiny niche software business can cross $1,000/month by combining long-tail SEO, disciplined paid acquisition experimentation, and content marketing — though accurate attribution remains frustratingly elusive.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports being overwhelmed by the response to his outsourcing request for help creating bingo card assets. He posted a job request for his micro-ISV and received far more willing helpers than expected within twenty hours. He teases an upcoming experiment involving dozens of PDF and GIF bingo card files, expecting the investment to be more productive than his AdWords spend. He hints at a weekend rollout, schedule permitting.
key_points: Outsourcing simple tasks can yield unexpectedly strong responses from willing freelancers
Comparing the cost of outsourced content creation to AdWords spend as a marketing investment
Building content assets (bingo card PDFs/GIFs) as an alternative customer acquisition strategy to paid advertising
Teasing a content marketing experiment that would become a signature SEO strategy for his business
money_quotes: I succeeded far, far beyond my expectations in finding folks willing and able to get the job done.

I'm sure that even though I'm going to end up buying a few more bingo cards than I was budgeting for for starters that it will be at least as productive as the giant amount I spent on AdWords last month.
posts.key_insight: Investing in content assets through outsourcing can be a more productive marketing spend than equivalent paid advertising budgets.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie confesses to forgetting a basic SEO best practice for fourteen months: setting up a 301 redirect from his bare domain to the www version. He discovered through WebsiteGrader that roughly half his inbound links pointed to each variant, meaning search engines treated them as two separate sites rather than combining their authority. The fix took fifteen seconds with a simple .htaccess rule. He shares the exact Apache configuration so others can avoid the same mistake.
key_points: Search engines treat www and non-www versions of a domain as separate websites, splitting link authority between them
A 301 redirect permanently consolidates both versions so all inbound links count toward a single domain's ranking
McKenzie had roughly equal links to both variants (550 vs 553), meaning he was effectively throwing away half his SEO value
The fix is trivially simple—three lines in an .htaccess file—making the oversight especially painful
money_quotes: Fourteen months into marketing Bingo Card Creator and quite a bit into becoming an amateur SEO, I finally added a 301 redirect from bingocardcreator.com to www.bingocardcreator.com
I've been ignoring the mental equivalent of gigantic red warnings for a year.
All that work creating linkable content and I was throwing half of it away.
I'm especially embarassed because this is something that can be fixed in literally fifteen seconds.
The next stray thought was 'Well, I'm really the only person who ever links to the page, and I always choose www, so it won't really make a difference'.
posts.key_insight: Even experienced practitioners can lose significant value by neglecting trivial fundamentals, and the assumptions that let you dismiss a problem ('I'm the only one who links') are often wrong.

posts.summary: A brief status update where Patrick apologizes for not posting due to illness. He teases an upcoming post about linkbait strategies he's been developing. The post is essentially a placeholder promising content about SEO link-building tactics. At just 51 words, it's one of his shortest posts and reflects the casual, diary-like nature of his early blogging.
key_points: Patrick was sick with a fever/cold/flu combination, disrupting his posting schedule
He had been working on a linkbait project for a week and planned to write about the strategy
The post signals his commitment to regular blogging even when he had nothing substantial to share
money_quotes: It will be regarding the linkbait I have been working on for a week now, and how you can get linkbait to work for you.
posts.key_insight: Even brief status updates reflect the early blogging ethos of maintaining regular audience engagement, and hint at the SEO-focused marketing strategies Patrick was actively developing.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie celebrates crossing $10,000 in cumulative revenue from Bingo Card Creator, noting that sales are accelerating. He shares a revenue graph showing growth trajectory and projects October 2007 will exceed his conservative $1,500 estimate since he's already at $1,250 with a third of the month remaining. He mentions upcoming linkbait content and a version upgrade as further growth drivers.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator crossed $10,000 in cumulative lifetime revenue, a psychological milestone for a solo software business
Sales are accelerating rather than plateauing, with October on pace to significantly exceed projections
Strategic spending on linkbait content represents deliberate marketing investment to sustain growth
Seasonal factors (holiday) and product improvements (version upgrade) are being timed to maximize revenue
money_quotes: I'm stupefied by the number, I just crossed into the five figures category today… and sales are accelerating.
The October 2007 figure is an extraordinarily conservative estimate.
I've already sold $1,250 and the month is only 2/3rds over, with a holiday and a version upgrade coming around the bend.
posts.key_insight: Crossing $10,000 in cumulative revenue validated that a tiny niche software product could generate real and accelerating income through sustained marketing effort.

posts.summary: McKenzie recommends Firebug, a Firefox addon that lets you inspect and modify CSS properties in real time without page refreshes. He used it to fix long-standing visual bugs on Bingo Card Creator's site that he'd been putting off for over a year, including a 12-pixel alignment issue and undersized header fonts. The post illustrates his broader philosophy of incremental improvement and his self-awareness about lacking design skills.
key_points: Firebug enables real-time CSS inspection and modification, eliminating the edit-refresh cycle that discourages non-designers from fixing visual issues
Small visual bugs can persist for over a year when the barrier to fixing them is high enough, even when customers report them
Having the right tool gave McKenzie a sense of ownership over his design, lowering the psychological barrier to making changes
Progress in a small software business often comes through incremental improvements rather than dramatic overhauls
money_quotes: It can help answer the big (and befuddling) questions like What Line In My 2,000 Line CSS or HTML File Makes This Box Go Here, which throw non-designer me for a loop.
I always thanked them for the comment and made a mental note to do something about it, then (when I actually opened the CSS file) got lost and gave up.

It also gives me a feeling of ownership of the design, which (since I am not a designer and have the artistic acuity of a nearsighted mole) I generally treat like holy writ that I am not fit to alter.
11 pixels for a page header… not a quality choice! But I put up with it for forever.
It isn't a quantum leap in quality, obviously — just an evolution of inches, like most of the things I do.
posts.key_insight: The right tooling can eliminate psychological barriers that cause small but meaningful improvements to be deferred indefinitely.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie asks his blog audience for help testing the Mac version of Bingo Card Creator before a planned 2.0 release. He needs someone to verify the application doesn't crash on Mac OS X, and also asks if anyone knows of a remote desktop service for Macs. He mentions plans to launch version 2.0 that weekend alongside a linkbait project.
key_points: McKenzie lacks access to a Mac for testing, highlighting the resource constraints of a solo developer
He leverages his blog audience as free QA testers, a practical bootstrapper strategy
Bingo Card Creator 2.0 is being coordinated with a linkbait project for maximum launch impact
money_quotes: If anyone knows a service which lets me remote desktop into a Mac to do this myself I will be your best friend forever if you tell me about it.
No sense not getting things started with a bang.
posts.key_insight: Solo developers can compensate for limited resources by leveraging their blog community for testing and feedback.

posts.summary: McKenzie details his strategy for creating 'linkbait' content targeting non-technical audiences, specifically elementary school teachers. He built DailyBingoCards.com as a massive free resource site containing hundreds of bingo card activities, designed to attract links from educators rather than the typical Digg demographic. The strategy centers on overwhelming visitors with abundance, using clean URLs for SEO, creating evergreen content that compounds in value over years, and treating the entire free site as a subtle advertisement for his paid product Bingo Card Creator. He contrasts this long-tail approach with the ephemeral nature of Digg-style linkbait, arguing that non-technical audiences offer more durable SEO value.
key_points: Linkbait for non-technical audiences should be evergreen content that compounds over years, unlike Digg-style content that dies in 48 hours
Scale transforms a forgettable resource into a remarkable one — going from 15 handcrafted word lists to hundreds via automation and freelancers created something worth bookmarking and sharing
Clean, descriptive URLs serve triple duty: they help users understand content before clicking, signal relevance to search engines, and make links more clickable when shared by non-technical people
The entire free resource site functions as marketing for the paid product, achieving an effective CPM of $40 versus the $2 that AdSense would generate
Long-tail SEO works like a snowball — individual pages attract niche links that lift the entire site, which attracts more links in a compounding cycle
money_quotes: There are 10 kinds of people on the Internet: those that link, and those that don't.
Trust is the currency of the web and the currency of SEO.
The Digg crowd has the attention span of an ADHD squirrel on illegal substances and has negative a billion interest in yesterday's news, with the possible exception of classic Nintendo games.
If you have a non-technical audience, you are in the evergreen farming business. Plant them, water them, and watch them grow.

The general idea is to so overwhelm my visitor with abundance that they think 'Wow, I can't possibly take it all in right now, but I'm going to remember this place because its sure to come in handy later!'
Every cent spent on advertising on the Internet comes out of a dollar that someone made through something that was not advertising. You can have the cent or you can have the dollar — choose wisely.
That has to be the holy grail for marketing — people so want to be marketed to that they'll come to you for that express purpose and tell their friends!
posts.key_insight: For non-technical audiences, the most effective linkbait is evergreen free resources at industrial scale — content that compounds in SEO value over years while functioning as a subtle funnel to your paid product.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie writes a seasonal marketing post targeting Google searches for 'free Halloween bingo cards,' directing traffic to his Bingo Card Creator business. The post offers genuinely useful tips for parents and teachers on how to use Halloween bingo cards with children, including creative suggestions for markers and prizes. He closes with a transparent aside to his regular readers acknowledging the post is SEO-driven content. This is a textbook example of his content marketing philosophy: create useful content that naturally attracts search traffic to your product.
key_points: The post is a deliberate SEO play targeting seasonal search traffic for 'free Halloween bingo cards,' which was already sending hundreds of visitors daily
McKenzie provides genuinely useful content (activity suggestions for parents) rather than a thin sales pitch, demonstrating his content marketing approach
He uses a separate domain (halloweenbingocards.net) to capture niche seasonal traffic and funnel it toward his main product
The aside to regular readers reveals his transparent, analytical approach to marketing — he knows exactly why he's writing this post and isn't pretending otherwise
money_quotes: It is going to be Halloween shortly and Google is sending me hundreds of people looking for free Halloween bingo cards.
I am a former teacher, and we can be downright devious when it comes to incentivizing competition without crushing the spirits of the folks who don't end up first.
Apologies to my regular readers, but I was getting a hundred search hits a day. Back to your regularly scheduled content tomorrow.
posts.key_insight: Creating genuinely useful seasonal content that matches high-volume search queries is a low-effort way to funnel organic traffic toward your product.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that his new site Daily Bingo Cards is already ranking for long-tail search queries, and finds that he dominates the entire above-the-fold Google results for one query — competing only against his own other sites. He uses this as a lighthearted illustration of his core SEO strategy: dominating thousands of low-volume 'snowflake' queries that collectively drive meaningful traffic.
key_points: New sites can start ranking for long-tail queries relatively quickly if built with SEO fundamentals
Owning multiple sites in the same niche can lead to dominating entire SERPs for specific queries
The long-tail SEO strategy works by aggregating thousands of low-volume queries rather than competing for high-volume terms
Even queries with only 10 monthly searches have commercial value when you capture 80% of them and multiply across thousands of variations
money_quotes: I Googled it to see if there was any major competition for the term, and yep, there apparently is — me, myself, and I…
Multiply this by a couple thousand different snowflakes and you have my marketing strategy for Daily Bingo Cards in a nutshell.
Fifty billion snowflakes all I need is one…
posts.key_insight: A viable SEO strategy is to dominate thousands of obscure long-tail queries rather than compete for a few high-volume terms, because the aggregate traffic from many small wins adds up.

posts.summary: McKenzie previews his in-progress article on SEO for Ruby on Rails, explaining his strategy for using it as linkbait. He deliberately hosts the article on his commercial site (Daily Bingo Cards) rather than his blog, because the commercial site has more revenue potential despite lower PageRank. He also reveals his plan to use a 'Considered Harmful' title variant when posting to social networking sites to generate controversy and engagement. The post is a transparent look at the strategic calculus behind content placement and promotion.
key_points: Technical content can serve as linkbait when there's no definitive resource on a topic yet
Link placement should be driven by business value — a PR0 commercial site with revenue potential beats a PR5 blog with no direct revenue impact
Deliberately provocative framing ('Considered Harmful') is a legitimate tactic for generating discussion and links in technical communities
Creating the 'definitive resource' on a niche topic is a viable SEO strategy
money_quotes: My blog is PR5, has a few hundred inbound links, and has little direct impact on my monthly bottom line. Daily Bingo Cards is PR0, has about two inbound links, and has the potential to double my take-home pay.
Choosing to get the links over there rather than over here was not a hard decision.
There isn't a Definitive Rails SEO Resource yet, and that page has delusions of grandeur.
I expect this to make the rounds a few times in the Rails community, many of whom have their own blogs, and I expect it to get linked to heavily.
leaving the default routes in a publicly-accessible Rails application is a bad idea
posts.key_insight: Direct linkbait to the asset with the highest revenue potential, not the one with the most existing authority — SEO efforts should follow the money.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes implementing a 'popular bingo cards' feature on his Daily Bingo Cards site, which generated about 300 downloads across roughly 100 cards with minimal promotion. He references the long tail distribution visible in the download stats, where a few top pages dominate while many others contribute smaller amounts. The post also includes a candid aside about broken caching logic causing slow page loads due to Ruby iterating over hundreds of objects. It's a brief, informal update mixing product development with technical debt acknowledgment.
key_points: A simple 'popular items' feature generated 300 downloads across ~100 cards with very little promotion in two weeks
Download distribution follows a long tail pattern, with top pages capturing disproportionate traffic
Technical debt is acknowledged but deprioritized — broken caching causes slow page generation but isn't blocking users significantly
money_quotes: If you want to see the distribution of how many of those are due to the top five pages and how many are not, why not mosey on over.
Yeah, I know, I know, fix the cache or do the math at the DB, but I'm lazy and its late.
posts.key_insight: Even a simple content library with minimal promotion reveals long tail distribution patterns, where a few popular items drive most of the traffic while the rest collectively contribute meaningful volume.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie gives a rapid-fire update on his Bingo Card Creator business hitting a major milestone in October 2007. His Halloween bingo promotion drove significant traffic and downloads, while Google's Conversion Optimizer helped double his monthly sales to $2,000. He teases a mysterious offer from Google and notes the amusing reality that cheese bingo is his most popular search query.
key_points: Halloween-themed content marketing drove ~2,000 search hits and hundreds of downloads, proving seasonal content as a growth lever
Monthly sales doubled from September to October, reaching $2,000, driven by seasonal demand and Google's Conversion Optimizer
Google made him an unspecified offer, teased for a future post
The long tail of search produces unexpected winners like 'cheese bingo'
money_quotes: The most popular query is, at the moment, about cheese bingo. Oh, Internet, I love you so.
$2,000 in sales in October. Double September.
Google made me an offer I couldn't refuse.
Halloween Bingo at Daily Bingo Cards exceeded all expectations, getting me about 2k search hits on this blog and several hundred downloads.
If there were a 5th bullet point, I really would be late to work.
posts.key_insight: Seasonal content marketing combined with automated ad optimization can produce rapid, compounding growth for a small software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a humorous flowchart about Google searches and ties it back to his own advertising strategy. He notes the irony of laughing at About.com jokes while simultaneously paying them for high-performing ad placement. He teases an upcoming post about how Google's Conversion Optimizer generated significant revenue during his Halloween bingo cards campaign.
key_points: A humorous Google search flowchart resonated with McKenzie's SEO-focused audience
McKenzie was paying About.com $100 for ad placement on a high-ranking Halloween bingo cards page
Google's Conversion Optimizer reportedly generated around $500 in revenue during the Halloween season
money_quotes: I probably shouldn't laugh so much about the About.com joke, considering I sent them about $100 to put an incredibly high performing ad on that page sitting at #1 on 'Halloween bingo cards'.
posts.key_insight: Even the sites you might mock in the SEO world can be valuable advertising partners when they hold top rankings for your target keywords.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines his planned blog schedule for the upcoming week, listing specific topics for each day. He explains the scheduling is partly to avoid a Sunday crunch where church, family, bug fixes, writing, volleyball, and Mario Galaxy all compete for his time. The post gives a window into the juggling act of running a micro-ISV alongside a full-time life in Japan.
key_points: McKenzie experiments with pre-scheduling blog content to manage his packed weekly commitments
His Sunday priorities reveal the hierarchy: God, family, Mario Galaxy, customers, health, and professional duty all rank above timely blogging
The planned topics span Google Optimizer, SQL optimization for Rails, vendor recommendations, and product updates — showing the breadth of a solo founder's concerns
money_quotes: Something had to go and God, family, Galaxy, customer satisfaction, health, and professional duty won out over timely blog updating.
Tuesday is Heroes night.
posts.key_insight: A solo software founder's week is a constant triage between product work, content marketing, personal life, and everything else — and being transparent about that triage is itself good content.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his best month ever for Bingo Card Creator, crossing $2,000 in gross sales and $1,000 in profit for the first time. The growth was driven by Google's Conversion Optimizer and an unplanned Halloween advertising campaign. He breaks down expenses including AdWords ($407), freelancer content ($315), and CD fulfillment ($175). He also discusses his Daily Bingo Cards side project's long-tail SEO strategy, where niche educational bingo cards rank quickly for low-competition queries with high conversion rates.
key_points: October 2007 doubled September's revenue, reaching $2,036 gross and $1,062 profit — his first month over $1,000 in profit
Google's Conversion Optimizer and an accidental Halloween campaign were the primary growth drivers
Daily Bingo Cards operates on a content investment model: pay once for content that generates downloads indefinitely, breaking even at just four trial downloads per card
Long-tail SEO for niche educational queries beats head terms — his snowflake queries convert at triple the rate of generic terms like 'bingo cards' and rank in a week with no competition
AdWords remains the largest expense at $407, though the investment is justified by the revenue it drives
money_quotes: I had my best month ever, beating my previous best month ever (September 2007) by about a factor of two.
I only have to pay to get the cellular biology activity written once, but it will be up for years happily collecting downloads.
At my typical 2.5% conversion rate, I break even on a bingo card which delivers about four trial downloads.
It is often said by quite knowledgeable SEOs that you can't rank for anything of importance in less than about a year and a half.

Let other folks fight it out over [bingo cards] and its 8% conversion rates — my snowflakes do about triple that, you can rank for them in a week, there is no competition, and the volume will be quite nice after I start ranking for even more of them.
The first sale a month goes to hosting/domain renewal fees and it is all gravy after that.
posts.key_insight: Investing in long-tail content that ranks quickly for niche queries with no competition and high conversion rates is more profitable than competing for high-volume head terms.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie details his experience with Google's Conversion Optimizer for AdWords Content Network advertising. After months of frustration with fraudulent Made-for-AdSense sites draining his budget through deceptive click practices, Conversion Optimizer automatically allocated his spending toward high-converting sites and away from bad actors. The result was a doubling of revenue and profit margin in a single month, with the system acting as an autonomous campaign manager that scaled spending into seasonal traffic spikes like Halloween without manual intervention. He provides concrete before-and-after metrics showing a 50x increase in profit from Content Network advertising.
key_points: The AdWords Content Network was plagued by dishonest sites that disguised ads as content, generating high CTRs but terrible conversion rates that drained advertiser budgets
Conversion Optimizer works by automatically bidding based on cost-per-conversion targets rather than cost-per-click, effectively blacklisting bad sites and scaling spend on profitable ones
The system acted as a free campaign manager, automatically shifting budget toward seasonal traffic (Halloween bingo) without any manual intervention or landing page creation
Content Network profit jumped from $10 to $500/month while costs per click dropped by a third, achieved primarily through intelligent scaling rather than rate improvements
Trial-to-purchase conversion rates on Optimizer-generated traffic were 60-100% higher than typical, suggesting it found more motivated prospects
money_quotes: Conversion Optimizer makes me bid on Good sites, not on Bad sites. And that is why it works phenomenally, phenomenally well.
Why write landing pages when the rest of the Internet has already done it?

A soulless algorithm decided 'Hmm, Bingo Card Creator is having its best return on investment on these sites. I'll put most of his advertising there.' Its like my own private campaign manager… who works for no salary.
I was getting a new site using a similar technique two to three times a day.

If I pay $3 for a trial download, which converts at approximately 2.5% to a $25 purchase, I am spending $120 to make $25. Not a good idea, and I'm supporting the pollution of the Internet in the bargain.

Conversion Optimizer, unlike my hand-edits to AdWords, just kept scaling as I tossed more money at it. I ended up with a 100% ROI on the hundreds, instead of ending up with a 20% ROI on my previous tens.

I have a day job at a Japanese company, with a 3 hour commute. When I get home at 11 PM at night, I don't want to sit down and have to immediately check AdWords to see if my pocket is getting picked yet.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic optimization that targets cost-per-conversion rather than cost-per-click can dramatically outperform manual campaign management by automatically scaling spend toward profitable traffic sources and eliminating fraudulent ones.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie promotes free Thanksgiving-themed bingo cards as a seasonal marketing strategy for his Bingo Card Creator software. The post offers free downloadable cards while naturally funneling readers toward his free trial and paid product. He includes practical tips for playing Thanksgiving bingo with families, particularly with young children. The post is a textbook example of content marketing disguised as a helpful holiday resource, creating seasonal SEO-friendly content that drives organic traffic.
key_points: Seasonal content marketing: creating free holiday resources that naturally funnel users toward paid products
The free-to-paid conversion funnel is embedded organically: free cards → free trial (15 cards) → paid version (unlimited)
Practical family-oriented suggestions add genuine value and make the content shareable
The post targets seasonal search traffic for 'Thanksgiving bingo cards' as an SEO play
money_quotes: I run a business which sells software that makes custom printable bingo cards and every time a holiday comes around people come out of the woodwork looking for them.
Between working at an office supply store and then being a teacher of young students I have learned far, far too much about the personality of various types of markers.
Brown is a royal nuisance, but black, black is the killer.
Personally I'm a fan of giving everyone their cards before the big cooking marathon and letting them get a free space on the card for any dish that they help prepare.
You might to stick a little thumb on the scale for the little ones — it is unlikely they can make cranberry sauce but they can certainly help you wash cranberries, so give them credit for it.
posts.key_insight: Creating free seasonal content that targets holiday search queries is an effective way to drive organic traffic into a software product's conversion funnel.

posts.summary: Patrick announces his participation as a contributing author in a book about blogging success, edited by Steph Granger of LandLordMax. The book will feature essays and interviews from notable bloggers and uISV community members including Seth Godin and John Chow. Patrick solicits reader input on what topics to cover in his chapter, noting he's leaning toward technical SEO aspects of blogging since marketing is well-covered by other contributors.
key_points: Patrick was invited to contribute a chapter to a collaborative book about blogging success
The book features a mix of big-name bloggers and micro-ISV community members
He plans to differentiate his chapter by focusing on technical SEO rather than marketing, which other authors will cover
money_quotes: There are a bunch of big names participating (Seth Godin, John Chow), quite a few folks from our little uISV community (Andy Brice, Ian Landsman, etc), and for some strange reason I was asked to contribute a chapter.

Given that there are marketing folks aplenty on the author list I was thinking of covering some of the technical nuts&bolts of blogging, probably from an SEO angle, but that is hopefully not the only thing I know how to talk about.
posts.key_insight: When contributing to a collaborative project, differentiate by covering the technical gaps others won't address rather than duplicating what the bigger names will already provide.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains how high-ranking 'evergreen' blog posts can be strategically leveraged to bootstrap traffic for new products. He describes how his 'Free Bingo Cards' post ranks highly in search engines and generates 2,500 hits per month with zero ongoing promotion. By adding timely, topical content to the top of evergreen posts, he both refreshes their search rankings and redirects traffic to new projects like Daily Bingo Cards. The technique exploits the fact that users disproportionately click the first link in any content.
key_points: Evergreen blog posts gradually lose search rankings over time, but strategic updates can extend their shelf life and redirect their traffic value
Adding topical content to an evergreen post serves double duty: it signals freshness to search engines and creates a traffic funnel to new projects
The first link in any long piece of content gets the lion's share of clicks from both users and search engines
