Bootstrapping a new product faces a chicken-and-egg problem — hard to get visitors without ranking, hard to get ranking without links, hard to get links without visitors
money_quotes: An 'evergreen' puts the lie to blogs being a medium which only covers breaking news and the controversies of the day — they keep producing value forever, typically by ranking highly for search terms of consequence.
Hard to get visitors without ranking, hard to get ranking without links, hard to get links without visitors — it's a vicious cycle.
In the Internet and in most economic activity, winners win.
The first link in any long bit of content gets the lion's share of the clicks.
Non-motivated traffic is worthless to you, and you're not developing the sort of repeat users that you want for your site(s).
posts.key_insight: High-ranking evergreen content can be strategically refreshed with topical additions to both extend its search ranking lifespan and funnel traffic to new products.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces that his Rails SEO tips guide is nearly complete, sharing the table of contents covering on-page and off-page optimization techniques. The guide uses his Daily Bingo Cards site as a case study, covering topics like URL structure, metadata, duplicate content, sitemaps, and linkbait strategies. He notes he's been implementing his own suggestions in his Rails application as he writes about them. The post is a brief progress update requesting feedback and sharing.
key_points: The Rails SEO guide covers both on-page optimization (URLs, metadata, duplicate content, sitemaps) and off-page optimization (linkbait, bookmarks, permalinks)
McKenzie practices what he preaches by implementing the SEO techniques in his own Daily Bingo Cards site as he documents them
The guide is structured as a practical resource for Rails developers, using a real case study rather than abstract advice
money_quotes: Too many projects, too little time.
Default Routes Considered Harmful
Don't Repeat Yourself… In Your Content
posts.key_insight: Documenting your own SEO implementation process as a guide for other developers serves double duty as both content marketing and a forcing function to actually implement best practices.

posts.summary: McKenzie demonstrates what Google's duplicate content penalty looks like in practice by showing how his own blog post was outranked by every site that linked to or scraped it. Searching the exact title of his article returned ten results about the article, but not the article itself. He explains this is caused by Google mistakenly believing his content was plagiarized from scrapers or social bookmarking sites. He offers diagnostic tests for detecting the penalty and notes that inbound links from trusted sites can cure it.
key_points: Google's duplicate content penalty can cause the original article to be outranked by sites that merely linked to or scraped it
You can diagnose a duplicate content penalty by searching your exact page title — if linkers appear before the original, you've been penalized
The penalty may be page-specific rather than site-wide, which can be tested by checking rankings for other known queries
Scrapers and social bookmarking sites copying your content verbatim can trigger the penalty against you, the original author
Inbound links from trusted sites are the primary remedy for a duplicate content penalty on important pages
money_quotes: All ten results on the first page are about the article, but the article itself doesn't appear at all.
Welcome to the Duplicate Content penalty — Google thinks I am plagiarizing one of those results and, as a result, assumes my blog is not a relevant result for the query.
Any time you can punch in a title verbatim and have folks who linked to it appear before the article itself, you can be positive you've been penalized.

Since you can't control people scraping or linking to you, I recommend not worrying about it, but should this happen to you on a page you care about, an inbound link or three from a trusted site will generally cure it.
Really freaking irksome, either way.
posts.key_insight: Google's duplicate content penalty can ironically punish original authors when scrapers and linkers reproduce their content, but the penalty is diagnosable by searching exact titles and curable with trusted inbound links.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes how he became the #1 Google result for 'conversion optimizer' despite being a small blogger, by combining early adoption with radical transparency and real numbers. He explains that new niches present outsized SEO opportunities because there are zero pre-existing links and less competition. The key was not just being first, but providing uniquely compelling content—actual performance data rather than repackaged press releases. He argues this 'firstest with the mostest' approach creates a self-reinforcing advantage, as the top result becomes the canonical reference others link to.
key_points: New niches offer massive first-mover SEO advantage because there are zero pre-existing links and limited competition
Being first isn't enough—you need a compelling hook like real data that differentiates you from everyone else covering the same news
Once you reach the top of search results, a self-reinforcing cycle keeps you there as others link to you as the canonical reference
Radical transparency with actual business numbers is an extraordinarily effective differentiation strategy against higher-profile competitors
money_quotes: I was (to my knowledge) the only person who backed up the first impressions with numbers, and as a result I attracted just a few more links than the next guy and, wham, the search engines think I'm the expert on the topic.
It's not just enough to be there first, you have to be there 'firstest with the mostest.'
The 'real numbers' hook is extraordinarily more compelling than the 'news you already read from Google's blog' hook.
After you're already on top, you'll probably stay on top, because the guy on top becomes the canonical result to refer to the subject when anyone else just needs to introduce it.
I'm hardly the expert on this subject, just expert in the eyes of the computer algorithm that people trust to identify experts these days.
posts.key_insight: In emerging niches, the combination of early adoption and radical transparency with real data creates a self-reinforcing SEO advantage that even much larger competitors cannot easily displace.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces he's making his Daily Bingo Cards website statistics publicly available in real time using a Rails plugin called Sitealizer. He teases strong performance from seasonal search queries around Thanksgiving bingo cards, noting that owning top 10 spots on long-tail variations of a keyword can be worth more than ranking just outside the top 10 for the head term. The public stats are meant to keep him honest when discussing traffic numbers with his audience.
key_points: Long-tail keyword variations collectively outperform a single competitive head term ranking
Ranking top 10 for every variation of 'thanksgiving bingo cards' is worth 1.5X ranking 11th for the exact phrase
Making website stats public builds credibility and accountability with readers
Simple tools (Sitealizer Rails plugin, five minutes of work) can provide adequate transparency without enterprise analytics
money_quotes: The snowflake queries are loving me and owning a top 10 spot on every possible variation of 'thanksgiving bingo cards' is worth 1.5X owning the 11th spot on [thanksgiving bingo cards] itself.
Don't ask me how you can rank for a phrase that competitive in less than 2 months of work.
It is good enough to keep me more or less honest when discussing traffic numbers.
posts.key_insight: Dominating long-tail keyword variations collectively delivers more value than narrowly missing a top ranking on the competitive head term.

posts.summary: McKenzie recommends an SEO guide for bloggers and its companion piece revealing the marketing strategy behind it. He argues that content can be both genuinely useful and strategically designed for viral distribution without being cynical, citing Rails as another example of quality work that was also professionally marketed. He also promotes an upcoming blogging book to which he's contributing a chapter, and closes with a plug for his Christmas bingo cards product.
key_points: Genuinely useful content and strategic viral marketing are not mutually exclusive — you can design something to spread while still providing real value
Understanding how linkbait works (via the followup article) is as valuable as the SEO advice itself
Rails serves as a case study that professional marketing and viral design don't compromise quality
money_quotes: There is no reason you shouldn't promote things which are useful to your audience for your mutual benefit.
Just because you're professionally marketed and designed to go viral doesn't mean you have to suck.
posts.key_insight: Content marketing works best when the content is genuinely useful — designing something to go viral and designing something valuable are complementary goals, not conflicting ones.

posts.summary: McKenzie highlights a developer named Ezell who bet a friend he could create a sellable software business in just six days with twenty dollars, inspired by McKenzie's own rapid business creation story. McKenzie endorses the attempt and believes it's achievable. The post identifies baby tracking as a very underserved niche with real business potential. McKenzie also draws a sharp distinction between game development as a hobby versus a viable path to building a software business.
key_points: A developer is attempting to prove that a viable software business can be launched in six days with only $20, directly inspired by McKenzie's example
Baby tracking software (similar to TrixieTracker) represents a very underserved niche with strong business potential
Independent game development is a poor choice for someone wanting to start a business, despite being a fine hobby
The constraints of game development (art assets, music, polish) make rapid low-budget launches fundamentally harder than utility software
money_quotes: Ezell said 'I'll prove it!', and has decided to one-up me in the process, by shaving a few days and dollars off my schedule.
I believe this is a very underserved niche which one could really do a lot of good in with the right offering.
Developing videogames is an excellent hobby if you like it but it makes a very poor choice for someone wanting to start a business.
For a game developer to produce a game worth paying money for, 8 days and no budget for art or music assets will probably not cut it.
posts.key_insight: Utility software targeting underserved niches can be launched as a viable business with minimal time and capital, whereas creative products like games have fundamentally higher barriers to minimum viability.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces free Christmas bingo cards available on his site, offered in multiple varieties to accommodate different preferences around holiday celebrations. He splits the cards into 'traditional' (religious Christmas story) and 'seasonal' (Santa and reindeer) versions, plus a combined set. The post doubles as content marketing, driving holiday search traffic to his bingo card business. He also mentions Hanukkah cards and uses the popular cards page as a real-time gauge of which Christmas framing resonates more with users.
key_points: Holiday search traffic is a predictable, recurring opportunity that McKenzie deliberately prepares for in advance
Segmenting content into 'traditional' vs 'secular' Christmas categories serves both user needs and SEO by capturing multiple search intents
Free holiday content functions as a lead generation funnel, bringing thousands of Google searchers to his paid product ecosystem
The popular bingo cards page provides real-time market data on which holiday themes have the most demand
money_quotes: I run a small business which sells software that makes bingo cards, and every time a holiday runs around Google sends me a couple thousand people looking for holiday bingo cards.
So you know what? I decided to make some early for Christmas this year, and give them away.
But every year we hear about how some grinch saw a creche which didn't have the requisite plastic elf next to it and decided to sue everybody in sight.
Please, it's Christmas — give them to anyone you think would want them.
For the curious, wondering about which type of Christmas is big this year, take a gander at the list of most popular bingo cards, which my website keeps constantly updated.
posts.key_insight: Creating free seasonal content timed to predictable search traffic spikes is an effective strategy for driving users into your product funnel.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a brief holiday greeting to fellow micro-ISV developers, noting his travel from Japan to Chicago for family time. He teases upcoming announcements for early January including a Google partnership, the launch of Bingo Card Creator 2.0, and a year-end review. The post also doubles as an SEO play, with a postscript directly targeting Google searchers looking for Christmas and New Year's bingo cards, linking them to his free offerings.
key_points: McKenzie uses even a casual holiday greeting post as an opportunity for SEO by targeting seasonal search queries for Christmas and New Year's bingo cards
He teases multiple business milestones for early 2008: a Google announcement, BCC 2.0 launch, and site redesigns
The post illustrates how consistent content creation and strategic keyword targeting work even in low-effort blog posts
money_quotes: See you in early January for my announcement with Google, the official launch of Bingo Card Creator 2.0, the year-end wrapup for 2007, and (possibly, depending on the amount of time I have) a redesign of the Bingo Card Creator and Daily Bingo Cards web sites.
Hiya, Google searchers : You're probably looking for Christmas bingo cards or maybe New Years bingo cards.
Yep, I've got them and yep, they're free — just click the underlined blue text.
Feel free to pass those links around.
posts.key_insight: Every blog post is a marketing opportunity — even a holiday greeting can be optimized to capture seasonal search traffic and drive conversions.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie kicks off 2008 with a quick update on his plans for Bingo Card Creator and his blog. He announces version 2.0 with improved printing and 200 new word lists, a price increase, and a pending Google-related announcement. He also teases upcoming blog posts about competitors and product naming, while acknowledging his month-long absence due to work and family visits.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator 2.0 adds improved printing readability and nearly 200 new word lists leveraged from the Daily Bingo Cards side project
Price increase planned alongside a 'Back to School' sale to soften the impact — a classic anchoring strategy
A mysterious Google announcement is teased as imminent, suggesting a meaningful partnership or feature
Upcoming content planned on why competitors are good to have but mostly worth ignoring
money_quotes: If you've got it, flaunt it.
I recommend having them, but mostly ignoring them.
Sorry, gents, but I have my priorities in order.
posts.key_insight: Consistently shipping product improvements, raising prices, and leveraging side projects for content are the unglamorous building blocks of a growing micro-ISV.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his first full year running Bingo Card Creator, reporting 4x growth in both profits and sales to reach $10,500 gross income and ~$6,200 profit. He breaks down expenses including AdWords, hosting, and freelancers, noting his effective hourly rate beats his day job at ~$60/hour. He highlights key wins including SEO improvements, launching Daily Bingo Cards, Google's Conversion Optimizer, and blogging as a marketing channel. He sets ambitious 2008 goals of doubling sales to $20,000-$25,000 and releasing a new product.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator achieved 4x growth year-over-year, hitting $10,500 in gross income with ~$6,200 profit on 406 sales
AdWords was the largest expense at $1,724, but Google's Conversion Optimizer dramatically improved campaign effectiveness while reducing time spent
Blogging generated outsized returns including a Google case study feature, eight job offers, and legitimate backlinks in a niche where customers lack web savvy
The effective hourly wage of ~$60 substantially exceeded his day job salary, validating the side business model
Daily Bingo Cards launched with promising early traction and he projects it could eventually double total sales
money_quotes: 2007 was my first full year running Bingo Card Creator, and I had impressive growth over last year (about a factor of four on both profits and sales, looking at my last tax return).
Rough estimate of wage per hour worked: ~$60 (beats my day job — substantially)
Continuous improvement at very boring things, like web page design and on-page SEO.

Besides the fact that it opens up great opportunities for me, like the above Google case study and (at last count) eight job offers, it is one of the reasons I'm able to collect legitimate links in a field where most of the customers do not possess the web savvy to link by themselves.

Conversion Optimizer, which has made my AdWords campaigns much more effective than my manual tweaking ever did, while decreasing greatly the time I spent fooling with them when I could be doing stuff that mattered.
$20,000 in sales (might have to revise that to $25,000 later — I want it to be a challenge)
posts.key_insight: A solo software business can achieve meaningful income growth through compounding small improvements in boring fundamentals like SEO, page design, and ad optimization, while blogging creates outsized opportunities far beyond its direct marketing value.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a job listing for a freelance web designer to reskin a bingo card template for a new linkbait project. He offers $100 for what he estimates is 1-2 hours of work, payable via check or PayPal. The post reveals his strategy of using topical, viral content tied to bingo cards to drive traffic and links. He leverages his blog audience and reputation to recruit directly rather than using freelancer marketplaces, and sweetens the deal with a footer link that he expects will get tens of thousands of views.
key_points: McKenzie uses his blog as a recruiting channel for freelancers, bypassing freelancer marketplaces based on a previous successful experience
He's building a linkbait project around topical bingo cards, expecting it to go viral and reach tens of thousands of people
He offers a footer link as a bonus, demonstrating his understanding of SEO value as currency for freelancers
He openly acknowledges his own design limitations and outsources accordingly, focusing his own time on Rails integration and content
The post doubles as content marketing itself — transparently showing his process while recruiting help
money_quotes: Based on the stunning success of my last call for freelancers, I decided to do this here rather than wasting time and money going through one of the freelancer sites.
After you get the theme done, I'm going to integrate it with a bit of Rails and some content that I'll develop, and then launch it. And, mark my words, this is going to spread.
I'm not doing it myself because, let's face it, my design and visual skills suck.
If you're reading this blog, presumably you have trust in me already.
No, I will not let you use the link to sell Viagra or mortgages.
I don't really care where you live and what language you grew up speaking as long as you can follow directions.
posts.key_insight: A blog with a trusted audience becomes a more effective recruiting channel than freelancer marketplaces, letting you hire faster, cheaper, and with better cultural fit.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a small but meaningful moment: discovering an archived mailing list where educators were enthusiastically recommending Bingo Card Creator for teaching reading. He deliberately doesn't link to the discussion out of respect for the participants' privacy, noting that most people don't realize how much of their online activity is searchable. He reflects that while the income and intellectual challenge are nice, the most satisfying aspect is knowing his software genuinely helps teachers and kids.
key_points: Monitoring backlinks can surface genuine unsolicited endorsements from real users
Most people don't yet understand how thoroughly Google indexes and exposes their online conversations
The deepest satisfaction in building software comes from seeing it meaningfully used, not from revenue alone
money_quotes: Awareness of how much Google does to upend the expectation of privacy hasn't quite seeped in yet.
I'm most happy when folks mention how psyched they are about using it to teach their kids.
posts.key_insight: The most rewarding signal for a small software business isn't revenue but discovering that real users genuinely love what you've built.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares that his brother's blog post went viral after being featured on Fark, overwhelming their shared VPS hosting. He spent an hour stabilizing the server configuration to handle the traffic spike. The post serves as a brief anecdote about viral content and a practical warning about server administration.
key_points: His brother's 'List of Superhero Powers' post successfully achieved viral distribution through Fark
Viral traffic can easily overwhelm a VPS, requiring emergency server administration
Version controlling Apache config files is essential insurance against breaking things during crisis fixes
money_quotes: Put your Apache config files in version control because if you bork your redirection code and don't remember how you got it working the first time you will be sorry.
posts.key_insight: Always version control your server configuration files, because emergency changes under pressure are when you're most likely to break things irreversibly.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes how Google selected his Bingo Card Creator business as a case study for their Conversion Optimizer product. He attributes being chosen to his early adoption, high search rankings for the product name, radical transparency with business statistics, and authenticity. He argues that transparency and authenticity are powerful differentiators for small software businesses because most competitors won't or can't replicate them. He also reports continued strong results from Conversion Optimizer across both content and search campaigns.
key_points: Google chose McKenzie for their Conversion Optimizer case study largely because he was already ranking highly for the product and was transparently sharing real statistics
Radical transparency creates a competitive moat: if you're the only business willing to share real numbers, you effectively have no competitors for opportunities that require openness
A business's soul — humor, customer devotion, domain expertise, emotional connection — is the one thing competitors cannot clone
Conversion Optimizer continued delivering strong results at ~24 cents per trial download on the Content Network and under 30 cents on search, while freeing up 4+ hours monthly
money_quotes: On an Internet which is chock-full of scam artists and folks wanting to make a fast buck at others' expense, you can stand out pretty easily just by bucking the trend.
If you have 15 competitors and you're the only one who is radically transparent about your business, then for many opportunities you have no competitors.
The software can be cloned, the keyword lists duplicated, the advertising imitated, but no one has come up with a copy machine which can Xerox the soul of an organization that has one.

If your differentiating factor is a sense of humor, fanatical devotion to customer support, personal expertise in your domain, an emotional connection to your niche, or just the bare fact of being the little guy, that helps you stand out in a sea of mediocrity.
I don't think it compromises it nearly as much as helping me make a few thousand bucks, because that buys me an awful lot of laptop bags.
posts.key_insight: Radical transparency and authenticity are among the few truly uncloneable competitive advantages for small software businesses, creating opportunities that competitors with identical products simply cannot access.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that hiring an SEO consultant is fundamentally irrational because the incentive structure of SEO means anyone truly skilled at it would make far more money applying those skills to their own projects. He walks through the math using his own bingo card business, showing that a #1 ranking on even a minor search term could generate $10,000-$20,000/month. He systematically dismantles every possible billing arrangement (hourly, upfront, equity, partnership) and shows why none work for a genuinely skilled SEO. The conclusion is that the SEO consulting market suffers from adverse selection: the best practitioners leave to run their own businesses, leaving clients to choose among the unskilled remainder.
key_points: A single minor search query like 'bingo cards' can generate $10,000-$20,000/month for the #1 result, making SEO an extraordinarily lucrative skill to apply to one's own projects

No billing model works for truly skilled SEOs: hourly massively undercharges, upfront fees are too risky for clients, equity creates misaligned incentives, and the SEO can simply cut out the client entirely
The SEO consulting market experiences perverse adverse selection — the most successful practitioners are the first to leave for their own ventures, leaving clients with the least competent
Even top SEO firms like SEOMoz are arguably leaving money on the table by doing client work instead of applying their skills to capture niches directly
SEO products (education, tools) have a different incentive structure than SEO services, but even they can't teach the advanced skills that separate competent from exceptional
money_quotes: If you can afford to hire a particular SEO firm, they probably aren't competent enough for your needs.
SEO is a flowing river of cash if you're good at it.
In a wee, little niche, in a wee little section of the Internet, one single, insignificant query can quite handsomely support a small businessman and his family.
No sane person will charge me $X an hour when I stand to gain $20,000 a month from your services.
It is a quirky world where you get paid less as you get better at doing your job, but that is what hourly billing amounts to.
This is, fundamentally, why you can't find many good SEO consultants for hire: if they're as good as you need, they don't need you.
It is sort of like a perverse anti-Darwin evolution: successful specimens are the first to die.

Being a minorly successful little bitty fish is worth somewhere in the $80+ range an hour. If I'm absolutely clueless about this field, this implies that someone who has a clue will be dreadfully expensive.
posts.key_insight: The SEO consulting market is broken by adverse selection: anyone skilled enough to deliver results can earn far more applying those skills to their own ventures, so the talent pool available for hire is systematically depleted of its best practitioners.

posts.summary: Google released a major update to Google Analytics requiring users to update their tracking scripts in exchange for significant new features. The update includes download tracking, dynamic content tracking, improved e-commerce conversion tracking, and new reports. The old version will continue working for at least a year but will receive no new updates. McKenzie notes the migration is slightly more complex than previous Analytics setup but manageable for programmers.
key_points: Google Analytics released a new version (ga.js) requiring script updates for access to new features
Key new capabilities include tracking downloads, dynamic content, Flash/AJAX elements, and improved e-commerce conversions
The old tracking code will continue working for a year but is effectively deprecated
Migration complexity is moderate — more involved than previous Analytics setup but straightforward for developers
money_quotes: You can keep the old one if you don't want the goodies. It will continue working for at least another year, but will receive no new updates.
This doesn't require Peter Parker levels of scientific accumen but it isn't a Hulk Smash Puny Variable project, either.
Track e-commerce conversions better (here's hoping it works this time)
Free link to whoever figures out the best way to do this first, it is 2 AM and I am not doing Javascript at this hour.
posts.key_insight: Google Analytics' migration to ga.js offered ISVs meaningful new tracking capabilities — especially download and e-commerce conversion tracking — worth the modest migration effort.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he's hiring a web designer to redesign the Bingo Card Creator website. The project involves merging Daily Bingo Cards into the main BCC site to leverage BCC's superior link juice for ranking DBC's content while providing fresh daily content to BCC. He provides detailed design requirements targeting his core demographic of non-technical women in their 30s-40s, specifying a clean Web 2.0 aesthetic. The budget is $300-500 with a two-week timeline.
key_points: Merging two properties (BCC and DBC) to consolidate SEO link equity while gaining fresh daily content
Deep understanding of target demographic (female, 30-40s, non-technical) drives specific design constraints
Pragmatic approach to outsourcing: using a site redesign as an opportunity during a necessary content migration
Budget-conscious execution funded by recent revenues, treating design as an investment with clear ROI expectations
money_quotes: I figured as long as I was going to have to edit every page on a website might as well use the opporunity to make it look pretty.
I am paying for your visual and design expertise, so do what you think is best.
No visual element can suggest that Bingo Card Creator is related to gambling in any way — bingo balls, etc, are out.
I don't particularly care who you are, where you live, etc — if you can follow instructions well enough to get the job done that is good enough for me.
posts.key_insight: Strategic site consolidation can simultaneously improve SEO through link equity consolidation, provide fresh content, and justify a design refresh — turning a technical migration into a marketing upgrade.

posts.summary: McKenzie asks his readers what topics they'd like him to cover on his blog. He mentions he's working on a longer piece about SEO and information architecture for software businesses with large sites. Beyond that, he's open to writing about almost any topic his audience finds useful.
key_points: He's planning a detailed post on SEO/information architecture for sites with hundreds of pages rather than typical small brochure sites
He actively solicits reader input to guide his content strategy
He signals willingness to stretch beyond his core expertise to serve his audience
money_quotes: I'm in principle open to writing about almost anything within my capabilities, and quite a bit of the stuff outside of them, too.
posts.key_insight: A brief reader engagement post signaling upcoming SEO content for software businesses with large-scale sites.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that blogging serves as a powerful personal marketing tool by establishing expertise and opening professional doors. He cites being contacted by the CEO of Dzone to repost his content as evidence. He advocates for prioritizing content spread over control, and for creating evergreen content that retains value over years. He also reveals he avoided posting his photo for two years due to fear of age-based judgment.
key_points: Blogging establishes you as an expert in your field, which opens doors you wouldn't otherwise access
Prioritize ability to spread content over maintaining control — you still get credit as the original source
Invest in creating evergreen content that remains valuable years after publication
Reputation built through useful content creation attracts inbound opportunities from industry figures
money_quotes: One of the reasons to have a blog is that it allows you to establish a reputation as an expert in your field.
Having that reputation leads to all sorts of doors opening up for you.
I am more inclined to pick ability to spread over control — after all, you will still get credit as the original source, and you can think of it as investing in the success of your next project.
If you're going to invest time in creating content you might as well make the content evergreen.
I have resisted putting up a photo on my blog for the last two years for one reason: I have had a somewhat irrational fear of getting judged by my age.
posts.key_insight: A blog that demonstrates genuine expertise creates compounding returns by attracting inbound opportunities that would never arise through traditional networking alone.

posts.summary: Patrick announces that Bingo Card Creator 2.0 is finally complete after a month-long delay caused by a UTF-8 byte order mark compatibility issue between Windows Notepad and Java. The release adds roughly 200 new bingo card wizards, better international character support, and improved printing. He plans to release it alongside a site redesign and has deployed it to Daily Bingo Cards as an early test. He also notes he forgot to pre-announce a planned price increase and will delay it another month.
key_points: A UTF-8 BOM incompatibility between Windows Notepad and Java delayed the release by nearly a month
The update adds ~200 new bingo card wizards and better foreign character set support
He eased trial restrictions that were hurting user experience without driving sales
Automated testing gave him confidence in the release quality
He delayed a price increase because he forgot to pre-announce it, showing discipline around customer communication
money_quotes: stupid Windows Notepad inserting a byte order mark into UTF8 text, stupid Java not reading BOMs in UTF8 despite them being in the standard, stupid me for letting this delay a release by almost a month
eased some trial restrictions that were only causing problems as opposed to sales
In the crazy busyness of January I forgot about pre-announcing the price increase, so I'm going to put that off for another month.
posts.key_insight: Small technical gotchas like character encoding issues can delay releases for weeks, and trial restrictions should be evaluated by whether they actually drive sales rather than just frustrate users.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes transitioning Bingo Card Creator from GoDaddy shared hosting to a Slicehost VPS as his site outgrows shared hosting. The move is driven by plans to integrate multiple sites and do serious Rails development, including custom conversion tracking and automated marketing experiments. He details the smooth migration of email to Google Apps for Domains and his strategy of serving static HTML to handle traffic spikes. Despite leaving, he recommends GoDaddy as a solid choice for new micro-ISVs.
key_points: Outgrowing shared hosting is a natural milestone for a growing software business, not a rejection of the provider
Building custom conversion tracking because Google's 'last click wins' attribution model is inadequate — preferring 'first click wins'
Serving key pages as static HTML is a pragmatic strategy to survive traffic spikes on a budget VPS
Google Apps for Domains provided a smooth, low-friction email migration path away from hosting-bundled email
money_quotes: I am planning on doing some serious Rails-based work on the main site itself, starting with some backend improvements (conversion tracking the way Google *should* be doing it, for starters — "first click wins" not "last click wins")
As a result, I have no further need for shared hosting, and want my $7 a month back.
I will be serving key pages as static HTML until I know that traffic spikes won't bring the whole business to its knees.
This is not a rejection of GoDaddy so much as it is just a natural growth in where the site is going.
For those starting uISVs looking for hosting, I think GoDaddy is an excellent choice.
posts.key_insight: Infrastructure decisions for a small software business should be driven by what the business needs next, not loyalty to a provider — and custom analytics that match your actual business model beat off-the-shelf defaults.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he doesn't monetize his blog with affiliate links or ads despite regular suggestions to do so. He argues the blog's value lies in building trust, establishing expertise, opening doors to opportunities, and networking — all of which would be degraded by chasing affiliate revenue. He breaks down the math showing affiliate income would be modest compared to what he earns by directing effort toward his own product. The post articulates a philosophy where long-term relationship capital vastly outweighs short-term monetization.
key_points: Blogging is an investment in trust and reputation, not a direct income-generating activity — the returns come through opened doors, job offers, and expert status
Affiliate monetization creates perverse incentives to review high-commission products over genuinely useful ones, eroding reader trust
The math on affiliate revenue is unfavorable: ~$50 per optimized post versus spending that same effort improving his own product where he keeps 96% of revenue
His blog has already yielded eight job offers, a Google case study collaboration, and integration into a professional community — none of which would have happened with a monetized blog
Linking to competitors and giving away knowledge freely builds trust that is harder to duplicate than any product
money_quotes: I don't see blogging as an income generating activity. I think of it more as an investment — do the work now, reap the benefits later.
How many of you would be interested in reading the Make Patrick Five Bucks By Acting On This Post blog? Yeah, that's right. There is no reason for you to read that blog, whatsoever.

To monetize this blog, I would have to burn my hard-won trust with my readership and contacts to convince them to clicky-clicky on the little blue thingees and then hand over money to whoever was on the other end of the link.

Rather than spending the time optimizing one of my posts so that Vendor X can make 70% of the results of my labor, I should spend the same amount of time, anywhere in my business, to increase my effectiveness at doing anything by a half a percentage point.
If most of my big successes are because my readers or my customers trust me, why should I sell them down the river for short-term financial advantage?
The trust and authenticity this philosophy engenders are much harder to duplicate than my program.

It is sort of scary to contemplate, but at some point in the last year I transitioned from 'mediocre programmer with a tiny business' to 'mediocre programmer with a tiny business who generally knows what he is talking about'.
posts.key_insight: A blog's greatest monetization is the trust and expertise it builds over time, which opens doors worth far more than affiliate revenue would ever generate.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces a brief one-week blogging hiatus because a high school friend is visiting him in rural Japan. He notes the rarity of such visits given his remote location and prioritizes hosting over writing. The post is essentially a short personal note to readers.
key_points: McKenzie is taking a week off from blogging to host a visiting friend from high school
He highlights the unusual nature of receiving visitors in rural Japan
He maintains a backlog of articles he plans to write
money_quotes: Friends from high school do not routinely drop by Japanese rice fields.
posts.key_insight: A brief personal note revealing McKenzie's life in rural Japan and his commitment to keeping readers informed even during short breaks.

posts.summary: McKenzie recommends Aaron Wall's Search Value Pyramid as the single most important resource for understanding niche marketing. He illustrates the concept with his own experience: losing rankings on broad, generic terms like 'bingo cards' matters less than gaining rankings on specific, high-intent search queries like 'need bingo cards for reading class.' The post is a brief but pointed reminder that search traffic quality matters far more than quantity.
key_points: The Search Value Pyramid from SEOBook is essential reading for anyone doing niche marketing
Generic high-volume search terms drive traffic but not necessarily revenue
Specific, long-tail search queries with clear purchase intent are far more valuable
Losing rank on vanity keywords while gaining rank on buyer-intent keywords is a net win
money_quotes: It's so true it hurts, as I have to keep reminding myself every time I slip another place on [bingo cards] (oh no, my traffic numbers!) and gain another place on [need bingo cards for reading class] (oh yes, my wallet!)
posts.key_insight: A single ranking gain on a specific, high-intent search query is worth more to your business than ranking on a broad, high-traffic keyword.

posts.summary: McKenzie asks readers for help testing his software after a customer reported installation failures on five Windows XP machines. He's trying to determine whether the issue is a problematic customer or a corrupted executable, which has happened before on GoDaddy hosting. Recent slow sales add urgency to the investigation. He notes the software works fine on his own Vista machine but needs XP-specific confirmation.
key_points: A single customer reported installation failures across five XP machines, raising the question of user error vs. corrupted download
Past experience with GoDaddy hosting corrupting executables makes a server-side problem plausible
Correlating slow sales with potential installation issues shows diagnostic thinking about conversion funnels
As a solo developer without access to all OS configurations, he relies on his blog audience as a testing resource
money_quotes: That suggests to me either a problem customer or a borked executable, which has happened to me on GoDaddy in the past.
posts.key_insight: When sales dip unexpectedly, investigate whether customers can even complete the download and installation process before assuming a marketing problem.

posts.summary: McKenzie asks readers to help beta test his Bingo Card Creator software after discovering a Java compatibility bug. He had been compiling with Eclipse set to Java 1.3 compatibility, but switching to an Ant build script silently changed the compilation target to Java 1.6. Since he only had Java 1.6 on his own machine, he never noticed the breaking change for users running older Java versions.
key_points: Build tool defaults can silently override your intended configuration, causing compatibility breaks
Testing only on your own development machine masks compatibility issues that affect real users
McKenzie leveraged his blog audience as a free QA resource for quick compatibility checks
money_quotes: I had assumed, having set the compiler to use version 1.3 in Eclipse, that I was getting Java 1.3 class files.
I made an ant script and, boom, it stopped working the first time I ran the clear target and Ant used the (defaults to 1.6) javac target to make the new build.
Of course, having 1.6 on this machine, I never noticed the change.
posts.key_insight: Build tool defaults can silently break backward compatibility when you switch toolchains, and you won't catch it if you only test on your own machine.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bob Walsh's e-book 'Micro-ISV Sites that Sell!' despite his strong general dislike of e-books as a format. The book treats marketing concepts like Unique Selling Propositions (USPs) as engineering design patterns, making them accessible and systematic for software developers. McKenzie highlights how the book helped him recognize marketing improvements on his own Bingo Card Creator site, and praises Walsh's case-study approach. While noting some structural issues and arguing the $19 price is far too low for its value, McKenzie gives the book a strong recommendation as a practical marketing resource for software engineers.
key_points: Treating marketing as a design pattern — naming, studying, systemizing, and replicating — makes it accessible to engineers who would otherwise learn through painful trial and error
A USP must permeate every element of your site: tone, word choice, feature descriptions, and visual design should all reflect your target customer's worldview
The distinction between features and benefits is critical: features matter to developers, benefits matter to customers, and your site should optimize for the latter
Pricing a $19 book aimed at software business owners undervalues the product and attracts fence-sitters rather than serious practitioners who would pay much more
Writing in your customers' language improves both conversion and SEO simultaneously
money_quotes: Essentially every uISV starts out with a five page site which functions as a product brochure and can't sell their product worth garbage.

After you've named something, you can study it, and how it interacts with other named things. After you've studied it, you can systemize it. And after you've systemized it, you can replicate the success of your implementation of it.
Features are important to you, benefits are important to your customers, optimize accordingly.
One of the best ways of connecting with a given market is to speak as they speak, to share their worldview.

If your intuition about your market says that they will respond positively to your choice of language, and your market does not respond positively to your choice of language, then your intuition is wrong.

I don't think that price communicates that this is a serious resource for an owner of a software company, which is designed to make you thousands of dollars once you implement the very actionable suggestions.
Pricing for the folks who least benefit from what you have to offer is not sensible as a long term strategy.
posts.key_insight: Marketing becomes tractable for engineers when treated as a design pattern — something you can name, study, systemize, and iteratively test rather than learn through years of painful trial and error.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his experience outsourcing web design for Bingo Card Creator through eLance, from writing the project spec through selecting a designer and iterating on the final product. He was flooded with low-quality copy-paste proposals but found one standout designer, Gursimran, whose proposal demonstrated she had actually read his requirements and understood design rather than just listing tools. After several iterations on color schemes and cultural icon adjustments, he received a solid template that he then customized himself. The post serves as a practical guide to outsourcing design work as a technical founder with no visual skills.
key_points: A well-written, specific project description attracts better freelancers and signals you won't be a nightmare client
Most freelancer proposals on bidding sites are low-quality copy-paste templates that demonstrate zero engagement with the actual project requirements
The winning proposal stood out because it discussed design concepts rather than tools, showed the designer had read the spec, and asked intelligent questions before promising deliverables
Cultural context matters in design — a download button that looks like a stop sign to American users is a costly mistake
Even after receiving professional design work, a technical founder still needs to invest significant time adapting and integrating it into a real production site
money_quotes: I really lack design skills. Sure, I can edit HTML and modify CSS if given a template to work with, but visually I'm just totally lost.
After 1.5 years of growth, though, Bingo Card Creator throws off enough cash so that I can comfortably smooth over my weak points by hiring people with talent to fill them.
Holy cow, a designer who talks about design and not tools. Pinch me.
I told Gursimran that red + hand displayed palm up does not mean 'You're cordially invited to download this software' in America.
The more specific your proposal is, the better informed your designer will be and the more likely you'll attract a quality designer who, naturally, want quality clients.
Get ready to be flooded by a bunch of low-quality, copy/paste proposals on the freelancer. Ignore them, and focus on finding the diamonds in the dust.

English is funny sometimes. Examples you can point to sometimes help a lot more than our limited design vocabulary, especially when you're an engineer and your design vocabulary contains only the words 'pretty' and 'not pretty'.
posts.key_insight: When outsourcing creative work, the quality of your project brief determines the quality of proposals you attract, and the best freelancers reveal themselves by engaging with your actual requirements rather than listing their tools.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie promotes his Bingo Card Creator's free Saint Patrick's Day bingo cards, using the post as both SEO-driven content marketing and personal storytelling. He shares his personal connection to Saint Patrick, including taking the name at Confirmation, and recounts the history of the saint as a nobleman's son who was enslaved in Ireland and returned voluntarily. The post pivots to a charming anecdote about the St. Patrick's Day parade in Nagoya, Japan, which starts and ends at a Buddhist temple. A Japanese attendee explains the cross-cultural appeal: both nations are islands that love festivals and know how to party.
key_points: The post is a textbook example of McKenzie's SEO and content marketing strategy: creating holiday-themed content pages to capture seasonal search traffic spikes
McKenzie weaves personal narrative and cultural commentary into what is essentially a product promotion page, making it genuinely engaging rather than purely transactional
The Nagoya St. Patrick's Day parade anecdote illustrates McKenzie's recurring theme of cultural bridge-building between Japan and the West
The post demonstrates McKenzie's early blogging approach of mixing business promotion with personal storytelling to build audience trust
money_quotes: My name is Patrick McKenzie and I run a little business called Bingo Card Creator, which Google and company send big spikes of traffic to every holiday.
He was the son of a nobleman, got captured and sent as a slave to Ireland, escaped and made it back to his native England, and then came back to help the people he had grown to love.
It is, quite possibly, the world's only St. Patrick's Day parade which starts and ends at a Buddhist temple.
He saw a bunch of white people with strange clothes and bagpipes walking around the city, so he just tagged along and has been tagging ever since.
We have a lot in common with the Irish. We're an island nation, we love our festivals, and we know how to party.
posts.key_insight: Holiday-themed content pages are a reliable SEO strategy for capturing seasonal search traffic, and embedding genuine personal narrative makes promotional content feel authentic rather than cynical.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his experience getting new logo candidates for Bingo Card Creator after feedback that the existing logo clashed with his site redesign's color scheme. He used LogoSamurai, a service whose art style appealed to him despite concerns it might be too trendy for his target audience. The bulk of the post focuses on his admiration for LogoSamurai's checkout upsell strategy, which offered two additional logos for $50 more on a $67 purchase. He highlights how this upsell reduces buyer risk while increasing average order value.
key_points: Logo design should complement the overall site color scheme and branding rather than being treated in isolation
There's a tension between choosing designs that appeal to the business owner versus what resonates with the actual customer base
LogoSamurai's upsell strategy brilliantly reduces perceived risk (getting a logo you don't like) while increasing revenue per customer
The best time to upsell is when a customer has already committed to buying — they're easiest to persuade at that moment
money_quotes: I have some reservations that it might be a little too hip for my customers.
You're about to buy a logo for $67. But wait, for just $50 more, you could get 2 additional logos. What is not to like?

Indeed, that is a great deal and minimizes the perceived risk of the transaction (getting a logo which you don't like) while grabbing them additional sales from the folks they have already persuaded to become customers.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed upsell that reduces the buyer's perceived risk while increasing order value is one of the most effective conversion tactics, especially when the customer has already committed to purchasing.

posts.summary: McKenzie details three significant changes to his Bingo Card Creator website: adding customer testimonials to the purchasing page, implementing a smart sidebar linking strategy for SEO, and fixing a duplicate content issue caused by capitalized URLs. The testimonials were added after persistent internal prodding about credibility markers. The sidebar linking strategy rotates holiday-themed and random content links to distribute link juice across pages. The URL fix involved 301 redirects from capitalized URLs to their lowercase canonical versions to avoid duplicate content penalties.
key_points: Customer testimonials on purchasing pages serve as credibility markers that support conversions
Internal linking architecture should strategically distribute link equity across pages, with seasonal content promoted ahead of relevant holidays
Duplicate content from URL capitalization variants splits link juice and risks duplicate content penalties from Google
A 301 redirect from uppercase to lowercase URLs is the correct fix for case-sensitivity duplicate content issues
money_quotes: The little Marketing Bob angel on my shoulder kept saying 'Patrick, Patrick, where are your credibility markers?' until I gave in.
I then pad out the rest of the slots with cards and categories chosen randomly. That is iteration 1.0 of my linking architecture.
Suffice it to say its what linking would look like if the goal were to redistribute the wealth between pages.
Same content, different URLs — uh oh!
This was also catastrophically bad news, because if two URLs share the same content, they split their link juice and invite the duplicate content penalty to chase both URLs out of the index.
posts.key_insight: Small technical details like URL canonicalization and strategic internal linking architecture have outsized impact on SEO and conversions for small software businesses.

posts.summary: McKenzie discusses Google Sitelinks appearing for his small software business, noting that this branding feature previously reserved for major corporations has been democratized to include micro-ISVs. He observes that 70% of his traffic enters through pages other than the homepage, making it critical to design every page as a potential landing page. He also highlights the defensive value of sitelinks in protecting brand searches from download sites and typosquatters who arbitrage off his brand equity.
key_points: Google Sitelinks, once exclusive to major brands, have been extended to small software businesses and micro-ISVs
70% of website traffic enters at pages other than the homepage, so every page must function as a standalone landing page
Free trial pages need to sell the product independently without relying on the homepage for context
Sitelinks protect brand equity by directing most clicks to the actual site rather than typosquatters or download sites
McKenzie pays ~$25/month in defensive AdWords spending to prevent competitors from capturing his brand traffic through download site ads
money_quotes: Heck, this is the Internet: you should ALWAYS consider the possibility of folks accessing your site in essentially a random manner.
Something like 70% of my traffic comes in at an entrance other than the home page!
If you have a free trial page, you need to try 'selling' the free trial without the benefit of the homepage explaining what it does.
It seems that Sitelinks, which used to be primarily the domain of the big brands with gazillion dollar advertising budgets, got democratized recently.

Its, of course, an unalloyed good thing when you have them, since it means a search for the brand that you worked so hard building directs almost all the clicks to you, as opposed to folks trying to arbitrage off your brand equity.
I end up paying about $25 a month to download sites put ads for BCC right next to the actual download link for BCC and have customers hit it by mistake.
posts.key_insight: Every page on your site is a potential landing page, and brand protection in search results matters even for the smallest software businesses.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie discovers that Regsoft, a payment processor for software sales, has adopted the same deceptive practice previously used by SWREG: adding a hidden subscription charge during checkout that bills customers $9/month unless they actively cancel. He notes the irony that the same processor flagged his legitimate purchase for fraud screening due to his Japan IP address and US billing address, while simultaneously running what he considers a scam. The post serves as a warning to fellow micro-ISV developers about predatory payment processor practices.
key_points: Regsoft adopted SWREG's deceptive practice of sneaking a $9/month subscription charge into the checkout flow
The scam works by placing a 'Continue' button that, if clicked, silently enrolls customers in a recurring charge
Fraud screening systems flag legitimate cross-border purchases while the processor itself engages in deceptive billing
Payment processors that serve software developers have a conflict of interest when they monetize the checkout flow against customers
money_quotes: The scam is that if you hit that Continue button or the link right next to it, your credit card gets charged $9 a month until you call up the number on your statement to cancel it.

IP address in Japan + billing address in America = fraud screening. I need to remember to use my US proxy server next time, so that my profile looks like a smart crook rather than a dumb, legitimate customer.
To add insult to injury, they put my order through extended verification because they're afraid that I'm trying to scam them.
And if it were anybody but Robosoft, I would probably have called Visa already to report a probable fraud in progress.
posts.key_insight: Payment processors that serve small software developers sometimes exploit the checkout flow with deceptive subscription charges, creating a conflict of interest where the processor profits at the expense of the developer's customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a quick marketing tip about adding a free icon set to his Irish bingo cards blog post to increase click-through rates. He argues that visual elements make content more linkable and clickable, especially when the content is already useful and timely. The post is a brief observation timed around St. Patrick's Day seasonal traffic.
key_points: Adding images to content increases click-through rates and linkability
Free icon sets can be leveraged to spruce up seasonal marketing content
Visual content grips readers more effectively than text alone
Timing content to seasonal events (St. Patrick's Day) drives thousands of views
money_quotes: Add a cool looking image to useful content and you'll get much better linkable action out of it.
Folks are much more likely to click it.
posts.key_insight: Adding relevant images to useful content significantly increases engagement and link-building potential, especially for seasonal marketing.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a JavaScript technique for streamlining the software download funnel by eliminating a separate download page. The approach uses a setTimeout redirect to send users to a confirmation page after their download initiates, avoiding browser security warnings. He also implements server-side user-agent detection in Rails to serve the correct installer for Mac vs PC users. The post includes practical warnings about Rails send_file blocking and bot crawling issues.
key_points: Eliminating unnecessary steps in a conversion funnel can recover a large portion of the ~20% of users who bounce at the download page
A setTimeout-based redirect avoids browser security warnings that HTTP Refresh or immediate JavaScript redirects trigger
Server-side user-agent detection provides a platform-agnostic download URL that serves the correct installer automatically
Rails send_file can block the entire Mongrel process during file transfer, requiring architectural awareness of your server setup
money_quotes: One less step in the conversion funnel, and instantaneous recovery of a large portion of the 20% of folks who bounce out of the funnel at the download page.
If I do an HTTP Refresh or Javascript redirect, many browsers pop a security warning.
Obviously since 92% of my downloads are PC users I want to err on the side of caution.
send_file will cause your Rails process to block while that IO transfer takes place under certain older versions of Rails.
You don't want bots hitting that action, so its time for a good-old robots.txt exclusion of it.
posts.key_insight: Removing unnecessary intermediate pages from your download funnel and using clever JavaScript timing can recover a significant fraction of users who would otherwise abandon the process.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports the results of merging two sites (Daily Bingo Cards and Bingo Card Creator) and redesigning the combined site. Traffic, engagement, and trial downloads all improved significantly, but actual sales dropped from 2 per day to slightly under 1. He hypothesizes this could be natural fluctuation or existing trial users being confused by the new branding. He also shares CrazyEgg heatmap data showing how the redesign focused user attention.
key_points: Site merger and redesign dramatically improved all pre-sales metrics: visitors up 33%, pages per visit up 40%, time on site up 67%, and trial downloads up 25-50%
Despite improved engagement metrics, actual sales dropped roughly in half — a puzzling result that highlights how upstream metrics don't guarantee conversion
Brand continuity matters: users in the trial pipeline who remember old branding may distrust the redesigned site
CrazyEgg heatmaps provided actionable visual data on where users focused attention after the redesign
money_quotes: I'm sort of at a loss as to what is causing the sales to go down. Its possible that is just natural fluctuation, as all of the pre-sales indicators are way the heck up.
I also might have some folks working through the trial pipeline who remember the old branding and get turned off by the new design — "This isn't the right site!" is a very serious worry in my market.
I just upgraded to their $19/month plan because I'm getting too much traffic for the $9 plan to be useful — please, God, send me more problems like that one.
Sales: 2 -> slightly under 1.
It came out to a bit over $800 on $6,300 odd of profits.
posts.key_insight: Improving all upstream engagement metrics through a site redesign doesn't guarantee improved sales — brand continuity and the existing user pipeline can cause conversion to temporarily drop even as every other number improves.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes adding backend statistics tracking to Bingo Card Creator to supplement Google Analytics. He's teaching himself the Plotkit JavaScript library to create client-side charts that won't burden his server. He shares a proof-of-concept graph of bingo card downloads and outlines two goals: better data visualization for decision-making, and automating monthly sales reporting to reduce manual effort.
key_points: Backend stats tracking supplements third-party analytics by enabling arbitrary queries through the Rails console
Client-side charting libraries avoid server load while providing visual data presentation
Better data visualizations directly improve business decision-making, a lesson learned from CrazyEgg
Automating reporting through programming eliminates recurring manual work — strategic laziness as a feature
money_quotes: Better data visualizations lead to better decisionmaking.
Programming is the art and science of being strategically lazy, after all.
I really started believing this after working with CrazyEgg for a while.
If I can hook this sort of stuff directly into the backend sales tracking I can have the website report my monthly sales without having to do any work.
While most of it is completed, I have to get the stats out of the Rails console, which while quite useful (arbitrary queries!) leaves something to be desired in the eye candy department.
posts.key_insight: Building custom analytics and automating reporting is a form of strategic laziness that both improves decision-making and eliminates recurring manual work.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his ongoing A/B testing strategy for website buttons on Bingo Card Creator. He commissioned custom button designs from two different freelancers—LogoSamurai and his web designer Gursimran—to test against his existing buttons. He emphasizes letting data drive design decisions rather than personal opinion, and argues that paying freelancers fairly is both ethical and strategically smart for maintaining reliable talent relationships.
key_points: A/B testing should extend to every visual element including buttons, not just major page layouts
Personal aesthetic opinions should never override actual conversion data from real customers
Professionals should charge for their work and clients should insist on paying fair rates
Even small conversion rate improvements (1%) can far exceed the cost of design work
Maintaining good freelancer relationships prevents the costly gamble of finding new talent
money_quotes: I don't want my opinion on what my customers want to calcify and blind me to the actual facts of what they act upon.
Professionals should not be afraid to charge money for services, in my opinion.
If the winning set of button increases my trial conversion rate by 1% and/or my purchase conversion rate by 1%, that would work out to be quite a bit of money.
I also like keeping all of my freelancers happy with me, as I feel it will tend to get me work done in a timely and high quality manner.
I had to convince Gursimran to take money for the buttons, as she wanted to just throw them in with the price I already paid for the web design.
posts.key_insight: Every visual element on your site is a testable hypothesis, and the cost of testing design variations is trivially small compared to the potential revenue gains from even marginal conversion improvements.

posts.summary: Patrick announces a new feature for semi-automatically generating monthly sales reports for Bingo Card Creator. He explains that while the more impressive backend features can't be shown publicly due to customer data integration, he has made the monthly sales report completely anonymized. He emphasizes his paranoid approach to customer data privacy, noting the backend code isn't even on the production server.
key_points: Built semi-automatic monthly sales report generation for radical transparency
Customer data privacy treated as paramount — backend analysis code kept off production servers entirely
Public sales stats shared openly as a form of transparent business reporting
money_quotes: It is physically impossible for anyone to get from the sales data to any identifiable information about customers, which would be 101 flavors of bad.
The actual backend code is not even uploaded on the production copy of the server. I'm a little paranoid, what can I say.
posts.key_insight: Sharing business metrics publicly builds trust and community, but customer data privacy must be treated as an absolute constraint rather than a tradeoff.

posts.summary: McKenzie highlights his brother's blog optimization experiments for a superhero novel site, noting that clearer header art communicating the blog's unique selling proposition increased reader retention by 25%. He connects this to his own experiments with evergreen content on Bingo Card Creator, where reducing the number of free bingo cards offered on a page paradoxically increased downloads, doubled time on site, and shifted conversions from low-value card downloads to higher-value trial downloads.
key_points: Making a blog's unique selling proposition visually clear in header art can increase reader retention by 25%
Rewriting and improving old evergreen content pays dividends for any online business
Reducing the amount of free content offered can paradoxically increase engagement and conversion quality
Shifting users from low-value conversions (pennies) to higher-value ones (trial downloads worth 60 cents) matters more than raw conversion volume
money_quotes: Rewriting old evergreen content to make it better bears some fruits.
I can vouch for my brother's finding on "adding by subtracting" — decreasing the number of free bingo cards offered on one page greatly increased the percentage of visitors who downloaded.
It also drove many folks from a conversion to a card (low value — measured in pennies) to a conversion to the trial (much higher value — 60 cents or so).
This has apparently caused about 25% more readers to stick around and actually keep reading when they're drawn into his copious linkbaiting efforts.
I do wee little experiments with my most popular pages quite frequently to see what I can do to increase their popularity and also increase the amount of value they pump into the rest of my business.
posts.key_insight: Offering less free content can increase both engagement and the quality of conversions by pushing users toward higher-value actions.

posts.summary: McKenzie recommends several newer micro-ISV (uISV) blogs to his readers, noting he tends to link only to established names. He highlights an A/B testing optimization blog, a tracker following the 'Class of 2007' micro-ISVs, and Planet MicroISV as an aggregator. Along the way, he offers a brief but pointed observation about the diminishing returns of optimizing for vanity metrics rather than revenue.
key_points: The micro-ISV blogging community was growing with newer voices worth following in 2008
A/B testing and conversion optimization were central preoccupations of the uISV community
Optimizing for metrics that don't translate to dollars or customer interest hits diminishing returns quickly
Community knowledge-sharing through blogs was a key growth mechanism for solo software entrepreneurs
money_quotes: There is a point after which optimizing for metrics not measured in dollars or other objective measurements of customer interest ceases to be productive.
I don't link out to uISV bloggers often enough and when I do it is often to the usual suspects.
Feel free to drop a link to your own blog, unless it is PartyPoker-HighestPayouts.co.ru.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing for metrics that don't directly measure dollars or genuine customer interest eventually becomes unproductive, no matter how satisfying the charts look.

posts.summary: Patrick reviews Steph Grenier's ebook on generating website traffic, finding it too introductory for anyone already familiar with internet marketing basics. The book covers 11 topics in roughly 11 pages each, resulting in surface-level treatment that won't teach experienced ISV operators anything new. He singles out pages 52-54, a case study of using a free calculator as linkbait, as genuinely excellent and wishes the entire book matched that level of specificity. He also raises concerns about affiliate links embedded within ebook content, warning that the practice blurs the line between genuine advice and paid promotion, and could turn the microISV community into a pyramid of people selling marketing ebooks to each other.
key_points: The ebook covers too many topics at insufficient depth — 11 pages per subject yields only beginner-level introductions useless to anyone already reading ISV blogs
Case studies with real examples, like the three pages on using a free calculator to double traffic, are dramatically more valuable than abstract advice
Affiliate links embedded in ebook content create a conflict of interest that makes it impossible for readers to distinguish genuine recommendations from commission-driven ones
The microISV community risks becoming an MLM-like ecosystem where members sell marketing products to each other rather than building software that generates external revenue
Keyword density is a useless SEO metric compared to link-building strategies, yet the book prioritizes the former over the latter
money_quotes: If you have done any significant reading on the topics, this e-book will not teach you much that you don't know.
If you're running an ISV, this is almost certainly going to be akin to having a computer programmer sit through a middle school Algebra I lecture.
If the entire book was like these three pages I'd be telling everybody I knew to go out and buy it today, but sadly they are an anomaly.

Once you start down this road, it becomes difficult for the reader to differentiate between the advice that you're giving because it is solid advice and the advice you are giving because it offers a solid commission.

Instead of focusing on selling products of value to customers, we start down the merry path of cannibalizing members of our community for revenue by selling them on the dream of being a successful uISV.

Instead of being an involved community of software entrepreneurs, it would be a community of MLM hucksterism, which does not bring value to anyone and doesn't generate any revenue from outside the pyramid.
posts.key_insight: Marketing advice grounded in specific case studies with real data is exponentially more valuable than generic introductory material, and communities should be wary of affiliate-driven content ecosystems that generate revenue from within rather than from external customers.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents ongoing graphical updates to his Bingo Card Creator website after the Easter traffic surge. He shows before-and-after screenshots of the site redesign, including new logos and buttons created by Logo Samurai with integration by a contractor. He acknowledges the lack of rigorous A/B testing for the header change due to technical limitations, settling instead for a simple before-and-after metrics comparison.
key_points: Website redesign included new logos, header, and matching button styles across card download pages
Easter caused a predictable traffic surge followed by a dip, affecting when design changes could be evaluated
Technical limitations prevented proper split testing of the header, forcing a less rigorous before-and-after comparison
Outsourced design work to Logo Samurai with integration handled by contractor Gursimran
money_quotes: Since it is technically difficult to split test the header on my current setup I'm going to have to sort of fudge it (i.e. look at the metrics for the next two weeks and see if it causes major changes or not).
Not the world's best experimental design but oh well…
posts.key_insight: Even when proper A/B testing isn't feasible, monitoring metrics before and after a change still provides useful directional data.

posts.summary: McKenzie addresses the common question of whether download sites matter for micro-ISV promotion. He presents data showing that download sites drive virtually zero confirmed installs for Bingo Card Creator. While he still recommends submitting to download sites for SEO backlink benefits, he concludes they are worthless for driving actual downloads. Users simply Google what they need rather than browsing download directories.
key_points: Download sites drive almost no confirmed installs, as shown by March 2008 data
The SEO backlink value of download site submissions still makes them worth doing for new micro-ISVs
Users search Google for software rather than browsing download directories like download.com
Tools like Robosoft can automate the submission process to capture backlink value with minimal effort
money_quotes: In terms of driving downloads, download sites are all but worthless.
It is easy to figure out why if you watch users looking for software: rather than going to www.download.com and searching for it, they just Google it to start out with.
I'd still recommend doing submission via Robosoft for the SEO benefits of the backlinks it will get, particularly for a new uISV.
posts.key_insight: Download sites are worthless for driving actual software downloads because users search Google directly, but they retain marginal value as a source of SEO backlinks.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains how browser download queues were causing his sidebar conversion buttons to load last, hurting conversions. He discovered that browsers maintain a FIFO queue per hostname and only download two assets at a time per queue, meaning large screenshots were blocking his important button images. His solution was to set up multiple subdomains (asset hosts) via Nginx to parallelize downloads, then hand-optimize which images loaded on which domain to prioritize conversion-critical assets. The post demonstrates his characteristic obsession with optimizing every element of the conversion funnel, down to browser rendering order.
key_points: Browsers maintain a per-hostname FIFO download queue with only two concurrent downloads, meaning asset order in HTML directly affects perceived load time
Placing sidebar content last in HTML (for SEO reasons) inadvertently pushed conversion-critical button images to the back of the download queue
Setting up multiple subdomains pointing to the same server tricks browsers into downloading assets in parallel across separate queues
Hand-optimizing asset host assignment beats random distribution because you can prioritize conversion-critical images
CrazyEgg data revealed users were clicking text links instead of buttons because the button images loaded too slowly
money_quotes: My front page can potentially take 4 seconds to load (longer than a significant portion of visitors stay!) I didn't want that.

This is to push up the main content area in the document, because search engines treat stuff near the top as more important and I want pages to rank for their own content not the content of pages they happen to link to on the sidebar.
The browser then, following HTTP specifications, starts downloading two at a time per queue.
One of the screenshots isn't even visible when you open the page (it is far below the fold, most people won't even see it!)
I set up 4 domains this way and used that to do a bit of manual queue optimization to ensure that the images with the highest payoff (big beautiful buttons!) load fastest.
They were waiting so long they had already found a text link before engaging the image.
Randomizing access to domains results in good average case load orders but if you can hand-optimize things you blow it out of the water.
