There is about a 40% chance that the large image would block one of my conversion buttons with random host names.
posts.key_insight: Page load performance is a conversion optimization problem — understanding browser download mechanics and prioritizing which assets load first can directly impact whether visitors click your conversion buttons.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces moving his blog from WordPress.com to a self-hosted WordPress installation at kalzumeus.com. He explains the origin of the Kalzumeus name — a dragon character from a story he wrote years ago — and acknowledges it's not ideal branding. The bulk of the post provides a detailed technical walkthrough for migrating a WordPress.com blog to a self-hosted domain without losing search engine rankings or breaking existing links. He uses the 301 redirect approach via WordPress's $10/year domain mapping feature as a bridge to preserve SEO juice during the transition.
key_points: Self-hosting gives control over domain, design, commercialization, and integration with other content on the same domain
The key to migrating from WordPress.com without losing SEO value is using their $10/year domain mapping as an intermediate step that provides 301 redirects
Bad naming choices early on stick with you — obscure in-jokes that are hard to spell make poor brands
Side projects often die not from bad ideas but from day job demands and lost faith in the business model
money_quotes: If you're just starting out your uISV journey, do as I say and not as I do here: obscure in-jokes which are not easy to spell are probably not the best of ideas.
I swear, even in the all-Java, all-the-time world of my day job I find myself dreaming in Rails.
He was a sarcastic, intelligent, and slightly hyperactive dragon, and makes as good a mascot as any.
You're now paying WordPress $10 a year for, essentially, a 301 redirect from your old blog to your new blog.
Nobody will even notice the second changeover, including Google.
posts.key_insight: When migrating a blog to a new domain, preserving your search engine equity through proper 301 redirects is more important than the move itself.

posts.summary: McKenzie briefly reviews Clicky, a new web analytics tool positioned as an alternative to Google Analytics. He notes it doesn't offer much unique functionality for micro-ISVs but has some usability advantages like automatic tracking of downloads and links. He highlights the importance of visual design in web applications, admitting that Clicky's attractive appearance was the primary reason he signed up.
key_points: Clicky offers a privacy-conscious alternative to Google Analytics for those wary of sharing data with Google
The tool has usability wins over Analytics but limited unique value for micro-ISVs
Visual design and 'new car smell' can be a powerful driver of user acquisition, even for analytically-minded users
Individual user tracking features exist but raise privacy concerns that need policy alignment
money_quotes: One thing folks sometime neglect when making a webapp, and I'm certainly guilty, is making it look gorgeous.
I'll be honest: I signed up precisely because it had the "new car smell" to it.
posts.key_insight: Even technically sophisticated users are swayed by attractive design, making visual polish a legitimate competitive advantage for web applications.

posts.summary: McKenzie presents a comprehensive guide to link building for small software businesses, drawing from his experience promoting Bingo Card Creator. He argues that getting links is fundamentally a sales problem — convincing webmasters that linking to you serves their audience. The post outlines eight strategies ranging from community building and blogging to creating scalable resources and writing authoritative evergreen content. He emphasizes that commercial sites can overcome the anti-commercial bias of the web by providing genuine value to communities beyond their direct customers. The core philosophy is that generosity, community engagement, and creating genuinely useful resources naturally attract links without explicit solicitation.
key_points: Getting links is essentially selling someone on the proposition that you're worthy of their time, attention, and social capital — most software developers underinvest in explaining this value

Creating free resources tangential to your product (like Dolch sight word lists for a bingo card app) generates organic links from authoritative institutions like school districts and government agencies
Link-building strategies are super-scalar: having 100 resources makes you qualitatively different (an authority) rather than just quantitatively bigger than someone with one resource
Expanding your content reach beyond your direct customer base to link-rich communities (like writing Rails SEO tips for programmers) builds domain trust even if links don't go to product pages
Evergreen content that establishes authority becomes self-reinforcing through the 'Filthy Linking Rich' phenomenon — the authoritative page on a topic continues accumulating links indefinitely
money_quotes: Us software developers don't typically start with massive amounts of marketing or sales talent, and getting a link is effectively selling someone on the proposition that you're worthy of them spending their time, attention, and social capital on you.
Folks who make money with websites are not that great at explaining the value of linking to that website to people who will not see money from the link.

Find your local community, for any value of local you can name, and engage in it. Use all of that collected wisdom from kindergarten — share, play well with others, don't pull little Suzy's ponytails.
The only difference between your customers and 'people like them' is that the second group hasn't given you money. Yet.

If you are good with information architecture, and site design, and in quickly communicating the value of what you have to the reader, I think that doing things in larger numbers turns you into something qualitatively different instead of just quantitatively different.
In any field where the cost of replicating a success is zero there is going to be one far-and-away winner and then there is going to be a massive cliff separating them from second place.

Wikipedia is quite rarely the best single resource on the Internet for something you want to know about, but it is often the first that springs to mind, and thus it is the best at being Just Good Enough For Right Now, which is apparently a market segment worth owning.
posts.key_insight: Link building for small businesses succeeds not through direct solicitation but by creating genuinely valuable resources and community relationships that make linking to you an obvious choice for others.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes feeling the entrepreneurial urge to build something new after a period of focusing on his day job. A coworker's simple question about why he doesn't just build something forces him to confront his own inertia. He acknowledges he has the funds, knowledge, and capability but lacks time due to his demanding job. He's considering a web application that can be built in short sprints, and mentions a recurring dream about an application with a terrible business model.
key_points: The entrepreneurial itch often starts from something tangential — in this case, admiring icon sets triggered the desire to build
Having resources (money, knowledge, experience) doesn't automatically overcome inertia — sometimes it takes someone else asking the obvious question
Practical constraints shape product decisions: limited time after midnight shifts points toward web apps over desktop software
McKenzie recognizes the tension between building something he'd love to use versus something with a viable business model
money_quotes: Every time I see a good, pretty icon set I get the urge to make an application just to be able to use them.
I certainly am not hurting for startup funds this time around, and I know what needs to be done to get a business up and running.
I have also been having persistent dreams of a particular application. They've gotten so vivid as to include database schema…
The waking me knows that the business model is terrible. Just asking for total failure. Nobody could ever pay money for it.
It will have to be something where I can see progress with a bunch of 2 hour mini-sprints after getting in the door around midnight.
posts.key_insight: The gap between knowing you should build something and actually starting is often not resources or knowledge but simple inertia, easily broken by someone asking the right question.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a casual life update after a month-long blogging hiatus. He describes his day job responsibilities managing Indian colleagues visiting Japan, minimal progress on Bingo Card Creator, and early research on a next project. He offers a pointed aside about Japan's engineering shortage, attributing it to poor pay rather than cultural factors. Most of his free time has been consumed by a Japanese video game rather than his software business.
key_points: Japan's engineering shortage is better explained by economics ($24k starting salary, 12-hour days) than by cultural malaise
As the only English speaker at his company besides the president, McKenzie is drafted into management responsibilities by default
Side businesses stall when day job demands and personal life consume available energy and time
money_quotes: I have a simpler explanation: there is no pay premium for engineering over a normal college degree, which means that the starting salary for a computer programmer is about $24,000, and you're more or less required to work 12 hour days at a minimum.
Given that I'm the only person at the English who speaks English and doesn't answer to "Mr. President", it looks like I'm going to end up being their manager one way or another.
I'm torn by a desire to be supportive when she says "I was grading homework until 6:30 last night!" and a desire to remind her that I'm a Japanese salaryman and that 6:30 is practically a day off.
So basically, when I get home at midnight and have two hours to kill before going to bed, its generally been here instead of the ISV.
Bingo Card Creator — Answering emails and not a whole lot else.
posts.key_insight: Japan's engineering talent shortage is fundamentally an economic problem — when engineering pays no premium over other degrees and demands brutal hours, rational people choose other careers.

posts.summary: Patrick announces he's identified a new software product opportunity while working on Bingo Card Creator. The problem he encountered meets his criteria for a viable micro-ISV product: it requires technical skill, affects many non-technical users, and has a solve-once-apply-everywhere model. He commits to a 30-day development sprint starting June 1st, inspired by the Business of Software forums' 30 Day Challenge, with a goal of offering the product to paying customers by July 1st. He invites other developers to join him in an informal parallel development effort, blogging about progress together.
key_points: A good micro-ISV product opportunity has specific characteristics: requires programming effort, is hard for non-technical users, affects many people, blocks revenue, and has a solve-once-apply-everywhere model
Artificial deadlines and peer accountability are effective motivators for side-project development
After shipping one product, the fear of being cloned diminishes significantly — execution matters more than secrecy
The barrier to entry for community building should be as low as possible — just blog about it rather than building infrastructure
money_quotes: It requires programming effort, it is really difficult for a technically disinclined person, it affects a lot of link-chucking people, it gets in the way of some people making money, it has a solve-once apply-anywhere model to it.
So, basically, it screams 'I Am A Viable uISV Problem'.
I started to see code and database schematics and promotional schemes. (Hey, THAT certainly didn't happen last time… guess I'm learning…)
I've got little interest for anything which purports to be for complete Internet-commerce newbies, as it smacks of Make Money Online and I have my sights a little higher than $10.
We'll have plenty of issues building websites without having to build a website about building our website.
Having done this once I'm much less worried about getting cloned these days than I used to be.
posts.key_insight: A viable micro-ISV product opportunity reveals itself when you encounter a problem that is technically solvable, painful for non-technical users, widely applicable, and blocks people from making money.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces unexpectedly strong interest in his proposed 30-day cooperative development sprint for June 2008. What he expected to attract one or two participants has drawn a substantial group of indie software developers. He and co-organizer Sohail set up a unified RSS feed to aggregate participants' blog posts about the challenge, keeping administration minimal. The post lists roughly a dozen participants and invites others to add their feed URLs.
key_points: Community-driven accountability structures like cooperative sprints can generate surprising enthusiasm among indie developers
McKenzie deliberately keeps the organizational overhead minimal — just a shared RSS feed — rather than building elaborate infrastructure
The participant list shows the active microISV blogging community of the late 2000s, with developers publicly committing to ship something in 30 days
money_quotes: I was sort of expecting maybe one or two folks to actually get back to me about the 30 day June cooperative development sprint. There are, hmm, substantially more than two folks interested.

In the interest of not totally overwhelming everyone and also keeping the amount of administration to an absolute minimum (I will be, shall we say, sort of busy that month…) we are going to set up a unified RSS feed.
Come back on the 1st for the official kickoff of my project, and hopefully my first of many comments on what other folks are coming up with.
posts.key_insight: Public commitment and lightweight community structures can generate surprising momentum for indie software development sprints.

posts.summary: McKenzie kicks off a 30-day sprint to build a new product: a widget-building platform for small businesses. He argues that while the Internet made spreading ideas cheap, the actual mechanics of sharing — linking — remain surprisingly hard for non-technical people. He proposes a service that lets businesses create embeddable widgets without programming expertise, combining data sources and presentations through a simple interface. The business model is subscription-based, with tiered pricing tied to feature access. He sets deliberately modest growth goals: one customer, then ten, then a hundred, then a few hundred paying subscribers — enough to support him indefinitely.
key_points: Linking is the Internet's foundational building block, but it's surprisingly difficult for non-technical users and limited in its expressiveness
Widgets represent an evolution beyond links — they carry their own reason for spreading and embed their own sharing mechanism
Most businesses can't create widgets because it requires web app development, databases, JavaScript, and hosting infrastructure
The proposed platform would let non-technical users snap together pre-coded building blocks to create widgets in minutes instead of paying thousands for custom development
A few hundred paying subscribers at $10-100/month would generate enough revenue to be sustainable, rejecting Silicon Valley-style growth fantasies
money_quotes: I pay about twenty two cents to get my software in front of people. If you had said that to an AOL exec in the nineties, he would have paid me a few hundred million and told me to print enough CDs to wallpaper every home in Africa.
After all that effort spent in getting your fans to care about you, we consistently fail at giving them the tools to show the world they care.
Its so foundational, so ingrained into the soul of us tech folks, that we forget how difficult and limiting it is.

And what did mankind come up with once it started embracing widgets? Well, a lot of Would You Survive The Zombie Apocalypse quizzes and What Kind of My Little Pony Are You badges. But there is the potential for so much more.

Now, if I were a Silicon Valley entrepeneur I would say 'We're looking at the potential of 100,000 customers by next year, giving us a valuation of $8.7 billion'. Happily, I don't live in Silicon Valley, and thus have more modest aspirations.
I'm going to start by getting myself a customer. Then I'm going to get 10. Then I'm going to get a hundred. And then I'm going to get a few hundred.
Its a lot like AdSense, except minus the ad blindness, the constricting limitations of that ad format, and, oh yeah, the cost.
posts.key_insight: The Internet made idea-spreading cheap but left the actual mechanics of sharing — embedding rich, interactive content — inaccessibly hard for non-technical businesses, creating a market opportunity for a widget-building platform.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports a low-productivity day during his 30-day development sprint, having only sketched some interface screens after a long workday and typhoon-delayed commute. He highlights other participants in the 30-day challenge, particularly a poker graphing utility, noting his curiosity about marketing in a spam-saturated niche. The post is a brief daily check-in reflecting the reality that not every day in a sprint produces meaningful output.
key_points: Long work hours and external factors like weather can derail side-project productivity on any given day
Marketing a legitimate product in a niche dominated by scammers is an interesting and difficult challenge
Community accountability through shared development sprints helps maintain momentum even on low-output days
money_quotes: After getting out of work at 8 PM (early) and back home at 12 PM (typhoon) I find myself with not an amazing amount of productive energy for today.
I'd be interested in seeing how he plans to promote something cleanly in an online field saturated with scum and villany.
posts.key_insight: Even during focused development sprints, the reality of a day job and life circumstances means some days produce little more than throwaway sketches, and that's acceptable.

posts.summary: McKenzie reflects on the difficulty of maintaining a side project development schedule while working a demanding day job with long hours and a brutal commute. He describes an intellectually satisfying day at work where he wrote 800 lines of elegant code solving a complex constraint satisfaction problem, comparing it to a zoo exhibit placement puzzle. Despite making no progress on his own projects, he finds satisfaction in the craftsmanship of his day job work, while acknowledging the tension between employment demands and entrepreneurial ambitions.
key_points: A demanding day job with long hours and commute leaves little energy for side project development
Complex constraint satisfaction problems are among the rare cases where a CS degree's theoretical foundations prove directly useful
The intrinsic satisfaction of solving hard technical problems can keep you working late even when it's not your own business
Having 600 automated tests pass on elegant code is a deeply rewarding engineering experience
money_quotes: This was much easier last time, when I was at the job which got out at 4:30 and has a 20 minute commute, rather than the one that got out at 9:00 with the 2 hour commute…
I did manage to put some of the finishing touches on the best 800 lines of code I've ever written.
Sadly the day job owns it so I can't go around showing it off, but darn, when all six hundred automated tests worked it was a beautiful, beautiful thing.
Its probably one of the few times where I actually got to put my CS degree qua CS degree to use, rather than just using programming skills.
I stayed a little later than I was planning not so much because this was urgent to get done today but because I was so close to beating the stupid thing.
posts.key_insight: The tension between a demanding day job and side project ambitions is real, and the intellectual seduction of hard engineering problems at work can further delay entrepreneurial progress.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a brief update on the 30 Day Sprint challenge, pointing readers to a fellow participant's summary chart of all participants. He outlines his own modest agenda for the evening: watching BSG, setting up a Rails environment, and building a first proof-of-concept RSS widget.
key_points: Community collaboration in the 30 Day Sprint challenge, with participants tracking each other's progress
Starting with the simplest possible proof of concept — an RSS feed widget — rather than overengineering
The value of others doing organizational work you planned to do yourself, freeing time for building
money_quotes: I love it when people do things I was meaning to get to… less time typing means more time…. typing.
posts.key_insight: Start with the simplest possible proof of concept and let community members handle organizational overhead so you can focus on building.

posts.summary: McKenzie highlights 'Don't Wreck My PC' as the best app name among participants in a development sprint. He praises the name for its brandability and ability to serve as both a product name and unique selling proposition. He also compliments the logo's bright, colorful design and recommends skinning the app to match. He shares that making his own app look more 'Fisher Pricey' improved conversions by about 50%.
key_points: A program name that doubles as a USP is extremely valuable for marketing
Keywords in program names help with SEO through anchor text, but brandability matters more for sales
Bright, colorful B2C design that looks approachable (Fisher Price-style) can dramatically improve conversions
Visual consistency between logo and app UI helps drive conversions
money_quotes: Your program name is also your USP. Sweet deal.
Bright and colorful does great things in B2C.
Oh crikey does that help conversions (by about 50% when I made my app look more Fisher Pricey).
posts.key_insight: The best product names simultaneously serve as a unique selling proposition, and matching your app's visual design to a bright, approachable aesthetic can dramatically improve conversion rates in B2C markets.

posts.summary: Patrick reports progress on his 30-day sprint to build a widget product, having solved a key technical challenge around making widgets viral using a modified Lightbox library. He has a working JavaScript front-end prototype but needs to build the back end. His weekend plan is to create a Bingo Card Creator-specific widget as a proof of concept that can drive immediate business value while he continues building the full widget creation platform.
key_points: Found an open-source solution (Lightbox Gone Wild) that solved the core virality challenge by displaying widget source code inline without redirecting visitors away from the host page
Strategic approach of building a specific widget for his existing product first to generate business value before the general platform is ready
Acknowledges the long tail of work beyond the core product: account management, billing, analytics, marketing copy, and scalability
money_quotes: I *love it* when other people do my work for me.
Plus, score, it will start driving business value for me even without having the widget creator website up.
No point in being viral if you can't tell how viral you are.
posts.key_insight: When building a platform product, start by creating a specific instance for your own existing business to validate the concept and generate immediate value before tackling the general case.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts a day where his planned prototype work was derailed by helping college friends migrate an old VBulletin forum to his VPS. He describes the horror of the forum's previous security state, including outdated PHP, MySQL running as root, and a trivially guessable password. He pivots to praising VPS hosting over shared hosting for technically skilled developers, noting he runs multiple sites cheaply on Slicehost. The post closes with his hope that his new project will need serious infrastructure scaling.
key_points: Technical favors for friends can consume an entire day of productive development time
Many small web applications run with shockingly poor security configurations that invite exploitation
VPS hosting offers dramatic advantages over shared hosting for developers with Linux administration skills
McKenzie runs multiple businesses and blogs on cheap VPS instances for about $20/month each
money_quotes: Nice update interface, very user friendly — a pity about it taking 400 clickthroughs to complete installation (I kept track).
I wanted to cry when I saw its previous state — using an outdated PHP and MySql running as root, naturally, with a four letter password the same as the name of the previous site owner.
I continue to be amazed by how awesome it is to have a VPS over shared hosting, provided that you have some skill in Linux administration.
First Prayer of Computer Programmers with Business Sense: Please Lord, send me scaleability issues.
Suffice it to say I got a lot of use out of rand() today.
posts.key_insight: The best problem a software entrepreneur can have is needing to scale, and cheap VPS hosting makes it easy to run multiple small businesses affordably while maintaining control.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide for micro-ISV owners using e-junkie as their payment processor to hide the e-junkie branding from customers. He covers three specific issues: redirecting customers to your own thank-you page after checkout, fixing email deliverability problems caused by SPF record mismatches, and using DNS CNAME records to mask e-junkie URLs. The post reflects his philosophy that professional branding matters even for small software businesses. He emphasizes security considerations when implementing URL-based parameter passing and notes that these small details affect conversion rates and customer trust.
key_points: Redirect post-checkout pages to your own domain to display CD keys and instructions, rather than showing customers an e-junkie branded page
Publish SPF records so e-junkie's servers are authorized to send email on your behalf, preventing spam filters from rejecting purchase confirmations
Use DNS CNAME records to alias your own subdomain to e-junkie, hiding third-party branding from the URL bar
Customers don't read post-purchase pages unless given obvious reasons to, so make key information prominent to reduce support costs
money_quotes: I really, really can't stand the name — it says absolutely nothing positive to my customers about my business.
Make it big and bold because people do not read this page unless you give them a darn obvious reason to. Getting them to read it saves you support costs, trust me.
Either way, please secure your website against ye-olde insert-arbitrary-content-by-rewriting-the-URL trick. You'll thank me later.
Bad news bears, this means that many automated checks on that email will say 'e-junkie.com is spoofing honestmicroisv.com, oh no, its spaaaaam!'
You aren't using the Fat Free Cart? OK, fix that right now. And watch your conversion rise!
posts.key_insight: Small software businesses should invest effort in hiding third-party service branding from their checkout flow, because professional presentation directly affects customer trust, email deliverability, and conversion rates.

posts.summary: McKenzie notes that the Antair team, fellow micro-ISV entrepreneurs he has followed, are moving up to bigger operations with new offices. He reflects on how Andrey from Antair used to benchmark his sales against McKenzie's, but has since surpassed him significantly. The brief post serves as a collegial shout-out within the small software business community.
key_points: Antair has grown from a micro-ISV to requiring real office space, signaling meaningful business growth
McKenzie reflects on the early days when peers benchmarked against each other's modest sales numbers
The micro-ISV community was tight-knit enough that founders tracked each other's progress openly
money_quotes: I remember back when Andrey was still comparing his sales to mine to see if he was on a good track.
Suffice it to say that he's since made it necessary for me to play a wee bit of catchup.
posts.key_insight: In small software entrepreneurship communities, founders openly benchmark against peers, and watching colleagues outgrow you is both humbling and motivating.

posts.summary: Patrick shares an early proof of concept for an embeddable widget feature for Bingo Card Creator during his 30-day development sprint. The widget allows content to be distributed across external sites like teacher blogs. Despite its rough appearance, he sees the strategic value: viral distribution that spreads content at near-zero marginal cost, replacing expensive CPC advertising. The next step is generalizing the widget so non-technical users can deploy it without Rails programming knowledge.
key_points: Embeddable widgets enable viral content distribution at zero marginal cost compared to paid advertising
The proof of concept prioritizes functionality over aesthetics, with design help coming later from a collaborator
The widget strategy deepens relationships with core followers by giving them free, useful content to share
The key engineering challenge is making the widget self-service rather than requiring custom Rails development
money_quotes: I have the artistic ability of paint. Not MSPaint, no, just paint.

It propagates itself, for no marginal cost whatsoever, spreads the content I spent so much time and money creating, and gets me new visitors and eventually customers at a bare fraction of the cost of CPC advertising.
That is a win-win-win.
Now if I can just generalize it and make it possible to do without a few hours of Rails programming, I can start printing money hats. Small, modest money hats to start with.
Imagine that spread over a few dozen teacher blogs — it propagates itself, for no marginal cost whatsoever.
posts.key_insight: Embeddable widgets turn your existing customers into a zero-cost distribution channel, replacing expensive paid advertising with viral content that deepens relationships while acquiring new users.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents a productive day working on his new SaaS product during a 30-day development sprint. He spent most of the day wrestling with deployment tooling (Capistrano, Deprec) to get Rails running on a new Slicehost VPS. He outlines a 12-item implementation agenda for the product, which appears to be a widget platform designed to help users achieve viral distribution. The post captures the unglamorous reality of early-stage product development where deployment infrastructure consumes most of a 'productive' day.
key_points: Deployment tooling and infrastructure setup consumed most of an ostensibly productive development day
The product is a widget platform designed to enable viral distribution, with embeddable off-site components
His 12-item implementation agenda reveals the full scope of building a SaaS product: from core features to billing, analytics, and admin tooling
He deliberately limits what he shows off to features that will ship, avoiding premature announcements
money_quotes: I spent most of the day fighting old, outdated versions of Capistrano and Deprec to let me get Rails to a deployable state from my Vista workstation to my shiny new 512 MB Slicehost slice.
the entire point of the system is to help people go viral. (Hopefully not TOO viral…)
I only want to show off stuff that will, for the moment.
Billing (ugh — not even decided on a payment processor)
posts.key_insight: Even highly productive development days are mostly consumed by infrastructure and deployment plumbing rather than building the actual product features.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that a stressful personal situation has resolved, though not in his favor. The two weeks lost to worrying have put his 30-day development sprint behind schedule, making it unlikely he'll have a saleable application by the deadline. He's resetting his goal to have something publicly demonstrable by the 1st, with an actual release to follow later. He explains why he can't just ship a half-finished product: since widgets live on third-party sites, bugs could break other people's websites.
key_points: Personal stress and worry can consume productive development time just as effectively as technical blockers
When building products that run on third-party sites, the quality bar for shipping is higher because your bugs affect other people's properties
Adjusting launch timelines and setting realistic revised goals is better than pretending the original deadline is still achievable
money_quotes: Sadly, it did not resolve itself to my benefit, but it is no longer hanging in the air, which means I can get on with life.

I would happily launch with a half-finished app that doesn't even have billing integrated, but the nature of widgets is that they exist on third party sites, and half-finished could very well mean that widgets break on third party sites, which is just not good behavior.
posts.key_insight: When your product runs on other people's websites, you can't ship fast and break things — their reputation is on the line, not just yours.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that open source software is a powerful strategic asset for proprietary software developers, not a threat. He demonstrates how OSS dramatically reduces development time by letting developers leverage thousands of man-months of existing work while focusing on their unique value-add. He outlines multiple business benefits: lower development costs, SEO advantages from community engagement, and a 'quality cliff' that acts as a barrier to entry against competitors not using OSS. The post reframes the typical proprietary-vs-open-source debate as a false dichotomy, showing how commercial developers can strategically contribute to and consume OSS simultaneously.
key_points: OSS lets solo developers leverage thousands of man-months of existing work while adding only months of their own glue and secret sauce
Rather than open-sourcing your core product, contribute boring infrastructure pieces tangentially connected to your business for social and SEO benefits
OSS creates a 'quality cliff' barrier to entry: competitors not using it must build from scratch what you integrated in minutes
The skill of integrating diverse OSS projects into a coherent product is itself a competitive advantage that's harder than straight application development
OSS developers are high-value linkerati who provide SEO benefits and community engagement even if they never buy your product
money_quotes: I honestly think that it is impossible, flat out impossible, for any one person to be expert at all the technologies which are implicated by a trivial web application running on a modern web stack.

I'm probably literally using several thousand man months worth of software, adding two man months worth of glue and secret sauce, and then if all goes according to plan making a bit of money off of it.
You can't just whistle your fingers and snap together 15 diverse and unconnected OSS projects into a usable application — the design and integration of complex systems is still a skill.

Every time you spend a small quantum of effort to integrate a new feature that you didn't have to write, your user-perceived quality gaps up. Someone running along on your old curve, trying to keep up, runs straight into the quality cliff-face.
Now Bob isn't just running the race against you — he's running against the team at Google doing the Charts development and you're already done and busy working on your next feature.
We live in an era where, for better or worse, GoogleBot is a judge of character and strongly prefers people who share.
OSS developers are typically not poor aesthetics who need to beg for their next cup of coffee.
posts.key_insight: Open source software is not a threat to proprietary developers but a force multiplier — the real competitive advantage lies in the skill of integrating diverse OSS projects into a coherent product that competitors cannot easily replicate.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie introduces Widget Bakery, a new project developed during a 30-day sprint, which allows users to create embeddable widgets for third-party websites. He shares progress from an intensive design session with his designer, showing the front page design and a screenshot of a functioning widget. The project involves complex Rails, JavaScript, and iframe integration to inject widgets into any webpage. He notes significant remaining work including GUI development, caching, error handling, and CSS fixes before launch.
key_points: Widget Bakery is a tool for creating embeddable widgets that can be injected into third-party websites using iframes, JavaScript, and DOM manipulation
Rails does not automatically add database indexes on id columns, leading to costly full table scans when pulling linked objects — a gotcha that nearly melted his laptop during testing
Working with a designer who is faster at design than Patrick is at web programming highlights the value of collaboration and complementary skills
The project is being developed during a 30-day sprint with ambitious goals including launching with a 'baker's dozen' of widget types
money_quotes: Word to the wise, kiddies: despite it being so obviously necessary you'd expect them to do it for you, Rails will not automatically slap database indexes on your id columns, and then you get to do full table scans every time you pull in linked objects.
I really can't show you anything useful this early in development.
I would really, really hate if one of my widgets actually became popular before I had, oh, put indexes on the table ids or turned on caching.
Sadly he is much better at design than I am at web programming, so I did not progress quite as far, but things are looking up.
Lots of work to do, no time to do it — time to shake and bake!
posts.key_insight: Launching a new product during a time-boxed sprint forces pragmatic tradeoffs between showing progress and building robust infrastructure, with database performance gotchas lurking beneath seemingly simple frameworks.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents a frustrating 2.5-hour debugging session caused by Rails 2.1's new partial updates feature silently breaking serialized column saves. When modifying a hash stored in a serialized column, Rails didn't detect the change as 'dirty,' causing database writes to skip the updated data. He presents both a hacky workaround (cloning and reassigning the hash to trigger the dirty bit) and a cleaner solution (disabling partial updates on the model). The post critiques Rails' 'opinionated software' philosophy for introducing breaking changes that are on by default.
key_points: Rails 2.1's partial updates feature only saves 'dirty' attributes, but modifying the contents of a serialized hash doesn't trigger the dirty flag
The workaround involves either cloning/reassigning the hash to force the dirty bit, or disabling partial updates entirely on the model
Upgrading frameworks on a whim—even when you think there's little code to break—can introduce subtle bugs against your mental model
Rails' 'opinionated software' defaults can be hostile when breaking changes ship without obvious warnings to upgrading developers
money_quotes: I stupidly upgraded to 2.1 on a whim, thinking that I hadn't written any significant code yet so incompatibilities wouldn't bother me — true enough, the only incompatibility was with my mental model of how ActiveRecord worked.

The fact that this is on by default appears to be one of those opinionated software practices where 'opinionated' means 'it is our opinion that you should be as familiar with the change log as the core team before you install a new production release'.
Inconsistent out-of-date data. Failure.
I was absolutely clueless how this was happening, but I successfully diagnosed the why — a dirty bit wasn't getting set for the serialized object.
No problem, the same thing happens at the day job with our Java system — which means I can use the same hacky solution to the problem.
posts.key_insight: Framework upgrades can silently break your mental model of how things work, and 'opinionated' defaults that change behavior without obvious signals create insidious bugs that waste hours of debugging.

posts.summary: McKenzie reviews 'Blog Blazers,' a book he contributed a chapter to, which collects interviews with 40 successful bloggers including Seth Godin and Jeff Atwood. He highlights the fascinating diversity of answers to identical questions about blogging success, and notes areas where the authors disagree with each other. His own chapter focuses on practical blogging tips for small businesses, and he playfully acknowledges disagreeing with his marketing idol Seth Godin on the value of list posts. He recommends the book as a valuable resource for small software businesses trying to figure out how blogging fits into their marketing strategy.
key_points: The book's strength lies in the diversity of perspectives from 40 bloggers answering identical questions, revealing genuine disagreements about monetization, post length, SEO, and writing style.

McKenzie disagrees with Seth Godin on list posts, arguing they turn a blog into a commodity provider while acknowledging the underlying principles of promising benefits in titles and using formatting to engage readers.
His core blogging advice is to build relationships with two groups: aspirational peers who've achieved your goals, and near-peers who are slightly ahead of you on the path to success.
He recommends buying through Amazon rather than direct because publishing is a winners-win game where higher Amazon rank creates a virtuous cycle of visibility.
He humorously acknowledges not following his own advice about sustainable blogging pace, a recurring self-aware theme in his writing.
money_quotes: Recognize that you will be blogging until you stop blogging. That sounds simple, but many people start out with a burst of post-every-day fever, which they cannot sustain over the long haul. Pick a pace which is predictable and sustainable.
I personally can't stand the 'Top Ten Ways To Write A Blog Post' type articles, as aside from being boring and aesthetically unpleasant they turn your blog into a commodity provider of lists.
Titles which promise immediate benefits are a good idea and judicious use of formatting such as bullet points, bold text, and pictures can turn a scanning surfer into an engaged, active reader.

The folks who have achieved something close to what you want to achieve give you examples to emulate and objectives to strive for, and the folks just a wee bit farther than you should become your new best friends.
Seth Godin is one of the world's best living theorists about marketing. I am not Seth Godin. You are not Seth Godin. Please, for all that is holy, don't write list posts.
Publishing is a winners-win game, and purchases through Amazon increase the book's Amazon rank, which results in more prominent placement and indirect marketing opportunities.
posts.key_insight: The best blogging advice comes from studying both aspirational successes and near-peers, and the real skill is extracting principles from common tactics rather than blindly copying formats like list posts.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains that PayPal does allow customers to purchase without a PayPal account, despite widespread belief to the contrary among even experienced sellers. He walks through how PayPal's checkout flow presents different interfaces based on whether the customer has a PayPal cookie. He then explains the financial incentives behind this design: PayPal maximizes profit by steering existing users toward balance or ACH payments (which cost less than credit card interchange fees) while still allowing guest checkout to avoid losing conversions from non-members.
key_points: PayPal guest checkout without an account is possible and well-supported, despite being poorly documented and widely misunderstood by sellers
PayPal's checkout flow is cookie-based: cookied users see a login prompt, while non-cookied users see a split screen with guest checkout and sign-in options
PayPal's revenue model depends on interchange fees, and they profit most when users pay via PayPal balance (free) or ACH (cheap) rather than credit cards
The guest checkout exists because requiring PayPal accounts would cost conversions, which costs PayPal revenue
money_quotes: This fact isn't very well documented and I've seen folks who have sold $100,000+ with Paypal tell you it is impossible.
You have to understand that Paypal's primary source of revenue is interchange fees. And their primary expense? Also interchange fees.
If, however, that customer had a Paypal balance and paid through Paypal, completing the transaction is free to them — all they have to do is a database update on their own records.
Conversions are much higher when all you need to do is convince people to signin and click 'Purchase' rather than filling out a dozen fields in a form.
For those folks who are holdouts about getting a Paypal account, requiring them to get one costs conversions and thus costs Paypal money.
Paypal's check out experience is optimized to make them money, but as a side effect that means it is also optimized to make you money.
posts.key_insight: PayPal's checkout flow is engineered around their interchange fee economics, which incidentally means it's also optimized to maximize conversions for sellers by automatically presenting the right purchasing option to each customer.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes discovering that a caching bug had been silently killing his content-driven SEO strategy for months. A cron job running under the wrong Unix user failed to clear cached pages, making his dynamic bingo card site appear static and dead to Google. The bug was masked by his development deploys, which incidentally cleared the cache. After fixing the five-minute bug, Google reopened the 'long-tail floodgates' and his traffic more than doubled. The post is a cautionary tale about testing infrastructure with the same rigor as application code.
key_points: A trivial Unix permissions error in a cron job caused cached pages to never refresh, making a dynamic site appear dead to search engines for months
The bug was invisible during development because deploys had the side effect of clearing the cache, so McKenzie only ever saw fresh content
Google demonstrably values fresh content — when the site went stale, traffic cratered; when it came back to life, traffic more than doubled
No user reported the problem, but many noticed when it was fixed, illustrating that users silently abandon broken experiences rather than complaining
The biggest impact on his business wasn't a feature, redesign, or ad spend increase — it was fixing one infrastructure bug
money_quotes: Caching is a wonderful tool. You can use it to solve just about any performance problem. It solves it by replacing the problem with a cache expiration problem.
No performance problem? Oh, you get to deal with cache expiration anyway, don't worry.
And they all pale in comparison to squashing one stupid little bug.
Test your website with the same diligence you test your application.
Pay attention to your website. Even the boring bits. Especially the boring bits that make you money.
Never send a committed Windows programmer to do a Unix sysadmin's job.

No user thought this was important enough to email about — and would you email if a favorite website went dead for a while during the summer? — many sure thought it important enough when it was fixed to take notice.
posts.key_insight: The infrastructure that silently delivers your content to search engines deserves the same testing rigor as your application code, because a trivial ops bug can dwarf the impact of any feature or marketing initiative.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his experiment with exact match domain names as an SEO strategy, purchasing christmasbingocards.com for $149 to capture seasonal search traffic for Christmas bingo cards. He outlines a seven-step process for creating a focused mini-site designed to rank quickly for specific keywords, from domain purchase through WordPress setup to content creation. The strategy prioritizes link acquisition over direct sales, reasoning that an attractive, purpose-built free resource will naturally attract links from parents while converting teachers into paying customers. He frames this as a test of Aaron Wall's teachings about exact match domains outranking older, more established sites.
key_points: Exact match domain names can help new sites rank quickly in Google, even past older sites with more links
The mini-site strategy separates link acquisition (free resource) from monetization (commercial product), since people link more readily to non-commercial sites
Seasonal traffic patterns represent massive business opportunities — holidays account for nearly a quarter of his downloads, with Halloween driving his best month every year
The total investment ($157 for domain) compares favorably to ongoing AdWords spend that evaporates weekly, while the domain produces value for years
If the single mini-site experiment works, the strategy scales to multiple seasonal or niche-specific sites
money_quotes: I'd make a mostly non-commercial mini-site focused like a freaking laser on giving people exactly what they wanted, and making it as easy to link to as humanly possible.
It is fairly difficult (though by no means impossible) to get people to link directly to a commercial site.
I'll spend more on AdWords this week alone than the domain cost me, and as of next week its like that money was never spent… but the domain stays up for years.
You're creating a site to be the One Canonical Source On The Internet for your little subniche — it should look the part.
An attractive site makes it very easy for it to collect links from your customers saying 'Hey, Ethyl, I just saw the best site on the Internet for Christmas bingo cards.'
Look at the design! Look at the domain name! Look at the Zen-like purity of purpose!
If it works… why stop at one mini-site?
posts.key_insight: A purpose-built mini-site on an exact match domain, designed to be the definitive free resource for a narrow niche, can acquire links and search rankings far more effectively than trying to rank a commercial site directly.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie promotes a charitable Christmas drive organized by Andy Brice, who is selling programmer t-shirts with proceeds going to charity. The two benefiting charities are Jaipur Foot, which provides low-cost prosthetics in India, and Sightsavers, which treats preventable blindness. McKenzie offers a personal matching grant of $30 per person who participates and shares a photo, capped at ten participants. The post is a lighthearted call to action that combines programmer culture humor with genuine charitable giving.
key_points: Andy Brice's Christmas drive sells programmer t-shirts with proceeds donated to Jaipur Foot and Sightsavers
McKenzie offers a $30 matching donation per participant who shares a photo, capped at 10 people
The post demonstrates how small software community influencers can amplify charitable giving through their platforms
McKenzie weaves in self-deprecating humor about programmer culture and his life in Japan throughout the pitch
money_quotes: my office will let me wear a T-shirt the day after I get elected Prime Minister of Japan
Or something which would approximate funny if programmers had a sense of humor.
For every person who does so by Christmas, I'll chip in $30 direct to the charities.
What It Lacks In Subtlety It Makes Up For In Lack Of Subtlety!
I suggest "I'm in your world debugging your illnesses!"
posts.key_insight: Software community influencers can leverage their platforms and matching grants to amplify charitable giving, even with modest individual contributions.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces a programmer-focused charity drive he co-created with Andy Brice. The project involved building a dedicated website with linkbait and Flickr integration to support the cause. He highlights the amusing international nature of the collaboration — a UK developer working with an American in Japan for beneficiaries in India. The site sells programmer t-shirts for charity and includes an essay on how open source principles can be applied to charitable giving.
key_points: McKenzie and Andy Brice collaborated across 9 time zones to create a programmer-focused charity website
The project applied marketing techniques (linkbait, dedicated site, Flickr integration) to charitable fundraising rather than just blogging about it
McKenzie wrote an essay connecting open source software principles to charity, targeting the technical community
The drive encouraged both monetary donations and contributions of time and attention as 'non-monetary alternate currencies of the Internet'
money_quotes: I thought, hey, why stop at blogging — it would probably work better with a dedicated website, some linkbait, a little Flickr integration, and the like.
In the annals of offshoring, this may well be the first time somebody from the UK "hired" an American in Japan on the behalf of "clients" in India.
Giving generously of your time and/or attention, the non-monetary alternate currencies of the Internet.
posts.key_insight: The same marketing and web development skills used to sell software can be repurposed effectively for charitable causes, applying linkbait and community engagement to fundraising.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents a failed $600 Christmas marketing experiment where he created a mini-site to give away free Christmas bingo cards, hoping parents would link to it and boost his SEO, driving teacher customers to his paid product. The experiment failed on multiple fronts: PDFs acted as 'flypaper' trapping users who never interacted further with the site, the SEO timeline was impossibly aggressive for a seasonal promotion, and paid advertising on StumbleUpon and AdWords generated traffic but no valuable links or conversions. He recovered roughly $50 in marginal sales against $600 in costs, nearly producing his first monthly loss. The post-mortem extracts six concrete lessons for future iterations.
key_points: PDFs are 'flypaper' for non-technical users: once they get the download, all site interaction ceases, making PDFs terrible for marketing funnels that depend on further engagement
Time-sensitive promotions and SEO are fundamentally incompatible because new sites take months to rank, not weeks
A high AdWords click-through rate (11%) on a free offering is actually a disaster — you're paying to give things away efficiently
StumbleUpon traffic is essentially worthless for conversion: 5 cents buys 2 seconds of attention from someone browsing channels, not someone with intent
Giving away too much eliminates the market segmentation that drives conversions — the 32-card giveaway removed any reason for core customers to upgrade
money_quotes: PDFs aren't for whitepapers, they are for flypapers.
If a non-technical user enters a PDF, they overwhelmingly do not exit.

I did it so darn well. You want to know the recipe for a high click-through rate on an AdWords ad? ... Blam! 11% click through rate to a paid listing. Which means Google gives me the top spot, and I get to have the maximum opportunity to give my money away for nothing.

When someone searches for 'christmas bingo cards' and then clicks on a link to christmasbingocards.com, you know what they are looking for? Christmas bingo cards. That blazingly, achingly clear intent is present in no other search mediums.
Paying nothing for traffic of no value is still not a good deal.
Stumbleupon: channel surfing for the Internet… or ADHD with an API?
Funny how things look cheap when you're buying them in 8 cent increments but expensive when you're buying them in $40 increments, even when the 8 cent promotion method is ultimately more costly.
Next time, use freelancers, they're cheaper than I am.
posts.key_insight: Marketing experiments fail when you optimize individual tactics (high CTR, free content, paid traffic) without a coherent funnel connecting attention to conversion, especially when your content format and timeline work against you.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bingo Card Creator's 2008 performance, reporting $20,707 in sales against ~$10,000 in expenses for ~$10,700 profit, exactly hitting his goal of doubling the previous year's revenue. The biggest win was paying a freelancer $1,100 to create niche bingo card content that dramatically improved SEO and organic traffic. Google AdWords Conversion Optimizer continued to be effective despite consuming $5,850. His side project Widget Bakery stalled due to exhausting day job hours, and ChristmasBingoCards.com underperformed expectations. He sets goals of $30,000 in sales, releasing a second product, and potentially going full-time in 2009.
key_points: Niche content creation (paying $1,100 for freelance bingo cards) was the highest-ROI investment, dramatically improving SEO by matching specific search queries to dedicated pages
BCC doubled revenue year-over-year from $10,600 to $20,707 while maintaining roughly the same expense structure, proving the business model scales
The day job's grueling schedule (out at 10 PM plus 2-hour commute) was the primary obstacle to launching a second product that could enable going full-time
Google AdWords consumed 28% of gross revenue but remained worthwhile through Conversion Optimizer, showing the tension between paid acquisition costs and profitability
The business was growing into a small ecosystem supporting five part-time incomes across freelance writers, a designer, and McKenzie himself
money_quotes: The $1,100 paid to a freelancer might stick out in the expenses — I assure you, it is like paying for straw so you can spin it into gold.
Nothing sucks it out of you like getting out of work at 10 PM and then facing a 2 hour commute.
As nice as BCC is I have dreams of eventually going full-time and while BCC provides a quite nice foundation for that I think the next project will be the one that pushes me over the top.
Number of people who sat down at their computer one day and said 'You know, I really need to play some Cheese bingo': 52
SEO is one of my favorite parts of this job — great intellectual challenge, combining a bit of engineering, a lot of marketing, and heaping helpings of pure magic.
And the loom is 95% automatic, whee.
posts.key_insight: A small software business can double revenue by investing modestly in niche content creation that captures long-tail search traffic, even while the founder works a demanding full-time job.

posts.summary: McKenzie discusses how entering overcrowded software markets (backup apps, photo organizers, project management) is a losing strategy unless you find a differentiating angle. He advocates for niching down to serve a specific, addressable audience rather than competing as a generic commodity. He highlights ProjectThingy as an example of smart differentiation: project management software that can be embedded in your own website, appealing to technically disinclined small businesses. The creator's portfolio of embeddable apps demonstrates how a single differentiating concept can be applied across multiple products.
key_points: Overcrowded markets like backup apps, photo organizers, and project management tools should generally be avoided unless you have a differentiating angle
Niching down (e.g., 'World's Best Photo Organizer For Wedding Photographers') creates an addressable market of customers eager to pay
ProjectThingy differentiates in the crowded project management space by being embeddable in customers' own websites, appealing to non-technical SMEs
A portfolio approach of multiple embeddable apps sharing the same differentiating concept can compound the advantage
money_quotes: The trouble with getting into an uber-crowded market, in a niche which is essentially a commodity service, is making yourself heard above the din.
So instead of being Generic Photo Organizer #1278, maybe you could be World's Best Photo Organizer For Wedding Photographers.
This allows you to carve out a little niche which is addressable (i.e. you have some way of reaching them) and which will leap at the chance to pay you money.

It is *yawn* project management software. But it is project management software which you can embed in your own website, and I'm convinced that makes all the difference in the world to a certain population of technically disinclined SMEs.
Rails is after all 'Basecamp, minus the domain expertise'.
posts.key_insight: In crowded software markets, the winning strategy is not to build a better generic product but to find a specific differentiating angle that makes your product uniquely valuable to an addressable niche.

posts.summary: McKenzie notices an unexpected spike in downloads for Football Bingo cards and can't identify a referral source, concluding it must be organic search traffic. He discovered the anomaly while researching long tail search patterns for an upcoming blog post. The brief post illustrates how niche software can attract surprising audiences through search alone.
key_points: Unexpected organic search traffic can drive significant downloads for niche content without any identifiable referral links
Long tail search patterns produce anomalies that reveal untapped demand in surprising niches
Monitoring analytics data closely reveals opportunities that would otherwise go unnoticed
money_quotes: Several hundred people have started downloading Football Bingo all of a sudden. Not that I mind, of course, but I can't square it with what I know about my target users.
It seems to just be organic search traffic, and a whole mess of it.

I only noticed this because I'm writing one of my longer, 'evergreen content' pieces on the nature of the Long Tail (and how to extract money from it) and I've been chasing down some of the anomalies in my data.
posts.key_insight: Organic search traffic can surface unexpected demand in niches you never anticipated, which is exactly the long tail phenomenon at work.

posts.summary: Patrick details a highly productive weekend of coding on Bingo Card Creator version 2.5, triggered by a customer's UTF-8 bug report. The update includes several feature improvements: consistent column placement for words, a reworked Wizards menu supporting 500+ word lists, automatic update functionality for word lists, and smarter registration key handling. He also announces a partnership with Excelsior to test whether eliminating the Java dependency increases sales. The post serves as both a development log and a call for beta testers.
key_points: Customer bug reports can be catalysts for productive development sprints that go well beyond just fixing the bug
Investing in content (507 paid word lists) creates compounding value when the software can dynamically deliver that content to users
Building automatic update functionality turns an existing marketing expense (freelance writers) into double the value by eliminating manual rebuild cycles
A/B testing a native compilation versus Java dependency is a smart way to isolate whether technical friction costs sales
money_quotes: On Friday a customer reported a bug with UTF-8 files, which had creeped in literally months ago. Since that was blocking her from getting work done I had a fire lit under my behind this weekend, and spent all three days of it coding like mad.
I've paid for 507 (and counting) lists to be written, so why not ship them all?
Double the bang for the buck out of money I was spending on marketing anyhow.
I'm always willing to do an A/B test in the service of making me money, and if it helps them out so much the better.
Finally I join the 1960s of user interface design.
posts.key_insight: A single customer bug report can spark a burst of development that compounds existing investments — turning 507 paid word lists and freelance writing spend into significantly more customer value through better software delivery.

posts.summary: McKenzie requests beta testers for the latest build of Bingo Card Creator after fixing errors that broke the Mac and native Windows builds. He highlights the challenge of developing Mac software without access to a Mac, relying on Java's cross-platform capabilities. The update includes Windows shell integration with .BCF files and under-the-hood improvements. This is a routine development update showing the realities of solo software development with limited resources.
key_points: Bug fixes resolved issues that broke Mac and native Windows builds in the previous release
McKenzie develops Mac software without owning or having access to a Mac, relying entirely on Java's cross-platform promise
New features include Windows shell integration with .BCF files and cross-platform debugging improvements
The native Windows installer ballooned to 13 MB because he previously shipped it without required dependencies
money_quotes: I'm in the very unenviable position of having to release Mac software without a) having touched a Mac in 10 years and b) without having access to a Mac to test on.
That fact that this is even possible is near miraculous (yay for Java).
A honking 13 MB install means that this time I actually included the right stuff!
posts.key_insight: Solo developers often ship software for platforms they cannot directly test on, relying on cross-platform tools and community beta testers to bridge the gap.

posts.summary: McKenzie reveals that for years he tracked whether users reached his purchase page from the trial version, but never tracked which specific trial restriction prompted the purchase. After realizing this oversight, he added tracking in just 8 minutes. The post illustrates how small, obvious instrumentation gaps can persist for years and cost valuable business intelligence.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator's free trial had three specific restrictions designed to drive purchases: no text import, no saving, and a 15-card print limit
McKenzie tracked trial-to-purchase conversions but never tracked which specific restriction triggered the purchase decision
The fix took only 8 minutes to implement, making the years-long oversight especially painful
Understanding which restrictions drive conversions is essential for optimizing trial-to-paid funnels
money_quotes: I have, for a long time, tracked whether people get to my purchasing page via coming from the trial or not — but I have never tracked from where in the trial.

It would be very, very helpful for me to know which of those restrictions are most effective at driving the sales… but I never thought of tracking it before, at least not enough to spend the 8 minutes it took me to add that in.
Do you know what drives people to upgrade from your trial?
posts.key_insight: The most valuable analytics instrumentation is often trivially easy to implement but goes unbuilt for years because no one thinks to ask the question.

posts.summary: McKenzie posts a final call for beta testers for Bingo Card Creator 2.5, listing all reported bugs and his responses to each. He categorizes issues into fixed, by-design, and deferred, showing pragmatic triage of a solo developer shipping cross-platform software. The post reveals the friction of building a Java desktop app that must work on both Windows and Mac, with platform-specific UI expectations creating constant tension. He closes with a technical tip for Ant users about zip file permissions on Mac.
key_points: Cross-platform desktop software requires constant compromise between platform conventions, especially Mac vs Windows UI expectations
McKenzie triages bugs pragmatically: fixing what impacts customers, deferring cosmetic issues, and defending intentional design choices
He takes responsibility for unclear UI rather than blaming users, noting that if testers missed button descriptions it's his fault
Backwards compatibility with existing paying customers constrains what a solo developer can change, even when the change would be objectively better
money_quotes: if you guys didn't see it that is my fault.
I'm sort of loathe to remove functionality, particularly functionality that a few hundred paying customers may be using already.
You may be interested in knowing that applications can write to Program Files in Vista. Really, try it — MS hacked around their own security system to avoid breaking legacy apps.
Much love to the testers that discovered this before my users did.
posts.key_insight: Effective bug triage for a solo software business means fixing what impacts paying customers, owning UX failures, and deferring edge cases without guilt.

posts.summary: Patrick announces the release of Bingo Card Creator 2.5, which includes 400+ new cards, improved functionality, and most-requested features. He thanks community members who helped beta test, particularly Mac users. He shares a cautionary tale about accidentally breaking his free trial download for four days due to an SVN deployment mistake. He also announces an upcoming price increase and reflects on balancing his day job with his software business.
key_points: New release includes 400+ cards and a most-requested feature, with plans to A/B test natively compiled executables against Java versions
A deployment mistake with svn update broke the free trial download for 4 days — a costly error for a solo developer
Announcing a future price increase rather than implementing it immediately, using the pre-announcement as a marketing tactic
The post illustrates the reality of running a side business alongside a 14-hour day job
money_quotes: I'm also interested in seeing whether natively compiled .exes beat Java-requiring versions in a head to head test.
I neglected to notice that the same controller also implemented the split test for windows downloaders, which broke downloading the free trial for 4 days.
I also went ahead and did something I've been threatening for a long time now: raised prices. Or, rather, announced that I will be raising prices in about a month.
Now after 14 hours of day job and pushing out a release in the same day I richly deserve a nice warm shower and a few minutes of the new Naruto game before bedtime.
posts.key_insight: Even minor deployment oversights — like an SVN update that touches more than intended — can silently break critical conversion paths for days, underscoring the need for deployment discipline in solo software businesses.

posts.summary: McKenzie contrasts the satisfaction of solving customer problems in his own software business with the frustrations of his day job. A customer who purchased Bingo Card Creator in December 2006 contacts him about poor Mac support, and McKenzie discovers the purchase date through his Rails console before cheerfully resolving the issue. He reflects on how owning a business gives him complete authority to solve problems directly, unlike his corporate job where he fights for authority over basic decisions. The post culminates in a hilarious anecdote about an outsourcing team filing a bug report about a typhoon warning displayed on a login screen, which was actually intended behavior.
key_points: Running your own software business gives you complete authority to solve customer problems immediately, which is deeply satisfying compared to corporate bureaucracy
A customer requested a refund on a purchase made over two years prior, highlighting both the long tail of customer relationships and the importance of good record-keeping
Corporate outsourcing creates absurd friction when teams lack context, English documentation, and direct access to domain knowledge
Even small brushfires in a solo business feel rewarding because you have full agency over the solution
money_quotes: I have the absolute right to change anything I want to solve problems for myself and the customer, and oh is that intoxicating.

Your time is too valuable to use on translation is a lovely thing to hear, but I'm closer to the opinion that my time is too valuable to spend the next 6 months dealing with code that is totally orthogonal to the customer's actual needs.
I love this job.
Why is the login screen showing a weather report? That is probably a bug, too.
Because the weather report reads 'Typhoon Warning'. Closing this bug as 'by design'.
posts.key_insight: The freedom to directly solve problems without bureaucratic permission is what makes running your own business intoxicating compared to corporate work.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers a critical bug in Bingo Card Creator at 1:30 AM where printing crashes silently when a card has exactly as many words as spaces. The bug makes the application effectively useless since printing is its core function. Despite extensive testing, the edge case was missed because his test methodology rarely produced the exact condition. He rolls back to a previous version using Capistrano and plans to send replacement CDs and apology emails to affected users.
key_points: A silent crash bug in printing code was triggered by an exact-match edge case between word count and card spaces
Testing blind spots emerge when test methodology doesn't mirror actual user behavior patterns
Automated deployment rollback tools like Capistrano are essential for damage control during production incidents
Taking responsibility for bugs means proactive outreach to affected customers, not waiting for complaints
money_quotes: This is about the worst thing that could happen to me at 1:30 in the morning, short of a crash bug. Without the capability to print, the application is useless.
Printing is always the thing I spend the most time testing, but I typically test by loading up Wizards and when picking randomly. I rarely get one that has exactly twenty-four words in it.
Unfortunately, that behavior (filling the card exactly) is really common in actual use.
Thank God for Capistrano. The whole shebang of my website, including the executables, can be rolled back to a known good version at the touch of a button.
Memo to self: need to start using JUnit before I touch anything in the bowels of the printing code ever again.
posts.key_insight: The most dangerous bugs hide in the gap between how developers test and how customers actually use software.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes squashing a bug in Bingo Card Creator after spending hours trying to find a clean solution. He eventually resorted to a dirty hack that touched only 2 lines instead of the clean solution's six files. The impact was minimal, affecting only 8 out of 560 word lists and only noticeable with non-default settings. He accepts the pragmatic tradeoff and commits to shipping replacement CDs to six affected customers.
key_points: The 'clean' solution required touching six files while the 'dirty' hack only needed 2 lines changed
Pragmatic shipping decisions sometimes trump architectural purity, especially when customer impact is minimal
Slacking on testing has real costs: he had to mail replacement CDs to six customers who received buggy copies
money_quotes: After hours of fruitlessly trying to solve the problem in some clean way, I eventually dirty hacked my way around it.
Ironically, the "clean" solution required touching six files, the "dirty" solution required touching 2 lines.
I think I'll chalk this one up to "Eh, sometimes you just have to ship it."
Now to pay my penance for slackadaisical testing earlier: new CDs for six people, who each got one with the bug on it.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the 'dirty' two-line fix that ships is better than the 'clean' six-file refactor that doesn't.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents how he reduced the download size of his Bingo Card Creator native executable by 4.5 MB through a combination of Excelsior JET optimizations and Inno Setup packaging. He was running a split test comparing a self-contained native executable against a Java-dependent version, trading download size for eliminating the Java installation requirement. The process involved removing unused Java packages, using a specialized JAR compression tool, and carefully testing that detached packages weren't needed at runtime. He shares his complete Inno Setup script and notes his philosophy of minimizing installer decision screens for non-technical users.
key_points: Reducing download size matters for conversion rates — every MB of friction can cause potential customers to abandon the process
Excelsior JET's detached package option lets you strip unused Java libraries (sound, RMI, XML) from native executables, with green/red light indicators for safety
A specialized compression tool for JET's runtime JAR file achieved far better compression than standard ZIP, saving 4 MB with negligible install time impact
Installer UX should minimize decisions for non-technical users — no install location prompts, no shortcut options, just install and go
money_quotes: The idea is to see whether the tradeoff of vastly increased download size of the native version makes up for eliminating the Java dependency.
Launch4j will prompt people to download Java if they don't have a sufficient version installed, but that is one of those decision points where people might stop using the app.
Slimming the download 4 MB is easily worth it.
It is optimized largely to remove as many screens from the installer as I possibly can — no asking where to install, no asking whether to deposit shortcuts or not.
I have non-technical users and don't want to fatigue them with decisions that they're largely not qualified to make anyway.
posts.key_insight: Every friction point in the download-to-install funnel — including download size and installer prompts — is a potential conversion killer, so obsessively optimizing these details pays off for small software businesses.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes how a simple CrazyEgg heatmap analysis led him to add a second download button on his main page, resulting in a 20% conversion increase. After noticing he hadn't used his $20/month CrazyEgg subscription in months, he identified that users reading his benefits list had no nearby call-to-action. Adding one button captured 10.4% of page clicks and translated to roughly $160/month in additional revenue. The post illustrates how small, data-driven UX changes yield outsized business results.
key_points: A single additional download button placed after the benefits list captured 10.4% of all page clicks from 4,200 visitors
The change increased main page conversion by approximately 20%, translating to an estimated $160/month in added revenue
Users who scroll past content to read benefits need an immediate call-to-action rather than being forced to scroll further or backtrack
The first text link in a content area gets a disproportionate share of user interaction and should point to the conversion goal
Even experienced practitioners neglect obvious optimizations when they stop regularly reviewing their analytics
money_quotes: 86 clicks, or 10.4% of clicks on the page, on that one stinking extra button.
