This increases the conversion of my main page by about 20% (some clicks are presumably "stolen" from other ways to reach the same goal).
The naive assumption is that 20% more trials is 20% more sales.
Putting a button there is kind of jawdroppingly obvious… I'm not sure why I didn't do it before.
The first text link in the content area gets a huge portion of user interaction.
If someone scrolls down to actually read the benefits list, then they're left high and dry with nowhere to click unless they scroll past the huge image or scroll upwards.
Neither of those activities "flows" well after you've just read a benefits list, not nearly as well as "click here" does.
posts.key_insight: Placing a call-to-action immediately after your persuasive content — rather than forcing users to scroll or backtrack — is an obvious but commonly neglected optimization that can meaningfully increase conversions.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces plans to build a custom shopping cart to replace e-junkie's hosted solution for Bingo Card Creator, despite his long advocacy for the service. His motivation is eliminating friction points he believes hurt conversions: redundant zip code entry for CD purchases and perceived slowness of the third-party cart. He notes his sales are on track to hit $3,000/month with 73% year-over-year growth, driven by AdWords near holidays, organic SEO improvements, and subtle product tweaks.
key_points: Third-party solutions that work well initially can become limiting as your skills and needs grow — knowing when to build custom is a maturity signal
Small friction points like redundant form fields and multi-second load times measurably kill conversions
He tested Buy Now buttons vs. shopping carts and found the cart wins — contradicting the common advice to simplify to a single button
Bingo Card Creator hit 73% year-over-year growth during a recession, driven by SEO, AdWords around seasonal events, and incremental product improvements
money_quotes: I'm starting to chafe at a few of the necessary restrictions from using a third party solution.
I just want the perceived speed to be 'bugs in my teeth fast'.
I've tested both alternatives and the cart makes me oodles of money by simplifying the customer experience.
I'm thinking the lift in conversions is going to be sizeable enough to justify a few hours of fighting with Javascript (once).
Recession, what recession, 73% year over year growth.
Subtle changes, big difference in effects.
posts.key_insight: Even small UX friction in your checkout flow — redundant fields, loading delays — compounds into meaningful lost revenue, making custom solutions worth building once you can measure the cost.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents completing an 8-hour sprint to build a custom shopping cart for Bingo Card Creator, replacing the previous e-junkie-hosted solution. The new cart moves pricing logic client-side for speed, uses a lightbox overlay instead of page navigation, and eliminates unnecessary UI elements like 'Continue Shopping' and 'Update Cart' buttons. He acknowledges remaining bugs but is pleased with the performance and UX improvements, planning to A/B test the new cart against the old one and eventually release it for other micro-ISVs.
key_points: Moving pricing logic from server-side to client-side JavaScript dramatically improved cart loading speed
Simplifying the shopping cart UI by removing unnecessary buttons (Continue Shopping, Update Cart) reduces friction in the purchase flow
The traditional 'shopping cart' metaphor may confuse users of simple software products and should be replaced with clearer branding
A/B testing the new cart against the old one will provide data on whether the UX improvements translate to revenue gains
money_quotes: I use this feature to move the pricing logic from the e-junkie servers to my Javascript, where it gets "bugs in your teeth fast" speed improvements.
While you and I know this is a shopping cart, I think that whole metaphor just confuses users. Gone. Instead, slap in logo and text.
Because I no longer incur an AJAX roundtrip to another server every time the user does anything, I don't need to tie their updates to the Update Cart button, hence it is gone.
Try adding a CD to your cart in both and tell me which you like better. I think this is going to be worth significant money for me.
After pouring 8 hours into the massive black hole of time that is Javascript (aaaaaaaahhhhh I hate you so), I got the new and improved cart working.
posts.key_insight: Owning your checkout experience by moving logic client-side and stripping away unnecessary UI conventions like the shopping cart metaphor can meaningfully reduce purchase friction.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents fixing a scrolling bug in the iBox JavaScript overlay library that affected Safari, Chrome, and Firefox 3. He shares the specific code fix for Google searchers, then updates readers on three bugs in his new shopping cart integration with e-junkie. He reflects on JavaScript's potential for improving user interaction while lamenting the pain of cross-browser development, and prepares to A/B test his new cart against the existing one.
key_points: The iBox overlay failed to scroll with the page in Safari, Chrome, and FF3, requiring a manual patch to track scroll offsets
Writing blog posts that solve specific technical problems serves dual duty as SEO content and genuine community contribution
JavaScript enables dramatically better user interactions but cross-browser compatibility makes development painful
Even small e-commerce improvements like cart redesigns deserve proper A/B testing before full deployment
money_quotes: Hello, Google searcher! I spent 2 hours so you don't have to!
I patched iBox to avoid this issue, and tested it in five browsers because I just have no life whatsoever.
All websites I've ever used are missing massive opportunities to make their user interaction sexier than they can possibly imagine.
I can understand why, because coding Javascript is the most painful programming I've done since C in college.
Oh, God, the cross-browser issues! The sheer complexity of the DOM model! The paucity of good documentation available in my IDE next to autocomplete!
posts.key_insight: Documenting solutions to obscure technical problems creates valuable SEO content while genuinely helping other developers, turning debugging frustration into marketing.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces a comprehensive article about shopping cart development published on his Bingo Card Creator site. The article covers high-level theory, real statistics from his business, and includes the complete cart code released into the public domain. He explains the strategic decision to publish it on his product site rather than his blog, both for technical integration with live data and as a deliberate SEO play to attract inbound links.
key_points: Published a comprehensive guide to shopping cart development based on his experience rewriting one for Bingo Card Creator
Released the entire shopping cart code into the public domain as a form of community contribution and marketing
Strategically placed the content on his product domain rather than his blog to build SEO authority through inbound links
The article combines theory, real business statistics, and working code — a content marketing trifecta
money_quotes: I wrote up approximately everything I know about shopping carts, plus everything I learned from my recent experience rewriting one, and made a proper article about it.
I released the whole cart into the public domain.
I'm kind of hoping folks will say 'Ooh, that is some wonderful stuff' and link to it.
posts.key_insight: Publishing high-value technical content on your product domain rather than a personal blog is a deliberate SEO strategy to attract inbound links that boost your business site's authority.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports a dramatic increase in February 2009 sales for Bingo Card Creator, with projections exceeding $3,000. He attributes the growth to multiple simultaneous improvements: a new shopping cart showing dramatic A/B test results, an optimized Check for Updates page that upsells existing users, native compilation testing with Excelsior JET, UI changes that clarify the product's key value proposition, and a soft timed trial mechanic that nudges long-term free users toward purchasing.
key_points: A new shopping cart is showing dramatic conversion improvements in A/B testing, though McKenzie wisely waits for statistical significance before revealing numbers
The Check for Updates page was repurposed as a sales opportunity — users who check for updates have demonstrated engagement and are ripe for conversion
UI changes addressed a comprehension failure: users didn't realize the software could randomly scramble bingo cards, despite it being the primary value proposition
A soft timed trial mechanic frames the upgrade as 'getting something extra' rather than 'losing something you had', which is psychologically more effective
Multiple small improvements compounding simultaneously produced outsized revenue growth
money_quotes: Suffice it to say that if I told you the improvement I am seeing you would not believe me. I'm not even sure I believe me.
As long as I have you on my webpage because you've demonstrated that you care about having the latest and greatest, why don't I sell you on the benefits of getting the latest and greatest forever?

The number one reason to buy my software is to get randomly scrambled bingo cards. Despite the fact that I hammer away at this on my website, many people don't actually know that the program does this.
I get asked sometimes 'How do I scramble the cards?', which indicates that the whole Sample Cart element was an epic failure at comprehensibility.
The nice thing about this one is it sounds like 'getting something extra' rather than 'getting something which was taken away from you'.
Someone who has used the application for 15, 30, 45, etc days is clearly happy with it and just needs a bit of a nudge over the finish line.
posts.key_insight: Compounding multiple small conversion optimizations — better cart, smarter upsell timing, clearer UI communication of value, and psychologically-framed trial nudges — can produce dramatic revenue gains that exceed any single change.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents a specific customer support optimization in his sales system. He discovered that some customers were entering their Registration Key into the 'Lookup My Registration Key' form, which searches by order number and email, causing it to fail and unnecessarily directing them to email support. His fix detects when input resembles a Registration Key and either redirects the customer to visual instructions for using their key, or tells them to email if the key doesn't exist.
key_points: Observing actual user behavior reveals mismatches between how you designed a system and how customers use it
Small programmatic fixes to anticipated support requests can eliminate entire categories of support emails
Good error handling means detecting what the user actually intended, not just what you expected them to do
money_quotes: Some customers put Registration Key into Lookup My Registration Key form. This looks up transactions by order number and email address, thus failing to find any matching records, then informs customer to email me to find their Registration Key. This is suboptimal.
If input into LMRK form resembles a Registration Key, search for a line item which had that Registration Key assigned.
If one exists, then it was actually issued to a customer — redirect them to explanatory text with pictures on how to enter a Registration Key into the application, not the website.
posts.key_insight: The best customer support improvements come from observing how users actually misuse your systems and engineering the confusion away rather than answering the same emails repeatedly.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts a deployment mistake where he committed unfinished code to trunk, then deployed a quick fix that accidentally broke the lightbox on his front page for three days. He laments his recurring failure to follow the discipline of only committing deployment-ready code. The silver lining is that the outage provided yet another natural A/B test confirming that lightboxes significantly outperform direct image links for conversions.
key_points: Never commit code to trunk that isn't ready for immediate deployment, because unrelated hotfixes will inevitably drag it into production
Silent failures are dangerous — the broken lightbox went undetected for three days
Accidental outages can serve as unintentional A/B tests that confirm feature value
Lightboxes significantly outperform direct links to images for engagement and conversion
money_quotes: I will learn to not, under any circumstances, commit any code to the trunk which is not ready for immediate deployment.
I inevitably do "just a quick deploy to fix up that unrelated mistake" and then, boom, the lightbox on the front page silently fails for three days.
I once again have experimental confirmation that functional lightboxes beat the stuffing out of direct links to images!
posts.key_insight: The discipline of only committing deployment-ready code to trunk matters because quick unrelated fixes will inevitably pull half-finished work into production.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes an A/B test on his Bingo Card Creator homepage, replacing a standard Lightbox screenshot popup with an iBox that includes a download link. He explains his reasoning: loading screens cost money because any friction loses potential customers. He previously tried adding instructional text to lightboxed images but it failed because clicking the text dismissed the image. Now he's testing whether embedding a real download call-to-action within the screenshot overlay improves trial downloads, while worrying it might trap users and increase bounces.
key_points: Loading screens and delays in user interactions directly translate to lost revenue — even a screenshot preview delay matters
A previous attempt to add instructional text inside a lightbox failed because clicking the text dismissed the overlay, creating a broken experience
The new test embeds a download call-to-action within the screenshot overlay to convert screenshot viewers into trial downloaders
About 20% of homepage visitors click to view the screenshot, making it a high-leverage optimization target
money_quotes: I hate load screens. When I see them I think "This is me, losing money".
If two seconds to load the shopping cart cost me half of my potential sales then 2 seconds to look at a screenshot must likewise be suboptimal.
That was a crashing failure — it looked like a link but if you clicked it, wham, the image vanished.
My worry is that the people are going to get stuck in the lightbox and bounce, since it is much harder to dismiss now. Oh well, that is what testing is for.
It really is about 100% improved over the old version.
posts.key_insight: Every point of friction in a user's journey — even a two-second loading screen on a screenshot preview — is a measurable leak in your conversion funnel that should be tested and optimized.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces a revamped public stats tracking page for Bingo Card Creator, consolidating all business metrics into one location. While working on 2008 taxes, he provides a detailed financial breakdown showing $21,117 in sales, $11,783 in expenses, and $9,334 in profit (roughly 45% margin). Advertising, primarily Google AdWords, was his largest expense category. He notes that pre-2008 expense data is incomplete due to difficulty accessing old credit card statements.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator generated ~$21K revenue and ~$9.3K profit in 2008, a 45% profit margin
Google AdWords was the overwhelming majority of his advertising spend, his single largest expense category
He practices radical transparency by making all business stats publicly available at a single URL
Tax preparation forced a useful discipline of properly tracking and categorizing all expenses
money_quotes: Sales for 2008 (less returns): $21,116.65
Profit: $9334
The big green slice is profit (~45% — a bit lower than I expected).
Unsurprisingly, my number one cost is advertising — the overwhelming majority of it is Google AdWords.
I don't have easy access to all my old 2006/2007 credit card statements, and don't even get me started about Paypal.
posts.key_insight: Radical financial transparency — publishing exact revenue, expenses, and profit margins — builds trust and credibility with an audience of fellow software entrepreneurs.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports the results of a month-long A/B test comparing a natively compiled version of Bingo Card Creator (using Excelsior JET) against the standard Java version. Of 75 trackable purchases, 44 came from the JET-compiled version versus 31 from Java, which is statistically significant at the 8% level using a binomial probability test. He walks through the tracking methodology using properties files, Google Analytics cookies, and query string parameters. Despite a Vista compatibility bug with the JET version, he concludes the native compilation helps sales enough to make it the default for all Windows users.
key_points: Native compilation via Excelsior JET produced 59% of trackable sales versus 41% for the Java version in a 75-purchase sample
The split test was implemented by repurposing the application's properties file strings and tracking via Google Analytics cookies on purchase pages
Statistical analysis using binomial distribution shows only 8% probability the result is due to chance, sufficient to conclude JET helps sales
A Vista compatibility bug with the JET version prevented saving to Program Files, yet the JET version still outperformed
The result was strong enough to make native compilation the default despite the incomplete data (only 75 of 116 conversions were trackable)
money_quotes: In the last 30 days I have had 118 sales of Bingo Card Creator. (Wow, that number shocks me.)
If we assume hypothetically that it doesn't matter which version you were using, we would expect that 50% of the total number of conversions would turn out each way.
In only 8% of all samples of 75 sales would we find 44 or more sales for JET. Accordingly, we conclude that JET is helping sales.

Vista prevents programs from writing to subdirectories of Program Files to prevent bad behavior. However, it emulates writing to Program Files to avoid breaking legacy software, and that emulation is sufficient for BCC Java but apparently not good enough for BCC JET.
Ah, we live and we learn.
posts.key_insight: Native compilation of a Java desktop application measurably increased sales conversion rates, demonstrating that perceived software quality and installation experience directly affect purchasing decisions.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents his migration from WordPress.com to a self-hosted WordPress installation after two years of planning. He provides a step-by-step technical guide covering domain registration, DNS changes, content export/import, and post-migration setup. The move was motivated by wanting full control over plugins, themes, and site customization as his business expanded beyond just blogging about Bingo Card Creator. He notes practical details like the importance of maintaining WordPress.com's 301 redirects for SEO continuity and the immediate spam problem that hits self-hosted blogs.
key_points: Maintaining WordPress.com's $10/year domain redirect is essential for preserving SEO value during migration via 301 redirects
Self-hosting gives control over plugins, themes, and site direction — important as a business outgrows a simple blog
The migration process is straightforward but requires patience, particularly waiting for Google to recognize the domain change
Self-hosted WordPress blogs get hammered by comment spam immediately — Akismet is a necessity, not an option
money_quotes: The main reasons is to make me the captain of the blog's destiny, as opposed to WordPress.
WordPress will 301 redirect to your new domain IF AND ONLY IF you continue paying them the $10 a year — its a steal, do it.
I got 50 lovely comments welcoming me to my new digs within the first 15 minutes. They all seemed to be by people who work in the pharmaceutical industry.
posts.key_insight: When migrating a blog with established SEO value, preserving link equity through proper 301 redirects is worth far more than the nominal cost.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports that his side business Bingo Card Creator earned $2,925 in February 2009, surpassing his monthly salary of $2,890 as a Japanese salaryman. He tempers the milestone by noting that Japanese salaries include biannual bonuses and that self-employment would increase his tax burden. He also reveals that BCC had $995 in expenses that month. The post hints at a dramatic difference in hours worked between his day job and his side business.
key_points: BCC monthly revenue ($2,925) exceeded his monthly Japanese salary ($2,890) for the first time
The comparison is less dramatic when accounting for biannual bonuses, expenses, and tax differences
Japanese programmers in their mid-to-late twenties earn roughly $40k USD, which is considered good locally but far below US engineering salaries
The hours-to-income ratio between his day job and side business is dramatically different in favor of BCC
money_quotes: This brings BCC sales for February to $2,925.
I won't tell you how many hours are required to get either of these numbers… principally because the difference is too depressing for words.
The total salary of a programmer in his mid/late twenties in Japan is generally somewhere around the 4 million yen mark (~$40k USD). It is considered fairly good for someone that age.
We're crazy underpaid next to engineers in America, I know, I know — my family reminds me frequently.
Still… yikes.
posts.key_insight: When a side project's monthly revenue surpasses your salary despite requiring far fewer hours, it forces a serious reckoning about the value of traditional employment.

posts.summary: Patrick briefly explains that he misconfigured his DNS records when migrating his blog, accidentally creating a CNAME record pointing to Google instead of an MX record. This caused the non-www version of kalzumeus.com to go down. He notes it will take about a week for the TTL to expire and the fix to propagate. He also set up a redirect via Google Webmaster Console as a stopgap.
key_points: A DNS misconfiguration (CNAME vs MX record) took down the non-www version of the blog after migration
DNS TTL propagation means the fix will take up to a week to fully resolve across the internet
Google Webmaster Console redirect was set up as an interim mitigation
money_quotes: Fun stuff, DNS, fun stuff.
posts.key_insight: DNS misconfigurations during blog migrations are easy to make and slow to fix due to TTL propagation delays.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that commercial B2C software can successfully compete with open source alternatives by excelling in areas OSS typically neglects. Drawing from his experience selling Bingo Card Creator alongside free OSS alternatives, he identifies six key advantages: marketing that speaks to user problems rather than software features, attractive visual design, superior user experience from download through daily use, non-technical language, accessible customer support, and technical execution. His core argument is that 99% of OSS projects are not Firefox-level polished products, and most customers don't even realize they have a software problem—they have a domain problem that software happens to solve.
key_points: Less than 1% of customers perceive their problem as a software problem—they search for solutions to domain problems like 'how do I make bingo cards,' not for software downloads
OSS projects routinely fail at basic user experience: downloading from SourceForge is so frustrating that non-technical users often cannot even complete the process
Visual design directly impacts sales—McKenzie doubled his sales simply by replacing default Java icons with attractive stock icons
The median number of developers per OSS project on SourceForge is 1, and perhaps one project in five will ever leave beta
Customer support's main purpose isn't answering questions—it's decreasing perceived risk by demonstrating a safety net exists
money_quotes: Less than 1% of customers perceive their problem as a software problem.
Many of my most enthusiastic customers despise every minute of using their computers. They put up with it because it gets them back to their kids faster than any non-computer alternative.
Wow, it sounds that I'm saying that most prospective users of OSS can't even manage to download it. Let me be clear: that is exactly what I am saying.
When our users can't use our software, that means we have failed in our jobs, whether we're proprietary or OSS developers.
The Internet sucks the literacy out of people, so be prepared to explain the same thing several times to get the message across.
All OSS is not Firefox in the same way that all proprietary software is not Microsoft Office.
Often in B2C the first glimpse of the software makes the sale and everything after that is just justifying to the customer that their gut decision was the right one.
posts.key_insight: Commercial software beats open source not through technical superiority but by solving the 90% of the business that happens outside the IDE—marketing to real user problems, designing attractive interfaces, and making the entire experience from download to daily use frictionless.

posts.summary: Patrick reports completing his 2008 taxes early, sharing his Bingo Card Creator business financials for the year. The business generated $21,720 in sales with $12,318 in expenses, yielding $8,798 in profit. He credits his own expense tracking feature and TaxAct for making the process smoother. He also notes setting up estimated quarterly tax payments for the coming year, anticipating higher earnings.
key_points: BCC generated $21,720 in sales and $8,798 in profit for 2008, showing meaningful growth as a side business
Expenses included costs for a second product in development, hinting at business expansion
TaxAct is highlighted as the only online filing option supporting Americans abroad with the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion
Setting up estimated quarterly payments signals confidence in continued revenue growth
money_quotes: Thanks to my new expense tracking feature on the BCC website and the magic of TaxAct, for literally the first time in my life I have my taxes done well ahead of schedule.
I also ended up scheduling the IRS to take four nice big bites out of my checking account during the year because the amount of taxes I expect to owe next year is going to be a few thousand more.
If I have the money in my account I'll probably do something stupid like spending it on a TV or, worse, investing it.
posts.key_insight: Transparent financial reporting of a side software business shows that even modest profits require real tax planning, especially for Americans living abroad.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his favorite WordPress plugin, 'What Would Seth Godin Do?', which cookies blog visitors to show different content to returning readers versus first-time visitors. He explains the marketing logic of treating these two audiences differently, noting that someone arriving from a Google search has completely different needs than a long-time follower. He places the differentiated content subtly below posts rather than above them, since most of his traffic comes from search queries about bingo cards rather than repeat blog readers.
key_points: Different visitors to a blog have fundamentally different needs — returning readers vs. first-time Google searchers require different calls to action
Cookieing visitors allows you to customize the experience based on their relationship with your site
McKenzie prioritizes unobtrusiveness over maximum visibility, placing new-visitor content below posts to avoid distracting his primary search traffic
money_quotes: he would cookie everyone who came to the blog, so that you could show different views of it to people who had been following for years and people who just arrived fresh off a Google search
I don't need the distraction for the (several thousand people) who get here for some variant of I want to pay someone money to make bingo cards please tell me oh great Google where I can do this
posts.key_insight: Smart marketers show different content to first-time visitors versus returning readers, because a stranger arriving from Google has completely different needs than a loyal follower.

posts.summary: McKenzie uses a personal anecdote about failing to buy socks in Japan due to a language ambiguity to illustrate how customers abandon purchases when they encounter confusing information but feel too embarrassed to ask for help. He argues that developers who dismiss non-technical users as idiots are leaving money on the table, because customers with 'stupid' questions simply leave rather than seek clarification. The post provides concrete tactics for preemptively answering customer confusion through formatting, plain language, FAQs, and welcoming support tone. The core message is that the obstacle between your customer and a sale is often a simple question they're too embarrassed to ask.
key_points: Customers who encounter confusion on your site don't contact support — they bounce, because they don't want to feel stupid or impose on someone's time
Being a smart, educated person doesn't prevent you from being stumped by domain-specific knowledge gaps, so dismissing confused users as idiots is arrogant and costly
Preemptive clarity through formatting, plain language, analogies, and FAQs eliminates the 'stupid questions' before they cause lost sales
The tone of your support and copy matters enormously — welcoming language signals safety for future problems, while gatekeeping language drives people away
Always blame yourself for customer confusion, never the customer — even when it feels unfair
money_quotes: When customers have a 'stupid' question, they don't spend time searching for your contact information. They just bounce.

I'm a college-educated professional who has been speaking Japanese for about eight years now, I do technical translation for engineering specs on Big Freaking Enterprise Software, I am generally not a stupid person: and yet here I am, able to read the words right in front of me but unable to make sense of their import.
No matter what your customer asks you, no matter what went wrong, no matter what they did: it is your fault. Even if you don't feel like this is true, learn to fake it.
If you are selling to customers who are not professional buyers of software, don't use words like 'license' or 'SaaS' or 'support contract'.
We love talking to customers! Feel free to send us an email if you have a question, a comment, or just want to say hi.
Rather than letting your customers find their own analogies, you should when appropriate guide them to things in their experience that will make sense.
Their money is as green as anyone else's.
posts.key_insight: The biggest sales killer isn't customers who ask stupid questions — it's the customers who have stupid questions and leave without asking them.

posts.summary: This is Patrick McKenzie's Japanese translation of his English post 'How To Successfully Compete With Open Source Software,' prepared because Japanese blogs had linked to the original. The translated portion covers how commercial B2C software can compete with free OSS alternatives, using his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study. He argues that marketing is the primary advantage commercial software has over OSS, since 99% of users don't think of their problem as a software problem. The translation was left incomplete after the marketing section, with a note that the design section would follow the next day.
key_points: Less than 1% of potential users frame their problem as needing software — they search for solutions to their actual problem, not for software downloads
OSS projects focus obsessively on the software itself (implementation, bug trackers, mailing lists) while ignoring the problem domain that users actually care about
Commercial B2C software can beat free OSS by marketing to the problem domain rather than the software category
The vast majority of OSS is not Firefox-level quality — it's obscure projects people use but have never heard of, and that's the real competition
money_quotes: B2Cでも、値段が０円であるOSSに勝てます。
ユーザには問題があります。ソフトは問題を解決するツールです。と言っても、ユーザは「ソフト不足」との問題を意識していません。
1%以下のユーザが「問題はソフトが足りない」と意識している
OSS活動はソフトに注目し、ソフトでできることを無視する
すべての有料ソフトはMicrosoft Officeじゃあないと同じように、OSSはFirefoxには限らないのです。
posts.key_insight: Commercial software beats free OSS not on features or price, but by marketing to the user's actual problem rather than treating software as the starting point.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines the features planned for Bingo Card Creator 3.0, a modest update to his small software business. The update includes Vista compatibility fixes, a cloud storage feature for saving/opening files across locations, and an improved import workflow. He hints that the cloud storage feature will support an upcoming new product. The post captures a typical solo developer sprint planning session with realistic time management challenges.
key_points: Vista compatibility drives two of the planned changes, showing the importance of keeping desktop software current with OS changes
The 'Save to Internet' cloud feature serves dual purpose: user convenience and laying groundwork for a future product launch
McKenzie planned 6 hours of work but only managed 1.5, illustrating the common gap between planned and actual development time for side projects
Even small feature updates like moving default save directories can meaningfully improve user experience and reduce support burden
money_quotes: Bingo Card Creator is going all web 2.0 and storing its information in the clooooooooooooud. Just kidding.
I spent 1.5 hours today working on the newest version of Bingo Card Creator, after hoping to spend 6 or so. Drats.
It also paves the way for an upcoming new product I'm going to launch. (Three guesses as to what.)
Funny trivia: 3.0 raised to the 3.0 power is 27, which will be my age on my birthday.
This is for Vista compatibility and, as an added bonus, it will eliminate the 'reenter your license key' headache for upgrading Mac users from here on out.
posts.key_insight: Strategic product development means building features that serve immediate user needs while simultaneously laying infrastructure for future products.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a cascading bug in his shopping cart pricing logic where a quick fix for multi-unit purchases introduced a worse problem: customers being charged $20 instead of $29.95. After multiple failed attempts to patch the issue involving interactions between e-junkie, Google Checkout, and his own code, he reverted to the original system that worked correctly for 99.9% of customers. The experience cost him roughly $120 in lost revenue plus potential scared-off customers.
key_points: A fix for a rare edge case (1 order/year) introduced a far more damaging bug affecting many daily transactions
Complex interactions between multiple payment systems (e-junkie, Google Checkout, PayPal) create hard-to-predict failure modes
Sometimes reverting to a known-good state that handles 99.9% of cases correctly is better than chasing a perfect fix
Quick midnight changes to pricing logic are disproportionately risky compared to other code changes
money_quotes: About a week ago I noticed a deficiency in my understanding of how my shopping cart actually worked for purchases of 2+ units.
Not quite sure why. So I fixed it, by making the default item be a single copy at $29.95 and only having the pricing logic apply when buying through my cart.
Then it hit me — it wasn't actually that edge case, it was a different edge case — involving an ugly mess of Google Checkout, e-junkie, and my code.
I reverted to the cart which charges 1,499 customers a year correctly and causes 1 guy to wait a bit.
I lost about $120 that I know about in arbitrary discounts, plus more if anyone was scared off by the weird inconsistency between my site's pricing and the pricing at Paypal/Google Checkout.
Memo to self: self, you've said it before, but avoid doing 5 minute changes at midnight.
posts.key_insight: Fixing a rare edge case in pricing logic can easily introduce worse bugs, and when money is on the line, reverting to a known-good state beats iterating on broken fixes.

posts.summary: Patrick reports major progress on Bingo Card Creator 3.0's web application, specifically the PDF generation for printing bingo cards. After years of struggling with Java's primitive printing facilities, he found the Ruby Prawn library produced better results in 8 hours than he achieved in 2 years with Java. He outlines remaining work including user management, copy rewriting, and integration challenges, while noting his biggest concern is whether non-technical users will pay for a web application.
key_points: Switching from Java Swing canvas printing to PDF generation via Ruby's Prawn library dramatically improved output quality and reduced code complexity to a quarter of the original size
The web version solves long-requested features (titles, watermarks) that were too difficult to implement in the Java codebase due to accumulated technical debt
The biggest remaining risk isn't technical but commercial: convincing non-technical users that a web application is worth paying for
He plans to keep the desktop version alongside the web app rather than deprecating it, hedging against user resistance to paying for web software
money_quotes: I've got better behavior out of it in 8 hours of playing around then I've been able to coax out of BCC in 2 years.
There is still some ugly spaghetti code hiding in the bowels of it, but its literally a quarter of the size of the stuff in BCC itself, and much, much more comprehensible.
My biggest worry is the perennial one for web applications: can I convince non-technical users that "web sites" are worth paying money for?
This is one of the main reasons I'm not planning on deprecating the downloadable version anytime soon.
I don't want someone printing 800 cards for a Fortune 500 retreat to lock up the server accidentally, but I do want to support that use case.
posts.key_insight: Choosing the right technology stack can compress years of painful workarounds into hours of productive development, but the hardest problems in software products are often commercial rather than technical.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares his task list for Bingo Card Creator 3.0 development during Japan's Golden Week vacation, acknowledging he hasn't made as much progress as hoped. The post reveals the full scope of work needed to ship both a desktop update and a new web application version. He uses public accountability as a motivational tool, publishing his incomplete task list to embarrass himself into working harder. The tasks span client-server architecture, licensing, PDF generation, user workflows, and marketing infrastructure.
key_points: Public accountability through blogging can serve as a motivational tool for solo developers
Shipping a major version requires coordinating across many domains: licensing, server infrastructure, UI, payment integration, and marketing
McKenzie is building both a desktop client-server version and a web app version simultaneously, showing the transition from desktop to web software
Even small software businesses require substantial infrastructure work like mailing lists, autoresponders, staging servers, and caching
money_quotes: I'm wrapping up day two out of the five-day vacation I have for Golden Week, and have not quite gotten as much accomplished on getting the new version out as I had hoped.
Task list to embarass myself into working hard tomorrow:
posts.key_insight: Solo software developers can use public transparency about their progress and task lists as an accountability mechanism to stay motivated.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reports on a productive but frustrating three-day programming sprint working on Bingo Card Creator 3.0. The update adds cloud-based word list storage and server-side PDF generation, but he spent significant time fighting Java's limitations and Linux permissions issues. He's integrating Delayed Job to handle heavy PDF generation asynchronously rather than blocking web requests. The web version of the software still needs to be built, and he's looking for beta testers.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator 3.0 adds cloud word list storage and server-side PDF generation, though Java's standard library gaps made development painful
Delayed Job integration prevents heavy PDF generation from tying up Mongrel web servers, moving work out of the request/response cycle
Linux file permissions created a frustrating blocker where the job execution user couldn't write to its own directory
Moving from a Windows-based rendering pipeline (printer + Adobe Acrobat + Paint screenscraping) to Prawn + ImageMagick yielded a 50x speed improvement
money_quotes: It can interact with the server to save user's word lists in the cloooooooooooud. Which is apparently the $10 buzzword for a client-server application these days.
Did you know that there is no way in Java's standard library to copy a file from point A to point B?
If somebody tries to generate a thousand bingo cards on Prawn, that essentially takes one of my Mongrels and ties it up for a minute.
I have tried everything I can think of to fix this, with no dice, so its time to walk away and come back fresh later.

Prawn + Imagemagick is literally fifty times faster than rendering through the Windows printer and then screenscraping Adobe Acrobat reader then pasting into Paint — automated, naturally — for cropping out a GIF screenshot.
posts.key_insight: Real product development is mostly fighting unglamorous technical problems — Java standard library gaps, Linux permissions, and request queue bottlenecks — rather than building the features themselves.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a brief productivity update on his Rails development work. He resolved a permissions error related to symlinks and chmod, made his first push to GitHub, and published a fork of Delayed::Job that supports multiple workers. The post reflects his transition from SVN to git while noting the friction of Rails development on Windows.
key_points: Symlink permissions errors can be caused by the target directory not having proper chmod settings, even when the symlink and files appear correct
McKenzie made his first GitHub push, finding it slightly painful as a Windows Rails developer but manageable
He extended Delayed::Job to support multiple concurrent workers and open-sourced the fork
money_quotes: If you symlink a to b, and are wondering why you can't access a/c, a/d, and a/e despite the preferences on a, c, d, and e all being right, check to make sure you've chmod-ed b/. properly.
Windows user — I'm used to Rails programming having its share of pain.
I think I'm really going to like git for source control, although since I already have a SVN repository I'll probably keep that as my main method.
posts.key_insight: Small debugging victories and incremental tooling improvements — like moving to git and contributing open source extensions — compound into meaningful productivity gains for solo developers.

posts.summary: Patrick recounts how his Vista laptop's hard drive began failing, and how Dropbox saved his important files despite his procrastination on backups. He had signed up for Dropbox casually after a coworker mentioned it but hadn't seriously used it for backups. After fellow software developers suffered hard drive failures, he at least started backing up his servers, but his laptop remained unprotected. When the inevitable crash came, he managed to boot into safe mode and stream his critical files to Dropbox just in time.
key_points: Procrastination on backups is universal even among technically sophisticated people who know better
Having critical business data distributed across cloud services (Google, Slicehost, PayPal) provided natural redundancy even before a formal backup strategy
Dropbox's symlink trick on Vista allowed syncing files outside the Dropbox folder without risky copy operations on a failing drive
The post serves as a real-time testimonial for Dropbox's value proposition during an actual emergency
money_quotes: "I'm going to start a backup any day now" ranks right up there with "Hmm, well, we haven't heard from that freaky serial killer in a while. I think I'll stay in the house."

Most of my critical business data is already off of my laptop — its at a Google server farm, or chilling on a Slicehost server, or over at Paypal, but there is still quite a bit of sentimental value in my photos.
Today the inevitable happened: my Vista-using laptop decided to start the sputter and die deathspiral.

I think the hard disk covering some of the code loaded by all the bells&whistles that autoload when the machine boots is toast. Luckily untoasted is my ability to read the rest of the disk and run Dropbox.
Here's $100, Dropbox — thanks a million.
posts.key_insight: Even technically savvy people procrastinate on backups, making frictionless cloud sync tools like Dropbox valuable precisely because they work passively before disaster strikes.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a frustrating cron job failure on his Rails server after upgrading to Ubuntu Hardy. Cached files for seasonal content (Mother's Day, Cinco de Mayo) weren't being deleted despite logs showing the commands ran successfully. He could manually delete them but the automated process silently failed. It's a brief venting post about the maddening nature of server administration.
key_points: Cron jobs can silently fail even when logs suggest successful execution, creating a false sense of reliability
Seasonal content caching requires timely cleanup — stale holiday pages lingering weeks later is a real business problem
System upgrades (Ubuntu Hardy) introduced subtle breakage in previously working automation
money_quotes: Once again, cron decided that it would stop deleting my Rails cached files after I upgraded to Hardy.
The logs say that the rf -rm happened at the scheduled times for the last two weeks, and I was able to successfully do it by sudoing into the appropriate user, but the files were still on disk.
Argh, I hate computers some days…
posts.key_insight: Server automation that appears to work according to logs can still silently fail, and OS upgrades are a common culprit for breaking previously reliable cron jobs.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the web application version of Bingo Card Creator, three years after the original desktop release. The new version uses AJAX for live preview of bingo cards as users type, though it lacks some power-user features from the downloadable version. He details the technical architecture involving Prototype, Rails, Prawn PDF generation, and ImageMagick, estimating his server can handle about 20 simultaneous users. The post serves as both a progress update and a request for beta feedback, with a candid acknowledgment that significant work remains on copy, design, and purchasing funnel integration.
key_points: The web version represents a major platform shift from desktop software to a web application, though feature parity hasn't been achieved yet
Live preview of bingo cards consumed half the development time but was considered worth it for user responsiveness
The technical stack chains Rails, Prawn PDF generation, and ImageMagick together with AJAX polling, achieving 300-800ms response times per preview update
The remaining work reveals his systematic approach: copy rewriting, purchasing funnel integration, usage stats, and UI polish all planned before launch
money_quotes: It was ugly, underfeatured, and bug-ridden. Now, it is ugly, underfeatured, and bug-ridden… with AJAX!
A few of the power-user features, particularly design-focused things like picking fonts, are shockingly difficult to implement in a good way in a web application.
I think getting users super-responsive feedback to their actions is worth every minute of it.
Pretty buttons sell B2C software.
Total time per request: on order of 300 to 800 ms.
posts.key_insight: Investing heavily in responsive user feedback — even at the cost of half the total development time — is justified because immediate visual results drive engagement and conversion in consumer software.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a brief progress update on the web version of Bingo Card Creator after spending a day working with a local designer. The redesign covers most screens except the main editing interface. He shares his remaining to-do list before launch, including password recovery, analytics, and purchase integration. The post is a short development log typical of his early blogging style.
key_points: Working with a designer improved UI and usability beyond what McKenzie could achieve alone
Prioritizing launch readiness over perfection — analytics can be postponed but purchasing integration cannot
Even a brief update post includes a natural SEO-optimized internal link to holiday bingo cards
money_quotes: He has a much better eye for design issues, including UI and usability issues, than I do.
It actually now looks almost pretty.
I would include screenshots except I am on Linux and GIMP scares me.
posts.key_insight: Hiring a designer to complement your technical skills produces better results than trying to do everything yourself.

posts.summary: McKenzie solicits reader input on what statistics they'd like to see him track and share publicly following the launch of the web version of Bingo Card Creator. He outlines plans to upgrade his internal stats tracking and lists several metrics he's considering, including signups, logins, customer lifecycles, cohort analysis, and popular activities.
key_points: The web version of Bingo Card Creator enables richer data collection than the desktop version
McKenzie practices radical transparency by sharing business metrics publicly
He plans to track customer lifecycle metrics including time-to-purchase and retention
He recognizes the value of cohort analysis for understanding user behavior patterns
money_quotes: I'm going to spend a portion of my weekend upgrading my internal stats tracking, which means "more stuff to share with you guys".
posts.key_insight: Transitioning from desktop to web software unlocks deeper analytics capabilities around customer lifecycles and behavior that drive better business decisions.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his initial foray into email marketing for Bingo Card Creator's web application. He chose MailChimp for its API and integrated it using the hominid Ruby gem in about 4 hours. His strategy includes monthly newsletters timed to seasonal demand peaks and a two-email onboarding sequence for new signups. He sets a clear profitability threshold of one sale per thousand emails at 2 cents each.
key_points: Email marketing enables re-engagement of seasonal users who signed up months earlier, turning one-time visitors into repeat customers
A two-email drip sequence after signup serves dual purpose: onboarding education and purchase nudging
At 2 cents per email, the break-even point is roughly 1 sale per 1,000 emails sent, making the economics straightforward to evaluate
Integration with MailChimp via API took only 4 hours, demonstrating low barrier to entry for email marketing
money_quotes: Since an absolutely ginormous portion of my business is seasonal, this will let me get in touch with the thousands of users who will sign up before Halloween again at Halloween, and remind them that they can have a great activity ready for only $30.
The emails run me 2 cents apiece, so I'll be looking to see if they generate enough marginal business (about 1 sale per every thousand) to justify.
One more lever to optimize…
The first covers basic how-to-use instructions and is just to jog people's memory that they signed up.
The 2nd is to show some of the more advanced features of BCC and it will probably include a strong suggestion that they buy it… maybe a coupon, too.
posts.key_insight: Email marketing turns a free web app's user registrations into a remarketing channel, letting you re-engage seasonal users at minimal cost with a clear, measurable ROI threshold.

posts.summary: McKenzie advocates for releasing software early and iterating based on customer feedback and usage statistics rather than waiting for perfection. He shares early metrics from the web version of Bingo Card Creator, showing strong engagement (3,138 cards printed, 200 trial signups) but zero sales in the first week. He notes that summer is a natural dead zone for his education-focused market and remains optimistic that engaged users will convert to paying customers when the school year begins.
key_points: Release as early as your application provides value to anyone, then iterate based on real usage data
Customer behavior statistics are often more useful feedback than what customers say they want
Strong engagement metrics (3,138 cards printed in a week) don't automatically translate to immediate revenue
Understanding your market's seasonal cycle prevents panic over predictable slow periods
Building a base of engaged free users during off-season creates a pipeline for conversions when demand returns
money_quotes: I'm a heavy subscriber to the notion that you should release your application as early as it possibly provides value to anyone, then start iterating based on customer feedback.
Since it isn't always practical to get feedback, and since customers do not always know what they want, I'd also consider stats about what they do to be among the most useful forms of feedback.
If you add a quick start guide to your software, and twice as many customers manage to save their first document as did the day before, that is probably a sign that you're doing something right.
Sales directly attributable to online version: 0. OK, anti-booyah.
Summer is a natural deadzone in my market so I'm not excessively worried by that, but hopefully things will pick up again like crazy come the schoolyear.
posts.key_insight: Ship early, measure what users actually do rather than what they say, and don't mistake a seasonal lull for product failure when your engagement metrics are strong.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares practical conversion optimization tips for his Bingo Card Creator software, drawn from analyzing CrazyEgg heat maps collected over several years. He identifies that users on his purchasing page frequently click navigation links that take them backward in the funnel, and removes those distracting options. He also fixes a misleadingly-labeled 'Information' button and adjusts his purchase page copy to work for both his downloadable and online product versions. The post emphasizes that small improvements at each funnel step have multiplicative effects on revenue, and that all changes should ultimately be validated through A/B testing rather than intuition alone.
key_points: Heat map analysis revealed users on the purchasing page were clicking Free Trial and other navigation links, moving backward in the funnel instead of completing purchases
Removing distracting navigation options from the purchase page follows the same pattern used by Amazon and other major retailers during checkout
Copy framing matters enormously: changing 'Purchase a single copy for only $24.95' to emphasize instant access over the transaction was worth over a thousand dollars
Each step in the conversion funnel has multiplicative effects, so a 5% improvement at any step translates to roughly 5% more revenue
Confidence in best practices should always yield to actual testing evidence in your specific market
money_quotes: Every step is crucial because the effects are multiplicative: a 5% improvement anywhere essentially results in 5% straight to the bottom line.
I love giving users free things that make them happy, but there is a time and place for it, and that time and place is not right in the middle of me asking them for money.

Despite four people telling me 'You know, people seeing a button titled Information will click it thinking that it offers Information and will get upset when it just causes their screen to blink', I never got around to changing that.
Confidence loses to evidence every day and twice on Sunday.
Conversion optimization is a science, not black magic: test, test, and test again to see what works in your particular market and circumstances.
When you're selling something which is specifically designed to avoid the hassles associated with being downloaded, offering instant download is perhaps not the best idea!
posts.key_insight: Conversion optimization is about systematically finding and fixing the places where your funnel leaks attention, and every improvement compounds multiplicatively across the entire purchase flow.

posts.summary: McKenzie identifies a conversion flaw on his own site: clicking the large homepage screenshot opens a lightbox with no call-to-action, creating a dead end. He fixes this by adding download buttons directly inside the lightbox, eliminating an unnecessary click from the conversion path. The change was inspired by advice he gave someone else about maintaining clear user direction on every page element. He considers the improvement obvious enough to skip A/B testing.
key_points: Every clickable element on a page should move the user closer to conversion, not into a dead end
The homepage screenshot captured 10%+ of clicks but led to a lightbox with no next action
Reducing clicks in the conversion funnel by bringing CTAs to where users already are is a reliable optimization
Sometimes improvements are obvious enough that A/B testing is unnecessary overhead
money_quotes: Clicking on it currently brings up a maximized version of the screenshot in a lightbox. The lightbox is a wonderful effect, but what is the user supposed to do with the maximized version?
That is two more clicks, bringing to three the total of clicks they have made to my page! Clearly, there is an opportunity to eliminate a click.
All I need to do is bring the buttons to them, in the constrained environment of the lightbox.
For obvious usability wins like this one, I'm not going to bother A/B testing prior to pushing it live.
posts.key_insight: Every interactive element on your page should have a clear next step toward conversion — if a click leads to a dead end, you're wasting the user's intent.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers his Google AdWords campaigns have been essentially turned off for over a week, dropping from 10,000 daily impressions to 20, with no notification from Google. After filing a support request and receiving an unhelpful automated response, he investigates and finds a possible Rails bug that prevented conversion tracking for his web application's trial signups. He's uncertain this was the actual cause since his conversion metrics remained near historical norms. The post is a detailed account of the frustration of depending on an opaque advertising platform with effectively non-existent customer support.
key_points: Google AdWords silently reduced his ad impressions by a factor of 1,000 with no notification, directly threatening his business revenue
Google's customer support is effectively useless — buried contact forms, character-limited bug reports, and responses that don't address the actual problem
A subtle Rails bug in conversion tracking may have caused the issue, where a missing instance variable initialization prevented Google from receiving conversion pings for web app signups
Platform dependency is dangerous: when AdWords works it's excellent, but when it breaks you have almost no recourse
Even as a literal Google AdWords case study, McKenzie couldn't get meaningful human support
money_quotes: I have as convincing evidence for the existence of Google technical support as I have evidence for the existence of Santa Claus.
Google hates dealing with customers. We're little annoying things that don't scale well when we can't be handled perfectly algorithmically like all the world's information.
When the system is operational it rocks my socks off, but when it goes off the rails you are screwed.
My email was limited to 512 characters, because Google apparently after indexing the entire freaking Internet didn't have enough hard disk space in BigTable to save bug reports of a useful length.
You weren't even worth enough of our time to have one of our $6 an hour Indian callcenter employees sign her name to this.
Despite our expertise at ordering the world's information, they are neither relevant nor even plausibly related to your inquiry, and most are an insult to your intelligence.
I'm blogging about this, obviously, because I want anybody else in the same situation to have some support which is not a Markov Chain.
posts.key_insight: Depending on an opaque advertising platform for revenue is terrifying because when automated systems break, the lack of meaningful human support means you're left debugging a black box with no recourse.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares practical tips for designing effective landing pages, drawing from his experience redesigning Bingo Card Creator's landing page which increased AdWords conversions from 18% to 28%. He advocates for ruthless focus on a single goal, stripping away all navigation and visual elements that don't serve that goal. The post covers trust markers like testimonials, money-back guarantees, pleasing Google's landing page quality signals, and maintaining continuity between ad copy and landing page messaging. He emphasizes that users skim rather than read, and that the entire flow from ad click to conversion should feel like one continuous experience.
key_points: Landing pages should have exactly one goal — strip out all navigation, eliminate distractions, and make the desired action blazingly obvious
Testimonials work but need editing: bold the selling bits, cut what doesn't sell, while keeping them authentic enough not to seem manufactured
Internet users skim more than they read and feel more than they think, so design for scanning behavior rather than careful reading
Continuity between ad copy and landing page call-to-action reduces friction — visitors arrive mentally invested in the action your ad promised
Google is a shadow audience for your landing pages: include trust markers, avoid looking like an affiliate, and handle duplicate content with noindex tags
money_quotes: Landing pages should have one goal.
I cannot stress enough how much people do not read on the Internet.
They are not professional marketers and should not be expected to write like them. You are, so give them a minor hand.

I don't feel like elaborating on this, other than to say I once walked out the door without pants on. That is a pretty serious oversight, but it still isn't as bad as forgetting your trusty money-back guarantee.
Technically speaking there are more search engines than Google to worry about. Hah. Sorry, where was I.
Seeing your ad, clicking on your ad, reading your landing page, and then interaction with The One True Goal should be one continuous flow, free of jarring transitions and mental static.
Polishing it until it is too good is another danger. Testimonials should remain authentic.
posts.key_insight: A landing page should ruthlessly serve a single goal, with every element — from stripped navigation to edited testimonials to ad-matched copy — aligned to move the visitor toward that one action.

posts.summary: McKenzie briefly announces a detailed article he wrote about integrating Mixpanel analytics with Ruby on Rails. He highlights Mixpanel's funnel tracking as particularly valuable despite being more technically demanding than most micro-ISV owners might prefer. He notes that the analytics insights have already led to changes in Bingo Card Creator, and mentions he's on an optimization kick before the busy school year season.
key_points: Mixpanel's funnel tracking capability is exceptionally valuable for understanding user conversion paths
There's a tradeoff between analytics power and technical complexity that matters for solo software businesses
Seasonal business cycles (school year) create urgency to optimize before peak demand
money_quotes: It is probably slightly more technically involved than most uISVs would care for, but the funnel tracking is solid gold.
Got to get ready for the school year! Three more weeks or so and I won't be able to break the website NEARLY so often.
posts.key_insight: Funnel tracking tools like Mixpanel provide outsized value for conversion optimization even when they require more technical setup than typical small-business analytics.

posts.summary: McKenzie reports that his landing page redesign dramatically improved AdWords conversion rates from ~21-22% to 28%. The redesign focused on a clear 'One True Goal' button that captured over 180 clicks per 1,000 visitors. This improvement effectively reduces his customer acquisition cost by about 25%. He anticipates Google's Conversion Optimizer will respond by increasing his CPC bids, which should drive even more impressions and conversions.
key_points: A focused landing page with a single clear call-to-action ('One True Goal' button) boosted conversion rates from ~22% to 28%
A 28% conversion rate means customer acquisition costs dropped by roughly 25%, significant since AdWords was his largest expense
Google's Conversion Optimizer creates a positive feedback loop: better conversion rates lead to higher bids, more impressions, and more conversions
Heatmap tools like CrazyEgg provided the visual evidence to measure where users actually clicked
money_quotes: I am almost embarassed how well this is working.
For the last 1,000 visitors, the One True Goal button got over 180 clicks.
The conversion rate is about 28%. Yowza!
The cost of customer acquisition for me just declined by about 25%, which is good because AdWords is my largest expense by far.
This means that the same number of impressions results in a third more trial downloads for me, and hopefully thus a third more sales.
posts.key_insight: A focused landing page redesign with a single clear call-to-action can dramatically improve conversion rates, creating a compounding effect through lower acquisition costs and algorithmic bid optimization.

posts.summary: Patrick announces A/Bingo, his open-source A/B testing framework for Ruby on Rails, built to replace Google Website Optimizer. He found GWO too cumbersome to set up, code, and maintain, so he spent a week building his own solution. A/Bingo requires just one line of code to set up tests and track conversions, includes built-in statistical significance testing, and is optimized for performance. He considers it the most technically impressive code he's ever written and expects it to dramatically increase his testing velocity.
key_points: Google Website Optimizer's friction in setup, coding, and URL maintenance motivated building a custom solution
A/Bingo reduces A/B test setup to a single line of code, removing the friction that prevents developers from testing
The framework includes built-in statistical significance testing with human-readable output, lowering the bar for non-statisticians
Reducing friction in tooling directly increases how often you use it — he expects to run significantly more tests as a result
money_quotes: Setting up a test takes one line of code. It is so easy you'll find yourself doing it automatically when writing copy or making new features.
It does statistical significance testing for you, producing human-readable output which tells you exactly how confident you can be that the test results are accurate.
A/Bingo is, without question, the most technically impressive code I've ever written.
I expect that the ease of use and copious features will mean I run a lot more tests.
I scored a 16.9% improvement to funnel completion thanks to Mixpanel.
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to A/B testing isn't understanding its value but the friction of existing tools — reducing test setup to one line of code transforms testing from an occasional effort into a default habit.

posts.summary: McKenzie celebrates reaching $50,000 in cumulative sales of Bingo Card Creator after three years, while noting approximately $27,000 in pre-tax profits. He reports strong momentum heading into the school year sales season, with the online version converting at 1.7% of trials. New ad campaigns are posting his highest conversion rates ever, and his first email newsletter via Mailchimp generated a 30x return on investment.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator reached $50,000 in cumulative revenue over three years with roughly $27,000 in pre-tax profit, showing the modest but real economics of a micro-ISV
The web-based version of the product is dramatically improving conversion rates and making ad campaigns more effective by lowering signup friction
Email marketing via Mailchimp showed exceptional ROI: $2 in costs generated $60 in sales from the first newsletter
money_quotes: I'm cautiously optimistic that the next $50,000 won't take 3 years.
Profits before taxes are somewhere in the $27,000 range if I've managed to tally all my receipts correctly — always unlikely until tax time rolls around.
My first newsletter, sent yesterday via Mailchimp, resulted in 2 sales ($60) for about $2 worth of email postage.
posts.key_insight: A solo software business can compound steadily over years, with each new channel (web version, email marketing, better ads) accelerating growth beyond what the initial trajectory suggested.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares early results from A/B testing on Bingo Card Creator after building his own testing framework (A/Bingo). He finds that removing guest login and hiding email permission checkboxes had no significant effect on trial signups. However, reordering buttons to prioritize the trial registration call-to-action over downloads increased conversion from 11.9% to 19.8%. He also previews two ongoing tests around purchasing page design and landing page copy.
key_points: Removing the guest login option didn't increase trial signups but massively decreased guest users, so he reverted the change
Hiding email subscription checkboxes had no measurable effect on trial signups, suggesting users don't find them off-putting
Reordering buttons to put the trial CTA above the download link increased conversion from 11.9% to 19.8% — a significant win
He acknowledges a methodological mistake in his email test (removing reassurance copy along with the checkboxes) and plans to rerun it properly
Having an easy-to-use A/B testing tool (his own A/Bingo) dramatically increased his testing velocity
money_quotes: I am going hog wild with A/B testing now that I have an easy way to do it.
There is no significant difference in the number of trial signups I get if I put a discrete login as guest link on the registration form.
There is, however, a massive decrease in the number of guests (obviously, they have to decline by at least 50%, right?).
Reordering the bingo card pages to include the Make Your Own Cards over the Download These Cards buttons increases the conversion to the trial from 11.90% to 19.82%.
I stupidly included the 'We do not spam you' verbiage in the block excised for the test. That should have been left in both alternatives.
posts.key_insight: Most A/B tests produce null results, but the simple act of reordering calls-to-action to prioritize what you actually want users to do can yield dramatic conversion improvements.

posts.summary: McKenzie briefly recounts having so many domain names that a bulk renewal triggered a fraud alert from his credit card company. He spent $600 at GoDaddy for multi-year renewals across dozens of domains, plus $160 for a buyer's club membership to get discounts. The post is a lighthearted aside about the hidden costs of being a serial project starter who accumulates domains for various business ideas.
key_points: Bulk domain renewals can trigger credit card fraud alerts, illustrating how quickly costs accumulate
GoDaddy's buyer's club membership can pay for itself if you own enough domains, particularly .net domains at 53% off
Owning many domains without developed sites creates a backlog that requires outside help to address
money_quotes: you have to phone Chase in the middle of the night to say the $600 charge to GoDaddy was authorized and it was a very good deal, too, thank you very much.
A few dozen domains times two year renewal adds up really, really quickly…
I really need to get some freelancers to help me fill these things out.
posts.key_insight: The entrepreneurial habit of registering domains for every business idea quietly accumulates into a surprisingly expensive portfolio of unrealized projects.

posts.summary: McKenzie walks through the technical process of running a WordPress blog as a subdirectory of a Rails application using Nginx as a reverse proxy. He argues that despite Rails being the main app framework, WordPress is the superior blogging platform and worth integrating. The key architectural decision is placing the blog on a subdirectory rather than a subdomain for SEO benefits. The setup involves creating a WordPress theme from the Rails app's design, installing WordPress on a private URL, and configuring Nginx to proxy requests.
key_points: Blogs should live on subdirectories rather than subdomains for SEO reasons, even when the main app runs on a different stack
WordPress is vastly superior to Rails blogging platforms, making cross-stack integration worthwhile
ThemesPress can generate a WordPress theme from existing HTML/CSS for $10, saving significant development time
Nginx reverse proxy lets WordPress appear to be part of the Rails app while running independently on a private server
money_quotes: Since Bingo Card Creator is powered by Rails, ideally I'd be using a Rails blog platform, but frankly, they're all vastly inferior to WordPress.
I could have slapped up a WordPress blog on a subdomain and called it a day, but I always advise people to put blogs on subdirectories for SEO reasons, so I thought I'd do it properly.
If you're not a PHP-speaking web designer, this step can take a lot of time. Or, alternatively, you can just pay $10 and use ThemesPress.
This is well worth your time.
It is easy to bork this setup if you don't understand WordPress and Nginx configuration files.
posts.key_insight: Pragmatic integration of the best tool for each job — WordPress for blogging, Rails for the app — beats ideological purity about using a single stack, especially when SEO demands subdirectory placement.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a freelance writing job listing for his Bingo Card Creator business. He needs someone to write content for 13 seasonal mini-sites targeting holiday bingo activities like Halloween and Christmas bingo. The role involves writing warm, grammatically flawless copy aimed at teachers, plus basic WordPress configuration. He offers $100 per site and emphasizes his reputation as a prompt-paying, no-nonsense client.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator's intense seasonality drives the need for 13 holiday-specific mini-sites to capture seasonal search traffic
The target audience of teachers demands grammatically flawless writing, showing deep understanding of customer expectations
McKenzie's approach to hiring emphasizes clear scope, reference implementations, and pre-built infrastructure to minimize freelancer friction
The job listing itself doubles as content marketing and SEO, published on his blog rather than a freelance marketplace
money_quotes: Before I go to one of the body shopping sites and get inundated with proposals by folks offering to clone eBay for $25, I thought I would put the word out here.
Bingo Card Creator, my small software business, is intensely seasonal.
Most importantly: the writing needs to be warm, inviting, and grammatically flawless. I sell to teachers. They notice these things.
I am a very no-nonsense client who provides clear direction, does not micromanage, and pays scarily promptly.
I have previously paid in excess of $2,000 to freelancers without incident.
posts.key_insight: A well-scoped freelance project with clear deliverables, reference implementations, and prepared infrastructure minimizes friction and attracts better talent than open-ended marketplace postings.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie briefly acknowledges the growing attention his A/Bingo Rails split testing framework has received from prominent Ruby on Rails community sites. He reflects that naming the tool with a pun tied to his bingo card business may have been limiting, since the framework's capabilities extend well beyond that niche. He invites users to reach out with questions or feature requests.
key_points: A/Bingo gained visibility through coverage on the official Ruby on Rails blog and Rails Inside
McKenzie recognizes that a punny name tied to his niche product may undersell the tool's broader utility
Open invitation for community feedback signals his commitment to maintaining the open source project
money_quotes: Next time, incorporating a pun into the name is fine, but incorporating a pun more limiting than the actual feature set is perhaps not a good idea.
posts.key_insight: When naming a product, a clever pun can backfire if it constrains people's perception of what the product actually does.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he's abandoning desktop software development in favor of web applications, based on concrete data from running both versions of Bingo Card Creator simultaneously. The web version converts trial users to paying customers at nearly double the rate of the desktop version, despite having fewer features and only two months of development versus three years. Web apps eliminate the lengthy shareware download funnel, drastically reduce support burden, enable real-time A/B testing and analytics, and make piracy essentially impossible. The cumulative advantages in conversion rates, customer acquisition costs, development velocity, and per-user customization make web apps strictly dominant for his business.
key_points: The shareware download funnel has 17 discrete steps where customers can drop off, while web apps eliminate most of them, resulting in nearly 2x better trial-to-purchase conversion rates
Better conversion rates cut AdWords cost per acquisition from $20 to $9, creating a structural competitive advantage against rivals selling desktop software
Web apps reduced support requests from 15 per 50 customers to 3, by eliminating installation issues, lost registration keys, and outdated software versions

Analytics on the web app revealed counterintuitive insights: the most common use case was baby showers (not what he expected), more features actually hurt sales, and most customers purchase within two hours of signup
Web apps enable per-user customization and rapid A/B testing — 67 updates deployed in 7 weeks as a side project, versus months for desktop distribution
money_quotes: There is no customer support experience so good as avoiding the problem in the first place.
Theoretical utility of desktop software is one thing, but I can't ignore what my numbers are telling me.
