The more features I add to the software, the worse it sells. This is, needless to say, highly unintuitive to most software developers.
Most customers purchase within two hours of signup, so it is absolutely imperative that their first use of the software exceed all their expectations.
I have released 67 updates to Bingo Card Creator in the past 7 weeks, and this isn't even my day job.
A button here, some textual edits there, seven A/B tests, etc etc, and pretty soon you're looking at the magic of compounding 1% improvements.
At the end of the day, I'm an engineer. I follow the numbers where they lead me.
posts.key_insight: Web applications structurally outperform desktop software for small businesses because they compress the sales funnel, enable rapid experimentation, and produce data that reveals counterintuitive truths about customer behavior.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes A/B testing a redesigned signup page for Bingo Card Creator, replacing a spartan Wufoo-derived form with a more visually engaging design featuring a sidebar for conversion arguments. He also addresses the jarring transition users experience when moving from themed mini-sites (like Halloween bingo) to the main site by customizing the signup page to match the referring mini-site's branding and theme.
key_points: A visually engaging signup page with a sidebar for persuasive content can improve conversions over a spartan form
Maintaining 'scent' — visual continuity between a landing page and signup page — prevents users from bouncing when they feel they've landed in the wrong place
Small investments in design consistency across domains (matching colors, headers, imagery) reduce friction in the conversion funnel
Even functional designs that empirically convert well may benefit from testing against more polished alternatives
money_quotes: I like this because it is more visually engaging and contains the sidebar, which I can stuff full of reasons they should sign up without impacting the readability of the main form.

The transition from that page to my site was sort of jarring — you move over domains, which normal users could care less about, but you also move from a black and orange color scheme to my soothing pastel blues.
I was worried about losing the 'scent' — having a user figure 'Whoops, this isn't getting me to what I wanted' and closing out prior to signing up.
It empirically works (fairly well, actually) but it is spartan.
All I have to do is upload a header image and write a wee bit of CSS and, presto-changeo.
posts.key_insight: Maintaining visual continuity ('scent') between landing pages and signup pages prevents users from bouncing when the design shift makes them doubt they're in the right place.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the upcoming release of Bingo Card Creator 3.0 during a five-day Japanese holiday weekend. He requests beta testers for Windows and Mac versions and outlines his ambitious weekend agenda including the release, new features, a major article, and A/B testing. The new version adds cloud save/load, PDF export for registered users, and a streamlined registration process that eliminates the need for most users to manually enter registration keys.
key_points: BCC 3.0 adds cloud storage and PDF export, moving the desktop app toward web integration
The registration process was simplified so most users never see a registration key, reducing friction
PDF export is implemented via a web service call rather than native Java, reflecting McKenzie's pragmatic approach to avoiding unnecessary coding
McKenzie maintains an aggressive shipping cadence, planning a major release plus A/B testing and content creation in a single holiday weekend
money_quotes: It can save and load files from the clooooooooooooud.
By calling a PDF creation web service, because I swear anything that reduces Java coding makes me happy.
I have improved the registration process, transparently to the user, such that the majority of them will never have to see a Registration Key. They just need to click OK.
posts.key_insight: Reducing user friction in registration and leveraging web services to avoid complex native coding are pragmatic ways to improve both the customer experience and developer productivity.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that working longer hours is a counterproductive strategy for business success, particularly for startups and small software companies. Drawing on his experience as both a Japanese salaryman working 70-hour weeks and a side-project entrepreneur averaging four hours weekly, he demonstrates that productivity should be measured by business value created, not hours invested. He introduces the concept of a 'pseudo-wage' to evaluate whether tasks are worth doing personally, then presents three concrete techniques: outsourcing low-value work, automating processes, and eliminating unproductive time. His freelancer content strategy generates $1,300/month in sales from five minutes of monthly oversight, proving that leverage beats effort.
key_points: Working longer hours is not a defensible competitive advantage — competitors can always match your hours, and well-resourced companies will always have more total man-hours than you
The 'pseudo-wage' concept lets you assign dollar values to tasks and compare them rationally, revealing that many common activities earn far below your target hourly rate
Outsourcing content creation to freelancers generated $1,300/month in sales from only five minutes of monthly management — results impossible to achieve by simply working more hours
Process improvement compounds over time: automating freelancer workflows went from 60 minutes/month to 5 minutes/month through iterative refinement
Most business blogging value follows a power law — 90% of value comes from fewer than 10% of posts, making long-form essays dramatically more efficient than frequent short posts
money_quotes: The notion that working longer hours is correlated to better business results is a pernicious social pathology.
Take it from a Japanese salaryman, guys: it is a disease so vicious that in addition to hobbling businesses it damages society itself.
Programmers at most companies work on a schedule designed to maximize the productivity of illiterate 18th century water loom operators, so expecting rationality might be excessively optimistic.
If you want to earn $100 an hour, you'd better not busy yourself with $5 an hour tasks.
No middle American schoolmarm perceives additional value over having my saliva on the envelope her software came in.
A startup's most important product is the process the startup uses to create products.
Five minutes isn't zero minutes, either, so I still have work to do.
Working for the sake of work is a waste of time, resources, and human potential.
posts.key_insight: Business productivity should be measured in value created per hour, not hours worked, and the highest-leverage move is replacing low-value labor with outsourcing and automation rather than simply working longer.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie outlines three tactical approaches for software businesses to capitalize on seasonal demand spikes: mini-sites optimized for holiday keywords, targeted PPC campaigns with seasonal landing pages, and strategic email marketing to existing trial users. Drawing from his experience selling bingo card software to teachers, he demonstrates how October/Halloween drives his best month annually. He emphasizes building reusable infrastructure for rapid campaign deployment and explains the powerful economics of trading digital IP for conversions. The post is a practical playbook showing that even niche software businesses have seasonal patterns worth exploiting.
key_points: Most software developers wrongly assume demand is static year-round; identifying and exploiting seasonal spikes can dramatically boost revenue
Mini-sites with holiday-specific domains and content can rank easily for seasonal keywords at low cost, serving as targeted funnels separate from your main site
Building reusable landing page infrastructure lets you launch seasonal campaigns in minutes rather than hours, making experimentation nearly free
Email marketing to existing trial users during seasonal peaks is highly efficient because they already trust your product and just need a timely nudge
Digital IP can be replicated at zero marginal cost, making it an extraordinarily efficient incentive to offer customers in exchange for moving toward conversion
money_quotes: Many software/web-app developers naively assume that demand for their product is roughly static year-round, despite the fact that this is true for very, very few industries.
If software developers can't respect license terms then we are doooooomed.
Not only can you do email marketing in a non-spammy fashion, some people actually enjoy it so much that they'll email you to ask why the September newsletter hasn't arrived yet.
Fear and distrust of the Internet and the evil virus-spreading Spammy McPhishertons on it runs rampant in my market, so I spend significant efforts trying to demonstrate that I'm on the up-and-up.

Any IP you create can be replicated essentially indefinitely for no marginal cost, much like your main product. However, customers don't see the world that way — they live in a world where scarcity is a reality, and accordingly perceive value in things they don't have but might want.
The economics of this are staggeringly efficient compared to e.g. PPC advertising, and you're giving the offer to folks who are already pre-disposed to liking you.
Both making the offer and fulfilling it can be automated, so it costs a static amount of labor no matter how many customers you have and how many take you up on the offer.
posts.key_insight: Software businesses should identify their seasonal demand spikes and build reusable infrastructure for mini-sites, PPC campaigns, and email promotions that can be deployed rapidly at near-zero marginal cost.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts how a seemingly trivial CSS change—replacing a standard HTML submit button with a graphical one—caused the sign-up and login buttons to vanish in IE6/IE7, going undetected for over a month during his busiest sales season. The text-indent CSS trick that hid button text also hid the background image in older IE browsers, effectively removing the ability to register. He estimates the bug cost him $3,750 in lost sales, more than his monthly salary. Despite multiple warning signals—shifting signup ratios, declining AdWords conversions, unusual support tickets—he rationalized each away individually. The incident illustrates how small, 'obviously safe' changes can have catastrophic effects and how confirmation bias prevents you from connecting dots you aren't looking for.
key_points: A one-line CSS change deemed too trivial to A/B test independently caused sign-up buttons to vanish for 65% of his browser audience
Multiple warning indicators (guest account ratio shift, declining AdWords conversions, unusual support tickets) were each rationalized away in isolation
The bug cost an estimated $3,750 in lost profit over 32 days—more than his monthly salary at his day job
Data visualizations only reveal problems you already know to look for; default views concealed the issue while a segmented view made it immediately obvious
Automated sanity checks and alerts on key business metrics would have caught the problem within 24 hours instead of 32 days
money_quotes: I thought this was such an obviously beneficial change that it was not worth taking on extra coding complexity to keep it the old way. Stupid, stupid, stupid.

Visualizations are wonderful for seeing problems that you already know to look for. However, they are poor for informing you of problems that aren't visible in the default visualization, since you won't spend the time to slice the data to see the problem if you don't know it exists.
I lost about 64% of my conversions since September 21st.

That unfortunately flows almost straight to the bottom line, since all of my major costs happen whether I make the sale or not — I probably lost in excess of $3,400 in profits. That is more than my monthly salary.
That was finally the eight sigma event that convinced me that my process was really, really out of control.
It is very hacky — sort of like patching a wound to your artery with duct tape — but you can't be too choosy when you're leaking over $100 a day and have the CSS skills of a vole rat.
This sequence of events is about as likely as flipping a coin and having it land on edge.
posts.key_insight: Small changes that seem too trivial to test rigorously can silently destroy your business metrics, and without automated sanity-check alerts, you will rationalize away every individual warning signal until the damage is catastrophic.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes tracking down a subtle bug where his analytics and A/B testing data was corrupted for months because a deterministic srand() call in his bingo card printing code was polluting the random seed used to generate unique visitor IDs. Trial users printing cards would reset the random seed to a fixed value, causing subsequent visitors to receive identical 'random' IDs, which Mixpanel then treated as duplicate events. The bug caused roughly 60% of analytics data to be silently discarded. He discovered it only when a massive discrepancy between his signup logs and Mixpanel's reported numbers became impossible to ignore during a Halloween promotion analysis.
key_points: A deterministic srand() call in one part of the codebase silently corrupted random number generation in completely unrelated code sharing the same process
The bug went undetected for months because analytics showed plausible-looking numbers rather than obvious zeros, making it easy to dismiss nagging doubts
A/B test significance measurements remained valid despite the data corruption because the errors were evenly distributed across test alternatives
Process-level shared state (like random seeds in Mongrel/Ruby) creates hidden coupling between unrelated features that can produce extraordinarily subtle bugs
The debugging process followed a logical chain: check API calls, check ID uniqueness, inspect distribution patterns, recognize exponential decay as srand signature
money_quotes: I have a confession to make: I often trust code that I thinks works.
I knew my conversion code worked and it wasn't displaying zeroes, so I mentally pushed it out of mind.

Given that my user login code was correct (or there would be much, much more serious problems than analytics failing — people would be seeing my admin screens, bingo cards would bleed across accounts, cats and dogs would be friends, etc)
Two months of my analytics and A/B test data is corrupted.

A/B test results are shockingly robust against programming errors like this: as long as the source of error doesn't correlate with your A/B choices, the errors will be evenly distributed across both alternatives, so it just washes out.

Don't use srand(constant) anywhere in your production code, even if it is the easiest way to get what you want, because sooner or later you will use rand() in some unrelated code called by the same process and now you have extraordinarily subtle bugs caused by reuse of random numbers.
posts.key_insight: Shared mutable process-level state like random seeds creates invisible coupling between unrelated features, and the most dangerous bugs are the ones that produce plausible-looking wrong data rather than obvious failures.

posts.summary: McKenzie demonstrates how Ruby metaprogramming can eliminate boilerplate code when storing object preferences, using his Bingo Card Creator's PrintJob class as a real-world example. Rather than adding database columns for each new property (requiring migrations) or using ugly has_many associations, he serializes options to a JSON hash and uses metaprogramming to auto-generate getter/setter methods. This approach saved roughly 200 lines of code, required zero controller or schema changes when adding new features, and still works with Rails validations and form helpers. He argues this pattern is widely applicable to any one-to-many relationship where the 'many' side is only accessed through its parent.
key_points: Three approaches to storing object properties in Rails: separate columns (requires migrations), has_many associations (ugly syntax, join overhead), or serialized JSON hash with metaprogrammed accessors (cleanest)
Metaprogramming saved ~200 lines of boilerplate and made the controller extraordinarily robust against change — adding five new properties required zero changes to controllers or schema
Rails doesn't care how methods come to exist on a model — metaprogrammed accessors work identically with validations, form helpers, and other Rails magic
The tradeoff is losing SQL queryability on those fields, which is acceptable when the data is only accessed per-user and per-id
Programmatically creating methods from user-controlled input is extremely dangerous and must only be done from trusted, developer-defined constants
money_quotes: 200 lines of negacode (code that you don't have to write) means 200 lines of code that you don't have to read, test, maintain, or debug.

The less boilerplate code you have to write, understand, read, test, debug, and maintain, the more time you can spend creating new features for your customers, doing marketing, or doing other things of value for your business.
Toy problems are great, but foos and bars don't get me home from the day job quicker or convince my customers to pay me money.

This controller is extraordinarily robust against change. When I added five extra options to the print jobs to accommodate my new features, I didn't change one single line of the associated controllers.
Programmatically instantiating methods is extraordinarily dangerous if you don't know what you're doing.
It takes about 5 seconds to count: 217,264 cards printed to date. Thanks users!
posts.key_insight: Metaprogramming's real value isn't cleverness — it's eliminating boilerplate so that adding new features requires changing only the parts that actually differ, leaving controllers, schemas, and tests untouched.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that a startup called HearABlog has been converting his blog posts into audio podcasts without his prior knowledge. He evaluates the quality positively, noting their skill at describing visual elements like screenshots and graphs. Rather than objecting to the unauthorized derivative work, he grants explicit blanket permission for them to continue. He later discovers they had actually tried to contact him but the email got lost during a busy period.
key_points: A startup created audio versions of his blog posts as a demo, which he discovered through referral logs
He appreciates the quality of the audio adaptations, particularly the oral descriptions of visual content
He grants blanket permission for the derivative works despite not being asked first, choosing to support cool projects
He corrects the pronunciation of his made-up company name Kalzumeus, noting even invented words have canonical pronunciations
The startup had actually emailed him for permission but the message was lost during his busy Halloween sales season
money_quotes: The quality is quite good — I'm especially impressed at their oral descriptions of the screenshots and graphs that were crucial to understanding some of those posts.
Because even made up words have a canonical pronunciation, even if only in my mind.

I'm not sure if strictly speaking creating derivative works from this blog is copyright kosher without asking me first, but in the interests of supporting people doing cool things, HearABlog has my explicit permission to produce and distribute audio recordings of all my blog posts on this site.
I have an email address and a marked aversion to saying 'No', so please, feel free to tell me about it in advance.
Unfortunately, he managed to email me when I was on a plane over the Pacific, and I didn't see the email later in the crush of payment notifications. A high class problem, to be sure.
posts.key_insight: When someone creates something cool with your work, default to granting permission and supporting them rather than enforcing copyright — but gently suggest they ask first next time.

posts.summary: McKenzie reviews Visual Website Optimizer (VWO), a new A/B testing tool from Indian startup Wingify that makes split testing accessible to non-technical small business owners. He explains how founder Paras Chopra pivoted from building an enterprise analytics system to creating an elegantly simple WYSIWYG A/B testing interface after discovering customers needed simplicity, not power. McKenzie praises the product's UI as vastly superior to Google Website Optimizer for non-technical users, while offering candid advice about the startup's Basecamp-inspired design and pricing strategy. He frames VWO as bringing a powerful technique to the 'Fortune 5 Million' — small businesses that couldn't previously access A/B testing.
key_points: VWO's genius is a WYSIWYG interface where users click page elements and type variations, eliminating the HTML/JavaScript knowledge that Google Website Optimizer still requires
Wingify pivoted from enterprise analytics to simple A/B testing after discovering through customer development that small businesses needed pick-up-and-go tools, not powerful systems
Small teams can deliver embarrassingly better UIs than Google because they can focus on a specific user need without enterprise compromises
McKenzie candidly warns the Indian startup about copying Basecamp's design, noting that people don't take copiers seriously and first impressions are hard to shake
The product is potentially worth ten times whatever they plan to charge, since a single additional conversion for a dentist or real estate agent could be worth hundreds or thousands of dollars
money_quotes: They need the Microsoft Word of Internet marketing, a simple pick-up-and-go tool that you can install, employ, and benefit from without needing cooperation from the operations, marketing, and engineering teams which you either a) don't have or b) can't get to do your bidding.
Paras' customers can't use GWO because it isn't nearly non-technical enough.
All the kings horses and all the kings men apparently can't deliver a UI as good as a small team.
People don't take copiers seriously, and it is a hard impression to shake once you've gotten it attached to you, fairly or otherwise.
Trust me, it would be cheap at ten times the price if it worked for dentist offices, real estate agents, car mechanics, and the other constituents of the Fortune 5 Million.
One conversion at the margin is potentially worth hundreds or thousands of dollars, and even a single lead would pay for a month of most software-as-a-service products.
I somehow unwittingly sleepwalked into being that most loathsome of creatures, a technology evangelist.
posts.key_insight: The biggest opportunity in software tooling is making powerful techniques accessible to the millions of small businesses that lack technical teams, not building more powerful features for the technically sophisticated.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bingo Card Creator's 2009 performance, reporting a 110% increase in profits to $18,525 on $31,156 in sales. The defining event was launching a web version of his desktop application, which doubled conversion rates and reduced support costs. He details successes including price increases, building the A/Bingo testing framework, email marketing via Mailchimp, and a network of holiday bingo mini-sites. He also candidly recounts failures including a $3,500 CSS bug, wasted development on unused desktop features, and ongoing AdWords frustrations. The post closes with his plan to quit his day job in 2010 and go full-time on the business.
key_points: Launching the web version of Bingo Card Creator doubled conversion rates (from 1.17% desktop to 2.33% online) and was the single biggest driver of a 110% profit increase
Email marketing through Mailchimp proved extraordinarily cost-effective at 2 cents per re-engagement versus 24 cents per new trial through AdWords
Building features without talking to users first resulted in three desktop features that only twenty people ever touched — a textbook Lean Startup cautionary tale
Systematizing mini-site creation with freelancers and automation produced greater than 10x ROI on holiday bingo properties
After three years of gradually building toward it, McKenzie has a specific date circled to quit his day job and go full-time on his software business
money_quotes: I'm currently making more than enough money to live off of on Bingo Card Creator (I live rather simply in a rural prefecture in Japan — think Kansas with rather less white people)

It costs me about 24 cents to get a trial download or trial signup through AdWords at the margin, but only 2 cents to remind someone that their account still exists and that it would be perfect for the Halloween Thanksgiving Christmas festivities around the corner.

I'm too cool to actually ask users about their feelings beforehand, of course. Fans of the Lean Startup can already predict how this story ends up, right? To date, twenty users have touched those three features.
Let's see: add marginal feature to application, make twenty users happy, or for just a few hours more develop and market a new application which doubles my revenue streams. Decisions decisions!

In 2007, I had vague dreams of someday going full time on this. In 2008, I had a half-formed wish that maybe I would go full-time on this in 2008. In 2009… I have a date circled in red on my calendar, forms from the tax office and immigration authority ready for submission as soon as the new year starts.
The great thing about it is the whole thing fits in a laptop bag, so aside from some irksome tax and visa issues I have a lot of flexibility in where I live.
Did I mention I lost $3.5k to a CSS bug?
posts.key_insight: A small software business can reach life-changing profitability through relentless conversion optimization, strategic use of web apps over desktop, and cheap re-engagement channels like email — but only if you talk to your users before building features.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experience discovering Twilio's phone call API as a solution to his problem of having family in Chicago call him in Japan. His previous forwarding service was unreliable, and he had no way to handle calls arriving at inappropriate hours. Using Twilio, he built a smart call routing system that checks the time in Japan and either forwards calls, routes to his cell, or warns callers it's the middle of the night. The entire solution required minimal code — five lines of XML for v1.0 and about 30 lines of Sinatra for the full version. He closes by noting Twilio is cheaper and more capable than his previous solution, and hints at wanting to use it for a future application.
key_points: Twilio's API made it trivially easy to build a smart international call forwarding system that would have been impossible with traditional telecom services
The solution required only 5 lines of hard-coded XML for v1.0 and ~30 lines of Sinatra code for the full time-aware routing version
Programmable telephony enables entirely new behaviors like time-of-day routing with emergency override that no off-the-shelf service offered
McKenzie's instinct is already to look for ways to productize Twilio in his next application, showing his constant product-opportunity radar
money_quotes: Aside from the dropped calls, poor call quality, times when they call and I am connected to an irate Chinese man wondering what happened to his wife, and service interruptions, they pretty much do what they say on the tin.

If I am likely to be asleep, then the website says 'Tell them it is 3:47 AM Japan time and ask them to call back tomorrow or, if it is an emergency, hit 1 to have Patrick woken up by Ride of the Valkyries.'
This took really disgustingly little code to accomplish.
My v1.0 of the 'app', which just redirected between two phone numbers with a message, was a hard-coded XML file that I whipped up in vi. It was five lines long.
This would have been a bit easier if I had just deployed another Rails app to the same server as BCC but that would be gratuitous overkill.
Anyhow, Twilio appears to be cheaper than my existing solution for my usage levels, have features more in line with my needs, and hopefully will not disrupt anyone's marriage.
posts.key_insight: When powerful APIs make previously complex infrastructure trivially programmable, individual developers can build custom solutions in minutes that no existing product on the market actually provides.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that engineers should redirect their programming skills from building marginal features toward building systems that improve marketing outcomes. He draws from his own experience where a client/server feature attracted only 22 users while simpler marketing-focused engineering like A/B testing and automated content generation drove significant revenue. The post catalogs specific engineering-to-marketing conversions: A/B testing frameworks, scalable content generation, lifecycle email automation, automated ad creative generation, and process friction removal. He credits Thomas Ptacek with crystallizing this insight and uses examples from his own Bingo Card Creator business and Facebook's retention engineering to illustrate the approach.
key_points: Engineers waste time building features nobody asked for instead of using engineering to solve marketing problems like SEO, A/B testing, and lifecycle emails
Benefits sell software, not features — customers buy PowerPoint to close deals and get promoted, not to read PPT files
Building reusable tools and processes is the key to decoupling productivity from hours worked, especially for small businesses
Lifecycle email automation is stupidly cost-effective: a penny to email an existing user versus a quarter to recruit a new one via AdWords
Process friction — boring manual steps that feel like an insult to your intelligence — causes important tasks to not get done, and computers are good at eliminating it
money_quotes: We're sitting on top of more software and programmer brain juice than Saudi Arabia has oil, and when we deploy it to maximize our sales… we build marginal features that nobody has asked for and that nobody will see.
Benefits, not features, sell software.
Customers of Microsoft Powerpoint buy it because it will let them close the deal, please their bosses, and get promoted.
Your mission as an engineer is to stop thinking the job is building features and start thinking that the job is building systems to answer interesting questions.
This notion of tools and processes to use engineering as a force multiplier for everything else you do is the key to decoupling productivity from hours worked.
It costs me a penny to send an existing trial user an email but about a quarter to recruit a new trial user via AdWords.
Since 97.6% or so of trial users aren't buying, scraping back a mere fraction of the waste generates great returns for me.
Processes which include steps that are boring, annoying, or tedious tend to fail to get performed.
posts.key_insight: Engineers should stop building features and start building automated systems that multiply marketing effectiveness — A/B testing, lifecycle emails, content generation, and friction removal yield far more revenue per hour than new product features.

posts.summary: McKenzie visualizes his SVN commit history to understand his own work patterns, creating graphs of commits per month and lines of code over time. He discovers he works in spurts driven by inspiration, prefers shipping v1.0 quickly and iterating, and sometimes loses momentum when his day job demands energy. He also graphs cumulative lines of code, showing steady growth over years. The exercise reveals that code output alone doesn't capture the full picture of running a software business.
key_points: Visualizing commit history reveals personal work patterns: McKenzie works in inspiration-driven spurts rather than consistently
His preferred approach is shipping a minimal v1.0 and iterating rapidly rather than building something ambitious upfront
Code output is an incomplete measure of business progress — significant work happens outside of writing code
Simple tools (Ruby scripts, Excel, SVN logs) can provide useful self-knowledge about work habits
money_quotes: I tend to work in spurts when I have inspiration.
I prefer to ship v1.0 of something and iterate like mad rather than spending more time shipping something more ambitious to start with.
I sometimes get slammed by the day job and am left with no energy for the business.
Code is not everything in the software business.
Loading the entire Rails framework 1,334 times will not make your CPU happy.
I guess slow and steady wins the race.
posts.key_insight: Visualizing your own work patterns reveals that running a software business involves far more than writing code, and that shipping quickly and iterating beats perfectionism.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie wrote a parody rap (to the tune of Eminem's 'The Real Slim Shady') celebrating solo software founders who build and market products without cofounders or VC funding. The song pushes back against Silicon Valley orthodoxy that startups require teams, funding, and exits to succeed. He argues that solo developers can handle both coding and marketing, that niche products with paying customers are legitimate businesses, and that profit can beat exits. The post also casually reveals he has quit his day job to go full-time on his software business. It's a humorous manifesto for the bootstrapped, one-person software company.
key_points: Solo founders can successfully handle both engineering and marketing — the 'engineers can't market' narrative is false
Niche software products with paying customers and high margins are legitimate businesses, even if they aren't glamorous
The VC-funded, team-based startup model is not the only path — profitability can beat chasing exits
McKenzie quietly reveals he has quit his day job to work on his software business full-time
The enormous addressable market of non-technical users is underserved by developers who only build for other developers
money_quotes: Sometimes a profit beats an exit.
Try selling to women -- you'll be swimmin' in it.
My customers can't even spell PHP. So what? Their money's green as your ping stats.
And if we can grok code, AdWords, and email, then there's no reason we can't do software retail.
Every single one of y'all could try it.
It's called 'Customers' -- what a concept.
posts.key_insight: Solo founders who combine technical skills with marketing ability can build profitable software businesses without cofounders, funding, or Silicon Valley's permission.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes building a personal phone secretary using Twilio, Sinatra, and Ruby to manage calls from US contacts while living in Japan. The app checks the local time and either forwards calls or politely tells callers it's the middle of the night. He provides a step-by-step deployment guide for running Sinatra on Ubuntu with Apache reverse proxying. He concludes by marveling at how APIs like Twilio let individual developers build telephony systems that once required enterprise budgets.
key_points: A simple Twilio + Sinatra app can solve the real-world problem of managing international phone calls across time zones
The code took 10 minutes but deployment took 3 hours, illustrating the gap between writing code and getting it running in production
Step-by-step guide covers daemonizing Sinatra, setting up reverse proxying with Apache/Nginx, and configuring Ubuntu init scripts
Having a phone number on a business website signals legitimacy, even if calls are handled by an automated system
Enterprise-grade telephony features can now be built by a solo developer for under $5/month in operating costs
money_quotes: The code for this took 10 minutes. Reasoning my way through a deployment took, hmm, 3 hours or so. I am a programmer not a sysadmin, what can I say.
Twilio development makes me feel like a kid in a candy store — you can affect the real world through an API, how cool is that?
I don't give folks my phone number because a) I live in Japan and b) they don't pay me enough to do telephone support.
Quoting a telephone number on your website instantly says 'There is a real business behind this!'
That sort of thing costs megacorporations bazillions and can be whipped up these days by a single programmer on Saturday morning for under $5 a month in operating costs.
posts.key_insight: APIs like Twilio democratize capabilities that once required enterprise-scale investment, letting solo developers build real-world telephony systems in minutes for almost nothing.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes how he built a cheap, effective user survey system for Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo forms, A/B testing, and clever incentivization. Despite running his business for 3.5 years, he realizes he still doesn't have solid data on who his actual users are beyond his initial customer hypothesis of elementary schoolteachers. He details the specific questions asked, the implementation using Rails and his A/B testing framework, and how he incentivized survey completion by offering users extra free cards at zero marginal cost. He also argues controversially that users don't actually care about privacy despite claiming otherwise, and that businesses should prioritize what users demonstrably want over stated privacy preferences.
key_points: After 3.5 years running a business, McKenzie still relied more on guesses than data about who his actual customers were, discovering belatedly that baby shower bingo was hugely popular
Offering users something that costs you nothing but has perceived value to them (extra free cards) is a powerful incentive for surveys, links, and other marketing activities

The entire intelligent, incentivized survey system with two A/B tests took only ~20 lines of code and ~20 lines of HTML, compared to enterprise environments where comparable work requires planning meetings and dedicated engineers
Users consistently choose convenience over privacy despite claiming otherwise, and businesses that prioritize privacy over what users demonstrably want create problems for themselves
Survey design should minimize length, integrate naturally into the user experience, and let users dismiss it easily rather than using pop-ups or buried links
money_quotes: Programmers live in a world where data is available in infinite abundance, and hence we often assume that data is valueless.
Yes, I am talking to you, Mr. Three Dollars Is Too Much For A Game On My Six Hundred Dollar Phone.
I tend to default to being generous to my users unless I have a darn good reason otherwise.
There's a place for that in the world, don't get me wrong, but I'm already collecting data.
Given the choice between privacy and convenience, they'll choose convenience every single time, and if you prioritize their privacy over their convenience it is your problem.

My theory for why Google didn't anticipate this failure mode is that Google assumes you care about your customers about as much as Google cares about its customers, and at Google forwarding customer complaints to Siberia would double the likelihood they were actually read.
That was my customer hypothesis, to use a buzzword much remarked on lately, and 3.5 years later I'm still not really sure if it is accurate.
posts.key_insight: You can build surprisingly effective user research systems by combining free tools, smart incentives that cost you nothing but have real perceived value to users, and lightweight integration into existing workflows.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that startups need a deliberate SEO strategy that goes beyond tactical page-level optimizations to core business decisions about content creation and marketing. He explains why startup SEO differs from large company SEO due to limited budgets, low domain trust, and cultural aversion, but argues startups have advantages in technical skills and access to link-rich communities. The core strategy is to become the best resource on the Internet for as many topics as possible that matter to paying customers, using scalable content generation approaches. He advocates for evergreen content, leveraging proprietary data, open source software as linkbait, and focusing on long-tail search terms where showing up is often enough to win.
key_points: Focus on long-tail search terms you can actually win rather than competing for head keywords against giants — conversion rates on specific queries are dramatically higher than on generic head terms

SEO is a self-reinforcing feedback loop where winners keep winning, making early content investment critical — startups that wait until launch to publish content are ceding the long tail to competitors

Scalable content creation should leverage unfair advantages: proprietary data visualization, evergreen resources that stay relevant for years, and open source software that attracts authoritative backlinks
The cultural aversion to SEO among startups is misguided — algorithms have no moral status, and good SEO is fundamentally about creating useful content and not breaking your site for Google
Treat SEO like product development: ship early, collect data, iterate on what works, and use agile methods to test ideas cheaply before investing in expensive resources
money_quotes: Algorithms have no moral status.
I could get your startup ranked for [fried squirrels with wasabi] by the end of the day, but unless you're selling a book of very eclectic recipes that probably won't do you much good.

If that you didn't expect people explicitly looking for free things to convert at five times the rate of undifferentiated searchers, welcome to the Internet. Nothing makes sense except the data you collect.
For a non-competitive tail term like [geography bingo], I'm #1 by virtue of showing up.
The 'media launch' where Steve Jobs comes down and presents the iCommandments works very well if you have a built-in base of millions of radical fans and a PR budget which could buy Chile.
This is the perverse incentive by which advertisers pay to make the Internet a mass of garbage.
My generic SEO strategy for a startup is a) be the best on the Internet for b) as many topics as you possibly can be that c) matter to your paying customers.
Does Github pay your salary? I love them. They're wonderful people. They contribute a lot to OSS. They are also quite good at marketing their business and do not require your help to do it.
posts.key_insight: Startups should treat SEO as a strategic content investment — becoming the definitive resource on long-tail topics their customers care about — rather than a tactical optimization exercise or something to be culturally avoided.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie answers detailed followup questions about the SEO strategies behind his Bingo Card Creator business. He explains his freelancer workflow for content creation, his preference for forward-facing analysis over backward-facing metrics, and how he reuses product data across multiple marketing channels. Most notably, he describes a conversion optimization technique where new trial users get pre-initialized accounts based on the landing page that brought them in, yielding a 20% improvement in funnel success.
key_points: Content creation is outsourced to a small team of freelancers (primarily one reliable person), with a streamlined CMS workflow that automates page generation from submitted word lists
Forward-facing analysis (finding new opportunities) is more valuable than backward-facing analysis (measuring past results), and domain-specific analytics beat general tools like Google Analytics
Pre-initializing trial accounts with content matching the user's entry point increases conversion by roughly 20%, unifying marketing and product experience
A single piece of content (a bingo card activity) gets reused across content pages, PPC landing pages, desktop software, web app, and cross-site widgets — data begets data
General-purpose analytics tools like Google Analytics provide precision without actionability; custom domain-specific queries yield better business decisions
money_quotes: I do very little backwards facing analysis ('Did that work?') and concentrate mostly on forward facing analysis ('What opportunities can I exploit now?').

You are roughly 20% more likely to successfully download a set of customized bingo cards if I give you the 'personalized' treatment than you are if I drop you at a blank dashboard and expect you to fight your way through.
I don't think enough software companies unify the marketing and product sides, incidentally. We tend to treat everybody coming in to the top of the funnel as absolutely the same.
We've got data that says they are different — why not use the data to enhance their experience and, not incidentally, improve their propensity to buy the product?
If you respond to an ad for Halloween Bingo Cards, then by jove I'm going to everything short of dropping a pumpkin on your desktop.
Data begets data.
posts.key_insight: The highest-leverage marketing optimization isn't more content or better analytics — it's connecting the marketing promise to the product experience so users land in a personalized context that matches why they showed up.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie writes four open letters to participants in the book industry's digital transition, centered on the Kindle pricing dispute between publishers and Amazon. As a voracious reader spending thousands annually on books, he makes clear that publishers have zero brand loyalty from consumers and that delaying Kindle releases to protect hardcover sales will simply drive readers to competing authors. He warns authors that siding with publishers over readers' preferences is a losing strategy. The post concludes with dismissive one-liners to bookstores and effusive support for Amazon, illustrating the power shift from publishers to platforms.
key_points: Publishers have no brand relationship with readers—McKenzie had never thought about which publisher published his favorite authors until the Amazon dispute forced him to check
The Kindle increased his book purchasing frequency roughly fourfold, making him an even more valuable customer through the Amazon channel
Windowing Kindle releases to protect hardcover sales fundamentally misunderstands reader behavior—price-insensitive customers will simply read competing authors rather than switch formats
Authors who side with publishers over reader convenience risk losing readers who are inherently promiscuous due to reading faster than any author can write
The real competitive axis is convenience and personalization (Amazon knows his preferences), not format or pricing
money_quotes: The fact that I didn't know this off the top of my head, and that this is the first time I've thought about individual publishing companies in my entire life, should be a preview of coming attractions for you as regards to which company I am backing in this fight.
Words cannot express how mistaken you are.
I will read books on my Kindle. Whether they are your books or the books of your competitors matters to me not one whit.
Let me be perfectly clear: I have no price sensitivity with regards to books. I read the books I want to read when I want to read them.
You think my likely behavior is to go to the bookstore where no one knows my name and pay extra so that I can have the hardcover on release day.
Any regret I feel over losing you will be quickly assuaged by epic heroism, vile betrayal, true love, and other themes of investing advice books.
Dear Book Stores: Good luck with that coffee thing.
I may well be the only heterosexual male in the entire world who spent more on urban fantasy alone than he does on video games, movies, and newspapers combined.
posts.key_insight: Publishers who delay digital releases to protect legacy formats misunderstand that their brand is invisible to consumers—readers are loyal to convenience and content, not publishing houses.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares results from a user survey he ran on Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo, revealing demographic data, feature preferences, and complaints. He A/B tested survey incentivization and found that offering free bingo cards more than doubled response rates versus altruistic appeals. The survey confirmed his customer base is mostly female teachers over 40 and revealed that customers value features he considered too obvious to mention. The number one complaint was that the software isn't free, which he uses as a springboard to explain why charging money actually makes the product better for everyone, including non-paying users.
key_points: Incentivized surveys (offering free cards) crushed non-incentivized ones by more than 2X in response rate, without reducing feedback quality
Displaying the survey prominently in the trial version had zero negative impact on sales conversions

Customers overwhelmingly valued features McKenzie considered too obvious to promote — card uniqueness and customizable word lists — suggesting copy should emphasize what users love, not what creators think is novel
The 80/20 rule applied to support: 80% of customers were comfortable with computers, but 80% of support emails came from the remaining 20%
The top complaint — that the software should be free — actually validates his business model, since charging funds the UX optimization that makes the product worth using
money_quotes: Detailed, actionable advice can be yours if you just ask for it.
I have never had a more lopsided A/B test.
If you want responses, give away free stuff.
The fact that Bingo Card Creator is so easy to use is directly related to the fact that I charge money for it.
The sites that I advertise on do not optimize for their user experience because if their website is better than my textual ad, they don't get paid.
Charging also subsidizes the experience of the 97.3% of trial users who don't actually pay me money.
I don't feel any rancor at folks who believe that everything should be free on the Internet. I just will not accommodate your preferences.
posts.key_insight: User surveys are cheap, don't hurt conversions, and reveal both what customers actually value and how to talk about your product in their language — but only if you incentivize participation.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes the custom dashboard he built for his Bingo Card Creator business, explaining how it consolidates customer support, sales tracking, A/B test results, and system health monitoring into a single view. The dashboard's design philosophy centers on minimizing repetitive work and surfacing actionable information rather than vanity metrics. He details specific features like the Omnibox for customer lookup, ghost login for debugging customer issues, and a daemon that automatically detects known bugs in print jobs. He argues that aggregate conversion rate is misleading because it conflates traffic mix changes with product changes, and that metrics only matter if they drive decisions.
key_points: A dashboard should minimize time on repetitive tasks, show business health at a glance, and provide easy drill-down into important details
The Omnibox universal search eliminates 80% of the manual work in finding customer records, illustrating the principle of 'do less work, get more done'
Aggregate conversion rate is misleading because it is dominated by traffic mix shifts rather than product changes — per-channel conversion rates are what matter
Critical payment processing exceptions override the entire dashboard with a blaze orange stacktrace until acknowledged, ensuring they cannot be ignored
A background daemon continuously checks recent print jobs against known bug signatures, turning bug detection from manual inspection into automated monitoring
money_quotes: If your metrics aren't giving you actionable insights to make decisions that matter to your business, well, you might as well go play WoW for all the good you're doing.
Ten keystrokes saved doesn't sound like a lot until you've done it 200 times.
Do less work, get more done.
Conversion rate for a channel or a creative or a landing page is a very important number. Conversion rate for all of your visitors, on the other hand, is very, very sensitive to traffic mix.
An exception happening in the callback for successful payments is a bug. A very critical bug, because it often means that a customer loses access to the software they paid for.
Regular exceptions get written to my log files. That is, candidly speaking, where data goes to die.
At all of ~200 lines of code, though, you can knock one for yourself out in an afternoon.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed business dashboard should ruthlessly prioritize actionable information and workflow shortcuts over comprehensive metrics, because time spent admiring numbers without acting on them is time wasted.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains how to use CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking on pages that require authentication, which normally blocks CrazyEgg's screenshot bot. The solution involves detecting CrazyEgg's user-agent string and bypassing the login procedure for their bot. He created a dedicated trial account for CrazyEgg's bot to authenticate as, enabling heatmap data collection on logged-in application pages. He acknowledges this approach has security implications but argues it's proportionate to his threat model.
key_points: CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking doesn't work behind login screens by default because the screenshot bot can't authenticate
You can detect CrazyEgg's bot via its user-agent string and bypass authentication for it
For security-sensitive applications, CrazyEgg provides IP addresses to whitelist instead of user-agent detection
The pragmatic solution is to create a dedicated trial account and auto-authenticate the bot as that user
money_quotes: They're seriously my favorite $19 to pay every month, even when I don't actually use them, because some day I know I'll get the itch again and then bam actionable insights into what my customers are doing.
I sell bingo cards to 60 year old women.
In Rails, doing something on the basis of the user agent is easy but, and I know this might come as a surprise, not covered in the documentation.
posts.key_insight: You can extend analytics tools like CrazyEgg behind login screens by detecting their bot's user-agent and auto-authenticating it as a dedicated account, calibrating security tradeoffs to your actual threat model.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the official 1.0.0 release of A/Bingo, his open-source Rails A/B testing framework. The release includes several community-requested features: a default dashboard, the ability to stop experiments, named conversions, Redis support, and a proper test suite. He establishes a versioning policy for the growing user base and closes with his characteristic evangelism for A/B testing as an essential business practice.
key_points: A/Bingo has grown beyond personal use to substantial community adoption, with McKenzie's own users representing less than 1% of total beneficiaries
The release prioritizes community-requested features like a default dashboard and experiment stopping, reflecting the shift from personal tool to public infrastructure
McKenzie added unit tests not because he personally values them but because the Rails community expects them, showing pragmatic responsiveness to ecosystem norms
Named conversions reduce the code needed to create most new tests from two lines to one, reflecting a DRY-focused design philosophy
The versioning policy signals maturity — acknowledging that breaking changes now affect real deployments beyond his own
money_quotes: The 5,000 or so people seeing A/B tests on my site on Valentine's Day are almost certainly less than 1% of the beneficiaries of the software now.
I want to make your use of the software as pleasant as possible to further evangelize the cause of A/B testing.
Unit tests are not exactly my cup of tea ("Argh, it works in production, what else do you want from me?!"), but Rails developers look askance at software that does not include them.
By default, it probably looks terrible. If you want to send me a patch to make it pretty, be my guest.
A/B testing is an easy, reproducible process that you can use to improve your marketing, website copy, product, user experience, etc.
It won't take you five minutes until you're getting actionable data which you can use to make money.
posts.key_insight: Open-source developer tools succeed when they shift from solving the author's problems to systematically addressing community friction points like documentation, dashboards, and testing expectations.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents a production outage where his Delayed::Job workers died during an Ubuntu server upgrade, leaving 60 users unable to print bingo cards for over 12 hours. He walks through his incident response: personally apologizing to affected paying customers, building a targeted in-app messaging system to notify trial users without spamming everyone, and conducting a Toyota Five Whys root cause analysis. The investigation reveals cascading failures in his processes — skipping checklist steps due to fatigue, incomplete monitoring, commented-out process management, and poor documentation. He credits his Japanese engineering day job for teaching him the discipline of written deploy procedures, checklists, and post-incident analysis.
key_points: Targeted incident communication is superior to broadcasting outage notices to all users — only the 60 affected users needed to know
Toyota's Five Whys methodology reveals that outages rarely have a single cause but rather a chain of process failures compounding each other
Monitoring that doesn't test every critical subsystem creates false confidence — his checks tested nginx and the database but not background job workers
The discipline of written checklists, deploy procedures, and documentation is a net time-savings over emergency incident response
Fatigue and shortcuts (skipping checklist steps, commenting out configs with vague notes) create technical debt that eventually causes outages
money_quotes: A quick check of the server showed that this was indeed the case. Six thousand. Oof.
It allows me to apologize directly to affected users, makes minimal demands on their attention while still almost certainly reaching them, and does not cause any issue for the other 25,000 users.
God sees every sparrow, but god only knows about the processes you tell it to manage, and my god_config.rb file has the Delayed::Job bits commented out with the notation 'This is buggy.'
I don't remember why it was buggy and my notes in SVN are similarly unhelpful.

All of the writing, test suites, automated test processes, and monitoring takes some time to set up and much of it generates additional overhead on all your tasks. However, in the last three years, I've come to recognize that it is a net time-savings over writing apology letters and doing emergency incident response.
Quit day job and, regardless of how tired you are, follow the freaking checklist.
Unbeknownst to me, my use of memcached fails totally silently: if Rails can't find the data in the cache it just regenerates it.
posts.key_insight: Rigorous operational discipline — checklists, monitoring, documentation, and structured post-incident analysis — is not bureaucratic overhead but a net time-savings over the emergency response and customer apologies that inevitably follow its absence.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that Silicon Valley's diversity problem is really a symptom of deeper structural pathologies: the startup model itself is a terrible value proposition for most intelligent people regardless of gender. Rather than focusing on demographic headcounts, the Valley should fix its broken fundamentals — overwork culture, VC dependency, geographic concentration, and building products nobody pays for. These fixes would naturally make startups more attractive to a broader population, including women, while also producing better businesses.
key_points: Demographic diversity is a lazy proxy for diversity of thought — measuring checkbox demographics instead of actual cognitive diversity is like counting hits instead of conversions

The startup value proposition is objectively terrible: 100-hour weeks, poverty wages, geographic lock-in to an expensive area, and 90%+ failure rate — the real question is why anyone accepts this deal
The Valley creates a persistent undersupply of products for women and other underserved markets, which represents enormous untapped business opportunity
The diversity discourse wants more women but specifically more women who conform to existing Valley culture — equity-compensated, coding until 2 AM, building flip-to-Google companies
Practical fixes include bootstrapping over VC funding, building outside the Valley, making products people pay for, and normalizing humane working hours
money_quotes: True diversity, of course, is the 5-member iStockPhoto of attractive twenty-somethings sitting on the college quad who check different boxes on the demographic inventory and think alike in every way that matters.

We sound like we're counting hits because grepping the Apache log is easy and implementing conversion funnel tracking is hard, despite the fact that we know hits are meaningless and we're really interested in conversions.
My beef with the discourse of 'diversity' in a nutshell: it screams 'give us more women' and whispers 'give us more women like us'.
The question isn't what are we doing that's keeping ladies out of the Valley, gentlemen. The question should be why in God's name are we still here.
Unsurprisingly, the conversion rate for the above sales pitch has lagged expectations.
Persistent undersupply is one of those words that should be music to your ears if you are a capitalist, because market failures are opportunities to make lots and lots of money.
I checked my Ruby standard library: there is no TriedToHaveALifeOutsideOfWorkException.

After you're profitable, if you really want to, you can go to the Valley. My guess is they'll fall over themselves trying to give you money because they have no freaking clue how to create success in a reproducible fashion.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley's gender imbalance is a symptom of a fundamentally broken startup model that rational people of any gender should reject — fix the model and the demographics will follow.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experience testing the trendy 'madlibs-style' signup form that swept the internet after a viral case study. Rather than blindly adopting the design fad, he ran his own A/B test on Bingo Card Creator's registration form. The madlibs style resulted in a 22% decrease in conversion rates compared to the standard form, significant at the 95% confidence level. He emphasizes that other people's successful experiments should inspire you to run your own tests, not to copy their results wholesale. The original viral test changed seven variables simultaneously, making it impossible to attribute the improvement to the madlibs format specifically.
key_points: Trust your own A/B test data, not someone else's — you only make money when it improves your conversion rate, not theirs
The original viral madlibs test changed roughly seven things simultaneously, making it impossible to isolate which change actually drove the improvement
McKenzie's isolated test of just the madlibs style showed a 22% decrease in conversions, reaching statistical significance in only 34 hours
Having an A/B testing framework that makes tests trivial to run (a one-line proposition) removes the excuse for not testing before adopting trends
Design fads spread virally but their results don't transfer across contexts — what works for lead generation forms may hurt simple registration forms
money_quotes: There is a key point about A/B testing: trust your data, not somebody else's data.
You only make money when it improves your conversion rate, not their conversion rate.
You can feel free to use other folk's successful experiments for inspiration but for heaven's sake use them to inspire you to run tests, rather than inspire you to fire blindly.
When In Doubt, Test. (When Not In Doubt, Test Twice.)

I love the Internet because where else can you get 736 people to help you improve your website while you sleep, work at the day job on Saturday, have an evening out with friends, and then sleep some more?
Before adopting this or any other fad (or good suggestion, for that matter): do your own A/B tests.
posts.key_insight: Other people's A/B test results should inspire you to run your own tests, not to blindly copy their implementations — context matters more than creativity.

posts.summary: McKenzie discloses a significant one-character bug in his open-source A/B testing framework A/Bingo that affected z-score calculations. The bug could potentially change whether test results were reported as statistically significant. He fixed it remotely from a Japanese cafe while stuck in Nagoya and pushed the fix to the repository. After re-running confidence tests on all his historical data, no experimental results actually changed. He commits to writing unit tests for the statistics code to prevent similar issues.
key_points: A one-character bug in A/Bingo's z-score calculation could affect statistical significance reporting
The bug fortuitously overstated significance of already-significant tests and understated insignificant ones, limiting real-world damage
No historical experimental results changed after recalculation with the corrected code
The incident highlights the importance of unit testing statistical code, which McKenzie commits to adding
money_quotes: The bug is all of one character long.
I have fixed the bug (via the Slicehost console, on a Japanese cafe Internet PC, because I am stuck in Nagoya today again).

While the numerical calculation of the z-score was borked, it was borked in a subtle enough fashion that most statistically significant tests will retain their statistical significance under the borked calculation.

My quick eyeball suggests that it causes BCC to overstate the significance of tests which are very significant and understate the significance of tests which are insignificant, which is a very fortuitous set of properties for a random bug to have in an A/B testing framework.
I have re-run statistical confidence tests for everything I've ever done for BCC that I still have data for, and no experimental results changed as a result of the error.
posts.key_insight: A one-character bug in statistical code can potentially invalidate A/B test results, underscoring why statistical calculations need unit tests even when the rest of your framework works correctly.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he is quitting his day job effective March 31st to go full-time on his software businesses. He describes the mix of exhilaration and unease that comes with the transition. He updates readers on his current activities: running six simultaneous A/B tests on Bingo Card Creator, working with designers on mini-sites and button redesigns, and mentally committing to a new Twilio-based application. The post captures a pivotal moment in his transition from side-project entrepreneur to full-time independent software developer.
key_points: McKenzie is leaving his salaried position to go full-time on his software businesses, marking a major career transition
He is running six simultaneous A/B tests covering landing pages, microcopy, button designs, and signup flows — embodying his incremental optimization philosophy
Guest signup appears to have no effect on sales but generates support costs, illustrating how A/B testing reveals features worth removing
He hints at a new Twilio-based application, signaling expansion beyond Bingo Card Creator
He is systematically testing his way to growth through small 3-5% lifts rather than betting on big changes
money_quotes: I'm quitting my day job as of March 31st.

That weird not-scared-not-settled feeling one gets when one has run out the door in a hurry and is absolutely positive they have left something behind despite having verified the presence of cellphone, wallet, and day job ID card.
Oh, to no longer need the day job ID card.
I have six A/B tests currently ongoing, which I think is my personal record for simultaneous A/B tests.
Presence/absence of guest signup seems to have no effect whatsoever on sales. Good — I'm going to probably disable it, as they cost me support issues through the wazoo.
As always, I'm testing my way to victory, one 3~5% lift at a time.
posts.key_insight: The transition from side-project to full-time entrepreneurship is built on a foundation of relentless incremental optimization — testing your way to victory one small lift at a time.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares practical strategies for running a software business alongside a demanding full-time job as a Japanese salaryman, drawing on four years of experience with Bingo Card Creator. He introduces the framework of 'time assets' versus 'time debt' as the key mental model for part-time entrepreneurs, arguing that building systems, processes, and automation is far more valuable than squeezing more hours out of each day. The post covers scope management, freelancer relationships, avoiding deadlines, documentation, and choosing problems suited to part-time constraints. McKenzie emphasizes that code is only about 10% of a software business, with marketing and operations consuming the rest.
key_points: Time can be 'stored' through building assets like code, documentation, and systems that save future time, rather than trying to squeeze more out of each day
Code represents roughly 10% of a software business — marketing, SEO, copywriting, and customer support dominate the effort and results
Every line of code is time debt that must be debugged, supported, documented, and potentially rewritten, so cutting scope aggressively is essential
Publicly committing to deadlines or feature schedules creates avoidable time debt and should be replaced with noncommittal acknowledgments
Build repeatable processes instead of chasing one-off events — a system for contacting small blogs beats wooing one large blog
money_quotes: The key insight about time is that software lets us take the old saying about how 'Everyone gets the same 24 hours per day' and break it open like a pinata.
Every line of code you write is time debt: it is another line that has to be debugged, another line that has to be supported, another line that may require a rewrite later.
People don't buy software because of what it does, they buy it for the positive change it will make in the life.
I snort in the general direction of anyone saying a nine-to-five job is impossible to juggle with a business because 'businesses require 100% concentration'.
Bingo Card Creator has been memorably described as 'Hello World attached to a random number generator.' If anything, that probably overstates its complexity.
There is no magic 'completion' day on an engineer's schedule: 'complete' is 100% a marketing decision that the software as it exists is Good Enough.
I started my business with $60. Literally.
Always, always, always trust the data over me if the data says I'm wrong. That's the easy part. The hard part is trusting the data when it is overruling you.
posts.key_insight: Part-time entrepreneurs should focus on building 'time assets' — reusable systems, processes, and automation — rather than working more hours, because stored time compounds while spent time rots.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares how CrazyEgg's visual heatmap tool revealed a conversion problem on his AdWords landing page that no amount of quantitative analytics could have surfaced. Users were clicking on non-active sidebar text instead of the intended call-to-action button. The fix was trivial — making the bullet points into hyperlinks — but the insight required visual analysis. He emphasizes that CrazyEgg's value lies in making problems visually obvious rather than requiring deep data drilling.
key_points: Visual heatmap tools reveal UX problems that quantitative analytics tools like Google Analytics cannot surface
Users often click on elements that look clickable but aren't, wasting paid traffic
The fix for conversion problems is often trivially simple once you can actually see the problem
Every change should be A/B tested, even 'obvious' fixes — he adds a second line of code for the test
money_quotes: CrazyEgg is really one of my favorite secrets of being a metrics junkie, simply because it makes problems look so freaking obvious, when they could be buried if you relied on quantified analysis.
I paid good money to get folks to see it, and I want them clicking the photo or the big purple button, not clicking the non-active text in the sidebar!
Sure, now that I know the problem is actually happening, fixing it is a matter of adding one line in a Rails template.
One line to make the 'obvious' fix… and another line for the A/B test.
posts.key_insight: Visual analytics tools like heatmaps reveal conversion-killing UX problems that no amount of quantitative data drilling can surface, and the fixes are often trivially simple once you can see the problem.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces a video interview with Gabriel Weinberg (founder of DuckDuckGo) for an upcoming book on getting traction. He provides a summary of topics covered including Bingo Card Creator's SEO strategy, conversion optimization, A/B testing, and managing a business while employed. He also announces an upcoming interview with Andrew Warner at Mixergy and recommends watching Peldi from Balsamiq's interview.
key_points: McKenzie's SEO strategy for Bingo Card Creator includes mini-sites, widgets, and scalable content generation
He advocates for reading transcripts over watching videos, and transcribed his own hour-long interview to practice what he preaches
The multiplicative effect of funnel improvements is a key concept in his conversion optimization approach
His productivity framework for running a business while employed: outsource, automate, eliminate
The isolation of being a software entrepreneur in rural Japan, where even explaining your work is difficult, makes online communities essential
money_quotes: I always look for a summary of contents prior to committing myself to a video (since they're so much longer than reading a post), so here you go.
I was so convinced that I'd rather read videos than watch them 99% of the time that I took the liberty of transcribing it.
The clerk's response on hearing I was a software developer was 'Web applications? Wow, you're an iPhone developer!' *sigh*
It is nice to have people who speak your language, and I don't mean English.
I'm absolutely floored that I'm appearing on guest lists next to folks like the 37Signals crew or Eric Ries, who rank among some of the largest influences in how I run my business.
posts.key_insight: Building a public presence through interviews and community participation compounds over time, eventually placing you alongside the people who originally inspired you.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces an upcoming interview with Andrew Warner on Mixergy, scheduled for the next day. The interview will focus on his business biography, covering the story of Bingo Card Creator and his entrepreneurial journey. He acknowledges that regular readers may already know much of the material but suggests Andrew Warner's interviewing skills will surface new insights. He invites readers to suggest topics they'd like covered in the interview.
key_points: McKenzie is being interviewed on Mixergy, a well-known entrepreneur interview platform, signaling growing recognition in the startup community
The interview format is a live business biography with audience participation via chatroom
He actively solicits reader input on interview topics, demonstrating community engagement
money_quotes: Andrew has a way of wheedling secrets out of people so I'm sure you'll still enjoy it.
posts.key_insight: Getting interviewed on established platforms is a milestone that signals growing authority and provides marketing exposure for a small software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares a concise technical guide for setting up a Ruby on Rails development environment on Windows 7 64-bit. The post documents his solution after spending hours stuck in dependency issues, particularly around getting MySQL to work with Ruby on Windows. The key trick is copying a specific DLL file to Ruby's bin directory to resolve a common mysql_api.so loading error. It's a brief, practical troubleshooting post aimed at experienced Rails developers who found themselves on Windows.
key_points: Getting Rails running on Windows required navigating dependency issues that consumed hours of debugging
The critical fix was copying libmySQL.dll from InstantRails to Ruby's bin directory to resolve mysql_api.so errors
The post reflects the era when Rails development was heavily Mac-centric and Windows was an afterthought
money_quotes: Today I killed a few hours getting my Rails environment working on my brand new shiny 64 bit Windows Seven laptop.
I'm assuming you're a fairly experienced Rails developer and just ended in dependency purgatory like I did for the last few hours.
You'll get all manner of errors on that MySQL installation. That is OK.
You should now be able to successfully work with Rails as you have been previously, even from your Windows machine, and you will amaze your Mac-wielding friends.
posts.key_insight: A brief troubleshooting guide documenting the non-obvious DLL copy needed to resolve MySQL dependency issues when running Rails on Windows 7.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide for small companies to build reliable web services without dedicated ops teams. He argues that most downtime comes from predictable, preventable causes—especially operator error—rather than black swan events. Drawing from Toyota's manufacturing philosophy, he emphasizes that systems fail at their 'moving parts': dynamic components, databases, and human interventions. The post covers risk identification, failure mitigation through decoupling and automation, monitoring and notification systems, and root cause analysis using Five Whys. His core message is that reliability is achievable for small operators through checklists, automation, decoupling external dependencies, and disciplined post-failure analysis.
key_points: Operator error is the single greatest risk to small company systems—you are the least reliable component, and novel manual operations are the most dangerous moving parts
Decouple external API calls from HTTP request/response cycles so third-party downtime doesn't become your downtime
Automate recovery (e.g., process managers that restart failing services) and repetitive operations; use checklists for everything else, including bare-metal restore drills every quarter
Simple monitoring (Pingdom + custom status pages + phone alerts) provides sufficient coverage for small operations without enterprise budgets
Apply Five Whys root cause analysis after every failure—no failure has a single cause, and process failures enabled the surface-level problem
money_quotes: Software systems are also, overwhelmingly, killed by their moving parts.
