You are your own worst enemy, and you'll take steps to mitigate the threat you pose.
Serious engineers treat hard drives as a component that by grace of God has not died yet.
Don't be suckered into thinking that downtime is generally caused by unpredictable black swan events.
If you think checklists are a nuisance, that is fine: you can feel free to skip checklists for systems where catastrophic failure is no big deal.
Novel operations are moving parts and vastly more likely to fail than known-good operations that your system crunches millions of times per day.
I would rather tell the user a white lie than show them a red error message.
posts.key_insight: Reliability for small companies isn't about preventing every possible failure—it's about identifying your predictable moving parts (especially yourself), decoupling and automating what you can, and building the discipline to learn from every failure that gets through.

posts.summary: McKenzie recounts giving a presentation on data-driven software design to the Design Matters group at the Apple Store in Osaka. He adapted his originally technical presentation to suit a less geeky audience. He also met with MessaLiberty, a small Japanese company he found impressive, and did a video interview covering A/B testing, software design, and advice for working in Japan.
key_points: Adapting technical presentations for non-technical audiences requires retooling content to be less technical and more broadly interesting
MessaLiberty represents a hopeful model in Japan: a small team of young engineers working sane hours on meaningful products
A/B testing has diminishing returns and knowing when to redirect efforts elsewhere is as important as knowing when to test
money_quotes: I retooled the presentation into something less technical and more interesting on the same topic.
Incidentally, that Slideshare widget is great SEO now isn't it. I'm leaving their links attached out of sheer amusement.
All with a team of about seven young engineers working sane hours. Ah, there is hope for the future yet.

Topics include a brief overview of the above presentation, when you should start A/B testing versus when to redirect your efforts elsewhere, and my advice for getting a job in Japan (spoiler: learn Japanese).
posts.key_insight: Using data and statistics to drive software design decisions is valuable, but knowing when to apply A/B testing versus redirecting effort elsewhere is equally important.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes implementing a Dropbox-style two-sided referral incentive for Bingo Card Creator, where both the inviter and invitee receive bonus bingo cards. He explains why the double-sided incentive is psychologically superior to simple 'tell a friend' schemes, details the surprisingly complex implementation including Facebook sharing integration, and candidly shares a production bug where a database migration accidentally triggered 30,000 MailChimp API calls. The post balances optimism about the approach with honest uncertainty about whether it will work for his non-technical user base.
key_points: Two-sided referral incentives work because they make the invitation feel like a gift rather than spam, leveraging social reciprocity
The marginal cost of the referral reward (extra bingo cards) is essentially zero, making the customer acquisition cost trivial
Facebook sharing removes the technical barrier of knowing friends' email addresses, requiring only two clicks from users
A poorly considered ActiveRecord callback caused a mass database update to trigger 30,000 queued MailChimp API calls, demonstrating how side effects in callbacks create hidden coupling
The feature was deliberately designed with anti-abuse caps: 5 invites per user, 1 invitation per recipient, and 500 system-wide per day
money_quotes: The person who signs up for Dropbox through your referral link gets a better deal than they would have gotten from the homepage, and you also get a free bonus yourself. That is marvelous, marvelous psychology there.
Social relations being what they are, the person receiving the gift feels a wee bit obligated to accept it.
The cost of allowing users to print extra bingo cards is, of course, too low to measure.

One bragged to me that she found out how to make a link to the website on her desktop and then bring it to her sister on a floppy, and if they're getting over barriers to conversion that high, you know there must be something going on.
We've all got that one aunt who has not figured out netiquette for Farmville and sends 14 lost kittens a day: I do not want to be her enabler.
Facebook sharing: not just for blog posts.

I had a funny idea for a minimum viable tell-a-friend page: just ask for the friend's email address and then have it ping me when someone submits anything. I'd send the emails and credit both users manually.
posts.key_insight: Two-sided referral incentives transform spam-like 'tell a friend' schemes into genuine gift-giving by rewarding both parties, and the best implementations use rewards with near-zero marginal cost.

posts.summary: McKenzie recommends a Mixergy interview with Peldi Guilizzoni of Balsamiq, praising his insights on customer service, product development, early marketing, and competition. He uses the post to introduce readers to Mixergy, Andrew Warner's interview series with successful internet entrepreneurs. McKenzie highlights several favorite interviews including ones with Wufoo and Kissmetrics founders. He also mentions that his own Mixergy interview is forthcoming.
key_points: Peldi from Balsamiq is held up as a model for the micro-ISV community, particularly for his marketing advice
Mixergy interviews are recommended as a source of actionable business tips for small software entrepreneurs
McKenzie curates a short list of must-watch interviews: Wufoo, Balsamiq, and Kissmetrics/CrazyEgg founders
money_quotes: Everything he says about customer service, building remarkable products, early marketing, and competition just knocks it out of the park.
Most of them are inspiring, and many have killer, actionable tips that you can use in your businesses.
The interview and transcript will be up one of these days, after the editors have made me sound intelligible.
posts.key_insight: Curated interviews with successful software entrepreneurs are a high-value resource for micro-ISV founders seeking actionable marketing and product advice.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his interview on Mixergy with Andrew Warner is now live after being rescheduled due to technical difficulties. The interview covers his experience running Bingo Card Creator while working full-time as a Japanese salaryman, his early traction strategies, and hints at an upcoming new product. He notes the interview was recorded at 2 AM due to time zone differences between Japan and the US, which affected his coherence at times.
key_points: The Mixergy interview covers McKenzie's dual life as a full-time Japanese salaryman and solo software entrepreneur
Key topics include early traction strategies, process reliability, and maximizing limited time
McKenzie teases a new product but declines to reveal details, promising a May announcement
The practical challenges of being an international bootstrapper are illustrated by recording at 2 AM due to time zones
money_quotes: Given that it was about 2 in the morning when I recorded it due to time zone differences, sometimes my ability to speak in coherent sentences leaves a bit to be desired.
posts.key_insight: Building a software business while working full-time in Japan meant navigating not just limited hours but practical challenges like recording interviews at 2 AM due to time zone differences.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie unveils his second product, Appointment Reminder, a web application that automates phone, SMS, and email reminders for service businesses with appointment no-show problems. He identifies a massive underserved market of small service providers (hair salons, massage therapists, tutors) who can't afford the $300+/month enterprise healthcare-focused solutions. Using Twilio's API infrastructure and his B2C marketing skills, he plans to offer the service at $9-$79/month, deliberately pricing to enable the pitch that it pays for itself after preventing one no-show. Following Lean Startup methodology, he built only a demo as his MVP rather than a complete product, reasoning that making someone's phone ring from a web interface is the key selling moment. He plans to leverage SEO and AdWords while avoiding enterprise sales cycles.
key_points: The appointment reminder market is dominated by expensive enterprise healthcare solutions ($300+/month or $1,000+ installed), leaving small service businesses completely underserved
Twilio's API allows building a superior service at dramatically lower cost, with expected margins of 90-95% since most customers won't ride their plans to the limit
The MVP is just a working demo that makes your phone ring — everything after the demo is irrelevant to getting a credit card number, so it's out of scope for the minimum viable product
Pricing tiers serve as price discrimination across customer segments, and the enterprise tier at $669/month is a polite way to say 'not yet' to healthcare customers without turning them away

His competitive advantages mirror Bingo Card Creator: competitors are structurally incapable of serving the low end of the market, and he can use passive online sales techniques instead of enterprise sales forces
money_quotes: Two clients not at their appointments means two massage therapists not seeing anyone, and that costs them $2 a minute in direct economic losses.
I can demonstrate the sizzle without actually cooking the steak.
The first time I made my phone ring with an API call, I got all kinds of giddy.

There are a million ways to tell people 'no'. One way to tell people 'No, I don't want your business if you're in health care' is to make them check a box certifying they are not in healthcare at signup. That increases friction and demonstrates contempt for potential customers.
My customers don't care beans about Twilio: they care about making sure their expensive professionals don't idle for lack of work.
The last time I made a sales prediction for a new product was when I expressed the wild desire that Bingo Card Creator eventually sell a whole $200 per month. I hope to be every bit as mistaken.
Why spend 2-3 months getting v1.0 of the software ready if it turns out that users are cool on the entire concept.
posts.key_insight: You can validate a SaaS product by building only the demo that sells it — if the magic moment is making a phone ring from a web browser, everything after that is out of scope for your MVP.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his most ambitious A/B test: removing Bingo Card Creator's downloadable desktop application entirely and funneling all users to the web version. After finding that 78% of sales come from web app users while the desktop version generates 10x more support emails, he built an A/B test where half of visitors see a site where the download never existed. He expects a 25% sales increase by converting download users to web signups, which convert at twice the rate. The test was carefully designed to not disrupt existing customers, with override mechanisms and strategic summer timing.
key_points: The downloadable version generates 10x more support emails but only accounts for roughly 25% of sales, making it a net drag on the business
Rather than simply dropping the product, McKenzie A/B tests the removal to measure whether the impact is 'modestly positive or extraordinarily positive'
Higher conversion rates create a compounding flywheel with AdWords: better conversion lets you bid more per click, which gets more traffic, which generates more sales
Customers don't understand the distinction between desktop and web apps, so removing the choice simplifies their decision and likely increases total conversion
Strategic timing matters: launching during summer's sales crater means fewer customers are affected while kinks get worked out
money_quotes: Essentially, I'm A/B testing dropping a profitable product which has a modest bit of traction and thousands of paying customers.

I get literally ten support emails about the downloadable application for every one I get about the web application, and one of the first things I suggest to customers is 'Try using the web version, it will magically fix that.'
The last year has taught me quite a bit about the difference between web applications and downloadable applications. To whit: don't write desktop apps.
I think offering two choices probably confuses customers and decreases the total conversion rate. Eliminating one might help.
The A/B test is mostly to show me whether it is modestly positive or extraordinarily positive.
Darn, that is a bloody brilliant business model, where they extract rent every time I do hard work.
I can't allow myself to stay married to all old decisions just out of a desire to be consistent.
posts.key_insight: When a legacy product generates disproportionate support costs and lower conversion rates than its replacement, A/B testing its complete removal can reveal that killing it actually grows the business.

posts.summary: McKenzie discovers that his earlier intuition about bots not affecting A/B test results was mathematically wrong. Through algebraic analysis, he shows that bot traffic artificially depresses z-scores, potentially turning statistically significant results into null results. He implements a JavaScript-based bot detection system for his open source A/B testing library A/Bingo, requiring clients to perform arithmetic via AJAX POST requests — something resource-intensive for bots but trivial for browsers. The system retroactively scores participation when a visitor first proves human, integrating cleanly with existing test tracking.
key_points: Bot traffic doesn't cancel out in A/B tests as intuition suggests — the math shows bots artificially depress z-scores by pushing conversion rates toward zero, making significant results appear insignificant
User-Agent filtering catches barely half of bots, making it inadequate as a detection mechanism
Requiring JavaScript execution via AJAX POST is an effective low-friction bot filter because executing JS at web scale is resource-intensive for bot operators
The implementation defers scoring participation until humanness is proven, then retroactively applies results to previously seen tests
The same technique has broader applications: interaction-free CAPTCHAs for comments, load-shedding non-human traffic under peak load, and blocking destructive bot actions
money_quotes: I love when the market gives me exactly what I want.
Bots do bork statistical significance testing, by artificially depressing z scores and thus turning statistically significant results into null results at the margin.
It is a very bad idea to force all users to prove that they are human just so that you can A/B test them.
This isn't to keep a dedicated adversary out, it is to distinguish the overwhelming majority of bots from humans. This isn't a security feature.
While it is not specifically designed that Richard Stallman and folks running NoScript can't influence my future development directions, I am not overwrought with grief at that coincidence.
I am a big believer in not spending time creating features until you know customers actually need them.

I gave him my stock answer for folks who disagree with me on OSS design directions: it is MIT licensed, so you can fork it and code the feature yourself. If you are too busy to code it, that is fine, I am available for consulting.
posts.key_insight: Bot traffic doesn't harmlessly wash out of A/B tests — it mathematically depresses z-scores, and JavaScript execution challenges are an effective, low-friction way to filter bots without burdening real users.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie catalogs 40 false assumptions that programmers routinely make when designing systems that handle personal names. Drawing on his experience living in Japan and working with global enterprise systems, he argues that no computer system he has ever encountered handles names correctly. The post systematically demolishes assumptions ranging from names being ASCII-compatible to people even having names at all. McKenzie frames proper name handling as both a technical challenge and a matter of identity and respect. The piece became one of the most widely referenced posts in software engineering, spawning an entire genre of 'falsehoods programmers believe' articles.
key_points: Every assumption programmers make about names—from character encoding to structure to uniqueness—is wrong in some real-world case
Names are central to identity, and systems that reject valid names are causing real harm to real people
McKenzie's own experience with six different valid 'full names' across Japanese and Western systems illustrates how even well-designed global systems fail
The common database pattern of first_name/last_name columns encodes dozens of false cultural assumptions
The list builds from reasonable-sounding assumptions to absurd ones, revealing that even the most cautious programmer will get names wrong
money_quotes: Anything someone tells you is their name is — by definition — an appropriate identifier for them.
I have never seen a computer system which handles names properly and doubt one exists, anywhere.
I have lived in Japan for several years, programming in a professional capacity, and I have broken many systems by the simple expedient of being introduced into them.
People have exactly N names, for any value of N.
People's names sometimes have prefixes or suffixes, but you can safely ignore those.
People whose names break my system are weird outliers. They should have had solid, acceptable names, like 田中太郎.
People have names.
Refer people to this post the next time they suggest a genius idea like a database table with a first_name and last_name column.
posts.key_insight: Names are so culturally diverse and personally fluid that virtually every structural assumption a programmer might encode into a system will be wrong for some real person, and getting names wrong means getting identity wrong.

posts.summary: After Patrick's viral 'Falsehoods Programmers Believe About Names' post brought 100,000 visitors and crashed his server, he diagnosed the root cause as Apache's KeepAlive setting. With KeepAlive enabled at 3 seconds, each of his 20 worker processes spent most of its time idle waiting for follow-up requests, reducing effective throughput from 60,000 to roughly 380 requests per minute. Turning KeepAlive off immediately fixed the problem. He documents the debugging journey and shares specific Apache configuration recommendations for memory-constrained VPS environments.
key_points: Apache's KeepAlive setting, while sensible for normal traffic, becomes catastrophic under traffic spikes on memory-constrained servers by holding worker processes idle
With KeepAlive on at 3 seconds, each 250ms of actual work was followed by 3 seconds of waiting, reducing throughput by roughly 99%
The default settings effectively created a self-inflicted denial of service attack as new visitors queued faster than connections timed out
Caching alone wasn't sufficient — the bottleneck was connection management, not content generation
Nginx proved essentially unkillable for serving static assets, contrasting sharply with Apache's fragility under load
money_quotes: The site dies nearly every time I get Reddited — with only about 10,000 visitors each time, which shouldn't be a big number on the Internet.
If you're running a memory constrained VPS and get hit by a huge wave of traffic, KeepAlive will kill you.
For every 250ms of a worker streaming cached content to a client, it spent 3 seconds sucking its thumb waiting for that client to come back and ask for something else.
Other clients were stacking up like planes over O'Hare.
It was essentially a denial of service attack caused by the default settings.
The throughput of the server went from about 60k requests per second to about 380 requests per second. 380 is, well, not quite enough.
There seems to be no power on Earth or under it that can take Nginx down.
posts.key_insight: Apache's KeepAlive setting, a sensible default for normal traffic, can reduce server throughput by 99% during traffic spikes on memory-constrained VPS instances by holding worker processes idle waiting for follow-up requests that never come.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he is attending the Software Industry Conference in Dallas, where he will present on SEO strategies. He invites attendees to connect with him at the Hyatt Regency. He promises to share the presentation materials and written elaboration after delivering the talk, though he's unsure if video will be recorded.
key_points: McKenzie is speaking at the Software Industry Conference on SEO strategies
He actively encourages in-person networking with conference attendees
He commits to publishing presentation materials and elaboration after the talk
money_quotes: I'll be giving a presentation about SEO strategies on Saturday.
I'll be posting the presentation and some textual elaboration on it right after I finish delivering it.
posts.key_insight: McKenzie leveraged his blog audience to build connections at industry events while committing to share knowledge publicly after presenting.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie presents an advanced SEO framework for software companies, arguing that the core equation is simple: Content + Links = Success. He advocates treating company websites as first-class software products deserving continuous improvement, and demonstrates how scalable content generation through CMS systems, outsourced writing, and automation can turn SEO into a predictable revenue channel. Using his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study, he shows how paying a freelancer $3,000 over several years generated over $20,000 in sales in one year alone. He also covers linkbait strategies, particularly open source software as a uniquely effective link-building tool for developers.
key_points: SEO fundamentals reduce to a simple equation: Content + Links = You Win, but benefits compound multiplicatively rather than linearly
The biggest SEO mistake software companies make is treating their website as a second-class citizen — whipped up at 2 AM on release day and never touched again
Scalable content generation through outsourcing and automation turns SEO into a channel like PPC: invest money, measure returns, reinvest profits — except you keep SEO gains forever
The best content is modular, evergreen, competitively defensible, and process-oriented — treating content as durable capital rather than disposable news

Open source software is uniquely powerful linkbait because it's hard to duplicate, consultants will cite your site to justify their billing rates, and first movers in new categories capture disproportionate mindshare
money_quotes: People treat SEO like it is black magic, but at the core it is very simple: Content + Links = You Win.
The biggest single problem with software company's SEO is that they treat their website like a second class citizen.
You'd never do calculations by counting on your fingers when you have a computer available to do them for you. Hand-writing all content in Notepad is essentially the same.
The difference is that you get to keep your SEO gains forever rather than having to rebuy them on every click like PPC.
You are expensive. English majors are cheap.
$3.50 in investment, thousands in returns.
OSS which solves problems for businesses tends to pick up links from, e.g., consultants deploying it — they will cite your website to justify their billing rate.
The first semi-decent OSS in a particular category tends to win a lot of the links and mindshare. So get cracking!
posts.key_insight: Software companies should treat SEO as a scalable, automatable revenue channel by combining systematic content generation with strategic link-building, turning small investments in outsourced content into compounding long-term returns.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes an A/B test between his custom logo and a 99designs off-the-rack logo, sparked by a friendly wager with Thomas Ptacek of Matasano. The test ran sitewide for two weeks with 17,000 real users, and his original custom logo won decisively with 11.05% vs 9.92% conversion to free trial, statistically significant at 99% confidence. He argues this demonstrates that logos can potentially add or subtract 10% from a software company's enterprise value. The post emphasizes that A/B testing should be cheap and easy to run, requiring minimal code changes, and that most software companies never bother to test something with such significant business impact.
key_points: A custom logo beat a professionally-designed off-the-rack 99designs logo by over 1 percentage point in conversion rate, statistically significant at 99% confidence with 17,000 users
The entire test cost $99 for the logo, a few minutes of designer time, and a single line of code — making A/B testing infrastructure that reduces friction enormously valuable
Logos can potentially add or subtract 10% from a software company's enterprise value, yet most companies only design their logo once and never test alternatives
Bot traffic can drown out signal in sitewide A/B tests, requiring detection mechanisms to get meaningful results
Consolidated sitewide conversion rates are a poor decision-making metric compared to conversion rates on specific funnels like landing pages
money_quotes: If Thomas and I were bigwigs at a multinational firm with entire departments devoted to preventing anything from getting accomplished, that disagreement might have been settled by a great deal of harumphing. However, we're engineers, so we made a wager.

I can't possibly stress enough how valuable it is to have your A/B tests require only a single line of code. You'll test more frequently, you'll test more diligently, and you'll discover more valuable things about your business.
Your logo could potentially add or subtract 10% from enterprise value.
The majority of companies in our industry get a logo done early in their corporate life, generally exactly once.
Even though in this particular case the A/B test did not succeed in identifying a profitable change to make in my business, I consider it a wonderful use of my time.
I have the graphical skill of a molerat — I should be linking to this article on your blog, professional graphic designer, not writing it.
posts.key_insight: Most software companies treat their logo as a one-time decision, but A/B testing shows logos can swing conversion rates by 10% — making cheap, low-friction testing infrastructure one of the highest-leverage investments a small software business can make.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains how market seasonality significantly impacts software businesses, using his own Bingo Card Creator's dramatic sales swings as a case study. He advocates measuring year-over-year rather than month-over-month to avoid mistaking seasonal dips for declining traction. The post covers practical strategies including cash flow management during off-seasons, using slow periods for major engineering changes, and preparing marketing campaigns months before peak seasons. He also demonstrates how the same seasonal data can be presented to investors as either stagnation or growth depending on framing.
key_points: Year-over-year comparisons are essential for seasonal businesses because month-to-month changes can easily mask or exaggerate underlying trends
Off-seasons are ideal for deploying risky engineering changes and new features since fewer users are affected if something breaks
SEO and AdWords campaigns for seasonal peaks must be started months in advance to build ranking and campaign history
SaaS subscription models naturally smooth out seasonality that devastates one-time-purchase businesses
The same underlying data can tell completely different stories depending on whether you show monthly or year-over-year graphs
money_quotes: Market seasonality has non-trivial impact on many software businesses.
If possible, don't be surprised.
For highly seasonal businesses, get used to the words year-over-year.
It means that you'll need to model your behavior on the ant, not the grasshopper: when times are easy, save up for when times are going to be difficult.
The first business looks like it has had mostly flat sales for the last year, and a recent slump. The second business looks like it is killing it. They are the same business.
If someone comes into an ice cream shop in December, that is because they really, seriously are in the mood to buy ice cream. They'll also find the place absolutely deserted.
You need to educate them.
If this sounds like a whole lot of work, that is only because it is a whole lot of work, but you can have stupendously disproportionate returns to seasonal promotions.
posts.key_insight: Seasonal businesses should measure year-over-year, use off-seasons for risky engineering and new projects, prepare marketing months before peaks, and carefully frame seasonal data when communicating with stakeholders.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on the psychological difficulty of adjusting to self-employment after a lifetime of externally imposed schedules. Despite having complete freedom over his time, he finds the lack of structure disorienting and struggles with the feeling that he isn't being productive enough. Paradoxically, data shows he's actually getting more done than ever—running more A/B tests and increasing sales. He attributes the disconnect to decades of measuring productivity by hours worked rather than output. He explores hacks like getting an office for mental separation and using a gamification app to maintain discipline.
key_points: Complete schedule freedom is psychologically destabilizing after a lifetime of externally imposed structure, even when it's objectively productive
Self-employed people can feel unproductive despite data showing increased output, because they've been conditioned to equate productivity with time-at-desk
Decoupling customer happiness from personal availability is essential for running a global software business, but removes the urgency that drives daily work
Social validation of employment is deeply embedded—from waitresses questioning your schedule to police officers questioning your immigration status
Gamification and environmental hacks (offices, apps) can substitute for the discipline that schedules previously provided
money_quotes: They exist like the Greek gods: you didn't ask for them but they are there, there is no negotiating with them, and prolonged association means you are likely to get your dignity violated by a bovine.
Gainfully employed young men should be working at 2 PM on a Wednesday. I was having a late lunch and reading a novel at the coffee shop.
That's the thing about schedules: once you have one everyone else needs to, too, preferably as close to yours as possible.

I spend lots of effort decoupling their happiness from my personal availability. This does wonderful things for site uptime but it also means, perhaps regrettably, that there is generally no compulsion to work today.

It turns out that I am smoking something: I ran a shell script to compare my productivity prior and post quitting. I thought it would show me spinning my wheels. Turns out I am getting more done than ever.
I think after a couple of decades of living by the clock I have become habituated to measuring my productivity that way. Insane and irrational, I know.
Sounds funny until it happens from a police officer who does not quite understand immigration statuses.
posts.key_insight: The hardest part of self-employment isn't doing the work—it's the psychological disorientation of losing externally imposed structure, which can make you feel unproductive even when data proves you're accomplishing more than ever.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie translates Paul Graham's essay on emerging trends in startup angel investing from 'Financier into Geek.' He explains how convertible notes with rolling closes are replacing traditional fixed-valuation equity rounds, walking through valuations, discount mechanisms, and valuation caps with simple numerical examples. The key innovation is that early-committing angels get better terms, breaking the deadlock where investors all wait for social proof before investing. McKenzie notes this shifts the primary negotiation point from valuation to cap size, allowing founders to offer different 'price points' to different angels.
key_points: Traditional angel rounds create deadlocks because investors all want social proof before committing, leading to circular failures where willing investors collectively fail to invest
Convertible notes with discounts for early investors break this deadlock by rewarding angels who commit first with better terms, creating a stampede effect
Valuation caps protect angels from founders dragging their feet on raising, acting as a floor on the percentage of the company the angel receives
The primary pricing mechanism shifts from negotiating valuation to negotiating cap size, allowing founders to simultaneously offer multiple price points to different angels
McKenzie pointedly notes the essay was originally written in 'Financier instead of in Geek' — implying founders should recognize when information asymmetry works against them
money_quotes: If you haven't figured out the significance of it originally being written in Financier instead of in Geek, please, think it through.
Valuations are not connected to objective reality.
A convertible note is the result of a torrid affair between a loan and an equity instrument. It looks a bit like Mom and a bit like Dad.

We decided we'd all invest in you, but we don't think the company is worth $100. We think it is worth $50. Yeah, that has no basis in objective reality, but objective reality is that your company is worth $0 without the $100 in our collective pockets.
If a founder wants to reward an angel, they award them with a lower cap. If they don't, the angels get a higher cap, or no cap at all.
I rather like getting 100% of the returns.
posts.key_insight: Convertible notes with rolling closes and tiered valuation caps break the social-proof deadlock in angel investing by rewarding early-committing investors with better terms, shifting the negotiation from valuation to cap size.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces a curated index of his best 72 blog posts, organized by category. After four years and 500 posts totaling 300,000 words, he recognized the blog had become unwieldy for new readers. With help from a Hacker News volunteer, he grouped his best work by topic and linked related posts together, particularly experiment/results pairs. The post serves as a brief meta-announcement directing readers to his new 'greatest hits' page.
key_points: After 500 posts and 300,000 words over four years, the blog needed better information architecture for new readers
He curated 72 of his best articles and organized them by category with explanatory connections
He leveraged community help (a Hacker News assistant) to complete the curation project
The blog grew far beyond his initial expectation of a few dozen readers to several hundred thousand
money_quotes: When I started this blog four years ago, I never thought it would be seen by more than a few dozen people, so I never planned any sort of information architecture to it.
500 posts (300,000 words!) and several hundred thousand readers later, it is unwieldy to get to the good stuff unless you have been following along for the last couple of years, or have a week free.
posts.key_insight: A prolific blog eventually needs curated information architecture to remain accessible to new readers, and the effort to organize your best work is itself a valuable marketing investment.

posts.summary: A brief technical tip on how to automatically minimize JavaScript and CSS files in a Rails application. McKenzie points readers to a monkeypatch approach that integrates asset minimization into the normal Rails asset pipeline without requiring manual rake tasks during deployment. The post is essentially a four-step guide linking to external resources for the implementation details.
key_points: Rails apps can be monkeypatched to automatically minimize JS and CSS without manual rake tasks
The approach integrates with existing javascript_include_tag and stylesheet_include_tag helpers
Asset minimization can save users approximately 100kb of bandwidth per page load
money_quotes: If you obsess over your YSlow and/or Page Speed performance scores, you might be wondering how to get your Rails app to automatically crunch Javascripts and CSS files without actually requiring cumbersome rake tasks to be repeated during every deploy.
Watch your users save 100kb.
posts.key_insight: Asset minimization in Rails can be automated via a simple monkeypatch rather than requiring manual build steps during deployment.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie dissects critical security vulnerabilities he discovered in Diaspora's source code upon its pre-alpha release, detailing three classes of flaws: missing authorization checks, mass assignment vulnerabilities, and NoSQL injection. He demonstrates how each flaw could be trivially exploited to gain full control of any user's account, overwrite encryption keys, or extract private data from the database. McKenzie argues that releasing the code publicly was irresponsible because real users inevitably signed up to public nodes despite alpha disclaimers. He concludes that the Diaspora team lacks the security expertise to ship safely and that their codebase remains vulnerable even after patches.
key_points: Diaspora confused authentication (verifying identity) with authorization (verifying permission), allowing any logged-in user to perform any action on any other user's data
Rails mass assignment allowed attackers to overwrite arbitrary fields including encryption keys and account ownership, effectively enabling silent account takeover in two minutes with only Firebug
MongoDB string interpolation created a NoSQL injection vulnerability that could extract private encryption keys through binary search in roughly 1,024 requests
The 'pre-alpha' label provided no real protection because public nodes went live within hours and technically unsophisticated users signed up trusting their friends' endorsements
Crypto is not soy sauce for security — encryption systems in practice are defeated not by breaking the algorithm but by exploiting weaknesses in the surrounding application code
money_quotes: The bottom line is currently there is nothing that you cannot do to someone's Diaspora account, absolutely nothing.
This is what kills most encryption systems in real life. You don't have to beat encryption to beat the system, you just have to beat the weakest link in the chain around it.
Crypto is not soy sauce for security.
It literally took longer to get Diaspora running than it took to create a script weaponizing this.
You might also believe in magic code-fixing fairies. Personally, I'd be praying for the fairies because if Diaspora is dependent on the OSS community their users are screwed.

Don't interpolate strings in queries sent to your database! Use the MongoDB equivalent of prepared statements. If MongoDB doesn't have prepared statements, don't use it for your security-critical projects until it does, because you will be exploited.
Nobody should be making errors like these. It hurts us as developers, it hurts our ecosystem, and it endangers our users in spite of the trust they have put in us.
posts.key_insight: Security is not a feature you sprinkle on top — authentication without authorization, mass assignment defaults, and string interpolation in database queries are fundamental architectural failures that no amount of encryption can compensate for.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie recounts his experience at the Business of Software 2010 conference, synthesizing lessons from speakers including Seth Godin, Dharmesh Shah, Eric Ries, Scott Farquhar, Jason Cohen, Paul Kenny, Rob Walling, Peldi from Balsamiq, Eric Sink, Dan Bricklin, Derek Sivers, and Joel Spolsky. The talks covered themes from tribal marketing and customer happiness metrics to acquisition mechanics and the ethical responsibilities of founders. McKenzie weaves his own insights throughout, particularly around A/B testing, charging more, and the compounding nature of career experience. Joel Spolsky's closing argument — that founders have ethical obligations in how they deploy the leverage their businesses create — left a lasting impression on McKenzie, challenging his view that keeping a company small is a morally neutral lifestyle choice.
key_points: Technical competence is no longer scarce; marketing, community-building, and creating movements around software are now the key differentiators
Customer Happiness Index (CHI) as a predictive churn metric can be exploited across sales, product, and policy decisions to dramatically improve retention
Permission-based email marketing with autoresponder sequences is the highest-leverage channel for converting visitors into customers over time
Overnight success is a myth — Peldi's Balsamiq hockey stick disappears when you zoom out to include decades of skill-building
Founders have ethical responsibilities about what their businesses contribute to the world, not just personal lifestyle optimization
money_quotes: Ideas that spread, win.
As the cost of software approaches zero, you want to be the guy who owns the machine.
Absence of churn does not indicate the presence of delight.
Don't make awesome software, make awesome superstars. Make your customers awesome at their jobs.
If you work while they sleep, they won't know that you're ignoring them.
The most interesting people you will ever meet are the people who are most interested in you.
Customer faith in your product as a solution to their problem is directly proportional to how well they believe that you understand their problem.
You literally needed an entire lifetime to prepare for what you're doing today.
posts.key_insight: The most important assets in a software business are no longer technical — they are community, trust, and the ethical choices founders make about how to deploy the leverage their businesses create.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie documents his painful experience implementing SSL encryption for his Rails application, prompted by the release of FireSheep, a Firefox addon that trivially compromises web session cookies on shared wireless networks. He walks through the technical steps: configuring Nginx for dual HTTP/HTTPS, scrubbing absolute URLs, using Rails plugins for SSL requirements, and dealing with IE-specific download bugs. The process caused significant downtime and he admits he should have used a staging environment first. He urges other Rails developers to test FireSheep against their own admin sessions and implement SSL immediately.
key_points: FireSheep demonstrated that any wireless user could trivially hijack web sessions, making SSL encryption urgent even for seemingly low-risk applications
Implementing SSL in Rails required coordinating Nginx configuration, URL scrubbing, Rails plugins, and browser-specific workarounds — far more painful than expected
Mixed content (HTTP resources on HTTPS pages) triggers browser security warnings, and each browser has different rules about which resource types require HTTPS
IE has unique architectural issues with downloadable files over SSL that require specific header workarounds
Even tamper-proof CookieStore sessions are vulnerable if the session cookie is ever transmitted over unencrypted HTTP
money_quotes: Implementing SSL in Rails was very painful and resulted in my site being down or unusable for a large portion of my customers for much of the day.

My theory was that my customer's didn't store anything security critical in their elementary school bingo card games, but the increasing amount of information available to the admin plus a customer support story from this morning convinced me that compromise would be a Very Bad Thing.

If I were doing it again, I would have paid the extra few bucks to set up a staging environment with its own certificate and verified everything worked on that prior to trying to fight my way through the process.
Do you think you're going to remember that? Yeah, me neither.
Note there is a subtlety here: if you let Rails share a session cookie over both HTTP and HTTPS, then if it is ever used over HTTP, byebye cookie security.
This option will probably break your site, possibly subtly, the first time you switch it on.
I suggest you download FireSheep and see how quickly any idiot with wireless can compromise your admin session. Then, fix this as soon as possible.
posts.key_insight: SSL implementation is far more complex than flipping a switch — it requires coordinating server configuration, asset hosting, session security, and browser-specific workarounds, but the alternative is leaving every user session open to trivial hijacking.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie recaps his best-ever October for Bingo Card Creator, hitting $6,024 in monthly sales and crossing $100,000 in lifetime revenue. He details a multi-pronged Halloween promotion strategy: an SEO mini-site that drove underwhelming conversions, email marketing that delivered extraordinary 1,000% ROI on a $32 spend, and an A/B tested discount that surprisingly failed to move the needle for new customers. A critical SSL migration mistake during the month caused AdWords conversion tracking to break, costing thousands in wasted ad spend and disrupted campaign optimization. Despite the self-inflicted wounds, he views the month as a major success and plans to systematize seasonal promotions for future holidays.
key_points: Email marketing to a filtered list of 775 recent subscribers generated 15 sales on $32 of MailChimp credits, a scalable 1,000% ROI that dwarfed other marketing channels
The Halloween SEO mini-site drove 27,000 visits but only 15 attributable sales due to low click-through rates and audience mismatch between parent searchers and teacher buyers
A/B testing discounts for the general population showed no statistically significant lift over full price, and actually performed worse when accounting for the $5 per-copy discount
A hasty SSL migration broke AdWords conversion tracking for 48 hours, causing Google's Conversion Optimizer to throttle his traffic and costing thousands in wasted spend and lost sales
Time-limited urgency in promotions matters enormously: setting the discount expiry after the actual buying window closes undermines the psychological pressure to act
money_quotes: Big mistake: my customers empirically do not feel the same way.
Very little I have done has scalable 1,000% ROIs on marketing spends.
Surprisingly, discounts failed to make a dent in sales numbers in the general (non-email) population.
That was stupid of me because I know, from doing this for several years, that my window of opportunity for Halloween sales closes as soon as she goes to sleep on Thursday.
I think I probably made less in a 70 hour workweek than I did in those two days… while sleeping. I love self-employment.
The best time to start preparing for Halloween is in September… preferably, a September many years ago.
I anticipate the best thing to do to keep them down is to just email people early and often to keep myself in their mind, which is almost the opposite of my natural inclination.
Since I use Conversion Optimizer, this caused Google's algorithms to think 'Hmm, Patrick must really not want this flood of traffic we're sending him since it apparently converts like crap.'
posts.key_insight: Email marketing to an existing customer list can deliver orders-of-magnitude better ROI than SEO or paid acquisition, but only when the list is fresh and the offer is time-pressured against the actual buying window.

posts.summary: McKenzie documents the early development process for Appointment Reminder, his SaaS product for automating appointment reminders via phone, text, and email. After six months of procrastination following the MVP, he set an arbitrary end-of-November deadline and broke the work into half-day mini-quests to maintain momentum. He walks through his technology choices (Rails, jQuery, SASS/HAML), his ruthless feature scoping process where he cut anything not needed on launch day, and his incremental coding approach of getting visible progress quickly. The post serves as a real-time case study in how a solo developer ships a product by cutting scope aggressively and maintaining forward momentum.
key_points: Deadlines with small, measurable subgoals are more effective than open-ended timelines — breaking work into half-day quests creates consistent forward progress
Ruthless scope cutting is essential: features that won't be needed on launch day are out of scope, regardless of how useful they might eventually be
Starting with incremental visual progress (getting index views working, then show, then edit) creates momentum and clear pickup points for the next session
Technology choices should balance productivity with professional growth — Rails for speed, jQuery and SASS as deliberate learning investments
Shipping is only 10% of turning an application into a business; marketing, customer development, and organic SEO are the remaining 90%
money_quotes: While my life is firing on all cylinders, happiness does not write jQuery or Rails code.
Deadlines tend to coerce me into doing my best work. Specifically, deadlines with subgoals that have small units of measurable accomplishment work best for me.

Users need to be able to manage clients. OK. Hypothetically, they may have hundreds or thousands of them, so they'll need a search interface… but will they have hundreds or thousands on launch day? No? Search is out of scope. Next!
Uploading files is a pain in the keister — out of scope!
I am literally an order of magnitude more productive in Rails than I am with Java, and I lack the experience with Java architecture astronomy to build an application from the ground up.
Admin interface: God gave us console for a reason.
After shipping, of course, comes the 90% of the remaining work needed to turn an application into a business.
posts.key_insight: Shipping a product as a solo developer requires ruthless scope cutting — if a feature won't be needed on launch day, it's out of scope, and momentum comes from breaking work into small units of visible, measurable progress.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie documents the progress of building Appointment Reminder, his SaaS product for reducing no-show appointments. He covers his Rails deployment stack choices (VPS over Heroku, pragmatic technology selections), reports 70% code completion with core SMS and phone reminder loops working via Twilio. He describes outsourcing wins including Fiverr for voice recordings and a virtual assistant for bookkeeping, and shares critical customer discovery insights from phone conversations that revealed a broader market than expected, particularly service providers who travel to customers.
key_points: Pragmatic technology choices trump cutting-edge: McKenzie chose familiar tools (Rails 2.3, Mongrel, Ruby 1.8.7) over newer options specifically to ship faster
A $300/month virtual assistant eliminated a 10-month bookkeeping backlog in a week with only 30 minutes of management time, freeing hours for higher-value work

Customer conversations revealed a market segment (mobile service providers like exterminators) whose pain from no-shows is far more acute than the originally assumed market of appointment-based practices
The distinction between customers who lose revenue from no-shows versus those who lose revenue plus labor costs fundamentally changes pricing power and marketing positioning
Outsourcing voice recordings via Fiverr and planning creative scripts (Old Spice-style) as both product features and viral marketing tools
money_quotes: If I manage to bring my Slice to its knees, it will be because my revenue has hit several million dollars a month. That will give me many attractive scalability options, such as hiring someone to care about scalability while I sip chilled juices on the beach of a tropical island.
Martha is greatly inconvenienced by her no-show problem. Earnest is sputtering with rage about his no-show problem.
I now know this because I've gotten on the phone with three different Earnests now and just shut up while they talked about their problems.

Managing her and reviewing her output took perhaps 30 minutes in total, instead of somewhere on the order of three to six hours to do the data entry myself — plus, it didn't totally wreck me for other productive work that day.

Somebody who has a programming team and a very good idea for how many tens of thousands of dollars they lost last year to no-shows is, shall we say, quite willing and able to pay any price I want to charge.
Not only did Earnest just lose out on the revenue from killing your bugs, he just burned up three hours of salary at skilled labor rates because you forgot that today was, indeed, Tuesday.
Since customers don't pay me to do bookkeeping, fixing it went on the back burner.
posts.key_insight: Talking to actual customers reveals market segments with far more acute pain than you imagined, and the intensity of that pain directly determines your pricing power.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that essential engineering practices like staging servers, version control workflows, and automated deployments are rarely taught but dramatically improve software quality. Drawing from his experience at a Japanese megacorporation, he provides practical guidance for small teams to implement staging environments that mirror production, git branching strategies that decouple development from deployment, and deploy scripts that include sanity checks and rollback capabilities. He emphasizes that these practices should be documented, scripted, and reproducible rather than cobbled together manually. The post advocates for incremental improvement rather than perfection, noting that every step along the path yields clear benefits.
key_points: A staging server should be production minus users — as identical to production as possible, so failures happen there first instead of in front of customers
If setting up a staging server isn't easy, you already have an infrastructure problem: your production environment was cobbled together manually without documentation
Server configuration should live in source control via provisioning scripts, not splayed across fifteen config files on the server
Version control workflows must be documented, taught, and enforced — the specific workflow matters less than consistency across the team
Production emergencies represent systemic process failures, not one-off bugs, and deserve root cause investigation via five-whys analysis
money_quotes: Prior to doing so, my workflow generally involved a whole lot of bubble gum, duct tape, and praying.
Setting up a staging server should be easy. If it is not easy, you already have a problem in your infrastructure, you just don't know it yet.
Confidence is important since you'll probably be terrified and rushed when you need to do this, and rushed terrified people make unnecessary mistakes.
That sounded a little paranoid until that day when I accidentally created an infinite loop and rang every number in the database a hundred times.

Emergencies are not supposed to happen — anything that lets an issue get that far isn't just a one-off failure of whatever broke, it is a series of failures of the systems/processes designed to prevent failures from getting that far.
The sleep you save may be your own.
Best practices do not have to be passed from master to apprentice as oral lore.
posts.key_insight: Professional engineering practices like staging servers, version control workflows, and automated deployments aren't luxuries for big companies — they're essential infrastructure that small teams can and should implement incrementally, with each step yielding immediate benefits.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his 2010 business year, marking his transition from Japanese salaryman to full-time self-employment as the best decision he ever made. Bingo Card Creator grew 38% in sales to $42,590, driven by deprecating the desktop version and leveraging email marketing, while requiring minimal maintenance. He launched a consulting practice earning $45,000 from three clients, though juggling consulting with product work proved far harder than expected. Appointment Reminder finally launched in December after six months of delay, already attracting an acquisition offer he declined. His combined businesses generated roughly $60,000 in profit, validating his leap to independence.
key_points: Quitting his salaryman job was transformative: more family time, more money, better health, and genuine enjoyment of work, with little financial uncertainty thanks to years of gradual preparation
Bingo Card Creator's shift from desktop to web app cut support work by 10x, and email marketing via MailChimp generated over $1,000 in sales from $30 in costs and twenty minutes of writing

Consulting brought $45k in revenue but taught hard lessons about time estimation, scope management, and the hidden overhead of client communication that makes even 10 hours per week disruptive to product work

Appointment Reminder suffered six months of delay due to consulting distractions and post-salaryman adjustment, illustrating how consulting and product development compete for the same creative energy
Rising organic search traffic paradoxically lowered conversion rates by diluting the teacher-heavy target audience, but 1% of a much larger number still meant higher total sales
money_quotes: Self-employment is awesome. There are occasionally challenges to it, but since I spent a few years dipping my toes into the water prior to doing it full-time, I have had very little of the 'Uh oh, I might not be able to make rent' uncertainty that a lot of folks report.
I went 'full time' and work less than ever. This is mostly because BCC is mature software.
My Halloween, Thanksgiving, and Christmas mail blasts made well over a thousand dollars in sales for me, for about $30 worth of virtual postage stamps and perhaps twenty minutes of writing.

Charging a lot of money is a great idea in every possible way. It makes your clients respect your time more, keeps you motivated, and helps pay the rent during the lean summer months of the bingo calendar.

How hard could it possibly be to do 10 hours of consulting in a week and still get productive work done? Very freaking hard, because the 10 hours tended to be splayed across several days, require creativity and focus to execute, and not include the whole email/sales/support dance that comes with consulting.

April, May, June, July, August, September: that is six months, and I got very, very little accomplished. The distraction from consulting work, working on BCC, and reacclimating myself to a human existence after years of salarymanhood just totally destroyed any desire to do heavy lifting on a new project.
The downloadable version generates 10 times the work per customer that the web app version does.
posts.key_insight: Transitioning from employment to self-employment succeeds best when you spend years building revenue streams gradually, but the real challenge isn't making money — it's managing your attention across consulting, product maintenance, and new product development without letting any single activity crowd out the others.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes a data project he built to quantify the financial value of different college degrees by major. He combined Bureau of Labor Statistics salary data with Mechanical Turk crowd-sourcing to map occupations to degrees, then calculated net present value estimates for each major. The project was commissioned by an online education publisher as lead-generation content. He argues that the lack of transparent salary-by-major data from colleges is a deliberate information asymmetry that serves institutional interests, and that technical degrees perform very well while humanities and caring professions perform poorly.
key_points: Colleges could easily survey alumni on salary by major but won't because pricing all degrees identically requires the value to remain uncertain
Government data tracks salary by education level but not by major, leaving students without actionable comparison data for degree selection
McKenzie combined BLS occupation salary data with Mechanical Turk crowd-sourcing to estimate degree-by-degree financial returns
Technical degrees like Computer Science and Computer Engineering rank near the top, while humanities and caring professions like Social Work perform poorly even with advanced degrees
The project was built as consulting work for an online education lead-generation company, illustrating the massive economic ecosystem built around university enrollment
money_quotes: We have the Blue Book to tell you what a ten year old beater is worth down to the dollar, there are entire industries devoted to assessing every type of security to determine their valuation, and the closest thing most students have to insight on the degree selection process is getting advice from Aunt Sue.

No college will actually do this because transparency goes directly against their interests: if all degrees from a particular institution are valued at 'An uncertain, but certainly large and roughly constant number', then the standard practice of pricing them all identically makes sense.
If not, it is the academic equivalent of pricing stocks by length of ticker symbol.

Colleges may prefer to phrase this as 'students' to 'award' degrees to, because they are intellectually committed to a view of themselves as custodians of the lamp of human knowledge rather than rapacious money-grubbing institutions. I don't know of any reason they can't be both.
There might still be some Marxists on the faculty but it is all capitalists in the administrations.
Long story short: a lot of data, a bit of science, and a dollop of absolute voodoo — it's sort of like most social science, except I'm going to be honest about the voodoo upfront.
posts.key_insight: Colleges maintain deliberate information asymmetry about salary outcomes by major because transparent data would undermine their ability to price all degrees identically, and the government data that could fill this gap requires significant technical effort to make actionable.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that startups should treat customer acquisition as a hackable system, not just focus on building better products. He observes that incumbents are optimized for existing adoption cycles but often can't adapt, creating openings for startups to innovate on how they sell rather than just what they sell. Using examples like freemium models, monthly billing, and SEO expertise, he shows how changing the acquisition method can be as disruptive as changing the product itself. He advocates for treating marketing strategy with the same rigor as product development, iterating and measuring systematically.
key_points: Incumbents are optimized for current adoption cycles but may be running efficiently in a suboptimal direction, creating openings for startups
Freemium is a customer acquisition tactic, not a business model — it deputizes free users as internal sales champions to bypass expensive consultative sales
Monthly billing defeats corporate purchasing step functions by keeping individual charges below approval thresholds, opening up the no-man's land between $1K and $75K lifetime value
A perfectly viable business can be built from an inferior product solely by innovating on the method of selling it
Customer acquisition strategy should be iterated on with the same Lean Startup discipline applied to products
money_quotes: Customer acquisition is one of the easiest places to screw up as a startup, particularly for technical founders (who, in their previous lives, have probably never had to do it for anything).
You're competing with their business, not with their product.
Freemium isn't a business model so much as it is a customer acquisition tactic.
If you bill a Fortune 500 company product manager $80 a month, you are essentially conspiring with him against his accounting controls. Not that there is anything wrong with that.

I have a wee little heresy as an engineer: I think that you can make a perfectly viable business out of a product which is not better than competitors, solely by improving on the method of selling it.
They also couldn't spell SEO if you handed them a set of alphabet flash cards, which is good news for me.
In a world where encrusted mollusk discharges say 'I love you' anything is possible.
posts.key_insight: Startups can outmaneuver incumbents not by building better products but by hacking the customer acquisition cycle itself — innovating on how you sell is often more important than what you sell.

posts.summary: McKenzie details his ongoing frustration with Google AdWords' ad approval process, which flags his educational bingo software as gambling due to keyword-based blacklists. The review process introduces unbounded delays—ads can sit 'Under Review' for weeks—and the only reliable workaround is filing support tickets that trigger automated brush-off responses. He argues the system punishes compliant advertisers while being trivially circumvented by bad actors. Despite paying Google $10,000 annually, he finds their customer service essentially non-existent, and the lack of viable competitive alternatives in CPC advertising leaves him with no leverage.
key_points: Google's keyword-based ad blacklist cannot distinguish between gambling and educational software that uses the word 'bingo', creating persistent false positives

The ad review process punishes compliant advertisers—copying ads between groups or changing a single character triggers re-review—while bad actors can trivially serve whatever content they want via redirects
Google's stated 1-2 business day review timeline is fiction; ads can sit under review for weeks, and the only reliable fix is filing support tickets
Google's 'scalable communication methods' philosophy breaks down when processes require mandatory human interaction, creating a Catch-22
Despite being a $10,000/year customer, McKenzie has no leverage because Google faces no meaningful competition in CPC advertising for niche markets
money_quotes: Bingo Card Creator is not a form of gambling — it is a form of software which helps elementary schoolkids learn to read.
I guess all the eats-Bayesian-classifiers-for-lunch PhDs work in search and Gmail spam filtering, where they've clearly got an aptitude for understanding that words can have multiple meanings.
That's insane - it is extra work that is directly against the spirit of the rules and unlike actual compliance it works.
The first rule of Google Customer Service is that Google does not have Customer Service.
Anything which introduces a mandatory customer service interaction with Google is a process designed for failure.
Length of time from ad submission to approval is, in my experiences, unbounded. Length of time from complaining to Support to approval: a day or two.

As much as I appreciate your False! Enthusiasm! if the next line of your letter is going to be Eff Off And Die then maybe you should take out the exclamation points and give them to a Ruby programmer.
It is the Internet equivalent of putting a message in a bottle and then throwing it into the ocean, but that is still an improvement on the normal channels.
posts.key_insight: Google's ad approval system creates a Kafkaesque experience where compliant advertisers are punished by processes designed for bad actors, while the lack of competition means customers have no recourse except public shaming.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie posts a brief personal update following the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami in Japan. He confirms he and those close to him are safe in Gifu, far from the epicenter. He thanks readers for their concern and redirects charitable impulses toward disaster relief organizations, noting that future disasters will likely strike less-prepared countries than Japan.
key_points: McKenzie and his contacts in Japan are safe, as Gifu is far from the earthquake epicenter and landlocked
He recommends donating to disaster relief charities like the Red Cross, but frames it in terms of ongoing preparedness rather than just this event
He makes the pragmatic observation that Japan is better prepared for disasters than most countries, so donations today will likely help less-prepared populations hit by future incidents
money_quotes: We're landlocked so, unless the mountains fall into the sea, tsunamis are not an issue for us.
Every day is a good day for charity.

Disaster relief charities will use money collected today to help the folks affected by the next major incident, and it is highly probable that they are less well-situated than Japan is — we're probably as well-prepared as anybody could be.
posts.key_insight: Disaster relief donations have their greatest impact when directed toward future incidents in less-prepared countries, not necessarily the headline event.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie, a software business owner living in central Japan, wrote this post to counter sensationalized English-language reporting about the 2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami. He emphasizes that Honshu is enormous — comparable to the distance from Chicago to New Orleans — and that Tokyo suffered negligible damage despite dominating coverage. He details Japan's extraordinary disaster preparedness systems, from university emergency notifications activated within 90 seconds to trains stopping safely at 150+ mph. While acknowledging the devastating loss of life from drowning, he argues the earthquake was a triumph of engineering: buildings stood, trains stopped, factories didn't explode, and nuclear plants performed largely as designed. He closes with practical advice against well-meaning but counterproductive foreign volunteering.
key_points: Honshu is larger than Great Britain and 800 miles long, making Tokyo-centric earthquake coverage as misleading as asking Chicago's mayor about Katrina
Japan's disaster preparedness is so thorough that even minor municipal offices maintain English translator rosters and drill annually on emergency procedures
The engineering response was overwhelmingly successful: trains made emergency stops without derailments, buildings stood, factories with dangerous chemicals didn't explode

Nuclear plant coverage was disproportionately alarming — the reactors scrammed as designed, and radiation exposure levels were between 'ate a banana' and 'carries a Delta Skymiles platinum membership card'
Well-meaning foreigners volunteering in Japan would be illiterate, unable to understand instructions, and a productivity drag on capable local responders
money_quotes: Quite a lot of the reporting on Japan is the equivalent of someone ringing up Mayor Daley during Katrina and saying 'My God man, that's terrible — how are you coping?'
Japanese does not have a word for excessive preparation.
The overwhelming response of Japanese engineering to the challenge posed by an earthquake larger than any in the last century was to function exactly as designed.
Millions of people are alive right now because the system worked and the system worked and the system worked.
That this happened was, I say with no hint of exaggeration, one of the triumphs of human civilization.

Screw luck. Luck had absolutely nothing to do with it. Decades of good engineering, planning, and following the bloody checklist are why this was a serious disaster and not a nation-ending catastrophe.

When you hear news reports of people exposed to radiation, keep in mind, at the moment we're talking a level of severity somewhere between 'ate a banana' and 'carries a Delta Skymiles platinum membership card.'

When you get here, you're going to be a) illiterate b) unable to understand instructions and c) a productivity drag on people who are quite capable of dealing with this but will instead have to play Babysit The Foreigner.
posts.key_insight: The 2011 Japan earthquake was not a story of lucky survival but of decades of meticulous engineering, preparation, and procedural discipline that converted what could have been a civilization-ending catastrophe into a serious but survivable disaster.

posts.summary: McKenzie announces the publication of his Business of Software 2010 talk on 'Software For Underserved Markets.' He describes BOS2010 as a defining moment in his professional career and strongly recommends the conference for software business owners. The post is essentially a brief promotional note linking to his slides and video, while endorsing the BOS conference community as smart people running profitable businesses.
key_points: His BOS2010 talk on software for underserved markets has been published as video and slides
BOS2010 was a career-defining moment for McKenzie
The conference attracts people running real, profitable software businesses
He plans to speak again at BOS2011 with a longer, more serious talk
money_quotes: BOS2010 was one of the defining moments of my professional career.
It's choc full of very, very smart people who are running real, profitable software businesses.
I will probably be speaking again this year, but for longer and with less joking.
posts.key_insight: The Business of Software conference represents a valuable community for software entrepreneurs focused on building real, profitable businesses in underserved markets.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a six-month update on Appointment Reminder, his second SaaS product. Despite slow customer acquisition (~50 trials, ~10 paying), unit economics are strong with costs well below revenue per customer. He recounts a harrowing production incident where a queue failure led to hundreds of duplicate calls blasting customers' phones. He also discusses discovering the white-label sales channel, learning painful lessons about high-touch B2B sales, and deciding to drop his lowest pricing tier after realizing some prospects aren't worth pursuing.
key_points: Unit economics proved better than expected: most Professional plan customers cost under $3/month to service against $29/month revenue, making scaling the key challenge
A production incident during an apartment move caused hundreds of duplicate reminder calls, teaching hard lessons about idempotent queue design and incident response discipline
White-label reselling emerged as a promising channel but requires proactive sales support that doesn't come naturally to a low-touch marketer
Qualifying B2B prospects early is essential — spending 15 emails discovering someone can't afford $9/month is wasted effort that should be caught immediately
He plans to kill the $9 pricing tier, recognizing that a business is defined both by what it does and what it does not do
money_quotes: Picture me in a freezing, pitch black apartment at 4 AM in the morning crying in between calls to apoplectic customers of customers.
A business is defined both by what it does and what it does not do.
I don't want to spend time marketing the service to customers who think $9 is an appreciable amount of money.
I'm glad to have taken my licks when I had five customers as opposed to when I have five thousand — that would have made for a very long night of apologies.
Failure to deliver reminders in a timely fashion is not nearly the worst possible failure case.
It is a new experience when people are not trying to tell me about problems and this means I have work to do.
The worst affected user got 40 calls, back to back, essentially DDOSing their phone line for 15 minutes straight.
posts.key_insight: Building a SaaS business with strong unit economics is the easy part; the hard parts are incident response discipline, learning high-touch sales processes, and having the judgment to disqualify prospects who will never be profitable customers.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he will be speaking at MicroConf, a conference for small software businesses organized by Rob Walling in Las Vegas. He offers a promo code and a free ticket giveaway for someone with an existing small software business. He previews his talk concept: an iceberg metaphor about how businesses hide most of their value below the waterline, and how marketing and SEO are about maximizing the visible portion. He emphasizes his eagerness to personally connect with attendees and give advice about their software businesses.
key_points: MicroConf targets small, profitable software businesses — a niche community Patrick has long been part of
The iceberg metaphor: most value a business creates is hidden internally or only visible to existing customers, while marketing success depends on maximizing the visible portion
Conferences are most valuable when attendees actively engage speakers in conversation rather than passively attending talks
Patrick's ticket giveaway criteria reveal his values: actual product, actual sales, willingness to invest effort to find his contact info
money_quotes: Every business is an iceberg: of the value created by the business, much of it is hidden within the company or (at best) exposed to existing customers, and only a small portion peaks above the waterline, outside of the existing community around the business.
Both traditional marketing and SEO revolve around maximizing the visible bit of the iceberg.
I'm literally flying halfway around the world and I have no agenda item other than talking to you about your software business.
This is an explicit invitation: talk to me.
I can't promise it will be good advice, but I intend to give lots of it.
posts.key_insight: Most businesses fail to surface the value they create — marketing is the discipline of making more of that value visible to people outside your existing community.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie recounts his journey from underemployed technical translator in rural Japan to running a profitable software business (Bingo Card Creator) in just 5 hours per week. He details how he leveraged content marketing, Google AdWords Conversion Optimizer, and systematic optimization to grow a hobby project into a real business. The presentation covers his transition from salaryman to full-time entrepreneur, supplemented by consulting income. The tactical half offers five actionable strategies: charge more, build web apps, make your work publicly visible, master SEO, and optimize everything through A/B testing. He emphasizes that multiplicative improvements across the funnel compound dramatically over time.
key_points: A hobby project born from a mailing list request became a real business by applying SEO, AdWords, and content marketing systematically over years
Google's Conversion Optimizer automated the tedious work of banning spam sites and turbocharged ad spend on converting traffic, particularly during seasonal spikes like Halloween
Scalable content creation — partially automated, partially outsourced — generated nearly a thousand SEO-optimized pages that delivered free organic traffic
Converting from desktop app to web application roughly doubled sales due to improved conversion rates at every stage of the funnel
