All factors in software business success are multiplicative: small compounding improvements across the funnel yield outsized results over time
money_quotes: Most engineers severely undercharge for their products.

Almost all SaaS firms find that they make huge portions of their revenue from the topmost plan which is bought by people spending other people's money, but instead of optimizing for this we optimize for charging 'fair' prices as determined by other software developers who won't pay for the service anyway.
Businesses create value with almost everything they do. The lion's share of the value is, like an iceberg, below the waterline: it cannot be seen from anywhere outside the business.
Did you write software not core to your line of business? Great, OSS it. Get the credit.
If you increase conversions to the trial by 10% and conversions to sale by 10%, your sales go up by 21%, because 1.1 * 1.1 = 1.21.
What real company offers 70% raises per year just for doing an A/B test every week and collecting a +5% improvement on one out of every four?
I budgeted $60 (one video game) and one week to rewriting and productizing it, and set myself a goal: some day, after months of work, I wanted to make $200 in sales a month.
Gifu is the Kansas of Japan, with more rice and less white people.
posts.key_insight: A solo software business can be grown to substantial revenue in just 5 hours per week by systematically applying content marketing, conversion optimization, and automation — treating every improvement as a multiplier rather than an addition.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on how running his own business fundamentally changed his psychology, decision-making, and self-confidence. He traces his journey from being a praise-seeking student to an unhappy Japanese salaryman to a thriving independent software business owner. The post argues that career choices exist in a vast multi-dimensional preference space, not on simple linear trajectories, and that most constraints we accept about work are negotiable social conventions rather than laws of nature. He shares how business ownership taught him to value his time, charge appropriately for consulting, negotiate confidently with anyone, and ultimately optimize for happiness. He concludes by explaining why he turned down the idea of taking investment, recognizing it would move him away from what actually makes him happy.
key_points: The conventional narrative of 'work hard, get good grades, get a good job' is a story we tell because it's compelling, not because it's universally true — the relationship between effort and reward is far more complex and nonlinear than we pretend.
Career options exist in a vast multi-dimensional preference space, and optimizing for happiness rather than following prescribed paths requires honest self-knowledge about what you actually value.
Most workplace norms — five-day weeks, salary ranges, job descriptions — are negotiable social conventions, not immutable rules, and business ownership makes this viscerally obvious.

Confidence compounds through practice: quoting consulting rates, emailing strangers, and negotiating with bureaucrats all become less terrifying with repetition, and the feared catastrophic outcomes almost never materialize.
Money is a tool for buying back time and getting what you want, not an end in itself — a shift in perspective that transforms both business and personal decision-making.
money_quotes: The world isn't a meritocracy.
The way we work is, essentially, arbitrary. 40 hour work weeks for middling-good salaries are no more a law of nature than 90 hour workweeks for middling-poor salaries.
We don't tell it because it is a true story, we tell it because it is a crackling good story.
I'll save my loyalty for people. Companies are legal fictions which we find convenient to use to move capital around and balance accounting ledgers.
If you're not happy, and you're not moving to happy, you should do something else. We should be happy.
He told me that he would have paid $15,000, and it blew my mind.
Get comfortable with sending email to people and asking them to give you what you want. This will virtually never cause a mortal catastrophe.
Almost nothing is as big a deal as we think it is.
posts.key_insight: Running a business reveals that most career constraints are negotiable social conventions rather than fixed rules, and the key skill is knowing what actually makes you happy and having the confidence to optimize for it.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that engineers fundamentally misunderstand how their careers work because educational institutions fail to teach business realities. The core thesis is that engineers are hired to create business value—increasing revenue or reducing costs—not to write code, and they should position themselves accordingly. He covers salary negotiation, networking, the irrelevance of academic credentials, the overvaluation of startup equity, and the critical importance of communication skills. The post systematically dismantles engineer-culture shibboleths like technical stack identity, modesty, and the belief that merit speaks for itself. McKenzie contends that framing yourself as a business-value creator rather than a programmer is the single most important career move an engineer can make.
key_points: 90% of programming jobs involve boring line-of-business software, and engineers should embrace that software's value is measured by revenue generated or costs reduced, not technical sophistication
Calling yourself a 'programmer' makes you sound like an expensive cost center ripe for outsourcing; instead, describe yourself by the business outcomes you've delivered
Most desirable jobs are filled through personal networks before they're ever posted publicly, making relationship-building more valuable than resume optimization
Communication skills and the ability to frame your work in business terms matter more than raw programming ability for career advancement
Startup equity is almost always worth far less than engineers believe due to dilution, liquidation preferences, and high failure rates
money_quotes: If you call yourself a programmer, someone is already working on a way to get you fired.
It does not matter to the company that the reporting form is the world's simplest CRUD app, it only matters that it either saves the company costs or generates additional revenue.
You really want to be attached to Profit Centers because it will bring you higher wages, more respect, and greater opportunities for everything of value to you.
Nobody ever outsources Profit Centers. Attempting to do so would be the setup for MBA humor.
'What is your previous salary?' is employer-speak for 'Please give me reasons to pay you less money.' Answer appropriately.
You radically overestimate the average skill of the competition because of the crowd you hang around with.
Information about the position travels at approximately the speed of beer, sometimes lubricated by email.
Modesty is not a career-enhancing character trait.
posts.key_insight: Engineers are not hired to write code—they are hired to create business value, and the most successful ones learn to describe themselves and their work in those terms rather than in technical jargon.

posts.summary: McKenzie walks through debugging an extremely subtle bug in Bingo Card Creator where a Rails security upgrade silently changed CSRF protection behavior from throwing exceptions to quietly resetting sessions. This interacted with his A/B testing library's humanity-check AJAX POST, creating a race condition that sporadically logged users out and wiped referrer tracking data. The bug manifested differently in development vs. production due to timing differences in JavaScript execution, making it nearly impossible to reproduce consistently. He traces his debugging journey from noticing anomalous Halloween sales stats to spelunking through Rails source code across version changes, ultimately finding a one-line fix. The post serves as a meditation on how modern web applications sit atop layers of complexity that are usually invisible until they break in unexpected ways.
key_points: Rails 2.3.11 silently changed CSRF protection from raising exceptions to quietly resetting sessions, a behavioral change buried in a security patch that had far-reaching consequences
The bug was a race condition between JavaScript execution timing and user interaction speed, manifesting only in production where network latency to external scripts altered execution order
The first signs were anomalous analytics data — Halloween referral stats showed ~100 trials instead of thousands — but since revenue wasn't obviously affected, investigation was delayed
Race conditions in web applications can emerge from the interaction of concurrent browser processes even without explicit threading, making them extremely hard to reproduce and diagnose

Taking ownership of the bug entirely despite it originating from a framework change, emphasizing that reading patch notes thoroughly and investigating anomalies early would have prevented months of silent damage
money_quotes: If you're not technically inclined, you think 'Hmm, ghosts in the Googles. Oh well, here is my username and password again.'
Bingo Card Creator has been doing this pervasively to a fraction of my users for the last few months. I never noticed it, and no one ever complained.
Rails 2.3.11 changed the behavior of CSRF protection: instead of throwing exceptions, it would silently just clear the session and re-run the request.

Race conditions are why sane programmers don't program with threads or, if they do, they use shared-nothing architecture and pass all communication between the threads through a message queue written by someone who knows what they are doing (if you have to ask, it isn't you).
If my users are inconvenienced, it is my fault, always.
That's the optimistic way to say it. The pessimistic way is to say that this bug negatively affected the better part of a thousand people, and probably cost me sales from some of them.

Bingo Card Creator is not terribly complicated software when compared to most applications, but it sits on top of other pieces of code which collectively are orders of magnitude more complicated than any physical artifact ever created by the human race.

Most of the time that complexity is abstracted away from both the user and the developer, both as blissfully ignorant of the layers below as an ant walking on an aircraft carrier is ignorant of the depth of the ocean.
posts.key_insight: Silent behavioral changes in framework security patches can create race conditions that are nearly impossible to reproduce in development, causing subtle but widespread damage that only surfaces through anomalous analytics data.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes his experiment with image ad blending on BuySellAds, where he designed a banner ad for Bingo Card Creator that visually matches the host site's content style. The technique exploits banner blindness by making ads look like native content rather than traditional advertisements. He placed a $135/month ad on BusyTeacher.org targeting teachers and achieved a 2.2% click-through rate, far above banner advertising norms. The cost per acquisition dropped to roughly 8 cents versus 30-40 cents on Google AdWords, making the economics dramatically better. He reflects on the ethics of the approach and concludes it's aggressive but honest. He wishes more inventory were available in his niche and plans to pursue direct ad buys with smaller sites.
key_points: Ad blending — styling ads to resemble site content — defeats banner blindness and dramatically increases click-through rates
A $135/month BuySellAds placement on BusyTeacher.org yielded a 2.2% CTR and ~8 cent CPA versus 30-40 cents on Google AdWords
The ad is honest about being an ad but designed to not trigger automatic visual filtering by users
Direct ad buys can outperform Google AdWords on both cost and targeting, but finding appropriately-sized inventory is the bottleneck
The economics work out to roughly $4 to generate a $30 sale instead of $20 through AdWords
money_quotes: You want to not look like an ad at the first glance, but to look like an ad on the second glance.
Once upon a time I was an engineer totally scornful of effective marketing, but I have gradually gotten over it.
A lot of users think that yellow is used for marking the best results. Non-technical customers, yay.
That ad has a 2.2% click through rate, astronomical by the standards of banner advertising.
At typical conversion rates to a trial of about 1.8~2%, that means that this costs me $4 or so to generate a $30 sale rather than $20. Sold!
For an experiment that looks like it will net in the neighborhood of $1,000 a month in sales for $135 and ~10 minutes of pixel pushing, this one is going in the win column.
posts.key_insight: Designing image ads that blend with a site's visual style defeats banner blindness and can dramatically reduce customer acquisition costs compared to standard ad networks.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss their experiences working in tech in Japan, covering the stark differences in how programmers are valued compared to the US, the broken social contract of lifetime employment, and Japan's lagging adoption of modern web practices. They pivot to evangelizing A/B testing as a transformative business practice, sharing concrete examples of massive conversion lifts from simple changes like headlines and button design. The core argument is that A/B testing is criminally underused despite being nearly free to implement, and that the skill of conversion optimization is far more valuable than most engineering work. They also discuss how job titles matter enormously — calling yourself a 'system engineer' instead of 'programmer' can double your perceived value.
key_points: Japanese tech culture treats programmers as code monkeys while 'system engineers' command double the pay and respect — a dynamic that applies everywhere, not just Japan
A/B testing is so cheap to implement (one line of code, five minutes) that NOT doing it is almost always a waste relative to other business activities
A random suggestion from someone who knew nothing about Bingo Card Creator produced a 40% lift in sales just by changing a headline
Most businesses don't track basic metrics that are key to their success, like what percentage of users return after first use (typically a depressing ~40%)
Press credibility ('As seen in the New York Times') and faces on landing pages reliably boost conversions — people are biologically wired to track gazes
money_quotes: If there are 10 people in a fight and only one of them understands how to use science, I already know who's going to win.
People do not read on the Internet. If you take one thing from this podcast, people do not read on this Internet.
I guarantee if you run a business right now, there is some number that is key to your business that you are not tracking and that would be very eye-opening to you.
On the medical science scale, the discipline of design is closer to leeches than it is to medicine.
The feeling in the company was that even though we were a software company, writing software did not add value. That's almost a direct quote from the CEO.

If you can routinely quote numbers like that, you are no longer in the $60-to-$100-an-hour bucket with other programmers, you are in the bucket with strategic initiatives that add five percent to Bank of America's bottom line.

Think back. A week ago you were doing something for your business, right? Did it get you 40 percent to 60 percent improvement in your business's bottom line? If not, why weren't you doing A/B testing instead?
Whatever day God handed out the taste I was studying stats for D&D characters or something and totally missed it.
posts.key_insight: A/B testing is so cheap and so powerful that failing to do it continuously is one of the largest unforced errors a business can make, yet most companies — even sophisticated ones — leave enormous gains on the table.

posts.summary: McKenzie shares hard-won lessons from running Appointment Reminder, a production Twilio application, covering testing, security, deployment, and architecture. He opens with a passionate case for Twilio as transformative technology that turns every phone into a smartphone, then pivots to the painful realities of building production telephony apps. The core of the presentation addresses a catastrophic incident where a queue failure caused 600 phone calls, 400 SMSes, and 200 emails to blast 15 people in five minutes. He advocates modeling phone calls as state machines, using staging servers, implementing strict security controls, and designing systems to fail closed rather than fail open.
key_points: Twilio's Quick Start guides teach the minimum to get phones ringing but leave developers unprepared for production realities like testing, security, and error handling
Phone calls as state machines replace brittle case statements with testable, verifiable transition tables that prevent entire categories of bugs
Telephony apps have a superset of web security issues: confidential information stops being confidential the moment you speak it into a phone
Systems should be designed to fail closed — when in doubt, don't make that phone call — because telephony errors impose real-world consequences on real people
Applications can scale orders of magnitude faster than the businesses they serve, which creates its own category of problems
money_quotes: Twilio turns every phone into a smartphone.

The end-game for Zynga's take-over-the-world vision is the human race slaved to artificial dopamine treadmills. The endgame for Twilio's vision is that every $2 handset in Africa is the moral equivalent of an iPhone.

Smartphones aren't smart because of anything on the phones themselves, they're smart because they speak HTTP and thus get always-on access to a universe of applications which are improving constantly.
This resulted in approximately 15 people receiving a total of 600 phone calls, 400 SMSes, and 200 emails, in approximately a 5 minute period of time.

Twilio testing is inherently dangerous, because non-production environments could conceivably generate actual, real-world phone calls to phone numbers which were in your database but not actually under your control.
Our brains get lulled into a false sense of security by parallel structure.
Even if your free trial is discovered by 4chan, the world should not become a darker, more evil place.
When in doubt, don't make that phone call. Design your system to fail closed.
posts.key_insight: Production telephony applications require defensive engineering far beyond typical web apps because bugs don't just show error pages — they blast real people's phones at 3 AM.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2011 annual report covers his best year ever, breaking $100,000 in total sales across Bingo Card Creator, consulting, and Appointment Reminder. BCC was in maintenance mode for most of the year but remained roughly flat at ~$23k profit. Consulting generated $55k in revenue but consumed far more time than expected due to prospecting overhead and travel. Appointment Reminder grew despite neglect, with McKenzie weighing whether to take outside investment or grow organically. He candidly discusses how disorganization and lack of focus prevented him from executing on his primary goal of building AR, and closes with a personal engagement announcement.
key_points: Bingo Card Creator generated ~$45k in sales and ~$23k profit on essentially zero active work, proving the value of durable automated systems like SEO and email marketing
Consulting revealed that raising rates from X to 5X had virtually no impact on demand, but the overhead of prospecting, travel, and deal-making consumed far more time than the actual billable work
Appointment Reminder sits at a strategic crossroads between organic growth and venture investment, with McKenzie deliberately keeping options open while his runway is infinite
Enterprise sales emerged as unexpectedly important for AR, to the point where NDAs prevent public discussion of the business numbers
Immigration issues in Japan consumed two full months, highlighting the real operational costs of being a self-employed foreigner running a business outside Silicon Valley
money_quotes: I forecast 50% growth, reasoning 'Hey, most of the systems work pretty much without my intervention, so I think the overall growth of the Internet plus a few A/B test means, oh, 50% or so.' It mostly tread water.
SEO, AdWords, email marketing, and the usual scalable marketing stuff continued to be my bread and butter even when I was too lazy to actually cut and butter bread.
My first guesstimate at my rate, back in 2010, was $X. It turns out that I could do just about as much work as I wanted regardless of whether I charged $X, $2X, or $5X.
It takes approximately X units of work to set up an engagement with a previous satisfied customer, 5X units of work to get a new prospect to the go/don't-go decision on a new engagement.
My runway is infinite, the market opportunity is only getting bigger, and the perceived value of my involvement with a startup among investors does not appear to be depreciating.
One of my customers reports that just the delta in no-shows since starting to use AR would pay for his mortgage and his daughter's college education.
I was a shoe-in for a Japanese visa back when I worked at a large megacorp, was not a shoe-in for a visa when doing my own thing.
The last 3,300 words ultimately matter much, much less than the next 3: she said yes.
posts.key_insight: A software business built on durable, automated systems like SEO and email marketing can sustain itself with almost no active work, but growing beyond that plateau requires the focus and discipline that consulting income and life logistics constantly threaten to consume.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that engineers are terrible at salary negotiation and have turned this incompetence into a misguided badge of honor. He frames negotiation as a brief, high-leverage activity worth potentially $100,000+ over a career, using the analogy of reading poetry while standing on one foot for $100,000. The post systematically dismantles psychological barriers to negotiation, explains how employers actually think about compensation (in terms of fully-loaded costs where $5,000 is trivial), and provides tactical advice including never giving a number first, using the employer's own language, and trading across multiple dimensions of compensation. McKenzie emphasizes that negotiation should only happen after receiving a 'Yes-If' — agreement in principle that you'll be hired if terms are reached — and that the process is far less adversarial than most candidates imagine.
key_points: A $5,000-$15,000 salary increase compounds over a decade into six figures when factoring in raises, 401k contributions, and future salary anchoring
Employers think in fully-loaded costs (150-200% of salary), making small salary differences trivial to them but life-changing to you — the asymmetry is enormous
Never give a number first; every request for your salary history or requirements is an attempt to anchor you to an anomalously low number
Only negotiate after receiving a 'Yes-If' — the company has already invested thousands in interviewing you and desperately wants the deal to close
Salary is just one dimension; vacation days, project assignments, professional development, and other levers can be traded when salary hits a ceiling
money_quotes: We overwhelmingly suck at it. We have turned sucking at it into a perverse badge of virtue.
You can trivially pick up $5,000 in salary negotiations just by sucking less.
Virtually any amount of money available to you personally is mouse droppings to your prospective employer.
The absolute worst outcome of negotiating an offer in good faith is that you will get exactly the contents of that offer.
Volunteering a number when asked says the same thing to people with hiring authority that flunking FizzBuzz says to an engineer.
Everyone in this discussion is a businessman. Some might call themselves 'regular employees,' which just means they're businessmen with self-confidence issues and poor business skills.
Bob is not spending Bob's money to hire you. Bob is spending Bob's budget.
Resumes are an institution created to mean that no one has to read resumes.
posts.key_insight: Salary negotiation is a brief, high-leverage skill that engineers irrationally avoid, yet the asymmetry between what $5,000 means to you versus your employer means even modest negotiation effort yields outsized career-long financial returns.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he stopped self-hosting his WordPress blog and moved to WPEngine's managed hosting. After years of fighting Apache KeepAlive issues, WordPress security vulnerabilities, server crashes during traffic spikes, and routine maintenance that consumed billable weeks of his time, he was convinced by WPEngine founder Jason Cohen's technical expertise and the service's superior infrastructure. The migration resulted in faster page loads, zero downtime during high-traffic events, and freed him to focus on his actual businesses instead of server administration.
key_points: Apache's KeepAlive setting was the root cause of his blog crashing under modest traffic loads (10K-100K pageviews), and turning it off was the single biggest stability improvement
Self-hosting WordPress consumed billable weeks of time on security hardening, patching, backups, and infrastructure maintenance that produced zero business value
WPEngine's architecture (load balancer → Varnish → Nginx → Apache) was so superior that it actually reversed his KeepAlive advice — in a properly architected stack, you want KeepAlive on
The $2,500/year hosting cost was trivial compared to the opportunity cost of time spent on server administration instead of running businesses
Getting technically corrected and re-educated by Jason Cohen on infrastructure architecture was the pivotal moment that changed his mind
money_quotes: I've probably lost in excess of 100,000 readers over the years for the blog being down.
If it weren't the best blogging software available I'd take a hammer to my backups then burn the shards and bury them on sanctified ground so that they never troubled the world again.
You know how often I talk to software company CEOs and get not just corrected but destroyed and then re-educated about a point of technical fact? Suffice it to say it made an impression.
Hosting WordPress has been a black hole of my time.
$2,500 a year for blog hosting sounds a bit on the high side, but it is honestly nothing against the amount of time that I will no longer have to invest supporting this sucker.
I love being out of the hosting business. I never intended on being in it in the first place — it was always just something I needed to do to write for people.
posts.key_insight: The opportunity cost of self-hosting infrastructure you don't need to control vastly exceeds the dollar cost of paying someone who has solved the problem properly.

posts.summary: McKenzie describes commissioning a full redesign of Bingo Card Creator and A/B testing it against the old version. The redesign dramatically improved trial signups and user success rates, with 45% more visitors successfully creating bingo cards. However, sales remained completely unchanged because fewer users hit the trial limitations that trigger purchases. The result illustrates why big-bang A/B tests are frustrating: when dozens of changes ship together, isolating the cause of any observed effect is nearly impossible. He also notes that users credited the purely cosmetic redesign with imaginary performance improvements and new features.
key_points: A full site redesign increased trial signups by ~10% and user success through the funnel by 15%, compounding to 45% more users completing the core task — but sales were an exact null result (13/13 split in one week)
The conversion-to-purchase rate dropped precisely enough to cancel out all upstream gains, likely because fewer users hit the 15-card trial limit that drives purchases
Big-bang A/B tests are problematic because bundling dozens of changes makes it impossible to isolate which specific change caused which effect
Users attributed completely imaginary improvements (performance, Epson printer compatibility) to what was purely a cosmetic redesign with zero changes to business logic
If reskins make software feel 'new' and users perceive new software as worth paying for, that suggests a viable marketing strategy regardless of whether engineers approve
money_quotes: The first rule of A/B testing is that everything you know is wrong.
Teachers are so successful with the newly redesigned BCC that, out of any 100 using the software, less of them decide to purchase it.

It is actually functionally identical to the old version — not one line of model code or business logic changed — but I have received many compliments about the wonderful new features, performance increase, and improved compatibility with Epson printers.

Before you laugh, consider that probably 95% of software businesses don't A/B test yet, so my users are in good company making inferences from observed behavior changes over time even when their explanation for the changes has no relationship to reality whatsoever.

If new software is assumed to be worth money and reskins make software 'new' in the minds of the only people that matter (users), that probably suggests a viable marketing strategy. Your engineers don't have to like it.
This is one of the reasons I hate big-bang A/B tests — when you have a huge batch like that, isolating the exact cause of any observed effect is difficult.
If you're working with mature software and have already grabbed your low hanging fruit, the magnitude of that improvement is staggering.
posts.key_insight: Improving user experience and success metrics does not automatically translate to improved revenue — a redesign that made 45% more users succeed also removed the friction that caused them to pay.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie, Keith Perhac, and Amy Hoy discuss the trajectory from consulting to product businesses, pricing strategies for SaaS companies selling to businesses, and the lifestyle benefits of running your own software company. Amy describes building Freckle and her 30x500 course after leaving consulting, emphasizing that developers and designers absolutely do buy software despite conventional wisdom. The conversation covers why SaaS pricing grids should include high-tier enterprise plans, why selling to businesses beats B2C, and why competitors validate rather than threaten a market. They conclude with a philosophical discussion about work-life balance, arguing that the culture of overwork is self-imposed and that product businesses can generate substantial revenue on remarkably few hours of actual work.
key_points: Businesses are not price-sensitive the way individuals are — purchases under $500 often require zero signatures, making SaaS pricing grids that top out at $20-30 a missed opportunity
The conventional wisdom that 'developers don't buy software' is demonstrably false, as evidenced by GitHub, Basecamp, FreshBooks, Harvest, and dozens of other successful developer-focused products
Competitors are validation, not threats — a saturated market means proven demand, and a small company only needs to serve the segment being poorly served by incumbents
Selling to businesses lets you price on value (time saved, revenue generated) rather than competing on cost, which is fundamentally more sustainable

The culture of overwork in startups and corporate jobs is largely self-imposed cargo-culting — most people have only 2-4 genuinely productive hours per day, and successful product businesses can thrive on minimal weekly hours
money_quotes: If you routinely hang around people who run businesses, your perception of the world is going to get influenced by people who run businesses, and if you're routinely hanging around people who will perpetually have a business some day you have a risk of that warping your perspective on things.

Anybody who's doing a SaaS pricing grid where the top price tops out at $20 or $30 should really put anything you need to just get an enterprise level and price it at $250 or $500. This is free money.
Competitors are a wonderful thing because it's an engraved invitation from God that tells you that there's money to be made in a particular place.

People who are spending essentially other people's money, it just comes out of a budget so it doesn't matter to them whether it's 200 or 250 or 500. As long as it still only requires one signature or zero signatures it's whatever it is.
Our business grew even when I was on practically bed rest, and that was a really transformative moment for me.

People will happily pay you money, even if you're not working on the product every day, because people don't care if you're working on the product every day, they only care what they're getting out of it.

If you compare yourself to other people and think you're not successful unless you're beating them by some metric you will generally be less happy than you are if you're comparing yourself against either where you were previously or what your goals are.
So it's cargo-culting mixed with social contagion.
posts.key_insight: Product businesses selling to other businesses on value — not cost — can generate life-changing revenue on remarkably few hours of work, but founders must overcome both cultural pressure to overwork and the instinct to underprice.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that email is the most effective broadcast channel for business purposes, yet most geeks and software developers dramatically underutilize it due to misguided psychological blocks. Drawing on his own experience launching a mailing list and six years of blogging, he demonstrates that email vastly outperforms blog posts and RSS feeds in open rates, click-through rates, and actual behavior change. He shows how email keeps leads warm, builds trust through drip campaigns, and can extract meaningful revenue from existing customers. The post doubles as both a persuasive essay and a launch announcement for his own training email list.
key_points: Email open rates (60%) and click-through rates (15%) dramatically outperform blog post engagement, where links get maybe 1-3% clicks
Content presentation shapes perceived value — calling something a 'free PDF' versus a 'best-selling business manifesto' changes how people evaluate identical content
Drip email campaigns can scale the high-touch sales funnel while building trust, as demonstrated by the WPEngine campaign that educated prospects over eight emails
People are terrible at introspecting about their own behavior with email — most geeks claim to hate marketing email while demonstrably responding to it
Email keeps leads warm over time, counteracting the sharp decay in prospect value that occurs without regular contact
money_quotes: Email is the best broadcast channel ever invented for business purposes.
These perceptions were catastrophically wrong.
Reading and responding to email is my effing job.
Blog posts are very poor at changing actual behavior.
If you pay nothing for expert advice you will value it at epsilon more than nothing, if you pay five figures for it you will clear your schedule and implement recommendations within the day.
People are statistically speaking terrible at introspection.
Almost every first-class e-commerce company treats their house email campaigns like they are the goose that lays the golden eggs, chiefly because they are.
The probable value of a prospect goes down over time, the provable value of a customer goes up over time.
posts.key_insight: Email massively outperforms blogs, social media, and RSS for driving actual behavior change and revenue because it lands in a workflow people are already committed to reading, rather than competing with thousands of equivalent-looking items in a feed.

posts.summary: McKenzie details his experience integrating Stripe as a payment processor for Bingo Card Creator, praising its API design, developer experience, and support quality. He then walks through a series of A/B tests comparing Stripe's on-site credit card checkout against PayPal/Google Checkout off-site options, finding that customers overwhelmingly prefer credit cards (90% vs 5% each for PayPal and Google Checkout). The breakthrough came not from checkout design alone but from reducing the free trial limit from 15 to 8 cards, which combined synergistically with the redesigned Stripe-powered checkout to produce massive revenue gains. Year-over-year sales increased 38-108% across several months. He credits the combination of good infrastructure (Stripe), professional design, and rigorous A/B testing for the results.
key_points: Stripe's API integration took only 29 lines of code and 3 hours to go live, versus weeks with most payment providers — making transaction fee comparisons irrelevant when integration costs dwarf the savings
When given the choice, 90% of customers chose credit card checkout over PayPal or Google Checkout, but simply adding credit cards didn't initially increase total conversions
Reducing the free trial limit from 15 to 8 cards produced a synergistic effect with the redesigned checkout, nearly doubling conversion rates (1.04% to 1.89%) with 99% statistical confidence
Stripe's co-founder personally debugged a dependency conflict using git bisect and shipped a fix for the ruby library — exemplifying developer-first support culture
The combination of infrastructure quality, professional design, and data-driven decision making compounded into year-over-year revenue increases of 33-108%
money_quotes: We sell products which have margins that come very close to 100%, and saving pennies on transactions to spend tens of thousands on integration costs or to shave full percentages off our conversion rates is absolute madness.
A/B testing prints money. So does having sufficient metrics in place such that one knows where the high priority places to A/B test are.
I am not easily emotionally moved by git command lines, but this is clearly somebody who understands me and what I need in life.
That email was signed by the co-founder. Patrick Collison, when he isn't running a payments company, apparently found time to verse himself in arcane git commands.
It's like they found the sweet spot between 'Computers can make decisions in lightning-speed at scale' and 'Humans can actually be trusted with discretion.'
This is really, really important for developers because you get end-to-end control of the user experience.

I occasionally write about companies which I use in my business and I feel are relevant to you guys. Stripe isn't a client. I haven't accepted anything of value from them… well, OK, technically speaking they have deposited $30,000 into my bank account, but you know what I mean.
So I tested the stupidest thing that could possibly work to get more people to hit the trial limitations: decrease people's free quota from 15 cards to 8 cards.
posts.key_insight: Developer experience and end-to-end control of the checkout flow matter far more than transaction fees, because the revenue gains from better conversion optimization dwarf any savings on processing costs.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that SaaS companies drastically undercharge by using linear per-unit pricing when customer value scales exponentially with usage. Using Server Density as a case study, he shows how switching from per-server variable pricing to a tiered plan model ($99/$299/$499) doubled revenue per visitor cohort. The old $11/server model attracted price-sensitive hobbyists who generated disproportionate support costs, while the new tiers segmented customers by business seriousness. He emphasizes that most customers don't care about price, and those who do are disproportionately terrible customers. The piece advocates for radical pricing restructurings over incremental tweaks.
key_points: Linear per-unit pricing fundamentally misaligns with customer value: a 7-server company gets 50x the value of a 1-server company, not 7x, yet pays only 7x more
Switching from variable per-server pricing to 3-4 tiered plans doubled Server Density's monthly revenue per visitor cohort despite reducing free trial signups by 25%
Low price points attract pathological customers who value their own time at zero, generate excessive support load, and complain the most
Tiered pricing is easier to buy (fewer decisions), more predictable, and better aligned with business goals than configurable per-unit pricing
Price testing with radical packaging restructurings moves the needle far more than tweaking plan prices by $10
money_quotes: You don't get 7x the value from server monitoring if you increase your server fleet by 7x, you get (at least) 50x the value.
Most customers do not care about price. Those that do are disproportionately terrible customers.
They'll complain that Server Density costs infinity percent more than OSS, because they value their own time at zero.
At this point, there's someone in the audience saying 'That's illegal!' That person is just plain wrong. There is no carbon in a water molecule, and price testing is not illegal.
Serious businesses generate a lot of money no matter how many servers they have.
Virtually no one gets pricing right on the first try.
The variable pricing customers largely a) don't want to talk about things and b) need a password reset right now WTF is taking so long.

Are $13 accounts for server monitoring likely to be experienced sysadmins doing meaningful work for businesses who will solve their own problems and pay without complaint every month? No, they're going to be the worst possible pathological customers.
posts.key_insight: SaaS pricing should scale with customer value, not usage units — switching from linear per-unit pricing to tiered plans segments away pathological customers and captures the exponential value serious businesses derive from your product.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Ramit Sethi discuss practical strategies for acquiring first consulting clients, emphasizing that most business is won through relationships and research long before any formal pitch. They argue that engineers systematically undercharge and fail to communicate in terms clients care about — business outcomes rather than technical details. The conversation covers how deep customer research reveals the exact language and pain points that make sales inevitable, why charging too little destroys credibility, and how to position yourself as someone who understands the client's business at a visceral level. They stress that the gap between commodity freelancing and premium consulting is primarily about repositioning and communication, not technical skill.
key_points: 85% of winning a consulting engagement happens before you sit down in the room — through relationships, research, and positioning
Radically undercharging for early work destroys both income and credibility; $10/hour signals you're not worth taking seriously at the CEO level
Engineers fail at selling because they talk about technology instead of business outcomes — no client wakes up thinking they have a WordPress problem, they have a business problem
Deep customer research using their exact words is what separates premium offerings from commodity ones, and statistical significance is irrelevant in qualitative customer research
If everyone in an industry does something a particular way, the radical notion is that they're not all simultaneously making the same mistake — move from disparagement to curiosity
money_quotes: 85 percent of the work is done before you ever sit down in the room.
$10 is not a meaningful amount of money to any business.
No SEO wakes up in the morning and thinks, 'Damn, I have a WordPress problem.' They wake up in the morning and say, 'Damn, I have a business problem.'
Nothing motivates people like having their own words repeated right back to them.
Statistical significance is irrelevant when you're doing customer research.
If you can't sketch someone's business model on a napkin, I don't think you can ever sell them anything worthwhile.
I just don't give a damn about technology. I really don't. What I care about is, is my business going to generate revenue?
Focus on taking your B+ customers/prospects to be A customers. Don't focus on the D people, trying to turn them into A customers. It will never happen.
posts.key_insight: Premium consulting is sold by demonstrating deep understanding of the client's business problems in their own words, not by showcasing technical skills or competing on price.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Ramit Sethi argue that underpricing products and services attracts pathological customers who demand more while perceiving less value. Drawing from their own experiences — Ramit going from a $4.95 ebook to $12,000 courses, Patrick 7X-ing his consulting rates — they demonstrate that higher prices correlate with better customers, fewer support headaches, and greater perceived value. They discuss the psychology behind why engineers and freelancers chronically underprice, including fear of rejection and anchoring to hourly wage mental models. The core thesis is that pricing is strategic: it signals what market you play in, what customers you attract, and how seriously your work is taken.
key_points: Cheap customers generate disproportionately more support requests and unreasonable demands — Patrick's $9/month tier generated 7X the support load of his $199/month tier
Engineers underprice because they anchor to hourly wage mental models instead of framing their work as strategic business investments with measurable ROI
Quoting weekly rates instead of hourly rates moves you into a different 'taxonomy of value' where you're compared against strategic initiatives rather than labor costs
Disqualifying bad-fit customers and being willing to turn down money signals trustworthiness and actually increases your perceived value to the right clients
You can price for free or price for dear, but never price for cheap — cheap pricing creates the worst of all worlds with maximum headaches and minimum revenue
money_quotes: You can price it for free or you can price it for dear, but never price it for cheap.

If you are genuinely creating value for businesses, you are no longer in the 'trade defined small units of time for defined small units of money' business model. This is what most employees do. This is how you have spent most of your life working. You have left that life behind.
People have a preconceived notion of the proper cost of one hour of time.
If I know my product is good, if I have tested results, it's my obligation to get it out to the market.
I have never desired to be a peon, so I have always made a point of not talking like a peon.
A lot of fantastic value can be delivered with capable, workmanlike coding.
If you're scheduled at or near capacity, charge more. If losing a client would not meaningfully impact your standard of living, charge more.
We could pay that out of the petty cash.
posts.key_insight: Pricing is not just a revenue decision but a customer-selection mechanism: higher prices attract customers who perceive more value, demand less support, and create better outcomes for everyone involved.

posts.summary: This podcast transcript features Patrick McKenzie, Keith Perhac, and Brennan Dunn discussing how freelancers and consultants can dramatically increase their rates by repositioning themselves as business consultants rather than commodity programmers. They cover the mechanics of scaling from solo consulting to a multi-employee consultancy, including the harsh realities of cash flow, payroll obligations, and the gap between charge-out rates and employee costs. The conversation also explores using drip email campaigns and content marketing to build trust with prospects, making sales discussions feel like foregone conclusions rather than pitches. Throughout, they emphasize that the difference between low-rate and high-rate consultants is not skill but how they communicate value.
key_points: Consultants who own business objectives and communicate value in business terms can charge dramatically more than commodity programmers with identical technical skills
Scaling from solo consulting to a multi-employee consultancy requires substantial cash reserves, as fixed payroll costs combined with variable income creates dangerous cash flow gaps
Weekly billing is superior to hourly billing because it eliminates micromanagement, prevents time-tracking leakage, and makes rate negotiations less damaging to the bottom line
Drip email campaigns that educate prospects over weeks create trust and position you as the benchmark expert, making the eventual sales discussion a foregone conclusion rather than a pitch
The chasm between $50-75/hour and $150+/hour consultants is explained almost entirely by how they communicate value and refuse to let fear rule their pricing
money_quotes: Programmers build tools. Business consultants solve problems.

It is almost surreal how the people charging three times as much are not three times as better developers. The reason for the huge discrepancy is how they communicate their value and how they refuse to allow fear and uncertainty to rule their businesses.
Reframe your value in a way that the client can understand.
This math is catastrophically wrong.
Shut up. Never offer free work ever again.
Time tracking is a technological/process patch on top of a hole in the business model.
If you're in the position of educating someone, you largely get to determine their outlook on all further things in that space.
Everybody, regardless of whether they say they hate getting email or not or they hate being marketed to, is happy to receive things which produce genuine value for them.
posts.key_insight: The gap between low-rate and high-rate consultants is not technical skill but the ability to position yourself as someone who delivers measurable business outcomes rather than selling hours of labor.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his book on conversion optimization, SEO, and A/B testing for software companies, published through Hyperink. The book compiles approximately 20 of his best blog essays with 4 new essays, covering selling software, increasing conversions, and SEO strategy. He explains he avoided traditional publishing because the economics didn't make sense, but Hyperink's more equitable royalty model and modern workflow convinced him. The book targets solo software entrepreneurs, SaaS marketing/engineering teams, and anyone who wanted his blog distilled into a curated collection.
key_points: Traditional publishing economics are broken for niche technical authors — a likely $5,000 advance that fails to earn out isn't worth months of work
Hyperink represents a Publishing 2.0 model with more equitable royalty splits and workflows built on modern tools rather than legacy publishing infrastructure
The book includes never-before-seen accounts of abysmal failure, because failure teaches as much as success
The target audience is practitioners — people with working products who need to optimize marketing outcomes, not people still building products
money_quotes: The notion of spending months of work with a publisher to write a book that would likely fail to earn-out a $5,000 advance seemed to be a silly thing to do just to put "published author" on my resume.
Royalties are split more equitably than 93-7-but-with-accounting-practices-that-would-make-the-RIAA-proud.
I have the design sense of an addlebrained squirrel who fell into the Christmas eggnog and drowned.
Rather than reading through 600 posts in chronological order, could you just distill your blog down into the best twenty posts and categorize them for me? My time isn't totally valueless.
Never-before-seen tales of abysmal failure, because that sometimes teaches as much as the successes.

If you've considered trying your hand at writing a book but, like me, thought the traditional publishing industry is largely toxic and exploitative by construction, I'd encourage you to give them a whirl.
posts.key_insight: Traditional publishing economics are misaligned with niche technical expertise, but curating existing high-value content through modern publishing platforms can create a viable book without the typical author exploitation.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2012 year-in-review details how his profits roughly tripled from ~$70k to ~$200k across multiple business lines. Bingo Card Creator had its best year despite maintenance mode, driven by A/B testing and Stripe integration boosting conversion rates. His consulting practice grew by raising rates rather than hours, charging five-figure weekly rates for SaaS companies. He launched a productized consulting video course on lifecycle emails that earned ~$60k against a $20k target. Appointment Reminder quadrupled its monthly revenue run rate and landed first enterprise contracts, though it continued to receive less attention than planned. He closes with 2013 goals including systematizing sales, potentially hiring, and wrestling with whether to go all-in on AR.
key_points: A/B testing a site redesign and adding Stripe payments massively boosted Bingo Card Creator's conversion rates, increasing profits 66% on a business in maintenance mode
Consulting revenue hit ~$140k driven entirely by rate increases rather than more hours worked, with five-figure weekly rates for SaaS companies
Productized consulting via a video course on lifecycle emails earned ~$60k against a $20k target, demonstrating the power of a warmed email list
The most expensive Appointment Reminder plan ($200/month) accounted for over 1/3 of revenue after repricing eliminated a too-cheap $9 tier
Recurring theme of knowing what to do but struggling with execution and motivation, from dreading customer support to avoiding voicemail to losing weeks to League of Legends
money_quotes: Profits roughly tripled from ~$70k to ~$200k, exclusive of Appointment Reminder.
Wage per Hour: Approximately $1,000, given that I worked for approximately 15 hours integrating the new design and spend approximately 20 minutes a week doing support.

Not having investors or employees means that I have 100% control over the schedule, and AR has frequently lost out to pressing matters like consulting engagements, League of Legends matches, or wonderful opportunities to clean drains around my apartment.

Here's a replicatable strategy for making several hundred thousand dollars with a single email: start with a revenue base of $X million a year. Email all customers asking them to switch from monthly billing to annual billing.
We made so much money the accountant/bank called us to complain.
The $9 personal plan was a mistake, and I knew that when I created it, and even despite that I suffered a year and a half of it anyhow.
Being picky and choosy has been a major win for me, and as time goes on I'm getting better at it.
When you have a warmed email list of people who have pre-existing reasons to like what you have to say, doing product launches is a lot easier than 'Build it and pray that they'll come.'
posts.key_insight: Tripling profits came not from working harder but from relentlessly optimizing conversion rates, raising consulting rates, and productizing expertise — demonstrating that leverage beats effort at every stage of a software business.

posts.summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive breakdown of the January 2013 Ruby on Rails YAML deserialization vulnerabilities that allowed remote code execution on virtually any Rails application connected to the internet. He argues the severity is apocalyptic and that thousands of applications will be compromised for years, not just those under active development but forgotten staging servers, localhost instances, and downstream dependencies. He extends the analysis beyond Rails shops to show how any startup consuming services from Rails-based providers is also at risk. The post concludes with practical advice on security incident response, maintaining application inventories, and preparing for what he predicts will be an ongoing cascade of related vulnerabilities.
key_points: YAML deserialization in Rails enabled remote code execution on essentially every unpatched Rails app, exploitable in a fully automated manner requiring zero knowledge of the target
The blast radius extends far beyond Rails shops: any startup embedding JavaScript from, authenticating through, or sharing passwords with a Rails-based service is transitively compromised
Losing one server effectively means losing every server, as attackers pivot from the compromised machine to the rest of the network
Security vulnerabilities cluster in time because new exploit techniques unlock entire categories of related bugs, and increased attention draws more researchers to the same codebase
Every internet-facing application needs a security contact page, an incident response plan, and someone carrying a pager for drop-everything security patches
money_quotes: Fire four HTTP requests at every server on the Internet: if the server is added to your botnet, it was running a vulnerable version of Ruby on Rails.
Nobody needs to care about your application to compromise it using these vulnerabilities.
If you lose one server, you will lose every server, with very high confidence.
Security vulnerabilities often give you negative several hours notice and get worse for every minute left unchecked.
It would suck much, much worse if the Bad Guys had found these first and just proceeded to remote-own every Rails app on the Internet. That is, by the way, an achievable scenario.
Any page on the Internet which serves Javascript can, currently, root your Macbook if it is running an out-of-date Rails on it.
I will bet $1,000 at 100-to-1 odds that Rails suffers another code execution vulnerability before the end of January.
That is primarily because this is a #$()#%ing disaster.
posts.key_insight: Catastrophic framework vulnerabilities don't just threaten direct users — they cascade through the entire ecosystem of services, dependencies, and shared credentials, making every startup's security posture only as strong as the weakest link in its supply chain.

posts.summary: This podcast transcript covers the story of Stormpulse, a bootstrapped weather risk management SaaS that grew from a side project to being used in the White House Situation Room. Patrick McKenzie interviews founder Matt Wensing about the company's transition from freemium (6.5M free users) to a paid B2B model, their experience raising angel funding outside Silicon Valley, and the mechanics of selling enterprise software. The conversation covers convertible notes, the role of social proof in fundraising, why most people shouldn't angel invest, and how founders have unique advantages in enterprise sales. McKenzie and Wensing both emphasize that B2B software is chronically underpriced and that founders should charge far more than their instincts suggest.
key_points: Stormpulse bootstrapped to White House adoption through product quality alone — the Situation Room signed up via self-serve, proving that great products can sell themselves even to the most demanding customers

Fundraising follows a social proof cascade: Matt spoke to ~80 people, got almost all no's early, then yes's accelerated as momentum built — described in 65 characters as 'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNYNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNYNNNNYNNYYYYYYYYYYY'
Traction means evidence you can capture value at a rate that moves the business forward — 6.5M free users didn't count as traction until they converted to ~1,000 paying customers
Angel investing has terrible expected returns for most people; the median VC return is probably negative, and amateur investors picking from non-cream-of-the-crop companies will fare worse
Founders have an enormous advantage in enterprise sales because they can speak with authority about both business and technical details, making customers feel they're getting special attention
money_quotes: My definition of traction, if I can throw it out there, is evidence that you can capture value at a rate that moves the business forward.
I want to disabuse you from that notion, right now. For the vast majority of people listening to this, angel investment is not a good idea.
Most people will get wiped out totally.
One takeaway from this podcast, for anybody attempting to run a business on $3.95 a month, don't.
The one positive thing that I'll say about angel investing is at least it's better than Bitcoin.
No is the default, it has to be.
If KissMetrics costs $150 a month, it's done. If it increases sales by five percent over the year, it pays for itself in perpetuity.
The first time I got a check from somebody that was a real company, like a Fortune 500 company, paying an invoice that I had just made up was like this moment of clarity.
posts.key_insight: Traction isn't vanity metrics — it's evidence you can capture value at an accelerating rate, and the path from bootstrapping to funded growth requires mastering social proof both for fundraising and enterprise sales.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie's Microconf 2012 talk covers systems-based marketing for software companies, aimed at engineers who'd rather build than sell. He argues that engineers have a natural advantage in building scalable marketing systems because they can exploit platforms like Google, Twitter, and app stores programmatically. The talk covers practical tactics including SaaS pricing strategy (charge more, segment by customer value, name plans carefully), automated marketing email sequences that educate then sell, and optimizing the first-run experience of software to dramatically improve activation rates. He also briefly discusses his consulting practice, where he commands $20,000/week by framing his work as revenue generation rather than hourly development labor.
key_points: A job turns time into money, but a business turns systems into money — engineers should build marketing systems, not do marketing manually

SaaS companies should charge dramatically more: the highest-priced plan typically generates 33-50% of revenue because enterprise buyers spend corporate budgets without pain, and cheaper plans attract pathological customers with disproportionate support costs

Email marketing is the highest-leverage channel for software companies — it's actually read by decision-makers, sits alongside important work in their inbox, and teachers literally wrote in asking for missing newsletters
The first five minutes of software use determines whether 40-60% of users ever return, so script that experience like the invasion of Normandy rather than dropping users on a blank screen
Validate business ideas by paying potential customers for their time and showing iPad mockups — if they don't say 'shut up and take my money,' you haven't found a real problem
money_quotes: A job is a system that turns time into money, and a business is a system that turns systems into money.
Ford might well be a company that sells cars, but T-Corp is a company that builds organizations that builds cars.
Charge more, charge more, charge more, charge more, charge more. Anyone have a question about pricing? You should charge more.
I'm a massage therapist. If my hands are on a telephone, they're not on someone's back, and if my hands aren't on someone's back, I'm not making money.
At 99 cents, people have very unreasonable expectations. 'What? This flashlight app didn't do my taxes! One stars!'
You raise prices, and you deal with less crazy people.
You script their first five minutes like it is the invasion of Normandy. 'You are going to do this, and then you are going to do this, and then you are going to be F-ing happy.'
If someone says, 'That's kind of interesting, tell me when that exists,' you have not successfully identified a problem that people actually have.
posts.key_insight: Engineers have a unique advantage in business because they can build automated systems for marketing, sales, and customer activation — the companies that win are the ones that systematize everything from email sequences to first-run experiences rather than doing marketing manually.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie presents a comprehensive guide to selling software to businesses, covering low-touch, high-touch, and hybrid sales models. He argues that SaaS companies should price based on customer perceived value rather than cost, use tiered pricing with strategic plan names, and offer annual billing to improve cash flow and reduce churn. He demonstrates how product tours, lifecycle email campaigns, and drip sequences can dramatically improve conversion rates. He also shares how he personally won enterprise hospital contracts over an 800-pound gorilla competitor by investing time in relationships, and teaches specific tactics for navigating purchasing departments and enterprise sales cycles.
key_points: Price based on customer perceived value, not cost — an HVAC company pays $200/month for $1.96 worth of phone calls because the business value is enormous
Metered billing is an anti-pattern for low-touch SaaS because customers have a high perceived cost associated with uncertainty and will overpay for predictability
Product tours that demonstrate one awesome improvement in the first five minutes can reduce first-day abandonment from 60% and increase sales by 30%
Annual billing offered via email to existing customers with a small discount can convert 10-25% of monthly customers, generating massive upfront cash flow
Enterprise sales can be won by small operators who invest personal attention — the magic answer to almost any enterprise request is 'I can do that with a one-year commitment'
money_quotes: Your customers do not perceive the value of your applications as being dumb telephone pipes. If they wanted dumb telephone pipes, they could get them from AT&T for like $14 a month.
It cost me $1.96 to make those phone calls for him for last month, but I'm putting his daughter through Harvard.
Customers have a high perceived cost associated with uncertainty.
People hate uncertainty so much that they will overpay to never have to worry about it.
You are not taking your customer's money. You are taking the business's money. No customer feels a pain in their life.
If I'm going to submit this to my boss and it says 'hobbyist,' we're an enterprise. We need the enterprise plan.
You don't understand. It's his baby. You take care of your baby. If we ever have a problem with it, we know who we're going to be talking to.

Never give people a break on the unit price, because the purchasing department is only scored against whether they get the purchase in at lower than the best unit price that they have ever seen or gotten from you.
posts.key_insight: Software companies massively underprice by anchoring to their costs rather than to the business value customers receive, and the hybrid sales model — combining low-touch self-serve with high-touch enterprise engagement — is the most underexploited opportunity in SaaS.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie argues urgently that businesses running Rails 2.3 applications face certain security compromise since Rails Core has dropped support, and presents Rails LTS — a commercially supported fork by German consultancy Makandra — as the practical solution. He explains that upgrading to Rails 3/4 is prohibitively expensive for many businesses, self-maintaining a fork requires expertise most companies lack, and relying on informal forks like GitHub's offers no guarantees. McKenzie bankrolled Rails LTS as its anchor customer with $10,000, negotiating a model where patches are open-sourced after a 10-day delay. The post systematically dismantles objections to paying for the service, framing it as insurance rather than paying for code.
key_points: Rails 2.3 is completely unsupported and every unpatched Rails 2.3 server will inevitably be compromised — this is not a risk but a certainty
Upgrading from Rails 2.3 to Rails 3 is a serious engineering project that can cost $20,000 to six figures, making it impractical for many businesses with legacy apps
Rails Core declined to support 2.3 even when offered $10,000, which is their prerogative as an OSS project funded by companies with different priorities
Rails LTS provides a commercially supported fork with contractual guarantees on patch timelines, costing ~$200/month — roughly 1-2 engineer-hours
The arrangement benefits the community: all patches are open-sourced under MIT license after a 10-day delay, creating something that didn't exist before
money_quotes: If any server operated by your company is on an unpatched version of 2.3, that server will be compromised by the adversary.

You're not really paying money for OSS code which already exists, you're buying the guarantee that new code gets written at non-deterministic times in the future before the lack of it causes an emergency for your business.
Data security is serious business for many people.
It also could very well result in firings, so I wouldn't rush to this option.

The hypothetical person saying this is a textbook pathological customer: they're both deeply irrational and likely to be far, far, far too much headache for professional Rails engineers to have to deal with.
But horsepuckey, you're not going to write free patches for Rails 2.3, or you'd have commits you could point to demonstrating your willingness to do this over the last 6 months.

You can either pay your engineers to continue monitoring and patching Rails for you, or you can buy Rails LTS. Your call. Their entry-level plan costs ~$200 a month, which is approximately the cost of one or two engineer-hours.
Or you can just wait until your server gets owned, because that reliably makes people discover budget for security.
posts.key_insight: When open source projects drop support for versions your business depends on, paying for commercial maintenance is not a luxury but a basic cost of doing business — far cheaper than the inevitable security breach.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss strategies for transitioning from consulting to product-based revenue streams. Patrick explains why he wound down his consulting business after a Fortune 500 engagement collapsed and took months of his pipeline with it, highlighting the fragility of consulting income. They cover multiple productization models: retainer agreements with existing clients, licensing pre-written code, creating info-products (courses and books) from consulting expertise, and building products for other companies' platforms. The conversation emphasizes that retainer agreements would have been 'transformatively good' for Patrick's consulting business, and that even 90% of paid info-product content may be freely available but the curation and packaging create enormous value.
key_points: Retainer agreements transform feast-or-famine consulting into predictable recurring revenue by charging clients a fraction of your weekly rate monthly to maintain and iterate on delivered engagements
Info-products aren't cannibalizing consulting — they're gateway drugs that demonstrate expertise and drive higher-value consulting engagements
Building products for other companies' platforms (Twilio, AWeber, Infusionsoft) gives you access to their audience, as platform companies are incentivized to promote anything that drives adoption
Tiered pricing (1X, 2.2X, 5X) with increasing levels of access and guaranteed outcomes captures value from both price-sensitive and premium buyers
Having 300 customers instead of 3 clients means you no longer have bosses — you have people whose business you can take or leave
money_quotes: The first way to never get screwed about a $10,000 check is to not really need it.
If you are a W-2 employee, you have one boss. If you move into consulting and have three clients, who are each responsible for about one third of your billing, you now have three bosses.
Consulting clients really want to buy dramatic blog post readings as a service.
People who don't have employees don't understand how expensive employees are.
As an expert, you don't know what other people don't know.
They're going to be writing a $10,000 payroll check with the memo 'Reading free information on the Internet.'
Retainer agreements would have been absolutely transformatively good for my consulting business.
You don't phrase things as in, 'I don't have time,' because you always have time. You say, 'It is not a priority.'
posts.key_insight: Consultants should systematically convert one-time engagements into recurring revenue through retainer agreements, licensed tools, and info-products extracted from their existing expertise, because consulting without residual income is inherently fragile.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie and Nathan Barry discuss the strategy of 'teaching as marketing' — building an audience and credibility by publicly sharing expertise, then monetizing through productized knowledge (books, courses, email lists). They cover how Nathan launched multiple successful books without being 'Internet famous,' how tiered pricing packages can double or triple revenue compared to single-price offerings, and why email lists are the most valuable business asset for independent creators. The conversation emphasizes that you don't need celebrity status to build authority — consistent teaching creates perceived expertise that translates directly into sales.
key_points: Teaching publicly builds authority and audience simultaneously — the difference between two equally skilled people is that one shared knowledge while the other kept it private
Tiered pricing packages (e.g., $39/$99/$249) let customers self-segment by value received, consistently doubling or tripling revenue versus a single price point
Email lists are the most valuable business asset for independent creators — they function as 'recurring revenue for traffic' and provide a reliable, owned distribution channel

Critics who say 'nobody will pay for information' are almost never the target buyers — pricing objections from $30/hour freelancers are irrelevant when selling to businesses where the product cost rounds to zero
You don't get real market validation feedback unless you actually ask for money — verbal interest and willingness to pay are completely different things
money_quotes: The Internet has a short temper and a long memory.
Anyone who has employees, and accordingly must pay salaries every two weeks regardless of what they are doing, is literally incapable of finding things for free on the Internet.

We are literally incapable of tracking a number this low on our systems. The expected value of a single lead exceeds the price of this course by several times. That is making it difficult for me to convince my boss to buy it.
This is like the vegetarians complaining about the prices from the hot dog vendors.

There exists that psychograph of people who just are fundamentally unhappy with the notion that products sell. They're empirically not that good at predicting that because all products ever have sold.
Every time I've seen multiple packages in use, both when I do it and when other people do it, it consistently doubles revenue if not triples revenue.
You don't get real feedback unless you ask for money.
Pricing is generally not something you should ask customers for. That's something you should announce to them and get the up-vote or down-vote on it.
posts.key_insight: Building an audience through consistent public teaching creates authority and trust that converts directly into product sales, and smart packaging with tiered pricing lets customers self-segment so you capture value proportional to what each buyer receives.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his 2013 business year across four lines of business: Bingo Card Creator (maintenance mode, sales down 23%), Appointment Reminder (revenue doubled), consulting (quit mid-year), and productized consulting (~$62k revenue). Total disclosed Kalzumeus Software revenue was ~$112k with ~$60k profit, though Appointment Reminder numbers are kept private and now dominate his income. The year was marked by a severe cashflow crunch caused by poorly timed consulting exit coinciding with tax obligations, capital investments, and seasonal lows, which contributed to serious health problems. Despite the difficulties, he successfully transitioned from consulting to productized consulting, outsourced BCC operations, and set ambitious goals for 2014.
key_points: Quitting consulting was the right strategic decision but catastrophically poor in execution timing, creating a perfect storm cashflow crunch when tax liabilities, slow season, and capital investments all collided with zero consulting revenue

Appointment Reminder doubled revenue year-over-year despite receiving only ~4 weeks of full-time development effort, demonstrating product-market fit but also massive unrealized potential from lack of focus
Successfully outsourced BCC customer support and A/B testing, achieving ~$1,000/hour effective wage on the ~20 hours spent, proving that systematizing operations enables profitable neglect

Productized consulting generated ~$62k across books, courses, and workshops, validating the model of converting consulting expertise into scalable products that free you from the billable-hours treadmill
Fear of the unknown prevented hiring for Appointment Reminder, revealing that the bottleneck in growing a solo business is often psychological rather than financial or operational
money_quotes: I anticipate that phone call to take 5 minutes or less. $2,400 is approximately equal to an entire month's profits of BCC. Any questions?
It turns out that my Nagging Doubt Monster is an idiot and that my skills generalize impressively well up the sophistication chain.

Quitting a consultancy with no active engagements turns out to be really easy: all you have to do is stop saying Yes. Conversely, starting a consultancy is fairly easy if you're known to be good at something with high utility to potential clients, since all you have to do is stop saying No.
I was routinely selling new engagements at e.g. $30,000 per week.

I went from having a nice cushion in my checking account and thinking 'I'm going to close the largest deal in the history of my business any week now' to owing high five figures at credit card interest rates while staring down an empty sales and product development pipeline.
Is optimizing the schedule of dentists' offices your passion? No? Then why are you committing to working on that for the next several years?
After 5 years of saying 'Thanks for the email Miriam. I'm sorry, Bingo Card Creator does not support using clip art. Have a nice day.' I was going absolutely spare.
My target for errors is zero, not 'so few that customers are unlikely to notice.'
posts.key_insight: The most dangerous moment for a solo entrepreneur is when strategic transitions collide with financial obligations, because running multiple businesses on cash-basis accounting without a balance sheet means you can't see the cliff until you're already falling off it.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie writes a detailed, unsolicited-but-permission-granted consulting workup for Tarsnap, Colin Percival's encrypted backup service.
