He argues that while Tarsnap has world-class engineering, its business operations are so poor they actively undermine its engineering mission. McKenzie proposes repositioning from 'backups for the truly paranoid' to 'backups for servers of serious professionals,' replacing metered picodollar pricing with a 3-tier SaaS model ($50/$100/$500/month), redesigning the amateur-looking website, and improving onboarding. He contends that charging more would benefit both Colin and his customers by funding critical improvements like recurring billing, monitoring, and business continuity planning.
key_points: Tarsnap's metered picodollar pricing is unpredictable, opaque, and disconnects price from value — customers can't estimate costs and businesses can't get purchase approval for unknown amounts

Tarsnap will delete all your backups if you miss two emails and fail to top up your prepaid balance, which is an unconscionable failure mode for a backup service used by businesses with serious data obligations

Repositioning from 'geek-to-geek paranoia tool' to 'serious business backup infrastructure' would unlock enterprise customers who need things like multi-user accounts, compliance documentation, and a website that won't scare their boss

Colin is giving away enormous customer surplus — the difference between what businesses would happily pay ($100+/month) and what they actually pay ($2.40/month) — and this underinvestment actively harms the product and its users
A visual redesign, tiered pricing, better onboarding, and 'Convince Your Boss' content would collectively transform Tarsnap's business without changing the core engineering
money_quotes: Tarsnap (the business) is run with less seriousness than a 6 year old's first lemonade stand.
Yes folks, Tarsnap — 'backups for the truly paranoid' — will in fact rm -rf your backups if you fail to respond to two emails.

Lemonade stands which have been in business for 5 years have the take-money-for-lemonade problem pretty much licked, and when they have occasional lemonade-for-money transactional issues, the lemonade does not retroactively turn into poison.
Metered pricing is none of predictable, transparent, or fair.

Colin is not a great engineer who is bad at business and thus compromising the financial rewards he could get from running his software company. No, Colin is in fact a great engineer who is so bad at business that it actively is compromising his engineering objectives.
I pay Dead Man's Snitch 10X as much for doing 1/1000th the work.
Tarsnap is not AWS plus a little sauce on top. AWS is a wee little implementation detail on the backend for most customers.
Memo to OSS developers: I can pay money for software licenses, even if the license is just 'MIT, but we invoice you', but I cannot just put business funds in your tip jar.
posts.key_insight: Brilliant engineering undermined by amateurish business practices doesn't just leave money on the table — it actively harms the product's users by starving the business of resources needed for critical features like recurring billing, monitoring, and business continuity.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that the Heartbleed vulnerability's unprecedented rapid response was largely due to superior marketing: a memorable name, professional logo, dedicated website, and clear writing. He contrasts this with previous critical vulnerabilities like CVE-2013-0156 (Rails YAML deserialization) that failed to spur action because they were communicated through obtuse CVE numbers and plain-text mailing list posts. The post breaks down exactly why each marketing element worked — the name was emotionally evocative and technically accurate, the website copy was tight enough for executives to grasp in 30 seconds, and the logo conveyed danger and professionalism. McKenzie concludes that the open source community's cultural resistance to marketing is actively harmful, because getting people to patch systems is itself an engineering problem.
key_points: Memorable names like 'Heartbleed' drive action where CVE numbers fail — technologists couldn't even get VPs to approve remediation for the Rails YAML vulnerability because the name communicated no urgency
The Heartbleed announcement was masterful technical writing: accurate yet comprehensible to non-technologists, leading with severity and business impact rather than burying it in technical details
A dedicated web presence with a logo and clear copy creates a canonical, citable, authoritative reference that plain-text emails on Google Groups never can

The OSS community's cultural disdain for marketing is counterproductive — if critical patches don't get adopted because the communication was obtuse, the adversaries win regardless of engineering quality

Marketing is an engineering discipline: convincing hundreds of thousands of people to act immediately on a vulnerability is a coordination problem that requires the same rigor as writing the patch itself
money_quotes: We were all racing against for loops and the prize for 2nd place was 'Our customers' security gets horribly abused.'
This makes marketing an engineering discipline. We have to get good at it, or we will fail ourselves, our stakeholders, our community, and the wider world.
Marketing is just a tool, and it can be used in the cause of truth and justice, too.

The engineering reality of their compromises cannot be thwarted by effort or the feeling of self-righteousness we get by not getting our hands dirty with marketing, it can only be thwarted by successfully patched systems.
Data gets weighed by the gigabyte but business critical documents spur immediate action when threatened.
Man, would that have been an easier month if we had all been talking about DeserialKiller.
If we get an A for Good Effort but do not actually achieve adoption because we stick to our usual 'Put up an obtuse notice on a server in the middle of nowhere' game plan, the adversaries win.
The Heartbleed logo is probably one of the highest ROI uses of ~$200 in the history of software security.
posts.key_insight: Getting people to patch critical vulnerabilities is a coordination problem that demands the same engineering rigor as writing the fix itself — memorable names, clear writing, and professional branding aren't vanity, they're the delivery mechanism for security.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie presents a detailed walkthrough of conversion optimization for SaaS businesses, using Freckle (a time-tracking tool) as a live consulting case study. He covers the fundamental SaaS equation (traffic × conversion rate × ARPU × retention), then systematically analyzes each step of Freckle's signup funnel — from homepage H1 copy to pricing page design to the signup form itself. He demonstrates how to craft headlines that address specific customer objections, why buttons should look like buttons with benefit-focused copy, and how pricing pages should stomp objections rather than list features. He also emphasizes charging more as the easiest lever, shares how adding a $200 plan doubled his own revenue, and argues that in-app onboarding tours are critical for B2B SaaS retention.
key_points: Before A/B testing anything, map your funnel numerically to find the highest-leverage bottleneck — optimizing a step with 98.5% completion is wasteful compared to one at 30%
Headlines should address a specific hypothesis about why visitors leave: they don't understand what the product is, what it does for them, or why it's for them specifically
Pricing pages are your sales manager — they should stomp objections (trust, cost, team adoption) rather than list features nobody remembers
Adding a higher-priced tier to Appointment Reminder doubled revenue with zero other work, reinforcing 'charge more' as the easiest SaaS growth lever
In-app onboarding tours create a teacher-student dynamic where users trust business claims more than they would on a marketing page
money_quotes: In a world of infinite choices, if something isn't made exactly for me, then I'm gone.
Nobody woke up this morning and was, 'Do you know what I want to do today? I'm going to work, and I'm going to see plans and pricing.'
The biggest enemy of every company is the back button.
Features don't sell software. Benefits sell software.
Your lowest paying customers are pathological. They have higher churn rates. They have unreasonable expectations for your product.
People always trust engineers more than marketers and salesmen.
If you don't have 3,000 visitors a month, your entire A/B testing strategy is throw out everything you ever read about A/B testing. Talk to customers. Ship stuff to them.
Sell to businesses, not to teachers, because teachers have no money and have very unreasonable expectations. Businesses have lots of money and very reasonable expectations.
posts.key_insight: Conversion optimization starts not with A/B testing buttons but with mapping your funnel numerically to find the real bottleneck, then building a mental model of why prospects leave at that step.

posts.summary: This podcast episode covers launching new products, with Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discussing creditcard.js as an example of a well-scoped developer product, Keith's new SaaS Summit Evergreen for dripping online courses, and concierge onboarding as a powerful conversion tactic. They explore tiered SaaS pricing strategies, the importance of aligning price with customer success, and why B2B dramatically outperforms B2C for small software businesses. The conversation also covers using Standard Operating Procedures to delegate repetitive tasks, insights from MicroConf on scaling businesses, and the practical realities of customer acquisition costs and lifetime value calculations for SaaS.
key_points: Concierge onboarding can triple conversion rates (25% to 75%) by removing friction from the setup process, making it one of the highest-leverage investments in a SaaS business
Tiered pricing captures customer surplus through price discrimination, and even renaming a plan 'Enterprise' instead of 'Hobbyist' can justify dramatically higher prices in B2B contexts

B2C software is increasingly hostile territory for small businesses because platform companies commoditize software as a complement to their ecosystems, while VC-funded competitors can sustain negative margins
Standard Operating Procedures allow founders to delegate repetitive tasks to employees before automating them, preserving flexibility to iterate on the process
SaaS pricing should align with customer success metrics like team size or revenue rather than arbitrary technical measures like repository count
money_quotes: Someone out there will eventually crack the Da Vinci code of getting scalable customer acquisition for SaaS businesses. And that person is going to be a billionaire.
It's just pure spite and hatred for your customers that you don't tell them that email address is wrong or that email address is right when doing logins or password recovery.
You can often add 25 percent plus to the enterprise value of the company for two days of work on the pricing page.
I default to not rewriting code because code, after you've written it, it's nice that it keeps executing for forever. The downside is it keeps executing for forever. You need to maintain it.
People forget their passwords like you wouldn't believe.
His charge out rate is $500 an hour and he's arguing about $150 to save him two hours.
If you're not on my top two issue list, I'm not going to buy your thing.
We ROFLstomped capitalism, basically.
posts.key_insight: The highest-leverage activities in a software business are concierge onboarding to maximize conversion, thoughtful tiered pricing to capture customer surplus, and systematizing repetitive work through SOPs before automating it.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Steli Efti, founder of Close.io, about high-touch software sales for SaaS businesses. They discuss how engineers are socialized to avoid sales but can actually excel at it by reframing sales calls as customer development conversations. The episode covers practical tactics for getting started with inbound sales, handling rejection by tracking 'noes' as progress, qualifying leads by asking what it would take to become a customer, and pipeline management. Steli also shares the story of pivoting from a successful 60-person sales consultancy to focus entirely on Close.io's SaaS product, driven by the software's natural momentum and the consulting business's painful cash flow dynamics.
key_points: SaaS businesses with customer lifetime values above $2K-5K are leaving significant money on the table by not making phone calls to trial signups, even if it feels uncomfortable or 'old-school'
The most powerful sales question is 'What would it take for you to become a customer?' — it reveals buying intent, maps the purchasing process, and eliminates surprise obstacles
Rejection should be reframed as measurable progress: if nine noes yield one yes, each rejection is a checked box moving toward the sale, not a personal failure
Telling prospects they shouldn't buy your product builds extraordinary goodwill, generates referrals, and reduces costly churn from poor-fit customers
Steli shuttered a multi-million-dollar consultancy because SaaS revenue with 90%+ margins and natural momentum beat the painful cash flow cycles and management overhead of a services business
money_quotes: If you actually want to convince somebody to sell somebody on something, a phone call or in-person meeting are still a lot more richer environments, more successful environments, to make that happen.

Instead of focusing on the 10 wins, what you can do is focus on the losses, and say, 'I know that for every nine noes I get, I will get a yes.' I'm not trying to earn the success, I'm trying to work my way through the failure.
Sales is not about just extracting moneys from people's pockets unwillingly. That's theft. That's a great business model until you're thrown in jail.
'Maybe' is a deathtrap. 'Maybe' doesn't point in any direction or any timeframe. You know nothing, and it occupies mind space and mindshare. Yes and no are equally good. They are a result.
If you're not face-planting once in a while, no matter how good or successful you are, you know that you're not learning anything. You're not pushing hard enough.

One business is literally pushing a little stone up a mountain and it gets bigger and bigger, but it gets harder and harder — and the other business is tipping the little stone down the mountain, and it just naturally gains more and more momentum.
Software is a gigantic industry which itself consumes a lot of software, for the obvious reason. It is as valid a market as any other industry for B2B products, and is flush with cash.

If you ask them for the close — which is basically asking the other party to become a customer — you've separated yourself already from the majority of the market, or even the majority of sales people.
posts.key_insight: Engineers who reframe sales as structured customer development — asking questions, qualifying intent, and creating outcomes rather than 'maybes' — can dramatically increase SaaS revenue without becoming the manipulative salespeople they fear.

posts.summary: McKenzie uses an elaborate Harry Potter fanfiction dialogue to explain how the Stellar cryptocurrency protocol works at a conceptual level. The piece walks through fundamental concepts of money, debt, trust, IOUs, gateways, currency exchanges, and distributed consensus by having Hermione, Harry, Ron, and a Defense Against the Dark Arts professor (representing Bitcoin maximalists) interact with the 'Stellarmus' system. Along the way, it contrasts Stellar's trust-based consensus model with Bitcoin's proof-of-work system, highlights the counterparty risks inherent in any financial system, and skewers Bitcoin maximalist ideology. McKenzie concludes that Stellar is an interesting proposal that may someday matter, while reaffirming his skepticism of Bitcoin.
key_points: Stellar is fundamentally a protocol for transferring IOUs between trusted gateways, not a standalone currency — the stellar token is just a convenience for bridging disconnected trust graphs
All financial systems require trust somewhere: Bitcoin trusts miners and incentive compatibility, Stellar trusts a set of known validators, and fiat trusts governments and regulated banks
Currency exchange rates on trust networks are determined by market makers, and naive users can lose significant value if they don't understand how the trust graph and order book work

The Bitcoin maximalist position that 'trustless' currency eliminates counterparty risk is undermined by the fact that Bitcoin holders still need exchanges, which reintroduce exactly the trust problems they claim to solve
Network effects arguments for cryptocurrency dominance are self-defeating: if network effects were truly insurmountable, fiat currencies would have prevented Bitcoin from gaining any traction at all
money_quotes: All things are worth what someone will happily trade you for them.
No one is worth unlimited trust, young Master Potter, not even a goblin.

Galleons are a currency, Master Potter. They happen to have a bit of gold in them, to be true, but the real magic of them — a strikingly ordinary kind of magic — is that the inhabitants of Wizarding Britain want to have them.
If you want to get rich, you should study hard in school and create something of value.
People who trusted that institution deserve what they got for trusting anyone. This just reinforces the need for a trustless currency.
Wouldn't that have been an insurmountable barrier against the success of your own network, which you appear to think is succeeding?
You think a far-reaching conspiracy can't simultaneously capture all your trusted institutions? I love the young and naive.
It looks like a very interesting proposal for something that may some day be a toy, and some people I trust believe the toy may eventually be more than a toy.
posts.key_insight: Every financial system — including supposedly 'trustless' cryptocurrencies — ultimately requires trust in someone, and the honest question is not whether to trust but whom and how much.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Samuel Hulick, a user onboarding expert, about how SaaS companies fail at onboarding and how to fix it. They discuss how organizational structure (Conway's Law) causes onboarding gaps, with 40-60% of free trial users never logging in a second time. The conversation covers lifecycle emails, product tours, empty states, and the importance of guiding users toward accomplishment rather than just showing features. The second half shifts to how Samuel built his reputation as 'the user onboarding guy' through public teardowns, grew an email list to 11,000+, and earned $70K from a self-published book. Both emphasize the power of building assets, owning your niche, and shipping things rather than perpetually preparing.
key_points: Conway's Law directly impacts onboarding: marketing teams stop caring after signup and product teams focus on features, leaving nobody responsible for bridging the gap to engaged users
40-60% of SaaS free trial users never log in a second time, making the first five minutes of product experience critically important
Lifecycle emails triggered by user behavior are among the highest-leverage onboarding improvements, including automated ROI calculations and trial extension offers for inactive users
Product tours fail when they ask users to learn by remembering rather than learn by doing — the best tours guide users through accomplishing something real
Building a career niche requires finding the 'known groan' — a specific problem people have an emotional reaction to — and consistently producing useful public work in that space
money_quotes: Much of the time, if there's an onboarding issue in the user experience, it's a lot of times derived from the way that the teams were split up to begin with.
You do lots of stuff that doesn't scale, and then use the stuff that doesn't scale as fuel for the mass-scalable approaches that affect the rest of the customer base that doesn't talk to you.
Somewhere between 40 and 60 percent of the people who start a free trial will never log into the software a second time.
Why are we telling a human to do something that a computer can do better? Let's fix this.
I would strongly encourage, from both a UX point of view and a maximizing the future value of your passport to your future self point of view, that you put your best work on your own darn website.
You can have a very happy, fulfilling, rewarding career just by being the spackle between different teams in an organization.
I wouldn't say just start a blog to have a blog or start a podcast to have a podcast. The lens that I would use on that is just start contributing things that people find useful.
Everything about life gets better after shipping things.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental feedback loop of successful SaaS companies is doing unscalable things — like live chat and manual customer interactions — to fuel the scalable improvements in onboarding, lifecycle emails, and product experience that serve the thousands of customers who never talk to you.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a comprehensive insider's guide to doing business in Japan, drawing on his decade of experience as a foreign entrepreneur. The essay centers on the salaryman system as the defining institution of Japanese professional life, explaining how it creates a near-total dependency between employee and employer that pervades housing, relationships, social life, and identity. This system makes startups extraordinarily risky in social terms, constrains hiring, and shapes how software is sold (high-touch, relationship-driven). Despite these challenges, McKenzie argues Japan represents a massively underserved market with weak competition, where even adequate technology feels like wizardry from the future. He balances frank discussion of systemic racism and bureaucratic friction with genuine affection for Japan's culture of personal service and relationship-based trust.
key_points: The salaryman system functions as a total institution: employees trade absolute loyalty for lifetime security, and opting out permanently damages one's social standing and career prospects
Japanese startups must hire from the margins of society — women, foreigners, and 'misfits' — because the salaryman system monopolizes conventional talent
Selling software in Japan virtually requires high-touch, relationship-driven sales even for low price-point products, making enterprise software more viable than SMB software
Japan's technology market is 5-10 years behind the US skill curve in web/mobile but represents a massive, underserved opportunity with weak competition from both startups and megacorps
Being a foreigner in Japan involves navigating real institutional discrimination alongside a culture of extraordinary personal service and loyalty
money_quotes: The salaryman/employer relationship is best characterized as 'You swear yourself to us, body and soul, and in return we will isolate you from all risks.'
Most people want to become wealthy so they can consume social status. Japanese employers believe this is inefficient, and simply award social status directly.
Otaku, Koreans, foreigners, dropouts, I'll hire anybody who can do the work. You're bargains.

In an ideal world there would be no racists, but in the less than ideal world that you may find yourself living in, at least hope to run into ruthlessly capitalist racists, because that's something you can work with.
UX and design which Silicon Valley companies would consider barely adequate for an internal admin app would strike Japanese customers like wizardry from the future.
I was immediately asked to leave the apartment 'at your first available convenience' because 'self-employed' is about one half-step above 'homeless vagabond' in terms of social esteem in Japan.
There exists a customer of the bank who habitually makes USD wire transfers using Lloyds and, accordingly, it is my business to know this.
Few things in life are worth fighting over. Fights that are worth fighting are usually worth winning.
posts.tone: candid, witty, deeply informed, affectionate yet unflinching
posts.key_insight: Japan's salaryman system creates a total social institution so powerful that opting out carries enormous personal risk, but this same rigidity leaves the market massively underserved by technology, creating extraordinary opportunities for those willing to navigate the cultural complexity.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie's 2014 year-in-review reveals his businesses grossed roughly $200,000 with $120,000 in profit across Appointment Reminder, Bingo Card Creator, and productized consulting. He candidly admits that Appointment Reminder, despite being his main focus since 2010, hasn't reached the $500k/year level he expected, largely because he doesn't find the problem space exciting. BCC declined 33% in revenue on autopilot while he struggled with burnout and health issues. His biggest regret is failing to ship a pre-sold conversion optimization course, which blocked his entire product pipeline. He moved his family from rural Ogaki to Tokyo, finally hired help for AR, and decided definitively against raising venture funding.
key_points: Appointment Reminder grew MRR 35% to ~$6,500/month but remains far below McKenzie's 2010 projection of $500k/year, largely because he finds the problem space boring and avoids work on it
Bingo Card Creator declined 33% in revenue to $33k but required less than 5 hours of work all year, yielding an implied ~$4,000/hour wage
McKenzie's failure to ship a pre-sold conversion optimization course became his biggest regret, blocking his entire productized consulting pipeline and causing substantial stress
He finally hired a sales rep and virtual assistant for AR, acknowledging he waited far too long to bring people on, and built sales automation software that reinvigorated his interest
He decided definitively against raising venture funding for AR because he doesn't want to commit 5+ years to a problem that's 7/10 fun on its best days
money_quotes: Is it weird to say that is distinct from actually wanting the money? I wouldn't mind a little bit more to establish more of a buffer for my family, but other than that, money qua money doesn't buy me anything I don't already have. It might as well be WoW gold.
Life is too short to do that. I certainly do not intend on devoting 90%+ of my career for the next five years to Appointment Reminder, so I won't take investment for it.

Peldi told me I should do a business which excites me. I told him that Appointment Reminder was a boring problem space but a great business, and that I'd find some challenge in it which was different than what I'd done with BCC.
I walked it into the bank and was shaking because I thought they'd shoot me for attempting a robbery. They didn't.
How can I trust you with my business long-term if you won't answer an easy question on day 1?
The $8.95 I paid for the domain name is probably the best ROI of anything I've ever bought.
The slip date shipped repeatedly — that spelling mistake was unintentional but I like it so much I'm leaving it in.
Some days I feel like I have a few mana pools of creative energy — one for programming, one for writing/speaking/podcasting, one for generic business stuff.
posts.key_insight: Passion for the problem you're solving matters more than the business opportunity — without it, even a smart operator will chronically underinvest in a viable product.

posts.summary: Patrick and Keith discuss how having families has transformed how they run their businesses. They cover updates on their respective products (Appointment Reminder and Summit Evergreen), then dive into how children create forcing functions for better time management, delegation, and productivity. The conversation touches on Silicon Valley's youth-obsessed culture, Japan's structural problems with work-life balance and declining birth rates, and the importance of tracking key business metrics. Both argue that family constraints actually improve business outcomes by forcing prioritization and delegation rather than endless tinkering.
key_points: Having children creates hard stops and forcing functions that paradoxically improve business productivity by requiring delegation and ruthless prioritization
The Silicon Valley mindset of grinding in your 20s and deferring family is a youth-focused myopia — most career value is created later, not earlier
Japan's declining birth rate stems from overwork culture and lack of institutional support for nuclear families, not simply personal choice
Menial tasks feel productive but are toxic because they substitute for high-leverage work — the seductive quality of feeling like work without driving the business forward
Tracking a small number of key metrics visibly and constantly creates a psychological effect that drives subconscious focus on what matters
money_quotes: Since failure is not an option, given that we're fathers, fatherhood succeeds where a bunch of New Year's resolutions in the previous years did not.
Menial tasks are easy to do, and they certainly feel like you're winning when you're doing them, which is the most toxic thing ever.
Someone who read to me as being about 23 asked me when I was 30, 'Aren't you supposed to be a VC now? I mean, your career is pretty much over, right? You're 30.'
I think both of us plan on doing this essentially forever. Probably 98 percent or so of the value that we're going to create in our careers is in the forward mirror as opposed to the rearview mirror.
I like this identity of myself as solo entrepreneur but I'm not willing to compromise on my family, because that's a terminal value for me. That's not on the table.
If you explain babysitting to an average, middle class Japanese person, you get looks of unrestrained terror on their face.
Back when I was salaryman, I had no time and I had no money. 100 percent of why Bingo Card Creator worked was savvy, savvy and more savvy on the media marketing front.
posts.key_insight: Family constraints are not obstacles to business success but forcing functions that drive better delegation, prioritization, and ultimately faster growth than the unconstrained solo-founder lifestyle.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie details the design and implementation of CSV/Excel upload for his SaaS product Appointment Reminder, a feature he delayed for four years due to its estimated $100K engineering cost. By leveraging two open-source libraries—SheetJS for in-browser spreadsheet parsing and Handsontable for grid editing—he reduced implementation to 3.5 days. The post covers the technical architecture including client-side parsing, Redis-backed job processing, feature flags for gradual rollout, and the UX challenges of column mapping and error handling. He concludes with a meditation on OSS sustainability, arguing that SaaS businesses should pay for commercial licenses rather than relying on the donation model.
key_points: CSV import is deceptively complex due to column mapping ambiguity, pervasively unclean data, and inconsistent file formats—making the typical upload-parse-display-errors workflow enormously frustrating for users
Client-side parsing with SheetJS eliminates the upload round-trip entirely, letting users edit and fix data in-browser before submission, which is a transformative UX improvement
Feature flags enable gradual rollout where internal users absorb the catastrophic errors before customers ever see them, turning the product owner into the canary in the coal mine
Redis serves as the upload data store for four compelling reasons: encryption, built-in expiry, architecture-independence from local filesystems, and elimination of file execution vulnerabilities
OSS projects serving commercial users should offer commercial licensing instead of donations, because companies can easily buy software but culturally and structurally resist donating
money_quotes: Most data which will be new to your system is not actually new. It presently exists somewhere in the customer's organization.
I assessed this feature as probably costing $100,000 in engineering time to implement well, so I punted and implemented a quick 'concierge upload feature.'
You would think the CSV file format is fairly well-standardized. Hah. Hah. Hah. *cries*
I shipped it anyhow, because shipping beats not shipping.

This forces clients to continue emailing us their CSV files to get them uploaded, which lets me have the catastrophic, terrible UX, blow-up-straight-in-my-face errors that still exist in this feature happen to a patient and incredibly invested product owner (me) rather than an impatient user on their first day.

What is the difference between a commercial license that is completely discretionary and a donation? I can donate money, and like most middle class people, my actual behavior with regards to donations is 'occasionally donates tens of dollars to poor people and other deserving causes.' My company is literally not allowed by the tax code to donate money, but it can buy any software it feels like.
Nobody's landlord accepts pull requests as currency, so I prefer contributing with money.
Payments between parties in the global wealthy class (i.e. most software companies and developers) is a solved problem — don't let whole minutes of hassle scare you away from trying it.
posts.key_insight: The combination of client-side spreadsheet parsing and pragmatic engineering tradeoffs can reduce a $100K feature to days of work, but the real insight is knowing when to ship imperfect software behind feature flags rather than waiting for perfection.

posts.summary: This podcast episode features Jay Winder, CEO of MakeLeaps (a FreshBooks for Japan), discussing his experience raising a $750K seed round through AngelList syndicates after bootstrapping for four years. The conversation covers how AngelList's syndicate mechanism dramatically simplifies angel investing logistics, reducing transaction costs for both entrepreneurs and investors. They discuss negotiation tactics around standardized deal terms and the power of negotiating from abundance. The episode also explores Japan's underdeveloped SaaS ecosystem, the cultural tendency of Japanese companies to build rather than buy software, and the counterintuitive social proof that comes with funding announcements versus bootstrap success.
key_points: AngelList syndicates consolidate dozens of small angel investors into a single cap table entry, making remote fundraising from Japan feasible and dramatically reducing legal and administrative overhead.
Standardized deal documents (like Y Combinator SAFEs) combined with oversubscription give founders strong leverage to reject requests for preferential terms from individual investors.

Japan's SaaS ecosystem is underdeveloped because the country lacks the cycle of successful exits creating experienced angel investors, and large Japanese companies prefer building competitors over acquiring startups.
Japanese companies approach software development with a hardware mindset — requiring completeness before shipping rather than iterating — which puts them years behind competitors who ship MVPs.
Bootstrapped companies doing well get significantly less media attention and social proof than funded companies, even when nothing material has changed except the funding announcement.
money_quotes: The build-versus-buy decision for anything at a SaaS company which isn't something you actually sell should come down to 'Can I do this before lunch?' If yes, build. If not, buy or adopt OSS.
Angel investors are the limiting factor to any startup ecosystem.
Japan is still 15 percent of the entire world's GDP. It's still the third largest economy in the world, full of affluent businesses and consumers.
The magic words for negotiation are 'mutually beneficial agreement.'
This is one deal out of a universe of many abundant deals that are going to happen over the course of your business career.
Typically, when it comes to relationship breakdowns, it's just a lack of communication about what everybody is thinking. In business and in life!
Literally nothing has changed between now and when the money hit our bank one week ago. Why are you interested to interview me now?

Japanese companies approach software the same way as they have successfully in the past approached hardware. You can't show someone hardware that's 20 percent complete because that's zero percent complete.
posts.key_insight: AngelList syndicates are transforming seed-stage fundraising by collapsing transaction costs, standardizing terms, and enabling founders — even those outside Silicon Valley — to negotiate from strength through oversubscription.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces Starfighter, a company cofounded with Thomas and Erin Ptacek that will publish free-to-play Capture The Flag programming games designed to teach rare programming skills and serve as work-sample tests for hiring. The business model is contingency recruiting: companies pay Starfighter a fee when they hire players who demonstrate exceptional skill through the games. McKenzie argues that traditional tech hiring is fundamentally broken, filtering on resumes and keywords rather than ability, and structurally excluding qualified candidates. Starfighter aims to replace this with direct skill measurement, connecting top-performing players with CTOs rather than HR inbox black holes. The post also notes McKenzie is selling Bingo Card Creator and transitioning away from self-employment to focus on Starfighter full-time.
key_points: Starfighter CTFs are free work-sample tests that teach rare programming skills while simultaneously assessing candidates for employers

The tech industry's hiring process is structurally broken, filtering on credentials and keywords rather than demonstrated ability, which excludes qualified candidates including those from underrepresented backgrounds
The business model is contingency recruiting — companies pay a percentage of first-year salary when they hire Starfighter-sourced candidates, so players never pay
The only way to 'game' the assessment is to actually learn the skills employers want, which Starfighter considers an epic success rather than a problem
McKenzie is leaving self-employment and selling Bingo Card Creator to focus on Starfighter full-time
money_quotes: We're not here to fix the technical interview: we're here to destroy it, and create something new and better in its place.
You will code to play. You will not pay to code.
The technology industry is fundamentally unserious as to how it presently identifies and employs engineers.
Donald Knuth would be selected out of the hiring process for senior C programmer with Unix experience before any human had ever considered him at most tech companies.
The technology industry has, through neglectfully embracing hiring policies which are so irrational as to shock the conscience, created a gigantic mountain of free money.
Starfighter claims that lonely mountain as its birthright. We are going to slay the dragons guarding it and then strip-mine it.
We come here not to serve technology recruiters, but instead to replace them with a small shell script.
Are we worried that players will teach themselves these skills just to play Starfighter? We're counting on it.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's reliance on resume filtering and whiteboard interviews over work-sample skill assessment represents both a massive market inefficiency and a structural barrier to entry that a well-designed CTF platform can profitably dismantle.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie shares his complete salary and consulting rate history to support the #talkpay movement, arguing that information asymmetry in compensation negotiations systematically disadvantages employees. He traces his journey from $10/hr order entry through Japanese salaryman wages to consulting rates of $30k-$50k per week, demonstrating that the difference between low and high compensation is rarely about skill but about positioning, packaging, and market knowledge. He argues that well-compensated professionals have a moral obligation to share compensation data to help those earlier in their careers. The post also covers equity valuation, business sales, and negotiation tactics.
key_points: Employers have near-perfect salary information while candidates often have none, creating a structural negotiating disadvantage equivalent to trading on a stock exchange where only the counterparty can see the ticker

The difference between $100/hr and $30k/week consulting was not about skill improvement but about targeting higher-value engagements, better packaging, and overcoming impostor syndrome about charging premium rates
Charging premium rates creates a virtuous cycle: you can be picky about engagements, deliver better results, build stronger case studies, and negotiate from a position of strength
Never disclose a previous salary because it pegs your new compensation to that number plus a small increment rather than your actual value to the new employer
Well-compensated professionals should share salary data as breadcrumbs for those still leveling up, since nobody is born with reliable market data
money_quotes: Compensation negotiations are presently like a stock exchange where only your counterparty can see the ticker and order book. You'd never send an order to that exchange — it would be an invitation to be fleeced.
I was nearly exactly the same level of capabilities at $30k a year as I was at $30k a week, I just got better at organizing the business to expose me to opportunities to capture the better rate.

There is virtually no difference in the mechanics of work done between $100 an hour, $200 an hour, and $30k a week — all of the leveling up there is in sophistication on who you go after, what engagements you propose and deliver, and how you package things for clients.
Disclosing a previous salary is almost always against your interests because it pegs your new salary to that plus 5% rather than your value to the new firm minus a discount.
Pick a number, any number: somebody does a lot better than that. For most numbers you'd naively think of, its an awful lot of somebodies. $100k? Not the top. $250k? Not the top. $500k? … Not the top.
If a client says you're out of the budget, start talking about the scope of the engagement to find something they can afford rather than slipping your rate.
The main thing holding my rate down for the early years was personal discomfort with being 'worth' the types of rates which I desired to charge.
Don't be hesitant to be compensated equitably for the value you are creating.
posts.key_insight: The gap between low and high compensation is primarily about information asymmetry and self-positioning, not skill — the same person can earn 50x more by targeting higher-value work and overcoming the discomfort of charging what they're worth.

posts.summary: McKenzie details the design and engineering behind Stockfighter, a programming game built by his company Starfighter to improve engineering hiring. The game casts players as Wall Street traders who must write code to interact with a realistic stock exchange simulation via REST APIs. He explains why they chose a finance setting (it naturally encompasses diverse engineering challenges), how they built a high-performance stock exchange in Go capable of processing orders in 100-200 nanoseconds, and how simple trading bots produce surprisingly complex emergent market behavior. The piece covers their tech stack decisions (Go for performance-critical exchange code, Ruby for game logic, React for the trading UI, NSQ for messaging), level design philosophy emphasizing specified goals with unspecified solutions, and their commitment to browser-based accessibility to avoid gatekeeping.
key_points: The stock exchange processes orders in 100-200 nanoseconds using Go, with the order book held in memory as a red-black tree — a real engineering workout compared to typical CRUD applications
Simple, non-colluding bots with diverse strategies produce emergent market behavior sophisticated enough to model phenomena like market panics, making level design far cheaper than expected

The finance setting was chosen not for domain specificity but because Wall Street naturally encompasses networking, APIs, databases, distributed systems, concurrency, and virtually any other engineering challenge
Starfighter deliberately avoids gaming-specific cultural signifiers like fantasy settings, rejecting the idea that tech should be gated by enthusiasm for geek culture

The game requires browser-based playability because requiring toolchain setup would create heavy dropoff, especially for less experienced engineers who find installing a Rails toolchain to be a hard, frustrating experience
money_quotes: Starfighter doesn't ever intend to build gates (gates close; that's what they are for). We dynamite gates.
Business has less dragons and better loot.
Markets are distributed systems. The exchange's view of reality and your trading system's view of reality are, by necessity, separated by the great firewall known as 'physics.'
What if players reverse engineer your world simulation? Then I pin a freaking medal on that player (and ask whether they're happy in their current career).
Retail investors should not trade actively. If someone thinks Stockfighter has given them the confidence to try actively trading for real, I will bang my head into the wall several times.
If you have never worried about the speed of light between your systems, you're not doing HFT.
It was particularly wild when I realized that our cute little toy hosted on a t2.micro was a $X0 million or $Y00 million engineering project back in living memory.
Every project which ships ships with one decision that you feel the need to say 'Look, I know, I know, but it actually worked.'
posts.key_insight: A realistic stock exchange simulation built with simple components produces emergent complexity that naturally tests the full spectrum of engineering skills — from systems programming and distributed systems reasoning to algorithm design and data analysis — without artificially constraining solutions.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains the fundamentals of stock exchange mechanics and algorithmic trading for generalist programmers, using Stockfighter (Starfighter's programming game) as the vehicle. He walks through order books, limit orders, market makers, spreads, liquidity, and risk management, then describes how Stockfighter lets developers practice these concepts via a REST API without risking real money. The post doubles as both a trading primer and a product pitch for Starfighter's hiring-through-gameplay business model. He argues that most web developers underestimate their ability to tackle problems like algorithmic trading, and that the barrier is psychological rather than technical.
key_points: Stock exchanges are built around order books—prioritized queues where bids and asks cross to create trades—and the core algorithm is surprisingly simple to implement
Market makers provide liquidity (time travel and teleportation as a service) by taking on price risk, and the spread is their compensation for that risk
HFT firms are mostly just market makers competing on speed, which benefits retail investors by compressing spreads down to a penny
Most developers believe algorithmic trading is beyond them, but a basic market maker can be written in ~144 lines of Ruby
Starfighter's business model is using programming games to identify talented engineers and connect them with employers
money_quotes: Every great developer you know got there by solving problems they were unqualified to solve until they actually did it.
I have a sneaking suspicion Wall Street invented many of these words to give customers the impression 'This is all really, really difficult — pay us money and the complexity goes away.'
A market maker's job is TTTaaS: Teleportation and Time Travel as a Service.
Bad news: teleportation and time travel aren't actually possible. This means you take on risk.
I ran the math and, on my portfolio of ~$80,000, I've paid approximately $6 to market makers over the last ten years.
Liquidity really does exist primarily because market makers are actively adding it.
The market doesn't believe there exist enough engineers who thrive when given a novel, hard problem. We think you're out there, in multitudes.
You can drive a car with your feet if you want to, but that doesn't make it a good idea.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic trading is far more accessible than most programmers assume—the core data structures and algorithms are straightforward, and the real barrier is the belief that finance is too complex to approach.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews salary negotiation expert Josh Doody about tactical approaches to negotiating compensation at every stage of employment. They cover how to deflect the 'what is your current salary' question without lying, why you should always negotiate upward from any offer, how to trade across multiple compensation axes (salary, vacation, equity), and how to build a formal written case for raises at your current company. The conversation emphasizes that negotiation behavior signals competence to employers — confident professionals who hold boundaries are perceived as stronger candidates. McKenzie estimates his original salary negotiation blog post has generated over $2.3 million in added annual salary for readers.
key_points: Never disclose your current salary — it caps your negotiating range and costs you compounding losses over your entire career, potentially $100,000+
Always counter-offer no matter how good the initial number sounds, because you don't know the employer's maximum willingness to pay
How you negotiate signals your professional competence — employers respect candidates who hold boundaries and distrust those who fold under pressure
When negotiating raises internally, put your case in writing with specific accomplishments and a target number so your manager can circulate it through the approval chain
If you can't get a raise, determine whether the firm can't afford it or you've topped out your pay grade — the solution may be pursuing a promotion instead
money_quotes: This question doesn't sound like, 'Hey, I'm trying to cheat you out of $100,000' but they're trying to cheat you out of $100,000 when they ask this, and they know this.
No matter what their offer is, I will negotiate upwards.
If you perform wishy-washy in the negotiation, they count it as a strike against you.
Don't work for people who don't appreciate what you bring to the table.

Your whole goal is to essentially make them a little bit uncomfortable, push them right up to the tippy-top of their comfort zone so that you know that they're giving you the best possible salary that they can give.
Professionals, when they want something done, they put it in writing. That's a core professional skill.
I think that people who roll over immediately are less trustworthy in their eyes than people who don't. Don't roll over immediately in the face of opposition.
If they don't believe you're an honest professional, then get out of this conversation, because you definitely don't want to be working there.
posts.key_insight: Salary negotiation is not adversarial — it's a professional competence test where holding your boundaries and refusing to volunteer information signals exactly the kind of confident, trustworthy employee companies want to hire.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Thomas Smale of FE International about the process of buying and selling online businesses, drawing on Patrick's experience selling Bingo Card Creator through the brokerage. The conversation covers how SaaS businesses command a ~25% valuation premium over other online businesses due to recurring revenue predictability, with typical multiples of 3-5x annual seller discretionary earnings. They walk through the full acquisition process from initial consultation through prospectus creation, buyer matching, due diligence, contract negotiation, and handover. Patrick emphasizes the importance of preparing months in advance by separating shared infrastructure, documenting code, and keeping financials clean. The episode also reveals that many small SaaS buyers are non-technical, and that working with a reputable broker provides critical value when deals hit unexpected snags.
key_points: SaaS businesses sell at roughly 3x projected next-year earnings or 5x trailing twelve months, with a ~25% premium over content or e-commerce businesses due to recurring revenue stability
Sellers should begin preparing months before a sale by separating shared infrastructure, documenting code, getting financials in order, and having a third party review the codebase
Many buyers of small SaaS businesses ($100-200k range) are non-technical, making clean documentation and available freelancer support essential for closing deals

Continuing to run the business normally during the sales process is critical — declining metrics significantly reduce multiples, but artificially juicing numbers (like discounted annual plans) is equally damaging
Using a reputable broker provides value through structured processes, backup buyer pipelines, deal recovery when things go wrong, and standardized contract terms that protect both parties
money_quotes: The real driver there is the fact that a SaaS business has that recurring income and that baseline. From a buyer's perspective, worst case scenario, even if you can't grow the business or even if it starts to decline, you've still got that baseline of subscribers.
It always amazes me how many small business owners run businesses and they have no idea what they've got going in or out each month.
No one wants to buy a business on a decline. Businesses that are declining are going to attract a significantly lower multiple than either stable or a growing business.
Did you know it costs $200k to open a Subway or $750k in capital to open a McDonalds? The Internet is enabling a disruptively better path towards yeoman entrepreneurship.
Your $1 million offer might actually be better than the $1.2 million offer that has slightly worse terms attached to it, and a buyer that you don't necessarily think is going to be a good fit.

Many of my SaaS friends who were using legacy payment processors end up using natural churn to transition accounts between processors, since legacy processors hate doing this. This process can take years and is unimaginably frustrating.

You shouldn't really be entering into a transaction if you don't trust the other party. It should very much be fundamentally built on trust and goodwill, and ultimately two parties who actually want to deal with each other.
posts.key_insight: Selling a small online business successfully requires months of unglamorous preparation — separating infrastructure, documenting code, cleaning financials — and a structured process with a trusted broker who maintains backup buyers and standardized terms to navigate the inevitable complications.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie announces he's joining Stripe to work on Atlas, a product that aims to make company incorporation and back-office operations as simple as spinning up a cloud server. He traces his journey from a Japanese salaryman building Bingo Card Creator to becoming a prominent voice in the small software business community. He argues that the number of internet-enabled businesses will inevitably explode due to falling costs in payments, distribution, and manufacturing, and that Atlas can remove the remaining logistical barriers preventing entrepreneurs worldwide from starting companies. He frames the opportunity in terms of macroeconomic impact, noting that interventions early in the business funnel could show up in global economic indicators.
key_points: Atlas aims to abstract away all the crufty back-office work (incorporation, banking, bookkeeping, taxes) that distracts internet businesses from building products, much like Stripe abstracted away payment integration.
McKenzie argues there is no conceivable future where starting internet businesses gets harder — costs of payments, manufacturing, distribution, and customer reach will only continue falling.
The global community of small software entrepreneurs grew from forums and blogs, and McKenzie sees his Atlas role as scalably educating the next wave of millions of internet-enabled entrepreneurs.
He negotiated a clause in his employment contract allowing him to continue criticizing Bitcoin in his personal capacity, as a test of Stripe's commitment to employee autonomy.
He's winding down Starfighter and selling Appointment Reminder to focus entirely on Atlas, marking his return to employment after a decade of self-employment.
money_quotes: I knew nothing — nobody had ever told me that 'creating intellectual property' is something that you don't need to ask permission to do and my background therefore suggested it was either forbidden, risky, or risked being forbidden.
They're a society organized around attempting to find the optimal level of crazy.

Stripe solved the problem like an engineer: establishing one company requires an annoying amount of form-filling so instead of buckling down and doing it you just make a company-establishing web application and abstract away form-filling for all time.
There are probably about a hundred thousand We Who Work For The Internet. There will shortly be a few million, and there will eventually be hundreds of millions.
There is no conceivable future in which Internet-enabled firms are less numerous than e.g. insurance agents, which the US alone has ~450,000 of.

It cost $250,000 and six months of integration to charge a single credit card online in 1999. By 2006, that was down to hundreds of dollars and weeks of effort. In 2016, the integration can be done in a morning.
I work at Stripe; I work for the Internet. If you're on the Internet, I work for you, too.
Some people would feel overwhelmed if asked to write Ruby, negotiate with vendors, and construct lifecycle email sequences — that sounds to me like a fulfilling way to spend any given Tuesday.
posts.key_insight: The barriers to starting internet businesses are falling so rapidly that the main remaining obstacles are logistical and informational — incorporation, banking, bookkeeping — and products like Atlas can remove those barriers at scale to unlock an explosion of new businesses worldwide.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on a tumultuous 2016 that saw three major transitions: the shutdown of Starfighter (his CTF-based recruiting startup), joining Stripe to work on Atlas (a product to help entrepreneurs worldwide incorporate businesses), and selling Appointment Reminder to pay down $120,000 in debt accumulated during the Starfighter years. He candidly discusses how personal burn rate, leverage, and family obligations shaped his decisions, and explains why he chose employment at Stripe over restarting consulting or launching a new SaaS. The post captures the emotional and financial realities of transitioning from indie software entrepreneur to employee at a fast-growing company.
key_points: Starfighter achieved its technical goals (including an impressive insider-trading detection game) but failed as a business, leading to its wind-down and the co-founders starting Latacora
McKenzie accumulated ~$120K in credit card debt financing living expenses during Starfighter, effectively spending equity in Appointment Reminder to buy equity in the startup

Selling Appointment Reminder through FE International went extraordinarily fast—listed September 3rd with four offers within two days—thanks to meticulous preparation, professional bookkeeping, and sustained MRR growth
He chose Stripe/Atlas over starting a new business because Atlas's mission to reduce friction in starting Internet businesses worldwide offered leverage far beyond what he could achieve independently

The transition to employment brought both relief (infrastructure support, regular salary, no on-call anxiety) and adjustment challenges (slower shipping velocity, resisting the urge to work on everything)
money_quotes: A business failing because the market wasn't ready for it is natural. A business failing because it cannot get a checking account or because it operates in a jurisdiction where incorporation requires a bribe, on the other hand, is a bug.

It is not widely appreciated how fragile companies are in the early days. They grow into self-perpetuating institutions filled with skilled professionals, armies of lawyers and accountants, and the resources to solve problems great and small… but they don't start that way. The world expects them to be mighty oaks, but they all start as seeds in the wind.

When my software business actually afforded me the opportunity to take an unbounded vacation on a beach, at about week three I started telling Ruriko 'This has been wonderful and I want to come back someday but I would really, really like to get back to the usual routine.'
It's like an MMORPG made out of money.
The day after I was totally free of the business, I slept with my phone off, for the first time in five years. It was wonderful.

I was so scared about the sum that I physically walked to a Bank of America branch, presented my passport, and said 'If you have any questions, I have a binder full of documentation about where that money came from and where it is going.'
Having a low personal burn rate is really useful when starting a software business, whether you're trying to make Bingo Card Creator or the next big thing.
If I attempted to work on every intellectually interesting thing at Stripe that I'd be good at and that would be worthwhile if successful, I would die of exhaustion.
posts.key_insight: The arc from indie entrepreneur to Stripe employee was driven not by ambition but by the hard math of leverage, debt, and family obligations—and by finding a mission (reducing friction for new businesses worldwide) that offered more impact than any solo venture could.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Jonathan Siegel, a Tokyo-based entrepreneur who buys and operates small software businesses as a portfolio. Siegel describes his evolution from building products (with a ~4% hit rate) to acquiring existing businesses, starting with Exceptional and Airbrake in the error-monitoring space. The conversation covers the practical economics of selling small SaaS businesses, including seller financing, typical valuations, and why founders often sell despite ongoing revenue. They discuss separating creative passion from profit-driven discipline, the challenges of scaling teams, and how Silicon Valley culture distorts founders' definitions of success, causing them to reject life-changing acquisition offers in pursuit of billion-dollar outcomes.
key_points: Buying existing small businesses with real customers is far more capital-efficient than starting from scratch, where ~96% of new products fail
Creative passion and profit-driven business should be mentally separated — doing things for passion allows you to be a 'bad businessperson,' while profit requires discipline over creativity
Small SaaS businesses are hard to sell through traditional channels because the transaction costs of packaging a business for sale can exceed the effort that built it
Silicon Valley culture warps founders' definitions of success, causing them to reject meaningful acquisition offers that would be life-changing outside the Bay Area bubble
Running a portfolio of ~5 businesses works when you hire strong teams for day-to-day operations and focus your own time on product and go-to-market strategy
money_quotes: I buy and operate really, really miniscule businesses and I grow them to 'tiny.'
I thought, 'Maybe I'm God's gift to entrepreneuring, because look at all this great stuff that happens when I build stuff.' It turns out I was wrong.

When you have a creative outlet, it's really important to recognize when you are doing things because you need that creative control, and you want to have the passion from it, versus where you want to create profit and actually make money.

I don't even know how many friends of mine in the online software community had a weekend thing and just plugged away at it consistently for four or five years. Suddenly, 'Wait, I woke up in the morning. I've got this thing. I loved it three years ago. I don't love it anymore. But I think it's probably worth more than my house.'
If you don't take that time to write down what success is, then I think you start to become susceptible to other people's idea of success, rather than your own.

Everyone outside of San Francisco is like, 'Oh my God, this is a lottery ticket. I just got a ticket to this kind of wealth creation in such a short amount of time and all I have to do is go work for a great company afterwards?'

When I was 25 I felt like I had all the time in the world and very few high-leverage opportunities, but at 35 I feel like I have lots of high-leverage opportunities but that spending two years doing something is now a decision to be pondered carefully.
Being able to actually ship a software product is still a scarce ability in the world, and even at YC they probably wish they knew more people who could reliably do it.
posts.key_insight: The most reliable path to building wealth through software isn't chasing unicorn outcomes but acquiring and growing small existing businesses while keeping your creative passions separate from your profit-driven discipline.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide to resolving identity theft and erroneous credit report entries, drawing on his unusual hobby of ghostwriting hundreds of dispute letters from 2004-2007. He explains how credit reporting agencies and banks operate, their incentives, and their regulatory obligations under the FCRA and FDCPA. The core strategy is to present yourself as an organized professional building a paper trail, communicate only in writing, and leverage the fact that regulated institutions fear well-documented complaints far more than angry phone calls. He walks through the exact sequence: file a police report, write the bank, then the CRAs, using calm professional language that signals awareness of legal deadlines and willingness to escalate.
key_points: Never call CRAs or debt collectors — communicate only via certified mail to build a verifiable paper trail that terrifies regulated institutions
Present yourself as a calm, organized professional who tracks deadlines and keeps records, not an angry consumer demanding satisfaction — emotional detachment signals real danger to banks
Credit reporting agencies will do the bare minimum required by law and are optimized to make you go away; your leverage is making non-compliance more expensive than compliance
Never pay a penny of debt that isn't yours — paying waives legal rights and paradoxically doesn't improve your credit score on previously delinquent accounts
The system is broken at scale but can work for individuals who are patient, literate about their rights under FCRA/FDCPA, and willing to escalate through regulatory channels
money_quotes: Mean words cannot hurt a bank. Threats cannot hurt a bank. Paper trails, though, are terrifying to regulated institutions.

You never want identity theft treated like a customer service problem. Their CS department is scored on number of tickets resolved per hour, and each rep's incentives are simply to classify you as something requiring no followup and get you off the phone.

People who can file a regulatory action while being emotionless about it are terrifying, because they suggest that their day job is e.g. administrator for a hospital, that they're very comfortable with pushing papers around government agencies, and that they will remember deadlines, keep copious records, and consult with other professionals where appropriate.
Angry people demand; professionals 'require.'
Do not sign up for credit monitoring; it is a great revenue source for credit reporting agencies but almost never a good purchase for consumers.
The thing which causes regulatory incidents is well-organized people taking paper trails to regulators which allow a regulator to trivially follow up with an investigatory letter.
Never pay a penny of a debt which isn't yours. Paying waives your legal rights, because the system assumes that nobody would pay something they didn't actually owe.
Professionals know that if they want emotional support they could just buy a dog.
posts.key_insight: Regulated institutions don't fear anger or threats — they fear calm, organized professionals with paper trails who understand legal deadlines and will escalate to regulators without hesitation.

posts.summary: Japan's Furusato Nouzei (Hometown Tax) system allows taxpayers to redirect up to 40% of their residence tax to any city of their choice, receiving a 1:1 tax credit. Created to address the imbalance between Tokyo (which absorbs educated workers) and rural regions (which pay to educate them), the system evolved far beyond its original intent. Cities began offering gifts in return for donations, leveraging Japan's gift-reciprocation culture, which escalated into a competitive bidding war where towns offer near-cash equivalents worth up to 50% of the donation. Participation exploded from 33,000 people in 2008 to 2.2 million in 2016, with $2.5 billion donated, creating a thriving intermediary industry. Despite the gaming, the system broadly achieves its goals of redistributing resources and reconnecting migrants with their hometowns.
key_points: Japan faces a severe urban-rural divide where Tokyo absorbs educated workers that rural regions paid to raise, creating a free-rider problem on human capital investment
The Hometown Tax system was designed as an opt-in compromise that Tokyo accepted because it assumed low uptake due to friction, but cities got creative with gift incentives
Japan's cultural norm of gift reciprocation (roughly 30% of value) naturally bootstrapped a competitive market where cities bid for taxpayer donations with increasingly liquid rewards
The system created an entire e-commerce industry of intermediary platforms that broker donations, taking 5-10% while making participation nearly frictionless
Despite widespread gaming toward cash equivalents, the system largely achieves its redistribution goals and gives rural cities meaningful revenue and connections to their diaspora
money_quotes: There is a real sense that your children moving to Tokyo causes them to lose connection with their culture and that the rewards from the national enterprise aren't being allocated fairly.
Silly country bumpkins; can't even math.
Someone at a city government figured that it was just not appropriate to let someone just give ~3% of their salary to the city without receiving a token of appreciation in return.
And then some bureaucrat realized that this created a market: you, as a city government, can bid for taxpayers to select you as a hometown.
It's like running an e-commerce business with the special wrinkle that your customers are entirely price insensitive.

It's a great game theory problem: unless a supermajority of cities collectively agrees to limit gifts to a token number, it's strongly in a city's interest to duck the central government's questionnaires and not express any objection to the status quo.
A tax optimization that virtually anyone can take advantage of, has the explicit backing of the government, and feels wholesome.
posts.key_insight: Japan's Hometown Tax evolved from a modest redistribution scheme into a competitive marketplace because gift-reciprocation culture and unfettered donor choice created incentives that turned tax policy into e-commerce.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on his first two years at Stripe, explaining why he chose to work there instead of running his own business. He frames the decision around leverage — Stripe's scale allows his work to impact far more businesses than he could reach independently. He describes his unusual role as a senior individual contributor, his work on Stripe Atlas, security red-teaming, internal writing, and mentoring. The post also covers the challenges of hypergrowth, remote work from Japan, and how working at a large organization changed his views on ambition and impact.
key_points: Leverage is the core reason to join a larger organization: Stripe's scale means individual decisions can have outsized impact across the entire portfolio of businesses on the platform

Being a senior individual contributor means spending increasing time on metawork — culture, strategy, mentoring — rather than just 'the work' itself, and companies should have explicit IC career tracks

Hypergrowth breaks intuitive understanding of organizations: at a company doubling annually, half your coworkers always have less than a year of experience, and material impact thresholds constantly shift

Working at scale corrected McKenzie's views on ambition — properly harnessed ambition is enormously productive, and the culture of mocking big visions discourages scrupulous people from attempting extraordinary things
Internal tooling and composability are massively underinvested in at most companies; each additional tool makes wide swathes of operations more valuable in unanticipated ways
money_quotes: Stripe is a celebration of the written word which happens to be incorporated in the state of Delaware.

Mediocrity doesn't happen because a villain kills good work then cackles maniacally. BigCo doesn't set out to be mediocre… BigCo just has an awful lot of people with an awful lot on their plate, and many of them are incentivized to make locally-optimal decisions at the expense of competing global goals.

There is a great, great difference between 'Build a credit card processor? That's impossible.' and 'Build a credit card processor? That probably involves compliance with an enumerable set of regulations and writing a finite number of lines of code.'
I've also had some time to crawl around the ducts of Internet commerce, which is a beautiful fractal described by an equation capable of summoning horrors from beyond spacetime.
If you think of it as less a Strategy Memo and more a pull request for a very complicated program which directs the activities of dozens or hundreds of people, it gets a lot easier to stomach.

Sometimes the reason for an outrageous result isn't a principal/agent problem or 'Maximize the evil' or incompetence, it is that writing a dashboard which would have surfaced the problem in time to fix it was beyond the SQL capabilities of the most directly responsible individual.
Leadership is just programming for organizations.
The route to great work is mostly hire small teams of very talented people who very intently care about something and incentivize great work amongst numerous other competing worthy priorities.
posts.key_insight: Joining a high-growth company trading autonomy for leverage can multiply your impact far beyond what solo entrepreneurship allows, but it requires accepting organizational overhead and the constant recalibration that hypergrowth demands.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie dissects how discount brokerages actually make money, revealing that the vast majority of revenue comes from net interest on customer cash balances rather than commissions or the controversial 'payment for order flow.' He walks through the revenue breakdown of Schwab, TD Ameritrade, E*TRADE, and Interactive Brokers, showing that net interest accounts for 49-67% of revenue while payment for order flow is typically 1-8%. He argues that public outrage over payment for order flow is misplaced, since it actually results in better execution prices for retail investors, while the real hidden cost is the interest spread brokerages earn on idle cash. The piece concludes that commissions will go to zero, roboadvisors are a bad business below scale, and technologists holding concentrated stock positions should investigate securities lending.
key_points: Net interest on customer cash balances is the dominant revenue source for discount brokerages (49-67%), dwarfing commissions and payment for order flow

Payment for order flow actually benefits retail investors by giving them better execution than public markets, since internalizers can offer tighter spreads when counterparties lack informational edges

The real hidden cost to brokerage customers is the ~200 basis point spread between what brokerages pay on cash and what money market funds offer, equivalent to a 20 bps fee across the entire portfolio
Robinhood's business model concern isn't payment for order flow itself but product decisions that encourage unsophisticated users to trade options
Roboadvisors face a structural disadvantage: they must be transparent about low fees while brokerages hide much larger implicit costs in cash management spreads
money_quotes: Stocks, bonds, mutual funds, branch offices, call centers, blah blah blah, it all exists to justify the only pricing page that matters, and all the verbiage on the pricing page is about how much you pay the customer.
The firm could literally give away every other service; discount the mutual fund fees to zero, do away with commissions, etc etc, and they would still be profitable.
That is the cost that does not call itself a cost.
Nobody writes books about Interest Rate Spreads Are Just Too Darn High.
Citadel does not run a machine learning algorithm on your neighbor's GM trades in their IRA to inform their thinking of the true value of GM as a business.
The thing they buy vis your neighbor is the opportunity to transact with him, because doing so is virtually riskless given their setup.
Schwab charges 18 bps for cash management alone and virtually no customer has ever even thought there could be a number there.
A RIA is, basically, an investment salesman who moonlights as an amateur psychologist with better math skills than most psychologists.
posts.key_insight: The outrage over payment for order flow is misplaced because it represents a tiny fraction of brokerage revenue and actually benefits retail investors, while the real hidden cost — the interest spread on customer cash balances — goes almost entirely unnoticed.

posts.summary: McKenzie presents a comprehensive exposé of Tether and Bitfinex as interconnected components of what he calls the largest fraud since Madoff. After Bitfinex lost banking access following a 2016 hack and Wells Fargo cutting ties, they intensified use of Tether as a pseudo-dollar system and routed funds through Crypto Capital Corp, a money launderer whose principal was skimming 10% of deposits. When CCC's accounts were frozen and funds embezzled, Tether became insolvent but survived through increasingly desperate measures including paying withdrawals with cryptocurrency, using employee friends' personal bank accounts as money mules, and raiding Bitfinex customer funds. The piece traces how each operational crisis necessitated a new layer of fraud, deepening the hole and creating systemic risk for the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem.
key_points: Tether was never reliably backed by USD reserves as claimed; its reserves were actually receivables from money launderers, related-party IOUs, and cryptocurrency, with the company admitting in court to approximately 74% backing at best
