Crypto Capital Corp, Tether's primary banking partner, was a money launderer whose architect skimmed 10% of all deposits for personal use including funding a failed football league, while other funds were frozen by regulators

The fraud follows a recurring pattern where each operational crisis requires a new, deeper fraud to conceal the previous one — identical to the Mt. Gox collapse pattern McKenzie had previously documented

Tether represents systemic risk to cryptocurrency markets analogous to money market funds in traditional finance, functioning as critical load-bearing infrastructure whose failure could cascade through the entire ecosystem
McKenzie published SHA-hashed predictions to Twitter months before facts became public, demonstrating he had identified the insolvency and fraud mechanisms through independent analysis
money_quotes: Tether is the internal accounting system for the largest fraud since Madoff.
Every dollar that goes out to buy the clichéd Lambo is a dollar that went in from a speculator.
Some cryptocurrency exchanges have an attitude towards compliance which is best described as 'Bond villain.'
When your fraud has an operational hiccup, you have to fraud harder, or your fraud will be discovered!
A lot of people currently think they are richer than they are.

Bitfinex and its principals have not yet been indicted by the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York, but crucially, not in the same sense that you have not been indicted by the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York.
The community extremely underestimates the degree to which the designed intent and best use case of Tether is facilitating money laundering.
I have looked, quite a bit. I have not found a good use case yet, or the revolutionary technology advances that my friends tell me exist, but I have found some frauds.
posts.key_insight: Tether and Bitfinex form a self-reinforcing fraud where each crisis demands a deeper deception, creating systemic risk for the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem while the community collectively looks away because acknowledging the fraud would collapse the asset prices sustaining their businesses.

posts.summary: This white paper, primarily authored by Patrick McKenzie as part of a pseudonymous Tokyo working group, argues that Japan's official covid-19 case counts in March 2020 were massively understated, possibly by 5X or more. Using multiple lines of statistical evidence—Diamond Princess asymptomatic rates, Singapore airport detection data, and case fatality ratio modeling—the authors demonstrate that Japan's cluster containment strategy had already failed. The paper warns that absent aggressive intervention, Japan would face a national health crisis with over 500,000 infections by end of April 2020. It directly challenges the governmental and media consensus that Japan was weathering the pandemic well, calling voluntary behavioral changes insufficient.
key_points: Japan's testing policy systematically undercounted asymptomatic infections, detecting only ~10% asymptomatic cases versus the 48-50% rate observed in exhaustively tested populations like the Diamond Princess
Singapore's airport detected infections in Japan travelers at 5X the rate implied by Japan's official numbers, suggesting massive community transmission outside surveilled clusters
The cluster containment strategy had already failed, with Osaka and Nagoya reporting infections with no link to known clusters and hospitals approaching capacity
Japan was taking minimal societal precautions—shinkansen ridership down only 8%, mask wearing below 30% in central Tokyo, bars and restaurants open—while facing exponential growth
The paper projected 500,000+ true infections and healthcare system breakdown in major cities by end of April, calling it a crisis worse than any since the war
money_quotes: The governmental and media consensus is that Japan is weathering covid-19 well. This consensus is wrong.
This is not a strategy. We are already observing breakout infections.
This requires us to believe the evidence of our eyes and our instruments, not the evidence of our hopes.
The cluster containment strategy has failed. Medical care in epicenters will be modified to adjust to a worsening reality, within days.
As a nation, we believe we are in a peaceful spring. We are not.
We will, within the month of April, confront a crisis worse than any since the war.
Japan's testing capacity is underused, as a matter of policy.
If tourists are only getting affected by community transmission, this implies the official count is understated by 25X or more.
posts.key_insight: Multiple independent statistical analyses converge on the same conclusion: Japan's official covid-19 numbers were a gross undercount, its cluster containment strategy had already failed, and the country was weeks away from a public health crisis it hadn't yet acknowledged.

posts.summary: McKenzie explains the practice of 'dropping a hash' — publishing a cryptographic hash of a document to prove you possessed it at a specific time without revealing its contents. He walks through the properties of cryptographically secure hashing algorithms like SHA-512, explains why they are considered unbreakable by modern science, and shows how security researchers use this technique to claim credit for discovering vulnerabilities while allowing companies time to fix them. The post is explicitly written as a canonical reference for non-specialists, filling a gap he noticed in available explanations.
key_points: Cryptographically secure hashes produce a short, unique fingerprint of any data, and altering even one character of the input radically changes the output
Security researchers use hash-dropping to timestamp their discoveries without disclosing dangerous information prematurely
The guarantees of cryptographic hashing are not ritual or social convention — they are reproducible technology backed by mathematics that the global economy already depends on
The technique solves a professional incentive problem: researchers can prove priority of discovery while still practicing responsible disclosure
money_quotes: The definition of 'cryptographically secure' rounds to 'Even with hundreds of millions of dollars of computer time and the smartest mathematicians on the planet, it is impossible to produce two documents with the same hash, or to go from a hash to the document which produced it.'

It is not magic. It is not simply a shibboleth or ritual unique to the security community. It is a reproducible technology, available to anyone, that makes guarantees backed by the present limits of human understanding of the world we live in and the math which governs it.
If anyone had proof to the contrary, the global economy would be in substantial jeopardy, because much secure communication over the Internet relies upon this property.
This is not optimal from a professional incentive standpoint, because it is a very professionally significant event to have discovered how to break into Google.

All people professionally relevant to you will understand that they are certain, to far beyond the threshold they would need to bet their life savings on the matter, that you had the document as of the publication of the hash.
It is secure enough to stake a material portion of the economy on, and factually a material portion of the economy is staked on it this very second.
posts.key_insight: Dropping a hash lets anyone prove they possessed a document at a specific time without revealing its contents, using the same mathematical guarantees that secure the global economy.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reveals that he organized an anonymous working group in Tokyo that, by March 25, 2020, had concluded Japan was experiencing a widespread coronavirus epidemic despite the public consensus that Japan was handling the situation well. The group pre-registered their findings via cryptographic hashes on Twitter, then circulated a white paper through economist Tyler Cowen and other channels to accelerate policy response. Their core prediction—that Japan would face a public health crisis in April comparable to peer nations—proved correct, with Japan eventually declaring a nationwide state of emergency. McKenzie reflects on the institutional failures that allowed the epidemic to go undetected, particularly how Japan's cluster-based testing strategy (Plan A) was structurally incapable of detecting its own failure due to insufficient testing of asymptomatic patients outside known clusters.
key_points: Japan's cluster containment strategy (Plan A) was structurally blind to its own failure because it concentrated testing on symptomatic patients near known clusters, missing widespread asymptomatic community transmission
A group of four non-expert professionals in Tokyo produced a correct epidemiological forecast in 54 hours by synthesizing publicly available data, official documents, and epidemiology papers

The concept of 'sontaku'—intuiting and facilitating the preferences of other parts of a system without explicit instruction—explains institutional inertia better than conspiracy theories about sporting events
Pre-registering predictions via cryptographic hashes served both as scientific rigor and as a coded signal to trusted networks that something urgent was discovered
Too many people and organizations lost precious days waiting for permission to act on evidence they already had, suggesting epistemic humility can be invoked to dangerous excess during crises
money_quotes: It may not have been anyone's job to wake up every morning, assume Plan A was no longer viable, and search for proof that that had happened.
Imagine how surprised you would be if someone turned into a cat in front of you. That was how surprised I was.
There are few conspiracies in the world. There are many systems with complicated decision-making processes, internal data flows, and incentive structures for actors within them.
No professional courtesy, no personal discomfort, no preservation of modesty, no fear of repercussions should delay the response. Get a right enough answer. Make a right enough decision.
The alarm, buttressing training delivered far before the alarm rings, gives you unquestionable and immediate permission to evacuate.
As a nation, we believe we are in a peaceful spring. We are not.
The virus will not wait. Humanity must act faster than the disaster.
Too many people and too many organizations have passed precious days this year waiting for permission to take actions reasonable given evidence they already had.
posts.key_insight: Systems designed to monitor a specific strategy can be structurally incapable of detecting that strategy's failure, and institutional inertia means no one may be assigned the job of looking for evidence that the current plan has already collapsed.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on four years at Stripe, describing how his role evolved from hands-on Atlas work to cross-functional publishing and developer experience. He details the organizational challenges of hypergrowth, where the company grew from ~650 to ~3,000 employees while constantly reinventing its processes. He argues that Stripe's economic model is uniquely powerful because it compounds on the success of its customers, who themselves compound on their customers' success. The piece also candidly addresses his struggle with depression in 2020, the surprising value of corporate infrastructure he once dismissed, and how working at a prestigious company reshaped his network access in ways that feel meritocratically uncomfortable.
key_points: Hypergrowth means half your coworkers always have less than a year of experience, making organizational metawork a permanent tax on execution
Stripe's business model adds recursive loops on top of B2B SaaS: indexing on the count and success of customers who themselves index on their customers' success

Corporate functions like HR exist because scaling companies inevitably hit predictable challenges, and every startup that hires a VP of Sales is publishing an empirical result that the role is necessary
Operating cadence compounds continuously — consistently asking 'could we do that faster?' produces dramatic returns at surprisingly low cost
McKenzie's network access and professional credibility changed materially upon joining Stripe despite no corresponding change in skill, illustrating class dynamics within meritocracy
money_quotes: I pick a peer group wisely because you're giving them write access to both your conscious thoughts and your entire worldview.

There is a meritocracy in our class system and a class system in our meritocracy, and I feel that this is underexamined because the nuances discomfit both people who understand the nature of merit and those who understand the nature of class systems.

Every scaling startup is an experiment in empirical microeconomics research on 'What parts of the typical corporate form are necessary and which are pageantry which we only keep around due to anchoring, the sunk cost fallacy, and tradition?'
The returns to pushing your cadence to faster are everywhere and they compound continuously, for years.
A huge portion of the value creation of Silicon Valley is through directly intervening on the ambition of impressionable people.
Most organizations operate at nowhere near the frontier of their capabilities. That is a choice, and strikes me as a valid choice, but you can choose to move closer to the frontier, too.
I feel like I've done the best work of my career to date but that if it ever becomes the best work looking forward something went deeply off the rails.
The software economy is larger than you think, even after taking into account Patio11's Law.
posts.key_insight: Stripe's economic model is recursively compounding — it indexes on the success of customers who themselves index on the success of their customers — and this structural advantage, combined with relentless operating cadence, explains why insiders consistently underpredict the company's trajectory.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie describes VaccinateCA, a non-profit he led that expanded into Vaccinate The States, building the country's largest public dataset on COVID-19 vaccine availability. The project started as a hackathon-style effort where volunteers called pharmacies directly to gather ground truth about vaccine stock, then evolved into a data broker between community efforts, government agencies, and large publishers like Google Maps. McKenzie argues that the government's software procurement processes were structurally misaligned with the speed the pandemic demanded, and that a scrappy volunteer effort filled a critical gap. He calls for continued urgency in vaccinating the remaining half of America and helping other nations.
key_points: Ground truth about vaccine availability could only be obtained by calling providers directly, since official databases and county policies were routinely stale or inaccurate
Government software procurement processes move too slowly for pandemic response — counties needed months-long RFPs while VaccinateCA delivered working tools in 30 minutes
The most common publishing platform for vaccine availability in America was Facebook pages, revealing how fragmented and low-tech the public health data infrastructure was
The project evolved from a scrappy volunteer phone bank into a national data broker working with the CDC, Google Maps, and state governments
America developed a COVID-19 vaccine in two days but lacked the relatively pedestrian web application infrastructure to distribute it efficiently
money_quotes: The core insight of VaccinateCA on Day 1 was that, if doses were actually being injected, that the person actually doing the injecting both a) knew whether or not they had more doses and b) to whom they could administer them. Essentially all other data in the ecosystem were stale or lies.

It took America two days—two days—to develop a covid-19 vaccine. That is a triumph worth celebrating. Next time, we should match our world-leading scientific expertise with the relatively pedestrian web application the country needs.
It can't be harder to get vaccinated than it is to buy something on Amazon or sign up for Facebook.
The pandemic moves faster than public digital infrastructure.
It turns out that the most common publishing platform in America for vaccine availability is, I kid you not, a Facebook page.
Many governments need a months-long RFP process to get software written. We had a working URL 30 minutes after they emailed us, and localized it to 7 languages the following day.

This is not the typical startup gig; you'll get no equity, your employer will burn through their funding as quickly as possible, our TAM is declining by millions daily, and successful execution in your job duties will see all of us unemployed within months.
posts.key_insight: When government software procurement moves at bureaucratic speed during a crisis, scrappy volunteer efforts that prioritize ground truth over official data systems can fill critical gaps and save lives.

posts.summary: Apple's new policy allowing app developers to collect user contact information in-app and pitch payments out-of-app will have a much larger impact than most people expect. Game developers, who generate 60% of App Store revenue, are uniquely positioned to exploit this because they already have sophisticated systems for incentivizing user behavior through in-game rewards. Games like Genshin Impact can trivially offer small in-game rewards for providing email addresses, then redirect purchases to web storefronts at a ~10% discount, improving margins from 70% to 85%. McKenzie predicts with 90% confidence that major games will implement this within six weeks, and the ripple effects will extend to SaaS businesses and other platform ecosystems.
key_points: Apple's steering policy change is more significant than it appears because game developers already have the infrastructure to incentivize user behavior changes through in-game reward systems

Games like Genshin Impact can use virtual currency rewards (nearly free to produce) to collect emails and redirect purchases to web storefronts, improving margins by roughly 20% in a single engineering sprint

The gacha model and virtual currency systems give game developers extraordinary leverage to influence payment behavior at massive scale—Genshin Impact alone has transacted over a billion dollars through app stores
Paying users to change transactional behavior is standard practice in payments—credit card rewards programs do exactly this to compete for share of wallet
Apple's decisions on steering will likely influence policies across many other platform ecosystems, as Jobs originally established the 30% take rate as an industry Schelling point
money_quotes: This improves the margin on currency purchases from about 70% to about 85%, at a stroke, which is an incredible 20% lift for one engineering sprint.
Not implementing this costs millions of dollars per week you delay.
Paimon could be employed in a B2B SaaS company's marketing department, and would be crushing her KPIs, because unlike the typical marketer she has literally infinite gold to incentivize uptake with.
The implicit cash value of 20 primogems is approximately 25 cents but they're, of course, free to the Federal Reserve of Primogems.
Some might ask whether it is sporting to pay the user to change their transactional behavior. This is, to put it mildly, not unprecedented in payment ecosystems!

Steve Jobs effectively established a Schelling point that owning a customer relationship in digital goods was worth a 30% take rate, and everyone since has either been on that Schelling point or positioned in direct opposition to it.
All's fair in love, war, and payments.
Every credit card company has whales, too.
posts.key_insight: Apple's seemingly incremental steering policy change will trigger massive payment behavior shifts because game developers already possess the sophisticated reward systems needed to redirect billions in transactions away from app stores.

posts.summary: McKenzie argues that Tether's own quarterly reserves report from March 2022 proves it became undercollateralized during the May 2022 crypto selloff. With only $162 million in equity cushion and nearly $5 billion in volatile crypto and investment holdings, a mere 3.27% decline in those holdings would wipe out all equity — and Bitcoin alone fell 36% in that period. He demonstrates that even under the most generous assumptions and taking Tether's own numbers at face value, the math is inescapable. The essay is written explicitly for journalists and lawyers, with archived documentation and a cryptographic hash to prove the claims weren't edited after the fact.
key_points: Tether's $162 million equity cushion against $82 billion in liabilities means a 3.27% decline in its ~$5 billion crypto/investment holdings wipes out all equity
Between the June 2021 and March 2022 attestations, Tether made ~$3 billion in marginal investments that almost certainly lost substantial value in the subsequent crash
The Celsius Network investment alone — down 86% — ate more than 10% of Tether's equity, and that's just one line item
Tether's own admitted history shows reserves were fully backed only 27.6% of the time during 2016-2018, per the CFTC settlement
The crypto community's real defense of Tether is not that reserves exist but that Bitfinex will bail it out — an argument fundamentally different from Tether's public claims
money_quotes: I described Tether as 'the internal accounting system for the largest fraud since Madoff.' This remains true.
A 3.27% decrease in the value of these investments wipes out all Tether equity and causes their tokens to be undercollateralized.
Tether would have needed near omniscient prediction of the future to avoid a substantial drawdown in the value of any BTC which entered its reserves in this interval.
Impairment of 1% of one line item on their balance sheet ate more than 10% of their equity, under assumptions which are extremely generous to Tether.

Tether bragged that they had more than 24 hours of liquidity, which occasioned disbelief from financial commentators. Similar to a memorable scene in The Big Short, that's bragging one's way into a confession.

Even if we believe Tether's reserves report for the sake of argument, and we grant them very favorable assumptions as to their asset mixes and sagacity as traders, we still arrive at the result that they required recapitalization of the reserves. Their own numbers indict them.
Yep, we print money on demand and lie about this fact. It has worked out pretty well for us so far.
I wrote this essay today with attached supporting documentation for the benefit of present journalists and future lawyers.
posts.key_insight: Tether's own published financial attestation, taken at face value with generous assumptions, mathematically proves its reserves became undercollateralized during the May 2022 crypto crash due to a reckless decision to back fixed liabilities with volatile assets and a wafer-thin equity cushion.

posts.summary: McKenzie analyzes Tether's November 2022 attestation and argues that, even taking their own numbers at face value, Tether was again insolvent. With only $250 million in equity against $68 billion in liabilities and 12.86% of reserves in risk-on assets, a mere 2.86% impairment would wipe out their cushion — an impossibility to avoid given Bitcoin's 10%+ decline. He notes their accountants' careful disclaimers about extraordinary market conditions, their historical largest counterparty Alameda Research being vaporized, and the mystery of where $120 million/month in Treasury interest income disappears to. The piece frames Tether as the largest fraud in history, drawing explicit parallels to Madoff's unraveling.
key_points: Tether's own attestation shows only 0.36% equity-to-assets ratio with 270:1 debt-to-equity, meaning even trivial impairment of risk-on holdings causes insolvency
Their accountants explicitly disclaim the valuations under 'extraordinary market conditions' — exactly the conditions occurring during the FTX/Alameda collapse
Tether generates ~$120 million/month in Treasury interest yet maintains only $250 million in equity, raising serious questions about where the money goes
The crypto ecosystem may have appetite but not capacity to bail out Tether, since systemic crises drain the very dry powder needed for rescue
Tether's press releases quietly stopped claiming to be 'fully backed,' shifting focus from solvency to liquidity
money_quotes: Their equity is already vapor. They are leveraged 35:1 against their risk-on assets alone.
I challenge anyone to find a portfolio construction for digital token investments that possibly survived this environment with only a 2.86% impairment.
Tether is a gigantic piggy bank, run by confessed liars.
Their debt to equity ratio of about 270:1 would place them comfortably in the leagues of the firms that were at ground zero of the global financial crisis in 2008.
Where is it, though. How do they only have $250 million in equity if just their Treasuries are generating about $120 million in interest a month?
Tether's accountants carefully avoid saying that Tether's reserves are adequate, presumably because they like money but do not like the prospect of jail.

Even if we believe Tether's reserves report for the sake of argument, and we grant them very favorable assumptions as to their asset mixes and sagacity as traders, we still arrive at their reserves having again become insufficient. Their own numbers indict them.
Madoff lasted more than 17 years. Tether won't.
posts.key_insight: Tether's own published attestation, even if taken entirely at face value, mathematically demonstrates insolvency given any non-trivial market decline — their 0.36% equity cushion cannot survive the 12.86% of reserves held in risk-on assets.

posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie responds to a retraction request from Anchorage Digital CEO Nathan McCauley regarding McKenzie's earlier essay on debanking. McKenzie methodically dismantles each of McCauley's objections—regarding Silvergate's solvency, omission of regulatory guidance, and the definition of debanking—finding that none identify actual factual errors. The piece doubles as a meditation on the power dynamics between banks and writers, arguing that retraction demands from well-resourced financial institutions carry implicit legal threats that chill speech. McKenzie also highlights the irony that McCauley's own bank signed a consent order with the OCC for BSA/AML deficiencies while criticizing McKenzie's analysis of compliance failures at other crypto banks. The essay concludes by contextualizing Anchorage Digital as a trust company that cannot take deposits, and situating the retraction demand within the crypto industry's broader pattern of preferring to be judged on potential rather than track record.
key_points: McCauley's retraction request fails to identify a single factual error in the original essay, instead expressing disagreement with analysis and emphasis—neither of which are grounds for retraction.

The power asymmetry between banks and writers means retraction demands carry implicit legal threats that silence most publications, making it important for well-resourced independent writers to publicly resist them.

Anchorage Digital Bank is a trust company forbidden from taking deposits or making loans, and McCauley himself signed a 2022 OCC consent order for BSA/AML compliance deficiencies—undermining his authority to dismiss compliance concerns.

The crypto industry's pattern of catastrophic failures (Prime Trust, Voyager, FTX/Alameda) provides rational grounds for banks to view crypto clients as higher-risk, independent of regulatory pressure.

Crypto advocates strategically conflate losing access to specialized settlement networks with being unable to get basic bank accounts, and conflate private risk decisions by banks with government-directed debanking.
money_quotes: If one's bank does not employ an individual who understands the above paragraph, Anthropic will sell you artificial cognition for $20 a month.
Crypto advocates love a bit of risk, and love even more when somebody else has to buy them out of the downside, while they keep the upside.
A demand for a retraction by the CEO of a well-resourced financial institution is reasonably read by most outlets as being backstopped by potential legal consequences if they do not comply.
The Internet is real life. A statement made by a bank using the Internet is a statement by a bank.
This conduct is unwarranted, unprofessional, and dishonorable. It should cease.
I have never signed a consent order with the OCC regarding deficiencies in my bank's BSA/AML program, however.
Crypto advocates would prefer to be judged on their potential rather than their track record.
Kalzumeus Software does not consider this a bug and WONTFIX.
posts.key_insight: A retraction demand from a bank CEO that fails to identify a single factual error reveals more about the crypto industry's desire to suppress unfavorable analysis than about any deficiency in that analysis.
